Document 1 of 252

I hiked through prehistoric landscapes at a UNESCO World Heritage site in Newfoundland, a place of otherworldly beauty

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-hiked-through-prehistoric-landscapes-at-unesco/docview/2933925107/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+hiked+through+prehistoric+landscapes+at+a+UNESCO+World+Heritage+site+in+Newfoundland%2C+a+place+of+otherworldly+beauty&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In western Newfoundland's Gros Morne National Park, the red mesa of the Tablelands protrudes like a wound amid the 1,805-square-kilometre swath of emerald boreal forest and blue fiords. Its presence in the landscape provokes a head-scratching dissonance - and that's because it's a geologic anomaly. This part of Gros Morne holds the key to the park's 1987 designation as a UNESCO World Heritage site.
From a wind-pummelled ridge, my eyes follow the sweep of umber rocks down toward the marked Tablelands Trail in the distance. My husband and I left the trail's boardwalk behind earlier that morning, putting our navigation skills to use on the 14-kilometre off-trail trek, picking our way through loose rock and scrambling up the steep, sun-baked slopes of Winter House Brook Canyon.
Although we've reached the peak of the trail, we're actually standing on the Earth's mantle (the layer between the planet's crust and molten core). Nearly 500 million years ago, two tectonic plates collided here, thrusting up rocks that were once deep beneath the ancient Iapetus Ocean, a precursor to the Atlantic.
The site was instrumental in proving the theory of plate tectonics - garnering the park UNESCO status - which only gelled in scientific communities in the late 1960s. "The smoking gun of plate tectonics and continental drift was finding little bits of oceans preserved on continents or between continents, and that's what the Tablelands is," says Andrew Kerr, adjunct professor of earth sciences at Memorial University of Newfoundland, in St. John's.
Treading across the subterranean depths of an ancient ocean up on the Tablelands' barren plateau is as otherworldly as it sounds. In fact, the area serves as a Mars analog site for researchers at NASA and the Canadian Space Agency. The peridotite rocks here, which are green beneath their oxidized red husk, are also found on Mars, so looking at the Tablelands' ecosystem gives scientists insight into potential life on other planets. The austere terrain is laden with toxic metals, so not much survives up here beyond a patchwork of grassy bog, murky ponds and the odd carnivorous plant.
Although the upper plateau doesn't cover a huge expanse of land, it's easy to get turned around in the monochromatic rubble, and we have to consult our GPS a couple of times as we plod along the outer rim. But soon we fall into the slow, hypnotic rhythm that comes with walking for long periods, where time seems to bend and stretch. Coupled with the imperceptible centimetres-per-year pace of our shifting continents embedded in the ancient rocks beneath my feet, I feel tuned into a powerful, primordial frequency.
This sense of deep time nearly defies human comprehension, but what I can wrap my mind around continues to make my experience of the landscape here so much richer.
"That's part of why we put Parks Canada's signature red Adirondack chairs in spots like Green Gardens, to encourage people to do more than snap a quick picture, [and] to sit and contemplate the place," says Rob Hingston, a geologist and Parks Canada visitor experience officer for Western Newfoundland and Labrador.
On another morning, we make our way to the coastal Green Gardens trail to see it for ourselves. The path snakes down (and mercilessly up on the way back) through a fragrant spruce and pine forest, finally opening onto a lush green cliffside above the sea, where lashing winds roar in off the Gulf of St. Lawrence.
I follow a small footpath dotted with purple wildflowers to the edge of the bluff and peer over, greeted by a popcorn-like cliff face of grey pillow basalt and, in the turquoise shallows, towering sea stacks. These elements make for a striking tableau, and they're also further evidence of plate tectonics.
These volcanic rocks at Green Gardens are 600 million years old, predating the nearby Tablelands, and were formed when continents pulled apart to create the Iapetus Ocean. "So, we have evidence of an ocean forming here, and then evidence of an ocean closing at the Tablelands," says Hingston. "We know an event must have happened to stack all of these rocks close together, and that was a continental collision."
At another geological site in the park, Green Point, travellers can see layered shale that would have formed in the depths of the Iapetus Ocean, and Gros Morne Mountain, a monumental fist of quartzite and limestone, was once an ancient tropical beach. It's here at the park's namesake mountain, which means "great sombre" in French, that we spend our last day in Newfoundland.
The knee-knackering scramble up loose scree to reach the mountain's summit and the bone-rattling, boulder-strewn descent on the other side make for a challenging 17 kilometres. But at the peak, there's a worthy reward: a vast slate-coloured Arctic tundra that camouflages ptarmigan and Arctic hare, and a bird's-eye view of sapphire fiords and perfect jewel-like lakes.
In another 250 million years, this landscape will have shifted again; that's when scientists predict a new supercontinent will have formed. There's a melancholic relief in knowing Earth's movement continues on, centimetre by centimetre, year by year, outside of human time and influence.
Heading down the mountain, I look up to see Gros Morne live up to its name as a brooding sky gathers. It starts to rain, and I listen to my footsteps on the dark, slick trail.
Just the facts
To reach Gros Morne National Park by air, the closest airport is Deer Lake Regional Airport, about a 30-minute drive from the park's southern edge. Travellers can hike on their own, or on a guided tour with a company like Wild Gros Morne.
The summertime, from about June to September, is generally considered the ideal window for hiking. (Gros Morne Mountain is closed until the end of June to protect wildlife during a critical seasonal period.) Visit pc.gc.ca/grosmorne for detailed trip-planning tips from Parks Canada.
Chloe Berge travelled as a guest of Go Western Newfoundland, which did not review or approve this article.

CAPTION: Gros Morne's Tablelands is made of rock from beneath Earth's crust.The Green Gardens trail will take you to the coastline for views of towering sea stacks.The writer Chloe Berge on the Gros Morne Mountain trail.Gros Morne National Park's spectacular scenery includes this famous view of a fiord known as Western Brook Pond.Gros Morne National Park's spectacular scenery includes this famous view of a fiord known as Western Brook Pond.
CREDIT: Chloe Berge Special to the Star

Document 2 of 252

One premier is threatening to kill Justin Trudeau's controversial carbon levy. What happens if he succeeds?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/one-premier-is-threatening-kill-justin-trudeaus/docview/2933924956/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+premier+is+threatening+to+kill+Justin+Trudeau%27s+controversial+carbon+levy.+What+happens+if+he+succeeds%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-It has been a season of political discontent for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon price scheme. And now his government's most contentious climate policy risks having its credibility gutted in an escalating feud with Saskatchewan - a fight that is challenging the bedrock principle of the rule of law.
If that sounds dramatic, that's because it is.
This week, Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe upped the ante in his tilt against the carbon levy when he threatened to go beyond his province's refusal to collect the federal levy on natural gas home heating, and stop gathering it on all fossil fuels. The levy, as the Liberals created it, applies to all fossil fuels, including those used to heat homes and fuel cars.
That's because of how Ottawa responded to Moe's initial gambit - which included passing a bill to legalize Saskatchewan's decision to disobey the federal carbon price law - by stating it will withhold the carbon levy rebates that are supposed to be worth $1,504 for a family of four in the province over the coming year.
"If Saskatchewan people stop getting the rebate entirely, Saskatchewan should stop paying the carbon tax entirely," Moe said in a statement Thursday.
"No rebate equals no tax."
It's a potentially unprecedented situation that could have serious political consequences for a federal government that has been on its heels in its attempts to defend its flagship climate policy, one expert says. And it could also raise questions about provincial sovereignty and Crown immunity.
That's because, while Saskatchewan is threatening to defy federal legislation, it's unclear which court or remedy the federal government could pursue in an effort to impose the charges on another level of government, said Andrew Leach, a professor of law and economics at the University of Alberta.
"This is uncharted territory," Leach told the Star. Unless the federal government finds a way to respond and prove the move is a legal dead end - or Saskatchewan backs down - other provinces antagonistic to the federal carbon levy, like Alberta, could look to follow suit, he said.
"There's no clear answer to the question of what happens now."
For Keith Stewart, climate and energy campaigner with Greenpeace, the carbon levy conflagration is verging on a "problem for Confederation," with one province challenging a law - the Greenhouse Gas Pollution Pricing Act that created Canada's national carbon pricing scheme - that the Supreme Court upheld in 2021 as constitutional.
"This is a disruption of normal federal-provincial relations that shouldn't pass without consequences," said Stewart, who argued that Ottawa should withhold money for other programs if Saskatchewan breaks with the carbon price law.
"You don't get to pick and choose which rules you follow, if you're a government," he said.
But so far, the government is only willing to withhold the rebates from Saskatchewan.
"I still hope that Saskatchewan will see the light and will actually come to its senses in terms of deciding that it actually will abide by the law," Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson told reporters last Thursday.
Recall that all of this rises from the smouldering aftermath of the federal government's decision last fall to suspend the carbon levy on heating oil. Under pressure from MPs, particularly in Atlantic Provinces, worried about the burden of the levy on rural residents, the Trudeau Liberals removed the levy from home heating for three years, while boosting carbon price rebates for people in areas defined as rural and pumping an extra $500 million into programs to help people replace oil heaters with electric heat pumps.
That decision sparked blowback from opposition parties and several provinces, including Ontario, which argued it's not fair to exempt only heating oil from the federal levy. Part of the opposition stemmed from the fact that while a higher proportion of the population in Atlantic Provinces uses oil heating, federal statistics show 75 per cent of Canadians who use oil to heat their homes live west of New Brunswick - including more than 266,000 households in Ontario.
The ensuing debate grew more intense with speculation that Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault would resign if his government carved out more exemptions to the climate policy, one that his department estimates will be responsible for roughly one-third of the emissions cuts needed to hit Canada's 2030 emissions target. (Guilbeault eventually rebuffed suggestions he might step down.)
And there is another hitch. Canadians eligible for the expanded rural carbon price rebate might not get the extra money they were promised when the cash next gets distributed in April - despite the fact that multiple cabinet ministers have said they would. For this, the Liberals blame the opposition Conservatives for clogging up the legislative process in Parliament by holding up a bill they say must get passed before increased rural rebates are available.
"We call on all parliamentarians to pass this legislation so people living in rural and remote parts of the country can receive additional financial support through the Canada Carbon Rebate. For a family of four, this means hundreds of dollars back in their bank account a year," said Katherine Cuplinskas, a spokesperson for Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland, in an email to the Star last week.
"If we put forward the 'Act to Call the Sky Blue,' they would obstruct it. There is nothing that the Conservatives don't obstruct," Government House Leader Steven MacKinnon told reporters last week.
Asked about the filibuster claims, Conservatives spokesperson Sebastian Skamski accused the Liberal government of increasing taxes and pledged to scrap the carbon levy.
Over all of this is Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's incessant campaign to "axe the tax" if his party wins power in the next election. Since the fall carve-out for oil heating, the Liberals have made a concerted effort to highlight the rebates in a bid to emphasize carbon pricing as an affordability measure, not just a climate policy. As the Star first reported, those efforts included renaming the quarterly payments to the more specific "Canada Carbon Rebate," though experts have questioned whether such a rebrand could save the federal carbon levy.
Beyond the political thrust-and-parry looms the larger question: can Canada still meaningfully contribute to the global climate fight without a credible carbon price?
Poilievre has already said he wants a "carbon tax election." He might just get his wish.
CAPTION: Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe speaks during the Saskatchewan Party convention in Regina, on Nov. 4, 2023.

Document 3 of 252

We need to incentivize doctors to keep their doors open

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-need-incentivize-doctors-keep-their-doors-open/docview/2933924707/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+to+incentivize+doctors+to+keep+their+doors+open&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "I didn't go into medical school for the money, but I certainly didn't sacrifice all the time and money and years of studying to enter a career just to be running a failing business." - Ottawa family physician Dr. Ramsey Hijazi
Recently, Dr. Hijazi announced that after a decade, he is leaving his family practice in Carp, Ont., to take on a hospital job in transitional care - leaving 1,500 patients.
Ironically, with fanfare on Feb. 9, Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced an additional $3.1 billion in federal funding would be spent to improve health care in Ontario during the next three years - part of an $8.4-billion 10-year agreement.
This amount was much less than requested by the premiers. Targeted funding would more quickly assess the competency of foreign-trained physicians, enrolment in medical schools would be expanded, and 60 per cent of postgraduate positions would be devoted to family medicine.
The number of "orphan" patients without a family physician or nurse practitioner is currently 2.3 million in Ontario and 6.5 million across Canada.
In Ontario, this number will double in the next two years. Patients will then be forced to rely on walk-in clinics or visits to emergency departments with wait times of many hours.
The main issue is the staffing crisis.
Consider the severe shortage of physicians. In a recent Medical Post article, Dr. Corli Barnes, a second-year resident in family medicine, describes the disincentives to going into traditional family practice.
She is already $350,000 in debt, and one physician commented that the CRA allows her to deduct only 15 per cent of her tuition fees. Her income is too high - even as a resident - to qualify for loan assistance.
Stress, burnout and paperwork have contributed to an exodus of existing family practitioners, as has a 20 per cent pay cut in the past few years. In a recent op-ed article in the Toronto Star, OMA leaders Drs. Nadia Alam, Sohail Gandhi, and Silvy Mathew outlined why new graduates should not yet set up a family practice in Ontario.
According to a poll, 47 per cent of physicians are considering leaving their practice in the next five years.
Clearly, short-term measures are urgently needed to reverse this trend. I have two suggestions pertaining to older MDs: reduce their annual licence fee and provide government-funded short-term disability coverage.
Some 16 per cent of Ontario physicians are at least age 65 and eight per cent age 70. Earlier in their careers, most medical and surgical specialists and many family physicians derived much of their income from hospital work, where there was little or no overhead.
In later years, most work part-time in private clinics, and are charged overhead of 30 to 40 per cent plus HST.
Most colleges of physicians and surgeons charge about $2,000 per year for a licence, regardless of age or hours worked per week. Ontario's fee is $1,725.
For older MDs on a tight budget, receipt of an invoice for this amount may be the "last straw" that induces them to retire. Older MDs in P.E.I. and New Brunswick have recently been given a discounted licence fee, and annual dues for senior MDs are also much lower for all Canadian medical associations.
In the U.S., the licence fee is much lower, i.e. $735 in New York, $389 in Florida, $500 in Illinois and Arizona, and $423 in Oregon.
In September 2022, I urged Dr. Nancy Whitmore, CEO of the College of Physicians and Surgeons of Ontario, to discount the licence fee for older doctors. I have received no written reply.
Older physicians lack adequate short-term income protection when ill. They may require time off work because of a heart attack, knee or hip replacement, cancer or cardiac surgery, etc. Private insurance is unavailable.
Therefore, Ontario and all provincial governments should provide them with income protection with no waiting time, so that they eventually return to the workforce rather than retire permanently.
Consider some precedents: All provinces except for P.E.I. have parental leave programs that pay up to $2,000 per week - one for up to 26 weeks.
Recall that in 2003, Ontario created the "SARS Income Stabilization Program." This compensated doctors for 80 per cent of their previous income if lost due to illness with SARS, quarantine, or an income drop because of closure or an office or loss of operating room time.
Hopefully, my suggestions will help to lessen the rising critical physician manpower shortage, as creation of health teams and other reforms are implemented, rates of remuneration are significantly increased, new graduates emerge from medical schools and foreign graduates are "fast-tracked."
CAPTION: The number of "orphan" patients without a family physician or nurse practitioner is currently 2.3 million in Ontario and 6.5 million across Canada, Charles Shaver warns.
CREDIT: Charles Shaver

Subject: Nurse practitioners; Surgeons; Physicians

Document 4 of 252

Basic income could provide the breathing space so many need

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/basic-income-could-provide-breathing-space-so/docview/2933810115/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Basic+income+could+provide+the+breathing+space+so+many+need&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Twenty years ago, the prominent English historian Tony Judt concluded his magnum opus by looking back at the long boom that unfolded after 1945.
The so-called golden years (roughly 1945-late to 1970s) had faded into the mists of history. In the postwar years, Judt pointed to "glaring contrasts between wealth and poverty, prosperity and insecurity, private affluence and public squalor." Working-class people, particularly a growing precariat, were now exposed to "levels of economic insecurity unprecedented in living memory."
Two decades later, two Canadian writers from different ends of the political spectrum were tightly focused on insecurity. Former Bank of Canada head Stephen Poloz came out with a book called "The Next Age of Uncertainty." At the same time Astra Taylor published "The Age of Insecurity," the title of her 2023 Massey Lectures. Each recommends basic income as one way to address the insecurity gnawing away at so many Canadians.
Scientists say we have entered an age of global "polycrisis": food and housing insecurity; climate breakdown; stagflation; environmental degradation; war; ideological extremism; authoritarian politics and a crisis of democracy; massive economic inequality; and a general mistrust of institutions.
Each would be bad enough on its own. The polycrisis, however, signals the deep interconnections of systems - political and economic, environmental and cultural - under stress. A crisis in one system produces crises in the others.
The global COVID-19 pandemic did not produce this polycrisis. But it sharpened existing tensions. Pandemics are deeply unsettling, producing destabilizing effects that can last for years or even decades. Hardly a wonder that a sense of insecurity stalks the land.
The affordability crisis. Record numbers visiting food banks. Encampments of poor people mushrooming in big cities and smaller centres, with one newspaper chain publishing a massive series called "Tent City Nation." Young people, having given up on owning a home, move back to live with parents. Rents are stratospheric. Signs of climate breakdown abound, with record levels of snow and rain, soaring temperatures and unprecedented firestorms.
Unsurprisingly, a populist Official Opposition has gained political traction by repeating ad hominem attacks, insisting that Canada is "broken." The Liberal government is reeling, its polling numbers in free fall. Canada's system of public provision has been shredded by decades of market fundamentalism. There's a desperate need for fresh ideas.
"Will we leave each other to sink or swim, or will we buoy strangers through life's rough waters?" asks Astra Taylor. As 2024 began, Canada's stubborn basic income (BI) movement was taking heart, telling itself that the idea of an income floor, universally available to all who need it, would provide a sense of stability in these uncertain times. While no silver bullet, BI could begin to help to address affordability. It could help restore a common sense purpose, mending frayed social bonds. The income security that comes with basic income could provide some breathing space for people to imagine and implement new, less destructive ways of living together on the planet.
Last November 20, senior economists, policy specialists and politicians published a groundbreaking study providing evidence that it is now time to put BI to the test using our smallest province as a laboratory.
Prince Edward Island's Marie Burge is the dean of BI promotors in Canada. The octogenarian's optimism is based in part on the need to address the stain of poverty. Under her tireless leadership, P.E.I.'s four political parties have come to a consensus in their support for basic income. She knows Canada "has the resources to make it happen." Her tiny province would be an ideal launching pad for an eventual national program. It just needs federal co-operation and funding.
Coalition Canada, a national BI advocacy group, is busily gathering petitions for members of Parliament to present in the House of Commons, calling on Ottawa to begin immediate negotiations with P.E.I.'s government. The aim? Implementation of a five-year BI demonstration program in the province.
P.E.I. clinical psychologist Susan Hartley points to the province's crumbling health-care system, its rising population of unhoused people and the grinding life-long health effects of growing up poor. With one of the highest poverty rates in the country, one in four children on the Island lives in poverty. "If poverty disappears, the health effects of living in poverty will also disappear."
A 2023 University of British Columbia study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences showed that unconditional cash transfers reduce homelessness - and save money in social services. According to co-author Dr. Jiaying Zhao, "When people received the cash transfer, they actually spent it on things that you or I would spend it on - housing, clothing, food, transit - and not on drugs and alcohol."
Burge insists Ottawa must negotiate with the government of P.E.I. to fund a demonstration project. Why is nothing happening? The easy answer is political will. Away from P.E.I.'s political consensus, there's skepticism in the political class.
BI clearly has its opponents, right and left. Ontario's Ford government took just a few weeks after its 2018 election to break its campaign commitment to keep funding Ontario's high-profile basic income pilot. If completed, the pilot would have been Canada's - and the world's - most comprehensive test of BI.
The B.C. New Democrats formed a minority government with Green Party support in 2017. The two-party deal committed the NDP to conduct a study of how to inaugurate a BI pilot. But the NDP proceeded to assign BI critics to examine whether BI was even a good idea. The predictable thumbs down amounted to a betrayal.
The polycrisis will not disappear if politicians cling to old policies no longer suited to urgent contemporary realities. Basic income offers a hopeful stabilizing force in an era of destabilization. A practical means of reducing insecurity.
In November, the Newfoundland and Labrador government launched a basic income program for people aged 60 to 64. As things fall apart, it's an idea that won't go away.
CAPTION: Tracey Mechefske, left, has her book signed by authors Jamie Swift and Elaine Power titled "The Case for Basic Income - Freedom, Security, Justice" during a 2021 book launch in Peterborough.
CREDIT: Jamie Swift and Elaine Power

Subject: Poverty; Politics; Homeless people; Basic income; Pandemics

Document 5 of 252

Construction firms that built critical Ontario infrastructure projects during COVID say the province stiffed them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/construction-firms-that-built-critical-ontario/docview/2933809994/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Construction+firms+that+built+critical+Ontario+infrastructure+projects+during+COVID+say+the+province+stiffed+them&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has been hit with scores of claims from construction companies that say they were left on the hook for money they spent to keep critical public infrastructure projects moving ahead during the pandemic.
Several of the firms have taken the province to court, where they are seeking hundreds of millions of dollars in damages.
So far, the province has reached settlements for 67 different claims, government officials said. However, the government will not say how much it paid in settlements or how many other claims it's facing.
Despite declaring a province-wide state of emergency in March 2020 to slow the spread of COVID-19, several companies say the government refused to admit that the pandemic constituted an emergency for their projects - what the firms allege was a breach of contract. This emergency status would have entitled the companies to more money and time.
The province has argued in court that project agreements made construction companies responsible for added costs. As they negotiate claims, government officials say they are trying to shield taxpayers from runaway COVID-related expenses.
Details of settlements are 'confidential'
Michael Lindsay, president and CEO of the provincial agency Infrastructure Ontario, said details about the settlements are "commercially sensitive" and "confidential."
Lindsay said the pandemic presented "unprecedented realities" that project contracts did not fully anticipate. He said he is confident that a review of the agency's response to COVID claims would show it is protecting taxpayers, while "acting reasonably to address the validated costs" experienced by the construction firms.
The public has a "right to know" how much the pandemic added to construction costs, said Matti Siemiatycki, director of the Infrastructure Institute at University of Toronto.
Temporarily withholding settlement details may protect the province's position in negotiating ongoing claims, but that information should ultimately be released, Siemiatycki said.
"Having transparency is important, so that our community understands the costs of these mega projects, and can understand how public money is being spent," he said.
Major P3 projects account for 17 settlements
Most of the 67 COVID-related claims were settled through dispute resolution mechanisms built into the projects' agreements, Infrastructure Ontario said.
Seventeen of those settlements stemmed from major public-private partnerships (P3).
In P3 projects, a consortium of private companies inks a deal with the government, and typically delivers private financing, as well as project maintenance and operation. Governments have often touted the P3 model as a way to shift risks associated with large-scale projects from taxpayers to the private sector.
But those risks do not include "extraordinary circumstances like the COVID-19-pandemic," said Jean-Louis Servranckx, president and CEO of Aecon Group, which is part of a consortium of companies suing the province.
The consortium had been hired in 2019 to widen a stretch of Hwy. 401, from Mississauga to Milton. The $640-million contract included an "emergency provision" that was standard across all of Ontario's major P3 projects and should have been triggered by the pandemic, entitling contractors to more time and money to implement COVID-safety protocols and navigate supply-chain hiccups, the lawsuit claims.
"Nearly four years after the outbreak of the pandemic, we are still seeking fair and reasonable compensation for these costs," Servranckx told the Star.
The province had deemed P3 projects "essential," and released COVID guidelines for construction sites that mandated the sanitization of equipment, the social distancing of workers and other safety protocols.
However, to avoid triggering the emergency provision in its contracts, and "minimize its exposure to P3 contractors," the province decided to refuse to acknowledge the existence of an emergency for its projects, alleges the consortium, which also includes developers Amico and Parsons.
"In formulating and implementing this strategy ... the Ontario Crown ... breached its obligations of honest and good faith performance," the lawsuit alleges.
Infrastructure Ontario has not yet filed a statement of defence in the Hwy. 401 case, and declined to comment on the allegations, which have not been tested in court.
Eglinton Crosstown LRT project sparked court action
The companies building the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, another P3 project, have also taken the province to court. That consortium, called Crosslinx Transit Solutions, includes Aecon, which is also on the Hwy. 401 project, as well as ACS-Dragados, EllisDon and SNC-Lavalin.
In the fall of 2020, Crosslinx took legal action against Infrastructure Ontario and Metrolinx over their alleged repeated refusal to declare an emergency for the project. The consortium said the pandemic had left it grappling with new safety protocols, supply chain problems and absenteeism.
The provincial agencies disputed the claims. They argued that the $8.25-billion project agreement made Crosslinx responsible for risks associated with the pandemic. The agencies said they didn't declare an emergency on the project because Crosslinx was already required to maintain a safe and healthy workplace, including implementing COVID protocols.
The case wound its way through various levels of court. In December 2021, the provincial agencies announced they had reached a $325-million settlement to resolve various disputes with Crosslinx related to the project, including COVID costs.
After it was sued by another consortium, the province came out swinging.
The lawsuit stemmed from a $616-million contract to widen a stretch of Hwy. 427, which wasn't completed until September 2021 - a year behind schedule. The companies blamed the "long and torturous road to opening" on a series of alleged missteps by the government, including its "repeated" refusal to declare an emergency for the project during the pandemic.
Infrastructure Ontario attributed the delay to the companies' "own poor execution and poor progress" and issued a counterclaim against the consortium for $11 million, court documents state.
The province and the companies agreed to take the dispute to arbitration, which "remains ongoing," a spokesperson for Industry Ontario said.
An 'expensive' lesson?
While the province's alleged refusal to declare an emergency on projects has rankled contractors, from the perspective of taxpayers, "the government did the right thing," said James McKellar, professor emeritus at the Schulich School of Business.
Rather than giving contractors carte blanche on COVID costs, McKellar characterized this approach as "a big stick" that would have made contractors "come to the table a little more humble."
NDP infrastructure critic Jennifer French (Oshawa) said the P3 claims - and the unknown amount spent to settle them - could be an "expensive" lesson in the dangers of moving away from traditional public financing.
"We're now finding out that the risk is actually multiplying and falling back on us," she said. "It's very frustrating as a member of the public just to watch so many dollars disappear ... We are not allowed to know where. We're not allowed to know how much."
In 2014, the province's auditor general found that Infrastructure Ontario's reliance on public-private partnerships had cost $8-billion more than traditional public financing. But the agency's then-CEO pushed back against the finding, saying that number did not account for cost overruns and delays that large-scale publicly funded projects often incur.
Even before the pandemic, Infrastructure Ontario's Lindsay said the province had been reviewing its contract models and making adjustments. Since COVID struck, he said the agency has made changes to all its contracts, including for P3 projects, "to provide a more predictable way to deal with the risks and the costs associated with pandemics moving forward."

Document 6 of 252

'It's really dire for us': Norfolk mayor seeks new funding deal from province

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/really-dire-us-norfolk-mayor-seeks-new-funding/docview/2933809810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+really+dire+for+us%27%3A+Norfolk+mayor+seeks+new+funding+deal+from+province&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Norfolk County Mayor Amy Martin puts a premium on having good relations with the province. But hearing Premier Doug Ford recently accuse municipalities of "gouging" taxpayers made her bristle.
During a mid-February news conference, Ford said all levels of government needed to cut spending to "start giving people relief."
"Property taxes, who's there? Municipal governments gouging you for more money," Ford said in a clip posted to the premier's social media.
"Why don't you run the government efficiently and stop taxing the death out of people in Ontario?"
Martin took issue with Ford's critique, pointing out provincial funding cuts have left municipalities holding the bag.
"We raise property taxes to pay for critical infrastructure," Martin commented on Ford's post.
"This one stings," the mayor added. "I go out of my way to be a positive, helpful partner with your government. This doesn't demonstrate the same respect."
In an interview, Martin told The Spectator rural communities like Norfolk are grappling with spikes in homelessness, addictions and mental health while Queen's Park has downloaded nearly $4 billion in provincial responsibilities onto local councils.
Norfolk was forced to add more than $500,000 to the 2024 operating budget to fund stormwater management, social assistance and the provincial offences court, Martin said, while the Haldimand-Norfolk Health Unit has cut five programs and 11 full-time staff positions since 2020 to make up for a shortfall in provincial funding.
Councils have more responsibilities while municipal revenue has not keep pace, the mayor argued, even as property taxes in Norfolk have risen 30 per cent since 2019.
At council on Feb. 21, Martin tabled a motion calling for a "new deal for Norfolk" that would see the province step up with more money.
The aim of the motion, Martin told The Spectator, is "advocating and educating our provincial partners on what a modern municipality actually looks like."
"It's starting the conversation on how municipalities are funded and what problems they're currently facing," she said.
No frills
Martin's "new deal" pitch is a nod to the funding agreement the province struck with the City of Toronto in December. The mayor said Norfolk's "financial concerns" are just as serious.
Norfolk plans to spend $1 billion over the next decade to upgrade and maintain the water and sewer system, roads and bridges, and county facilities.
That level of spending is almost double what was approved in the previous 10-year capital budget, which was set at $541 million in 2020.
Treasurer Amy Fanning blamed the increase on "skyrocketing" construction costs, supply-chain challenges, high interest rates and rising inflation.
The danger of the price tag ballooning even further was illustrated last month when councillors begrudgingly approved more money to upgrade Port Dover's water treatment plant, a project whose 2020 budget of $8.4 million has reached $21.3 million.
County staff made the numbers work by cancelling necessary but not critical upgrades planned for Simcoe's water treatment plant, but project director Mariana Balaban warned councillors that "unorthodox" approach to budgeting could backfire if future costs rise.
The municipality has little wiggle room to take on more debt.
The single largest expense in Norfolk's 2024 operating budget is not road repair or emergency services, but debt servicing, while the infrastructure plan is expected to push the county over its self-imposed internal debt limit of 15 per cent of revenue.
Norfolk's debt rate is already 58 per cent higher than the provincial average, according to a study by BMA Consulting Inc. that Martin cited in her motion, while the county is in the midst of a years-long process to replenish reserves past councils emptied to artificially keep taxes low.
"As Norfolk's debt burden increases, financial flexibility becomes extremely constrained," Balaban wrote in a Feb. 21 report to council, noting Norfolk is on track to become the most indebted municipality per capita in Ontario.
Without help from the province, Martin predicted more tax hikes, service cuts and bare-bones budgets in the years ahead.
"It's really dire for us," the mayor said.
Many municipalities need infrastructure dollars, but Martin argues Norfolk is a special case because of the added cost of delivering municipal services across a far-flung and sparsely populated rural county.
The BMA study found Norfolk's population is older and poorer than that of other municipalities in southwest Ontario and across the province.
"We deserve special attention (from the province), and here's why - we earn less, we have more land mass, we don't have enough water users, we're taking on more debt," Martin told The Spectator.
"We're not asking for the frills anymore."
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com

Document 7 of 252

Tributes, condolences for 'giant in Canadian politics': State funeral planned for former prime minister

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tributes-condolences-giant-canadian-politics/docview/2933744093/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tributes%2C+condolences+for+%27giant+in+Canadian+politics%27%3A+State+funeral+planned+for+former+prime+minister&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The flag on Parliament's Peace Tower fluttered at half-mast Friday morning as Canadians paid tribute to former prime minister Brian Mulroney.
"Canada is in mourning," said Liberal House leader Steve MacKinnon inside a sombre House of Commons, where a book of condolences has been set up for members of Parliament to sign.
Mulroney died Thursday in a Florida hospital following a recent fall at his Palm Beach home. He was 84. He had been treated for prostate cancer almost a year ago and underwent a heart procedure in August.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau confirmed a state funeral will be held to honour the Progressive Conservative titan, who held the prime minister's post for nearly nine years.
Trudeau said details on the timing are being worked out with Mulroney's family, and there will also be opportunities for the public to offer their condolences.
The House of Commons was scheduled to sit Friday before a two-week break, but MPs agreed to suspend the sitting until March 18, when they will officially pay tribute to Mulroney.
The outpouring of condolences so far make it clear he has left a lasting mark on this country and its people.
"It's just hard to imagine politics in Canada without Brian Mulroney," said David McLaughlin, Mulroney's former chief of staff.
"I'm sad for him, sad for his family, and I'm kind of sad for the country because I don't think we'll see the likes of a Brian Mulroney again. He was truly a giant in Canadian politics."
McLaughlin said he was a prime minister who constantly tried to do big things.
Mulroney, a charismatic and compelling leader with convictions as deep as his trademark languid baritone, led the country as the leader of the Progressive Conservatives from 1984 until 1993.
He reinvented cross-border relations thanks to a close friendship with then-U.S. president Ronald Reagan, a relationship that helped usher in the era of continental free trade and bilateral environmental treaties.
He led the way on a global treaty to rid the world of substances depleting the ozone layer, and with president George H.W. Bush signed a cross-border pact to cut down on the air pollution that causes acid rain.
For many, Mulroney will always be the prime minister who introduced the Goods and Services Tax - a bold and necessary move, he insisted, but one that came with lasting political damage.
He also twice tried to garner agreement from the provinces to amend the Constitution in a bid to bring Quebec on side.
McLaughlin said although both of those constitutional amendments failed, they show how high Mulroney was willing to reach.
Mulroney's prime ministerial tenure took the public on a giant mood swing. So popular in 1984 that his first government still holds the distinction of being the largest majority Canada has ever seen, his popularity plummeted so far that his successor, Kim Campbell, could only win two seats in the 1993 election.
But in his post-prime ministerial life he took on the role of an elder statesman, sought out by premiers, cabinet ministers and prime ministers for his political wisdom and acumen.
The Canadian Press

Document 8 of 252

Pondering the planet on a prehistoric landscape

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pondering-planet-on-prehistoric-landscape/docview/2933744081/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pondering+the+planet+on+a+prehistoric+landscape&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In western Newfoundland's Gros Morne National Park, the red mesa of the Tablelands protrudes like a wound amid the 1,805-square-kilometre swath of emerald boreal forest and blue fiords. Its presence in the landscape provokes a head-scratching dissonance - and that's because it's a geologic anomaly. This part of Gros Morne holds the key to the park's 1987 designation as a UNESCO World Heritage site.
From a wind-pummelled ridge, my eyes follow the sweep of umber rocks down toward the marked Tablelands Trail in the distance. My husband and I left the trail's boardwalk behind earlier that morning, putting our navigation skills to use on the 14-kilometre off-trail trek, picking our way through loose rock and scrambling up the steep, sun-baked slopes of Winter House Brook Canyon.
Although we've reached the peak of the trail, we're actually standing on the Earth's mantle (the layer between the planet's crust and molten core). Nearly 500 million years ago, two tectonic plates collided here, thrusting up rocks that were once deep beneath the ancient Iapetus Ocean, a precursor to the Atlantic.
The site was instrumental in proving the theory of plate tectonics - garnering the park UNESCO status - which only gelled in scientific communities in the late 1960s. "The smoking gun of plate tectonics and continental drift was finding little bits of oceans preserved on continents or between continents, and that's what the Tablelands is," says Andrew Kerr, adjunct professor of earth sciences at Memorial University of Newfoundland, in St. John's.
Treading across the subterranean depths of an ancient ocean up on the Tablelands' barren plateau is as otherworldly as it sounds. In fact, the area serves as a Mars analog site for researchers at NASA and the Canadian Space Agency. The peridotite rocks here, which are green beneath their oxidized red husk, are also found on Mars, so looking at the Tablelands' ecosystem gives scientists insight into potential life on other planets. The austere terrain is laden with toxic metals, so not much survives up here beyond a patchwork of grassy bog, murky ponds and the odd carnivorous plant.
Although the upper plateau doesn't cover a huge expanse of land, it's easy to get turned around in the monochromatic rubble, and we have to consult our GPS a couple of times as we plod along the outer rim. But soon we fall into the slow, hypnotic rhythm that comes with walking for long periods, where time seems to bend and stretch. Coupled with the imperceptible centimetres-per-year pace of our shifting continents embedded in the ancient rocks beneath my feet, I feel tuned into a powerful, primordial frequency.
This sense of deep time nearly defies human comprehension, but what I can wrap my mind around continues to make my experience of the landscape here so much richer.
"That's part of why we put Parks Canada's signature red Adirondack chairs in spots like Green Gardens, to encourage people to do more than snap a quick picture, (and) to sit and contemplate the place," says Rob Hingston, a geologist and Parks Canada visitor experience officer for Western Newfoundland and Labrador.
On another morning, we make our way to the coastal Green Gardens trail to see it for ourselves. The path snakes down (and mercilessly up on the way back) through a fragrant spruce and pine forest, finally opening onto a lush green cliffside above the sea, where lashing winds roar in off the Gulf of St. Lawrence.
I follow a small footpath dotted with purple wildflowers to the edge of the bluff and peer over, greeted by a popcorn-like cliff face of grey pillow basalt and, in the turquoise shallows, towering sea stacks. These elements make for a striking tableau, and they're also further evidence of plate tectonics.
These volcanic rocks at Green Gardens are 600 million years old, predating the nearby Tablelands, and were formed when continents pulled apart to create the Iapetus Ocean. "So, we have evidence of an ocean forming here, and then evidence of an ocean closing at the Tablelands," says Hingston. "We know an event must have happened to stack all of these rocks close together, and that was a continental collision."
At another geological site in the park, Green Point, travellers can see layered shale that would have formed in the depths of the Iapetus Ocean, and Gros Morne Mountain, a monumental fist of quartzite and limestone, was once an ancient tropical beach. It's here at the park's namesake mountain, which means "great sombre" in French, that we spend our last day in Newfoundland.
The knee-knackering scramble up dusty scree to reach the mountain's summit and the bone-rattling, boulder-strewn descent on the other side make for a challenging 17 kilometres. But at the peak, there's a worthy reward: a vast slate-coloured Arctic tundra that camouflages ptarmigan and Arctic hare, and a bird's-eye view of sapphire fiords and perfect jewel-like lakes.
In another 250 million years, this landscape will have shifted again; that's when scientists predict a new supercontinent will have formed. There's a melancholic relief in knowing Earth's movement continues on, centimetre by centimetre, year by year, outside of human time and influence.
Heading down the mountain, I look up to see Gros Morne live up to its name as a brooding sky gathers. It starts to rain, and I listen to my footsteps on the dark, slick trail.
Just the facts
To reach Gros Morne National Park by air, the closest airport is Deer Lake Regional Airport, about a 30-minute drive from the park's southern edge. Travellers can hike on their own, or on a guided tour with a company like Gros Morne Adventures.
The summertime, from about June to September, is generally considered the ideal window for hiking. (Gros Morne Mountain is closed until the end of June to protect wildlife during a critical seasonal period.) Visit pc.gc.ca/grosmorne for detailed trip-planning tips from Parks Canada.
Chloe Berge travelled as a guest of Go Western
Newfoundland, which did not review or approve this article.

Document 9 of 252

Too much of the stick and not enough of the carrot

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/too-much-stick-not-enough-carrot/docview/2933744063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Too+much+of+the+stick+and+not+enough+of+the+carrot&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.20&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When I was going through university, and for a while afterwards, I worked at a number of cafés.
You get to see a lot of interesting things with those kinds of service jobs, and the memories have stayed with me. I was reading the news the other day when an article brought back a great one. I was working front counter one day when the cook tried a new dish. It was an intricate vegetarian sushi she'd spent a lot of time on, and it turned out that the customer loved it. All the servings were gone in a flash. The owner hurried into the kitchen and said, "We need more sushi! Right away!" The cook, who was working on several other dishes by then, threw up her hands. "Jesus! I can't pull sushi out of my ..."
When I read the Canadian mayors' response to Pierre Poilievre's latest solution to the housing crisis, I felt they were saying just the same thing as that cook I worked with, but in more diplomatic terms. The mayors reasonably pointed out that they, actually, don't build the houses, and they can't control the rate of building. However, we can likely guarantee that if the federal government withheld infrastructure funding, as Poilievre is suggesting, there will be less houses built in the future. Unless every house comes with its own septic field and well for water and the developers put in their own roads.
Conservatives like to think that the lack of housing is all about bureaucratic foot-dragging and NIMBYism, and with councillors on Hamilton's general issues committee coming to the spirited defence of a handful of parking spots rather than build affordable housing, I can see where that idea might come from. But cities are wise to be cautious of developers.
One just needs to look at all the stalled developments dotting our downtown, including Hamilton City Centre, the lonely façade of James Street Baptist Church or the partially built tower at 15 Cannon St. W. to see why. Let's not even mention the Jamesville social housing debacle, which was started with fanfare in 2019, the housing still empty and decaying today as the tents line up across the street from it.
It's not just Hamilton struggling with getting developers to actually build things. The Toronto Star recently reported that Tarion, the non-profit that provides new home warrantees to Ontarians, as well as theoretically regulating parts of the new home industry, is predicting it will pay out damages of $90 million in lost deposits as builders walk away from projects. This will be Tarion's biggest payout ever. Tying infrastructure dollars to an industry this turbulent is a recipe for disaster, as the Ford Progressive Conservatives seem to have recently realized. The housing targets in Bill 23 now include new basement suites, laneway housing and long-term-care beds, a shift that is raising some eyebrows, but is allowing money to flow to municipalities that might not have otherwise qualified under a more stringent definition of housing starts.
Private businesses, such as developers, don't operate for the public good. They're there to make money. That's not a bad thing, unless we're expecting them to solve public problems. This is why we've seen all the recent chaos around private/public partnerships at Ontario colleges, why Shoppers Drug Mart management was recently accused of pressuring their pharmacists to cold call customers for unnecessary prescription checks they could bill back to the Ontario government at $75 each and why, tragically, for-profit long-term-care homes had such high death rates over COVID-19.
If we want things done for the public good, they're best done by the public sector.
Withholding funds from our crumbling public institutions to make the private sector work faster is not just bad policy, it can be dangerous. And it punishes those often working hardest to make change. While federal and provincial conservatives carry on with the carrot and stick routine, public workers struggle on, with our city staff literally trying to keep buildings from falling on top of us.
Maybe it's time for all our politicians to put down their sticks and start helping the people who do the work best to rebuild our cities. It's not like we can pull housing out of thin air.
Noelle Allen is the publisher
of Wolsak and Wynn. She lives
in Hamilton.
CAPTION: Private businesses, such as developers, don't operate for the public good. They're there to make money. That's not a bad thing, unless we're expecting them to solve public problems, Noelle Allen writes.The Associated Press file photo

Document 10 of 252

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2933744060/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.20&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: System too soft on killers
Re: 'He's coming for us': Victims' family terrified when convicted killer goes missing while on parole (Feb. 29)
To quote the Parole Board of Canada, "The family has been given no details about his disappearance. They say the PBC told them doing so would violate George Lovie's privacy."
When will the parole board, judges and lawyers ever have some sympathy for victims? People are living their lives in terror, while a convicted killer is allowed to go for a drive down the highway with no supervision, go golfing and be allowed out in civilization after murdering a family. A cold-blooded murderer in prison should not be entitled to privacy. Why do these people in charge of criminals constantly think they deserve to be treated as equals with the law abiding population?
Since we don't kill murderers, the least we can do is keep them away from any other innocent victims.
Constantly I read about someone committing a crime who has violated parole. Or we read about a criminal on parole "disappearing" and the police unit then has to spend time and resources to track them down. Imagine the money saved by keeping criminals locked away to complete their sentence. Then give that extra money saved to the police budget, instead of a tax increase.
Andy Price, Ancaster
Putin can't be trusted
Re: It's time to push for talks (Feb. 28)
The author suggests the war in Ukraine is the fault of western military industries and advocates mediation. She states some want a negotiated settlement while the West continues to support the war. The reality is Ukraine would only agree to full Russian withdrawal from illegally obtained Ukrainian territories. Any agreement with Putin/Russia would be worthless as Russia has a history of lying and ignoring agreements, e.g. the Budapest Memorandum of 1994, signed by Russia, with assurances that "prohibited Russia, the U.S. and the U.K. from threatening or using military force or economic coercion against Ukraine ... except in self-defence or otherwise in accordance with the Charter of the United Nations." So much for Russian "assurances." Any mediation would be a win for Putin and would not promise further Russian aggression.
Joe Scozzaro, Hamilton
The numbers don't add up
Re: Ford's sneaky act pushes sprawl (Feb. 29)
I take issue with the much quoted 129 hectares or 319 acres per day quoted by Bonnie Crombie and many others. This number is originally obtained from Statistics Canada and the 2016 and 2021 census. The definition of what is considered farmland was significantly changed between these two censuses. At 319 acres per day, that adds up to 116,435 acres per year.
Most single-family suburban homes are at a density of six units per acre. In Ontario, we built about 18,000 single-family dwellings in 2023 requiring about 3,000 acres per year or eight acres per day. About 70,000 higher density dwellings were built last year averaging about 23 units per acre, requiring another eight acres per day. Most of the high density homes are built within existing core areas requiring no additional land and many houses built are on non-productive land.
The 319 acres of farmland per day lost to development is scare mongering and playing politics.
Gary Aikema, Hamilton
Drop the scare tactics
Re: Brace yourself for Hamilton's
looming perma-gridlock (Feb. 27)
I will love the day I can leave my car at home and take a trip into Westdale or Dundas by bus/LRT and have the travel time be near equal to driving, and the cost to do so will be less than parking my car. Stop it with your scare tactics. It's getting old, just like the outdated transportation policies that have been used for the past near century.
Erin Shacklette, Hamilton
A mind-boggling fiasco
I agree 100 per cent with Scott Radley's column. Has any member of council even bothered to get the opinion of constituents? I grew up in the lower end of the city during the '70s and '80s when the downtown core was vibrant. Today the city is facing numerous problems within its city centre and merchants are struggling to make ends meet with vacant businesses everywhere. The reason traffic even today crawls through our downtown core is because there are only two major arteries that can be used to flow traffic from the 403 eastward through town. Those are the York and Main street exits. I use to enjoy a trip downtown, but not anymore. Everyone I talk to from my neighbourhood feels the same way. Nobody enjoys being tied up in gridlock traffic. This condition is only going to get worse with the conversion of Main Street. Scott is absolutely right when he says we can all expect to be paying years later to correct this mind-boggling fiasco.
John Cordeiro, Ancaster

Document 11 of 252

Norfolk mayor asks province for 'new deal': Cash-strapped county plans to spend $1 billion over next decade to upgrade, maintain infrastructure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/norfolk-mayor-asks-province-new-deal/docview/2933743568/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Norfolk+mayor+asks+province+for+%27new+deal%27%3A+Cash-strapped+county+plans+to+spend+%241+billion+over+next+decade+to+upgrade%2C+maintain+infrastructure&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Norfolk County Mayor Amy Martin puts a premium on having good relations with the province. But hearing Premier Doug Ford recently accuse municipalities of "gouging" taxpayers made her bristle.
During a mid-February news conference, Ford said all levels of government needed to cut spending to "start giving people relief."
"Property taxes, who's there? Municipal governments gouging you for more money," Ford said in a clip posted to the premier's social media.
"Why don't you run the government efficiently and stop taxing the death out of people in Ontario?"
Martin took issue with Ford's critique, pointing out provincial funding cuts have left municipalities holding the bag.
"We raise property taxes to pay for critical infrastructure," Martin commented on Ford's post.
"This one stings," the mayor added. "I go out of my way to be a positive, helpful partner with your government. This doesn't demonstrate the same respect."
In an interview, Martin told The Spectator rural communities like Norfolk are grappling with spikes in homelessness, addictions and mental-health issues while Queen's Park has downloaded nearly $4 billion in provincial responsibilities onto local councils.
Norfolk was forced to add more than $500,000 to the 2024 operating budget to fund stormwater management, social assistance and the provincial offences court, Martin said, while the Haldimand-Norfolk Health Unit has cut five programs and 11 full-time staff positions since 2020 to make up for a shortfall in provincial funding.
At council on Feb. 21, Martin tabled a motion calling for a "new deal for Norfolk" that would see the province step up with more money.
The aim of the motion, Martin told The Spectator, is "advocating and educating our provincial partners on what a modern municipality actually looks like." Martin's "new deal" pitch is a nod to the funding agreement the province struck with the City of Toronto in December. The mayor said Norfolk's "financial concerns" are just as serious.
Norfolk plans to spend $1 billion over the next decade to upgrade and maintain the water and sewer system, roads and bridges, and county facilities. That level of spending is almost double what was approved in the previous 10-year capital budget, which was set at $541 million in 2020.
The danger of the price tag ballooning even further was illustrated last month when councillors begrudgingly approved more money to upgrade Port Dover's water treatment plant, a project whose 2020 budget of $8.4 million has reached $21.3 million.
County staff made the numbers work by cancelling necessary but not critical upgrades planned for Simcoe's water treatment plant, but project director Mariana Balaban warned councillors that "unorthodox" approach to budgeting could backfire if future costs rise.
The single largest expense in Norfolk's 2024 operating budget is not road repair or emergency services, but debt servicing, while the infrastructure plan is expected to push the county over its self-imposed internal debt limit of 15 per cent of revenue.
Norfolk's debt rate is already 58 per cent higher than the provincial average, according to a study by BMA Consulting Inc. that Martin cited in her motion, while the county is in the midst of a years-long process to replenish reserves past councils emptied to artificially keep taxes low.
Without help from the province, Martin predicted more tax hikes, service cuts and bare-bones budgets in the years ahead.
"We're not asking for the frills anymore."
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian
government through its Local Journalism Initiative.
jpantonacci@thespec.com
CAPTION: Mayor Amy Martin is pushing the province for a "new deal for Norfolk" in light of the rural county's "dire" financial situation.

CREDIT: J.P. Antonacci Local Journalism Initiative Reporter

Document 12 of 252

The perplexing legacy of Brian Mulroney

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/perplexing-legacy-brian-mulroney/docview/2933743531/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+perplexing+legacy+of+Brian+Mulroney&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Brian Mulroney - Canada's 18th prime minister who died at age 84 - will be remembered for many things, but his most significant decision during two terms in office was to link Canada's future with the United States.
Unlike Pierre Trudeau, Canada's Liberal prime minister who had a rocky relationship with several U.S. presidents during the 1960s, '70s and '80s, Mulroney was an unabashed Americanophile. After all, he grew up in Baie-Comeau, Que., a town founded by a wealthy American industrialist - Robert Rutherford McCormick - to produce cheap newsprint for New York and Chicago papers.
Mulroney's admiration for American capitalism was evident in his political polices. Within a year after being elected with a large majority in 1984, Mulroney stated he wanted to negotiate a free-trade agreement with the United States. Shortly after that, Mulroney hosted then U.S. president Ronald Reagan for what was called the "Shamrock Summit" in Quebec City. The two leaders, both of whom were proud of their Irish heritage, took to the stage at the summit and famously launched into a rendition of "When Irish Eyes Are Smiling."
While some Canadians may have cringed at the sight of the two men warbling together, Mulroney's close relationship with Reagan was a political asset for the Progressive Conservative leader.
Mulroney and Reagan signed the U.S.-Canada Free Trade Agreement on Jan. 2, 1988. Mulroney campaigned on the deal during Canada's general election in November of that year and won a second consecutive majority. Some international media outlets dubbed Mulroney "the Father of North American Free Trade" in stories about his death.
The Mulroney years marked the end of a two-decade reign by the Liberals under Lester Pearson, Trudeau and John Turner. Mulroney shifted Canadian policy to the right when he negotiated the free trade agreement. Other controversial policies - the privatization of Crown corporations like Air Canada and Petro-Canada, and the introduction of the goods and services tax - would last and not be undone when the Liberals returned to power under Jean Chrétien in 1993.
Mulroney, more than any modern day prime minister, sought to atone for the actions of his predecessor Trudeau in constitutional reform. Investing enormous political capital in the Meech Lake Accord and then the Charlottetown Accord, Mulroney tried to increase the jurisdiction of the provinces, reform the Senate and recognize Québec as a distinct society.
He wanted to extensively change the Constitution and correct what was not done, or in his view done poorly, in the patriation of the Constitution in 1982 and the introduction of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
After pitched battles across the nation, both accords failed to meet the constitutional bar for ratification. In fact, the collapse of the accords revived Quebec separatism and led to the rise of the Bloc Québécois. The failure of the accords was a lesson subsequent prime ministers took to heart. None has dared to even hint at any kind of constitutional reform.
When in power, Mulroney led the Progressive Conservatives. After retirement from politics, Mulroney never felt at home at the Conservative Party of Canada.
During one of his last major public events in June 2023, Mulroney sat on stage with Justin Trudeau at St. Francis Xavier University in Nova Scotia. Mulroney praised the incumbent to such an extent that Trudeau said: "It's ... embarrassing when you're speaking about me in such glowing terms."
Mulroney leaves a perplexing legacy. A charismatic politician who led his party to two majority governments. A prime minister who made major and lasting changes to Canada's economy. A successful business leader before and after his years in politics.
Yet, he was also a prime minister who failed to bring in constitutional reforms that seemed within his grasp and a leader who unleashed political turmoil in his home province that has had a lasting impact on the Canadian political landscape decades after he left office.
Thomas Klassen is a
professor in the School of Public Policy and Administration at York University.
This was originally published in The Conversation.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Thomas Klassen;

Full text: System too soft on killers
Re: 'He's coming for us': Victims' family terrified when convicted killer goes missing while on parole (Feb. 29)
To quote the Parole Board of Canada, "The family has been given no details about his disappearance. They say the PBC told them doing so would violate George Lovie's privacy."
When will the parole board, judges and lawyers ever have some sympathy for victims? People are living their lives in terror, while a convicted killer is allowed to go for a drive down the highway with no supervision, go golfing and be allowed out in civilization after murdering a family. Or we read about a criminal on parole "disappearing" and the police unit then has to spend time and resources to track them down. I agree 100 per cent with Scott Radley's column. Has any member of council even bothered to get the opinion of constituents? A matter of perspective
Re: Why Doug Ford's plan for 'like-minded' judges is a terrible move (Feb. 27)
As Doug Ford doubles down on his plans to stack the Ontario judiciary with judges whose views align with his Conservative Party policies, I wonder if he recalls the rants of fellow Conservative, Pierre Poilievre, over the years that Justin Trudeau has stacked judicial and civil inquiries with judges favourable to Liberals? Seems a politicized judiciary is only a bad idea when the judges are not on your team.
Jim Young, Burlington
Kudos for excellent journalism
Re: Elizabeth Grandbois should have died decades ago. An experimental ALS treatment gave her hope (Feb. 21)
I thoroughly enjoyed Susan Clairmont's lengthy and detailed article on ALS and new research endeavours for a cure or at least better ways to slow its insidious progression. The article was well researched, easy to read, well written and full of great information. She is to be complimented on her excellent journalism.
Linda Kremer, Niagara Falls
World was safer under Trump?
Re: A test of how we view age (Feb. 23)
This editorial focuses upon Joe Biden's age and mental and physical capacity at age 81 and also Donald Trump's at age 77. I agree with the majority of what this editorial expresses; however, I would like to point out a few details.
Biden has not done too many formal press briefings because his people know he is vulnerable to mental mistakes. Trump has also made some mental mistakes, but if you watch them both, it is not hard to determine that Trump is mentally and physically ahead of Biden. Trump can be reckless in his speeches, the example this article uses of threatening NATI is true. However in reality, Trump will not leave NATO (in my opinion). He will just continue to bully its members into paying their fair share. He got many billions of additional dollars from many NATO countries as president. Russia initially invaded Crimea in 2014 under Obama and Biden. He invaded all of Ukraine in 2022 under Biden. He mysteriously did not invade Ukraine from 2017 to 2020.
Also China is now threatening Taiwan. Iran has sponsored the Hamas war with Israel, and North Korea is sabre rattling and missile testing again. Biden is not the cause of the wars and threats, but they did not occur under the former president. Except for COVID-19, one could make a case that the world was a safer place from 2017 to 2020.
David Gallo, Ancaster
Always look forward to Motherlode
For a lifelong Hamiltonian living on the other side of the world, the Spectator is a link to home. Aside from the local news, I especially look forward to, and love, Lorraine Sommerfeld's excellent column, Motherlode. As she recounts her own experiences, whether they are funny, sad, good or bad, most of us can relate to something she has written about. The column on Feb. 23 about the optometrist visit is in the funny, relatable category. Thank you for letting me, and countless others, know we're not alone! And thank you for your insightful writing and particularly your humour.
Linda Reid, Perth, Western Australia
CAPTION: A reader asks why people in charge of criminals constantly think they deserve to be treated as equals with the law abiding population.
