Document 14 of 252

Basic income could provide the breathing space so many need

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/basic-income-could-provide-breathing-space-so/docview/2933743055/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Basic+income+could+provide+the+breathing+space+so+many+need&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Twenty years ago, the prominent English historian Tony Judt concluded his magnum opus by looking back at the long boom that unfolded after 1945.
The so-called golden years (roughly 1945-late to 1970s) had faded into the mists of history. In the postwar years, Judt pointed to "glaring contrasts between wealth and poverty, prosperity and insecurity, private affluence and public squalor." Working-class people, particularly a growing precariat, were now exposed to "levels of economic insecurity unprecedented in living memory."
Two decades later, two Canadian writers from different ends of the political spectrum were tightly focused on insecurity. Former Bank of Canada head Stephen Poloz came out with a book called "The Next Age of Uncertainty." At the same time Astra Taylor published "The Age of Insecurity," the title of her 2023 Massey Lectures. Each recommends basic income as one way to address the insecurity gnawing away at so many Canadians.
Scientists say we have entered an age of global "polycrisis": food and housing insecurity; climate breakdown; stagflation; environmental degradation; war; ideological extremism; authoritarian politics and a crisis of democracy; massive economic inequality; and a general mistrust of institutions.
Each would be bad enough on its own. The polycrisis, however, signals the deep interconnections of systems - political and economic, environmental and cultural - under stress. A crisis in one system produces crises in the others.
The global COVID-19 pandemic did not produce this polycrisis. But it sharpened existing tensions. Pandemics are deeply unsettling, producing destabilizing effects that can last for years or even decades. Hardly a wonder that a sense of insecurity stalks the land.
The affordability crisis. Record numbers visiting food banks. Encampments of poor people mushrooming in big cities and smaller centres. Young people, having given up on owning a home, move back to live with parents. Rents are stratospheric. Signs of climate breakdown abound, with record levels of snow and rain, soaring temperatures and unprecedented firestorms.
Unsurprisingly, a populist Official Opposition has gained political traction by repeating ad hominem attacks, insisting that Canada is "broken." The Liberal government is reeling, its polling numbers in free fall. Canada's system of public provision has been shredded by decades of market fundamentalism. There's a desperate need for fresh ideas.
As 2024 began, Canada's stubborn basic income (BI) movement was taking heart, telling itself that the idea of an income floor, universally available to all who need it, would provide a sense of stability in these uncertain times. While no silver bullet, BI could begin to help to address affordability. It could help restore a common sense purpose, mending frayed social bonds. The income security that comes with basic income could provide some breathing space for people to imagine and implement new, less destructive ways of living together on the planet.
Last Nov. 20, senior economists, policy specialists and politicians published a groundbreaking study providing evidence that it is now time to put BI to the test using our smallest province as a laboratory.
Prince Edward Island's Marie Burge is the dean of BI promoters in Canada. The octogenarian's optimism is based in part on the need to address the stain of poverty. Under her tireless leadership, P.E.I.'s four political parties have come to a consensus in their support for basic income. She knows Canada "has the resources to make it happen." Her tiny province would be an ideal launching pad for an eventual national program. It just needs federal co-operation and funding.
Coalition Canada, a national BI advocacy group, is busily gathering petitions for members of Parliament to present in the House of Commons, calling on Ottawa to begin immediate negotiations with P.E.I.'s government. The aim? Implementation of a five-year BI demonstration program in the province.
P.E.I. clinical psychologist Susan Hartley points to the province's crumbling health-care system, its rising population of unhoused people and the grinding lifelong health effects of growing up poor. With one of the highest poverty rates in the country, one in four children on the Island lives in poverty.
Burge insists Ottawa must negotiate with the government of P.E.I. to fund a demonstration project. Why is nothing happening? The easy answer is political will. Away from P.E.I.'s political consensus, there's skepticism in the political class.
BI clearly has its opponents, right and left. Ontario's Ford government took just a few weeks after its 2018 election to break its campaign commitment to keep funding Ontario's high-profile basic income pilot. If completed, the pilot would have been Canada's - and the world's - most comprehensive test of BI.
The B.C. New Democrats formed a minority government with Green Party support in 2017. The two-party deal committed the NDP to conduct a study of how to inaugurate a BI pilot. But the NDP proceeded to assign BI critics to examine whether BI was even a good idea. The predictable thumbs down amounted to a betrayal.
The polycrisis will not disappear if politicians cling to old policies no longer suited to urgent contemporary realities. Basic income offers a hopeful stabilizing force in an era of destabilization. A practical means of reducing insecurity.
As things fall apart, it's an idea that won't go away.
Kingston writer Jamie Swift is the author of "The Case for Basic Income: Freedom, Security, Justice" with Elaine Power, professor of Kinesiology and Health Studies
at Queen's University.
CAPTION: Tracey Mechefske, left, has the book "The Case for Basic Income - Freedom, Security, Justice" signed by authors Jamie Swift and Elaine Power during a 2021 book launch in Peterborough.Clifford Skarstedt Peterborough Examiner file photo

CREDIT: Jamie Swift and Elaine Power;

Document 15 of 252

In photos: A look back at Brian Mulroney's life in politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/photos-look-back-at-brian-mulroneys-life-politics/docview/2933614159/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+photos%3A+A+look+back+at+Brian+Mulroney%27s+life+in+politics&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Brian Mulroney became prime minister in 1984, he did so with a whopping majority of seats in the House of Commons and a margin of victory that has yet to be matched.
And the population will be divided on whether that is even among the most significant moments of his political career. Mulroney, who died Thursday at 84, would be prime minister until 1993; his tenure is rememberd for its significant political legacy, from environmental advocacy, to relationships with international leaders such as U.S presidents Ronald Reagan and George H.W. Bush, and economic policies that were divisive at the time but shaped Canada's economy for years to come.
We look back at some of the biggest moments of his career and life.
Brian Mulroney becomes prime minister
Brian Mulroney and Mila wave from the stage on election night Sept. 4, 1984. Mulroney became prime minister when his Progressive Conservatives won 211 seats in the House of Commons - the largest margin of victory for a political party in Canadian history and a feat that has never been repeated.
Mulroney greets the Pope
Pope John Paul II on arrival in Ottawa from Vancouver on Sept. 19, 1984. He was greeted by Mulroney and wife Mila at Uplands airport.
Mulroney and the Queen
Queen Elizabeth II and Prime Minister Brian Mulroney in Quebec City, Oct. 23, 1987. Following the Queen's death in 2022, Mulroney recalled a moment when he and his wife Mila were preparing to host a small dinner for the Queen during a visit to Canada, and how the monarch requested that Mulroney's young children also be there.
"What struck me was the kindness and the interest that she lavished on the children," he said. "She treated them with the same kind of respect that she would the chancellor of the exchequer in London."
Meech Lake accord
Mulroney shakes hands with former Quebec premier Robert Bourassa in 1987. With a decisive mandate, Mulroney embarked on twin talks to reshape Canada: the Meech Lake accord and a free trade deal with the U.S. The accord was the sequel to the repatriation of the Constitution in 1982, which Quebec had refused to sign.
Among the outcomes: a resurgence of the Quebec separatist movement, and the creation of a new federal separatist party the Bloc Québécois, led by Mulroney's old friend Lucien Bouchard.
Free trade deal with the United States
Though Mulroney had not campaigned on free trade with the U.S., talks towards a new trade agreement began in 1985, and concluded at the 11th-hour in 1987, when, in Mulroney's telling, he won a last-minute concession from the U.S. for a trade dispute mechanism, and signed off on the deal being pushed through Congress.
Introduction of a national sales tax
Mulroney reviews a budget with then-finance minister Michael Wilson in 1991. Through his time in office, Mulroney attempted to rein in government spending, embarking on rounds of cuts and freezes to social spending and then ultimately did what many thought unthinkable for political survival - introduced a new tax, the Goods and Services tax or GST.
Signing of North American Free Trade Agreement
Prime Minister Brian Mulroney signs the North American Free Trade Agreement during a signing ceremony in Ottawa on Dec. 17, 1992. In 2018, Mulroney played a role in the renegotiation of the NAFTA deal, acting as Canada's unofficial emissary to the political elite in Washington.
Inducted into the Order of Canada
Mulroney (middle) stands proud with hockey legends Jean Beliveau (left) and Maurice Richard (right) after being invested into the Order of Canada at a ceremony in Ottawa in 1998.
-with files from the Star Archive and Stephanie Levitz
CAPTION: Brian Mulroney election.JPGBrian Mulroney and pope.JPGBrian Mulroney and queen.JPGBrian Mulroney MeechBrian Mulroney and ronald reagan.JPGmulroney GST.JPGmulroney signs nafta.JPGBrian Mulroney Order of CanadaFormer prime minister Brian Mulroney is shown on Parliament Hill on June 6, 2012. Mulroney died Thursday.

Document 16 of 252

'Mr. Mulroney never stopped working for Canadians: Former prime minister's death prompts outpouring of reflection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mr-mulroney-never-stopped-working-canadians/docview/2933613859/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Mr.+Mulroney+never+stopped+working+for+Canadians%3A+Former+prime+minister%27s+death+prompts+outpouring+of+reflection&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The death Thursday of former Progressive Conservative Prime Minister Brian Mulroney, a joyfully combative and proud partisan, drew profound reflections by friends, admirers and former political rivals alike.
Former Prime Minister Jean Chrétien told reporters on Parliament Hill Mulroney was a formidable adversary who had "a good political career, was "an important prime minister" and his family "can be proud" of him.
Chrétien said they had similar backgrounds. "We were both from rural Quebec," and Mulroney was "himself from a minority. He was an Anglo in Quebec, while I was a Franco in the rest of the land."
And throughout their many political debates, "I never had any bitter personal discussion with him," Chrétien said."He would poke me and I would poke him."
"In politics, opposition is opposition," said Chrétien, but "Like playing hockey, you can fight on the ice and take a beer together after that."
Chrétien, who succeeded John Turner as Liberal leader and faced Mulroney as Opposition leader before Mulroney left politics in 1993, said he thought the two men probably both would have liked to have faced each other directly in a federal election.
But "history" determined it was time for Mulroney to leave the political stage, Chrétien said.
Mulroney was replaced by Kim Campbell as PC leader who went on to lose the 1993 election to Chrétien. His beloved PC party was battered and bruised for years afterward, and later merged with the Canadian Alliance party in 2003.
"History will write what we've done," said Chrétien, but the former Liberal prime minister for whom electoral wins were always the big trophy hailed Mulroney's achievement in the 1980s of rebuilding the Conservative party, especially in Quebec - a feat that brought Mulroney to his sweeping 1984 majority government, the biggest in Canadian history.
One of Mulroney's later successors, Peter MacKay, the Progressive Conservative leader who founded the modern Conservative party with Stephen Harper, recalled Mulroney's retail political skills and acumen.
He said he got to know Mulroney and his young family when the Quebecer came to Nova Scotia to run in his father's riding after Elmer MacKay stepped down to make way for the then-newly elected PC leader who needed a seat in the Commons.
"He really invested in people and knew that that was the very essence of politics," MacKay said on CBC television.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau who sought Mulroney's advice on the renegotiation of NAFTA with the Trump administration said he learned of his death "with great sadness."
He said Mulroney had a "role in building the modern, dynamic, and prosperous country we all know today."
Mulroney's legacy includes nailing a Canada-U.S. trade deal and expanding it to include Mexico, enacting environmental protections, standing up against apartheid in South Africa, and trying to bridge the divide between English and French Canada through constitutional negotiations, Trudeau said.
"Mr. Mulroney never stopped working for Canadians, and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home," Trudeau said in a written statement.
Speaking later Thursday in Thunder Bay, Trudeau spoke of how he valued Mulroney's advice during Canada's renegotiation of NAFTA from 2016-2019.
"He shaped our past, but he shapes our present and he will impact our future as well," Trudeau said. "He was an extraordinary statesman. And he will be deeply, deeply missed."
"As we mourn his passing and keep his family and friends in our thoughts, let us also acknowledge - and celebrate - Mr. Mulroney's role in building the modern, dynamic, and prosperous country we all know today."
Former Ontario premier David Peterson who knew Mulroney before politics as a young lawyer, and who went on to work with Mulroney to rewrite the Constitution in the great Meech Lake accord debates of the late 1980s expressed deep "sadness" in an interview.
"He was just the kindest and most generous man I've known, terribly Irish in his affections and his loves and his excesses and his generosity," said Peterson, now chair of Torstar.
Peterson said Mulroney was "fun and generous and inclusive. And he can always spot the person in the room that needed an encouraging word." That gift, he said, was one of the reasons Mulroney was such a successful politician.
Through the Meech Lake debates, Peterson said, Mulroney would often phone him on Sunday nights as the Ontario premier was trying to squeeze in family time with his then three young kids, chuckling as he recalled how "Brian loved to talk."
In the tense last days as the constitutional accord needed to be ratified, but failed to win approval in the Manitoba legislature due to Indigenous MP Elijah Harper's objection, and then, in Newfoundland, former premier Clyde Wells withdrew the accord for a final vote, Mulroney and Peterson were "both devastated," he said.
They believed "it was a mistake of historical proportions" by the Manitoba and Newfoundland politicians, and Peterson hailed Mulroney's "patient" leadership throughout. "People were under enormous pressure," he recalled. "It was in a way politics at its finest."
Mulroney's political legacy aside, Peterson said Canadians should remember that he exemplified "generous, decent, good, well motivated people in politics, that did not hate the people on the other side and worked very hard to get along and find common consensus."
Wells in an interview with the Star said although he and Mulroney did "not always see eye to eye, we had our differences, but we also had some pleasant exchanges too. I greatly respected him for what his objectives were and what I sensed was his dedication to the country, and I admired him for that. But I didn't see the objective to be achieved in quite the same way he did."
Wells said while he did not agree with Mulroney on the constitutional changes proposed, he admired his fight against apartheid and thought Mulroney's greatest legacy was in negotiating the Canada-U.S. free trade deal.
"I wasn't totally certain at the time that it was going to be successful, I had mixed feelings about it but I supported it in the end because I thought on the whole I was going to be beneficial, and he was absolutely right."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre called Mulroney "one of the greatest ever statesmen" in expressing condolences to his family, and "the love of his life, his best friend and wife, Mila."
He hailed Mulroney's working-class roots as the son of an Irish electrician who went on to become a lawyer and achieved business success as the head of Iron Ore Company. And Poilievre celebrated his success in politics as "even greater."
"But it was the transformational change he brought to his nearly a decade as prime minister that is most important. He unleashed free enterprise, crushed inflation, restored fiscal sanity and concluded one of the greatest free trade agreements the world has ever seen, which remains largely in place today."
Poilievre cast Mulroney as a leader on the world stage who "stood firmly on the side of Western allies against communism and for freedom."
The Conservative leader said he "will always be thankful for his candid advice and generous mentorship to me personally. All Canadians are grateful for his immense sacrifice and the lasting legacy he leaves us all."
Mulroney's death was announced by his daughter Caroline, an Ontario cabinet minister and the only one of his four children - including Mark, Ben and Nicholas - to enter electoral politics.
The Mulroney family did not specify the cause of death, but last year the former prime minister had been treated for prostate cancer. He had been optimistic about his treatment and recovery.

CAPTION: Brian Mulroney and wife Mila greetPope John Paul II in Ottawa on Sept. 19, 1984.Former U.S. President George Bush has a word with former Prime Minister Brian Mulroney as they enter a luncheon as part of a conference marking the tenth anniversary of the North American Free Trade Agreement on June 5, 1999 in Montreal.Hockey legend Jean Beliveau, left, former Prime Minister Brian Mulroney, and hockey great Maurice Richard after being invested into the Order of Canada at a ceremony in Ottawa in 1998.

Document 17 of 252

Brian Mulroney, former prime minister, has died

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brian-mulroney-former-prime-minister-has-died/docview/2933613826/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brian+Mulroney%2C+former+prime+minister%2C+has+died&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Brian Mulroney, the prime minister whose trade talks with the United States and introduction of a new sales tax forever reshaped the Canadian economy, has died.
He was 84.
"On behalf of my mother and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, The Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th Prime Minister," his daughter Caroline posted on social media late Thursday afternoon.
"He died peacefully, surrounded by family."
Mulroney became prime minister in 1984 when his Progressive Conservatives won 211 seats in the House of Commons - the largest margin of victory for a political party in Canadian history and a feat that has never been repeated.
A self-professed "failed crooner" who as a child made $50 a pop for singing ballads at the paper mill in his hometown of Baie Comeau, Quebec, Mulroney's unrelenting ambition won him friends among some of the most powerful leaders of his generation, but also made enemies of countless Canadians who scorned what they saw as his obsession with his own image and that of his legacy.
By the time he resigned in 1993, the consensus he'd built between Western populists and Quebec nationalists had collapsed, and the goods and services tax his government introduced was so wildly unpopular that it led to the near-annihilation of the PC party in the subsequent election, setting in motion a realignment of conservatism in Canada that continues to this day.
The Airbus affair, an alleged kickback scheme connected to the purchase of new aircraft for Canada, would further tarnish his political legacy, and he would spend the decades after he left office working to polish his image, ever confident that history would erase the blemishes.
"One hundred years from now, what will be remembered was that it was done," he told reporters after clinching the Canada-U.S. free trade deal.
"The naysayers will be forgotten."
Martin Brian Mulroney was born on March 20, 1939, the third of six children. He was the first of them to be born in Baie Comeau, a town set up in the 1930s to house a paper mill.
In a biography by L. Ian MacDonald, Mulroney recounted a childhood spent playing sports, devouring books from the library, and making a name with mill owner Col. Robert McCormick as a boy who could sing any tune, upon request, and being paid $50 for the job.
He left Baie Comeau for high school in New Brunswick, and from there went on to St. Francis Xavier University in Antigonish, N.S.
His undergraduate years laid the groundwork for his life in politics, as he became active in a Progressive Conservative campus club and started working on regional political campaigns.
At Laval University's law school, his political network grew to include Lucien Bouchard, who would later commit a Shakespearean act of political sabotage by double-crossing Mulroney during negotiations over changes to the Constitution known as the Meech Lake accord and siding with Quebec separatists.
After law school, Mulroney found work as a labour lawyer in Montreal and it was there, at age 33, he met then-18-year old Mila Pivnicki, spying her at the Mount Royal Tennis Club one weekday afternoon.
They would marry two years later and go on to have four children - Caroline, Ben, Mark and Nicolas - and 15 grandchildren.
Caroline Mulroney is now a minister in Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government.
In a statement Thursday, Ford said he sees some of Mulroney's best qualities in his children.
"Brian was also so generous with his time. When faced with tough decisions, I often leaned on him for advice and benefited from his experience and his political instincts," Ford said.
"He was a role model to me and taught me countless lessons on how to be a better leader."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau also reflected on the insights Mulroney shared with him over the years.
"He never stopped working for Canadians, and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home," Trudeau said in a written statement, calling him generous, tireless and incredibly passionate.
"As we mourn his passing and keep his family and friends in our thoughts, let us also acknowledge - and celebrate - Mr. Mulroney's role in building the modern, dynamic, and prosperous country we all know today."
By the time of the 1984 general election, the Liberals had been in power for nearly all of two decades and Mulroney - who had been elected leader of the Progressive Conservatives in 1983 - capitalized on numerous concerns with their track record, perhaps summing it up best in the knockout punch he landed on Liberal leader John Turner during a leaders' debates.
With Turner deflecting Mulroney's attacks on his patronage appointments, Mulroney jabbed a finger across the top of the lectern and his baritone voice belted out what would become a classic political quip: " You had an option, sir."
Mulroney would go on to win a landslide victory - 211 seats, including a record 81 in Quebec, where fallout from the constitutional talks of 1982 had led to great frustration with the Liberals.
With a decisive mandate, Mulroney embarked on twin talks to reshape Canada: the Meech Lake accord and a free trade deal with the U.S.
The accord was the sequel to the repatriation of the Constitution in 1982, a bill that Quebec refused to sign at the time.
The first stage of the deal was negotiated and agreed to by all the premiers by 1987. It would see Quebec ink its support for Canada's new constitution in exchange for greater provincial autonomy over certain areas, and a pledge that the province was a "distinct society."
The accord, however, needed to be formally ratified by all the provinces and the subsequent three years of debate saw the national consensus around its provisions collapse as the political landscape in some of the provinces shifted toward staunch opposition to the proposals.
Among the outcomes: a resurgence of the Quebec separatist movement, and the creation of a new federal separatist party the Bloc Québécois, led by Mulroney's old friend Bouchard.
Current Quebec Premier François Legault called him a visionary Thursday because of the Canada-U.S. trade agreement, also celebrating his fight against apartheid in South Africa and his work against acid rain, one of the biggest environmental challenges of the day.
"He was a real ambassador who made Quebec and Canada shine in the world. My thoughts are with his family and friends," Legault said.
Though Mulroney never campaigned on free trade with the U.S., he'd first met U.S. President Ronald Reagan when he was just the Opposition leader in the House of Commons.
The two men developed an affinity for one another, rooted, as Mulroney himself would later observe in his biography, in their shared Irish heritage and their conservative global outlook.
That they were allies was cemented in the so-called "Shamrock summit" of 1985, when the two men took to the stage to belt out the song "When Irish Eyes are Smiling."
Mulroney would repeat the move decades later at a fundraising gala where then-U.S. President Donald Trump was in attendance; during the rehash of trade talks between Canada and the U.S., Mulroney was often Canada's unofficial emissary to the political elite in Washington, working to fend off American protectionist efforts.
Mulroney's initial push toward a free trade deal with the U.S. flowed from a growing consensus in Canada that the protectionist policies of the 1970s were harming the economy and greater harmonization with the U.S. was crucial to further economic growth.
"The maturity and self-confidence of our country make it possible," he told a New York crowd months after he was elected, "to examine options that a few years ago produced emotional reflexes that made rational discussion difficult."
Talks towards a new trade agreement began in 1985, and concluded at the 11th-hour in 1987, when, in Mulroney's telling, he won a last-minute concession from the U.S. for a trade dispute mechanism, and signed off on the deal being pushed through Congress.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, right, and former prime minister Brian Mulroney speak during the Atlantic Economic Forum at St. Francis Xavier University in Antigonish, N.S. on June 19, 2023.Former prime minister Brian Mulroney arrives to speak at a conference put on by the University of Ottawa Professional Development Institute and the Canada School of Public Service in Ottawa on March 5, 2019.

Document 18 of 252

Brian Mulroney will have a state funeral, Justin Trudeau says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brian-mulroney-will-have-state-funeral-justin/docview/2933515728/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brian+Mulroney+will+have+a+state+funeral%2C+Justin+Trudeau+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Former prime minister Brian Mulroney will receive a full state funeral, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Friday.
"We're working with the family closely to ensure that all of their wishes are respected and that it be the right and fitting tribute to him," Trudeau said. "We will be sharing more details of course ... But I can also let everyone know that there will be opportunities for Canadians to express their gratitude and share tributes to the former prime minister as the coming days and weeks unfold."
Flags on Parliament Hill flew at half-mast, and the House of Commons cancelled what would have been an NDP opposition day and adjourned to mark Mulroney's death.
Formal tributes from MPs are expected when parliament resumes after a scheduled constituency break, during the week of Mar. 18.
Many other reflections and tributes from across the political spectrum and around the world flowed Friday, the morning after Mulroney died at age 84.
Speaking in Sudbury, Trudeau, highlighted Mulroney's example as one modern politicians could learn from.
"The reality is he was a statesman who was totally devoted to Canada," said Trudeau, and although he had differences with his political opponents also displayed respect for them despite ideological differences.
"He was an excellent politician, but at the same time, there was a great deal of respect for the role and responsibilities that he had, and for those who were serving even if they were members of a different party. This is a reminder that politics can and must be rooted in service and respect," Trudeau said in Sudbury.
Recalling Mulroney's environmental record, Trudeau said he "stickhandled" the relationship with the U.S. to advance environmental protections, and was rightly recognized as "one of the greenest prime ministers Canada has ever had."
Trudeau said today's Conservatives could take a lesson from Mulroney's record, that "just being a conservative doesn't automatically mean that you don't care about the environment. There is a choice that the current Conservative Party is making that they would be good to reflect upon because Brian Mulroney understood that caring for the environment, caring for the planet, caring for the people who live on it is part of building a strong economy for the future.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford, whose cabinet includes Caroline Mulroney, the former prime minister's daughter who announced his death on behalf of the family, said Friday "I loved the prime minister...I'm going to miss him dearly."
He said Mulroney charmed his mother when he called and sang happy birthday to her. "My mum absolutely loved him and so he gave her a CD of all the songs that he sang."
"He even came by our caucus meetings and you know, when you have a tough day and always give him a call, he boosts you up like I've never seen before, and we just lost such a great Canadian," Ford said Friday.
NDP leader Jagmeet Singh on Friday hailed Mulroney's gesture to offer kind words upon the death of former NDP leader Ed Broadbent.
Mulroney "was actually in the hospital himself, dealing with the health crisis, and the thought of someone dealing with a health crisis taking the time to share some kind words about a political opponent really is a testament to the kind of person Prime Minister literally was," Singh said. He too hailed Mulroney's environmental legacy, and the political breakthrough in Quebec - showing that modern federal parties had to cross the linguistic divide and work to unite the country.
James Baker, a former U.S. Secretary of the Treasury and Secretary of State who counted Mulroney as a friend of 30 years, in a written tribute in the Globe and Mail said, "With Mr. Mulroney as its prime minister, Canada punched well above its weight on the world stage."
"Regardless of how one gauges the ups and downs of his public life, Mr. Mulroney built a considerable legacy that continues to benefit his country and the world today, particularly through his advancement of free trade."
Baker said that Mulroney worked former U.S. presidents Ronald Reagan and George H.W. Bush to construct the continental free trade pact that created "world's biggest and most successful trading bloc."
Foreign Affairs Minister Melanie Joly welled up as she recalled "Brian was a friend" who "was there" when times were tough for her.
Joly said she consulted him when she became foreign affairs minister, he aided the government on the renegotiation of NAFTA and on lifting the steel and aluminum tariffs. She called him every time before she headed to Washington, and said he knew people on the Republican and Democrat side of Congress.
Joly said she was texting the Mexican foreign affairs minister who was former chief of staff to Kofi Annan, former UN secretary general, and shared memories of Mulroney with her.
"It just showed how much he had friends and allies around the world."
Joly said Mulroney's political career and how he dealt with political opponents highlights just how toxic current political debates have grown.
"You don't need to the hate the person in front of you. You just need to agree to disagree," Joly said on Parliament Hill.
"And I think this is something that we should all bear in mind. Because politics is, particularly these years becoming more toxic and polarized. At the end of the day, we all have our different values and ideas. But we're all there for building a greater country."
Former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper, travelling in India, issued a written statement extending sympathies to Mulroney's wife and family, calling him "a historic " and "transformational" figure in Canada's modern era.
Harper first arrived in Ottawa as a junior PC staffer during Mulroney's first mandate, and swiftly became disillusioned with Mulroney's political willingness to give up a deficit-cutting measure to drop the indexation of senior's pensions to the rate of inflation. Harper returned to Alberta and would go on to be a key player in the Reform Party, its successor Canadian Alliance, and ultimately the united modern federal Conservative Party that dropped the word "Progressive" from its title.
It was Harper who called the Oliphant inquiry into Mulroney's role in the Airbus affair, but Harper outlined Mulroney's many accomplishments late Thursday and laid out a political legacy Conservatives lay claim to today.
"Prime Minister Mulroney fought to secure the Free Trade Agreement with the United States, from which Canada has benefitted immensely ever since. Prime Minister Mulroney stood for freedom and democracy on the world stage, in his principled opposition to apartheid in South Africa, his enduring support for Israel, and his advocacy of independence for Ukraine and the other European nations long under the yoke of Soviet communism."
Harper said Mulroney championed "significant international environmental measures" including the Montreal Protocol on ozone-depleting substances, and the Canada-US Acid Rain Treaty."
And like former prime minister Jean Chretien, Harper hailed Mulroney's historic political accomplishment as "the first Canadian Conservative leader to win back-to-back majority mandates since Sir John A. Macdonald, including the largest electoral victory in Canadian history."
Former prime minister and PC leader Joe Clark in a statement posted on "X" hailed Mulroney as "one of Canada's great change-makers."
Mulroney was "determined to move the country forward and with an instinct to respect differences and an extraordinary capacity to reconcile. He was bold, a big dreamer, and an unparalleled negotiator and persuader, both at home in Canada, and in the larger world."
"Often then we were allies, sometimes competitors and then became colleagues in and beyond national government. I am grateful for his extraordinary service, and his friendship, and mourn his loss."
CAPTION: Former prime minister Brian Mulroney leaves Parliament Hill in 2012. His family announced late Thursday that the former Tory leader died peacefully, surrounded by loved ones.

March 1: Frustration not worth the trip downtown and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/march-1-frustration-not-worth-trip-downtown-other/docview/2933515667/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=March+1%3A+Frustration+not+worth+the+trip+downtown+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Frustration not worth the trip
Re: Traffic doomers at it again (Feb. 28)
I would love to live in this letter writer's alternate universe. In my universe, a trip to Theatre Aquarius that once took a leisurely 20-25 minutes now takes over an hour. The last time I tried to use an alternate route. Surprisingly it too was clogged. My wife and I have decided not to renew our season tickets after more than 20 years, as the frustration and effort involved in getting to the play detracts from the enjoyment of the play.
I used to work downtown and could get to work in under 30 minutes. The last time I tried to use York Boulevard that same trip was one hour.
While I want the roads to be safe for everyone, the frustration and resulting anger and irrational behaviour that will result from dangerous two-way streets is definitely not the way to go. The obvious solution is to re-synchronize the stoplights to a lower speed. That will be much safer than the proposed solution and save millions of dollars that can be put toward affordable housing.
Paul John Phillips, Dundas
Change will bring traffic havoc
I don't think that city council knows or cares about the havoc it will create with the Main Street conversion. Of all the stupid things this city has done, other than the LRT and the Red Hill Inquiry, this will take the cake. It's time for our mayor to step up and put a stop to this goofy idea.
Bruce Scott, Hamilton
Conversion's impact will be serious
Re: Brace yourself for Hamilton's looming perma-gridlock (Feb. 27)
Picture this. In a "Hamilton anti-car world" the Main-West conversion has taken place. As pointed out in Scott Radley's column, there are backups from the 403 onto Main Street East all the way back to the York Street exit. Those living in Dundas and Ancaster have found themselves in gridlock as they approach Dalewood on Main Street West. Some try to get off at the Toronto-bound 403 exit off of Main Street thinking they all jump to the front of the line at the next exit. That doesn't work because they are in the beginning of the conversion as they head further east toward Dundurn.
Back to the Main Street East exit. There is a way around that. Just take the Aberdeen exit and take that as far as you can go. Oh, that's right. You can turn right onto Herkimer from Queen. This is a street which now has been reduced to one lane because of parking and bike lanes. Look out! An ambulance wants to make its way to the St. Joseph's Hospital emergency entrance, but increased traffic on Herkimer has no place to go to let the ambulance through.
Speaking of Aberdeen, all traffic wanting to go up the Queen Street hill from Dundas, Ancaster, Burlington and Oakville have also taken the Aberdeen exit and are in gridlock because they will be late for classes at Mohawk College and Hillfield Strathallan and other businesses and appointments on the mountain.
A comment made by Coun. Maureen Wilson that she is "not interested in the supremacy of vehicular movement as the No. 1 determinant in our evaluation matrix" is a slap in the face to parents transporting children in their vehicles, seniors who drive to the supermarket and those who can't cycle for any number of reasons.
When traffic finds other ways of getting around the Main Street conversion by going through neighbourhoods filled with children and established bike lanes, councillors will have to answer to this terrible decision.
At a public meeting held at city hall last fall concerning the conversion, both Coun. Wilson and head of traffic planning were asked if they were at all concerned about gridlock on both Main Street and York Street if three major events were happening at the same time in downtown Hamilton. Their reply was that Toronto goes through it all the time. Since when did we want to be Toronto?
Sue Prestedge, Hamilton
West crucial to Ukraine defence
Re: Diplomacy, not drones, needed to end the war in Ukraine (Feb. 27)
Tamara Lorincz states "Russia now controls more than 20 per cent of (Ukraine)." Indeed. And had it not been for the billions of dollars in military aid provided by NATO and others, it would have been 100 per cent.
Steve Dylag, Dundas
Don't politicize our judges
Re: Why Ford's plan for 'like-minded' judges is a terrible move (Feb. 27)
Doug Ford has put his foot in it again. Our judiciary must not be politically motivated. They must rule according to our laws. If bail requirements are too lenient, then work on changing the law that covers that. You can't have judges imposing different requirements because of their personal feelings. That would result in more and more appeals. More people would be released until trial dates are set. Of course, some appeals would be lost. As our Progressive Conservative government should know from experience.
Sharin McKibbon, Hamilton
Flag would spark more learning
Re: Niagarans want England to return War of 1812 flag (Feb. 28)
What a great and interesting article! I sincerely wish every success to Bob Ciupka, Rob Dubciak and Ron Gibson in their efforts to repatriate this important historic artifact to Niagara. Way back when I was in public school and learning about the War of 1812, there was no mention of the brave fighters of Robert Runchey's Company of Coloured Men, and the valiant Indigenous fighters were only mentioned as an aside. Bringing this flag home would be a stellar way to render a full acknowledgment to all the participants of this crucial battle for our nation. I look forward to the flag's return.
Nancy Hill, Hamilton
CAPTION: Spectator readers are anticipating traffic nightmares if proposed changes are made.

Document 20 of 252

Leap Day deserves to be a stat holiday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leap-day-deserves-be-stat-holiday/docview/2933370892/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Leap+Day+deserves+to+be+a+stat+holiday&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is no point in getting an extra 1,440 minutes if you are locked in routine.
Thursday was LEAP DAY. I capitalized it to make it sound more dramatic since Thursday was also be just another Regular Day. There will be no leap parties, leap concerts, leap food, leap TV specials or leap sales on Amazon.
You will just leap once again into the ho-hum.
A leap day is about as exciting as rush hour.
And this quadrennial plot twist to the Gregorian calendar is hardest on service journalism. How do you find "fun facts" on a cultural quirk that is devoid of meaning?
Ah, the Earth's axial rotation and orbital revolution can be roughly but not precisely measured in an uneven number of days per year! Ah, the Summer Olympics are held on leap years! Ah, those born on Feb. 29 areleaplings!
Even the leapling Tony Robbins can't give a motivational speech on why any of this matters in the hurly-burly of 2024.
NBC's "Today" recently tried to stoke the leaper madness with, "25 Leap Year Activities To Celebrate February's Bonus Day." It was like reading a grocery list you find in a Sobeys parking lot.
The generic suggestions included "get organized," "get outside," "make a special meal" and "play leapfrog." Yes, that seems sensible. After I'm done work on Thursday, I will ask my wife to bend over while resting her hands on her knees. Then I will press down on the small of her back and catapult the full weight of my body over hers and hopefully stick the landing without ripping out her earrings with my rickety knees.
Then we will crawl into the kitchen to "shake up a leap day cocktail" and prepare a "special meal" while wearing neck braces and reeking of Tiger Balm. Before bedtime, hopefully, we can also "make a time capsule" or "reminisce about past leap years."
Honey, remember Feb. 29, 2020? Just before the lockdown? Me neither!
Leap Day needs a drastic overhaul. Leap Day needs to be a national holiday.
The Romans didn't think this through. Screw you, Julius Caesar, or whoever came up with this astronomical hack. There is no better reason for a day off than an extra day. But right now, Leap Day is just an extra day of work.
It's like getting a free lottery ticket and discovering the grand prize is you pay Doug Ford five bucks. It makes no sense to pretend Leap Day is a bonus. Don't invite me over for dinner and ask me to cook. Don't give me an extended warranty and charge me for the floor mats.
Other countries at least try to make Leap Day seem special. As the Washington Post reported Wednesday: "In Ireland, for example, women are encouraged to propose to their partners on leap days, flipping traditional gender roles. In parts of China, children give their parents gifts. In some countries, leap days are popular days for weddings."
Imagine only having to remember your anniversary every four years.
But on Thursday, my wife won't ask me to renew our wedding vows. My daughters won't lavish me with presents. It will be just a Regular Thursday. And why must this "extra day" be shoehorned into the winter doldrums?
Why can't there be an April 31 or Oct. 32? Why can't there be one day every four years in which Kanye's new wife, Bianca Censori, does not go commando in see-through leggings while strolling European streets looking like a zombie with a Skittles fetish?
I am this close to buying that poor woman underwear from the Bay.
An "extra day" in 2024 sounds good until you realize it's a grand illusion. There is no "extra" to be found anywhere these days. Come Friday, you can't even get a plastic fork in your food delivery order. The culture is now all about the clawback. We do more for less.
But what is the one thing we all complain about? Not having enough time.
Life has never been busier. That's why Leap Day should be a statutory holiday.
We deserve to get paid to do nothing for one more day.
Give us a bit more breathing room to catch up on life. Give us a few more hours to calculate the hike to our property taxes. Give us a bonus day to stay abreast of the dystopian circus that is American politics as do-nothing Republicans turn their backs on Ukraine while parroting Russian talking points.
Give us 1,440 minutes to wonder why Mitch McConnell announced Wednesday he's stepping down when he never once stepped up for democracy. Give us a bonus day to make sense of the revolting antisemitism on display around the world in recent months, including here after a man was charged with vandalizing a statue of Al Waxman in Bellevue Square Park with a pro-Hamas message.
But as it stands, Leap Day is a total waste of time for the rest of us. That's why you read Leap Day suggestions that include, "Visit a distant relative." Yes. Hop on a transatlantic flight. Surprise your great aunt while scrambling to get back home before your lunch break ends.
Make Leap Day a national holiday. We need time to make sense of the madness.
CAPTION: The Romans didn't think this through, Vinay Menon writes. Screw you, Julius Caesar, or whoever came up with this astronomical hack. There is no better reason for a day off than an extra day. But right now, Leap Day is just an extra day of work.

CREDIT: Vinay Menon OPINION

Document 21 of 252

Mulroney remembered for being a divisive figure: He signed the historic free trade agreement but was shadowed by scandal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mulroney-remembered-being-divisive-figure/docview/2933370627/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mulroney+remembered+for+being+a+divisive+figure%3A+He+signed+the+historic+free+trade+agreement+but+was+shadowed+by+scandal&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Brian Mulroney, the prime minister whose trade talks with the United States and introduction of a new sales tax forever reshaped the Canadian economy, has died.
He was 84.
"On behalf of my mother and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, The Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th Prime Minister," his daughter Caroline posted on social media late Thursday afternoon.
"He died peacefully, surrounded by family."
Mulroney became prime minister in 1984 when his Progressive Conservatives won 211 seats in the House of Commons - the largest margin of victory for a political party in Canadian history and a feat that has never been repeated.
A self-professed "failed crooner" who as a child made $50 a pop for singing ballads at the paper mill in his hometown of Baie Comeau, Quebec, Mulroney's unrelenting ambition won him friends among some of the most powerful leaders of his generation, but also made enemies of countless Canadians who scorned what they saw as his obsession with his own image and that of his legacy.
By the time he resigned in 1993, the consensus he'd built between Western populists and Quebec nationalists had collapsed, and the goods and services tax his government introduced was so wildly unpopular that it led to the near-annihilation of the PC party in the subsequent election, setting in motion a realignment of conservatism in Canada that continues to this day.
The Airbus affair, an alleged kickback scheme connected to the purchase of new aircraft for Canada, would further tarnish his political legacy, and he would spend the decades after he left office working to polish his image, ever confident that history would erase the blemishes.
"One hundred years from now, what will be remembered was that it was done," he told reporters after clinching the Canada-U.S. free trade deal.
Martin Brian Mulroney was born on March 20, 1939, the third of six children. He was the first of them to be born in Baie Comeau, a town set up in the 1930s to house a paper mill.
After law school, Mulroney found work as a labour lawyer in Montreal and it was there, at age 33, he met then-18-year old Mila Pivnicki, spying her at the Mount Royal Tennis Club one weekday afternoon.
They would marry two years later and go on to have four children - Caroline, Ben, Mark and Nicolas - and 15 grandchildren.
Caroline Mulroney is now a minister in Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government.
In a statement Thursday, Ford said he sees some of Mulroney's best qualities in his children.
"Brian was also so generous with his time. When faced with tough decisions, I often leaned on him for advice and benefited from his experience and his political instincts," Ford said.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau also reflected on the insights Mulroney shared with him over the years.
"He never stopped working for Canadians, and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home," Trudeau said in a written statement, calling him generous, tireless and incredibly passionate.
As prime minister, Mulroney embarked on twin talks to reshape Canada: the Meech Lake accord and a free trade deal with the U.S.
The accord was the sequel to the repatriation of the Constitution in 1982, a bill that Quebec refused to sign at the time.
The first stage of the deal was negotiated and agreed to by all the premiers by 1987. It would see Quebec ink its support for Canada's new constitution in exchange for greater provincial autonomy over certain areas, and a pledge that the province was a "distinct society."
The accord, however, needed to be formally ratified by all the provinces and the subsequent three years of debate saw the national consensus around its provisions collapse as the political landscape in some of the provinces shifted toward staunch opposition to the proposals.
Among the outcomes: a resurgence of the Quebec separatist movement, and the creation of a new federal separatist party the Bloc Québécois, led by Mulroney's old friend Bouchard.
Current Quebec Premier François Legault called him a visionary Thursday because of the Canada-U.S. trade agreement, also celebrating his fight against apartheid in South Africa and his work against acid rain.
"He was a real ambassador who made Quebec and Canada shine in the world. My thoughts are with his family and friends," Legault said.
CAPTION: Former prime minister Brian Mulroney "died peacefully, surrounded by family" his daughter Caroline posted on social media late Thursday afternoon.Sean Kilpatrick Canadian Press file photo

Letters

Full text: Frustration not worth the trip
Re: Traffic doomers at it again (Feb. 28)
I would love to live in this letter writer's alternate universe. In my universe, a trip to Theatre Aquarius that once took a leisurely 20-25 minutes now takes over an hour. The last time I tried to use an alternate route, surprisingly it too was clogged. My wife and I have decided not to renew our season tickets after more than 20 years, as the frustration and effort involved in getting to the play detracts from the enjoyment of the play.
While I want the roads to be safe for everyone, the frustration and resulting anger and irrational behaviour that will result from dangerous two-way streets is definitely not the way to go. The obvious solution is to re-synchronize the stoplights to a lower speed. That will be much safer than the proposed solution and save millions of dollars that can be put toward affordable housing.
Paul John Phillips, Dundas
Change will bring traffic havoc
I don't think that city council knows or cares about the havoc it will create with the Main Street conversion. Of all the stupid things this city has done, other than the LRT and the Red Hill inquiry, this will take the cake. It's time for our mayor to step up and put a stop to this goofy idea.
Bruce Scott, Hamilton
Conversion's impact serious
Re: Brace yourself for Hamilton's
looming perma-gridlock (Feb. 27)
Picture this. In a "Hamilton anti-car world" the Main-West conversion has taken place. As pointed out in Scott Radley's column, there are backups from the 403 onto Main Street East all the way back to the York Street exit. Those living in Dundas and Ancaster have found themselves in gridlock as they approach Dalewood on Main Street West. Some try to get off at the Toronto-bound 403 exit off of Main Street thinking they all jump to the front of the line at the next exit. That doesn't work because they are in the beginning of the conversion as they head further east toward Dundurn.
Back to the Main Street East exit. There is a way around that. Just take the Aberdeen exit and take that as far as you can go. Oh, that's right. You can turn right onto Herkimer from Queen. This is a street which now has been reduced to one lane because of parking and bike lanes. Look out! An ambulance wants to make its way to the St. Joseph's Hospital emergency entrance, but increased traffic on Herkimer has no place to go to let the ambulance through.
A comment made by Coun. Maureen Wilson that she is "not interested in the supremacy of vehicular movement as the No. 1 determinant in our evaluation matrix" is a slap in the face to parents transporting children in their vehicles, seniors who drive to the supermarket and those who can't cycle for any number of reasons.
When traffic finds other ways of getting around the Main Street conversion by going through neighbourhoods filled with children and established bike lanes, councillors will have to answer to this terrible decision.
Sue Prestedge, Hamilton
Ukraine depends on the West
Re: Diplomacy, not drones,
needed to end the war in Ukraine (Feb. 27)
Tamara Lorincz states "Russia now controls more than 20 per cent of (Ukraine)." Indeed. And had it not been for the billions of dollars in military aid provided by NATO and others, it would have been 100 per cent.
Steve Dylag, Dundas
Don't politicize our judges
Re: Why Ford's plan for 'like-minded'
judges is a terrible move (Feb. 27)
Doug Ford has put his foot in it again. Our judiciary must not be politically motivated. They must rule according to our laws. If bail requirements are too lenient, then work on changing the law that covers that. You can't have judges imposing different requirements because of their personal feelings. That would result in more and more appeals. More people would be released until trial dates are set. Of course, some appeals would be lost. As our Progressive Conservative government should know from experience.
Sharin McKibbon, Hamilton
Flag would spark more learning
Re: Niagarans want England
to return War of 1812 flag (Feb. 28)
What a great and interesting article! I sincerely wish every success to Bob Ciupka, Rob Dubciak and Ron Gibson in their efforts to repatriate this important historic artifact to Niagara. Way back when I was in public school and learning about the War of 1812, there was no mention of the brave fighters of Robert Runchey's Company of Coloured Men, and the valiant Indigenous fighters were only mentioned as an aside. Bringing this flag home would be a stellar way to render a full acknowledgment to all the participants of this crucial battle for our nation. I look forward to the flag's return.
Nancy Hill, Hamilton

Document 23 of 252

Former judge slams Ford's comments: Appointing 'like-minded' officials will lead to erosion of judicial independence, says retired jurist

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-judge-slams-fords-comments/docview/2933368843/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+judge+slams+Ford%27s+comments%3A+Appointing+%27like-minded%27+officials+will+lead+to+erosion+of+judicial+independence%2C+says+retired+jurist&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is wrong if he thinks appointing "tough" judges will solve violent crime, says a veteran Ontario jurist with a reputation for being a tough judge.
"We want judges who care about the community, give to the community; (who are) decisive, and fearless," said Norm Douglas, a former prosecutor and Ontario criminal court judge who retired in 2021 after nearly a half-century in the criminal justice system.
"You don't want 'Judge Judy' on the bench," he said, referencing the tough, moralizing style of the American TV character.
Ford has touched off a firestorm with his public comments responding to a Star exclusive on the appointment of two of his former staffers to the provincial committee responsible for selecting judges for the Ontario Court of Justice. Asked to justify what critics had called "blatant" patronage appointments, Ford explained Monday that he wants "like-minded" judges who share his belief that the "massive crime waves across our cities" are caused by judges releasing too many criminals on bail.
The committee ultimately sends a ranked list of recommendations to the attorney general, who makes the final decision on which judges to appoint.
Earlier in his career, Douglas, 77, earned the nickname "Stormin' Norman" from defence lawyers for his aggression as a prosecutor, which included serving as senior counsel and director of criminal prosecutions for Ontario. In many ways, it's a resume that made him exactly the kind of "tough-on-crime" judge Ford says he wants to appoint - but even so, Douglas doesn't buy the suggestion that there's an epidemic of violence due to judges granting bail too often.
"You can't take a broad brush and say the system is wrong because a judge or justice of the peace let this guy out on bail, and he should never have been let out, so why don't we toughen up the system," he said. "You don't know the details." (Ontario bail hearings are, with few exceptions, subject to publication bans that prohibit journalists from reporting the rationale behind any bail decision.)
In his recently published book, "You Be the Judge," Douglas set out to show the complexities involved in a judge's decision-making process, including summaries of some of his cases. Readers are invited to decide on their own before Douglas reveals his own verdict and reasoning. The book, he says, includes cases that demonstrate his reputation for toughness was undeserved.
But a stiff sentence is sometimes called for, he explains, such as when he "whacked a guy" with a 14-year prison sentence for sexually abusing his stepchildren. Nevertheless, Douglas says he was never under the illusion that such a punishment would solve "the problem of stepfathers' abusing children."
And details matter in court cases. Douglas once sentenced an 84-year-old man to six months of jail time for writing graffiti on walls in Guelph. Some might regard that as harsh - jail time for a non-violent crime is unusual for someone that old - but the man adored Hitler and wrote things such as "kill Jews" and other reprehensible comments.
Judges are also not the only ones responsible for the perceived leniency that's now become the target of politicians like Ford, Douglas added in an interview Tuesday in a Collingwood coffee shop.
His book includes a case of a man he prosecuted for sexually torturing and killing a neighbour. He was convicted and handed a life sentence, but Douglas was shocked to later learn the man was released on parole after 10 years before going on to kill a 21-year-old criminology student working in a halfway house.
"He should never have been released," Douglas said - but the decision was made by a federal parole board, and had nothing to do with how tough the judge was.
Douglas also takes exception to Ford endorsing the "politicization" of the appointment process. Such an American-style system would lead to governments of different stripes getting elected and making appointments of their own "like-minded people."
The problem with appointments based on political leanings is that it leads to the erosion of judicial independence and increases the possibility of miscarriages of justice, he said.
The central occupation of the justice system is to avoid convicting an innocent person, but that "is more likely to happen with a tough prosecutor and a tough judge than it is with anyone else," Douglas noted. Nor do we want judges who might convict in a high-profile case under pressure from political masters, he added.
"We can't be governed by what the community thinks of us. That's not why we're appointed, we're not politicians."
Nevertheless, as University of Guelph politics professor Troy Riddell wrote this week, Ford might find choosing judges who decide cases consistently in a certain direction is a difficult task. "Not only do judges have guarantees of independence but, once appointed, professional norms tend to lead judges to impartially apply the law (as best as they understand it) to the facts," wrote Riddell in an article published by The Conversation.
Riddell's article included a link to research on judicial behaviour that shows in cases where judges have latitude, such as in granting bail, "one cannot assume former prosecutors or Conservative appointees are going to be "tough on crime" as envisioned by Premier Ford."
As for the system that selects Ontario judges, Douglas, who became a judge in 1994 after being approved by the judicial advisory committee, says it works and has produced an excellent bench.
He also notes this.
This "tough Crown attorney, Stormin' Norman, when I was appointed to be a judge, you know the (Ontario) government in power when I was appointed? The New Democrats," he says with a smile.
CAPTION: Former Ontario judge Norm Douglas, shown in 2011, says politicizing the judicial appointment process will increase the potential for miscarriages of justice.Betsy Powell Toronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Betsy Powell Toronto Star

Document 24 of 252

He helped shape Stephen Harper's 'tough on crime' agenda. It's why he says Doug Ford is wrong to stack the courts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/he-helped-shape-stephen-harpers-tough-on-crime/docview/2933365619/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=He+helped+shape+Stephen+Harper%27s+%27tough+on+crime%27+agenda.+It%27s+why+he+says+Doug+Ford+is+wrong+to+stack+the+courts&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's intention to appoint tough "like-minded judges" who will put even more people in jail is a "deeply disturbing" stance that fails to grasp what's really needed to keep Ontarians safe, says University of British Columbia law professor Benjamin Perrin.
And Perrin would know a thing or two about a "tough on crime" approach with a heavy focus on incarceration.
He was once the lead criminal justice advisor to former Prime Minister Stephen Harper, whose federal Conservative government dramatically increased the number of mandatory minimum sentences, among other measures that drove up the number of incarcerated people - only for many of those laws to later be struck down as unconstitutional.
"If the goal here is to increase the safety of Ontarians, this is completely the wrong way to do it and it's just political grandstanding and bluster," Perrin said in an interview this week.
He spoke to the Star as a crisis of constitutional proportions is erupting in Ontario as Ford "quadruples" down on wanting judges who think like him and rule the way he wants. Advocacy group Democracy Watch announced Thursday it would be launching a legal challenge over the appointments system. A number of major legal organizations have decried Ford's intentions as nothing short of a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts and the public's confidence in the justice system; a move that risks tainting the integrity of future judges and calling their decisions into question.
In short, it's a threat to the rule of law.
Perrin said he was alarmed to hear that Ford is intent on "stacking the judiciary" with lawyers who believe in throwing more people in jail.
"To suggest that a series of appointments will be made to fundamentally alter the way that the judiciary makes decisions to suit a premier's particular political agenda is deeply disturbing," Perrin said.
"Imagine a premier saying, 'We're only going to appoint judges who believe in a pro-life agenda,' or only a pro-choice agenda, or only advance the interests of real estate developers."
Once himself an ardent believer that people convicted of crimes were being punished too leniently, Perrin's views on criminal justice have changed dramatically over the years as he researched the root causes of crime, as well as solutions.
"We have very clear research dating back for decades now that consistently shows that incarceration actually increases reoffending," he said. "When people are sent to prison, they tend to come out worse than they entered. Incarcerating someone is often seen as making them a better criminal."
He pointed to the lack of services and programming for people who are incarcerated, particularly in pre-trial detention in provincial jails, which does little to help rehabilitate people, most of whom will inevitably be released.
What's really needed to increase public safety, Perrin said, are widespread access to mental health and substance use disorder supports, vocational training, affordable housing and a guaranteed minimum income, which would largely be provincial responsibilities.
"The research is really, really clear that when people have that chance, the majority of people do better," he said.
As the Star first reported last Thursday, the Ford government placed two of the premier's ex-senior staffers on the arm's-length committee that vets applications and recommends candidates for the Ontario Court of Justice.
Former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy is now the chair of the 13-person Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), while ex-director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has also been a public member of the committee since December.
Global News also reported Wednesday that Bondy and Vandrick are registered to lobby the Ontario government, with Bondy lobbying for a gun manufacturer, among other firms. The Tories said both had been vetted for potential conflicts, and are not lobbying the attorney general, to whom the JAAC makes recommendations on judges.
WATCH: Doug Ford defends 'patronage' appointments of ex-staffers, says he wants 'like-minded' Ontario judges
Ford has been unrepentant about putting Bondy and Vandrick on the committee and wanting tough, like-minded judges. At Queen's Park on Wednesday, he repeated a familiar talking point that far too often, judges and justices of the peace in Ontario are releasing accused violent people on bail pending their trials, who then go on to re-offend.
"I'm not gonna double down, I'm not gonna triple down, I'm gonna quadruple down" to make sure violent criminals go to jail, Ford said in response to NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who asked during question period if he would rescind the appointments of Bondy and Vandrick.
There is a lack of data nationwide when it comes to bail, something the federal government acknowledged last year when it nonetheless implemented changes to make it more difficult for people accused of certain crimes to get bail.
About 80 per cent of people incarcerated in Ontario's overcrowded jails are on remand - meaning people who have been denied bail and are awaiting trial or sentencing - a number that has steadily increased over the years.
The office of Ontario Court Chief Justice Sharon Nicklas declined to comment on Ford's stance this week, while the Association of Ontario Judges has not issued any public statements on the matter.
Attorney General Doug Downey has accused the former Liberal government of appointing a number of party donors to the bench. He went further during question period on Thursday, saying the Liberals filled two associate chief justice positions when they weren't open yet, on the same day that former premier Dalton McGuinty stepped down.
"I will take no lessons from these people on how the system should work," Downey said.
Perrin was a lifelong conservative who volunteered for the Reform Party before he could even vote, and worked in campaign war rooms and on policy development before becoming Harper's criminal justice advisor for 2012-13. He strongly supported the increase of mandatory minimum sentences and other Criminal Code changes designed to send more people to prison - "very much along the tough-on-crime agenda that both federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford are advancing today," he said.
"There are no fresh ideas, there are no new ideas, we're seeing a rehashing of old, discredited tough-on-crime ideas."
At the end of the day, judges are still required to follow the law, Perrin said. The Charter of Rights and Freedoms is very clear: people accused of crimes have the right to be tried by an "independent and unbiased tribunal," and they have the right not to be denied reasonable bail.
"And I am concerned with Premier Ford's statements that he's looking to appoint judges who will be more minded to incarcerate people, because that suggests he would be appointing biased judges," Perrin said.
"That undermines our constitutional rights and freedoms as Canadians."
CAPTION: "If the goal here is to increase the safety of Ontarians, this is completely the wrong way to do it and it's just political grandstanding and bluster," saysUniversity of British Columbia law professor Benjamin Perrin, a former advisor to Stephen Harper, left, about the crisis brewing over comments made by Premier Doug Ford.

Subject: Committees; Crime; Law; Attorneys general; Advisors; Imprisonment; Politics; Lobbying; Trials; Judges & magistrates

People: Harper, Stephen

Title: He helped shape Stephen Harper's 'tough on crime' agenda. It's why he says Doug Ford is wrong to stack the courts
