Document 25 of 252

Closing schools for the eclipse would be unfair to working families, Ford's education minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/closing-schools-eclipse-would-be-unfair-working/docview/2933614423/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Closing+schools+for+the+eclipse+would+be+unfair+to+working+families%2C+Ford%27s+education+minister+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Closing schools on the day of the solar eclipse would be "unfair" to working families and won't be tolerated, says Education Minister Stephen Lecce.
While deeming April 8 a professional activity day is fine - as long as parents are given notice - Lecce said boards shouldn't be shutting down scheduled classes if they've opted to keep it a regular learning day.
Lecce also slammed the Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers union for pushing to have a virtual P.A. day so members can work from home for "safety" reasons, something their board has already rejected.
"I think every citizen on Earth will be showing up to work - every front-line officer, every front-line worker, from nurses to transit workers - will be going to work, and I expect the same for our educators," Lecce said at Queen's Park on Thursday when asked by reporters about schools closings.
"I've expressed a great deal of concern that the instinct of that local union is to try to keep their teachers home when every single person in the economy is going to work," Lecce said, adding he has indicated to all 72 school boards across the province "that educators will show up, work with our kids and support them as they do every day and that extends on the day of the solar eclipse."
The minister also said he's not in favour of "the unilateral closure of schools that really sets back working parents, particularly the most vulnerable families, single parents, who don't have the luxury of a child-care space. I find it very unfair on them to close schools. When we have a (professional activity day) with notice, we can tolerate that. What I won't tolerate is indiscriminately closing schools, affecting working families and frankly, reducing the ability of a child to learn in a dynamic way about science and astronomy and exciting things that are happening."
Some boards, like the York Region District School Board, have opted to end the school day early rather than send kids home in the midst of the solar eclipse.
Liberal MPP John Fraser said he agrees there is no reason to close schools.
"We've had solar eclipses before," he told reporters. "It's not the end times."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "schools can take measures if there are any health and safety concerns and use it as a learning opportunity for students."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she "would like to understand a little bit better what their concerns are. But you know ... it's pretty tough to be a teacher right now or any education worker in our schools."
Last weekend, Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers, also known as TECT, sent a memo to members saying "in anticipation of the upcoming solar eclipse, the (professional activity) day was changed from April 19 to April 8 to better ensure student safety. TECT supported this change and also advocated that the April 8 P.A. day be virtual to prioritize the safety of our members. At this time, the board has not extended similar protective measures for our teachers."
School boards across the province have swapped April's professional activities to the day of the eclipse so that students aren't headed for home around the time skies are dark or when looking at the sky could damage their eyes.
In the Thames Valley public board in the London area, a recent public survey showed about half of respondents said they were unhappy about a scheduled P.A. day at the start of the next school year, right after Labour Day.
The survey, which was small with less than 200 taking part, found that respondents believed it caused child-care issues for parents.
The board, however, cannot declare a professional activity day before Labour Day based on collective agreements with educators - and hasn't had one for the past five years - and has said that the health and safety issues covered are essential for teachers to know before classes resume. Parents are to be notified as soon as the education ministry approves the board's 2024-25 school year calendar.
CAPTION: Ontario Education Minister Stephen Lecceslammed the Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers union for pushing to have a virtual P.A. day so members can work from home for "safety" reasons during the solar eclipse.Elementary teachers at the Toronto Catholic have asked to work from home during the April 8 solar eclipse saying there is a safety risk going to work.

Subject: Parents & parenting; Families & family life; Solar eclipses; Elementary schools; Learning; Education; Teachers; Labor unions; School boards; School closures

Business indexing term: Subject: Labor unions

Title: Closing schools for the eclipse would be unfair to working families, Ford's education minister says

Document 26 of 252

Will pharmacare become an empty dream for Trudeau's government?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-pharmacare-become-empty-dream-trudeaus/docview/2933613774/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+pharmacare+become+an+empty+dream+for+Trudeau%27s+government%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A national universal pharmacare program that would lay out government-funded coverage for prescription drugs in Canada remains a hope, not a strategy.
Even after Thursday's big reveal of a long-promised bill that said many of the right things in the eyes of the NDP, it is clear the patchwork of public drug coverage that is now in place across Canada will remain, possibly for a long time.
The Trudeau government's promise in Bill C-64 is to start slowly and offer drug coverage for birth control and diabetes. The bill first has to win passage in a minority Parliament - which is likely with NDP support - but it still faces a jammed legislative schedule and relatively few sitting days between now and the end of June.
Though it has big ambitions for a broader plan, it sets out a timeline that even Health Minister Mark Holland concedes may not see initial contraceptive and diabetes coverage start before spring 2025, months before a scheduled federal election.
Beyond that, it's anyone's guess.
The Conservatives who, polls show, may well form the next federal government, have vowed to cut costs, not build new national health spending programs, and have long argued they'd leave health decisions to the provinces. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre on Thursday offered no guarantees he'd honour any deal cut between "two incompetent politicians" - Justin Trudeau and Jagmeet Singh - and backed Quebec's right to opt out of any federal plan with full compensation.
And that's where some of the threads of the patchwork quilt, even if Holland can stitch one together, begin to fray.
Holland's plan assumes that provinces responsible for health care will agree once they see there is money on the table. In this case, he pegged the cost of contraceptive and diabetes coverage at $1.5 billion.
That hope worked when the Liberals negotiated the transfer of billions for $10-a-day daycare deals and health deals to hire more doctors and nurses, enhance mental health services, and modernize health data collection.
But on pharmacare, there is deep skepticism among big provinces.
Quebec has already said no thanks - it has its own program. Just hand over the money.
Alberta grumbles it has not been consulted and is not all that interested.
And Ontario, Canada's largest province, is distinctly cool.
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the province is "waiting for details" from Ottawa on how a national pharmacare program would work.
"We have OHIP+, so we have a pretty robust system, particularly on the diabetes side with the assistive devices program," said Jones, referring to the provincial pharmacare system that provides more than 5,000 different medications to people 24 years or younger who are not covered by a private benefits plan.
"So we need to see and assess what the federal government is proposing and how it matches or conflicts with the existing system that is already here," she said in Toronto.
"I was very clear with the minister when we spoke that I'm not writing off anything, but I'm also not buying into something where I don't know exactly what's there and the assessment truly has to happen. I want to make sure that those very valuable programs can continue in Ontario."
Alberta's United Conservative government on Thursday appeared to soften its vehement opposition after it accused the Liberals earlier this week of ushering in a plan bent on overstepping federal jurisdiction to maintain their alliance with the NDP.
Alberta Health Minister Adriana LaGrange wrote on X, "We expect the federal government will invest in Alberta's pharmacare program to enhance the existing comprehensive programs we have available to Albertans."
B.C. and Newfoundland and Labrador have signalled they are interested in further talks, the minister said, adding he's "optimistic" about the rest.
Still, there are big objections from the national group that represents Canada's insurance industry which said Ottawa's plan puts "at risk the workplace benefit plans that 27 million Canadians count on."
The Canadian Life and Health Insurance Association in a written statement said the bill unveiled Thursday will only waste billions by spending money on drugs for people who already have coverage, rather than targeting benefits to "the relatively small number of Canadians who lack public or private drug coverage now."
For the Heart and Stroke Foundation, the proposed bill is a good first step, but not a comprehensive one to address the high cost of prescription drugs that leaves many Canadians splitting pills, skipping doses, or not filling or renewing much-needed prescriptions.
The group - which Holland once worked for - wants Ottawa to quickly expand the program "in a fiscally prudent way by adding to it a list of essential medicines, including prescription drugs for heart conditions and stroke, as recommended in the government's 2019 Hoskins Report on pharmacare."
Eric Hoskins, the former Ontario health minister who led that report and concluded a public, universal, single-payer system was necessary to replace Canada's patchwork of private and public plans, told the Star in an email that he wasn't concerned about Ottawa's approach potentially preserving gaps in the system.
"This is a big, and important, undertaking that will take years to fully implement," wrote Hoskins, who also advised the Liberal and the NDP during the negotiations.
He said the first step is "such a powerful and impactful way to start" that he has no doubt provinces and territories will sign on.
Hoskins said the significance of Thursday's bill is that the government has enshrined its intentions in legislation, which makes it more difficult for a future government to roll it back.
"If a future government wants to roll back pharmacare, they will have to amend or repeal the legislation through votes and a full, public legislative process in Parliament. I think it will be difficult, if not impossible, to take these critically important medications away from 4 million diabetics and millions of women who both need and deserve them."
However, Patrick Fafard, a professor who teaches at the University of Ottawa's schools of medicine, and public and international affairs, said the bill really only makes it "a little bit politically harder" to turn back.
Fafard agreed the Liberals' intentions are ambitious. And real savings may be found if governments agree on - as the bill sets out - "a pan-Canadian strategy regarding the appropriate use of prescription drugs and related products." At the same time, he said, the bill stipulates only that the health minister "may, after discussions with the provinces and territories," request a report within three years on the progress to advance a national drug strategy.
"The legislation as written - 'may'- seems pretty permissive. So a future Conservative government could simply do nothing so as not to draw attention to what it's not doing or not progressing," he said.
With files from Robert Benzie, Queen's Park bureau chief

CAPTION: Federal Health Minister Mark Holland rises during Question Period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Feb. 29, 2024.

Subject: Provinces; Diabetes; Legislation; Prescription drugs; Cost control; Insurance industry

Document 27 of 252

Ontario invests $1.6B in new buildings at CAMH

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-invests-1-6b-new-buildings-at-camh/docview/2933058558/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+invests+%241.6B+in+new+buildings+at+CAMH&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mindful of the toll that mental health and addiction take on Ontario society and the economy, the province is spending $1.6 billion on new CAMH facilities.
"Without health, you're not going to have a healthy economy," Associate Minister for Mental Health Michael Tibollo said Thursday at the official groundbreaking for the expansion of the Queen Street West campus.
"Over 200,000 people every week are not going to work because of a mental health or addiction issue," said Tibollo.
"These are significant, staggering numbers and they need to be addressed," he said.
To that end, Premier Doug Ford said Queen's Park would spend more than $1.6 billion toward the design and construction of two massive new buildings at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health.
That will help bankroll a "discovery centre" for research and a "secure care and recovery" building for an additional 214 in-patients with complex mental illness who are under court-ordered care and require enhanced treatment and higher security for their own safety and for that of the public.
"This expansion is a major step forward in improving mental health care here in Toronto and throughout our province," said Ford.
Dr. Aristotle Voineskos, CAMH vice-president of research and director of the Campbell Family Mental Health Research Institute, said the discovery centre "isn't just a building."
"It's a beacon of hope, a magnet that will attract the best and brightest minds from around the world to CAMH and this great province to transform mental health care," said Voineskos.
"Hope is the most powerful thing in the world and that's what research gives us," he said.
With private donors are expected to foot the rest of the tab for the two buildings, famed architect Bruce Kuwabara said the new campus is a reminder of the progress made on mental health and addiction over the past few decades.
"Twenty years ago, no one would put their name on a building for mental health," said Kuwabara, whose firm designed the curvy research centre under construction at Queen and Shaw Streets.
He pointed out that when the facility was founded in the 19th century, its 27-acre site was "as far away from downtown Toronto as you could possibly be -- out of sight, out of mind."
CAMH president and CEO Sarah Downey stressed that "the physical transformation of our Queen Street West site is turning what was once a walled institution into a symbol of hope for the future of mental health care.
"With the construction of the secure care and recovery building and discovery centre, we will continue to provide the highest quality care to our most complex patients and accelerate research discoveries and developments that will improve the lives of those living with mental illness," said Downey.
"Because mental health is health."
CAPTION: Associate Minister of Mental Health and Addictions Michael Tibollo, shown in Peterborough, Ont. on May 2, 2023.

Subject: Addictions; Mental health care; Mental disorders

Document 28 of 252

The Future We Choose: Time is running out to change course on energy choices

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/future-we-choose-time-is-running-out-change/docview/2933058453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Future+We+Choose%3A+Time+is+running+out+to+change+course+on+energy+choices&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At last November's global climate summit, Canada joined 120 countries in pledging to triple renewable energy by 2030. But in Ontario, which uses 40 per cent of Canada's energy supply, we are going backwards under this government's gross mismanagement of the energy file.
If Premier Doug Ford has his way, Ontario taxpayers will pay billions to subsidize their own electricity rates when we could be investing in renewables, while even more CO2 will get pumped into our atmosphere. Not good, with less than six years to keep warming below the 1.5 C threshold.
Over 70 per cent of Canadians are worried about climate change, according to a poll conducted last September. Unfortunately, short-term worry over inflation is distracting many people from prioritizing climate action. This plays right into Ford's hands, allowing him to get away with a so-called "climate action plan" that has no clear strategy and lurches from one bad decision to the next. But if you think today's inflation is bad, you haven't seen anything yet. Climate change will reduce crop yields, pushing food prices up while floods and wildfires will destroy property and insurance rates will likely skyrocket.
Meanwhile, Ford's answer to meeting Ontario's growing demand for energy won't just delay getting more electricity produced, it will end up costing all of us more while driving up emissions that feed climate change. Already, the cost to taxpayers of artificially lowering expensive electricity rates is diverting funds from other critical priorities like investment in clean energy, affordable housing and our ailing health-care system.
Ontario's Financial Accountability Officer has estimated that electricity rate subsidies will cost taxpayers $118 billion over the next two decades, or roughly $6 billion per year. This is bad enough, but increasing our dependence on nuclear and gas-fired power will just add to the problem.
Based on work done by Bloomberg New Energy Finance, by 2050 we can expect solar and wind power, currently supplying nine per cent of global energy, to meet 62 per cent of global electricity demand. And the primary driver of such staggering growth is economic, over and above its power to confront climate change. Simply put, this energy will be cheaper to produce that fossil fuel or nuclear energy.
Now consider these Ontario Clean Air Alliance figures on some future electricity scenarios for Ontario: we could build offshore wind power and get electricity at an estimated 14.3 cents/kilowatt hour, or we could pay 22.7 cents/kWh for gas-fired energy and 24.4 cents/kWh for new nuclear energy.
Why, then, is Ford pushing to rebuild the aging Pickering nuclear power plant? What makes this "plan" even more ridiculous and worrisome is that while this time-consuming project lumbers along, the added reliance on gas power will increase emissions by as much as 600 per cent.
Despite 35 Ontario communities having endorsed a gas-plant phase-out, Ford is ignoring opposition while conjuring up yet another back-room deal to refurbish the Pickering site, despite the absence of accurate cost projections for the work. Not to mention the fact that nuclear builds are notorious for extensive time and cost overruns, while adding to our burden of nuclear waste, which can take 100,000 or more years to lose its radioactivity and is very costly to store safely.
The people of Ontario deserve better. We deserve lower cost energy and a viable plan for meeting energy demand while reducing greenhouse gas emissions. It's time to speak up, speak loudly and demand the Ford government get serious about cheap, renewable energy. Then we'd have a more sustainable future, with the added bonus of more money in our pockets.
CAPTION: If you think today's inflation is bad, you haven't seen anything yet. Climate change will reduce crop yields, pushing food prices up while floods and wildfires will destroy property and insurance rates will likely skyrocket, Jane Jenner writes.
CREDIT: Jane Jenner

Subject: Electric rates; Nuclear energy; Offshore; Emissions; Climate change

Location: Canada

Title: The Future We Choose: Time is running out to change course on energy choices

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 29, 2024

Document 29 of 252

Ford's sneaky act pushes sprawl

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-sneaky-act-pushes-sprawl/docview/2932837043/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+sneaky+act+pushes+sprawl&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford claims his new so-called "Get it Done Act" will make it easier to build houses in Ontario, but in reality it's a disingenuous piece of work that quietly slips in changes that will make Ontario's housing crisis worse.
Hidden in the middle of this messy omnibus bill is language that will require many Ontario municipalities to allow new development on previously protected farmland, forests, river valleys and wetlands.
It's clear that Ford's government intends to continue leaning into the repeatedly debunked myth that we don't have enough land already set aside in Ontario for housing development.
The premier used this falsehood to justify his scandal-plagued plan to open up Greenbelt land for development, which he had to reverse after a major public outcry induced by his previous commitment to protect it and by the whiff of corruption.
Ontario is losing 129 hectares of farmland every day to development and sprawl. Yet studies show that there is ample serviced or service-ready land within municipal boundaries across the province.
Last year, Waterloo Region planner Kevin Eby used the government's own flawed, sprawl friendly land assessment approach to show that well over two million homes can be built on land already set aside for development.
But by once again pushing the myth that we need to pave more nature and farmland, the government sends a strong signal to municipalities and developers that will lead both them and the rest of us in the wrong direction when it comes to housing.
Ford's new bill pushes cities, towns and builders to divert hard-to-get skilled labour and construction materials away from building affordable, diverse housing units where people want to live and work.
Instead, if the bill is passed we'll see builders encouraged to put up more large, expensive houses that are unaffordable to average Ontario families. Mini-monster homes in spread out neighbourhoods require huge public investments in sewers, power lines, roads and highways far away from where most people live and work.
The last thing Ontarians need in a housing crisis is a new law that supports building the wrong type of housing in the wrong places at the wrong prices.
One thing most Ontarians do agree on is that we need more affordable housing, for well over a million new households in the coming decade. To achieve this, we need to ensure we're getting the most bang for the buck out of our construction workers and materials.
Unfortunately, Ford's new law diverts these resources away from where they're needed most. Every worker building a large, expensive single-family oversized home in a new suburb is not building an affordable home in the right location.
If Ford actually wants to "get it done" and encourage more affordable home construction, he can remove the section in his proposed legislation that requires municipalities to support wasteful construction.
Then, he can replace it with new policies proposed by housing specialists to promote sensible intensification. This would make it easy for municipalities and developers to build affordable projects within urban boundaries and get us the most number of homes in the shortest time.
This is common sense that is backed by decades of experience and hard data. Even his hand-picked Housing Affordability Task Force was heading in this direction.
It's not too late for Ford to amend his bill so it encourages urban affordable housing rather than paving farmland and green space.
David Crombie is a
former mayor of Toronto
and federal cabinet
minister. Anne Golden is
the former president of
the Conference Board
of Canada. Both are
founders of the Alliance
for a Livable Ontario.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: David Crombie and Anne Golden David Crombie is a former mayor of Toronto and federal cabinet minister. Anne Golden is the former president of the Conference Board of Canada. Both are founders of the Alliance for a Livable Ontario.;

Subject: Affordable housing; Housing developments; Roads & highways

Time running out to change course on energy choices

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-running-out-change-course-on-energy-choices/docview/2932837033/se-2?accountid=15090

Subject: Electric rates; Nuclear energy; Offshore; Emissions; Climate change

Location: Canada

Title: Time running out to change course on energy choices

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.19

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 29, 2024

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton, Ont.

Document 31 of 252

Here's why Doug Ford can get away with squeezing Ontario's colleges and universities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-why-doug-ford-can-get-away-with-squeezing/docview/2932835863/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+why+Doug+Ford+can+get+away+with+squeezing+Ontario%27s+colleges+and+universities&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The problem with Premier Doug Ford's pocketbook populism is that it's so infectious.
It catches on with voters. And latches on to opposition politicians.
Ford led the way by cutting and then freezing tuition for campuses across Ontario after taking power in 2018. Now, New Democrats and Liberals are parroting his tuition position - freeze for the foreseeable future - lest they be outflanked.
Ford sets the affordability agenda and his rivals flatter the premier by aping him. Students approve, parents applaud and politicians play along to harvest every vote.
In their shared political playbook, post-secondary populism can't be beat. Too bad it leaves Ontario's colleges and universities holding the bag.
You don't need a PhD in math to figure out that someone pays a price for a freeze and a free ride. The problem with pocketbook populism is that it's a shell game, not a serious strategy.
Ford understands that. Just look at his growing lead in the latest public opinion polls, where voters are rewarding him for his generosity and grandiosity.
Refund licence plate fees! Banish more road tolls! Cut gas taxes!
Few complain because - here's the magic - our roads still get built even if the taxes and tolls aren't collected. That's because the funding is found, magically, from another budgetary stash of cash - albeit at the expense of something else.
Now consider the higher education equation: Ford cuts funding by 10 per cent, then freezes it every year thereafter - all the while refusing to make up the difference.
Then his Progressive Conservative government goads colleges and universities into recruiting foreign students with extraordinarily extortionary tuition rates to bolster their bottom line. Until the resulting chaos forced the federal government to clamp down on this costly free for all, cutting off campuses from their newest addiction.
After five years of policy bankruptcy, Ford's Tories landed on the Canadian tradition of a commission. They empowered and empanelled a blue-ribbon panel, presumably to provide political cover to do what must one day - some day - be done.
Predictably, the panel urged the government to raise tuition rates amid rising inflation and/or bolster government funding to make up the difference. This week, the Ford government decided to ignore its own experts, proclaiming that tuition shall remain in the deep freeze now and for years to come.
"I will not apologize for continuing to freeze tuition," Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop proclaimed, echoing the premier's impromptu talking points earlier this month.
This is not the time, the Tories say, for a tuition increase. If not now in 2024, neither was it time in 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022 or 2023; nor will be 2025 or 2026 - you get the idea.
Which raises a reasonable question on tuition: Is there ever a good time for a politician to allow for an increase, if only to keep up with inflation?
Answer: Former NDP premier Bob Rae (later a Liberal) headed a similar tuition panel appointed by the Liberal premier of the day, Dalton McGuinty, two decades ago. It concluded that students so clearly benefit from the public investment in their education that they should also contribute their fair share - with the caveat that student aid should remove barriers to accessibility.
The last Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne built on that principle by boosting student aid to the point that those in need would effectively be spared out of pocket tuition, with more generous grants in lieu of loans. The Tories trashed that formula in favour of lower tuition for all - rich and poor - at the expense of campus operating budgets.
This week, Dunlop 's ministry had fun with figures by coughing up - and then dressing up - roughly $1 billion in one-time funding, stretched over three years, spread across so many areas that it amounts to a rounding error and a strategic error.
It allocates barely $700 million - or roughly $233 million a year - to be divvied up among colleges and universities in a post-secondary system with 47 campuses. That's a fraction of the nearly $2.5 billion funding infusion recommended by the government's own expert panel, which also called for a five per cent tuition increase that the Tories are ignoring.
This stingy strategy comes mere weeks after campuses discovered that the cash cow foisted upon them by the Ford government - high foreign student fees - can no longer be milked so murkily.
Are the premier and his Progressive Conservatives hostile to higher education? Or merely fixated on the bottom line?
In truth, their policy is neither idiocy nor ideology, merely expediency. There are no votes to be had in higher tuition for higher education, and there are no votes to be lost in starving the system of proper funding to keep it afloat (which is why the previous Liberal governments of Wynne and McGuinty also froze the base operating grants to colleges and universities year after year).
A government that dares to shortchange health care will hear about it from doctors and nurses, patients and their families; a politician who squeezes primary or secondary schools will face protests from teachers and parents.
But colleges and universities are the sacrificial lambs of Ontario politics: Underfunded, frozen and fleeced by all parties all the time.
(Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at Toronto Metropolitan University and at the University of Toronto's Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy.)
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks in the Ontario legislature on Feb. 26, 2024.

Subject: Higher education; Tuition; Colleges & universities; Voters; Politics; Funding; College campuses; Populism

Document 32 of 252

Trudeau government to unveil national pharmacare bill today

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-unveil-national-pharmacare/docview/2932835807/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+to+unveil+national+pharmacare+bill+today&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The Liberal government's plan to kick-start a national pharmacare program is set to be unveiled today, ending months of negotiations with the NDP over whether the proposed system would meet the New Democrats' demands and maintain the parliamentary alliance between the two parties.
Health Minister Mark Holland will introduce long-awaited legislation outlining a framework for a prescription drug coverage program, as is required under the governing pact that sees the NDP prop up Justin Trudeau's minority Liberal government.
In December, after the parties failed to agree on what form such a program should take, the NDP extended its end-of-year deadline to pass the bill. The New Democrats have insisted that the program should be a publicly delivered, single-payer system, something the cash-strapped Liberal government has been wary of supporting.
That extension, which expires Friday, came with strings attached: the Liberals needed to deliver more than just a piece of legislation sketching out a vision for a universal program, the NDP said.
The extra negotiations resulted in a deal reached last week that would see diabetes drugs and some related medical equipment, along with prescription birth control, covered across the country - if provinces and territories opt in.
That buy-in got off to a rocky start when Alberta Health Minister Adriana LaGrange rejected news of the yet-to-be-released plan this week, arguing that the province had not been consulted. Quebec has also signalled it wants Ottawa to stay out of the province's own drug coverage system.
"Alberta, like every province, should probably wait and see what the legislation is and what the scheme is before they make a decision," NDP health critic Don Davies told the Star on Wednesday.
"I think it's absurd that anybody would reject out of hand a proposal that they don't understand or haven't seen yet."
NDP deputy leader Alexandre Boulerice said it will be up to provincial governments to explain to their citizens why they prematurely opted out.
"I'm really sad today and surprised they are trying to close the door," Boulerice said. "They will see what is the real offer on the table. It's going to be really hard to say no."
Holland suggested Tuesday that provinces did have a hand in shaping his government's thinking.
"As I've had conversations with my provincial and territorial counterparts, I've been taking their feedback, their concerns, their issues, sort of holding them in my mind as I'm having conversations with other parties and with the New Democrats," he told reporters.
"Certainly now, I can come back to them and say here's some things that you expressed concern about, here are some things that you wanted to see if we were to move forward on foundational pharmacare legislation, and potentially some other actions, to demonstrate that that process of listening to them was included in the negotiations."
Thursday's bill is expected to outline more details about what types of drugs and devices the federal government will cover within the two agreed-upon classes.
Sources have previously told the Star that several types of insulin, plus birth control options like pills, intrauterine devices, injections and emergency contraception, will fall under the proposed plan.
The legislation is also expected to reference recommendations urging Canada to adopt a single-payer system, which the Parliamentary Budget Officer estimated would cost federal and provincial governments $11.2 billion in 2024-25, and up to $13.4 billion in 2027-28. The PBO also found that cost savings on drug expenditures would reach $2.2 billion by 2027-28.
It remains unclear, however, just how strongly the bill will commit to implementing a single-payer model.
"The NDP has been simply unceasing in our insistence that we build a system that constructs a single-payer formulary and a formula for pharmacare," Davies said.
"And I can tell you that the legislation does that."
Eric Hoskins, a former Ontario health minister who was also involved in the discussions, told the Star it's not surprising that provinces would need time to jump aboard.
"With medicare in the late 60s, it took about five or six years before all the provinces and territories had signed on," said Hoskins, who led a pharmacare advisory council convened by the federal government. It issued a 2019 report recommending that a public, universal, single-payer system replace Canada's existing patchwork of private and public plans.
"When we wrote our report, and we looked at this issue, like how to implement it ... and also respect provincial-territorial jurisdiction and health care, we also came to a similar recommendation that it be an opt in," Hoskins said.
As for whether the potentially languid pace of provinces signing on would compromise Canada's plans for buying a large volume of drugs in order to lower costs, Hoskins said it would be worth the wait.
"We may not get the maximum benefit from bulk purchasing at the beginning," he said.
"But over time, if it means that a few years from now, at least on these two drug classes, if it takes a few years for all the provinces and territories to come on board ... that's a really short period of time compared to how long we've been waiting to get around to this."
CAPTION: Health Minister Mark Holland speaks with reporters in the foyer of the House of Commons on Feb. 28, 2024.

Document 33 of 252

Ford taints the judicial process

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-taints-judicial-process/docview/2932382057/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+taints+the+judicial+process&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the things about Doug Ford that makes him different from a lot of politicians is that he generally says what he thinks - both for better and for worse.
That quality was on full display when the premier was asked last Friday whether he was OK with naming two of his former staffers to a committee that helps to select new judges, a move criticized as outright patronage.
Another politician might have fuzzed up his answer, given the importance of maintaining both the appearance and the reality of judicial independence, public confidence in the justice system, stuff like that.
Not Ford.
"One hundred per cent I am," he said proudly.
"We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments ... I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal. I've made it very clear where I stand with judges ... The justices of the peace and judges, they're letting criminals out ... I'm putting like-minded people that believe in what we believe in, keeping the bad guys in jail."
The premier's office made an attempt to deny the obvious - that the premier thinks it's legitimate for his government to arrange things so we get more judges and JPs who think like him, who won't be "letting the criminals out," who are "tough on crime" and so on.
It claimed he was just talking about people named to the selection committee, not the judges themselves. Until Monday, when Ford tossed away that fig leaf and said directly: "I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges."
So now there's no doubt where the premier stands, and it's not good.
The Advocates Society, a national legal organization, calls his comments a "substantial threat to the independence of Ontario's judges." The Federation of Ontario Law Associations says they undermine "public confidence in the administration of justice in a dangerous and anti-democratic way." And they reflect a "juvenile understanding of the role of an independent judiciary."
Now, there's a school of thought that maintains this is much ado about not much, that governments from time immemorial have named judges loyal to the party in power and in tune with their thinking. It's only an issue now, goes this line, because it's conservatives who are pushing their ideology. You can read that version in, for example, the Toronto Sun.
The trouble with this argument is that it ignores a decades-long attempt in Ontario - under Liberal, NDP and Progressive Conservative governments - to get away from that discredited old tradition of stuffing the judiciary with party hacks more noted for their partisan loyalty than their legal acumen.
A little bit of history: back in 1988 the Liberal government of David Peterson (now, full disclosure, chair of Torstar) set up a pilot project to develop a non-partisan model for appointing provincial judges.
It created the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), which was eventually made permanent by Bob Rae's NDP government in 1995. That committee, expanded over the years to include representatives of legal associations and the general public, was maintained by the Conservative and Liberal governments that followed. It was universally hailed as the "gold standard" in Canada for making sure judges were named for their professional, not their political, qualifications.
Until that is, the Ford government was elected in 2018. Almost immediately it started talking about wanting more "like-minded" judges and in 2021, Attorney General Doug Downey put through changes to the way the JAAC operates to make it easier for the government to get its own choices named to the bench.
The premier's latest comments are another big step along that road. His government clearly isn't content to name judges and JPs who simply apply the law. Based on Ford's own comments it wants them to rule not just on the facts and the law but on some preconceived notion of being tough on crime.
Lawyers are right to raise the alarm, but they're not the only ones who should be concerned. Ontario has long had an independent, non-political judiciary - or at least the best version of that in Canada. The last thing we need is to taint that system with politics, patronage and outright bias.
Andrew Phillips is a staff columnist with Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION Andrew Phillips is a staff columnist with Torstar.;

Document 34 of 252

'Complete garbage': Trudeau's immigration minister dismisses Ford government claim it was in the dark about international student cap

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/complete-garbage-trudeaus-immigration-minister/docview/2932568838/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Complete+garbage%27%3A+Trudeau%27s+immigration+minister+dismisses+Ford+government+claim+it+was+in+the+dark+about+international+student+cap&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Claims Ontario was blindsided by a cap on international student permits are "complete garbage," federal Immigration Minister Marc Miller said Tuesday as he threatened to go as far as shutting down institutions abusing the program if provinces don't pull their weight.
The latest round of finger-pointing over management of pressures on the immigration system comes a day after Ontario's universities' minister Jill Dunlop said the cap was imposed "without consultation and without warning, and without regard to the impact it would have on students and their families."
Miller scoffed, noting rounds of meetings between federal and provincial officials, and public statements from him both flagging issues with the program and coming changes.
He also pointed to the province's own auditor general's reports about the post-secondary sector's high reliance on international students, and Ontario also knowing about the high numbers of people with student permits making claims for asylum.
Considering all that, Dunlop's claim was completely false, and rubbish, he said in French, and then in English, "complete garbage."
"It's beneath me to share text messages with journalists, but the reality is that there was communication, it just was never followed up on," he told reporters on his way into the federal government's weekly cabinet meeting.
Dunlop's office did not return a request for comment from the Star.
The spat between Miller and Premier Doug Ford's government is the latest in rising tensions between different levels of government on the state of Canada's immigration system, not just for students but for those also coming to Canada in record numbers to seek asylum.
The cost of assisting refugee claimants is another sore spot between Parliament Hill and Queen's Park - as well as Toronto city hall - and the tension around international students follows a tense round of talks on the refugee question last month.
The financial risks facing Ontario's post-secondary sector were already well documented ahead of the imposition of the cap. On Monday, the Ford government announced a three-year, $1-billion funding boost for the province's colleges and universities, a pledge linked to a government-commissioned report last year on the financial sustainability of the sector.
The report also repeatedly raised the issue of how much schools depend on international student tuition - which is higher than domestic students - to remain afloat, a risk also raised by auditor general Bonnie Lysyk in 2022.
Dunlop, however, repeatedly dodged questions about how the province will now also make up for the coming shortfall of revenue with the cut to international student permits.
In January, Miller had announced that overall, Ottawa is cutting student permit applications by 35 per cent, with individual caps established for each province and territory.
For Ontario, that cap could see permits slashed by 50 per cent as more international students flock to the province than anywhere else.
The reduction in permits flowed from numerous issues being flagged with the student permit program.
Among them, "diploma mills" giving people ways to get a study permit to get into Canada, but never making them attend a single class, fraud rings exploiting students, and growing numbers of stories about inadequate housing and social support shortages for newcomer students, leaving some dependent on social housing and food banks to get by, have also attracted attention.
That situation was exacerbated by high inflation rates and the current housing crunch nationwide, raising political concerns that Canada's open arms stance to newcomers was being undermined by a system failing to support them; data from Abacus suggests immigration is rising as an issue in the minds of voters.
While the federal government ultimately makes the call on who is allowed into Canada, it has never before capped international student permits as long as the applicant could show they'd been accepted by an approved school. Still, some 40 per cent of applications are rejected annually.
What more Ottawa could do is under consideration, Miller said Tuesday.
A new model for how schools are recognized to accept international students could help, and if it doesn't, Ottawa could step in he said - though there are jurisdictional questions to consider.
"We will be able to separate the wheat from the chaff and perhaps even if, if provinces don't assume their responsibility, shut down institutions ourselves if they don't do a good enough job," he said.
CAPTION: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Minister Marc Miller arrives to a cabinet meeting on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Feb. 27, 2024.

Document 35 of 252

Where will MPPs go when Queen's Park closes for repairs?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-will-mpps-go-when-queens-park-closes/docview/2932567958/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+will+MPPs+go+when+Queen%27s+Park+closes+for+repairs%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's the $1-billion-renovation question and only a handful of people know the answer.
Where will the Ontario government operate from once the provincial legislature is closed for restoration the year after next?
"Not for announcement today," Legislative Affairs Minister Paul Calandra told reporters Tuesday at Queen's Park.
"We've been very clear that we are moving forward with a decant of the building so that we can begin to restore and deal with the health and safety issues that are inherent across the building," said Calandra.
Affectionately known as "the pink palace," the Legislative Assembly building opened in 1893 and is showing its age.
The ancient heating and cooling systems need replacing as does the plumbing - the tap water is unsafe to drink due to lead pipes - and there is asbestos insulation and exposed wiring throughout the building.
That's why the plan is for MPPs to soon be gathering to conduct the province's business in a new temporary chamber in an as-yet-undisclosed location near the existing legislature.
Calandra, noting an all-party committee is studying the move, said the aim is to have the new locale in place after the June 2026 election.
"That is where we are going for sure," the minister said, adding the legislative assembly staff had looked into whether it was possible to remain at Queen's Park during renovations that will take years to complete.
"Because of the way the systems are connected here, you can't do one half of the building and renovate it and do another half of the building," he said.
"So the only way you can accomplish this is with a full decant of the building."
MPPs on the committee examining the move confirmed the members have been touring sites, including a vacant Bay Street office building two weeks ago, but said they are sworn to secrecy due to commercial sensitivities.
All discussions about potential locations are held behind closed doors with no media present and no public transcripts.
Sources told the Star that 500,000-square-feet of space is being sought so the temporary lodgings would have the same number of offices as the existing legislature.
That is key because Premier Doug Ford, Official Opposition Leader Marit Stiles, Speaker Ted Arnott, dozens of MPPs, scores of journalists in the Queen's Park press gallery as well as hundreds of political aides, librarians and legislative staffers currently work from Queen's Park offices.
The Star - like other major media outlets such as CBC, the Globe and Mail, The Canadian Press, the Toronto Sun, CityNews, Global, Radio-Canada and CTV - has a four-person bureau in the legislative building.
But Calandra warned the legislative committee last November it may not be possible to have the 5,000-square-foot legislative chambers, where MPPs debate, in the same location as offices, committee rooms, caucus rooms, the library, Hansard and translation services and cafeterias.
The minister also cautioned about soaring bills.
"In the Netherlands, the cost of renovations has doubled as a result of the war in Ukraine," Calandra, who has been looking at similar restoration projects around the world, said at the time.
Last summer, the Ministry of Legislative Affairs put out a request for proposals for an interim legislature that could be used for a minimum of six years.
Sixteen bids were submitted and that has been narrowed down to short list of about four undisclosed options.
Security - and secure parking for elected officials and staff - is a major concern. Currently, visitors to Queen's Park must go through airport-style security screening with bags X-rayed before being allowed in.
Such measures mean any shared tenancy with another organization or business has effectively been ruled out.
Another MPP said the panel is looking at the four-tower Macdonald Block complex across the street from the legislature, which is owned by the government.
That sprawling space, at the southwest corner of Bay and Wellesley Streets is in the midst of its own $1.5-billion renovation that, as the Star revealed last October, will not be completed on schedule or on budget.
It was supposed to be finished this spring, but the COVID-19 pandemic and elevator engineering headaches have kept ministers, political staff and thousands of civil servants in leased office space throughout downtown Toronto.
The Macdonald Block work, which was first announced eight years ago, began in 2019 and there is no completion date in sight.
Challenges to updating the brutalist Macdonald Block, which only dates back to the 1960s, have officials well aware that 131-year-old legislature could be even more daunting.
Officials at Queen's Park are also mindful of complexities of the $5-billion, decade-long renovation of Centre Block on Parliament Hill that is supposed to be finished in either 2030 or 2031.
CAPTION: MPPs are shown in the Ontario Legislature on Sept. 17, 2018.

Document 36 of 252

Doug Ford wants 'like-minded' judges. Here's how to understand the controversy rocking Ontario courts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-like-minded-judges-heres-how/docview/2932167587/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+%27like-minded%27+judges.+Here%27s+how+to+understand+the+controversy+rocking+Ontario+courts&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's comments this week that he wants to appoint "like-minded judges" to the Ontario Court of Justice has thrown the province's judicial appointment process under the spotlight, with critics warning his words represent a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts.
Ford's comments were in reaction to a Star exclusive on Thursday that his government has appointed two of his ex-senior staffers to the committee that recommends Ontario judges.
Ford's former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy has been the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC) since Feb. 1, while his former director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has been a public member of the committee since December.
Here's what you need to know to understand the controversy:
Who is eligible to become a provincial court judge?The eligibility criteria are simple and straightforward: you must be a lawyer who has been a member of the bar for at least 10 years, and who promises to follow any courses as mandated by the chief justice of the court.
What does the Ontario Court of Justice do?The Ontario Court of Justice handles the bulk of the province's criminal caseload, as well as a large number of family matters. It has courthouses across the province.
Who handles lawyers' applications to become judges? The Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC) was created in the 1980s as an arm's-length, non-partisan body that vets applications for the bench and makes recommendations on possible judges to the Attorney General of Ontario.
Who gets to sit on the appointments committee? The JAAC is made up of 13 members: two provincial court judges appointed by the Ontario Court chief justice, and a third judge appointed by the Ontario Judicial Council. The Attorney General appoints seven members of the public. And three legal organizations - the Law Society of Ontario, the Federation of Ontario Law Associations, and the Ontario Bar Association - each have a lawyer representative on the committee. As public members, Bondy and Vandrick were both directly appointed by Attorney General Doug Downey.
But it's the attorney general who appoints the judges? Yes, Downey must make his selection from a ranked shortlist of candidates presented to him by the JAAC. He then takes his selections to cabinet for final approval.
What has changed under the Ford government? Downey made significant changes to the JAAC in 2021, sparking the ire of legal groups who said they were unnecessary and would risk opening the door to patronage appointments to the bench.
Downey now has the power to appoint almost all of the JAAC's members. He took away the ability of the three legal organizations to appoint their own representative; they must now each submit a shortlist of three candidates to Downey for his selection.
Downey also increased the number of judicial candidates that appear on the ranked shortlist submitted to him by the JAAC. Previously, the shortlist contained just two names, which the attorney general of the day could send back and request the next two. Downey increased the number to six, and is still permitted to send the list back and ask for the next six.
Advocates warned that by increasing the number of candidates on the shortlist, the provincial government would have greater access to the entire application pool, running the risk that lower-quality candidates get appointed.
On Monday, Downey's spokesperson told the Star the appointments process "is separate from the total independence judges have from government once they are appointed to their positions."
How is this different than what happens with federal appointments?The federal government is responsible for appointing judges to superior courts and courts of appeal across the country. Recommendations on judicial candidates to the federal justice minister are made by similar advisory committees that exist in every province and territory.
Those seven-person committees are chaired by a judge appointed by the chief justice of the province, and also include a representative of the province's law society, a representative of the province's attorney general, a representative of the Canadian Bar Association, and three representatives appointed by the federal government.
The federal Liberals have faced criticism over the perception they have favoured party donors in their appointments.
According to data reported by the National Post last year, 81 per cent of the approximately 1,300 judicial and tribunal appointments made by the federal government since 2016 had not donated to any party.
But of the remaining number who did donate to a party - 240 individuals - 76 per cent had donated to the Liberals at least once in the decade leading up to their appointment.
Why is Ford making these changes?The premier has been clear that he wants judges to keep people in jail for longer. His long-standing talking point on the justice system is that far too often, judges and justices of the peace in Ontario release accused violent criminals on bail pending their trials, who then go on to reoffend.
"Every single appointment I can, to find tough judges, tough (justices of the peace) to keep guys in jail ... I'm going to do it," Ford said on Friday.
There is a lack of data nationwide regarding bail, something the federal government acknowledged last year when it nonetheless implemented changes to make it more difficult for people accused of certain crimes to get bail.
Who's upset, and why?Legal organizations have expressed grave concerns that Ford's comments impact the independence of the courts and will harm the public's confidence in the justice system. They have also argued his comments could lead people to question the integrity of judges appointed by the Ford government going forward.
Two major legal organizations, the Advocates' Society and the Criminal Lawyers' Association, communicated those concerns directly to Ford in separate letters Monday, copying Downey.
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations, which has a representative on the JAAC, has said that Ford's comments "reflect a juvenile understanding" of the independence of the courts. The comments also suggest "that appointed judges were politically biased and take partisan positions that parallel Doug Ford's policies," the federation said.
CAPTION: Attorney General Doug Downey speaks in the house on Monday.Premier Doug Ford answers questions in Queen's Park on Monday.

Document 37 of 252

The data machine behind your grocery list: How loyalty programs track habits, predict next purchases

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/data-machine-behind-your-grocery-list/docview/2931931266/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+data+machine+behind+your+grocery+list%3A+How+loyalty+programs+track+habits%2C+predict+next+purchases&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Diana Skakavac scrolls grocery store loyalty apps, she's always looking for her "unicorn": personalized offers for Tide laundry powder.
"My grandma is a very strict user of the Tide powder. She always has me purchase that for her," said the Toronto woman behind the @HaveCouponsWillTravel account on Instagram.
But no matter how often Skakavac picks up Tide powder, the grocers never seem to reward her for the behaviour.
"My friend and I used to joke about that. She got one (Tide offer) like two or three years ago and she held onto that thing for dear life because for some reason, they never come around."
Why Skakavac never scores Tide discounts lies in the mysterious nature of grocer loyalty programs, which pump out a plethora of personalized incentives every week designed to lure in shoppers.
Retailers tell customers their offers are determined by how they shop. That's true, but it's a bit more complicated than simply granting rewards based on past purchases.
"It's like a constant test-and-learn process," said Grant Packard, an associate professor of marketing at York University in Toronto, who has advised Loblaw Cos. Ltd., Indigo Books & Music Inc. and Cineplex Inc. on their loyalty programs.
The more people shop, the more the technology powering the apps learns - and it's not only about what you reach for at the supermarket. They learn how price sensitive you are, how likely you are to try something new or switch to a competitor brand and even whether you have moved in with a partner or family member or are expecting a baby.
"We can throw hundreds of variables into this black box and let it tell us what they're going to do next without a human having to look at something and try to figure it out," Packard said.
While technology and machine learning do much of the data gathering, humans are still involved in deciding what offers shoppers get.
Packard said companies take insights the technology has gathered and order systems to prioritize offers that will often influence three things: how often people shop, how much they buy and their willingness to try new products.
"They could lure you to the store sooner than you would have gone, saying you can only have this promotion if you come by the end of Thursday ... or they can influence your basket size, saying that you only get this (deal) if you buy this," said Ceren Kolsarici, an associate professor of marketing and analytics at Queen's University in Kingston, Ont.
Instead of the Tide offers she covets, Skakavac occasionally gets promotions for laundry detergent from No Name, Loblaw's house brand. (She figures she doesn't get Tide offers because manufacturer Procter& Gamble focuses its promotional efforts elsewhere.)
How many offers shoppers get can vary. There is "no hard and fast number" of offers Loblaw gives individuals every week because how often and at how many of the company's banners people shop at varies, said Ryan Markle, senior vice-president of loyalty at Loblaw, which runs the PC Optimum program.
At Metro Inc., which runs the Moi program in some provinces, Alain Tadros said, "I wouldn't want to specify for competitive reasons on the number of offers ... but it varies from six to 10 offers per week per banner."
However, the vice-president and chief marketing officer said Moi's goal is not to change behaviour but to retain customers.
"Rewarding you for the stuff that you buy - not that you might buy - will actually make you a more loyal customer," Tadros said.
Eventually, apps might get an even better read on customers.
"The retail industry speaks a lot about personalization, but they're not at the level of personalization that we could be," Tadros said. "In the future, it's being able to even target specials and personalized pricing."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Though Diana Skakavac habitually buys Tide laundry detergent for herself and her grandmother, she says grocery chain loyalty programs never reward her for her consistent purchases. Instead, she says she occasionally gets promotions for detergent from a grocer's house brand.Chris YoungTHE CANADIANPRESS

CREDIT: Tara Deschamps The Canadian Press

Document 38 of 252

Educator Sylvia Kajiura an ‘incredible individual’ with a ‘great laugh’

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/educator-sylvia-kajiura-incredible-individual/docview/2931931261/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Educator+Sylvia+Kajiura+an+%26lsquo%3Bincredible+individual%26rsquo%3B+with+a+%26lsquo%3Bgreat+laugh%26rsquo%3B&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sylvia Kajiura led the way for Caribbean educators to teach at the Catholic board in Hamilton.
The St. Lucia native - who died Jan. 12 at age 91 - was the first teacher from the West Indies hired by the Hamilton Separate School Board in 1960.
The special education teacher - who later made a mark at many Hamilton agencies and boards - impressed the board with her personality and work so much that it began recruiting in the West Indies for teachers to come work in Hamilton in 1965. Over the next four years, the board hired 35 teachers from Caribbean countries.
Kajiura told The Spec in 2002 she didn't know until that year she was a pioneer and found it a "real surprise." She recalled, however, when she first arrived in Hamilton, its Black community was tiny and the city was still a white, European community.
Kajiura made some other firsts. She was the first Black and married woman to be appointed a principal by the Hamilton-Wentworth Roman Catholic Separate School Board in 1975, and the first chair of the Hamilton-Wentworth Council on Police, Race and Community Relations.
She was one of the first Black Hamiltonians to serve on the Hamilton-Wentworth Police Services Board. She was appointed in 1994 by the NDP government, replacing Black poverty activist Sharon Laffrenier, who had been appointed in 1991. Kajiura served two years.
"She was thoughtful," recalled former regional chair Terry Cooke, who sat on the board with Kajiura. "She was a calming presence on the police board."
She served on the boards of the Children's Aid Society of Hamilton-Wentworth, United Way, Caribbean Cultural Association, YWCA Hamilton, National YWCA of Canada, Hamilton Community Foundation and the Hamilton-Wentworth Grants Review Team of the Ontario Trillium Foundation. She also served on police chief Ken Robertson's citizen's advisory committee.
A 2000 Spectator article described Kajiura as an elegant woman who had always been a forward thinker.
The YWCA said in a statement that Kajiura was a beloved figure who left behind a legacy of compassion, dedication and an unwavering commitment to make a positive impact.
"Sylvia's association with YWCA Hamilton was both profound and enduring," it said. "As a long-standing supporter and former board member, she played an instrumental role in shaping the organization's initiatives and advocacy efforts."
On social media, Kajiura was also remembered for her community contributions and her style. Catherine Giovinazzo called her "a woman of integrity" and Nella Cipriani said she "was a wonderful and kind person."
"As a young child, I remember being struck by her vibrancy and strength," said Sandra Fazakas. "She dazzled me with her beautiful outfits, commanding presence and great laugh."
Robin Bryce Mech said Kajiura made a difference in many people's lives and that she would "never forget her elegant glide of confidence that she always presented with."
Heather Cozens called her "an incredible individual and such an inspiration."
Kajiura was born June 26, 1932 in St. Lucia to Alfred and Cecilie Archibald. She began teaching when she was 17 in 1949. She came to Canada in 1957 and first taught in St. Catharines. After she came to Hamilton, she got her teaching diploma, a BA from McMaster University and a master's degree of education from the University of Toronto.
She was appointed principal of St. Joseph's Catholic Public School in 1976 and retired there in 1994. In 1985, she piloted one of three system-wide French immersion centres at St. Eugene School.
In 2009, she received a lifetime achievement award at the 25th annual Caribbean Potpourri for her work helping young people. In 2016, she was nominated for a Women of Distinction Award in the Community Leadership category. She served as president of the local Retired Teachers Organization between 2003 and 2004.
Kajiura is survived by her husband Benjamin, children James and Pauline, and six siblings. She was predeceased by four siblings.
dannolanwrites@gmail.com

Document 39 of 252

Ontario leads jump in number of EI recipients: Rising unemployment drives 14.8 per cent increase in income support given out across Canada in '23

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-leads-jump-number-ei-recipients/docview/2931929750/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+leads+jump+in+number+of+EI+recipients%3A+Rising+unemployment+drives+14.8+per+cent+increase+in+income+support+given+out+across+Canada+in+%2723&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The number of Canadians receiving Employment Insurance (EI) benefits soared in 2023 amid increasing unemployment rates, according to recent data from Statistics Canada.
People in Ontario receiving EI - which provides temporary income support to unemployed workers - jumped by 34,000 in 2023 - a whopping 32 per cent increase, the highest of any province.
And despite claims from a new taxpayer-funded advertising campaign by Premier Doug Ford's government that Ontario is "a place where more people are going to work than ever before," more than 140,000 Ontarians were on EI benefits in December, according to Statistics Canada, compared with approximately 106,000 people a year before who were receiving income support. Across Canada, EI recipients rose 14.8 per cent in 2023.
"This is the result of higher unemployment over the course of 2023," said David Macdonald, a senior economist with the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives. "As a result, you see more people applying for and getting EI."
While the unemployment rate fell 0.1 per cent in January, the overall labour market has been losing steam as the economy slowed in 2023 under pressure of high interest rates, with the unemployment rate climbing 0.8 percentage points from April to November, according to Statistics Canada.
At the same time, population growth driven by immigration has also contributed to the rising unemployment rate. From 2022 to 2023, Canada took in some 1.13 million immigrants, the highest such figure on record, and almost half a million more than the previous year.
Douglas Porter, chief economist at BMO, pointed out that in Ontario, the increase in EI recipients grew even faster than the unemployment rate. In Ontario, the unemployment rate was 6.3 per cent in December according to Statistics Canada, up from 5.3 per cent the year prior.
Porter and Macdonald said EI applicants outpacing unemployment could indicate who is currently unemployed or losing their jobs.
For example, to be eligible for EI benefits, applicants must have worked between 420 and 700 insurable employment hours during the qualifying period.
"You need a fair amount of hours to get into the system," Macdonald said. "This may be a reflection of longer-employed people or more securely employed people losing work over the course of 2023 and therefore getting into the system."
More than 300,000 full-time jobs in Ontario have disappeared since July, according to data from Statistics Canada. Meanwhile, part-time jobs are on the rise.
In April, Canada's economy added 41,000 new jobs mostly in part-time work and in January, the slight uptick in employment was largely driven by an increase in part-time work, Statistics Canada said.
Macdonald said the number of Canadians seeking EI benefits will likely continue to increase.
The Royal Bank of Canada in a report from May said that the unemployment rate is projected to climb to 6.6 per cent by early 2024.
"I don't think there's any particular slowing in the rise of the unemployment rate," he said.
"As a result you're going to see more people seeking employment insurance benefits as they lose their jobs."
