Document 40 of 252

Doug Ford's call for 'like-minded' people picking judges is 'substantial threat' to Ontario courts, lawyers tell him

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-call-like-minded-people-picking-judges/docview/2931758353/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+call+for+%27like-minded%27+people+picking+judges+is+%27substantial+threat%27+to+Ontario+courts%2C+lawyers+tell+him&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's comments last week that he wants "like-minded" people picking Ontario judges pose a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts and the public's confidence in the justice system, a national legal organization told Ford in a letter Monday.
Ford made the comments responding on Friday to a Star exclusive that two of his ex-senior staffers were appointed by his government to the arm's-length committee that vets and recommends judges for the Ontario Court of Justice.
His former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy is now the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), while his former director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has also been a public member of the 13-person committee since December.
Ford said he was "100 per cent" OK with the pair being on the committee, a move denounced by critics as patronage that risks tainting what is supposed to be an independent process.
"We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments ... I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal," Ford said.
He later added: "Every single appointment I can, to find tough judges, tough (justices of the peace) to keep guys in jail ... I'm going to do it."
While Ford's office later clarified that he was talking about wanting "like-minded" people selecting judges - and not like-minded judges - critics and advocacy groups were unconvinced.
The Advocates' Society, the national organization representing lawyers who appear in court, told Ford in a letter Monday that they had serious concerns with what he said.
"Your comments - and the approach to judicial appointments that your comments convey - pose a substantial threat to the independence of Ontario's judges from the government and to public confidence in the administration of justice in this province," society president Dominique Hussey told Ford, cc'ing his attorney general, Doug Downey.
Hussey said that judges must be qualified, unbiased, impartial, and reflective of Canadian society, and appointed through a "robust and transparent" non-partisan appointment process.
"The fact that a candidate holds the same beliefs as the government of the day, or is a member of the party in power, should never factor in their selection as a judge," Hussey wrote.
"The role of a judge is to apply the law as it stands to the facts of the case before them as they find them to be, without fear or favour; it is not to implement wider government policies, such as being 'tough' or 'soft' on crime, by means of their judgments in individual cases."
To suggest otherwise, Hussey wrote, "undermines the judiciary's independence as the third, equal branch of government. Moreover, to suggest otherwise weakens public confidence in the independence of Ontario's judges and their ability to make fair, impartial decisions."
Downey's office has maintained that Bondy and Vandrick were appointed for their public policy experience and volunteer activities.
Putting them on the committee follows significant changes that Downey has made to the JAAC that were condemned by legal associations as unnecessary and opening the door for patronage appointments to the bench, with critics charging that the changes would water down a process that was once described as the gold standard for appointing judges.
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations, which has a representative on the JAAC, said last week that Ford's comments "reflect a juvenile understanding" of the independence of the courts.
They also suggest "that appointed judges were politically biased and take partisan positions that parallel Doug Ford's policies," the federation said. "All of this undermines public confidence in the administration of justice in a dangerous and anti-democratic way."
CAPTION: Matthew Bondy, left, Ontario Premier Doug Ford's former deputy chief of staff, and Brock Vandrick, right, his former director of stakeholder relations, were recently appointed to the committee that selects Ontario judges."We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments ... I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal," Doug Ford said on Friday.

Subject: Committees; Patronage; Appointments & personnel changes; Judicial appointments; Judges & magistrates

Business indexing term: Subject: Appointments & personnel changes

Title: Doug Ford's call for 'like-minded' people picking judges is 'substantial threat' to Ontario courts, lawyers tell him

Document 41 of 252

Educator Sylvia Kajiura was an 'incredible individual' with a 'great laugh'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/educator-sylvia-kajiura-was-incredible-individual/docview/2931758256/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Educator+Sylvia+Kajiura+was+an+%27incredible+individual%27+with+a+%27great+laugh%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sylvia Kajiura led the way for Caribbean educators to teach at the Catholic board in Hamilton.
The St. Lucia native - who died Jan. 12 at age 91 - was the first teacher from the West Indies hired by the Hamilton Separate School Board in 1960.
The special education teacher - who later made a mark at many Hamilton agencies and boards - impressed the board with her personality and work so much that it began recruiting in the West Indies for teachers to come work in Hamilton in 1965. Over the next four years, the board hired 35 teachers from Caribbean countries.
Kajiura told The Spec in 2002 she didn't know until that year she was a pioneer and found it a "real surprise." She recalled, however, when she first arrived in Hamilton, its Black community was tiny and the city was still a white, European community.
Kajiura made some other firsts. She was the first Black and married woman to be appointed a principal by the Hamilton-Wentworth Roman Catholic Separate School Board in 1975, and the first chair of the Hamilton-Wentworth Council on Police, Race and Community Relations.
She was one of the first Black Hamiltonians to serve on the Hamilton-Wentworth Police Services Board. She was appointed in 1994 by the NDP government, replacing Black poverty activist Sharon Laffrenier, who had been appointed in 1991. Kajiura served two years.
"She was thoughtful," recalled former regional chair Terry Cooke, who sat on the board with Kajiura. "She was a calming presence on the police board."
She served on the boards of the Children's Aid Society of Hamilton-Wentworth, United Way, Caribbean Cultural Association, YWCA Hamilton, National YWCA of Canada, Hamilton Community Foundation and the Hamilton-Wentworth Grants Review Team of the Ontario Trillium Foundation. She also served on police chief Ken Robertson's citizen's advisory committee.
A 2000 Spectator article described Kajiura as an elegant woman who had always been a forward thinker.
The YWCA said in a statement that Kajiura was a beloved figure who left behind a legacy of compassion, dedication and an unwavering commitment to make a positive impact.
"Sylvia's association with YWCA Hamilton was both profound and enduring," it said. "As a long-standing supporter and former board member, she played an instrumental role in shaping the organization's initiatives and advocacy efforts."
On social media, Kajiura was also remembered for her community contributions and her style. Catherine Giovinazzo called her "a woman of integrity" and Nella Cipriani said she "was a wonderful and kind person."
"As a young child, I remember being struck by her vibrancy and strength," said Sandra Fazakas. "She dazzled me with her beautiful outfits, commanding presence and great laugh."
Robin Bryce Mech said Kajiura made a difference in many people's lives and that she would "never forget her elegant glide of confidence that she always presented with."
Heather Cozens called her "an incredible individual and such an inspiration."
Kajiura was born June 26, 1932 in St. Lucia to Alfred and Cecilie Archibald. She began teaching when she was 17 in 1949. She came to Canada in 1957 and first taught in St. Catharines. After she came to Hamilton, she got her teaching diploma, a BA from McMaster University and a master's degree of education from the University of Toronto.
She was appointed principal of St. Joseph's Catholic Public School in 1976 and retired there in 1994. In 1985, she piloted one of three system-wide French immersion centres at St. Eugene School.
In 2009, she received a lifetime achievement award at the 25th annual Caribbean Potpourri for her work helping young people. In 2016, she was nominated for a Women of Distinction Award in the Community Leadership category. She served as president of the local Retired Teachers Organization between 2003 and 2004.
Kajiura is survived by her husband Benjamin, children James and Pauline, and six siblings. She was predeceased by four siblings.
CAPTION: Sylvia Kajiura - who died Jan. 12 at age 91 - was the first teacher from the West Indies hired by the Hamilton Separate School Board in 1960.

Document 42 of 252

Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives ad spending far outpacing Justin Trudeau's Liberals, data shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-conservatives-ad-spending-far/docview/2931524221/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+Conservatives+ad+spending+far+outpacing+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals%2C+data+shows&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Flush with cash after a record-breaking fundraising year, Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives are vastly outspending Justin Trudeau's Liberals on advertising, Meta data shows.
But Liberal insiders say they have no plans to go on an advertising offensive until the country is closer to an election.
Poilievre and the Conservatives have spent $230,000 on advertising on Meta in the last 90 days, about four times more than Trudeau and the Liberals, who have spent $55,000 in the same time period. It's a pattern political marketing experts say reflects the two parties' approaches to advertising since Poilievre won the leadership in September 2022.
It could be paying off for the Conservatives, who have been surging in the polls since the summer. The launch of an expensive ad campaign to boost Poilievre's image has also helped the opposition leader to become more familiar to Canadians.
"There's been a successful effort to show Poilievre as a Canadian that understands local issues and represents their economic anxiety," said Cole Hogan, a digital strategist whose worked for Ontario Premier Doug Ford and former Alberta Premier Jason Kenney. "The Conservatives also have a lot more money to play with than the Liberals do."
The Tories raised $35 million in 2023, compared to about $15 million from the Liberals, according to Elections Canada filings.
Current Conservative ads mainly link Liberal policies to Canadians' struggles with affordability, through repeated attacks on carbon pricing, government spending under the Trudeau government, and the cost of housing.
There are also more targeted ads that argue Trudeau is promoting censorship, and attack gun control policies, vaccine mandates and the World Economic Forum. In one advertisement written in Chinese and English, the Tories accuse the federal government of "handing out taxpayer-funded narcotics that fuel crime, chaos, drugs and disorder in our streets," specifically targeting a safe supply program in Richmond, B.C.
At the same time, the Conservatives are still running clips from a multimillion-dollar summer ad campaign that was meant to soften Poilievre's image, including one advertisement where the Conservative leader completes a puzzle with his daughter as he argues that "everything feels broken in Canada." It's been viewed more than a million times since January, mostly in Alberta and the Atlantic Provinces, a Liberal stronghold where the Conservatives are leading in the polls.
The Conservative Party of Canada did not return a request for comment.
The Liberals, meanwhile, have directed their ad spending toward defending their record on housing and affordability, especially in Ontario and Quebec. They've also been relying on organic content - that is, regular social media content from the party and Liberal politicians - as well as attacks on Poilievre from ministers, which have escalated in recent months, said Dan Arnold, the former director of research and advertising for Trudeau.
Senior Liberals who spoke to the Star on background in order to frankly discuss strategy said they believe it would not be useful or effective to spend more money on advertising against Poilievre until the country is closer to a real vote. They said Trudeau and his ministers already draw, and will continue to highlight, contrasts between the two leaders, and underscore their differences, such as what they call lukewarm Conservative support for Ukraine, the party's lack of a climate action plan, its anti-vaccine-mandate stance, as well as Poilievre's more restrictive view of transgender rights.
"While we don't publicly discuss our advertising strategy, Pierre Poilievre and his Conservative party continue to sow fear and division in their communications and advertising to import far-right American-style politics here to Canada and push for deep cuts to the middle class," Liberal party spokesperson Parker Lund told the Star.
In the last week, Poilievre's comments on transgender kids drew ire from the Liberals, who accused him of putting vulnerable Canadians at risk. Poilievre also attacked Trudeau personally, calling the prime minister racist over the blackface scandal that surfaced in 2019.
It's moments like these that some experts say could hurt the Conservatives' efforts to soften Poilievre's image, said Éric Blais, a political marketing commentator. But for the Liberals, passively defending your record and relying on Poilievre to shoot himself in the foot is not a strategy voters will buy into, he said. What's needed, he added, is an immediate, more aggressive advertising push that aims to regain the trust of Canadians.
"It's hard, particularly when you've got ArriveCan and memories of WE Charity and memories of (SNC-Lavalin)," he said.
"Plus, an election is rarely about the past. It's about the future."
With files from Tonda MacCharles
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, right, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in the House of Commons on Oct. 3, 2023.

Document 43 of 252

Ford government announces details of billion-dollar boost to colleges and universities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-announces-details-billion-dollar/docview/2931522714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+announces+details+of+billion-dollar+boost+to+colleges+and+universities&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's colleges and universities are getting a billion-dollar funding boost from the Ford government over the next three years, which is also introducing legislation that would require schools to publish all fees and even textbook prices so students have a clear idea of their costs.
The province will also freeze tuition fees for the next three years.
Post-secondary Minister Jill Dunlop will make the announcement Monday afternoon at Queen's Park. Premier Doug Ford said Friday it would be "fabulous" news.
Details of the new money, as first reported in the Star, include $903 million over three years, plus additional funds for student mental health and safety.
Some $200 million of the $903 million will be targeted at schools with the greatest need.
However, the funds are about half of what colleges and universities expected, based on recommendations from an expert panel set up by the province.
A year after Ford was first elected premier, he slashed tuition by 10 per cent and has frozen fees since.
That, combined with stagnant funding, inflation, unexpected COVID costs as well as salary increases due to now-unconstitutional provincial legislation, has left almost half of the province's universities running deficits and warning of cuts to stay afloat.
On top of that, the schools are now facing the loss of some lucrative international student tuition revenue as the federal government cuts their numbers for this fall - a move that will disproportionately hit Ontario give the explosion in foreign students studying here in recent years - blaming a housing shortage and questions about the quality of some of the programs run by public and private college partnerships.
Last year, Dunlop asked a panel of experts to look at the sustainability of the post-secondary sector, and they urged an immediate 10 per cent boost in government funding, with future increases set at a minimum of two per cent a year, as well as an immediate tuition bump of five per cent this fall.
In total, its recommendations came with an estimated price tag of $2.5 billion over three years for the province's 24 publicly assisted colleges and 23 universities.
The province has not implemented the panel's recommendations, but says it is still looking at them.
One secondary leader called the funding level the province is proposing "dangerous" for the sector.
CAPTION: Premier Doug at Toronto City Hall on Feb. 22, 2024.

Document 44 of 252

Doug Ford's Tories are surging at the expense of Marit Stiles' New Democrats, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-are-surging-at-expense-marit/docview/2931522647/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+are+surging+at+the+expense+of+Marit+Stiles%27+New+Democrats%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are riding high at the apparent expense of Marit Stiles' New Democrats, a new poll suggests.
The latest Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star had the Tories up three points to 41 per cent while the NDP had dropped four points to 19 per cent.
Bonnie Crombie's Liberals remain mired in second place at 27 per cent, unchanged from last month's survey, and Mike Schreiner's Greens are up to eight per cent from five per cent in January.
"The greatest risk for the NDP and Marit Stiles is a strong Liberal party and resilient Greens," said Abacus president David Coletto.
"I think the NDP is still figuring out how to be relevant while also managing demons within its own party. The infighting within that party is likely to blame for the NDP drop," said Coletto.
That was a reference to internal tumult within the official opposition after rookie Hamilton Centre MPP Sarah Jama was defenestrated for repeated insubordination last October. She is now an Independent MPP.
There was also sobering news for the Liberals, who have not surged as much as they had hoped after Crombie, a popular former Mississauga mayor, won the leadership Dec. 2.
"It's a tough job being an opposition leader in Ontario, especially when you don't have official party status, nor a seat in the legislature," said Coletto.
The Liberals, who governed from 2003 until 2018, have nine seats in the 124-member house, three shy of recognized standing as a party in the chamber.
That means the Grits don't get much time in the legislature's question period and lack funding for staff and research - a challenge for Crombie, who is also being targeted by a multimillion-dollar PC attack ad blitz.
"She will need to start talking directly to voters through advertising to break through. Take a page from Pierre Poilievre's strategy," said Coletto, referring to the federal Conservative leader's ongoing promotional campaign.
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians from Feb. 15 until last Wednesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Encouraging as February's findings may be to the Tories, Coletto noted the undecided vote remains "worryingly high" for the governing party.
When asked for their "current provincial vote intention," 32 per cent of respondents said PC, 21 per cent Liberal, 15 per cent NDP, six per cent Green, four per cent another party while 22 per cent were undecided.
"High undecideds could definitely be a vulnerability if those folks become engaged and consolidate around an alternative to Ford," said Coletto, noting it was 23 per cent undecided last month.
"Ford's approval rating has dropped over the past few months but it hasn't really impacted vote intentions," the pollster noted.
"That's often the last thing to shift, but also requires voters to feel there's a better alternative. If that condition emerges, Ford could be in trouble."
Mindful of current concerns over inflation, Abacus asked respondents "between your provincial government and the federal government, which has made decisions that have most increased your cost of living?"
About two-thirds - 64 per cent - of Ontarians said the federal government, while 36 per cent said Queen's Park. (In the rest of Canada, 70 per cent pointed at Ottawa while 30 per cent blamed their respective provincial government.)
"I think Doug Ford has built his personal brand around fighting for taxpayers," said Coletto.
"He's seen as empathetic and committed to making life more affordable and so that's insulated him from some of the same forces bringing down incumbents elsewhere," he said.
"There's a subset of the electorate unhappy with the Ford government, but not ready to commit to an alternative."
Fewer than one-third - 30 per cent - had a positive impression of Ford while nearly half - 47 per cent - had a negative impression with 20 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say.
That's a net impression rating for the premier of minus 17 per cent.
By comparison, the Liberals' Crombie was 27 per cent positive, 29 per cent negative, 28 per cent neutral and 16 per cent unsure for a net rating of minus two per cent.
Similarly, the NDP's Stiles had a 23 per cent positive, 25 per cent negative, 30 per cent neutral and 21 per cent unsure for a net rating of minus two per cent.
The Greens' Schreiner had a 19 per cent positive, 21 per cent negative, 37 per cent neutral and 24 per cent unsure for a net rating of minus two per cent.
These latest findings come against the backdrop of the Greenbelt land swap scandal that is under investigation by the RCMP.
In November, Abacus poll - conducted before Crombie won the Liberal leadership - the Tories were at 42 per cent, the NDP at 24 per cent, the Grits at 23 per cent and the Greens at seven per cent.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks during a press conference regarding housing development in the Greater Toronto Area, as Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow looks on at Toronto City Hall on Feb. 22, 2024.

Document 45 of 252

Why Canada's consensus on immigration is fraying

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-canadas-consensus-on-immigration-is-fraying/docview/2931156682/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Canada%27s+consensus+on+immigration+is+fraying&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Anti-immigration sentiment used to be politically taboo. Election after election, a majority of voters told party leaders they wanted more immigrants, not fewer. That vision of a Canada welcoming newcomers with open arms, however, is increasingly challenged. Unless governments address a growing perception that unbridled migration is making the country worse off, we may be walking towards a darker, more divisive path, one that makes us less wealthy in the long run.
This week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre signalled a large reduction in migration numbers will be part of his upcoming platform.
Speaking to reporters in Kitchener, Ont., Poilievre accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of ruining the entire immigration system by opening "the floodgates in a way that was disconnected from the number of homes to house people, and the amount of jobs to employ them." He said he'll use a mathematical formula to link population increases to the growth in the supply of housing, so Canada's housing stock outpaces the population - something that hasn't happened since the mid-1990s.
Though his office declined to elaborate on his plans, Statistics Canada's numbers and housing forecasts from the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation suggest that, without a huge increase in construction, Poilievre could be looking at capping annual migration, including non-permanent residents, to approximately 400,000 - a 65 per cent reduction from 2023 figures.
Just a few elections ago, that was unthinkable. During the 2015 campaign, the Liberals and NDP tried to outpromise each other on the number of Syrian refugees each was willing to welcome, while the Conservatives were lambasted for proposing a tip line urging neighbours to report "barbaric cultural practices" to the RCMP.
In the years that followed, People's Party of Canada Leader Maxime Bernier remained the only federal leader promising to severely restrict immigration.
He is no longer alone.
Public opinion surveys suggest concern over immigration has jumped in the past year.
More people now think immigration is making Canada worse off, said Abacus Data CEO David Coletto. Just under half of respondents believe their community needs fewer immigrants, and only one in four believe more immigrants are needed, he told the Star.
It is a dramatic shift from a year ago when support for immigration was at an all-time high, reflecting a rising trend over the past three decades. Blame seems to have landed on the Liberals, not for steadily increasing permanent immigration levels, but for allowing a huge rise in non-permanent residents - 2.5 million in the past two years - that appears to have shifted the social consensus around immigration.
"It's really dangerous in politics to argue with feelings," Immigration Minister Marc Miller recently told the Star's "It's Political" podcast. "Sometimes they reflect a reality, and sometimes not."
Immigrants aren't to blame for rising interest rates or the recent affordability crisis, he said, though the spike in non-permanent residents has affected shelter costs. Still, he argued, other parts of that immigration equation can't be ignored. Newcomers nearly single-handedly have grown the Canadian economy the last few years. A large proportion of nurses and health-care workers in Canadian hospitals are recent arrivals or second-generation immigrants.
"We are an aging population; that's probably the thing that keeps me up at night," Miller said. By 2031, one in four Canadians will be over the age of 65, according to Statistics Canada. "If we want that level of services ... a free health-care system - we do need immigrants. But it has to be done in a better way than what we've done in the past."
Miller's list is long.
Statistics Canada - which failed to forecast the boom in population growth - needs to provide more accurate projections, he said. Governments at all levels must do a better job of planning for growth and co-ordinating with each other. Post-secondary schools should ensure proper housing for international students. And the provinces should shoulder more financial responsibility for migrants, he said.
"If you want the ability to select in your town, your city, this type of person, which we don't doubt the labour needs, you also have to be able to accompany them and help them with integration," the minister said, "and not expect the Government of Canada to constantly shovel money over to you every time you ask or scream and yell."
Miller also believes governments and businesses have been "a bit lazy" championing the benefits of immigration. "Every chamber of commerce that I talked to is super bullish on immigration, but they don't go out selling it."
Miller has problems to fix in his own sandbox.
This winter, he brought in changes to the international student program, doubling the amount of money prospective students need to show to come to Canada. He reduced and capped undergraduate study permits to about 360,000 - a 35 per cent drop from 2023 levels, and redistributed those permits across the country, so that B.C. and Ontario have to cut what Miller calls "garbage programs" that offered fake MBAs and a subpar education. He also banned students in public-private college partnerships from accessing postgraduate work permits, as a way of curbing a rapidly-growing sector he said is rife with fraud and abuse.
He plans to make changes in other non-permanent migration streams, too, though those will require more time. Those categories include everything from agricultural workers here for the summer, to temporary foreign workers at Tim Hortons, to the 220,000 Ukrainians that have arrived since Russia's invasion two years ago.
"No one is asking us to take away work permits for the Ukrainians that are here," he noted. "But we have to be quite clear to people that if we do do cuts, it can have a clear impact and make any recession, that we may or may not be in, worse."
The minister also floated the idea of offering low-wage workers - and not just high-skilled migrants - citizenship. He noted labour needs exist across the spectrum.
This spring, Miller wants cabinet to offer undocumented workers a path to citizenship. His plan won't make everyone happy, and it is likely to be met with criticism about line-skipping and preferential treatment.
"I can't deny that there's a lot of resistance within the system ... perhaps even in our own party," he said. But he feels strongly that people without status, already living here, with housing, working under the table, should be paying taxes.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said he's been impressed by Miller's ability to fight the resistance within government and get results. The former Conservative MP told the Star he approached Miller in August after he was named to the portfolio. "He gave me his cellphone number, said if there's a problem, text me. I'm happy to work co-operatively."
They spent most of their time discussing international students and asylum seekers' needs. Asylum claimants used to account for two per cent of Peel Region's shelter beds, Brown said, but last fall that number jumped to more than 85 per cent. Shelters were full. Migrants were sleeping outside. In November, after a Nigerian man died in his makeshift tent, Miller called to Brown as he was walking up to his press conference.
"He essentially said, I know you're going to be hard on us," Brown recounted, "but I want you to know, we're going to fix this. Just give me time to figure it out. I'm getting a lot of pushback from my officials, but I'm going to find a way. And he did, almost a week later, he found a solution." Miller came forward with funding for 1,000 new beds and a welcome centre at Pearson Airport that Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow had pitched to address the growing number of arrivals.
Across the country, in each of the past five months an average of 15,635 asylum claims have been filed, a third more than the same time last year. And although wait times have dropped, there are still 165,000 claims waiting to be processed.
"I don't think anyone credible can tell you they know what the solution is in the long term, given the flows," said Miller. "But there are better things that we can do."
The stakes are high. People need to feel the border is under control, the minister acknowledged, and anti-immigration sentiment is blowing across Europe and the United States. He believes "that consensus" around immigration "is still there."
"What Canadians want is governments to be more organized, more co-ordinated in their way they do immigration," he said. "I think a country like Canada is up to it."
CAPTION: Federal Immigration Minister Marc Miller arrives for a meeting of the Liberal caucus on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, on Feb. 14, 2024.

Feb. 24: The face of courage, give the homeless cars and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-24-face-courage-give-homeless-cars-other/docview/2931156677/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+24%3A+The+face+of+courage%2C+give+the+homeless+cars+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Caring, respectful coverage
Re: Elizabeth Grandbois should have died decades ago. An experimental ALS treatment gave her hope (Feb. 21)
I felt compelled to respond to the recent online article of Feb. 21, written about my life. The article was beautifully written by Susan Clairmont with details so carefully considered and nuances so skilfully shared. Over the years, there have been numerous columnists and photographers at my home and also at the many ALS concerts that I organized and all have been caring, respectful and sensitive in their coverage. I often hear of the mistrust of journalists and reporters who tell our stories and give us the news, but I have found those working for The Hamilton Spectator are experienced, professional and considerate of people's stories. So I am sending my thanks to all of you who have followed my journey and with a special acknowledgment to Susan Clairmont.
Elizabeth Grandbois, Burlington
The face of courage, strength
Susan Clairmont has shed a glowing light on the efforts of a very inspirational woman we've all been longing to hear more about. Elizabeth Grandbois continues to be the face of courage and strength in her endeavours to connect with researchers around the globe in the hope of making life better for all those with ALS. Thank you, Susan Clairmont, for covering this story. Your wonderful way of writing keeps us always glad to have the Spec at our fingertips!
Marjorie Filice, Hamilton
Bike lanes not being used
Yesterday, while travelling down Victoria Street Hamilton, I was shocked to see bike lanes on both sides of the street, and not one bicycle anywhere in sight. A four-lane road reduced to two lanes; what a shame. I have nothing against biking, but this is too much. All that money spent and no, or little return. In fact, I haven't seen anyone using any of the bike lanes in Hamilton for months. I was recently in South America where bikes are used way more than here. I noticed their bike lanes are constructed as part of the sidewalks, not to infringe on vehicle traffic. It seems like we're spending money to penalize ourselves, and make it difficult for the majority of citizens, who ultimately are paying for all this.
Jack Terrana, Ancaster
Give the homeless cars?
Inspired by council's latest vote on affordable housing, perhaps the more cost effective solution is to give homeless people a car rather than an apartment. Council would then ensure they have a place to put their car, even if they only needed the place for a few hours per week. Further, if their car was stolen, Ontario's solicitor general would then see it as part of a "crisis" and something which "all levels of government need to work together to get a handle on."
George Sweetman, Hamilton
Homes would bring shoppers
Re: Plan to use Stoney Creek parking lot for housing rejected (Feb. 21)
Just a thought: 67 affordable housing units, possibly 120 or so full time occupants, who would shop locally all the time versus 57 parking spots who bring fewer people to the area to shop occasionally. Seems like the BIA didn't want too many shoppers!
Rob Millen, Hamilton
Canadians are better than that
I am fortunate to be spending some time in warmer climes in the U.S. However, I have been keeping up with the news back home in Canada. It is increasingly difficult to tell the difference between the drivel being pushed by Pierre Poilievre and the nonsense that I hear down here.
He seems to think that there is a market in Canada for narrow mindedness that promotes transphobia and labels anything even remotely progressive or compassionate as "wokeness." Well, I believe the vast majority of Canadians are better than that. And they will have a chance to prove it to him in the next election.
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
Refusing a friend in need
Re: Preventing the weeds from spreading (Feb. 19)
As Russia continues slowly to seize more of Ukraine, it would be appropriate to send Fr. Bohdan Hladio's insightful commentary in Tuesday's Spectator to each and every Republican member of the U.S. government. They are playing political games while the risk of further Russian success is increasing and using support for Ukraine as leverage in an effort to further their own vested interests. While there is no know biological difference between Republicans and Democrats (no evidence of any difference in intelligence), the former continue to insist strangely that all immigrants will pollute the U.S. as they are somehow second-class people. They also argue that Americans have a right to carry weapons because guns don't kill people. But it is impossible to understand their thinking on Ukraine, which amounts to helping Vladimir Putin by refusing to help a friend in desperate need. We all know now how vile the Russian government is, yet these same Republicans have no problem sacrificing Ukraine as they attempt to "make America great again." Sadly, we may all have to face very unpleasant consequences of their unbelievable antics.
Jim Warren, Hamilton
Poilievre has some learning to do
Re: Poilievre against transgender women in female bathrooms, changing rooms, sports (Feb. 21)
It seems pretty clear Pierre Poilievre hasn't clued into something: trans women are no more biological males than someone like me. Forget anything lined to anatomy at birth, all right. Trans women don't deserve recognition as biological men for a second. No matter anyone's path to womanhood, they deserve full recognition as women. May Poilievre manage to learn that lesson.
Amy Soule, Hamilton
Conservative leader is frightening
Well, Pierre Poilievre has finally broken his silence and it's as scary as I thought it would be. Banning trans women from using women's bathrooms and competing in women's sports is truly the right-wing way to go, because they don't care about people's rights. So trans women are now supposed to change in the men's bathroom. This is truly not safe for a woman. Also, not allowing trans youth puberty blockers until they are 18 is ridiculous. They need to be taken at the beginning of puberty or else they wont work. And refusing medical care for trans youth is wrong and cruel and will only lead to more mental-health issues and more suicides. As I've said before, Poilievre is frightening and will no doubt take more people's freedom away before he's done.
Jane Farrington, Hamilton
Don't we have bigger problems?
With housing out of sight, health care in a shambles and wildfires still smouldering under the snow, what do we hear from Pierre Poilievre? Check your crotch before heading into the bathroom. All hail the next prime minister of Canada.
Phil Beard, Dundas
Trans beliefs about fairness
I often do not agree with Pierre Poilievre's opinions and statements. I do, however, agree with his recent comment that trans women (biological males) have no place in female sports. Males are generally taller, heavier and stronger than females. To allow trans women to compete against biological females is ridiculously unfair. I have never read or heard about trans men competing against biological males, I wonder why?
Russell Pape, Stoney Creek
Government needs to get out of the way
Re: The private sector housing experiment has failed (Feb. 15)
You'd expect a Marxist to conclude that capitalism has failed, as Prof. Shauna MacKinnon has done. You'd expect a Marxist to say that only heavy intervention and control of economic resources by the government can solve whatever the problem is, as Prof. MacKinnon said of Canada's housing crisis. Calling Canada's history of housing construction an "experiment," something easily discarded and undeserving of respect, to say nothing of being a false misrepresentation of the facts, is what you'd expect a Marxist with an agenda to say.
Actually, the housing crisis has been caused by government: by municipal governments for not making land available in unlimited quantities on which to build new housing; and by the federal government for bringing in record numbers of immigrants without caring if there was housing for them.
American economist and Hoover Institution Senior Fellow Thomas Sowell has written about housing crises, which he first observed in Palo Alto in the 1970s, for over four decades. The cause and the solution are not hard to discover, unless you're a Marxist out to prove that capitalism doesn't work.
Get the government out of the way, let the free market work its magic, and the housing crisis will be solved quicker than by ham-fisted government intervention, the cause of the crisis in the first place.
Vincent J. Curtis, Hamilton
CAPTION: Elizabeth Grandbois was diagnosed with ALS more than 20 years ago and was given a couple of years to live. She is still here and believes it is because she took an experimental AIDS medication cocktail.

Briefs: Town bans Pride flags

Full text: Canada
Ford defends appointments
Ontario Premier Doug Ford is defending his government's appointments of two former staffers to a committee that helps select provincial judges. The Toronto Star first reported that a former deputy chief of staff to Ford is the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee and Ford's former director of stakeholder relations is on the committee. Ford said Friday his Progressive Conservative government got elected "to get like-minded people in appointments." He said, "I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal."
Town bans Pride flags
A town north of Edmonton has voted in favour of a bylaw that bans Pride flags and rainbow crosswalks on municipal property. A slim majority of Westlock residents who took part in a plebiscite Thursday voted in favour of the town flying only government flags and painting crosswalks in a white striped pattern. Mayor Jon Kramer said councillors didn't support the move.
Spain
Nine dead in apartment fire
The death toll in a fire that engulfed an apartment block in the Spanish city of Valencia rose Friday to nine as questions were raised about whether construction materials caused the fire to spread so rapidly. One person remained missing, according to forensic police. The fire started Thursday evening and quickly engulfed the two residential buildings. Neighbor Alejandra Alarcon said it took 15 minutes for the fire to engulf an entire building, as questions abounded as to how the fire spread so rapidly.
United States
Moon lander 'alive and well'
The moon's newest arrival was said to be "alive and well" a day after making the first U.S. landing in half a century, but flight controllers were still trying to get a better handle on its bearings. Intuitive Machines reported Friday that it's communicating with its lander, Odysseus, and sending commands. But it noted: "We continue to learn more about the vehicle's specific information" regarding location, overall health and positioning. With Thursday's touchdown, Intuitive Machines became the first private business to pull off a moon landing.
News services

Document 48 of 252

Pharmacare agreement reached: NDP, Liberals agree to cover cost of diabetes medication and birth control as a first step

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pharmacare-agreement-reached/docview/2931109972/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pharmacare+agreement+reached%3A+NDP%2C+Liberals+agree+to+cover+cost+of+diabetes+medication+and+birth+control+as+a+first+step&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal Liberals and New Democrats have reached a deal that would allow every Canadian with a health card to access free diabetes medication and birth control, the NDP confirmed Friday.
The coverage is to be included in the first piece of a national pharmacare program, with legislation expected to be introduced in the House of Commons next week.
It's a critical piece of the supply-and-confidence pact between the two parties, in which the NDP agreed to support the Liberals on key votes in the Commons in exchange for movement on shared priorities.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh threatened in recent weeks to pull out of the deal if the Liberals didn't agree to certain terms by a March 1 deadline.
A spokesperson for the NDP confirmed the pharmacare deal on Friday and said some final details could still be worked out over the weekend.
The deal includes all insulin for Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes, the spokesperson said, as well as additional diabetes drugs and a fund for supplies such as devices for glucose monitoring.
It will not cover Ozempic, a new drug for diabetes that has been used off-label as a weight-loss drug.
The government is also expected to fully cover contraceptives in line with what British Columbia covers. That includes IUDs and emergency contraception, most commonly known as morning-after pills.
Ontario also provides many contraceptives for people under the age of 25 who don't have private insurance. Manitoba's government has already pledged to do so as well.
Abortion pills, which are different than morning-after pills, are already offered for free at pharmacies across Canada.
The two parties had initially agreed to pass a pharmacare bill by the end of 2023.
But in December, both parties agreed to add negotiating time, pinning March 1 as a deadline for the government to table legislation.
The broad strokes of a deal were in place early last week, but the parties were still in talks about which drugs would be covered in the first go around of a national single-payer program.
Liberals made it clear they had about $800 million to spend for an initial program, said the source, who was granted anonymity because they aren't authorized to speak publicly about the details of the closed talks.
The source said that in addition to contraceptives, the parties considered whether to cover antibiotics, cholesterol medicines, blood pressure medication and psychological medications such as antidepressants.
Liberals had previously cited concerns about the potential costs of pharmacare. If the federal government moves towards fully implementing national pharmacare, that wider program is expected to cost roughly $40 billion a year.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Mickey Djuric and Mia Rabson The Canadian Press

Document 49 of 252

Doug Ford government officially repeals provincial wage restraint law Bill 124

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-officially-repeals/docview/2930691248/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+officially+repeals+provincial+wage+restraint+law+Bill+124&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An ill-fated attempt by Premier Doug Ford to cap the wages of public sector workers has been wiped off the books.
His government has followed through with a promise to repeal its controversial Bill 124 public sector wage restraint legislation, cementing a win for thousands of unionized workers.
Twice struck down by the courts as unconstitutional for restricting collective bargaining rights, the 2019 law limited pay increases for most public sector workers - including hospital nurses, teachers and civil servants - to one per cent annually for three years.
The legislation was revoked through an order-in-council, the government said Friday in a terse one-paragraph statement from the office of Treasury Board President Caroline Mulroney.
"Repealing the bill will solve the inequality of workers created by the recent court decision," the statement said.
While public sector workers awaited the court decision, labour arbitrators were awarding retroactive pay increases higher than one per cent to some whose bargaining units had "reopener" clauses in their contracts with employers. They include some teachers, nurses, personal support workers, lab technicians and dietary aides.
Many other workers have not been able to make those gains.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy has said the cost of reopeners and wage catch-ups have now been "incorporated" into the government's spending plans.
"After wasting years and countless dollars fighting workers in court, it's time to start fixing the mess Bill 124 created in our hospitals, schools and public services," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, who pressed Premier Doug Ford to apologize earlier this week.
He did not, telling the legislature "we think the world of the nurses."
The Progressive Conservative government signalled earlier this month it would axe Bill 124 after losing an appeal of a 2022 Superior Court decision that struck it down.
"Ontario has not been able to explain why wage restraint could not have been achieved through good-faith bargaining," Ontario's Court of Appeal ruled Feb. 12.
It has been blamed for an exodus of health-care workers during the COVID-19 pandemic, where nurses and personal support workers put in long hours and their own health on the line and felt disrespected.
The bill did not apply to police and firefighters, which are both male-dominated fields.
The government's move is one of several recent climbdowns from signature policies, such as its broken plan to open more of the Greenbelt to housing development.
The $8.28-billion land swap scandal is under criminal investigation by the RCMP for "irregularities" that include concerns developers were tipped off in advance.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford during Question Period in the Ontario Legislature on Feb. 20.

Document 50 of 252

International student cap 'blindsided' province, Doug Ford says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/international-student-cap-blindsided-province/docview/2930691112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=International+student+cap+%27blindsided%27+province%2C+Doug+Ford+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government "blindsided" the province with its announcement to cap the number of international students, a move Premier Doug Ford said was like taking "a sledgehammer to the system."
Speaking in Brampton at an unrelated news conference, Ford was also asked about a Star report that the government is set to provide more than a billion dollars in funding to colleges and universities over the next three years, which have been seeking additional funding to help deal with ongoing financial struggles.
Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop will make a "fabulous" announcement Monday, Ford said.
A government source has told the Star the money will help those schools with the greatest need.
"The government will be providing over $1.2 billion in new direct financial support," the source told the Star, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the plans. "As part of this funding, a new $903-million-dollar fund will be made available to all public colleges and universities" over the three years, the source said, with additional money for student mental health and safety.
The money, however, is far less than schools hoped for, based on recommendations from an expert panel set up by the province that had urged roughly double that in spending over the same time period.
But the government source said "the institutions with the highest need will be eligible to receive a portion of that funding that meets the blue-ribbon panel recommendation."
The overall funding will be contingent on schools proving they are operating as efficiently as possible, other sources told the Star.
In 2019, the Ford government slashed tuition by 10 per cent and then implemented a freeze. That, combined with stagnant funding and inflation, has left almost half of the province's universities running deficits and warning of program cuts.
And now, both colleges and universities are facing the loss of international student tuition revenue as the federal government chops down their numbers - a move that will disproportionately hit Ontario - amid a housing shortage and questions about the quality of some of the programs run by public and private college partnerships.
"Don't get me wrong - there's some bad actors in the private sector colleges," Ford said Friday. "But there's some good schools as well. And I just believe in working within the ministry to shoo out the bad actors and encourage the good colleges and universities to keep moving forward because everywhere we go, anywhere in the world ... our number one selling feature are the colleges and universities and the students that are coming out of (those schools)."
Last year, Dunlop appointed an expert blue-ribbon panel to look at the future of the post-secondary sector. It recommended an immediate 10 per cent boost in government grants, with future increases set at a minimum of two per cent a year, as well as an immediate tuition bump of five per cent this fall, among other increases.
In total, its recommendations came with an estimated price tag of $2.5 billion over three years for the province's 24 publicly assisted colleges and 23 universities.
The report noted a number of auditor general reports warning of low provincial funding for post-secondary institutions - among the lowest in the country - and the risk of reliance on international students as a revenue generator given they pay three to four times the tuition of domestic students.
Schools themselves have said that with no tuition increases, combined with inflation, unexpected COVID-19 costs as well as salary increases for staff in light of the province's unconstitutional wage cap legislation, they are in a financially precarious situation.
"What they are doing is very dangerous for the sector," warned one post-secondary veteran has warned.
Ford has already ruled out a tuition fee increase this fall, and sources have told the Star that is likely to be extended.
Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, has said schools are at a "breaking point" that the government must "urgently" address, with 10 of the 23 projecting deficits of a total of $175 million.
CAPTION: Ford has already ruled out a tuition fee increase this fall, and sources have told the Star that is likely to be extended.

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Private schools; Colleges & universities; Funding

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

Feb. 23: Province needs to step up, how about a 'Bad Bonnie Act' and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-23-province-needs-step-up-how-about-bad/docview/2930691080/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+23%3A+Province+needs+to+step+up%2C+how+about+a+%27Bad+Bonnie+Act%27+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Province needs to step up
One of the reasons the police budget is so high is because the province is starving our social services of funds. Doug Ford could absolutely fund affordable housing projects, mental-health services and drug addiction services. But because he is only meting out the bare minimum to the municipalities, starving our health services, not doing a thing about affordable housing and took away rent controls causing rents to shoot sky-high, the police now have to contend with all the social issues that happen with homelessness, mental health and addictions.
I know people say, why should I have to pay for someone else to get off of drugs or afford housing? Well, the thing is you are the first to demand the police "do something." So the police need funds to handle these issues, which is very expensive and very not effective at solving the problem. It's just taking a broom and pushing the dirt around. If you really don't like how high the budget is, call our premier and let him know he needs to fund these issues now. It's cheaper for the province to fund these solutions and more equitable. This is the Canadian way. Doug Ford's government is certainly trying to do things the American way, and it is not working.
Natasha Huyer, Hamilton
More helpful legislation
So our dear leader has decided to protect us Ontarians with useless legislation that borders on the nonsensical. Comical, even. No tolls on roads that don't have tolls. (But tolls stay on the only road that does have tolls - the road the Conservatives sold off for a song in one of the worst deals in provincial history.) No carbon tax in a province that doesn't have a provincial carbon tax.
What is wrong with Conservatives (of all stripes) across this country? Why are they so angry all the time? Why are they so fearful of progress?
But we are where we are, so allow me to offer some other useless legislation Doug Ford can enact.
The No PET Allowed Act. This law would prevent the late Pierre Elliott Trudeau from ever being elected as an Ontario MPP.
The Hands to Yourself Act. This law would implement a $500 fine on anyone who squeezes the Charmin bathroom tissue in a supermarket aisle.
The Leave it to Beaver Act. This law would take Ontario back to the 1950s (where Conservatives seem most comfortable). It would prevent wives from working outside the home and require them to serve their husbands a martini and a smile at the end of a working day. And a $50 fine on any wife who doesn't wear an apron in the kitchen.
The Bad Bonnie Act. This law states that every bad decision, every unbalanced budget, every poor piece of legislation that has happened in Ontario in the past or will happen in the future (even those that happen during a period of Conservative government) are the fault of unelected Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie. Ontario. Yours to discover.
Steve Buist, Burlington
Get feedback on new act
On Feb. 15, the article "Skelly plunges into Hamilton's stormwater fee controversy" noted MPP Donna Skelly during her public meeting only wanted to criticize the City of Hamilton's proposed stormwater fees, but she said that "she was unaware of the provincial regulatory changes referenced by (Coun. Maureen) Wilson."
Given that she has been on legislation break for two months, you would think she might be more up to speed on what her government has been doing. Perhaps a more productive use of her public meeting would have been getting public feedback on the omnibus bill "Get it Done Act" that will do everything from auto renew licence plates to streamline (remove?) the environmental impact process - an act that has no public input opportunities as of yet.
Dan McDonell, Hamilton
Council needs to tighten belt
This city council needs to shape up or ship out! The city's coffers aren't theirs to spend however they wish. The taxpayers of this city simply cannot afford any more of their pet projects. Some of those new hires need to be let go to trim expenses. LRT, bike lanes and two-way street conversion, none are a necessity and should not be pursued. Anything that will cost money and isn't a necessity simply cannot be entertained. This city's taxpayers cannot afford it.
Do they think that we are dim? Is saying we were facing a 14.2 per cent increase and then settling on 5.79 per cent supposed to make us sigh with relief?
If this council doesn't start tightening its belt, like everyone else does, it will drive anyone considering buying a house away. Exorbitant property taxes, horrid roads, tent ghettos taking over public parks, a disgraceful downtown core; who would want to live here?
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
CAPTION: Because Premier Doug is only meting out the bare minimum to the municipalities for social services and housing, the police now have to contend with all the social issues that happen with homelessness, mental health and addictions, a reader argues.

Document 52 of 252

Nurse recruitment is being hindered by too much red tape

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nurse-recruitment-is-being-hindered-too-much-red/docview/2930690895/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nurse+recruitment+is+being+hindered+by+too+much+red+tape&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Much of the Ontario legislative debate on Wednesday focused on our health-care system.
It was led by much harsh, persuasive criticism from the NDP leader Marit Stiles and boisterously defended by Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones. In watching this debate on TV, I saw no attention paid to what I have heard from at least one hospital in the University Health Network, that a major problem relates to the substantial departure from her hospital of nurses to the U.S., along with the lessened flow of qualified immigrant nurses.
In my legal practice, I represented the Ontario Nurses' Association Local 83 in some of its collective bargaining and dispute issues. At that time, my recollection is that the acceptance of qualified nurses in Ontario was based on a combined federal and provincial approach.
The federal Canada Health Act stipulates the provinces and territories must legislatively establish regulatory bodies for dealing with health issues, including nurses. Ontario met this obligation by giving regulatory authority to the College of Nurses of Ontario (CNO). In doing so, the CNO established a set of registration tests.
These require nursing applicants to satisfy the college concerning their backgrounds, to demonstrate the completion of CNO's own developed nursing education program, to assess the applicant's competence at different levels of cognitive ability, to demonstrate proficiency in either English or French, to be a Canadian citizen, a permanent resident of Canada or hold authorization under the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (Canada). As well, applicants must establish they have had no criminal offences and that their health and their past conduct satisfies the CNO.
In a recent contact with a long-serving nurse at one of the University Health Network hospitals, I was told the critical shortage of nurses at her hospital has resulted from two related factors: the first, substantial time taken by the CNO to complete its complex registration requirements; and second, the departure of nurses to the U.S., most of whose state registration requirements are less complex and more rapidly and efficiently administered by the state's authority.
This is a critical issue. Ontario's population was a little more than 15.5 million on April 1, 2023, and continues to increase substantially, representing about 40 per cent of the entire country's population. As well, there is evidence some other provinces, through their legislation, have established more effectively operating control units for the admission of nurses
There is, as well, the critical issue of the increasing number of aging Ontarians, with reported projections showing Ontario's senior population growing by 650,000 in the next five years. A recently released report concludes that combined with demographic population increase trend, there is persuasive evidence showing Ontarians use more health-care resources as they age.
From a foreign nursing source, who has permanent Canadian residency, I have been advised there is, for the above reasons, a reluctance in her country, the Philippines, for their highly qualified nurses to seek nursing employment in Ontario.
The Ford government should recognize these criticisms and take immediate action to analyze the CNO's registration requirements and take the necessary action, through regulation or otherwise, to ensure the college's registration requirements are appropriate and, more importantly, are being efficiently carried out as rapidly as possible. Otherwise, this extremely troubling trend will increase.
CAPTION: The substantial time taken by the College of Nurses of Ontario to complete complex registration requirements, and the departure of nurses to the U.S., where registration is less complex, has hampered recruitment in Ontario, Tim Armstrong writes.
CREDIT: Tim Armstrong

Subject: Health care networks; Population; Registration; Canada Health Act; Nursing; Nurses

Document 53 of 252

Omnibus bill is political theatre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/omnibus-bill-is-political-theatre/docview/2930423539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Omnibus+bill+is+political+theatre&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There once existed a general expectation that bills introduced in parliaments and legislatures bore neutral titles seeking to indicate their content and purpose.
That quaint custom seems to have passed. In contemporary practice, the titling of legislation is, as often as not, an act of blatant political propaganda or provocation.
To wit, the Ontario government's Getting It Done Act introduced this week at Queen's Park, an omnibus bill that is the namesake of the PC government's 2022 campaign slogan.
Subtle, the Doug Ford government is not.
It's not unreasonable to say that Ontarians might have gleaned a better understanding of the contents of this unholy mishmash had the province named it the Crazy Clown Car Act, or the Empty Political Gestures Act, or, as a political historian once joked, the Poke the Opposition in the Eye Act.
In the era of constant campaigning, it appears governments now see even the simple act of titling bills as just another opportunity for partisan messaging.
But there's more to the matter than mere mischievous wordplay. At core in this bill, the Ford government is again flaunting its chronic disregard for transparency and accountability.
The employment of omnibus bills - large pieces of legislation into which many diverse policy issues and manoeuvres are packed - is almost by definition designed to hasten the process, reduce scrutiny and limit debate, thereby minimizing public uproar and resistance.
The guiding imperatives of such bills are speed and limited opposition involvement. Which, for a government whose signature characteristic has been half-baked policy rashness and behind-the-scenes decision-making, is on the face of things alarming. As NDP Leader Marit Stiles noted, "where the government has gone wrong over and over again is not listening to people.
"They've spent more time in the last year reversing course and they haven't actually come up with practical solutions that will make life easier for people," she said.
In that, she is not wrong.
Granted, Ford's gambit is not as egregious, say, as the infamous "Bully Bill" of the Mike Harris government almost 30 years ago, a behemoth of more than 200 pages that contained massive structural implications for a wide range of public institutions in one fell swoop. Still, it is worrying.
It is a mélange of fluff and the seriously consequential. It is part arrogant time-machine - trying to control what future governments can do, particularly on highway tolls and carbon pricing, while revisiting previous efforts on the home-building front that ended in disaster. For instance, it would shorten environmental assessments to speed construction of new highways and hydro lines, allow for quicker expropriations before such assessments were completed, and change some urban boundaries.
Into the hopper were thrown elements that would require a referendum on any future provincial carbon pricing program, ban new tolls on Ontario highways and, tossing the merely administrative in with the potentially dramatic, automatically renew vehicle registrations for owners with no outstanding fines.
On the face of it, Ford's initiative violates parliamentary norms in seeking to bind the hands of future governments in a piece of legislation so convoluted as to defy coherent parliamentary review. The fundamental purpose of a parliament or legislature is not, after all, efficiency and productivity in racing through matters as fast as possible, but in scrutiny and accountability to craft the best possible policy.
But there are so many components to Ford's bill that MPPs cannot possibly decide on them all in a single vote - a reality that adds to an inexorable shift of power from legislators to the executive. In all, there is no discernible unifying theme in the Getting It Done Act other than the self-interested goal of advancing the re-election prospects of the Ford government.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said this week Ford's omnibus effort is "performative politics at its worst" and the bill should have been called the "Get it Done Wrong Act."
In that, he is quite right.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Subject: Legislation; Politics; Accountability

Document 55 of 252

Can nurse recruitment be streamlined?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/can-nurse-recruitment-be-streamlined/docview/2930422723/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+nurse+recruitment+be+streamlined%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Otherwise, this extremely troubling trend will increase.
Tim Armstrong, QC, was a deputy minister in the William Davis, DAVID Peterson and BOB Rae governments.

Title: Can nurse recruitment be streamlined?

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.10
