Document 56 of 252

Are Doug Ford's Conservatives obsessed with Bonnie Crombie?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/are-doug-fords-conservatives-obsessed-with-bonnie/docview/2930422707/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+Doug+Ford%27s+Conservatives+obsessed+with+Bonnie+Crombie%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Call it the Conservatives' Crombie compulsion.
Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have spent the early days of the new legislative session obsessed with rookie Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
Complementing their current multimillion-dollar attack advertising blitz - paid for by the PC party, not the provincial treasury - the Tories used the legislature's daily question period to blast Crombie's record as a three-term Mississauga mayor and one-term Liberal MP.
The governing party even inserted a section in omnibus legislation specifically designed to paint her into a corner over the federal Liberals' carbon pricing regimen.
Under the Get It Done Act, tabled Tuesday, a future premier would be required to hold a referendum before imposing any carbon tax.
While Crombie dismissed that as a "stunt," Ford's Tories were delighted by her repeated refusal to say whether she supported Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon levy during a live interview with Vassy Kapelos on CTV Power Play that day.
"As I said, there are many tools to combat climate change," the Liberal leader told Kapelos, declining to elaborate on what action a provincial Liberal government would take if elected in 2026.
"We're going to do our homework - as we should - before we announce any new policies," she said.
Replied Kapelos: "The question I had posed to you was how long do you plan to consult before you come to a decision on where you stand? Is it until the next election?"
Crombie would only say, "We will come to the right decision..."
In the legislature, the Tories crowed about her stance.
"One party has remained steadfast in their support for the federal carbon tax, and that's Bonnie Crombie and the Liberal Party of Ontario," said Energy Minister Todd Smith.
Smith did not mention the two carbon pricing schemes the Tories have introduced - the 2022 emission performance standards program that targets industrial polluters and his own clean energy credit registry cap-and-trade system he brought in last year.
Associate Housing Minister Rob Flack signalled Wednesday that Crombie's tenure as Mississauga mayor would also be relitigated in the coming months and years.
"Mayor Crombie had the worst housing record in the province of Ontario. Mayor Crombie's housing record was abysmal," Flack told the legislature, pointing to two high-density housing projects Mississauga council had opposed.
"Ontario families cannot afford Bonnie Crombie," he said.
Crombie countered that when she was mayor, her council "dramatically improved zoning laws to add unlimited density in our downtown, to build beautiful complete communities on our waterfront by reclaiming old industrial lands, and to allow fourplexes."
At a Toronto city hall news conference on Thursday, Ford, whose Tories lead the Liberals in every major public-opinion poll, railed against Crombie, insisting he is not afraid of facing off against her in the next election.
"The mayor failed, Bonnie Crombie failed. She had the lowest housing starts of anyone in Ontario - that's unacceptable," the premier told reporters.
"Bonnie Crombie raised taxes to the highest they've ever been in Mississauga," he continued as Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow stood patiently behind him.
"She is running to be the premier and we need (a) clear and decisive decision from her: is she for taxing people, is she for the carbon tax or isn't she?" said Ford, adding Crombie can't "dodge and weave" now that she's moved "into the big leagues."
For her part, the Liberal leader said the premier is scrambling to "deflect attention" from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
"This preoccupation with me - some call it an obsession - is just weird, it's odd," Crombie said in an interview.
"I'm not going to play by his rules. I'm not going to be pushed around. He wants to box me in, but I'm going to be setting my own agenda," she said.
"He may be used to weak opposition, but there's a new sheriff in town."
A senior Conservative source, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, suggested Crombie and Ford "live rent-free in each other's heads" because they each recognize the electoral threat the other poses.
There are almost a dozen Peel Region Tory MPPs skittish about facing off against a Liberal party led by a former mayor who won 78.5 per cent of the popular vote in the 2022 Mississauga election.
When the Liberals were in power from 2003 until 2018 under premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne, those kind of suburban ridings were reliably Grit.
Since then, those seats have been key to successive Tory victories.
While most Ontarians may not be thinking about the June 2026 election, Ford certainly is.
"People will have a decision in a couple of years," the premier said Thursday. " And it's going to be a very clear decision."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie takes questions from the media at Queens Park on Dec. 5, 2023.

Document 57 of 252

Ford government to provide $1 billion in funding to universities, colleges: sources

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-provide-1-billion-funding/docview/2930965224/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+provide+%241+billion+in+funding+to+universities%2C+colleges%3A+sources&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government will provide more than a billion dollars in funding to colleges and universities over the next three years, the Star has learned.
A government source told the Star the money will help those schools with the greatest need.
"The government will be providing over $1.2 billion in new direct financial support," the source said. "As part of this funding, a new $903-million dollar fund will be made available to all public colleges and universities" over the three years, the source added.
The announcement is expected to be made next week by Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop.
The money, however, would be far less than schools hoped for, based on recommendations from an expert panel set up by the province.
Sources were speaking confidentially in order to discuss the planned announcement.
The government source said "the institutions with the highest need will be eligible to receive a portion of that funding that meets the blue-ribbon panel recommendation."
Other sources said they also expect no tuition fee increases for up to three years - well past the next provincial election.
The announcement of the new funding is expected to include additional grants for student mental health and safety.
The overall funding will be contingent on schools proving they are operating as efficiently as possible, the sources said.
In 2019, the Ford government slashed tuition by 10 per cent and then implemented a freeze. That, combined with stagnant funding and inflation, has left almost half of the province's universities running deficits and warning of program cuts.
And now, both colleges and universities are facing the loss of international student tuition revenue as the federal government clamps down on their numbers.
Last year, Dunlop appointed an expert blue-ribbon panel to look at the future of the post-secondary sector, which recommended an immediate 10 per cent boost in government grants, with future increases set at a minimum of two per cent a year, as well as an immediate tuition bump of five per cent this fall, among other increases.
In total, its recommendations came with an estimated price tag of $2.5 billion over three years for the province's 24 publicly assisted colleges and 23 universities.
The report noted a number of auditor general reports warning of low provincial funding for post-secondary institutions - among the lowest in the country - and the risk of reliance on international students as a revenue generator given they pay three to four times the tuition of domestic students.
Schools themselves have said that with no tuition increases, combined with inflation, unexpected COVID-19 costs as well as salary increases for staff in light of the province's unconstitutional wage cap legislation, they are in a financially precarious situation.
"What they are doing is very dangerous for the sector," warned one post-secondary veteran.
Premier Doug Ford has been clear he doesn't support any tuition increase for domestic students this year, saying the current affordability crisis makes it unfair.
That's something NDP Leader Marit Stiles agrees with, but says that means the government has to step up.
"Our colleges and universities are deeply underfunded, funded at a lower rate by this government than any other province in Canada," she said. "The obvious answer is let's start to invest ... at a significant rate if you're going to make up the difference and not see post-secondary institutions diminished."
Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, has repeatedly warned that schools need the grants and tuition increases recommended by Dunlop's own appointed experts.
The panel also noted that government per-funding student for college students in 2021-22 was $6,891 in Ontario compared to an average of $15,615 nationally, and $11,471 for universities compared to $20,772 across Canada.
The report also noted the risk of relying on international student revenue. Then, last month, the federal government announced cuts to the number of new study permits issued to foreign students by 35 per cent, with spots to be allotted based on each province's population. That move will disproportionately hit Ontario, where more than half of Canada's current international students study.
Almost half of Ontario's universities are now in the red, with schools warning that student services will face cuts if the government does not provide a bump in funding and also allow tuition to rise as the panel recommended.
Orsini has said the government must "urgently" address universities' financial situations, with 10 of the 23 projecting deficits of a total of $175 million.
"Universities are at a breaking point now with years of declining real, per-student funding," Orsini has said, adding that they are already looking for - and implementing - efficiencies.
The blue-ribbon panel did not find that salaries were out of line, but did say there was "considerable room" for savings in administrative costs.
CAPTION: Ontario's Minister of Colleges and Universities Jill Dunlop during question period at the Ontario legislature on Nov. 28, 2023.

Document 58 of 252

Doug Ford's government is to blame for Ontario's teacher recruitment and retention crisis, unions charge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-government-is-blame-ontarios-teacher/docview/2930965221/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+government+is+to+blame+for+Ontario%27s+teacher+recruitment+and+retention+crisis%2C+unions+charge&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario schools can't recruit or retain enough teachers thanks to a "mess" created by the Ford government, Ontario's four teachers' unions charge.
Union leaders were responding to comments made Wednesday by Education Minister Stephen Lecce, who said the province is considering "every available option" to address a teacher shortage which has left school boards across Ontario scrambling.
They blamed the government for using "Band-Aid" solutions to fill growing gaps, such as extending the number of days retired teachers can work or allowing student-teachers to be at the front of the classroom.
Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association President René Jansen in de Wal said Lecce could do more to collaborate with educators.
"There are things he could do right now that would be far more effective, without anyone's permission ... and he's not doing them," Jansen in de Wal said. "The Ontario Teachers' Federation has made recommendations to him ... and he doesn't want to act on those things."
In a statement, Lecce noted that teacher shortages are a national problem. Since 2018, he said his government has added nearly 3,000 teachers and "reduced red tape" for new teachers entering the classroom.
But NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the shortage "isn't coming out of nowhere" and teachers under Premier Doug Ford's leadership are overburdened.
"People are leaving because they're exhausted, they're disrespected. We need smaller class sizes; we need more educational assistance in our schools. We probably do need to look at other avenues like reducing the number of years in teachers' college," Stiles said.
Teachers need to feel valued, Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation (OSSTF) President Karen Littlewood said.
"In every area of the province, there are multiple schools every day who have unqualified individuals in them who are covering classes," Littlewood said, referring to educational support workings filling in.
New teachers aren't filling full-time positions because they need to commit to one school board early in their careers and transferring to another board later is difficult, Littlewood said.
Qualified technology and science teachers are leaving for non-teaching jobs, which offer higher pay and less stress, Littlewood said.
She said some high schools can't run university prerequisites every semester because there aren't enough qualified teachers.
CAPTION: Ontario's Education Minister Stephen Lecce speaks to reporters outside the Ontario legislature on Feb. 20, 2024.
CREDIT: Sophia Coppolino Special to the Star

Subject: Teachers; School boards

Document 59 of 252

Ford government accused of helping developers and harming homeowners with bill to reverse natural gas ruling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-accused-helping-developers/docview/2930965216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+accused+of+helping+developers+and+harming+homeowners+with+bill+to+reverse+natural+gas+ruling&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is having gas pains in its bid to keep new houses affordable.
Critics say natural gas customers will get them, too, after Energy Minister Todd Smith introduced legislation to overturn an Ontario Energy Board (OEB) ruling on Enbridge gas rates - something industry observers called an "unprecedented" move.
The bill is intended to reverse a December decision by the arms-length board that developers should pay the $4,400 cost of connecting new homes to gas lines up front instead of amortizing the cost over 40 years and spreading it among all users, as has long been the standard.
"The OEB strayed out of their lane on this," the minister said Wednesday, calling the decision "disappointing" and noting it was opposed by one member of the board.
Smith maintained the upfront cost of $4,400 - growing much higher in rural areas with longer connection distances - would add to housing prices at a time when many people are struggling to make down payments.
"As the province navigates difficult headwinds like high interest rates and inflationary pressures on the costs of building materials ... it's important we don't allow new costs to be piled on top of that."
But critics said the energy board ruling was forward-looking and would encourage homebuilders to install heat pumps as the world increasingly moves away from fossil fuels to fight climate change.
"It was about reducing rates for existing gas users and saving money for new homeowners who would pay less if they have a heat pump instead of a gas furnace," said Keith Brooks of the interest group Environmental Defence.
In its ruling, the board said amortizing the cost of a connection over 40 years would leave homeowners with a "stranded asset" as many get off natural gas with the province moving toward electrification to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Heat pumps tend to be more energy-efficient than fossil fuel-based systems, are powered by electricity, and provide heating in cold weather and cooling on hot days.
Paying the cost of a gas connection up front would motivate homebuilders "to choose the most cost-effective, energy-efficient choice," the board said.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Smith's legislation is "outrageous" and New Democrat MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto-Danforth) said Smith is missing a golden opportunity to address "the realities of climate change."
"We the people of Ontario, who are ratepayers, are going to be subsidizing Enbridge's expansion of fossil gas infrastructure to install heating systems that will be obsolete," Schreiner told reporters.
"We should be installing the cleanest and lowest-cost options for heating and cooling new homes, even if that makes some of the Ford government's well-connected friends at Enbridge and in the property development industry unhappy," Keith Stewart of Greenpeace said in a statement.
Smith insisted the board's decision - reached after months of hearings - was "rushed" and "irrational" without broad-based consultation involving more from the building industry and without input from Ontario's Independent Electricity System Operator about the impact on electricity use and available supply.
That could be "significant," Smith said, noting about 70 per cent of Ontarians still use natural gas to heat their homes and water.
He announced the government will be appointing a new chair to the board this spring. The post is now vacant.
"I'll expect our appointee to help ensure that the board conducts appropriate consultation before reaching any decisions ... we have to make sure this doesn't happen again."
That smacks of "political interference," said Brooks and Schreiner.
"Appoint a qualified expert so the board can maintain its independence. It's an independent entity designed to protect the people of Ontario," the Green leader added.
Presuming the legislation is passed, the Enbridge application will go back before the board for reconsideration, Smith said, acknowledging the 40-year amortization period could be "reduced" but not committing to accepting that.
"I don't want to get into hypotheticals."
CAPTION: Energy Minister Todd Smith addresses a news conference at the Pickering nuclear generating station in Pickering, Ont. on Jan. 30, 2024.

Document 60 of 252

Feb. 22: Police budget the core concern, Hunter GO station a no-go and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-22-police-budget-core-concern-hunter-go/docview/2929976186/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+22%3A+Police+budget+the+core+concern%2C+Hunter+GO+station+a+no-go+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Police budget core concern
Re: Hamilton council whittled tax increase by pushing problems down the road (Feb. 17)
Scott Radley picked the wrong issue. Hiring staff for city services isn't the issue; the policing budget is. If you're worried about higher taxes in the coming years, then it's policing services you have to examine for savings. They take the largest percentage, year over year, of municipal taxes and have never been refused an increase, as this year's budget clearly indicates. Emptied reserves, new debts and a property tax increase can all be placed at the services' booted feet. If there are only "must-haves" and "absolute necessities," they are: 1. the re-building of trust in one another by creating "third spaces" so calls for police are reduced; and 2. the re-direction of the policing budget and surpluses toward care and concern for the least in the city, beginning with completely reimbursing the city's public health. To borrow from Coun. Jeff Beattie, it's very hard to climb that ladder and get to the top of that tree when all of the rungs have been cut, as Coun. Cameron Kroetsch and Dr. Anjali Menezes have taught us.
Joshua Weresch, Hamilton
Family Day's meaning
I think it is great that we set aside a special day now called Family Day, where families can rest, recreate and generally bond together. When I grew up, we called this Sunday.
Bob Connolly, Burlington
Hunter GO station a no-go
We recently used the Hunter Street GO bus station (Feb. 11) at 1 p.m. I wanted to use the bathroom, but it was closed. On Feb. 18 we returned from Pearson with our luggage, at 8:45 p.m., and the station was closed. We had to haul our luggage around the station to get to Hunter Street. I just wanted to let the genius that came up with the solution to homeless people using the station know that not all 80 year olds can hold it for over an hour to relieve themselves at Pearson. Or haul their luggage around the building to Hunter Street.
Ed Tymkow, Mount Hope
Changes needed at GO station
We read with interest a letter to the editor "Bad experience at station" on Feb. 9,authored by Enid S. Aaron of Hamilton. Reading of this letter was timely. As seniors, we too recently ventured out and took advantage of the GO bus option to the Toronto airport. We cannot agree with Ms. Aaron's sentiments and concerns enough. In our experience, our bus pulled us into the Hunter station on Feb. 11 just after midnight. The bus stopped along the south side of the back of the station in the middle - right between James and John streets. With no direction or information from the driver, we too discovered the station doors locked. As our transport home was waiting on Hunter Street, we needed to walk around the building. As we walked toward John Street en route to Hunter, we passed by a number of folks who appeared to be taking shelter, one of whom was urinating into a container of some nature. As we descended some stairs onto John Street with our luggage in tow, we found ourselves in considerable darkness. We walked north on John and then over to Hunter/James where our ride was waiting. In my view, there would be added value if someone were to undertake a review/assessment of the GO bus experience and confirm need for change(s). In the meantime, I would suggest GO have passengers exit closer to James/Hunter at night where lighting is better.
Della and Bruce Smith, Hamilton
Private school misconceptions
Re: Families in other provinces enjoy more school choice than Ontario families (Feb. 11)
I read with interest the Fraser Institute's advocacy for more parental "choice" in education by funding private education through tax dollars. While a thought-provoking piece, I would like to address one misconception the public has about private schools.
The popular belief is that private schools offer a superior education. While the main source of that "superiority" is attributed to smaller classes and pedagogy not practised in the public system, there is really a simpler explanation: private schools have complete autonomy in who they both admit and retain, options not open to public schools by virtue of their mandate. Private schools will say they cannot meet the needs of a particular "clientele" and be done with it, while the public system must work with all manner of students, from the supremely gifted to the most abysmally behaved.
Given that fact of educational life, surely more, not less, resources need to put into a public system that is thus constrained. To weaken public funding further will condemn it to a slow, painful death by a thousand cuts.
Lorne Warwick, Dundas
Redirect bike lane funding
Re: Hamilton public health cutting jobs, programming to balance budget (Feb. 16)
Now that the new budget has been voted on, we see the cuts that were finalized for the city. With the health department having cuts made, I should hope that the desire for bike lanes will be put on the shelf until a time when excess monies appear for them to be reconsidered. There should be more concern for all that the health department does for the citizens here than bike lanes. Sorry to those who want everything that they want, but priorities need to be set and followed. Yes, bicycling can help with your health but the health department does so much more for all.
Suzanne Bacik, Stoney Creek
Why do dogs matter more?
Re: Greensville dog owner devastated by 'horrific' suspected coyote attack (Feb. 17)
One dog is suspected to be eaten by a hungry coyote and it makes the front page of The Spectator. Daily, thousands of pigs are purposely and painfully gassed by humans in CO2 chambers at Fearmans slaughterhouse in Burlington and ... silence. Pigs (and cows, chickens, lambs and fish) are sentient beings. They have their own thoughts, feelings, preferences, desires and needs. Why the discrimination? Why don't their lives matter?
Linda Chenoweth, Hamilton
Letters the voice of the city
Re: Letters section is mean-spirited (Feb. 17)
The letters to the editor serve a very important function in our local community. No, they are not always sunny and bright because our city is not always what we need it to be,
A good politician, city, provincial or federal should read these letters every day. What better way to keep up with what our constituents are thinking and what the priorities are for Hamilton and area?
Obviously, our politicians do not read the letters at the Spec, because they have not paid any attention to local issues. We should have the homeless off the streets, we should have no huge expenses for roads and bike lanes. We would not have hired more staff people so that our councillors wouldn't have so much time to ignore the issues concerning Hamiltonians.
Hopefully, the letters at the Spec will continue to express the thoughts of our city.
Laurel Thompson, Hamilton
Support for NDP-Liberal deal
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh comes under constant criticism from both the left and the right for his party's ongoing support for the Liberal government. As Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives soar in the polls, Mr. Singh's critics wonder why he continues to save a struggling Justin Trudeau. Given that Mr. Poilievre's Tories may be the alternative, Mr. Singh is saving more than the Liberals from defeat, he is saving Canada from "Convoy Conservatives." I hope voters remember that when election time rolls around.
Jim Young, Burlington
Offer more than complaints
Re: Has Ford learned nothing from the Greenbelt scandal? (Feb. 18)
What was the point of MPP Sandy Shaw's piece on thespec.com? In typical NDP fashion, she lists a litany of complaints rejecting the opportunity to put forward anything remotely approaching a solution.
The NDP's recent history, provincial and federal, in Hamilton is risible.
Andrea Horwath, the erstwhile provincial NDP leader, "full of sound and fury" is reduced to a "tim'rous beastie" now that she is in a position to actually do something.
Matthew Green, always at the front of the line when it comes to taking offence, is now to be seen more often wearing a kaffiyeh sharing the stage with rabble rousers than forwarding any initiatives for his own riding.
And of course, there is the sad spectacle of Sarah Jama, whose biased, undisciplined tongue is too much even for the current NDP leader.
The only possible good that could arise from your decision to print Shaw's piece is that it might further confirm the absolute uselessness of the NDP as regards Hamilton.
Neville Newman, Hamilton
CAPTION: If you're worried about higher taxes in the coming years, then it's policing services you have to examine for savings, a reader writes.

Document 61 of 252

Justin Trudeau claims eight out of 10 Canadian households get more back from rebates than they pay in carbon pricing. Is that true?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-claims-eight-out-10-canadian/docview/2929386862/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+claims+eight+out+of+10+Canadian+households+get+more+back+from+rebates+than+they+pay+in+carbon+pricing.+Is+that+true%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-In the lead-up to the next federal election, the Star is taking a look at the leaders of Canada's major federal parties and fact checking some of their most common political slogans and talking points.
Today, the Star is diving into a claim often touted by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau: that eight out of 10 Canadian households get more money back through the carbon rebate than they pay under the federal carbon price.
It's a claim the Trudeau Liberals deploy frequently in defence of the highly-divisive policy, particularly as a rebuttal to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's relentless crusade against the carbon levy.
But is the prime minister providing the full picture? Here's what we know.
What's Trudeau talking about?To start, let's look at the policies behind the prime minister's claims.
Carbon pricing is one of many tools that can be used in the fight against climate change, and is built on the idea that imposing costs on pollution will deter people and businesses from burning fossil fuels and help reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
In Canada, provinces and territories can come up with their own pricing scheme. But if they don't, or if their plan fails to meet certain standards, they must adopt the federal pricing system.
There's two parts to Ottawa's system. The first is a charge on fossil fuels like gasoline and natural gas. That "fuel charge" trickles down to consumers when they pay their home gas bill and fill up their cars. It can also show up in other areas like food costs, though those impacts are less clear.
The second component of the federal carbon pricing scheme is aimed at industries that produce heavy emissions, but that's not what Trudeau is focusing on here.
The rebates Trudeau is referencing used to be called Climate Action Incentive payments, a jargony mouthful the Liberals last week rebranded as the Canada Carbon Rebate.
According to the government, ninety per cent of the money collected from the carbon price goes back to people in the form of quarterly rebates. If you spend less on the so-called carbon tax (like reducing how much you spend on gas), the more likely it is your rebate will cover what you spent, and even exceed it.
The price on carbon will increase each year until 2030, and the rebates increase alongside it.
OK, OK. Just tell me if Trudeau is telling the truth.In the words of Canada's Parliamentary Budget Officer (PBO), Yves Giroux, Trudeau's claim is "accurate, but it's incomplete."
The PBO is an independent and non-partisan office that provides economic and financial analysis to the government.
And it was a March 2023 report from the PBO that came to the conclusion Trudeau is citing, though other economic analyses have also arrived at similar conclusions.
But there are some big caveats at play.
Giroux concluded that most households in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador will get more from the rebates than the amount they pay in the federal carbon price and related GST.
But that conclusion only considers fiscal factors - that is, the actual amount of money Canadians directly or indirectly pay because of the carbon tax, such as buying a tank of gas, paying a household gas bill, or getting something at the grocery store.
The PBO took data on consumption patterns from Statistics Canada, and then subtracted the amount an average family in each province gets from the rebates.
So if you're only looking at the money, and not considering anything else, it's true that most Canadian households that pay the federal fuel charge are seeing a gain.
Some experts caution against interpreting that finding as Canadians making a material amount of extra cash.
"Most people come out about even because the reality is, whether you're a few dollars up or a few dollars down, it is pretty close to a neutral effect for the average person," said Michael Bernstein, the executive director of Clean Prosperity.
Is Trudeau wrong once you include other factors?If you take in the wider picture, yes. The issue here is that while the Liberals are citing a correct conclusion, they're not sharing necessary context about it.
Indeed, the PBO came to an entirely different conclusion when he added the economic impacts of the federal fuel charge - like reductions in employment, income and profits - to his analysis.
"If you think about the transportation sector, they will presumably be paying a higher proportion than other sectors because they rely more heavily on fossil fuels," Giroux told the Star. "That will affect the economic output of the sectors that are more heavily exposed to the carbon tax."
Under those conditions, which Giroux said in his report provides "a more complete picture" of the household impact of the carbon tax, "the net cost increases for all households, reflecting the overall negative economic impact of the federal fuel charge."
It's that framing the Liberals are downplaying, as the Conservatives trumpet it as proof that the politically-charged policy is misguided.
How did the Liberals respond to the PBO's wider analysis?They didn't think it was entirely fair.
That's because Giroux's report didn't factor in the costs of climate change, which prompted one Liberal MP to tell Giroux he was "deeply perplexed" and "shocked" by the PBO's decision.
Giroux told the Star it's "very difficult" to estimate the economic impact of climate change in the short-term. Giroux did, in 2022, analyze the long-term impacts of climate change on Canada's economy and estimated that even if all countries around the world meet their climate commitments, changes in weather patterns could slash Canada's real GDP by 5.8 per cent - $145 billion in 2021 dollars - by 2100.
Giroux said it's hard to look at a period of just a few years and determine what environmental events were caused by climate change alone.
And he said estimating the cost of doing nothing technically falls outside his mandate.
"We're providing information on the cost of what the government is putting forward ... and it's very difficult to cost or to estimate the cost of doing nothing, which is not government policy," Giroux said.
Trevor Tombe, an economist at the University of Calgary, who has previously come to some similar conclusions as the PBO, said the data Statistics Canada collects allows economists to estimate how the carbon tax is affecting Canadians.
But it's harder, Tombe said, to look at the future impacts of climate change because there are simply too many unknown and constantly evolving factors.
So what does this all mean?
"It's up to the government to explain and justify its own actions," said Giroux.
And if it doesn't, the PBO said, Canadians will need to inform themselves.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, right, tours a housing facility as he announces funding for housing in Edmonton on Feb. 21, 2024.

Subject: Economic impact; Costs; Fossil fuels; Emissions; Carbon; Environmental tax; Rebates; Canadians; Financial analysis; Emission standards; Climate change; Households; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Economic impact Costs Environmental tax Rebates Financial analysis

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 62 of 252

Ontario patients pleaded for help after 10,000 were dumped by their clinic. They say Doug Ford left them disappointed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-patients-pleaded-help-after-10-000-were/docview/2930046593/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+patients+pleaded+for+help+after+10%2C000+were+dumped+by+their+clinic.+They+say+Doug+Ford+left+them+disappointed&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As 10,000 residents of Sault Ste. Marie prepare to be dropped by their longtime medical clinic in a stunning move symptomatic of Ontario's chronic doctor shortage, their prospects for finding physicians look bleak.
Community representatives came to the legislature Wednesday seeking help, but left disappointed after Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones touted recent investments in health care without offering practical solutions.
"We knew it was going to be bafflegab," Algoma Steel retiree Burnie Thorp said in an interview after New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles pressed the government on their behalf.
In the legislature's daily question period, Ford and Jones cited $110 million announced Feb. 1 to expand 78 primary care teams in the province - including two in the Sault Ste. Marie area - helping 328,000 more patients get family doctors.
"We are committed to making sure the people of Sault Ste. Marie and across Ontario have access to primary care," Jones said.
Her office later told the Star the funding for the two Sault Ste. Marie clinics would help 4,550 patients get access to care.
That's fewer than half the 10,000 people being cut loose by the Group Health Centre founded by the United Steelworkers union before the advent of medicare.
About 2.3 million Ontarians do not have a primary care physician and the Ontario Medical Association says that number could double in the next two years given a growing population and doctor retirements.
The Group Health Centre - a major medical provider serving 60,000 patients in Sault Ste. Marie, a remote northern city of 73,000 - is struggling with a shortage of doctors because of retirements and departures. There are 31 physicians left.
Centre officials announced in January they had no choice but to remove 10,000 patients from its roster effective the end of May, a move Ontario Medical Association president Dr. Andrew Park called "unprecedented."
"The people in Sault Ste. Marie didn't hear from this minister any sympathy for the crisis that they're facing," Stiles told reporters following question period.
"People have been waiting for the government to swoop in and help figure this out and they don't seem to be focused on it at all."
Raising concerns in the legislature, Stiles was heckled by Sault Ste. Marie MPP Ross Romano, a Progressive Conservative, who accused her of mischaracterizing the situation.
"I will not take lessons from the member from Sault Ste. Marie, who has failed his constituents," she shot back.
Thorp said he never imagined he'd be on the hunt for a primary care clinic at the age of 77 after 35 years of going to the Group Health Centre, which has several locations offering a wide variety of services.
"I might look a little old to be an orphan, but I am an orphan ... you would expect to have health care continuing through your lifetime," added Thorp, president of the Steelworkers Organization of Active Retirees.
"We are obviously people who pay taxes. We have paid for this health care and so we think it is our right."
Thorp said he has been told he will be provided with a year's worth of prescription renewals before he is taken off the roster.
"That's taken a little bit of the pressure off, but around December I'm going to start thinking, 'Holy crap, where am I going to get these prescriptions?'"
Mike Da Prat, president of Steelworkers local 2251 in the Soo, who is also being taken off the clinic roster, told a news conference he was given a list of walk-in clinics in the area to try.
The Group Health Centre said it had been approaching the government for help over the last few months before issuing an open letter to the community stating patients whose doctors have "recently retired or left their practices" will have to seek treatment elsewhere.
"We recognize the profound effect this will have."
Because older doctors are retiring, there will be more impact on older patients, said Da Prat.
The health minister's office said it got just over a week's notice of the plans to chop patients from the roster, leaving little time to find replacement physicians.
CAPTION: Minister of Health Sylvia Jones during question period in the Ontario legislature on Feb. 20, 2024.

Ford government says law to punish local politicians who harass staff coming soon

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-says-law-punish-local-politicians/docview/2930046586/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+says+law+to+punish+local+politicians+who+harass+staff+coming+soon&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government says new legislation that could see local politicians thrown out of office for workplace harassment should be introduced before the summer.
Speaking to reporters at Queen's Park on Wednesday, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra called it "a very important piece of legislation. It has to be constitutional, but has to actually achieve the results that I think that everybody's asking for. So absolutely, we are going to bring something forward and continue to work" with municipalities.
Asked if the bill could come before the legislature rises in June, Calandra said: "I don't see why not."
The Ford government previously voted down a private member's bill from Liberal MPP Stephen Blais (Orléans), saying it would leave voters to decide who to remove from office at election time.
But with more than 200 municipalities supporting the idea and the non-partisan advocacy group The Women of Ontario Say No keeping the issue in the spotlight, last month Calandra changed course, promising the government would create "airtight" legislation.
Municipalities across the province have struggled with situations where elected officials have been found to have committed workplace harassment, but there is little that can be done beyond docking pay.
Emily McIntosh, of The Women of Ontario Say No, said removal from office is a must in any legislation.
"The premise is simple," she said. "You should go to work and you should not be subjected to abuse. We are asking municipally elected representatives to be held to the very same standard as every other worker in Ontario. Elected representatives are not above the people - they are of the people."
Blais recounted his own days on Ottawa council, where fellow politician Rick Chiarelli was found to have harassed and abused staff and job applicants for years.
His bill would have allowed for politicians to be removed from office after findings of inappropriate behaviour by an integrity commissioner and a judge's review.
"We absolutely need this change," added Aurora Coun. Rachel Gilliland. "No council is immune to it."
David Arbuckle, who is executive director of the Association of Municipal Managers, Clerks and Treasurers of Ontario, said "while most councillor-staff relationships and interactions are positive, respectful and focused on collaboration to achieve community goals, our members are telling us that the overall relationship between elected officials and professional municipal staff is increasingly confrontational" and that can impact staff recruitment and retention.
Farah Grozelle is a small-business owner in Bradford who has been working with The Women of Ontario Say No.
"A majority government can pass legislation in less than four weeks," she said. "It is now the winter of 2024, and here we are without accountability structures in place with respect to the safety and well-being of individuals and communities. I know we can do better, now."
Leaders of the opposition parties have also thrown their support behind such legislation.
"We need to close these loopholes," NDP Leader Marit Stiles told a press conference Wednesday morning at Queen's Park with Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner.
"We need transparency, accountability for those who do the wrong thing," Crombie added. "We know politics can be a nasty business, but it doesn't have to be."
McIntosh said she'd also like to eventually see legislation covering provincial politicians.
With files from Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Emily McIntosh is shown at the Barrie City Hall on March 18, 2023.
CREDIT: Lauren Nicol, Special to the Star

Document 64 of 252

'Very disappointed': Ford government says international student cap will hurt economy, calls out Ottawa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/very-disappointed-ford-government-says/docview/2930046086/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Very+disappointed%27%3A+Ford+government+says+international+student+cap+will+hurt+economy%2C+calls+out+Ottawa&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa did not consult with the provinces before announcing caps on international students, and it's not just colleges and universities that will feel the impact - Ontario's economy will too, says Post-Secondary Minister Jill Dunlop.
"We're very disappointed with the federal government," Dunlop said in the legislature on Wednesday.
She said she's heard from fellow MPPs and other ministries about "the impact this is going to have on our economy" and that the tourism sector in particular "is going to be devastated without these students" given the number of post-graduation work permits will drop.
"That's why, when we're working with the federal government on the allocation, we will be looking at the labour market needs across regions in Ontario as a number one priority."
Also, "ensuring that there is guaranteed housing for international students coming to our campus will be a priority, and ensuring that the economic needs of each region are a concern as well. There is going to be an impact across Ontario and the federal government did absolutely no consultations with the provinces or with the sector."
Last month, the federal immigration minister - feeling the pressure of the growing housing crisis - chopped the number of new study permits issued to international students by 35 per cent, with spots to be allotted based on each province's population. Ontario, where more than half of Canada's current international students study, will see a 50 per cent drop.
New Democrat MPP Peggy Sattler (London West), her party's post-secondary critic, said "colleges and universities are bracing for the impact of a 50 per cent reduction in international study permits at a time when the sector is already at a (financial) breaking point."
She said the province has seen decades of "chronic underfunding by this government and the Liberals before them, which has put our post-secondary institutions starved for resources and now they are on the brink," with 10 of 23 schools running deficits.
Premier Doug Ford has already ruled out a tuition increase this fall for domestic students - as recommended by the government's blue ribbon panel - but it remains unknown if Ontario will boost funding for colleges and universities as the panel also urged.
"We will be announcing our path forward shortly," Dunlop said.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), his party's colleges and universities critic, said schools "have been suffering for years" and the government should have already increased funding.
"There's not a moment to lose," he said, adding schools were forced to turn to international students to make up for gaps in funding - they pay three to four times the tuition of domestic students - but that they "should not be the piggy bank."
"What I really worry about is with a cap on international students, without the kind of funding that universities are asking, without the support that they need, what we're going to see is universities have no choice but to either cut back on programs, cut back on staff, or cut back on student services."
CAPTION: Jill Dunlop, Ontario's minister of colleges and universities, attends question period at the Ontario Legislature on Nov. 28, 2023.

Document 65 of 252

Legislature resumes with plan to Get It Done: Bill will 'accelerate construction of transit, housing and infrastructure projects,' minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/legislature-resumes-with-plan-get-done/docview/2928800080/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Legislature+resumes+with+plan+to+Get+It+Done%3A+Bill+will+%27accelerate+construction+of+transit%2C+housing+and+infrastructure+projects%2C%27+minister+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Progressive Conservative government introduced an omnibus bill Tuesday titled the Get It Done Act, borrowing from their 2022 election slogan, but opposition parties said the legislation will not actually accomplish much.
One part of the bill relates to undoing a previous policy, while two other sections purport to tie a future government's hands on carbon taxes and road tolls, though they could be easily undone themselves.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria introduced the bill Tuesday, on the legislature's first day in session since December.
"The Get It Done Act will allow Ontario to accelerate construction of transit, housing and infrastructure projects that we need to support our growing population," he said. "This bill will also make life more affordable for families and businesses across the province."
The bill would "streamline" certain environmental assessments to allow the government to build infrastructure such as roads more quickly. It would also enable automatic licence plate renewals, a move that follows the government eliminating the fees for those renewals several years ago.
As well, the legislation includes changes to some urban boundaries that municipalities requested.
The two pieces of the omnibus legislation announced to the most fanfare last week are both politically charged items the opposition parties have dismissed as meaningless and performative.
One headline-grabbing item would require any future government to put any new provincial carbon pricing system to a referendum.
It would not affect the federal carbon tax and the legislation could be repealed by a future government. Ford spent time during the announcement in Mississauga last week to attack the former mayor of that city, now Ontario Liberal Leader, Bonnie Crombie.

NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the bill's title is a misnomer.
"This is a sign that the government is not actually getting anything done," Stiles said. "They've spent more time in the last year reversing course and they haven't actually come up with practical solutions that will make life easier for people."
Crombie said letting municipalities decide the urban boundaries and official plans is the right move.
"We need the municipalities to lead on this," the Liberal leader said. "We need the municipalities to let us know if they can accommodate their growth numbers within their municipal boundaries."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Allison Jones and Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 66 of 252

Doug Ford refuses to apologize over wage-capping legislation, as MPPs return to Queen Park

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-refuses-apologize-over-wage-capping/docview/2929195728/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+refuses+to+apologize+over+wage-capping+legislation%2C+as+MPPs+return+to+Queen+Park&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was supposed to be a fresh start, but the past kept coming back to haunt Premier Doug Ford as MPPs returned to the legislature following the winter break.
A defiant Ford refused to apologize to thousands of nurses, teachers and other public servants who had their wages capped under his controversial Bill 124.
While he is backtracking on the law deemed unconstitutional by the courts last week, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said he has some explaining to do to workers for limiting their raises to one per cent annually.
"Will he apologize to Ontario's hard-working nurses, PSWs, teachers, educational assistants and all the public sector workers for suppressing their wages with Bill 124?" demanded Stiles.
"Look what we've done for the health-care workers, which we think the world of - we think the world of the nurses," shot back Ford, declining to express any contrition.
The forthcoming repeal of Bill 124 came against the backdrop of the Progressive Conservatives tabling separate omnibus legislation - dubbed the Get It Done Act after the 2022 campaign slogan.
It is designed to reboot a PC government that spent the past six months under a cloud due to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
The bill fast-tracks construction of highways and other infrastructure by streamlining environmental assessments, bans tolls on all roads except Hwy. 407, eliminates the need for licence plate renewals, undoes the government's unilateral changes to a dozen cities' official plans and attempts to force a future premier to hold a referendum on any carbon-pricing scheme.
Mocked by Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie as "another attempt to get it undone," the legislation corrects some previous mistakes the Tories made.
"This government is one that just reverses its decisions and its proposals. They need to do a lot more homework," said Crombie, who won the Liberal leadership on Dec. 2.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, whose caucus doubled in size with new MPP Aislinn Clancy taking her seat in the house Tuesday, derided Ford's legislation as the "Getting It Done Wrong Act."
"It's performative politics at its worst, distracting from the Ford government's failure in addressing the housing crisis, and the fact that they've made access to health care worse, people are sick and tired of having to wait for long times and emergency departments not having access to primary health care, not having access to mental health care," said Schreiner.
The Liberals made sport of former Ford cabinet minister Parm Gill suddenly resigning four weeks ago to join federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's team.
With a Milton byelection looming later this year to fill Gill's seat, the provincial Tories are once again promising to bring all-day GO trains there.
"The Milton service is only happening because one of their MPPs wanted to escape from the premier," said Liberal MPP Andrea Hazell (Scarborough-Guildwood).
"It appears that the only way to get this government to invest in a riding is to have the PC MPP jump ship from their caucus," said Hazell.
Crombie, who does not have a seat in the legislature, said she has not ruled out running in Milton.
"I'm taking a very serious look at it. I'm very familiar with their issues and I think very strongly about the need for all day GO train service on the Milton line," said the former Mississauga mayor.
When asked about the referendum portion of the bill - which is the Tories' attempt to tie Crombie, a former Liberal MP, to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon tax - she dismissed it as a "stunt ... to deflect from what's going on with the Conservative caucus with the reversals, the scandals, the insider deals."
Ontarians were exempt from paying the federal levy until Ford cancelled the previous provincial Liberal government's cap-and-trade alliance with Quebec and California in 2018, which costs the treasury $2 billion in lost revenue annually.
The Tories bristled when asked if there would be any retroactive plebiscites on the two carbon pricing schemes they introduced in 2022 and last year - the emission performance standards program that targets industrial polluters and the clean energy credit registry cap-and-trade system.
"We've worked exclusively on removing carbon taxes," insisted Government House Leader Paul Calandra before fleeing reporters to avoid answering more questions about the government's own greenhouse gas reduction efforts.
According to the Get It Done Act, any future premier would have to hold a referendum that "authorizes the carbon pricing program" before implementing it.
"A referendum question must be clear, concise and impartial in its wording and must be capable of being answered in the affirmative or the negative," the legislation says.
But the bill suggests no plebiscite would be binding on a new administration.
"A referendum shall not be interpreted to require the executive council of a subsequent government formed by another party to establish a carbon pricing program or give authority to establish a carbon pricing program as described in the referendum question."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford during question period in the Ontario Legislature on Feb. 20, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Legislation; Referendums; Legislatures; Emissions trading; Carbon; Nurses; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Emissions trading

Document 67 of 252

'Untenable': Halton police board chair discusses poor state of Milton courthouse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/untenable-halton-police-board-chair-discusses/docview/2928619371/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Untenable%27%3A+Halton+police+board+chair+discusses+poor+state+of+Milton+courthouse&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The chair of the Halton police board is calling on Ontario's attorney general to approve the building of a new Halton courthouse arguing that the one in Milton is beyond repair.
In a letter sent to Doug Downey and posted on X, formerly Twitter, on Tuesday, Feb. 13, chair Jeff Knoll issued a formal appeal concerning the long-standing issues plaguing the 491 Steeles Ave. E., facility, which he says have significantly impacted the administration of justice in Halton, as well as the health and safety of police, ministry staff, judges, legal professionals, accused persons, civil parties, witnesses, victims and the general public.
"The Milton courthouse's deficiencies have been an open secret, underscored by repeated closures due to mould, asbestos, and structural concerns," said Knoll in his letter.
"These issues have not only posed health and safety risks but have also led to significant delays in the judicial process, affecting the rights of those awaiting trial. The temporary solutions applied thus far have been akin to Band-Aid fixes, failing to address the root causes of the courthouse' inadequacies."
He said the situation has been further aggravated by the courthouse's inability to safely and efficiently manage the custody and transport of accused persons.
Chief Stephen Tanner of the Halton police expanded on these issues.
He said the cells in the lower part of the courthouse are no longer suitable given present-day standards.
"They don't provide the proper privacy or security for prisoners. There are always complications with keeping prisoners apart. Sometimes this is needed for gender-based reasons, sometimes this is needed for young-offender reasons," said Tanner.
"The cell area in that courthouse was built for the justice system in the '60s and it doesn't meet the needs of the 2020s."
When it comes to moving accused persons around the building, Tanner said officers must get into a very small, confined elevator with a potentially dangerous individual.
If the elevator is not working, Tanner said, the officers face issues in having to move the accused through public stairwells and hallways.
"There aren't even secure hallways to move people through in lots of cases, so a dangerous offender could come face to face with a witness or jurors or a victim, members of the victim's family or the judge," said Tanner.
The police chief argued the building is well past its prime and said he doesn't believe it is salvageable.
He went on to say the province should not invest significant funds on additional renovations, upgrades or add-ons for this building, but instead build a new courthouse for Halton that is properly scaled for the growth expected to take place in the community.
Knoll agrees, stating in his letter that a modern facility that can meet current and future demands is needed.
He noted the province gave the community hope by announcing plans for a new courthouse project on June 21, 2017, but this dream was dashed when the project was cancelled on May 21, 2020, by the Doug Ford administration.
"This decision has left us with a facility that is not only insufficient but increasingly untenable," said Knoll.
"Given these pressing issues, we urge your office to reconsider and reinstate the previously announced courthouse project in Oakville. The land designated for this project (behind Oakville Trafalgar Memorial Hospital) remains owned by the province and available for development."
Knoll said his letter is not merely a request for infrastructure investment but a plea for a commitment to the principles of justice, safety, and accessibility for the people of Halton Region.
While the previous courthouse plan called for a seven-storey, 45,000-square-foot building with a price tag of somewhere between $200 million and $499 million, Tanner said this may have been too grandiose and called for something more appropriately scaled.
When reached for comment, ministry spokesperson Keesha Seaton said that as part of the "Justice Accelerated Strategy," the government has made significant investments to upgrade security, technology, and building functionality at the Milton and Burlington courthouses.
She also said that in the fall of 2023 the attorney general, deputy attorney general and several stakeholders met at the Milton courthouse to discuss current and future challenges, growth pressures in the Halton Region, and to see the facilities firsthand.
"We will continue to work with our partners in Halton Region on the immediate and long-term needs in Milton," said Seaton.
CAPTION: Milton's aging courthouse on Steele's Avenue, home to the Superior Court of Justice and Ontario Court of Justice, was closed for several months in 2021 for renovations and mould mitigation, that required sending cases elsewhere while work was completed.

Feb. 20: Bloated HPS budget won't improve policing, proper police presence lacking in Flamborough and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-20-bloated-hps-budget-wont-improve-policing/docview/2928619070/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+20%3A+Bloated+HPS+budget+won%27t+improve+policing%2C+proper+police+presence+lacking+in+Flamborough+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You can't have it both ways
Re: Hamilton council trims budget hike to 5.79 per cent (Feb. 16)
I had previously known the phrase, "If a politician promises to cut your taxes and also promises no cuts to services, then they are a liar." I think this phrase needs to be updated to, "If a municipal politician promises to keep your tax increase as small as possible and also promises no cuts to important services, minimizing the substantial effects of inflation and downloading of new costs from the provincial government, then, while that is a different flavour of a lie, they are still also a liar. "
Roberto Quinlan, Dundas
Proper police presence lacking
As a resident of Flamborough, I am looking forward to an increase in police presence in an area of the city that has become a crime free-for-all due to the meagre presence of Hamilton police.
We have four officers assigned on each day and night shift to police the entirety of Flamborough. As a resident of the supercity of Hamilton - a gift from the Progressive Conservatives - I and all residents of this city are entitled to a proper police presence and presently I and all residents of Flamborough do not receive this service, despite being the bearers of a huge amount of tax dollars to the city coffers.
I will continue to support the Hamilton Police Service in their endeavours to correct this lack of service and the need to provide tax dollars to get this done.
Theresa Walmsley, Waterdown
Money won't improve policing
The belief that an increased police budget makes the city a safer place has been proven false time and time again. This is not an opinion, it is fact. Why anti-tax councillors do not see this connection is a mystery.
The council went on to pass the 2024 city budget and, with it, an increased tax rate to accommodate this bloated HPS budget (as well as provincial downloading, deferred spending by past councils, inflation, etc.).
Any councillor who protests against a rise in taxes has no business simultaneously supporting an increased, unquestioned budget for the police service.
Kris Gadjanski, Dundas
Negativity loses my vote
Re: Pierre Poilievre can dish it out. Why can't he take it? (Feb. 12)
I wanted to thank Susan Delacourt's opinion piece on the federal opposition leader. He tries to be so on point and seems so serious about what he says, but the hypocrisy just oozes out of him every time I see him speak. Justin Trudeau is the reason for all the ills in the country according to Pierre Poilievre.
Being a naysayer and spouting negativity at every turn isn't ever going to get my vote. What will he do if the next election doesn't go his way? Will he quote Trump and blame others for rigging the election? I can hear him already, blaming everyone else but himself.
When you refuse to take reporters' questions, I wonder what you have to hide.
Suzanne Bacik, Stoney Creek
Traffic is already a nightmare
Re: Parking fuels backlash against Stoney Creek affordable-housing pitch (Feb. 15)
We live in Village Green in Stoney Creek, a stone's throw away from these proposed apartment units. We only have one entrance and one exit out of Village Green. The traffic now is a nightmare. We have a stoplight at Battlefield Drive and when it is red, we cannot make our turn onto King Street with all the cars backed up and we wait an entire light change to turn left from King into our complex as the traffic is non stop - an advanced green signal would help.
These apartment units will only add more congestion into our area, as most have at least one or two vehicles. The proposed area off of Lake Avenue makes much more sense. Lake Avenue is two lanes, not one, and it has easy access to Centennial Parkway.
We had a Tim Hortons downtown but it closed and we had an ice cream shop that closed. It makes me laugh to suggest that they would stay open with these proposed apartment buildings. Are these new residents going to have a coffee and ice cream every day?
Lack of parking was a big issue and now we're taking away more parking? The Village Restaurant has a very small parking lot in behind, the on-street parking is limited and the municipal lot is a great alternative. We cannot afford to have any of those spaces taken away. Building these apartment buildings is such a bad idea for this area.
Linda C. Arnold, Stoney Creek
Americans wouldn't stand for it
Recently, the environment minister of Canada was asked why the Americans don't have a carbon tax applied to their everyday lives. As a politician, he danced around the question and answer.
The answer is, if any one person or political party were to suggest such a thing to the American people, riots would ensue and the person and party would be run out of town.
Ralph Corning, Stoney Creek
CAPTION: Hamilton's city council landed on a 5.79 per cent budget increase for 2024.

Document 69 of 252

Doug Ford hopes to finally 'Get It Done' when legislature resumes Tuesday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-hopes-finally-get-done-when-legislature/docview/2928510984/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+hopes+to+finally+%27Get+It+Done%27+when+legislature+resumes+Tuesday&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some 628 days after being re-elected on a campaign slogan to "Get It Done," Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are finally enshrining that promise in legislation.
As MPPs return to Queen's Park on Tuesday for the spring session, Ford will table the Get It Done Act, an omnibus bill the Tories hope will expedite infrastructure construction, address cost-of-living concerns - and deflect attention from their Greenbelt land swap scandal.
In hearkening back to his June 2, 2022 victory, the premier also signalled he is looking ahead to the 2026 election and the threat posed by new Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
As part of the Get It Done bill, any future government would be required to hold a referendum before implementing a new provincial carbon pricing program.
"People know where I stand on the carbon tax. I've opposed it from the very beginning. My record couldn't be more different from Bonnie Crombie," Ford said last week in Mississauga.
"She's the queen of carbon tax," he said, in apparent reference to Crombie once being a Liberal MP, although years before Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was elected in 2015 and imposed carbon pricing.
Ford did not mention that Ontarians were exempt from paying the federal levy until he cancelled the previous provincial Liberal government's cap-and-trade alliance with Quebec and California in 2018, costing the provincial treasury $2 billion annually.
Nor did he acknowledge that his Tory government slapped an industrial carbon tax on big polluters - known as the emission performance standards program - in 2022 and last year launched the clean energy credit registry that is effectively a cap-and-trade system.
Noting no opposition party is proposing a provincial carbon tax, Crombie said Monday that "Desperate Doug is pulling out all the stops to distract from his failures, flip-flops, scandals and the RCMP's criminal investigation."
"That's why he is spending all of his time talking about me. He seems to have conceded the next election and wants to bind what the next Liberal government may or may not even do," said the Grit leader, who is not an MPP and has no immediate plans to seek a seat in the legislature.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles told the Star's editorial board that Ford is flailing as he scrambles to shift the focus away from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt debacle currently under RCMP investigation, among other controversies.
"They keep making these mistakes. So much money and so much time is wasted. It's heartbreaking actually," said Stiles, pointing to the Tories' U-turn last week on the Bill 124 wage cap for public servants after losing a court challenge.
"When I think of the public service, think of how many people are wasting their time putting in policies that (Ford) is just going to flip-flop on again," the New Democratic chief said, shaking her head.
"There's a cost to all of this - for all of us."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner dismissed Ford's proposed referendum bill as "a performative, political moment to distract from the failures in housing and health care."
"We're talking about a federal carbon tax that isn't even provincial jurisdiction, for one thing, and, two, he knows as well as anyone he can do whatever he wants, it's not going to prevent a future government from overturning the legislation," said Schreiner.
"So it's purely performative and not even in his jurisdiction," he said.
Schreiner will have company on his benches Tuesday for the first time in the history of the Green Party of Ontario.
That's because he will be joined by a second Green MPP, Aislinn Clancy, who easily won the Nov. 30 Kitchener Centre byelection in a riding that had been held by the New Democrats.
Aside from the carbon tax referendum scheme, the Get It Done Act will also freeze any possible increase for drivers' licence fees, eliminate the need for licence plate renewals, outlaw the possibility of new tolls on all freeways except Hwy. 407 and streamline environmental assessments so highways, sewers and electricity infrastructure can be built faster.
The licence plate change comes after the Star revealed last week there are more than one million expired plates on Ontario roads.
In the wake of the renewal fee of up to $120 per vehicle being scrapped in 2022, many motorists incorrectly believed they didn't keep plates up to date, leading to a surge in fines of as much as $1,000 for driving with invalid plates.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford delivers remarks at the opening ceremonies of the Canadian International Auto Show in Toronto on Feb. 16, 2024.

Subject: Jurisdiction; Referendums; Emissions trading; Scandals; Carbon; Roads & highways; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Emissions trading Environmental tax

Title: Doug Ford hopes to finally 'Get It Done' when legislature resumes Tuesday
