Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/2928510950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You can't have
it both ways
Hamilton council trims budget hike to 5.79 per cent (Feb. 16)
I had previously known the phrase, "If a politician promises to cut your taxes and also promises no cuts to services, then they are a liar." I think this phrase needs to be updated to, "If a municipal politician promises to keep your tax increase as small as possible and also promises no cuts to important services, minimizing the substantial effects of inflation and downloading of new costs from the provincial government, then, while that is a different flavour of a lie, they are still also a liar."
Roberto Quinlan, Dundas

improve policing
The belief that an increased police budget makes the city a safer place has been proven false time and time again. This is not an opinion, it is fact. Why anti-tax councillors do not see this connection is a mystery.

Kris Gadjanski, Dundas

presence lacking

As a resident of the supercity of Hamilton - a gift from the Progressive Conservatives - I and all residents of this city are entitled to a proper police presence and presently I and all residents of Flamborough do not receive this service, despite being the bearers of a huge amount of tax dollars to the city coffers.
I will continue to support the Hamilton Police Service in their endeavours to correct this lack of service and the need to provide tax dollars to get this done.
Theresa Walmsley, Waterdown
Negativity
loses my vote
Poilievre can dish it out
but can't he take it (Feb. 12)
I wanted to thank Susan Delacourt's opinion piece on the federal opposition leader. He tries to be so on point and seems so serious about what he says, but the hypocrisy just oozes out of him every time I see him speak. Justin Trudeau is the reason for all the ills in the country, according to Pierre Poilievre.
Being a naysayer and spouting negativity at every turn isn't ever going to get my vote. What will he do if the next election doesn't go his way? Will he quote Trump and blame others for rigging the election? I can hear him already, blaming everyone else but himself.
When you refuse to take reporters' questions, I wonder what you have to hide.
Suzanne Bacik, Stoney Creek
Traffic is already
a nightmare
Parking fuels backlash against Stoney Creek affordable-housing pitch (Feb. 15)
We live in Village Green in Stoney Creek, a stone's throw away from these proposed apartment units.
We only have one entrance and one exit out of Village Green.
The traffic now is a nightmare. We have a stoplight at Battlefield Drive and when it is red, we cannot make our turn onto King Street with all the cars backed up and we wait an entire light change to turn left from King into our complex as the traffic is non-stop - an advanced green signal would help.
These apartment units will only add more congestion into our area, as most have at least one or two vehicles.
The proposed area off of Lake Avenue makes much more sense. Lake Avenue is two lanes, not one, and it has easy access to Centennial Parkway.
We had a Tim Hortons downtown, but it closed and we had an ice-cream shop that closed. It makes me laugh to suggest that they would stay open with these proposed apartment buildings. Are these new residents going to have a coffee and ice cream every day?
Lack of parking was a big issue and now we're taking away more parking?
The Village Restaurant has a very small parking lot in behind, the on-street parking is limited and the municipal lot is a great alternative. We cannot afford to have any of those spaces taken away. Building these apartment buildings is such a bad idea for this area.
Linda C. Arnold, Stoney Creek

Document 71 of 252

Has Ford learned nothing from the Greenbelt scandal?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/has-ford-learned-nothing-greenbelt-scandal/docview/2928015653/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Has+Ford+learned+nothing+from+the+Greenbelt+scandal%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's Conservatives sure love a holiday.
After several months and countless new Conservative scandals, Ontario's legislature is finally resuming on Tuesday, Feb. 20, and I couldn't be more relieved to get back.
Since the Conservatives made us rise for the winter break, we've seen Ford give away ServiceOntario contracts to Staples, we've seen endless flip-flopping on countless Conservative policy proposals, and we've seen a huge win for workers as Ford was forced to send Bill 124 to the dustbin of history, where it belongs.
But while Ford has been hiding from scandal after scandal, things have been getting tough for people in Hamilton.
During the past few months, I've been out in our community talking to people about what they would like to see as priorities in this upcoming legislative session.
Let me tell you - it's not enriching Conservative donors.
People are feeling like the health-care and social services that we've always relied on are starting to slip away. They're exhausted after long wait times in the ER, trying to get health care for an ailing parent. They're turning over every stone trying to find mental-health care for their children. They're concerned about being priced out of Hamilton as housing costs soar.
People are focused on housing, health care and the cost of living. And that's what the Ontario NDP is laser-focused on as well - bringing forward positive solutions to the crises we face, while continuing to hold this government to account.
We're expecting more policy reversals from Ford, as well as the introduction of a new bill that might see him trying to push through the urban boundary expansions once again, despite strong local opposition and a Ford reversal last fall.
Has Ford learned nothing from the Greenbelt scandal? Does he ever listen to our community? People in Hamilton raised their voices loud and clear last fall to protect our farmland and conservation areas - and I know they'll join me if Ford thinks he can pull something like that again.
As I head back to Queen's Park on Tuesday, I'll be focused on solutions and policies that can make a meaningful difference in people's lives and protect our precious green space.
Ford might not be listening, but my door is always open. My team will be working hard to bring your priorities straight to the premier, and I encourage you to join us.
Together, we can protect our province for generations to come.
CAPTION: The Ontario Legislative Assembly resumes sitting Tuesday.
CREDIT: Sandy Shaw

Subject: Conservatism

Document 72 of 252

Hamilton council whittled tax increase by pushing problems down the road

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-council-whittled-tax-increase-pushing/docview/2927856732/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+council+whittled+tax+increase+by+pushing+problems+down+the+road&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the end, council got the tax increase down to 5.79 per cent.
That's a whole lot less than the daunting 14.2 per cent bump we first heard we might be facing in September, to be sure. It's even less than the 7.9 per cent we heard about a few weeks ago when Mayor Andrea Horwath presented her draft budget.
Is this therefore a triumph? A glorious feat of fiscal restraint that brought the final number down to less than half the initial prediction?
Hardly.
While the final number is certainly down from those earlier worst-case-scenario figures, it can't hide the fact that this group increased spending by a record 19.5 per cent in one year. That's astonishing. As Coun. Brad Clark points out, it added something like 165 new full-time employees this year after adding more than 200 last year, which is miles ahead of the pace of previous councils.
Some councillors will argue that all this was driven by provincial downloads that had to be picked up by the city. It's Doug Ford's fault.
There's no doubt that was a factor. Nobody disputes that. But provincial downloads affect all Ontario cities, not just Hamilton. We're not specially targeted by Queen's Park. Every municipality has similar challenges. Yet few others were ever staring at an increase of greater than 14 per cent.
So don't be fooled by the final number being considerably lower than that. The enormous spending that created the issue hasn't been eliminated or corrected, it's just been camouflaged behind a thick barrier of reserves, new debt and a few trims.
That isn't a fix. It's a temporary firewall built with smoke and mirrors.
"It's unsustainable," says Coun. Tom Jackson who was one of six (along with Clark, Matt Francis, Jeff Beattie, Mike Spadafora and Mark Tadeson) who voted against the budget.
Indeed.
Clark says this council is now on pace to increase property tax rates by 31 per cent by the end of this term.
If it doesn't feel quite that painful to you yet, remember that the hundreds of millions in reserves that are being used work a little like a coupon for taxpayers. They get you the product without forcing you to pay full price.
But when the coupons expire?
"When we run out of those funds, the taxes go up dramatically," Clark says.
What council's really done is just push the problem down the road.
Some weeks ago, we learned next year's tax increase is already slated to be 7.7 per cent. That's with reserves being used again. On Thursday, we learned that since some of the whittling to get this year's number down was really just a deferring of expenses, the 2025 number is actually closer to 8.1 per cent.
That's the starting point.
"We can't spend a dollar next year and we're already at 8.1," Spadafora says.
So what do you think, is this group going to lock up the chequebook and spend absolutely nothing for 12 months? Operate on a strict dollar-in-dollar-out philosophy?
And when the reserves run out? To remember what happens then, go back a few paragraphs and reread what Clark says.
A big hit is coming. It might it be next year or it could be put off by a year or two with more deferrals. Maybe even just long enough to get beyond the next election which would leave quite a treat for the next council.
If you're trying to remain optimistic and thinking, we just have to get past this tough year or two and then everything will return to normal, think again. The 2026 hike is already expected to be 6.5 per cent and 2027 is at 5.23. That's before one more dollar is added. This is the new normal.
The simple truth is, each year's increase gets piled onto the increases of the years before, creating a bigger and bigger mountain of expenses. We're eventually going have to pay for all this.
So what can council do?
If it wants to show that keeping tax increases down is more than just a cheap talking point, the bar for what will be added to the city's bottom line should be extraordinarily high beginning right now. Nice to haves shouldn't do it. Must haves only. No pet projects. No bells and whistles. Only the absolute necessities.
Better yet, take some guidance from Beattie who said what council's done to this point is simply pick the low-hanging fruit.
"Somebody needs to get out the ladder and go to the top of the tree," he says.
Amen.
CAPTION: Hamilton city council whittled the tax increase to 5.79 per cent but set itself up for even bigger challenges down the road.

Subject: Tax rates; Councils; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax rates Tax increases

Document 73 of 252

More funding is needed for health-care ills

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/more-funding-is-needed-health-care-ills/docview/2927815533/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+funding+is+needed+for+health-care+ills&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To his credit, Premier Doug Ford looks to have accepted a couple of home truths. One, Ontarians prefer results to rancour between levels of government. Two, they want the health-care crisis addressed in a big way and in a hurry.
The recently announced agreement with the federal government that will see a $3.1-billion increase in health-care funding over three years is good news. The money should enable the Ford government to create new primary-care teams to increase access to family doctors, see the addition of hundreds of family physicians and nurse practitioners and thousands of new nurses and personal support workers.
The funding is part of an $8.4-billion injection over 10 years agreed to between Ottawa and the Ford government.
Under that deal, Ontario agrees to address federal priorities by improving access to family health services, including in rural and remote areas; boosting the health workforce; providing better access to timely mental health, substance abuse and addictions services; and allowing individuals access to their electronic health information shared between health professionals.
The balance of the promised money will be contingent on meeting timelines and targets.
The federal government had allotted $25 billion over 10 years for bilateral deals with provinces - Ontario is the fifth to sign on - to address the four federal priorities.
Reports from across the health-care spectrum have revealed crises in pediatric care, long-term care, emergency care, mental-health care and access to family doctors.
A system at the breaking point is the result of decades of failure by successive provincial and federal governments of various parties to make the necessary investments in infrastructure and personnel to meet the needs of an aging and growing population.
As of 2023, Canada had one of the lowest hospital-bed-to-patient ratios in the OECD (Organization for Economic Co-ordination and Development).
In health care, the system is interconnected and problems inevitably pile up downstream.
Too few family doctors means more people showing up in ERs. Too few beds results in extended wait times to see a doctor and delays in getting patients to surgical or medical wards. Rather than being back on the road, paramedics who bring patients in must stay with them until they can be seen.
As Ontario's acting auditor general recently reported, the rising delays can mean a matter of life or death.
"When emergency departments are unable to operate efficiently and effectively, patients may not receive timely and high-quality care, which can lead to negative or even fatal health outcomes," wrote Nick Stavropoulos.
In the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, targeting mental-health care is particularly laudable.
Dr. Eileen de Villa, Toronto's medical officer of health, has reported that student mental health declined sharply in the aftermath of pandemic, with visits to ERs related to self-harm among children and youth rising 30 per cent in the first year afterwards.
Prior to the pandemic, a strained health-care system was already struggling to meet demand, with huge backlogs and waiting lists. The social isolation and stresses of COVID-19 only made it worse.
Studies in the Canadian Medical Association Journal found the number of Canadian adolescents reporting self-harm or suicidal thoughts rose significantly. "If a kid seeks help, we can't wait 10 months to get a kid care," Dr. Natasha Saunders, a pediatrician at the Hospital for Sick Children, said last year.
Ford's office recently said the premier is focused on delivering on commitments in policy areas that matter to Ontarians. Not much matters more than health care.
To be sure, a complex, long-underfunded system will not be fixed in one fell swoop. Some of the remedies funded by the new cash - like new training spots for doctors - will only be felt years from now. So this funding - and the balance which is owed - is only part of the solutions needed to relieve the pressures on a strained system.
But the federal-provincial agreement is an important part of the long-term effort to rebuild capacity and prepare for the future.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Letters section is mean spirited It would appear the nastiness ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-section-is-mean-spirited-would-appear/docview/2927815084/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+section+is+mean+spirited+It+would+appear+the+nastiness+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.20&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Letters section is mean-spirited
It would appear the nastiness of Facebook and social media commentators is rearing its head in the letters to the editor. The vitriol between police budgets, LRT, two-way street conversion and - Lord help us - bike lanes makes the letters to the editors section a depressing morning stop. Not only have the social media giants stolen most of the ad revenue our newspapers and TV depend on to operate, they are now stealing common courtesy. My advice: buy a bicycle, go for a ride and look on the bright side of life.
Gary Ellis, Hamilton
Discourse has become toxic
The key political strategy employed by many political parties is their reliance on apathy. It seems a rather small percentage of voters can keep up and be informed about the many relevant political issues we face. It is difficult to be critical when life is hectic and many struggle financially, which is why parties rely on cute slogans and quick, short social media posts, knowing many will not necessarily inquire about facts wherein one can take the opportunity to employ critical thinking.
It's sad to see our taxes spent on government horn-blowing rather than on the many critical needs we understandably complain about. It's the quick hit parties rely on, and it works, particularly when there is constant media distraction and information (or disinformation) overload. Unlike the past, where real, respectful political discussions could take place, we are left with polarized views wherein discussions are either avoided or toxic. This only reinforces or entrenches the cycle of apathy. One is left to wonder: is the grass greener on the other side of the political spectrum? We will only know if we look closely and carefully.
Damian Borrelli, Hamilton
Police budget is insulting
It is an insult to every taxpayer that the police budget, which has enjoyed a compounded three per cent increase per year for over 35 years, has not been reduced this year by our well-paid councillors. The mounted unit should have been disbanded years ago with the advent of drones. That alone would be a savings of nearly $1 million. Get rid of it! There are, no doubt, many more areas where savings could be made from the outrageous increase. No increase at all for the police budget would be about right. Time for change.
Gord Baker, Carlisle
Budget ignores today's reality
Hamilton's police service, along with Mayor Andrea Horwath and a majority of its board members, have just given city taxpayers the middle finger by refusing to acknowledge today's economic realities, and conduct a serious review of a small number of suggested cost adjustments that appeared to be entirely reasonable in these economic times. Let's be clear, no one is suggesting the HPS should take a budget cut. However, considering the financial pressures we are all facing, any year-over-year increase should be kept to a minimum, and be for essentials only. If the requested budget increase was in the range of three per cent or less, we wouldn't even be having this discussion.
Does anyone believe paying for a largely symbolic mounted horse unit, or unnecessarily repaving a parking lot, rather than leaving that money in the hands of people struggling to buy food or pay rent, is money well spent? Based on recent history, we can be certain the HPS will be back next year, hat in hand, with another request for a massive and unreasonable budget increase. Shame on all of you!
Cameron Stringer, Hamilton
Move election day to February
Ideally, municipal elections would not be held in October. Instead, they would take place in February, shortly after budget approval and property tax bills are sent out. That way, at least every four years, we would have some serious trimming of the budget.
Mark Melanson, Hamilton
City hall too quiet on homeless
Re: Homelessness is getting worse (Feb. 14)
I have to admit, I don't always agree with Laura Babcock, but her column was excellent. I agreed with many statements and others made me think. Homelessness is out of hand and city council is pretty quiet on this. I also am surprised at how quiet Mayor Andrea Horwath has been since taking office; not the lady we watched as an New Democrat MPP. She's gone from being very vocal to not much being said on anything. Laura's comment on Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford was bang on, as was questioning why builders can't get together. Hamilton's lack of a solution for the homelessness problem is embarrassing.
Debbie Escott, Hamilton
Bad officers need to go
Re: Alberta Mountie accused of
accessing police records for Rwanda (Feb. 13)
To those hundreds of RCMP officers who each day wear their uniform proudly and uphold their motto, Maintiens le Droit (maintain the right), a great thank you for keeping us safe. But, to those few officers who discredit that motto, shame on you and please leave the service.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek

Council whittled tax increase by pushing problems down the road: Pared-down bump can't hide fact that Hamilton councillors raised spending by record 19.5 per cent in one year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/council-whittled-tax-increase-pushing-problems/docview/2927814813/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Council+whittled+tax+increase+by+pushing+problems+down+the+road%3A+Pared-down+bump+can%27t+hide+fact+that+Hamilton+councillors+raised+spending+by+record+19.5+per+cent+in+one+year&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the end, council got the tax increase down to 5.79 per cent.
That's a whole lot less than the daunting 14.2 per cent bump we first heard we might be facing in September, to be sure. It's even less than the 7.9 per cent we heard about a few weeks ago when Mayor Andrea Horwath presented her draft budget.
Is this therefore a triumph? A glorious feat of fiscal restraint that brought the final number down to less than half the initial prediction?
Hardly.
While the final number is certainly down from those earlier worst-case-scenario figures, it can't hide the fact that this group increased spending by a record 19.5 per cent in one year. That's astonishing. As Coun. Brad Clark points out, it added something like 165 new full-time employees this year after adding more than 200 last year, which is miles ahead of the pace of previous councils.
Some councillors will argue that all this was driven by provincial downloads that had to be picked up by the city. It's Doug Ford's fault.
There's no doubt that was a factor. Nobody disputes that. But provincial downloads affect all Ontario cities, not just Hamilton. We're not specially targeted by Queen's Park. Every municipality has similar challenges. Yet few others were ever staring at an increase of greater than 14 per cent.
So don't be fooled by the final number being considerably lower than that. The enormous spending that created the issue hasn't been eliminated or corrected, it's just been camouflaged behind a thick barrier of reserves, new debt and a few trims. That isn't a fix. It's a temporary firewall built with smoke and mirrors.
"It's unsustainable," says Coun. Tom Jackson who was one of six (along with Clark, Matt Francis, Jeff Beattie, Mike Spadafora and Mark Tadeson) who voted against the budget.
Indeed.
Clark says this council is now on pace to increase property tax rates by 31 per cent by the end of this term.
If it doesn't feel quite that painful to you yet, remember that the hundreds of millions in reserves that are being used work a little like a coupon for taxpayers. They get you the product without forcing you to pay full price.
But when the coupons expire?
"When we run out of those funds, the taxes go up dramatically," Clark says.
What council's really done is just push the problem down the road.
Some weeks ago, we learned next year's tax increase is already slated to be 7.7 per cent. That's with reserves being used again. On Thursday, we learned that since some of the whittling to get this year's number down was really just a deferring of expenses, the 2025 number is actually closer to 8.1 per cent.
That's the starting point.
"We can't spend a dollar next year and we're already at 8.1," Spadafora says.
So what do you think, is this group going to lock up the chequebook and spend absolutely nothing for 12 months? Operate on a strict dollar-in-dollar-out philosophy?
And when the reserves run out? To remember what happens then, go back a few paragraphs and reread what Clark says.
A big hit is coming. It might it be next year or it could be put off by a year or two with more deferrals. Maybe even just long enough to get beyond the next election which would leave quite a treat for the next council. If you're trying to remain optimistic and thinking, we just have to get past this tough year or two and then everything will return to normal, think again. The 2026 hike is already expected to be 6.5 per cent and 2027 is at 5.23. That's before one more dollar is added. This is the new normal.
The simple truth is, each year's increase gets piled onto the increases of the years before, creating a bigger and bigger mountain of expenses. We're eventually going have to pay for all this.
So what can council do?
If it wants to show that keeping tax increases down is more than just a cheap talking point, the bar for what will be added to the city's bottom line should be extraordinarily high beginning right now. Nice to haves shouldn't do it. Must haves only. No pet projects. No bells and whistles. Only the absolute necessities.
Better yet, take some guidance from Beattie who said what council's done to this point is simply pick the low-hanging fruit. "Somebody needs to get out the ladder and go to the top of the tree," he says.
Amen.
Scott Radley is a columnist with The Hamilton Spectator focusing on sports and politics. Reach him at sradley@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Hamilton city council pared the tax increase to 5.79 per cent but set itself up for even bigger challenges down the road.Cathie CowardThe Hamilton Spectator

Feb. 17: Spectator letters section is mean-spirited, Hamilton police budget an insult to taxpayers and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-17-spectator-letters-section-is-mean-spirited/docview/2927814424/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+17%3A+Spectator+letters+section+is+mean-spirited%2C+Hamilton+police+budget+an+insult+to+taxpayers+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Letters section is mean-spirited
It would appear that the nastiness of Facebook and social media commentators is rearing its head in the letters to the editor. The vitriol between police budgets, LRT, two-way street conversion and - Lord help us - bike lanes makes the letters to the editors section a depressing morning stop. Not only have the social media giants stolen most of the ad revenue our newspapers and TV depend on to operate, they are now stealing common courtesy. My advice: buy a bicycle, go for a ride and look on the bright side of life.
Gary Ellis, Hamilton
Discourse has become toxic
The key political strategy employed by many political parties is their reliance on apathy. It seems a rather small percentage of voters can keep up and be informed about the many relevant political issues we face. It is difficult to be critical when life is hectic and many struggle financially, which is why parties rely on cute slogans and quick, short social media posts, knowing many will not necessarily inquire about facts wherein one can take the opportunity to employ critical thinking.
It's sad to see our taxes spent on government horn-blowing rather than on the many critical needs we understandably complain about. It's the quick hit parties rely on, and it works, particularly when there is constant media distraction and information (or disinformation) overload. Unlike the past, where real, respectful political discussions could take place about current issues, we are left with polarized views wherein discussions are either avoided or toxic. This only reinforces or entrenches the cycle of apathy. One is left to wonder: is the grass greener on the other side of the political spectrum? We will only know if we look closely and carefully. It doesn't help however when real, thoughtful journalism is slowly eroding.
Damian Borrelli, Hamilton
Police budget is insulting
It is an insult to every taxpayer that the police budget, which has enjoyed a compounded three per cent increase per year for over 35 years, has not been reduced this year by our very well-paid councillors. The mounted unit should have been disbanded years ago with the advent of drones. That alone would be a savings of nearly $1 million. Get rid of it! There are no doubt many more areas where savings could be made from the outrageous increase. No increase at all for the police budget would be about right. Time for change.
Gord Baker, Carlisle
Budget ignores today's reality
Hamilton's police service, along with Mayor Andrea Horwath and a majority of its board members, have just given city taxpayers the middle finger by refusing to acknowledge today's economic realities, and conduct a serious review and consideration of a small number of suggested cost adjustments that appeared to be entirely reasonable in these economic times. Let's be clear, no one is suggesting the HPS should take a budget cut compared to last year. However, considering the financial pressures we are all facing, any year-over-year increase should be kept to a minimum, and be for essentials only. If the requested budget increase was in the range of three per cent or less, we wouldn't even be having this discussion.
Does anyone really believe that paying for a largely symbolic mounted horse unit, or unnecessarily repaving a parking lot, rather than leaving that money in the hands of people struggling to buy food or pay rent, is money well spent? Based on recent history, we can be certain the HPS will be back next year, hat in hand, with another request for a massive and unreasonable budget increase. Shame on all of you!
Cameron Stringer, Hamilton
Move election day to February
Ideally, future municipal elections would not be held in October. Instead, they would take place in February, shortly after budget approval and property tax bills are sent out.
Mark Melanson, Hamilton
City hall too quiet on homeless
Re: Homelessness is getting worse (Feb. 14)
I have to admit, I don't always agree with Laura Babcock, but her column was excellent. I agreed on many statements and others made me think. Homelessness is out of hand and city council is pretty quiet on this. I also am surprised at how quiet Mayor Andrea Horwath has been since taking office; not the lady we watched as an New Democrat MPP. She's gone from being very vocal to not much being said on anything. Laura's comment on Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford was bang on, as was questioning why builders can't get together. Hamilton's lack of a solution for the homelessness problem is embarrassing. There is so much to be done with so little effort. Just another embarrassment for Hamilton.
Debbie Escott, Hamilton

Document 77 of 252

Trudeau government considers walking back a key environmental pledge on power generation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-considers-walking-back-key/docview/2927876580/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+considers+walking+back+a+key+environmental+pledge+on+power+generation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa is proposing to water down its clean electricity regulations in the face of intense opposition from some provinces by further opening the door for natural gas power plants to keep burning well beyond the promised deadline for net-zero power in 2035.In response to objections brought by provinces and electrical grid operators, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault is now considering allowing new gas plants to operate with no limits past 2045, as well as loosening restrictions on existing plants. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "made a commitment to a net-zero grid in 2021," said Keith Brooks, programs director at Environmental Defence. "But provinces like Ontario and Saskatchewan, they have gone ahead (and started building new gas plants) in a direct challenge to federal regulations, and now the federal government seems to be waffling."In a statement Friday, Guilbeault said the changes are meant to "enhance the flexibility" of electricity operators in the provinces while "maintaining" Canada's objectives to reduce emissions causing the climate crisis.While these changes will likely still mean reduced carbon emissions from electricity generation in provinces like Alberta, Saskatchewan and Nova Scotia, which rely on fossil fuels for the bulk of their power, they could be so loose as to allow emissions to actually rise in provinces like Ontario, which currently derives about 10 per cent of its power from natural gas, critics allege."If these regulations allow new plants to be contracted, constructed and run completely unmitigated out past 2045, and the existing fleet is allowed to continue to operate for its 20-year end of life period as well, there's no limit on emissions for quite some time," said Brooks."It's important that this 20-year lifespan not be extended," he said. "Otherwise, it's going to be a huge problem."According to a summary of consultations released Friday, the changes that Ottawa is considering include:Allowing greater use of natural gas power when electricity demand spikes;Lifting a hard cap of 450 hours per year for natural gas plants without means to prevent emissions, and replacing it with a limit based on annual emissions;Allowing for the "limited use" of offsets for gas plants that exceed their emissions limits;"Slightly" extending the exemption for gas plants currently being planned and built, which aren't subject to the regulations until 20 years after they come online. This originally applied to plants that came online before 2025; the government is proposing to extend that by an unspecified amount of time. While some climate experts welcomed the changes, others raised concerns that they would allow emitting power plants to continue without restraints in the midst of the climate crisis. New Democrat MP Laurel Collins, the NDP's climate critic in Parliament, called the changes "disappointing" because they weaken what she sees as a critical climate policy for the federal government. "This is them, yet again, creating more loopholes," Collins said. "This is a critical piece of our climate policy and getting clean electricity by 2035 ... It is essential if we have any hope of meeting our climate targets." It's not yet clear how these changes would impact overall emissions from the electricity sector, which the Liberal government in Ottawa has pledged to reduce to "net zero" by 2035. The original version of the regulations already fell short of that goal. Guilbeault told the Star last year that there would have been fewer than 10 megatonnes of "residual" emissions from the country's power grids by 2035 under the first proposal - about 20 per cent of overall electricity emissions based on 2021 figures. The federal government maintains it can still attain net-zero emissions from electricity by 2035 through other policies outside of these regulations, which have sparked heated criticism from provinces like Ontario, Alberta and Saskatchewan. They argue the regulations will drive up electricity bills and possibly strain power grids. In a written statement, Dustin Duncan, the minister responsible for Saskatchewan's provincial power utility, told the Star that Friday's proposed changes are "an admission" the federal regulations "are unaffordable and technically and logistically unattainable." Duncan said the regulations infringe on provincial authorities and should still be scrapped entirely. The Ontario government welcomed the changes but indicated it's still not satisfied over concerns the regulations could affect the reliability of the province's power grids. "More work is still needed to ensure these regulations address all of the concerns identified by Ontario's expert system planners," read a statement from Ontario Energy Minister Todd Stone's spokesperson, Palmer Lockridge. Alberta's environment minister, Rebecca Schulz, added in her own statement that the regulations are still not "feasible or realistic" based on the 2035 timeline for implementation. Why is natural gas used?Natural gas was once considered a transition fuel because it produces less carbon when burned. It was favoured as a way for heavy industry like power generation to reduce emissions and stop burning coal. But research over the last few years has shown that because natural gas is mostly made up of the particularly potent greenhouse gas methane, leaks during extraction and along the pipeline network make the gas worse for the climate than coal.While efforts are underway to plug those leaks, satellite monitoring and flyover measurements show they are far greater than has generally been understood.All of this adds up to an argument to phase out natural gas as quickly as possible, rather than prolong its use, environmentalists say.Meanwhile, the grid operator in Ontario has contracted three new gas plants, in St. Clair, Windsor and Napanee, none of which would be built fast enough to be grandfathered into the regulations as they were originally proposed, and would have had to cease operation by 2035, except during peak or emergency periods.These plants, and others announced in Saskatchewan, could be allowed to run for a full 20 years under the proposed changes to the regulations, a prospect that worries clean energy advocates."Allowing unabated gas-fired generators to come online after Jan. 1, 2025 (the previous cut-off date) risks locking in gas emissions and higher electricity costs for decades," said Scott MacDougall, director of electricity at the Pembina Institute.Other clean energy advocates said the changes were necessary to ensure the targets were achievable in every province, and that the electricity regulations were never going to achieve net zero on their own."The clean electricity regulations were never a silver bullet for this," said Evan Pivnick, clean energy program manager at Clean Energy Canada.
CAPTION: Environment and Climate Change Minister Steven Guilbeault speaks in the House of Commons on Feb. 16, 2024.

Document 78 of 252

Ford government wants to fast-track construction projects but critics warn Greenbelt shows it can't be trusted

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-wants-fast-track-construction/docview/2927876539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+wants+to+fast-track+construction+projects+but+critics+warn+Greenbelt+shows+it+can%27t+be+trusted&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Still stinging from the Greenbelt land swap fiasco, the Progressive Conservatives insist streamlining approvals for more infrastructure projects will not be harmful to the environment.
"We're taking a hard look at our policies and processes and have found ways to make them smarter and more efficient," Environment Minister Andrea Khanjin told reporters Friday at a Brampton hydro corridor.
"We've consulted extensively with municipalities, Indigenous communities and key stakeholders on proposals to move more projects to a streamlined environmental process," said Khanjin.
"This means that more projects such as new highways, railways and electricity transmission lines like the ones behind us here could see up to four years taken off their building timelines, which is a massive improvement that saves time and money," she said.
The changes will take effect next Thursday two days after being included in omnibus legislation being tabled when the house resumes after the winter break.
They are designed to fast-track construction of projects such as Hwy. 413, the 60-km freeway between Milton and Vaughan and the 16.2-km Bradford Bypass linking Hwys. 400 and 404.
Noting similarly streamlined environmental assessments for public transit projects have been in place since 2008 - under changes made by the previous Liberal government - Khanjin emphasized oversight was not being undermined.
"This is going to help streamline low-risk projects," said Khanjin, adding the federal government, Quebec and British Columbia have similar policies.
The minister emphasized there would still be more comprehensive environmental assessments for complex projects like large landfill sites and electricity generation facilities.
But pointing to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal - in which Premier Doug Ford opened up 7,400 acres of protected land to housing development before cancelling that controversial move, which has triggered an RCMP investigation - critics are wary.
"The premier and his government have learned nothing from their Greenbelt scheme and keep pushing ahead with policies that they know are harmful," said NDP MPP Sandy Shaw (Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas).
"This government's disastrous track record on the environment speaks for itself. Today's announcement raises serious questions about the future of environmental protection in our province," said Shaw.
Environmental Defence's Phil Pothen said the best indicator of future behaviour is past behaviour.
"We've seen how a gap in process will be used by this government," said Pothen, land use and land development program manager for the environmental group.
Last August, separate reports from the auditor general and integrity commissioner found certain property developers with Tory connections were "favoured" when parcels of Greenbelt land were unexpectedly opened to build housing in late 2022.
The following month, Ford, whose popularity plummeted over public concern about the land deal, cancelled the swap and enshrined in legislation protections for the two-million acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
But Pothen warned that watering down environmental assessments for highways and hydro corridors will hasten "sprawl development."
"The public understands why they should take this seriously. It's not fantastical to imagine how (the streamlined assessments) will be used," he said.
"This is a planned curtailment of full-scale environmental assessments."
Colin Best, president of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario and a Halton regional councillor, countered that, saying "modernized environmental assessment processes are a critical enabler for housing, streamlining processes for municipalities to help them build infrastructure like roads, sewers, and water systems more quickly."
"The initiatives announced today take a risk-based approach to environmental management, removing red tape for straightforward municipal infrastructure projects and focusing resources where they are needed most," said Best.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford delivers remarks at the opening ceremonies of the Canadian International Auto Show in Toronto on Feb. 16, 2024.

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Feb. 16: Council folds like a house of cards, lets vote on LRT's future and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-16-council-folds-like-house-cards-lets-vote/docview/2927455250/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+16%3A+Council+folds+like+a+house+of+cards%2C+lets+vote+on+LRT%27s+future+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Folding like a house of cards
Re: Trims to Hamilton police budget rejected (Feb. 13)
"There would be a risk to public safety." That's all the police have to say every year, and council caves easier than a house of cards. No matter what shape the city finances are in or what the data says, the police never pay attention to any of that at all - in fact, they probably couldn't care less. No one is asking to take money away from the police budget or even refuse them a budget increase. But if you got sticker shock with your new property tax bill, you're not alone. We couldn't even find cash to fix some roads, never mind lowering our property taxes in this city.
I don't know how many more police and police cars we need, but after a crime has been committed and the threat is over, there's plenty of police cars there - doing what, we don't know. Maybe in case the bad guy comes back to the scene.
I've said it before and I'll say it again: Mayor Andrea Horwath will go down in Hamilton history as the biggest tax-and-spend mayor we've ever seen.
Red Taylor, Hamilton
Crime fighting over bike lanes
City council has no problem suggesting that the Hamilton Police Services Board trim its services, yet has no problem approving millions for bike lanes. Talk about a travesty of justice. One only has to look at the news to see the escalating increase of crime in the area and know trimming the budget will not help. Yes, it does cost a lot to keep crime at bay, especially when you are chasing the criminals down those super-duper bike lanes!
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Public safety is the priority
I live in the lower city where property taxes have more than doubled in eight years, so I'm not happy with a tax increase. However, I have more confidence in my safety knowing that Chief Frank Bergen and the police continue to serve and protect our community. Kudos to Mayor Andrea Horwath and city councillors who rejected the proposed cuts to the police budget.
Mardi Schwenger, Hamilton
Tolerant citizens pushed to their limits
Re: It's about progress and equality (Feb. 12)
In what possible fairy-tale world does this writer live?
The first paragraph alone is absurd in its illogical, extended attack on supposed "conservative" beliefs. To suggest that individuals are "anti progress ... deny equality for others, and believe that other races are less than yours," based solely on the writer's imaginings, is complete nonsense. The rest of the piece is simply another rambling excursion of the far left's distortions.
Unfortunately, this is exactly the type of prattle that passes for "enlightened" thought today. It is a foundation of the dangerous social radicalization and re-engineering of Canada. Most people I know are not Neanderthal knuckle-draggers, waiting to hitch a ride on the next clown caravan to Ottawa. However, intelligent, compassionate, tolerant citizens are being pushed to their limits by the never-ending onslaught and embrace of the far left's lunatic visions and a failure of traditional society and its institutions to confront these distortions.
This is not a healthy path forward for Canada.
Henry Bokor, Burlington
Beer simply isn't a necessity
Re: Ottawa should follow Ontario's lead and freeze beer taxes (Feb. 12)
I like beer. I like affordable beer. I don't like taxes. However, I also understand beer, like so much in life, is not a necessity - it remains a luxury.
Curbing inflation means, as I understand, reducing consumption; presuming producers pass along those costs, applying tax onto a luxury product might achieve a reduction in consumption of a luxury item, not a necessity, and hopefully reducing inflationary pressures. And comparing Canada or Ontario to the United States on luxury taxes, come on; population, land mass, provided services all add up to a situation where the lifestyle the writer and all attempt to enjoy, with or without beer, comes with costs that the United States doesn't and will never have until they bring their standard of life to match or exceed the services we're provided by our governments.
I, like Doug, want our beer, but we don't really need it.
Greg Cherwaty, Hamilton
Player should be charged with assault
1. Ridly Greig should have the Ottawa police charge Toronto Maple Leaf Morgan Rielly with assault with a weapon. His employer, the Ottawa Senators, should back him. The assault took place at work, on company grounds (ice).
2. I read Katherine Henderson's column, "Hockey must confront challenges." I agree completely with her comments. However, it would be most helpful if there were concrete suggestions and comments about the "how to change the culture," as we all need to be part of the process to effectively contribute to a culture shift in Canadian hockey.
Raymond Mahoney, Burlington
Canada's military has fallen far
Re: Feb. 13 editorial cartoon
By the winter of 1944, the Second World War was slowly grinding to an end in Europe, and by May 8, 1945, V-E Day, Germany would capitulate to the Allies. Japan would surrender in the Pacific campaign on Sept. 2, 1945. By war's end, Canada would have the fourth-largest air force and the third-largest navy in the world. Over a million Canadians would serve in the military.
The Canadian servicemen would be considered the best trained army in the world, feared by German soldiers for their ferociousness and tenacity. Now, thanks to 80 years of neglect by the federal government, the only military afraid of Canada are the nation's own soldiers asked to defend the country with a scrapyard of obsolete clunkers from other NATO countries.
Pat Howse, Hamilton
Basic income to remain a dream
Unfortunately, I feel a basic income will not become a reality. The one problem being wages and benefits would have to improve to retain employees. Why would anyone want to remain among the working poor? Business would really have to pay a livable wage, not minimum wage. Another factor is inflation. Cost of living is so high, what would a basic income even look like? Inflation is so high, it would be a hard sell for what it would take to keep people housed and fed. Factors affecting inflation/greedflation seem pretty convenient when an argument for a basic income is being made.
Jeff Campbell, Hamilton
Let referendum decide LRT's future
Re: Ford uses announcement of carbon tax referendum bill to take digs at Bonnie Crombie (Feb. 13)
So, Premier Doug Ford would like to call a referendum on carbon pricing. We like to think we are living in a democracy, but really it is a representative system, in which we elect someone like Ford to journey to the capital to represent our thoughts and feelings, evidently because we are too stupid to do it for ourselves.
Having a referendum is one step closer to democracy, so all politicians avoid them like the plague. Nevertheless, they are commonly offered in other jurisdictions - California has used them since 1911; a dozen items were voted up or down in the 2020 election.
Since the premier is suddenly in favour of the people's ability to choose, why don't we have a referendum on the LRT? Large capital items are commonly put to the public. In fact, when Keanin Loomis worked for the chamber of commerce, he sponsored a telephone survey (not quite an election) on whether to proceed with building the trolley line. I was in the council chambers when he announced they were cancelling the survey before it was completed because it was "flawed." Evidently the negative votes were 49 per cent, and assuming a number of respondents were neutral, the LRT was soundly defeated.
So why not have one of your referendums, Mr. Premier? It will cost some money to organize, but could save $3.4 billion.
Ron Johnson, Hamilton
CAPTION: Hamilton police investigate a recent homicide in Stoney Creek. Residents continue to debate the police budget.

National briefs: Watchdog cites security lapse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/2927101551/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+Watchdog+cites+security+lapse&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
Team set up to battle extortion
The RCMP says it has established a national team to help co-ordinate investigations and information sharing about extortion schemes targeting South Asian businesses in B.C., Alberta and Ontario. Mounties say the team is a "nationwide alliance" of police agencies that are all investigating extortion and violent threats, which have been tied to shootings and arson. The RCMP says the team is not taking over investigations, but providing support to facilitate "seamless" information-sharing at a national level.
Watchdog cites security lapse
Government departments lacked adequate protections to fend off a "sophisticated and co-ordinated" cyberattack that compromised the sensitive information of tens of thousands of Canadians, the federal privacy watchdog has found. In a report tabled Thursday, privacy commissioner Philippe Dufresne describes how the lapse at the Canada Revenue Agency and Employment and Social Development Canada in summer 2020 allowed hackers to fraudulently collect payments.
Manitoba
Landfill search set for 2024
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew says he is confident a landfill search for the remains of two slain First Nations women will begin this year. Kinew would not reveal details about how much progress has been made in preparing for the search, but says the province will work with families of the women and with Indigenous leaders. The NDP government has promised to search the Prairie Green Landfill, a privately run facility north of Winnipeg, where the remains of Morgan Harris and Marcedes Myran are believed to have been taken after they were killed in the spring of 2022.

Document 81 of 252

Is it time for the Liberals and NDP to break up?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-time-liberals-ndp-break-up/docview/2927101505/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+it+time+for+the+Liberals+and+NDP+to+break+up%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The deal between Justin Trudeau's Liberals and Jagmeet Singh's New Democrats has already lasted longer than most minority governments in Canadian history.
Within a matter of days, that deal will either collapse or carry on - depending on whether the two sides can come to some kind of agreement to bring pharmacare to Canada.
Two questions hang over that fork in the road for the two parties. Can the deal last? Beyond that, though, should the deal continue?
On the first question, cautious optimism surrounds the whole debate over pharmacare, for both sides, for now.
As the Star reported on Wednesday, Liberals and New Democrats appear to be circling around some kind of consensus on which drugs to include in whatever pharmacare deal they hammer out before their self-imposed, March 1 deadline. Diabetes drugs and birth-control pills seem to have made the cut.
It also appears that both sides agreed that full pharmacare - as the NDP envisions it and the Liberals have on-and-off promised - won't be coming to Canada in the near future.
"We're not saying the money's got to be in the budget. That's never been our ask. And we've never said that," Singh said during a tough-talking news conference earlier this week. "But we do expect legislation that lays the foundation, and that's what we're asking for."
Singh also threw a bucket of cold water over what the Liberals have called a major problem - the possible damage to Canada's credit rating if Trudeau introduces a whole new social program at this point in the mandate.
"There's lots of ways to clarify for credit-rating agencies that this is something that's going to require additional work - (that) it's going to require additional steps to happen."
New Democrats are saying that if the supply and confidence agreement ends over an impasse on pharmacare, they're going to be arguing it's the Liberals who walked away from the deal, not them.
That's where we come to the more strategic considerations, for both sides, on whether they want the deal to continue - the should-it-last question.
For Liberals, this is mostly a no-brainer. The collapse of the deal may not precipitate an immediate election, but it makes Trudeau's minority more precarious. And judging from all of the polls, odds are that an election could turn out badly for the Liberals.
If election-avoidance is the only issue, the New Democrats would likely be similarly motivated to keep the deal going. They may have limited clout as the fourth party in the House of Commons right now, but they would have zero influence, one assumes, in a Pierre Poilievre government.
But two former leaders of the NDP have openly questioned in recent months the wisdom of the party attaching its fortunes too closely to Trudeau's Liberals.
In one of his last interviews before his death last month, Ed Broadbent told the CBC's Rosemary Barton: "Maybe if the agreement were a year shorter, it might be a little better."
This week, former leader Thomas Mulcair said on CTV that it was time for the NDP to walk away from the Liberals, too. "I think that they have to draw that line in the sand and then say the deal is done ... They've got to show autonomy from the Liberals."
Mulcair said that walking away now would position Singh perfectly for a future election, as a principled advocate for pharmacare.
The other benefit, which he didn't mention, was that if Trudeau really is going into the next election as a liability for his party, it may be best for the New Democrats to start creating some distance between their party and Trudeau's team. Poilievre and his Conservatives have spent nearly two years branding the current regime as the "Liberal-NDP" government for their own strategic reasons.
At the risk of being overly earnest, however, the best reason for keeping the deal alive is the one offered when the agreement was first unveiled in March, 2022. It was, Liberals and New Democrats said, all about getting things accomplished.
It's worth remembering the state of politics in this country as this deal was being negotiated two years ago. Ottawa was neck deep in the midst of the so-called "Freedom Convoy" occupation and it looked like Canada was hurtling into the same kind of polarized chaos we're seeing south of the border. The idea of two political rivals rising above the fray to give Canadians dental care, pharmacare and other progressive pieces of legislation seemed like an antidote, especially if you're of the view that the best reply to anti-institutional populism is to prove that institutions work for people.
If the Liberals and New Democrats can find their way back to those first principles, there may be hope for the deal to continue, beyond practical or strategic disputes.
The average duration for minority government in Canada is roughly a year and a half. The Liberal-NDP deal will be two years old next month. That in itself is an achievement, but the progress need not stop there.
CAPTION: In this file photo taken on Sept. 9, 2021, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh participate in the federal election English-language Leaders debate in Gatineau, Que. In 2022, the two leaders signed on to asupply and confidence agreement that helps keep the Liberals in power.

Document 82 of 252

Foot-dragging delays justice

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/foot-dragging-delays-justice/docview/2927101468/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Foot-dragging+delays+justice&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The verdict is in - the federal government has done a lousy job at appointing judges and has no good excuse for not doing better.
In a judgment released this week, Justice Henry Brown, of the Federal Court, declared that the shortage of Superior Court and Federal Courts judges is at "both a crisis and critical level."
Sadly, that will not be news for those denied justice when the resulting court delays has pushed cases beyond 30-month time frame set by the Supreme Court and caused a stay of proceedings.
Toronto Star reporter Jacques Gallant has detailed serious criminal cases when that has happened, including the collapse of a human trafficking case this month, a firearms case and just before Christmas, a sexual assault case after the jury had already delivered its guilty verdict. The shortages also impact civil trials, too.
Brown was ruling on a case brought by Yavar Hameed, a human rights lawyer in Ottawa, who represents low-income and marginalized individuals. He argued that his clients faced significant delays in court proceedings and sought a judicial order compelling the government to swiftly fill judicial vacancies.
In a judgment that is both a sharp rebuke and pointed call to do better, Brown agreed, ruling that the federal government must act to fill the vacancies.
Federal Justice Minister Arif Virani responded on X, saying there are more federally appointed judges now than ever before. And he claimed the federal Liberals were appointing judges at a faster pace than the previous Conservative government under Stephen Harper.
"We are making judicial appointments at the fastest pace in history. We made 100 judicial appointments last year, a number never attained by the Conservatives," Virani said.
Given that the Liberals have been in power for almost nine years, such partisan comparisons are tiresome and pointless. Virani can cheer the current pace of appointments but the tossed trials and concerns previously raised by the country's top judge show that the government has failed in its duty to keep the judicial benches adequately staffed.
Indeed, Brown's judgment reprints verbatim the letter that Richard Wagner, chief justice of the Supreme Court of Canada, sent to Justin Trudeau last May in which he said it was imperative the prime minister act to ensure appointments are made in a timely manner.
"The positions that have been left vacant are having significant impacts on the administration of justice, the operations of our courts and the health of our judges," Wagner wrote.
At the time of his letter, there were 85 vacancies and Wagner noted that some courts were dealing with vacancy rates as high as 15 per cent "for years now." Senior judicial positions, including the chief justice positions in Alberta, Ontario and Prince Edward Island, were being filled "at a very slow pace.
"No clear explanation justifies these delays," Wagner wrote, noting that judicial vacancies are predictable, especially those caused by retirements.
"In this context, these delays in appointments send a message that this is simply not a priority for the government," he stated.
Wagner's lengthy letter to Trudeau doesn't seem to have moved the government to action. Nine months later, the number of vacancies for federally appointed judges stands at 75, prompting Brown to conclude that the government ignored Wagner's concerns.
"With the greatest respect, the Court finds the Prime Minister and Minister of Justice are simply treading water" and in the process are failing those who have "unsuccessfully sought timely justice," Brown declared.
It's essential then that the government take Brown's ruling seriously. He did not set a number on what would be a reasonable level of vacancies, though he suggested mid-40s. Nor did he set a deadline. But he left no doubt he expects action.
"How long should it take to fill a sufficient number of vacancies? In the Court's view the answer is plain and obvious: these vacancies must be materially reduced within a reasonable time to a reasonable level," he said.
Virani can pat himself on the back all he wants on the government's record. But the evidence from the courts is clear and persuasive. Whatever the government has been doing, it's not enough. The federal Liberals need to get serious about filling judicial vacancies and ensuring the timely delivery of justice in Canada.
CAPTION:

Document 83 of 252

Here's how many international student applications Ontario universities want

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-how-many-international-student-applications/docview/2927649516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+how+many+international+student+applications+Ontario+universities+want&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's universities say more than one-third of the province's international student applications should go to them, maintaining they are "not the cause" of the current troubles and "should not be asked to shoulder the consequences" of government clampdowns.
"Universities have been very careful and responsible in taking on new students," said Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, adding foreign undergraduate student enrolment has held steady over the past four years at around 23,000 despite explosive growth in public and private colleges.
"We've been doing it for decades - we've been very responsible and managing the growth. So when there's a gold rush for international students, universities didn't play that game. We were very measured and responsible to ensure that our students were put first" with proper services such as housing and mental health supports, said Orsini in releasing the council's proposal in response to the federal government's recent announcement curbing student visas and leaving it to provinces to divide them up.
The council is asking that about 82,250 of the 235,000 eligible undergraduate applications be given to the province's 23 publicly assisted universities, and be allocated based on enrolment.
Amid all the uncertainty - and the possibility of students ultimately choosing to study elsewhere - Orsini said the universities are hoping that translates into about 23,000 approvals, which will keep international enrolment stable at under 20 per cent of any given university's undergraduate enrolment.
"This will ensure we can continue to recruit the best and the brightest talent from across the globe," the council's proposal says.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller has singled out the Wild West landscape in Ontario on several occasions, highlighting the concerns raised in a 2021 auditor general's report with respect to the fast-growing public-private college partnerships.
The report found Ontario's public colleges, especially those outside of the GTA, have become increasingly dependent on international students as a source of revenue and "some of these partnerships did not uphold enrolment requirements." It said their quality assurance and student support processes should be strengthened.
"We have a challenge in Ontario in particular," Miller told a news conference last year. "What we are seeing in the ecosystem is one that has been chasing after short-term gain without looking at the long-term pain. And we need to reverse that trend."
The Council of Ontario Universities is also calling on the provincial government to allow tuition to increase by five per cent this fall, and boost funding by 10 per cent.
Shortly after taking office, Premier Doug Ford slashed tuition by 10 per cent and has banned any increases since for domestic students.
Ford has already ruled out a fee hike, saying students can't afford it.
Between that, stagnant provincial funding as well as inflation and unexpected COVID-19 costs, Ontario universities say they are in financial crisis and need the additional revenue from a tuition increase, as well as more government help - an amount estimated to bring in about $1.9 billion over three years.
Ten of the province's 23 publicly assisted universities are running deficits totalling more than $175 million this year.
"Ontario's universities are asking the federal and provincial governments to work together to take a more focused and targeted approach. We support enhancing the integrity of the international recruitment process, but are calling on both levels of government to focus their efforts on those organizations that have rapidly expanded international student enrolment without student supports," the council said.
Post-secondary schools have come to rely on tuition from international students, which can be three or four times the rate of domestic students, to make up funding gaps.
CAPTION: Outside of the law school at Toronto Metropolitan University.
