Document 84 of 252

Hamilton MPP calls PM's protest condemnation 'bonkers'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-mpp-calls-pms-protest-condemnation/docview/2926637086/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+MPP+calls+PM%27s+protest+condemnation+%27bonkers%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton Centre MPP Sarah Jama lashed out at Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for declaring a pro-Palestinian rally outside a Toronto hospital to be antisemitic.
The protest, which Jama says she attended, is under police investigation.
The Monday protest started at the Israeli Consulate in Toronto in opposition to Israel's continued attacks in Gaza that have killed thousands of civilians. It later moved to Mount Sinai Hospital, which was founded by the Jewish community. There, protesters chanted and a few of them climbed up the building and waved Palestinian flags.
The protest drew condemnation from political leaders, including Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Trudeau, who posted a statement on X calling it "reprehensible."
"Hospitals are places for treatment and care, not protests and intimidation," Trudeau wrote. "I strongly condemn this display of antisemitism. In Toronto and across Canada, we stand with Jewish communities against this hate."
Jama - who has attended several pro-Palestinian protests in Toronto since Israel launched is retaliatory attacks in Gaza following the Oct. 7 invasion of Israel by the terror group Hamas - rejected Trudeau's characterization of the protest, calling his tweet "bonkers."
"I was there last night and that's not what happened. People are doing all they can to get you to stop sending weapons to Israel in our names. You've shown no leadership and over a million Palestinians being ethnically cleansed," she wrote on X. "Stop tweeting. Do something useful."
While Canada and Israel have a military trade agreement, Global Affairs Canada has said only "non-lethal" equipment is being shipped to Israel. Details of what that equipment is has not been disclosed.
The protest was also condemned by Toronto area hospital leaders, who issued an open letter Tuesday saying the rally "ran the real risk of disrupting hospital operations and compromising the safety of staff, physicians, learners, patients and victors - all totally unacceptable."
Toronto police are investigating the protest, although no arrests have been made.
Jama has not commented on the issue of protesting at the hospital and her office declined repeated requests for an interview from The Spectator.
The conflict in Gaza has become a focus for Jama, whose commentary on the conflict resulted in her being removed from the Ontario NDP caucus. Ford's Progressive Conservative Party passed a censure motion that means Jama cannot speak in the legislature unless she apologizes.
Jama has said she won't apologize and has threatened to sue Ford for libel and fight the censure in court.
More than 28,000 people have been killed in Gaza since Israel launched its offensive, according to the Health Ministry in Gaza. Last month the International Court of Justice ordered Israel to take immediate steps to prevent a genocide in Gaza and allow humanitarian aid into the region.
However, the military strikes have continued and international opposition, including from Canada, has risen as Israel announced plans for a ground invasion of the city of Rafah, where more than a million people have fled to escape the violence and have nowhere else to go.
Regular protests about Gaza have triggered debate over what constitutes antisemitism.
Jaime Kirzner-Roberts from the Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs told The Spectator in November that criticism of the actions of the Israeli government is not in and of itself antisemitic. However, holding Jewish people or Jewish institutions responsible for the state of Israel's actions is antisemitic, said Kirzner-Roberts.
Monday's protest started at the Israeli Consulate and made its way to the American Consulate, passing by several Toronto hospitals. The only one they stopped at was Mount Sinai, founded in 1923 to serve the local Jewish community.
Social-media videos show several men climbing up on the hospital while a crowd outside chants for an "intefadeh," a call for an uprising for Palestinian liberation, which many Jews have seen as a call for violence against them.
Intefadeh, which comes from the Arabic word meaning "to shake off," refers to two Palestinian uprisings in the West Bank and Gaza Strip aimed at ending Israel's occupation and establishing an independent Palestinian state. Collectively, 5,000 Palestinians and some 1,400 Israelis were killed in the two conflicts.
Protest organizers have called accusations of antisemitism "baseless," saying people stopped to watch the men climb the building. However, in their open letter, the CEOs of 15 Toronto hospitals called it a "display of antisemitism" and said that hospitals are "dedicated to ensuring accessible and safe care for everyone regardless of their religious faith, face, gender or sexual orientation."
The leadership at Mount Sinai did not sign the letter and have not made a public statement about the protest.
-With files from the Toronto Star
Grant LaFleche is an award-winning investigative journalist with the Hamilton Spectator. Reach him at glafleche@torstar.ca.

Document 85 of 252

Doug Ford government to ban most highway tolls, freeze driver's licence fees and eliminate plate registration

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-ban-most-highway-tolls/docview/2926637065/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+to+ban+most+highway+tolls%2C+freeze+driver%27s+licence+fees+and+eliminate+plate+registration&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is slamming the brakes on any rate increases for drivers' licences and outlawing the possibility of new tolls on all freeways except Hwy. 407.
At the same time, Ford's Progressive Conservatives are eliminating the need for registering licence plates after the Star revealed there are more than one million expired plates on Ontario roads.
That's because after the renewal sticker fee, which cost up to $120 annually, was phased out two years ago, many motorists incorrectly assumed they didn't have to keep their plates up to date.
As a result, there has been a surge in Highway Traffic Act fines of as much as $1,000 for driving with invalid plates.
"We will be automating the licence plate renewal process starting this summer which will save vehicle owners more than 900,000 hours each year," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said Thursday.
That's an apparent reference to the amount time 13.6 million plate holders would spend renewing their permits online or in person at ServiceOntario outlets.
"The automatic renewal process will only be available to drivers in good standing, who do not have outstanding tickets or related fees and municipalities will still be able to use the renewal process to collect outstanding fees," the minister said.
But motorists who owe traffic fines or tolls would not receive renewals until those are paid.
Scofflaws will be notified that they must pay penalties or their plates will not be validated.
Sarkaria acknowledged "many families are feeling the pinch in their wallets" with the rising cost of living.
"The last thing they need to see is another unnecessary fee or toll coming their way," he told reporters in downtown Toronto.
"I am pleased to announce that our government is introducing legislation that would, if passed, ban tolls on provincial highways - that will not only apply to the DVP and Gardiner Expressway only minutes away from here, but also to the province's 400 series highways."
Queen's Park is uploading the Don Valley Parkway and the Gardiner from the city as part of Ford's new deal with Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow so the Tories wanted to assure motorists it will remain free to use.
But the most expensive toll road in the province - Hwy. 407, which can cost as much as $70.66 to drive 151 km from the Queen Elizabeth Way in Burlington to Hwys. 35/115 near Orono - will remain intact on both the privately and publicly operated stretches.
"Look, the 407 is a private entity," said a defensive Sarkaria, referring to the 1999 long-term lease signed by a previous Tory government that has seen tolls skyrocket on the 108-km private section of the highway.
In fact, Queen's Park owns and operates the remaining 43 km of Hwy. 407 from Brock Road in Pickering east to Orono.
"We're here to talk about the province's infrastructure like the DVP and the Gardiner," he said, emphasizing the changes are aimed at prohibiting new tolls, not existing levies.
Sarkaria said the government is also planning to make permanent the freeze on driver's licence renewal fees at $90 and the Ontario Photo Card at $35. The Tories first announced that after taking office in 2018 and the regulation took effect in 2019.
"Our freeze has saved Ontarians $22 million since 2019 and it will save drivers $66 million more this decade," he said.
The minister said the changes would be included in the Get It Done Act, an omnibus bill being tabled when the legislature resumes after the winter break on Tuesday.
As first reported Monday by the Star, there were exactly 1,015,139 expired licence plates in the province last month.
That means about seven per cent of the 13.6 million plates have not been renewed by forgetful vehicle owners who risk hefty fines.
According to the Ontario Provincial Police, charges have soared since the paid renewal stickers were eliminated.
Last year, there were 15,631 tickets issued to motorists lacking a "currently validated permit for the vehicle" compared with 5,341 charges in 2022 and 1,556 in 2021.
It is believed the COVID-19 pandemic that kept Ontarians off roads and highways could have contributed to those lower violations in 2021.
Removing the renewal charge costs Queen's Park more than $1 billion in lost revenue annually, but the Tories believe it was a key factor in their re-election in 2022 and it remains popular with voters.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford makes an announcement and answers questions at a press conference in Mississauga on Tuesday.

Subject: Fines & penalties; Automobile drivers

Title: Doug Ford government to ban most highway tolls, freeze driver's licence fees and eliminate plate registration

Document 86 of 252

Today's Tories lacking in vision, class and accomplishments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-tories-lacking-vision-class/docview/2926637011/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+Tories+lacking+in+vision%2C+class+and+accomplishments&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nostalgia can be a powerful part of the human experience.
How many of us have not looked back and said "things were better back in the day?" When we look at things objectively, however, in most cases I am sure we would agree that modern times are better. There is less racial and sexual discrimination. We are far more aware of the impact of pollution and, as a result, our environment is cleaner. Products such as automobiles are much safer. But one area that isn't better is when we look at conservative politicians.
I was born in the 1960s and became politically aware in the early 1970s. At that time, we had conservative premiers in Ontario in John Robarts and Bill Davis. Both were known for moderate but well-thought-out policies. Robarts passed legislation creating GO Transit, the community college system, the Ontario Science Centre, the Art Gallery of Ontario and OHIP and greatly expanded the public school system. He also balanced the budget regularly.
Davis was Robarts's education minister during these times and then, as premier, he created the first Ontario environment minister portfolio, he was a key player in creating Canada's new constitution and he gave full funding to the Roman Catholic school system. Prime Minister Brian Mulroney appointed him to a commission in 1985 to assist in solving the acid rain problem.
Both men felt government could be a source for good in people's lives. Even their opponents considered them kind, decent gentlemen.
The federal Progressive Conservative Party was led by Robert Stanfield and later Joe Clark, both of whom were viewed in the same way as Robarts and Davis. In the United States, Republican Richard Nixon was in the White House from 1969 to 1974. Nixon, to be sure, had his share of faults, but he was held in very high regard for his foreign policy, for creating the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and for signing into law the Clean Air and Water Acts. He was replaced in 1974 after the Watergate scandal by Gerald Ford - a truly decent gentleman who was well-liked by both parties in Washington.
Now fast forward to today, and what do we see? Doug Ford, a conservative like Robarts and Davis, has been premier since 2018. In six years in office, what has he accomplished or built? To be fair, after a rough start, his leadership during the pandemic was solid. But outside of that? How has his government made Ontario a better place to live? What is his legacy? Robarts certainly left a legacy that even today is recognized and helps Ontarians.
Federally, Pierre Poilievre is considered an "attack dog." He attacks viciously and makes those attacks very personal. To date, he has offered little to no positive vision for Canada, nor any plan as to how he would govern. All he appears to have to offer Canadians is anger and insults. He has nothing in common with gentlemen like Stanfield and Clark.
When we look at the United States, we have to consider Donald Trump. When people looked at Nixon, they saw a truly brilliant, intellectual man who had a streak of paranoia (to say the least), but had a very solid record of legislative accomplishments. Would anyone refer to Trump as a "brilliant intellectual?" (They may well agree with the paranoia.) Can anyone point to even one significant legislative accomplishment of the Trump administration that would be on par with creating the EPA, or environmental laws such as the Clean Air and Water Acts? Would anyone describe Trump as a "truly decent gentleman" like Republican Ford was?
The modern era for conservatives isn't positive. A detailed look at past conservative leaders in both our nations show the modern versions are sadly lacking in vision, class and accomplishments. They will be looking at a legacy of pointless anger and vacuous grievances. How very sad.
CAPTION: Conservative Ontario premiers Bill Davis, above, and John Robarts have a long list of accomplishments. Their modern counterparts? Not so much, Craig Wallace writes.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Subject: Sex discrimination; School systems; Scandals; Art galleries & museums

Business indexing term: Subject: Sex discrimination Art galleries & museums

Location: United States--US; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Environmental Protection Agency--EPA; NAICS: 924110

What will be Ford's legacy?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-will-be-fords-legacy/docview/2926411076/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+will+be+Ford%27s+legacy%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of
five books.

Document 88 of 252

Doug Ford's partisan ignorance has left 53,000 tenants and landlords dangling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-partisan-ignorance-has-left-53-000/docview/2926406609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+partisan+ignorance+has+left+53%2C000+tenants+and+landlords+dangling&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid the biggest housing and homelessness crisis in recent memory, with the premier promising to move heaven and earth (and the Greenbelt) to give people a roof over their head, his government is leaving tenants out in the cold.
Landlords, too.
The backlog at Ontario's Landlord and Tenant Board (LTB) has surged to more than 53,000 cases, compared to barely 14,000 under the previous Liberal government. And it shows no sign of abating, according to a new analysis by Tribunal Watch Ontario that documents a system out of control.
Doug Ford's Tories took power in 2018 with a hidden agenda to purge Ontario's tribunals of anyone inherited from the previous government - no matter their accumulated wisdom. Rather than approve the routine reappointments of seasoned adjudicators, each with expertise and experience, Ford's partisan and paranoid Progressive Conservative government rebuffed, rejected and refused as many as they could.
No matter how apolitical the appointments, almost all were suspect and stripped of their authority. Year after year, as the knowledge gap grew at the LTB, the waiting list exploded nearly fourfold -and the hardship for ordinary Ontarians mounted.
Today, a tenant facing a nightmarish maintenance dispute has to wait more than 14 months to have their case resolved, on average. That compares to a mere two months in 2018.
Yes, even the landlords who might be expected to be well served by Ford's Tories are left holding the bag. Getting an eviction order for nonpayment of rent used to take about a month under the previous Liberal government, but now takes nearly a year.
"There is little justice to be found for Ontarians who have to turn to Ontario's busiest tribunal," warns the report from Tribunal Watch. "The deplorable situation at the LTB is not getting better ... it is a dereliction of responsibility."
I first documented this injustice nearly four years ago, describing how the LTB and other tribunals were chronically short-staffed thanks to the self-inflicted wounds of the Ford government. To be fair, Attorney General Doug Downey inherited the chaos from his predecessor, Caroline Mulroney, who presided over the dismantling of this quasi-judiciary body in the early days of the Ford era at its worst.
In the years since, Downey has thrown more money and resources at the problem. To no avail.
Last year, Ontario's Ombudsman Paul Dubé weighed in with his own report condemning the LTB as a "moribund" tribunal that fails the fairness test. Yet even after Downey painstakingly rebuilt the tribunal's ranks - cleaning up Mulroney's mess - the backlog has only grown worse.
How can this be? Did the system get gummed up by COVID-era disputes and delays?
To the contrary, "the huge increase in the backlog cannot be blamed on COVID-19," the report argues. Evictions were frozen, and there was a 40-per-cent drop in the number of new applications at the height of the pandemic.
Landlord and tenant disputes are about peoples' lives and livelihoods, their homes and their properties. They require good judgment from trained adjudicators, not cronyism and partisan pork barrelling.
"We don't replace our judges when we have a change in government and we shouldn't replace our tribunal adjudicators either," Tribunal Watch argues. It is calling on all major political parties "to support the depoliticization of the appointment and reappointment processes for Ontario tribunals."
At the Human Rights Tribunal, the caseload has doubled under the Ford government, even while the number of full decisions has declined.
It's not just the personnel, but the process. Part of the problem has been a shift, pre-pandemic, to remote hearings instead of in-person facilitators in regional offices that have since been closed down.
The result is more waiting time, down time, and conflict time. Too often, the process goes off the rails when landlords and tenants aren't meeting and mediating in the same room, face to face.
Electronic justice also falls flat when tenants (usually lacking legal representation) are left on hold on a phone line - often disconnected - while their landlord adversaries are lawyered up and teed up in front of their computers on high-speed broadband lines. A mismatch within a morass.
After decimating the ranks of Ontario's tribunals, and then stuffing them with favoured political appointments, the government can't keep up. Throwing money at the problem hasn't helped it catch up.
One alternative would be to create a special supplementary, temporary panel, working in parallel, to take the load off the existing LTB, Tribunal Watch suggests. Nothing else seems to have worked.
"Ford says he stands up for the little guy, but apparently not the single mom in substandard housing or the small landlord who is about to lose his property because the rent hasn't been paid for months," says Kathy Laird, a human rights lawyer who authored the latest report.
"The impact is enormous in terms of personal cost - lost housing, miserable living conditions for tenants, loss of income and property, especially for small landlords."
Day after day, the premier insists his top priority is providing people with shelter. Year after year, tenants and landlords are still waiting for justice.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford greets members of the public following a press conference in Mississauga, Ont. on Feb. 13, 2024.After decimating the ranks of Ontario's tribunals, and then stuffing them with favoured political appointments, the Ford government can't keep up, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Subject: Political appointments; Politics; Human rights; Pandemics; Tribunals & commissions; COVID-19; Tenants

Title: Doug Ford's partisan ignorance has left 53,000 tenants and landlords dangling

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Document 89 of 252

Rental demand will slow, RBC says: Recent report expects government's temporary cap to have larger effect in Ontario and B.C.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rental-demand-will-slow-rbc-says/docview/2926406089/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rental+demand+will+slow%2C+RBC+says%3A+Recent+report+expects+government%27s+temporary+cap+to+have+larger+effect+in+Ontario+and+B.C.&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: RBC says a recently announced cap on new study permits won't lead to an outright decrease in demand for rental housing from international students this year.
But a new report from the bank about the cap finds the expected increase in rental demand from international students could fall by about 50 per cent as a result of the policy.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced a temporary cap on new study permits over the next two years, as the federal government tries to get a handle on sharp growth in international student enrolments at Canadian post-secondary institutions. The number of new visas handed out this year will be capped at 364,000, a 35 per cent decrease from the nearly 560,000 issued last year.
"Even though we're going to be limiting the number of international study permits to about half in 2024 relative to what was issued in 2023, that likely won't translate to an immediate fall in the total number of international students in Canada," said economist Rachel Battaglia, the author of the report. "And because of that, it likely won't lead to an outright decline in the number of rental units demanded by international students in Canada this year."
The Liberal government plans to allocate student permits to provinces in proportion to population share. The report says the cap will have a larger effect on rental markets in Ontario and British Columbia, given international student admissions in those provinces outweigh their share of the population.
If the cap for this year is extended beyond 2025, RBC says it would lead to a decline in the number of international students in the country and therefore relieve pressure on the rental market more meaningfully.
"In the meantime, post-secondary institutions and other stakeholders must find creative ways to grow the student housing stock amid challenges in quickly building new structures," the report says.
The Liberals' immigration policies, including the international student program, have come under heightened scrutiny as the country experiences rapid population growth.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Nojoud Al Mallees The Canadian Press

Subject: Foreign students; Immigration

Title: Rental demand will slow, RBC says:   Recent report expects government's temporary cap to have larger effect in Ontario and B.C.

Document 90 of 252

Battered by Pierre Poilievre, Liberals bet a new name will help them sell carbon pricing to inflation-weary Canadians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/battered-pierre-poilievre-liberals-bet-new-name/docview/2926812843/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Battered+by+Pierre+Poilievre%2C+Liberals+bet+a+new+name+will+help+them+sell+carbon+pricing+to+inflation-weary+Canadians&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The federal Liberals are attempting to redefine the rebate for the price on carbon as an affordability measure, as they roll out a rebrand of their politically contentious climate policy.On Wednesday, a group of cabinet ministers announced the "Climate Action Incentive Payment" will be renamed the "Canada Carbon Rebate," following exclusive reporting from the Star that the federal government was considering a rebrand of the program."If we can speak the language that people speak, because people say the words 'carbon,' they say the words, 'rebate,' right? And if we can speak that language, that's important, so people understand what's going on here," said Labour and Seniors Minister Seamus O'Regan. It's the latest attempt for the Liberals to defend - and reframe - their flagship climate policy in the face of repeated attacks by Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, who have blamed the levy for Canadians' affordability woes. Carbon pricing aims to encourage consumers and companies to produce less greenhouse gas emissions by making them more expensive. Revenue from the price on carbon has been returned to Canadians through a rebate since 2018, which Ottawa is now attempting to emphasize to counter the Conservatives' narrative.That was clear on Wednesday when it was O'Regan - and not Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault - who led the announcement. O'Regan was flanked by Guilbeault, Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau, Rural Economic Development Minister Gudie Hutchings and several Atlantic MPs as he announced how much Canadians will receive in rebates in the next year once the price increases by $15 per tonne."In Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario and New Brunswick, where the federal backstop is in place, families are taking home more money next fiscal year than they did in the previous one," O'Regan told reporters. In Ontario, a family of four will receive quarterly rebate payments totalling $1,120 this year."Without Canada's revenue neutral carbon pricing system, the cost to taxpayers and to the Canadian economy to achieve our climate goals would be far greater," said Guilbeault."Wildfires, floods, droughts, more intense storms - these all come with a substantial cost."Whether this change in approach will affect Canadians' awareness of the rebates and their approval of the Liberals' carbon pricing program remains to be seen. Experts and political insiders who previously spoke to the Star said a rebrand may not be enough to fix the government's communications on carbon pricing.At issue, they said, is a pattern of poor messaging that has not resonated with Canadians amid a cost-of-living crisis, and a Conservative opposition that has filled the vacuum and controlled the narrative."When the economy is in a difficult spot, it's really difficult to be able to talk about climate," Acadia University political scientist Alex Marland told the Star. He and others said nothing short of a full-on advertising push could win back the narrative on carbon pricing, which was a prominent policy for the Liberals in the two most recent election campaigns.It's a strategy the Tories have used in their attempts to frame the price on carbon as a driver of inflation."Trudeau's carbon tax on the farmer who grows the food, and the trucker who ships the food, is a tax on all who buy the food. Now he wants to quadruple the tax," Poilievre says in a recent advertisement on Meta platforms.Recent polling for the Star by Abacus Data suggests Canadians are split on whether carbon pricing is a good policy, even in Liberal strongholds such as Ontario and the Atlantic Provinces. And while 72 per cent of those eligible for the rebate reported being aware of it, only 48 per cent knew it had to do with carbon pricing.On Wednesday, the Liberals pushed back, touting carbon pricing as an affordability measure meant to help Canadians in addition to being a climate policy supported by scientists and economists."We need people to understand that when we talk about 'axe the tax,' we're talking about 'axe the rebates' and people are relying on that rebate," O'Regan said. "That rebate is $1,200 for a family of four in Newfoundland and Labrador, $600 for a single senior. For most people that I know, that is going to stuff that they need."The federal government has been measuring Canadians' awareness of the previously named Climate Action Incentive Payment through its polling on Canadians' attitudes toward climate change since at least early 2023, documents tabled in Parliament show."The previous name was a bit difficult to understand and for many people to remember," Guilbeault said. "This will likely make it easier."Following the announcement, Poilievre and the Conservatives were once again on the attack in the House of Commons as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau promoted the rebate."The prime minister cannot defend his policy, so he is changing the name," Poilievre said. "How much did he spend on consultants to come up with the new name?"
CAPTION: Labour and Seniors Minister Seamus O'Regan speaks to reporters in the foyer of the House of Commons on Feb. 14, 2024.

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Climate policy; Climate action; Environmental policy; Carbon; Prime ministers; Climate change

Document 91 of 252

Chronic homelessness is not a Hamilton problem, but it will be a Hamilton failure if we don't address it.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chronic-homelessness-is-not-hamilton-problem-will/docview/2926178526/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chronic+homelessness+is+not+a+Hamilton+problem%2C+but+it+will+be+a+Hamilton+failure+if+we+don%27t+address+it.&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All across this country, communities are facing an alarming rise in homelessness as Canada grapples with post-pandemic inflation, a housing crisis at a level not seen since the Second World War, an opioid epidemic, a growing population and outdated ideas about why people are living in tents and doorways.
We all know it's getting worse. It's harmful to our unhoused neighbours, our local economy and our civic pride.
We can blame successive past governments for not building enough housing stock, and while we need ambitious immigration with our aging workforce, it's reasonable to blame Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for not having adequate housing and support in place for the large number of immigrants coming to the country.
Likewise, we can blame Premier Doug Ford for removing rent controls, underfunding the Ontario Disabilities Support Program (ODSP) and exacerbating the affordability and rental crisis that is driving people of all ages into homelessness.
But blaming the other levels of government does not absolve us locally from our responsibilities.
Business leaders, developers and mayors nationwide are taking bold steps to build small cabins and start transitioning unhoused people to supportive housing. For instance, Canadian tech millionaire Marcel LeBrun has invested in 12 Neighbours, a planned community of 99 affordable tiny homes in Fredericton, N.B. Similarly, Vancouver city council approved tiny shelters in 2022. Many cities and towns across the country are progressing, and there are best practices to learn from.
Chronic homelessness is not a Hamilton problem, but it will be a Hamilton failure if we don't address it.
Make no mistake, it's a choice to let our neighbours spend each night exposed to the elements.
It's a choice to look past the throngs of cold, sick and desperate people on the street with their haunted faces, hopeless eyes and quivering hands.
It's a choice to see a community of dilapidated tents as a blight to remove instead of people and families who need food, water, supplies and support to survive until they can transition to supportive housing. A baby was recently born in one of those tents.
The good news is that we can make a better choice. A housing first strategy, where support for any mental health issues, substance use or other challenges is provided after a person is housed, has an over 80-per-cent success rate of people remaining housed after the first year. Other countries have done it. They've proven it works to shelter people to be safe, supported and successful. It's the right thing to do morally and economically.
Communities across Canada know a housing first approach works and are coming together to build tiny houses for their neighbours, like a good old-fashioned barn raising. In Hamilton, we keep making excuses: wrong location, wrong timing, wrong process.
Excuses are easy to make, but leadership is defined by the ability to see a problem, communicate a vision to solve it and bring people together to overcome any obstacles and realize the vision. Real leaders step up to meet the moment.
Where are the successful business people, many developers, who financed Mayor Andrea Horwath's campaign? Why aren't they working together to build tiny shelters and help transition people to housing first? Why aren't they using their immense resources and knowledge to help improve our city's safety, economy and appeal?
There has been some big talk and opposition to the Hamilton Alliance for Tiny Shelters' tiny cabin project, but there is no evidence of action. It's easy to point out the problems and tear down ideas, finding solutions and building up is much tougher.
So many communities have taken action, there can be no more excuses about why it can't be done here. Hamilton is becoming a national embarrassment as our mayor, council and business community fail to show leadership on the greatest challenge of our time.
It's time to step up as a community with compassion and commitment and build the shelter our neighbours need to transition to supportive housing. That's what real leaders do.
CAPTION: So many other communities have taken action on the homelessness file. There can be no more excuses about why it can't be done here, Laura Babcock argues.
CREDIT: Laura Babcock

Subject: Leadership; Mayors; Homeless people

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada

briefs: Plate renewals to be automaticHouse votes to impeach

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/briefs/docview/2925928995/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=briefs%3A+Plate+renewals+to+be+automaticHouse+votes+to+impeach&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
Mountie aided 'foreign actor,' RCMP allege
A front-line Alberta Mountie has been accused of accessing police record systems to help a "foreign actor," RCMP said Tuesday. The officer was arrested Saturday and the alleged breach involved records that were not top secret, the RCMP Federal Policing Integrated National Security Enforcement Team said in a news release. Const. Eli Ndatuje has been charged under the Criminal Code with breach of trust, unauthorized use of a computer and breach of trust in respect to safeguarded information.
Plate renewals to be automatic
Drivers in Ontario will soon no longer need to renew their licence plates, as the government is moving to an automatic system. Premier Doug Ford made the announcement Tuesday in response to a question from the Toronto Star, which reported this week that there are more than one million expired licence plates on the roads. Ford scrapped the $120 annual fee for the renewals in 2022, and since then, law enforcement officials have said that far fewer drivers are remembering to renew each year.
United States
Biden urges support for Ukraine
President Joe Biden on Tuesday urgently called for House Republicans to get behind legislation that would send $60 billion to Ukraine for its fight against Russia. He warned that opposing it would play into "Putin's hands." Biden said, "supporting this bill is standing up to Putin, opposing it is playing into Putin's hands. History is watching." Biden's comments came after the Senate early Tuesday passed a $95.3 billion (U.S.) aid package for Ukraine, Israel and Taiwan.
House votes
to impeach
The U.S. House voted Tuesday to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Alejandro Mayorkas, with the Republican majority determined to punish the Biden administration over its handling of the U.S-Mexico border after failing last week. The evening roll call proved tight, with Speaker Mike Johnson's threadbare GOP majority unable to handle many defectors or absences in the face of staunch Democratic opposition.
News services

Ford takes dig at Crombie as he unveils referendum bill

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-takes-dig-at-crombie-as-he-unveils/docview/2925928095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+takes+dig+at+Crombie+as+he+unveils+referendum+bill&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario plans to introduce a law requiring any future government to put a new provincial carbon pricing program to a referendum, Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday while using the opportunity to take shots at the new Liberal leader.
He made the announcement in Mississauga, where Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie was until recently the mayor, and who recent polls suggest could pose a threat to Ford's Progressive Conservatives in the next election.
"People know where I stand on the carbon tax," Ford said at a news conference.
"I've opposed it from the very beginning. My record couldn't be more different than Bonnie Crombie's."

Document 95 of 252

Toronto Conservatives accuse federal party of 'undemocratic' meddling in nomination of Karen Stintz

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-conservatives-accuse-federal-party/docview/2926264393/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+Conservatives+accuse+federal+party+of+%27undemocratic%27+meddling+in+nomination+of+Karen+Stintz&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The federal Conservative party's nomination of Karen Stintz in the Toronto riding of Eglinton-Lawrence has triggered an uproar among local Conservatives, prompted the executive to protest in writing to leader Pierre Poilievre, and led one board member who was shut out of the race to quit.
Lawyer Michelle Simard resigned late Monday night over what she said is an "undemocratic" move by party operatives to bypass a fair contest that should have been overseen by the riding association, not central party officials.
Eglinton-Lawrence is the second Ontario riding association executive to express grassroots party concerns and file a written objection to federal Conservative party brass over a hand-picked candidate.
In a letter dated Feb. 10, riding association president Allan Hoffman told Poilievre, deputy leader Melissa Lantsman and other top Conservative officials that the process by which Stintz, a former Toronto city councillor, was selected "created turmoil and has left a number of board members quite distraught and unhappy."
The Conservative nomination for the riding, which is currently held by Liberal MP Marco Mendicino, is one that at least four people wanted, sources said.
In his letter, Hoffman expressed concerns about the party's "conduct, behaviour and interference" in the riding's nomination process. He said senior party officials decided to close nominations within 24 hours on Feb. 8, without consulting local riding workers, and despite earlier assurances that nominations would remain open until late spring or early summer.
Other candidates were asked to withdraw or step aside, the letter said, flagging "questionable practices involving cutting access to the candidate platform portal before the deadline so that remaining candidates could not properly access it so as to complete their application."
"Taking away the constitutional and democratic rights" of the riding association or its members "is neither democratic nor grassroots," the letter said.
Hoffman asked for a meeting with the party's national council, and a reopening of the nomination period to allow "the democratic, constitutional, nomination process to play itself out," a request the riding association's board members approved at a meeting Monday night.
Their concerns are similar to those raised by Conservatives in the Wellington-Halton Hills area about the party's decision to forgo a full nomination contest and parachute in Parm Gill, a former Doug Ford cabinet minister, as the candidate for one of the two Milton ridings which will be created in an upcoming riding redistribution. Which riding Gill will run in has not been specified because, the party says, they don't exist yet.
Following Monday's emergency meeting, Simard resigned from the riding association board, alleging she had been "locked out" from the website where potential candidates could apply to run for the nomination.
"Why would I give my talent, energy, and precious time to an organization that doesn't want to follow the democratic values and rules?" she said in an interview.
Another would-be candidate, Howard Levitt, said in an interview he never got as far submitting a nomination application because "somebody from the party told me that I would not be allowed to run in that riding ... I was told that they viewed (Stintz) as the candidate."
"Do I think I'd be a better candidate by a country mile? Yes," said Levitt, a labour lawyer, prominent Jewish community activist and Financial Post columnist, who said he has more "status," "profile" and "visibility" in the riding .
Levitt said he was led to believe other candidates might have dropped out of the race in favour of him "because I had a better chance than they did of beating (Stintz) for a nomination, and they wanted more of a champion for our community, which she was not viewed as being."
Levitt acknowledged that party officials have the right to select people they deem as "star" candidates, but said "they should be open about it ... they should at least present a saleable case to the riding executives who control the funds and have a lot of volunteers among them that this is the right candidate for that riding, and not purport to have an open nomination fight and then not."
In an emailed statement, Conservative Party of Canada communications director Sarah Fischer said the request for the party's specific response to the letter and the riding's request for a reopened selection process was "ridiculous," the Star's reporting on the Wellington-Halton Hills riding association's concerns was "overtly biased," and cited the Star's "lazy reporting."
"Karen Stintz was nominated under the Party rules and in compliance with the Party Constitution," Fischer wrote.
In a followup email, Fischer wrote that "Karen Stintz was the only candidate to submit a completed application. All nomination rules and procedures under the constitution were followed."
Several local Conservatives with the riding executive declined to speak to the Star, with some fearing reprisals if they went public.
The Star contacted two other individuals said to be interested in the race, but they had not responded to requests by publication time.
Stintz was not available for comment on Tuesday afternoon.
CAPTION: Former city councillor Karen Stintz will be the Conservative candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence in the next federal election.

Document 96 of 252

Doug Ford government scrapping licence plate renewals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-scrapping-licence-plate/docview/2925580131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+scrapping+licence+plate+renewals&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford will eliminate the need to renew licence plates after the Star revealed there are more than one million expired plates on Ontario roads.
"We're getting rid of that totally - registering your vehicle," Ford told reporters Tuesday at a Mississauga gas station, where he also announced planned legislation designed to thwart a future government from imposing a provincial carbon tax.
"We did the first step getting rid of the sticker (in 2022)," the premier said at a campaign-style event where he attacked newly elected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who stepped down as Mississauga mayor last month.
"Now we're getting rid of the reregistration. They'll be automatically reregistered so people won't have to worry about that at all," he said, adding details would be unveiled later this week.
"We're going to make sure that's very soon, extremely soon."
WATCH: Ford takes digs at Bonnie Crombie during announcement
Dakota Brasier, senior media adviser to Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, said "the process will automatically renew for those in good standing."
But motorists who owe traffic fines or unpaid tolls would not be renewed until those are paid, she said.
Scofflaws will be notified by mail or via email or text that they must pay their penalties or their plates will not be validated.
That means municipalities should not be shortchanged on revenue from parking tickets that haven't been paid.
As first disclosed Monday by the Star, there were exactly 1,015,139 expired plates in the province last month.
That means about seven per cent of the 13.6 million plates on Ontario roads have not been renewed by forgetful vehicle owners - and face Highway Traffic Act fines of up to $1,000.
Ford scrapped the annual renewal fee of up to $120 before the 2022 election, leading to many motorists assuming they no longer needed to keep their plates up to date.
According to the Ontario Provincial Police, charges have soared since the paid renewal stickers were eliminated two years ago.
Last year, there were 15,631 tickets issued to motorists lacking a "currently validated permit for the vehicle," the OPP said.
That compared with 5,341 charges in 2022 and 1,556 in 2021, although the COVID-19 pandemic that kept Ontarians off roads and highways could have contributed to those lower violations.
Removing the renewal charge costs the treasury more than $1 billion in lost revenue annually.
Sarkaria's office has been urging motorists to check to see if their plates are valid at: https://www.ontario.ca/page/renew-your-licence-plate.
Those can be renewed online until the new measures take effect later this year.
All passenger vehicles - including cars, SUVs and vans - under 3,000 kilograms are covered under the policy.
Validation stickers are currently still required for heavy commercial vehicles, like large trucks and snowplows.
Because many motorists still have their old 2022 renewal stickers on their plates, there have been instances of drivers getting tickets out of province because local police believe the plates have expired.
The Canadian Automobile Association has recommended motorists keep a printout of proof of renewal although that is not legally required.
Ford blurted out the looming plate change at a press conference where he touted forthcoming legislation to put carbon-pricing on the front-burner at Queen's Park.
Under the proposed bill that will be tabled when the legislature resumes next Tuesday, any future government would have to hold a referendum before imposing any carbon tax or cap-and-trade system.
It would not impact federal carbon pricing measures.
Ironically, Ontarians did not have to pay the federal levy until Ford cancelled the province's cap-and-trade agreement with Quebec and California upon winning power in 2018. That exposed Ontario taxpayers to the federal carbon scheme.
Speaking to the Star editorial board on Tuesday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the premier's referendum proposal is a bid to shift the spotlight from controversies like the ongoing $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal and the surprise flip-flop on the Bill 124 wage cap.
"I absolutely think that things like the announcement today are just attempts to distract from whatever the latest thing is - like their failure to continue to suppress wages of workers in Bill 124," said Stiles.
"And (the Greenbelt issue) is not gone, there's a criminal investigation under way," she said, referring to the RCMP probe.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement at Seneca College in King City, Ont., on Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Automobiles; Automobile drivers; Emissions trading; Carbon; Roads & highways; Vehicles; Environmental tax

Document 97 of 252

How shamelessly has Doug Ford ground down Ontario's colleges and universities? Let me count the ways

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-shamelessly-has-doug-ford-ground-down/docview/2925411707/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+shamelessly+has+Doug+Ford+ground+down+Ontario%27s+colleges+and+universities%3F+Let+me+count+the+ways&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The education of Doug Ford comes at a high price.
Not for him - the premier is doing just fine.
But under his stewardship, post-secondary education has spiralled from crisis to catastrophe. It is a disaster of Ford's own making, with implications that go far beyond Ontario's colleges and universities.
Here's the problem with the premier's post-secondary playbook: He's been playing with other people's money - a shell game - while gambling on the outcomes.
What looked like a good deal for Ford has become a bad bet for the entire province. The chaos over surging foreign enrolments on campus, amped up by the premier, has created panicky headlines.
But if you dig a little deeper, the crisis has also created an unsavoury windfall for the province: A "head tax" on foreign students, on top of a shell game for colleges, which together buff up the provincial treasury by hundreds of millions of dollars.
That's on top of the savings for Ford's Progressive Conservatives by freezing funding for higher education in Ontario at a time of rising inflation (disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at Toronto Metropolitan University and also at the University of Toronto's Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy).
Upon taking power in 2018, Ford ordered every post-secondary institution to cut tuition by 10 per cent - without making up the shortfall. Those fees have been frozen ever since, while government funding stagnated year after year - inflation be damned.
Ontario's colleges, left to fend for themselves after the tuition cut and freeze, were encouraged to make up the difference by recruiting high-paying foreigners with abandon. The inflated cash flow propelled all but one of Ontario's 24 colleges into sudden surpluses worth more than $660 million in 2022.
Conveniently, that windfall benefited Ford's Tories because the colleges' balance sheets show up on the books of the provincial treasury. That's a sweet deal for Ford and his surplus-addicted finance minister, Peter Bethlenfalvy.
But the province's universities, most of which resisted the temptation to feast on foreign students - are now in dire financial straits, with 10 of 23 now running deficits.
Happily for Ford's Tories, those university deficits are seemingly not the province's problem - neither fiscally nor politically. That's because any university's red ink doesn't show up on the province's balance sheet, since they are deemed autonomous institutions (unlike colleges which report directly to the government).
It's an accident of history that manifests as an accounting quirk. But it amounts to a handy political payoff for the premier.
Moreover, Ford's Tories have been shamelessly milking foreign students with a de facto "head tax," which is dressed up as an international "recovery fee" for every warm body lured to Ontario. It's not a massive amount - more than $140 million a year - but it's profiteering all the same.
Campuses are at the breaking point. Communities are at the boiling point over housing shortages exacerbated by an unplanned foreign influx.
Forced into action by the province's inaction, the federal government imposed a cap on foreign students and work permits last month. With cascading abuses, Ottawa had little choice but to act - Ontario broke the system, and now Queen's Park has to take responsibility for fixing it.
The boom will fall especially hard on Ontario, which already fills 51 per cent of the permits with only 39 per cent of the population. And it will hit universities harder than colleges and private educational institutions, which cornered an outsized share of permits driven by a strategy of greedy growth.
Will the government play hardball with universities when it comes to those scarce international permits, in order to protect the foreign cash flow of colleges who are already Ford's preferred partners for his policy of promoting the skilled trades?
Ontario had been warned for years that its campus cash flow was overly exposed to foreign risk, amid diplomatic tensions with source countries such as Saudi Arabia, China and now India. The remedy is to start paying our own way, instead of recruiting rich (or indebted) foreigners to subsidize our world class education system.
Last year, it seemed the premier had seen the light. The Tories set up a fancy-sounding Blue Ribbon Panel on Post-secondary Education that quickly focused on fixing the distorted bottom line with straightforward advice:
Stop cutting tuition and stop freezing funding.
"The sector's financial sustainability is currently at serious risk, and it will take a concerted effort to right the ship," its report warned in November.
The outside panel recommended a one-time tuition hike of five per cent in 2024-25, followed by increases of two per cent (or higher, tied to inflation) thereafter. By the panel's calculations, it would take a tuition increase of 25 per cent to make up for lost monies - politically unpalatable, so it urged the Ford government to increase funding by about 10 per cent with subsequent increases of at least two per cent (plus inflation).
Its report urged the government to confront its growing addiction by moving to "reduce or eliminate the student recovery fee for international students paid by colleges," amounting to $750 a year.
Ford's reaction? Pull out the populist playbook:
"I just don't believe this is the time to go into these (Ontario) students' pockets, especially the ones that are really struggling, and ask for a tuition increase," he told reporters last month, calling instead for more "efficiencies."
Let's not confuse efficiencies with distortions. By profiting from the penury of post-secondary institutions - boosting his own bottom line while starving universities and contorting colleges - Ford is giving the province a costly lesson about false economies.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement at Seneca College in King City, Ont., on Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Colleges & universities; Funding; College campuses; Education; Cash flow

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities Cash flow

Document 98 of 252

Ontario to repeal wage-cap law after loss in Appeal Court

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-repeal-wage-cap-law-after-loss-appeal/docview/2925411614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+to+repeal+wage-cap+law+after+loss+in+Appeal+Court&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hours after the province's highest court ruled an Ontario wage-restraint law unconstitutional, Premier Doug Ford's government announced Monday it would repeal the controversial bill in its entirety.
The law from Premier Doug Ford's government - known as Bill 124 - capped salary increases for public-sector workers to one per cent a year for three years.
The Court of Appeal for Ontario found the law violated the collective bargaining rights of public sector workers, which includes nurses and teachers.
On Monday evening, the government said it would not take the legal fight further.
"The Government of Ontario will not appeal today's Court of Appeal decision and will instead take steps to repeal Bill 124 in its entirety in the coming weeks," the Ministry of the Attorney General wrote in a statement.
Unions hailed the ruling as a big victory for workers' rights and had urged Ford to listen to the court.
The Progressive Conservatives enacted the law in 2019 as a way to help the government eliminate a deficit.
A lower court struck it down as unconstitutional and the Appeal Court, in a 2-1 decision, largely upheld that decision, writing that the infringement couldn't be justified.
"Because of the Act, organized public sector workers, many of whom are women, racialized and/or low-income earners, have lost the ability to negotiate for better compensation or even better work conditions that do not have a monetary value," the court wrote in its majority opinion released earlier Monday.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

Document 99 of 252

A transgender policy rooted in politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/transgender-policy-rooted-politics/docview/2925411360/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+transgender+policy+rooted+in+politics&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It isn't the argument we could have predicted making and yet here we are. When it comes to transgender rights in Canada, other leaders in the nation would be wise to take a page out of Premier Doug Ford's book.
When asked this month if he would follow Alberta's lead on the issue of trans rights, Premier Ford responded with a definitive "no." "We have a law here," he said, "we're leaving everything alone."
Unfortunately, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is not leaving everything alone. On the contrary, Smith, who has an ancient Sumerian symbol for "liberty" tattooed on her forearm, intends to encroach on the liberty of others: in this case, gender-nonconforming youth and their parents.
Smith recently announced a host of proposed policies that seek to curb transgender rights and access to health care. They include a policy that would demand parental consent for students 15 and under who wish to change their names and pronouns at school. Students aged 16 and 17 will not need consent "but their parents will be notified."
Other policies include a ban on gender-affirming surgery for anyone under 17, as well as a ban on hormone treatment for those under 15. In Smith's Alberta, trans women will be prohibited from competing against cisgender women in single-sex sports leagues. And schools, the premier said, will require "parental notification and an opt in requirement for each instance" that gender and sexual orientation are addressed in class.
Although some of Smith's proposals echo pronoun policies in Saskatchewan and New Brunswick, the Alberta premier's gender-identity policy push marks the boldest effort by a Canadian leader to crack down on transgender rights.
These policies target a tiny and profoundly marginalized group. According to one Canadian study, transgender youth are five times more likely than their peers to contemplate suicide, and seven times more likely to attempt it. According to census data, 0.0037 per cent of Albertans over 15 identify as transgender or non-binary. Yet LGBTQ youth represent 25 to 40 per cent of Canada's homeless youth population.
This is because contrary to Smith's recent assertion it is "rare" for parents to reject or abuse a child on account of their gender identity, it is not nearly rare enough. It is almost certain that Smith's pronoun-consent policies will "out" children and teens to disapproving or abusive parents. And it is certain that the leader's health-care bans will deny trans people the medical care they require to live in dignity.
According to the Alberta Medical Association's Section of Pediatrics, which strongly opposes the premier's policies, gender-affirming care is safe for minors. "The effects of puberty-blocking agents are not irreversible," the AMA writes, "and once treatment stops, puberty goes forward. Treatment allows the patient time to determine their options without permanent effects. Puberty blocking actually has benefits for gender-divergent patients by preventing development of mature secondary sex characteristics so that, later in life, the most invasive gender-affirming surgery may not be necessary if the patient moves forward with gender-affirming care."
The premier may be correct when she asserts that internationally, "there is divergence in the medical community" regarding appropriate gender-affirming care for minors. However, if this is the case, surely medical experts are best suited to determine the appropriate treatment options for their patients, not an ideologically driven premier. As for "bottom surgery," which Smith says she will ban for minors, there is no need for such a ban because the procedure is already off limits to minors across Canada.
In other words, Smith's policies represent a solution in search of a problem. It is telling that when the premier is asked why she wishes to implement these rules - and why now - she consistently falls back on anecdotes about transgender people who regret their medical choices.
These anecdotes may be compelling, but health policy is not written in response to anecdotes. It should be dictated by facts and data. This is what transgender youth in Alberta deserve.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Ford government on track for $4.5B budget deficit this year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-on-track-4-5b-budget-deficit-this/docview/2925712616/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Ontario is on track for a $4.5-billion deficit in the fiscal year ending March 31, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Monday as he presented the province's third quarter results.
That figure is $1.1 billion less than the finance minister forecast in his fall economic statement in November, thanks to increased tax and other government revenue and lower debt interest.
However, the deficit projection remains more than three times higher than the $1.3-billion forecast in Bethlenfalvy's spring budget last year.
He pledged to deliver his next budget by the end of March.

'THROW THEM AWAY': More salads sold at No Frills, Food Basics, Metro, Loblaws, Shoppers Drug Mart, Loblaws, Fortinos, Real Canadian Superstore and other grocery stores recalled over Listeria fears

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/throw-them-away-more-salads-sold-at-no-frills/docview/2925712566/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27THROW+THEM+AWAY%27%3A+More+salads+sold+at+No+Frills%2C+Food+Basics%2C+Metro%2C+Loblaws%2C+Shoppers+Drug+Mart%2C+Loblaws%2C+Fortinos%2C+Real+Canadian+Superstore+and+other+grocery+stores+recalled+over+Listeria+fears&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) is warning grocery shoppers about more salads that have been recalled at some of the country's most popular grocery stores.
Less than a week after a major recall of salad kits impacting Costco, No Frills and other big-name grocers, the CFIA said more salad kits are being pulled from shelves due to possible Listeria monocytogenes contamination.
This time, Dole brand salad kits and another President's Choice brand salad kit is being recalled.
Dole's Chop Kit - Avocado Ranch, and Suprême Kit - Southwest Salad, are being recalled. Here are the affected products:
Metro spokesperson Stephanie Bonk confirmed the Dole salads have been removed from Metro and Food Basics stores, while Sobeys spokesperson Tshani Jaja said "all products pertaining to this recall have been removed from our warehouses" and said a recall notice was sent to Sobeys-owned Voilà warehouse.
Loblaw Companies Limited confirmed the President's Choice salad kit were sold in Ontario at Real Canadian Superstore, Fortinos, Loblaw, Valu-Mart, Your Independent Grocer, Zehrs, No Frills, Affiliate, Wholesale Club, BOX and Shoppers Drug Mart.
Here are the affected stores in other parts of Canada:
Atlantic: Atlantic Superstore, Dominion, Atl Your Ind Grocer, No Frills, Affiliate, Wholesale Club, Shoppers Drug Mart
Québec: Loblaw, Provigo, Ind Franchise, Maxi, Maxi & Cie, Axep, Intermarche, Wholesale Club, Shoppers Drug Mart
West: Superstore, Ind Franchise, Your Ind Grocer, Extra Foods, No Frills, Gas Bar, Independents(Retail), Retail RCWC, Wholesale Club, BOX, Shoppers Drug Mart
"Customers who purchased the impacted product (at the Loblaw-owned locations) are urged to follow the guidance of the recall notice and return applicable products for a refund," according to a Loblaw spokesperson.
The CFIA is telling shoppers to to contact a doctor if you think you became sick from eating a recalled product, and check to see if you have recalled products.
"Do not consume, serve, use, sell, or distribute recalled products," the "food recall warning" states. "Recalled products should be thrown out or returned to the location where they were purchased."
The CFIA says this recall is once again linked to a U.S. outbreak investigation of Listeria infections linked to dairy products manufactured by Rizo Lopez Foods.
"Check your refrigerators and freezers for any recalled products and throw them away," according to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA). "If you froze a product without the original packaging and can't tell if it is part of the recalls, throw it away."
In the U.S., there have been 26 illnesses to date, 23 hospitalizations and two deaths, according to the FDA.
CAPTION: Salad kit recallSome PC salad kits at No Frills and other stores being pulled from shelves.

Document 102 of 252

In hard times Ford is throwing billions of good money after bad

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hard-times-ford-is-throwing-billions-good-money/docview/2925082581/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+hard+times+Ford+is+throwing+billions+of+good+money+after+bad&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We live in unprecedented times.
A crisis in affordability in every level of health care and education, housing, rent, homelessness and record food bank use, all topped off by the climate crisis.
According to the Financial Accountability Office, Doug Ford's government has spent up to $6.9 billion annually to artificially lower, artificially high hydro rates since first being elected in 2018. The FAO reports Ford is going to spend another $6.8 billion this year and up to $7.1 billion next year for a total of more than $47 billion hiding and protecting Mike Harris's hydro deregulation scheme.
Ford has not kept his promise to lower rates 12 per cent, not by even one per cent.
How did we get here?
In 1995, the Harris government was elected without a single word about hydro deregulation in its campaign. Then, with absolutely no mandate, the Harris government eliminated your publicly owned hydroelectric system.
The first bill Harris' government passed was Bill 26, the "Savings and Restructuring Act." It was a massive bill, amending dozens of existing laws, granting arbitrary new powers to cabinet ministers, allowing them to rule by decree with no public consultation.
Inside this bill, the law requiring a binding public referendum any time a public asset was to be changed or sold was removed, then declaring Ontario Hydro was bankrupt because of so much debt, it had to be deregulated. Hydro was in debt, but by far the majority of that was nuclear debt showing just how expensive it is to build and run nuclear power.
The Harris government forgot to mention the final Ontario Hydro financial report from March 1999 showed $1 billion of its debt had been paid down and was on track to be paid off in 20 years, showing how false Harris's claim of bankruptcy was. At the time, critics said, "wouldn't we all like to have a 20-year mortgage on our home because then you have an asset that serves you well!"
Then in 1998, the Harris government passed three bills: the Energy Competition Act, the Electricity Act and the Ontario Energy Board Act. These were really just one very elaborate piece of legislation broken into three for easy passage with little scrutiny.
These bills changed every hydro utility in the province from a non-profit commission into for-profit corporations and the creation of the electricity market, all the while promising "lower rates."
This legislation also by gave the government the power to make decisions on hydro by decree. In 2002, after losing a court case that proved the government did not have the legal right to sell hydro, the Conservatives marched right back into the legislature and passed Bill 58, giving themselves that legal right.
Then, despite spectacular Enron market failures in California and around the world, Harris had Enron and a who's who of the banking and investment community design Ontario's electricity market. This is the main cause behind today's artificially high hydro rates.
Despite the promise of lower rates, rates had doubled by 2007. By 2010, rates tripled and by the time Ford is elected in 2018, rates had quadrupled.
Deregulation is working very well for the private investors who designed it, but not for us.
In a time of crisis, Ford continues to spend billions artificially lowering artificially high hydro rates, ensuring private producers and for-profit operators get their profits and taxpayers get the bill.
This market must be closed and rates must be regulated. Much of Harris' hydro deregulation must also be dumped, because electricity is so central to the climate crisis.
No matter who succeeds Ford, like it or not they will have to clean up and fix this 26-year-old mess.
CAPTION: In a time of crisis, the premier continues to spend billions artificially lowering artificially high hydro rates, Paul Kahnert writes.
CREDIT: Paul Kahnert

Subject: Electricity; Deregulation

Business indexing term: Subject: Deregulation
