Document 103 of 252

Ottawa should follow Ontario's lead and freeze beer taxes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-should-follow-ontarios-lead-freeze-beer/docview/2925082580/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+should+follow+Ontario%27s+lead+and+freeze+beer+taxes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Friday, the province of Ontario announced it is freezing the 4.6 per cent scheduled increase in beer taxes, and will hold off on any tax increases until 2026.
This is great news for beer drinkers in Ontario, but because of similar policies at the federal level, the national excise tax on all alcohol is scheduled to increase by 4.6 per cent on April 1.
That tax, the escalator tax, is indexed to inflation and gives all Canadians every year an unwelcome April Fools gift.
Add this tax hike to the fact that taxes alone account for around 50 per cent of the price of beer, 65 per cent of the price of wine, and 75 per cent of the price of spirits. This is cruel punishment for the crime of wanting to enjoy an alcoholic beverage and socialize, or relax.
Rather than have the tax increase again this April 1, Ottawa should follow Ontario Premier Doug Ford's lead and pause the escalator tax. The escalator tax removes policy discussion from the democratic process and eliminates consumers from the debate all together.
And by indexing taxation to inflation, it uncomfortably punishes consumers for inflationary pressures, and actually adds upwards pressure on inflation.
Ironically, having taxation automatically increase prices puts continued upward pressure on overall inflation, and the longer these inflationary times persist, the longer it will take for the Bank of Canada to begin cutting interest rates.
This is a vicious cycle where inflation indexed taxation fuels the problem of inflation, driving rates higher, making mortgages more expensive, and leaving everyone poorer in the long run, except the federal government.
And when we compare how alcohol is taxed in the United States versus Canada, it feels like we're rubbing salt in the wounds of Canadian consumers.
For the average American, buying a case of beer has $4.12 in taxes associated with it. For the average Canadian, the tax paid on that same case of beer is over five times higher, at $20.31.
The federal tax rate on beer in Canada is 2.8 times higher than in the United States, while the average provincial tax rate is over six times higher than the average U.S. state tax rate.
Of course, there have to be taxes on alcohol, but do taxes really need to be this high? And do they need to be mandated to increase each and every year with an escalator tax?
In the announcement for Ontario's pause, Ford said; "Our government is constantly looking for ways to make life more affordable for Ontario families by putting more money back into their pockets."
Wouldn't it be nice for Ottawa to do the same?
And what makes the prospect of a pause even more possible is the fact that Ottawa has shown an openness for giving consumers a break in the past. In 2022, Minister Chrystia Freeland's office did the right thing and eliminated the excise tax on non-alcoholic beer, and last year the government capped the escalator tax at two per cent.
If there isn't an appetite to follow Ford's lead entirely, Ottawa could simply repeat what they did last year and again cap the escalator tax at two per cent. With general inflation currently sitting at 3.4 per cent, a two per cent cap would put downward pressure on the consumer price index, which is the Bank of Canada's primary metric for deciding where its key interest rate should be.
Two per cent also happens to be the Bank of Canada's target inflation rate, which begs the question: if it is good enough for the Bank of Canada, is that not good enough for any tax that is indexed to inflation?
Inflation has brought havoc across the Canadian economy over the past few years, and Ottawa has the opportunity to either pause or cap the escalator tax and give Canadians something to raise a glass to.
CAPTION: The province has announced it is freezing the 4.6 per cent scheduled increase in beer taxes. Ottawa should do the same, says David Clement.
CREDIT: David Clement

Subject: Taxation; Tax rates; Alcohol; Excise taxes; Consumers; Beer; Interest rates; Elevators & escalators; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxation Tax rates Excise taxes Consumers Interest rates Tax increases

Document 104 of 252

Doug Ford's wage restraint legislation is unconstitutional, appeal court finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-wage-restraint-legislation-is/docview/2925082271/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+wage+restraint+legislation+is+unconstitutional%2C+appeal+court+finds&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a victory for nurses, teachers and thousands of unionized public sector workers, Ontario's top court has upheld a ruling that Premier Doug Ford's wage restraint legislation is unconstitutional.
The Court of Appeal released a long-awaited decision Monday finding the Progressive Conservative government infringed upon Charter rights to collective bargaining with Bill 124.
The legislation capped wage increases for most public sector workers at one per cent annually for three years and has been blamed for an exodus of health-care workers during the COVID-19 pandemic as they toiled long hours at the risk of their own health.
"Ontario has failed to demonstrate that the Act is minimally impairing of the respondents' collective bargain rights," said the court's decision.
Unifor President Lana Payne hailed the decision, calling it "a victory for free and fair collective bargaining."
Even as public sector unions awaited the ruling, labour arbitrators have been awarding some public sector workers retroactive pay in what has been a period of high inflation because their contracts included "reopener" clauses.
The latest such award was an extra 6.5 per cent for members of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union.
Nurses, some teachers and hospital workers including cooks, lab technicians, personal support workers and dietary aides have also been awarded pay increases.
The provincial government had appealed a 2022 decision by the Ontario Superior Court of Justice that the wage restraint legislation - passed as an austerity measure to help balance the budget before the COVID-19 pandemic emerged - violated Charter rights to collective bargaining.
"On my view of the evidence, Ontario was not facing a situation in 2019 that justified an infringement on Charter rights," Justice Markus Koehnen wrote in his original ruling.
"Unlike other cases that have upheld wage restraint legislation, Bill 124 sets the wage cap at a rate below which employees were obtaining in free collective bargaining negotiations."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement at Seneca College, in King City, Ont. on Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Legislation; Charters; Public sector; Collective bargaining; Pandemics; COVID-19

Business indexing term: Subject: Charters Public sector Collective bargaining

Title: Doug Ford's wage restraint legislation is unconstitutional, appeal court finds

Document 105 of 252

More than one million Ontario drivers with expired licence plates are risking $1,000 fines. Are you one of them?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/more-than-one-million-ontario-drivers-with/docview/2924895881/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+than+one+million+Ontario+drivers+with+expired+licence+plates+are+risking+%241%2C000+fines.+Are+you+one+of+them%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There are more than one million expired licence plates in Ontario because motorists are forgetting to renew them now that they're free, the Star has learned.
Even though Premier Doug Ford scrapped the annual renewal fee of up to $120 before the 2022 election, vehicle owners still must keep their plates up to date or face Highway Traffic Act fines of as much as $1,000.
But about seven per cent of the 13.6 million plates on Ontario roads have not been renewed by forgetful permit holders - leading to a rise in charges.
As of January, there were exactly 1,015,139 expired plates in the province.
That's an improvement over the 1,107,098 that were not up to date in December.
A spokesperson for Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria urged motorists to check at ontario.ca/page/renew-your-licence-plate to see if their plates were valid.
"We continue to encourage individuals to renew online at Ontario.ca/Renew if their licence plate is expired or cancelled," said Dakota Brasier.
Brasier said the government "has made life more affordable and convenient for over eight million vehicle owners by eliminating licence plate renewal fees" and is working toward other ways to make it easier to keep plates validated.
"Also, individuals can sign up for digital reminders to receive their renewal notices by email or text," she noted.
According to the Ontario Provincial Police, Highway Traffic Act charges have skyrocketed since the paid renewal stickers were phased out two years ago.
In 2023, there were 15,631 tickets issued to motorists lacking a "currently validated permit for the vehicle," said the OPP's Robert Simpson.
That compares with 5,341 charges in 2022 and 1,556 in 2021, though the COVID-19 pandemic that kept Ontarians at home and off the roads could be a factor in those lower numbers.
Removing the renewal charge in 2022, which costs the treasury more than $1 billion annually, has been a signature policy for Ford as his Progressive Conservative government tries to address cost-of-living challenges.
"We're on a mission to keep costs down," the premier told 1,000 Tory delegates Feb. 3 at the party's Niagara Falls policy conference.
"We cut 10.7 cents per litre off the cost of gas. We scrapped the Liberal road tolls and eliminated the licence plate sticker fee," he said.
Along with the Ministry of Transportation's digital tools, motorists can also check when their plate expires and renew them in person at ServiceOntario outlets.
All passenger vehicles - including cars, SUVs and vans - under 3,000 kg are covered under the policy and a plate can be renewed for up to two years at a time within six months of its expiry date. Plates for motorcycles and mopeds must also be kept up to date.
The expiry date is usually the birthday of the vehicle owner.
Validation stickers are still required for heavy commercial vehicles, like large trucks and snowplows.
Damaged, lost or stolen plates cost $59 to replace and that can only be done in person at a ServiceOntario counter. That's the same charge as for a new plate registration.
Because many motorists still have their old 2022 renewal stickers affixed to their plates, there have been instances of drivers getting tickets out of province because local police think the plates have expired.
The Canadian Automobile Association has recommended motorists keep a printout of proof of renewal in their glovebox even though that is not legally required in Ontario.
When the change was unveiled two years ago, the province said owners could bring their information to any ServiceOntario outlet for a printout free of charge if they need one for travel or employment.
CAPTION: Ontario drivers no longer have to pay to licence their vehicles but they do have to keep registering them. Many people have not.

Subject: Automobiles; Automobile drivers; Fees & charges; Roads & highways; Vehicles

Company / organization: Name: ServiceOntario; NAICS: 926120

Title: More than one million Ontario drivers with expired licence plates are risking $1,000 fines. Are you one of them?

An expensive lesson on the importance of democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/expensive-lesson-on-importance-democracy/docview/2924895591/se-2?accountid=15090

hydro worker and former spokesperson for the Ontario Electricity Coalition that worked to stop Mike Harris's sale of Hydro One.

Document 107 of 252

Ottawa should freeze beer taxes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-should-freeze-beer-taxes/docview/2924895536/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+should+freeze+beer+taxes&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

David Clement is the North American affairs manager for the Consumer Choice Center.
CAPTION:

Document 108 of 252

Justin Trudeau's attacks on Pierre Poilievre not working, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-attacks-on-pierre-poilievre-not/docview/2924336077/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+attacks+on+Pierre+Poilievre+not+working%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals keep taking punches at Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, but a new poll suggests they aren't landing.
The Abacus Data survey, provided exclusively to the Star, suggests a 19-point lead for the Conservatives over the governing Liberals, with the support of 43 per cent of those polled compared to 24 per cent.
The three-point jump for the Tories since the last time Abacus surveyed comes as its latest poll shows the government's approval rating is down by four percentage points, with 59 per cent of respondents saying they disapprove of the job Trudeau's government is doing.
In the survey, Abacus sought to probe people's perceptions of the Trudeau Liberals, putting five phrases to respondents to see which they thought best described the party - including whether the government is focused on the right priorities, listens to Canadians and is transparent and accountable. For each, more than 40 per cent said "not at all."
The poll surveyed 2,398 adults between Feb. 1 to 7. Online polls cannot be considered truly random, but a comparative random sample of this size would have a margin of error of plus or minus two percentage points 19 times out of 20.
"Overall, the results are not good and underscore just how challenging the environment is for this government and Mr. Trudeau," Abacus Data CEO David Coletto said.
From a cabinet retreat in late January through to the past week, the Trudeau Liberals have upped their attacks on Poilievre to tamp down his momentum with voters.
Just ahead of Abacus going into the field, Trudeau tried to link Poilievre to the right-wing rhetoric of U.S. TV host Tucker Carlson, and accused Poilievre of siding with apologists for Russian President Vladimir Putin.
After Alberta Premier Danielle Smith unveiled policies to restrict medical options for transgender youth, Liberals castigated Poilievre for not speaking up against them, accusing him of being indifferent to the lives of kids.
Trudeau's team also tried to undercut Poilievre's accusations that the government is responsible for high grocery prices, pointing out that the firm of his chief adviser Jenni Byrne has lobbied for Loblaws. The firm has lobbied for the grocery giant in Ontario but not specifically about food prices.
Poilievre and the Conservatives have been punching back. To the accusations on lobbying, they put together their own list of former and current members of Trudeau's inner circle who've also lobbied for the company.
They've blasted Trudeau for using transgender policies as a dangerous wedge issue, and also put the government on its backfoot over its main message for the last week - promised measures to combat auto theft.
Just before the Liberals could unveil a new plan, Poilievre unveiled his own, forcing the Liberals on the defensive.
In an interview with Citytv on Friday, Trudeau sloughed off the current polling landscape for his party in general, pointing out the next election is still a year-and-a-half away.
"We were way behind in the polls before the 2015 election where I won a majority, we were way behind in the polls before the 2019 election. Polls go up and down," he said.
The Tories did, in the end, vote against an updated free trade deal with Ukraine - that vote is the reason Trudeau accused Poilievre of being a Putin apologist. Poilievre insists it is because of the carbon price reference in the deal, and his party still backs Ukraine.
While Trudeau brought that up too in the Citytv interview, signalling his party won't let that issue drop, Coletto said the risk for the Liberals in general is that no one is listening to Trudeau anymore.
"They can't cut through because most people aren't listening anymore to Trudeau and until they start spending money on direct-to-voter advertising, that will be the case," he said.
"The fundamental problem facing the Liberals is not a popular Conservative leader they need to bring back to Earth. It's their unpopular leader who the country is largely tired of."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau attends an announcement at Seneca College, in King City, Ont. on Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Polls & surveys; Prime ministers; Transgender persons

Location: Ukraine

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 109 of 252

Feb. 10: Bike lanes a step forward, tall buildings are the new reality and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-10-bike-lanes-step-forward-tall-buildings-are/docview/2924335990/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+10%3A+Bike+lanes+a+step+forward%2C+tall+buildings+are+the+new+reality+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ignoring the big issues
Why does it appear that Conservatives stick their noses in places where they don't belong, yet obviously avoid dealing with real-life matters that affect the masses in significant ways like the failing health-care system? We've already got one of these "look-the-other-way" types in Doug Ford who still refuses to take on big important issues in this province. So to those that think (Conservative leader Pierre) Poilievre is going to be any different and save us, this example of what he wants to control (the puberty blockers) is and will be what your vote will get.
Robert Panchyson, Burlington
Pierre's silence is deafening
I've had enough of people siding with Pierre Poilievre and getting upset when we question him. He has a lot of answering to do. Is anyone aware that Poilievre has never had a job? No, never, however he seems well versed on all the things (Prime Minister) Justin Trudeau is doing wrong, but doesn't seem to have any answers.
What does he stand for? He is very good at remaining silent when necessary, especially regarding LGBTQ issues where he is so silent its deafening. He's a conservative and we know very well what's going to happen if he gets voted in. Women will lose their fundamental rights to their bodies and queer and trans rights will go right out the window.
One of his Conservative MPs was allowed to invite a dozen people from the "Save the Children Convoy" to the House of Commons. This is Canada's latest convoy that mixes anti LGBTQ paranoia and far-right conspiracy theories. Do you really want someone running the country with beliefs like that? Frankly it scares me and I worry for myself and other women, and I worry for my LGBTQ friends. I'm not saying Trudeau is the answer, but I am saying Poilievre is not.
Jane Farrington, Hamilton
Bike lanes a step forward
Re: Bike lanes are unnecessary here (Feb. 3)
I took offence when reading your letter. I'm guessing you're not a cyclist, serious or otherwise. It appears people against bike lanes are people who don't ride. I'm a senior and I ride 12 months a year. I also consider myself a serious cyclist who not only uses bike paths and trails, but city streets with or without bike lanes. Most of my cycling friends are seniors who also ride in the city using bike lanes. Maybe you're not aware of climate change and haven't noticed that our winters are milder therefore allowing us to ride 12 months a year.
We can only move forward by accepting if not embracing change and increasing the number of bike lanes in our city is a step forward toward a greener environment.
Charles Clark, Dundas
Bike lanes not a priority
Let's look at the priorities of our city council. Our council turned down a request for monies for our hospitals, the institutions with services that serve all of Hamilton and its citizens and others from outside of Hamilton. Our city council approved $66 million for bike lanes.
Hopefully, I am not the only one who sees skewed priorities. The bike lanes serves, when compared to the population of Hamilton, a minority.
The hospitals serve a majority on a 24-7 basis. Where I work part time I see very few, if any, using the bike lanes. At this time it's a waste of money. We are living in a time when there are more people retiring than graduating. More hospital services will be needed as the baby boomers age. A bike lane doesn't help someone who needs medical attention.
I believe it's time to pause funding for special interests and put that money toward more pressing needs. Let's get our priorities straight!
Sam Speakman, Hamilton
No more cranky boors
Re: Canadians should demand better behaviour in the House (Feb. 8)
I fully agree with Craig Wallace that banning heckling in the House of Commons, and introducing respectful, mature behaviour is urgently needed and long overdue. I hope our MPs will work hard to bring this about so that purposeful questioning and answering can take place in our highest level of government. We don't want to be represented by cranky, ill-mannered boors. We want respectful behaviour. Isn't that what we were taught?
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
Time for a paradigm shift
It appears our onerous residential tax burden is finally being recognized for what it is, an affordability crisis that given its current trajectory will only get worse. Laying blame elsewhere will not solve the problem. It is time for an annual legislated tax cap that forces council to make difficult decisions in order to live within our means. Citizens have every right to expect fiscal restraint from its representatives. Unfortunately, it appears the only way to change council's attitude is to make it illegal to fund their personal wish lists on the backs of local residents.
David Borsellino, Hamilton
Standing up for democracy
On Wednesday, federal Conservative and Liberal MPs defeated a motion that could have given regular Canadian citizens a voice in improving our ailing democracy. The motion was supported by the Greens, the NDP, and the Bloc Québécois, as well as three of 117 Conservatives and 40 of 156 Liberals.
Polls show a high level of support (76 per cent) across party lines among the Canadian public for a non-partisan citizens' assembly on electoral reform. The federal Liberals themselves at their national convention last May passed a resolution to urge the government to set up such a citizens' assembly. So much for representative democracy. Those who voted against this motion chose their own political ambitions over representing their constituents and giving citizens a voice. MP Chad Collins (Hamilton East - Stoney Creek) was among the Liberals who did vote in favour of the motion, and I would like to thank him for standing up for democracy.
Heather Yoell, Dundas
Health care in sorry state
Our health care is under attack and all three levels of government are failing Canadians. Our health care has never been in this bad shape in the last five years. There's a shortage of doctors and nurses and to make it worse a lot are leaving the field. That should open the eyes of all politicians.
I don't know of one person that doesn't want our health care fully funded. I couldn't believe that loans are keeping them afloat. I was in shock when I heard this. It's bad enough most have been doing lotteries to buy equipment and that's wrong. Canadians have paid enough in taxes to have adequate health care.
It's time Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and all the mayors sit down and work out a plan to fund our health care and put in pharmacare for all. That means stop paying bonuses to these CEOs, stop sending money to other countries, stop sending money for wars, stop spending billions to buy war planes, start taxing these rich corporations more, and stop giving money to these companies that made a killing during COVID and continue making a killing. Enough is enough.
Jake Lombardo, Hamilton
Taller buildings new reality
Re: Controversial plan for 45-storey tower on Hamilton waterfront heads to Ontario Land Tribunal (Feb. 7)
I am reading this story, and am very perplexed. Resident Scott Patterson thinks this isn't suitable on the waterfront. Look at cities all over the world, everybody takes advantage of the view from lakeshore suites, and enormous buildings go up. People pay quite a premium for that view. I don't even live on the lake, on Market Street, but I very much appreciate my spectacular view of the entire harbour and the Skyway bridge.
With the push for so much more housing, all of us need to expect a lot of buildings going up, and many of them, quite high. I expect a lot more very high buildings on our waterfront.
Robert Coxe, Hamilton
How will Hockey Canada react?
Re: All players from 2018 world junior team remain suspended by Hockey Canada pending appeal (Feb. 5)
On Monday, Hockey Canada stated that "all 22 players from its 2018 world junior team remain suspended from representing the country at any international tournaments where the federation has control over the rosters - including the Olympics and world championships - in light of sexual assault charges filed against five members of that team."
The alleged sexual assault took place after an event celebrating the gold medal win for the 2018 Canadian junior hockey team.
My question for Hockey Canada is if any or all five players are found guilty of the sexual assault charges against them how will they respond? I'm assuming it would be impossible to take their now tarnished gold medals from them. Maybe a lifetime ban from participating and representing Canada at any level at any international tournaments where the federation has control over the rosters?
Mary Elizabeth Toth, Ancaster
Property taxes on the rise
Several times during council's latest tax deliberations, references have been repeatedly made to a "residential property assessed at $385,000, according to the latest provincial evaluation in 2016."
The real estate landscape has changed drastically over the last several years.
No matter what council's "final tax increase" amounts to, once MPAC (Municipal Property Assessment Corporation) does its next round of assessments, taxpayers will undoubtedly see a probable doubling of their taxes.
One silver lining; in the past, this increase was mitigated by phasing in the tax increases over the next four years (until theoretically the next assessments were to be done).
Hamilton property values have seen a doubling and tripling of values (or more) in the past eight years.
If, at the bare minimum, one's assessment is to double, this will essentially result in a double-digit tax increase to every homeowner.
My message to council, if you do settle on a 7.9 per cent increase, property taxes will inevitably have doubled and MPAC will have already done your work for you.
Peter Dalle Vedove, Hamilton
Who should give meds at school?
A friend of mine has a young child with a health problem to which their doctor prescribed a medication that needs to be taken three times a day. The problem is that the child is in school and both parents work during the day.
After gathering all of the necessary paperwork from the doctor, the school was approached to administer the medication (orally). The response was a flat out no and that one of the parents would have to leave their job, go to the school, administer the medication and go back to work. One parent works out of town and the other is in medical support and neither can just leave work. The child is too young to self medicate and needs adult supervision.
What I don't get is why is OK for the school to administer epinephrine using an EpiPen, but not other needed medication?
I'm old enough to remember that schools that had kindergarten to Grade 5 actually had a nurse to take care of medical issues (like cutting oneself fall on the playground) or medication administration. Since that kind of service will never happen again, I believe the responsibilities of care for the children that the nurse did should become the responsibility of the school staff, as they are the caretakers of the children while they are at school without having a parent leave work and lose essential pay. While there some schools where the teachers or staff administer medication it is not the same for all schools.
Time for the trustees and government to set a common rule that applies to all schools regarding student health and medications.
Mike Lacroix, Hamilton
Put kiosks in Canadian stores
Just three questions:
1. Why were Service Ontario kiosks opened at an American-owned business?
2. Why not choose a Canadian-owned business, for example, Home Hardware?
3. Why was it not opened for tender and whose pocket are you lining this time, Doug Ford? Sorry that's four questions. I lied, but the premier can relate.
Patricia Iseppon, Hamilton
Do something about stolen carts
Today, Feb. 2, while walking in Gage Park I noticed there were 10 stolen grocery carts in front of an encampment which has been there for many months. I have contacted the city about this and am awaiting a reply. I sent a picture of the carts as well as they are basically in one area. I have established these carts cost the stores between $300 and $450 each to replace.
Previous letters to our ward have explained that council rejected a proposal to open city buildings to homeless people. Apparently they are now sanctioned. Is also sanctioned that they are allowed to steal private property from retail stores? What if anything is actually being done, other than more board meetings and town hall meetings?
Barbara Weigelt, Hamilton
It's Ford vs. the people
Re: Doug Ford hails Supreme Court ruling on ministers' mandate letters as 'good news' (Feb. 5)
The Supreme Court of Canada's decision on the Ford government's plea that cabinet ministers' mandate letters remain secret is indicative of two issues: the first, somewhat disappointing and the second, certainly a cause for concern and vigilance.
The first is the nature of the judiciary and its role and relationship to democracy. The judiciary is generally conservative, not revolutionary in its decision. This decision is reflective of this and is not necessarily bad and could be changed by the next government.
Democratic reform moves slowly and the court is mandated to provide wise second thought, unaffected by current politics. The courts are not ossified in the past as evidenced by the recent ruling in favour of those opposed to Bill 124, reaffirming the right to collective bargaining. While cabinet meetings should be secret as security, finances, privacy and ministers' ability to speak and debate freely are intrinsic to them. Mandate letters are broad statements of expectations and aims and are more P.R. devices. While their remaining secret is insulting to the citizenry, their publication is not vital to democracy.
The second issue is the radical Conservative trend in democracies and its relentless siege on democracy. This issue, and the Ford government's character and actions are in keeping with this assault on democracy that is world wide. Not since the era of the Family Compact in Upper Canada, has Conservative oligarchy been so aggressive and successful in pursuing the needs of the few at the peril of the many. A consistently strong response by the democratic leadership and an increasingly informed and engaged citizenry are vital to counter this existential threat to democracy.
Gerard Shkuda, Hamilton
CAPTION: Spectator readers continue to debate the wisdom in building more bike lanes in the city.

Document 110 of 252

Federal government signs $3.1B health accord deal with Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/federal-government-signs-3-1b-health-accord-deal/docview/2924183102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+government+signs+%243.1B+health+accord+deal+with+Ontario&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will spend $3.1 billion in federal health-care funding on increasing access to family doctors, reducing backlogs and adding more health-care workers, the premier and the prime minister announced Friday as they formalized a decade-long health accord.
The funding covers three years out of the $8.4-billion 10-year agreement in principle the federal government reached last year with the province, part of an overall $198.6 billion for health funding across the country. Ontario is the fifth province to officially sign on for its share.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said the money will help ease pressures that health systems across the country are facing.
"Access to family doctors and nurse practitioners is not what it needs to be," he said at a news conference.
"Emergency rooms are overwhelmed. People are waiting too long for surgeries and health-care workers ... are under immense pressure, working in incredibly trying conditions."
Premier Doug Ford said Ontario has made progress on shortening wait times and increasing access to primary care, but there is more to do. "This $3.1 billion investment will be used for targeted initiatives to continue building up our health workforce, improve access to family health services, enhanced mental health and addiction programs, and expand access to digital health," he said.
The Ontario Nurses' Association questioned the rosy statistics presented at the news conference by the premier and Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones, including numbers of new nurses in the province.
"If we have so many new nurses, why is it that Ontario is seeing hundreds of emergency departments continuing to have to close due to nurse staffing shortages?" president Erin Ariss wrote in a statement.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Laura Osman The Canadian Press

a step forward

It is time for
a paradigm shift
It appears our onerous residential tax burden is finally being recognized for what it is, an affordability crisis that given its current trajectory will only get worse. Laying blame elsewhere will not solve the problem. It is time for an annual legislated tax cap that forces council to make difficult decisions in order to live within our means. Citizens have every right to expect fiscal restraint from its representatives. Unfortunately, it appears the only way to change council's attitude is to make it illegal to fund their personal wish lists on the backs of local residents.
David Borsellino, Hamilton
Standing up
for democracy
On Wednesday, federal Conservative and Liberal MPs defeated a motion that could have given regular Canadian citizens a voice in improving our ailing democracy. The motion was supported by the Greens, the NDP, and the Bloc Québécois, as well as three of 117 Conservatives and 40 of 156 Liberals.
Polls show a high level of support (76 per cent) across party lines among the Canadian public for a non-partisan citizens' assembly on electoral reform. The federal Liberals themselves at their national convention last May passed a resolution to urge the government to set up such a citizens' assembly. So much for representative democracy. Those who voted against this motion chose their own political ambitions over representing their constituents and giving citizens a voice. MP Chad Collins (Hamilton East-Stoney Creek) was among the Liberals who did vote in favour of the motion, and I would like to thank him for standing up for democracy.
Heather Yoell, Dundas
Health care here
is in sorry state
Our health care is under attack and all three levels of government are failing Canadians. Our health care has never been in this bad shape in the last five years. There's a shortage of doctors and nurses and to make it worse a lot are leaving the field. That should open the eyes of all politicians.
I don't know of one person that doesn't want our health care fully funded. I couldn't believe that loans are keeping them afloat. I was in shock when I heard this. It's bad enough most have been doing lotteries to buy equipment and that's wrong. Canadians have paid enough in taxes to have adequate health care.
It's time Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and all the mayors sit down and work out a plan to fund our health care and put in pharmacare for all. That means stop paying bonuses to these CEOs, stop sending money to other countries, stop sending money for wars, stop spending billions to buy war planes, start taxing these rich corporations more, and stop giving money to these companies that made a killing during COVID-19 and continue making a killing. Enough is enough.
Jake Lombardo, Hamilton
Taller buildings
are the new reality
Controversial plan for
45-storey tower on Hamilton waterfront heads to Ontario Land Tribunal (Feb. 7)
I am reading this story, and am very perplexed. Resident Scott Patterson thinks this isn't suitable on the waterfront. Look at cities all over the world, everybody takes advantage of the view from lakeshore suites, and enormous buildings go up. People pay quite a premium for that view. I don't even live on the lake, on Market Street, but I very much appreciate my spectacular view of the entire harbour and the Skyway bridge. With the push for so much more housing, all of us need to expect a lot of buildings going up, and many of them, quite high. I expect a lot more very high buildings on our waterfront.
Robert Coxe, Hamilton
Put kiosks in
Canadian stores
Just three questions:
1. Why were Service Ontario kiosks opened at an American-owned business?
2. Why not choose a Canadian-owned business, for example, Home Hardware?
3. Why was it not opened for tender and whose pocket are you lining this time, Doug Ford? Sorry that's four questions. I lied, but the premier can relate.
Patricia Iseppon, Hamilton
How will Hockey Canada react?
All players from 2018 world junior team remain suspended by Hockey Canada pending appeal (Feb. 5)
On Monday, Hockey Canada stated that "all 22 players from its 2018 world junior team remain suspended from representing the country at any international tournaments where the federation has control over the rosters - including the Olympics and world championships - in light of sexual assault charges filed against five members of that team."
The alleged sexual assault took place after an event celebrating the gold medal win for the 2018 Canadian junior hockey team.
My question for Hockey Canada is if any or all five players are found guilty of the sexual assault charges against them how will it respond? I'm assuming it would be impossible to take their now tarnished gold medals from them. Maybe a lifetime ban from participating and representing Canada at any level at any international tournaments where the federation has control over the rosters?
Mary Elizabeth Toth, Ancaster
Do something
about stolen carts
Today, Feb. 2, while walking in Gage Park, I noticed there were 10 stolen grocery carts in front of an encampment, which has been there for many months. I have contacted the city about this and am awaiting a reply. I sent a picture of the carts as well as they are basically in one area. I have established these carts cost the stores between $300 and $450 each to replace.
Previous letters to our ward have explained that council rejected a proposal to open city buildings to homeless people. Apparently they are now sanctioned. Is it also sanctioned that they are allowed to steal private property from retail stores? What if anything is actually being done, other than more board meetings and town hall meetings?
Barbara Weigelt, Hamilton
Property taxes
on the rise
Several times during council's latest tax deliberations, references have been repeatedly made to a "residential property assessed at $385,000, according to the latest provincial evaluation in 2016." The real estate landscape has changed drastically over the last several years. No matter what council's "final tax increase" amounts to, once MPAC (Municipal Property Assessment Corporation) does its next round of assessments, taxpayers will undoubtedly see a probable doubling of their taxes.
One silver lining; in the past, this increase was mitigated by phasing in the tax increases over the next four years (until theoretically the next assessments were to be done).
Hamilton property values have seen a doubling and tripling of values (or more) in the past eight years.
If, at the bare minimum, one's assessment is to double, this will essentially result in a double-digit tax increase to every homeowner.
My message to council, if you do settle on a 7.9 per cent increase, property taxes will inevitably have doubled and MPAC will have already done your work for you.
Peter Dalle Vedove, Hamilton

Document 112 of 252

Trudeau and Ford government's finalize health deal with Ontario agreeing to hire more doctors and nurses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-ford-governments-finalize-health-deal/docview/2924041438/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+and+Ford+government%27s+finalize+health+deal+with+Ontario+agreeing+to+hire+more+doctors+and+nurses&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The Trudeau government has finalized a deal with the Ontario government to direct $3.1 billion in more money to improve health care, the Star has learned.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford are to announce the details the two governments have settled on when they meet in King City, north of Toronto on Friday.
In exchange for the infusion of new cash, the Ford government would commit spending to create new primary care teams to boost access to family doctors.
Ontario says it will add hundreds of new family physicians and nurse practitioners, as well as thousands of new nurses and personal support workers.
It will increase enrollment in health-care education programs by 700, with a specific allotment for Northern Ontario.
A portion of the money will be targeted at boosting youth mental health services and substance abuse services in rural, remote, and Indigenous communities.
As well, there will be new funding to modernize the collection of medical data, which should reduce paperwork and improve the system for Ontario patients.
It's the latest example of Trudeau's federal Liberals working closely with Ford's provincial Progressive Conservatives.
Last February, Ottawa set out the four priorities that the prime minister insisted the provinces must tackle to access the extra cash.
While some premiers - especially Quebec's François Legault - objected to strings being attached to new money, Ford welcomed the infusion of cash.
"This is good news," emphasized an official at Queen's Park on Thursday.
At the first ministers' meeting last year, Ontario reached an agreement in principle for $8.4 billion in new federal spending over 10 years.
In exchange, the province pledged to improve access to family health services, including in rural and remote areas; boost the health workforce; provide better access to timely mental health, substance use and addictions services; and allow individuals access to their electronic health information that is shared between the health professionals they consult.
However, that full amount is not on the agenda for Friday's announcement, which is only over three years for $3.1 billion, government officials say.
It will be revisited within the first three years to ensure targets and timelines are being met.
That likely will happen after a federal election that is expected within the next 20 months.
It is unclear what federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, whose party leads by double digits in public-opinion polls, would do with the accord going forward.
Poilievre has indicated he does not believe in Ottawa dictating health policy to provinces.
With the formalization of the agreement, Ontario is the fifth province to have signed a one-on-one deal with the federal Liberal government, after Alberta, British Columbia, Prince Edward Island and Nova Scotia outlined specific plans for how the money would be spent, as required by Ottawa.
A year ago, following his meeting with the premiers, Trudeau offered to significantly increase federal transfer payments to the provinces - by $17.3 billion over the next decade - which included an immediate $2 billion injection to tackle the crisis in pediatric hospitals and emergency rooms, and ongoing wait times for surgeries. Beyond that, the federal Liberal government set aside a separate $25 billion fund over 10 years for the bilateral deals to address those four federal priorities.
Ottawa had given provinces until March to reach deals to get their share of the funding.
Health Minister Mark Holland told The Canadian Press this week that so far, the agreements had invested mainly in community-based care, and provinces have agreed to make health data, including patient records, more compatible across the country.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Ontario's health system in particular struggled to integrate a patchwork of health data collection practices under different regional health authorities
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau delivers opening remarks at the National Summit on Combatting Auto Theft Feb.8, 2024 in Ottawa. Trudeau is expected to join Ontario Premier Doug Ford for a health announcement in King City on Friday.

Subject: Provinces; Mental health; Prime ministers; Health services

Document 113 of 252

Justin Trudeau blames Stephen Harper cuts for auto theft issue, as Ottawa moves to ban high-tech devices used to steal cars

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-blames-stephen-harper-cuts-auto/docview/2924041430/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+blames+Stephen+Harper+cuts+for+auto+theft+issue%2C+as+Ottawa+moves+to+ban+high-tech+devices+used+to+steal+cars&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The federal government said it would pursue all avenues to ban the high-tech devices used to steal vehicles, and opened the possibility of harsher criminal penalties for car thieves on Thursday, during a summit called to tackle auto theft in Canada.
"Organized crime is becoming more brazen, and the international black market for the stolen cars is expanding," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told the summit, which brought together police, federal officials, mayors and industry voices from across the country.
"It's become too easy for criminals to get sophisticated electronic devices that make it simple, for example, to copy car key fobs."
But as stakeholders gathered to discuss the issue, sparring over who's to blame for the growing problem continued in the House of Commons.
"When will the prime minister abandon their soft-on-crime approach so car thefts can come down?" Conservative House Leader Andrew Scheer said.
In his opening remarks at the summit, Trudeau blamed Stephen Harper-era cuts for the issue, as he dismissed the Conservatives' plan as a "catchy slogan" and a "two-minute video" that won't fix the problem. This week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre promised harsher penalties for organized-crime-related auto theft, as well as mandatory minimum sentences for three-time offenders. He also said a Tory government would hire 75 Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) staff and purchase two dozen X-ray scanners to crack down on stolen cars at Canada's major ports.
The Liberals left Thursday's summit with only one new announcement that the government would be banning devices used to steal vehicles through hacking methods, like flippers, according to Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne. On Wednesday, Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc announced $28 million in additional funding to CBSA to tackle the export of stolen vehicles.
Justice Minister Arif Virani said the criminal code already contains sufficient legislation to tackle the issues of organized crime and theft, but that there was "absolutely more to do" to bridge the two.
The Liberals also said they would capitalize on existing tools to attack the issue, and that more announcements, including a concrete plan, should be expected in the coming weeks. Expected actions include increased collaboration among police forces, modernizing the Canadian Motor Vehicle Safety Standards and security plans at ports, the government said.
Those at the summit described the issue as wide-ranging across Canada, especially in the GTA, Ontario and Quebec, and warned against increasingly violent carjackings. The federal government estimates 90,000 cars are stolen annually, many of which end up overseas, resulting in about $1 billion in insurance costs to Canadians.
Summit participants said conversations were meaningful in bringing stakeholders together to identify the root of the problem, but called for more urgent action to co-ordinate efforts.
Brian Kingston, president of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturers' Association, told the Star he had hoped for additional funding announcements.
"The success of the summit is going to depend on whether there will be immediate resources to take on organized crime and shutting down illegal exports," he said in an interview. Last week, the federal government announced $121 million in funding to Ontario to fight auto theft and gun crimes, though Kingston said increased funding is needed across the country.
Bryan Gast, a former OPP officer who's now vice-president of investigative services of Équité Association, told the Star more work is urgently needed to make it more difficult to steal vehicles in the first place, such as through implementing "robust anti-theft standards."
But to make a long-term impact on the issue, a national strategy is required, Toronto Police Service Chief Myron Demkiw said.
"We need all levels of government, law enforcement and private industry, including vehicle manufacturers, border and port authorities and shipping companies, working together in a co-ordinated manner," he said.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with Toronto Chief of Police Myron Demkiw before the National Summit on Combatting Auto Theft on Feb. 8, 2024 in Ottawa.

Document 114 of 252

'DO NOT EAT': National recall on certain salads sold at No Frills, Shoppers Drug Mart, Loblaws, Real Canadian Superstore, Zehrs, Valu-Mart and other stores triggers warning to shoppers across Canada over possible Listeria contamination

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/do-not-eat-national-recall-on-certain-salads-sold/docview/2923474209/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27DO+NOT+EAT%27%3A+National+recall+on+certain+salads+sold+at+No+Frills%2C+Shoppers+Drug+Mart%2C+Loblaws%2C+Real+Canadian+Superstore%2C+Zehrs%2C+Valu-Mart+and+other+stores+triggers+warning+to+shoppers+across+Canada+over+possible+Listeria+contamination&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) is warning shoppers across Canada Wednesday evening (Feb. 7) about a national recall on certain salads due to possible Listeria contamination.
The recall involves President's Choice brand and Taylor Farms brand Mexican-Style Street Corn Salad Kits.
The President's Choice affected product information is: Chopped Mexican-Style Street Corn Salad Kit, 285 g, UPC 0 60383 03491 7, with all best before dates up to and including 2024 FE 19.
The Taylor Farms affected product information is Mexican Style Street Corn Salad Kit (2 pack), 2 x 335 g, UPC 0 30223 06191 5, with all best before dates up to and including 24 FE 19.
The affected products are being recalled from the marketplace due to possible Listeria monocytogenes contamination, the CFIA said in its food recall warning.
In an email to Metroland, Loblaw Companies Limited confirmed Wednesday night the President's Choice salad kits were sold at Real Canadian Superstore, Fortinos, Loblaw, Valu-Mart, Your Independent Grocer, Zehrs, No Frills, Affiliate, Wholesale Club and Shoppers Drug Mart in Ontario.
"The impacted product has been pulled from shelves," a spokesperson said.
Here are the other Loblaw banners in Canada that sold the product:
 Atlantic: Atlantic Superstore, Dominion, Atl Your Ind Grocer, No Frills, Affiliate, Shoppers Drug Mart
Québec: Provigo, Maxi, Axep, Intermarche, Shoppers Drug Mart
Western Canada: Superstore, Your Ind Grocer, Extra Foods, No Frills, Independents(Retail), Retail RCWC, Wholesale Club, Shoppers Drug Mart.
This recall is in relation to an outbreak in the U.S.
The products were made using cheese recalled by Rizo-López Foods, Inc. in association with an outbreak investigation of Listeria monocytogenes in the U.S., the CFIA said.
"Do not eat, sell, or serve recalled brands of cheeses, sour creams (cremas), or yogurts manufactured by Rizo Lopez Foods, Inc.," the U.S. Food and Drug Administration said in its recall warning.
The CFIA is warning shoppers to contact a doctor if you think you became sick from eating a recalled product.
Check to see if you have the recalled products, the CFIA said, and if you do, "do not consume, serve, use, sell, or distribute recalled products. Recalled products should be thrown out or returned to the location where they were purchased."
Listeria symptoms usually start within two weeks after eating food contaminated with Listeria but may start as early as the same day or as late as 10 weeks after, the FDA said.
Mild symptoms may include a fever, muscle aches, nausea, tiredness, vomiting, and diarrhea. If the more severe form of listeriosis develops, symptoms may include headache, stiff neck, confusion, loss of balance, and convulsions, the FDA added.
Pregnant women, the elderly and people with weakened immune systems are particularly at risk, according to the CFIA.
"Check your refrigerators and freezers for any recalled products and throw them away. If you froze a product without the original packaging and can't tell if it is part of the recall, throw it away," the FDA added.
CAPTION: PC salad recallTaylor Farms recallRecall on certain salad at No Frills and other stores.

Document 115 of 252

Professors' logic on basic income exceptionally flawed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/professors-logic-on-basic-income-exceptionally/docview/2923409581/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Professors%27+logic+on+basic+income+exceptionally+flawed&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In "Basic income may not be the answer" (Feb. 6), the authors, two Ontario university professors, criticize those who advocate for a basic income (BI) for all Canadians on the grounds that they are naïve to think that politicians whose commitments are to the one per cent rather than the entire population, would be swayed by rational arguments about its benefits.
They then provide their solutions to the problem of poverty and inequality: increase the power of organized labour; tax the rich more appropriately; reform or transform the entire economic system. All of which require government action to accomplish the same fundamental goals of BI - redistributing wealth from the rich and powerful to the rest.
Why would governments dedicated to the one per cent undertake their suggestions anymore than they would undertake a BI scheme? The result is the same, they would displease the one per cent. This is exceptionally flawed logic.
I doubt very much that advocates of BI ignore issues of power and influence. I also suspect, as with most manufactured dichotomies, this is not an either/or proposition. All these policy options should be undertaken to some extent.
Federal conservative governments will never do this. Liberal governments will tinker at the edges. Canadian voters have always easily fallen prey to propaganda about the NDP, to the extent that they cannot see that this is the party that is most likely to discard the concerns of the one per cent in favour of those of the entire population, and, therefore, have never elected a federal NDP government.
For its part, the NDP has aided the other party's efforts by never being honest enough with Canadians to also tell them that to truly develop an equitable society that provides the services we all seem to want, it is the top 30 per cent that should be paying increased taxes, not just the top one per cent. I think being honest might be worth a try.
CAPTION: Basic Income Now, a campaign organized by a coalition of organizations supporting a basic income, march in Toronto in 2022.
CREDIT: Michael Blythe

Subject: Basic income

Business indexing term: Subject: Basic income

Title: Professors' logic on basic income exceptionally flawed

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 8, 2024

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Document 116 of 252

Professors' logic is exceptionally flawed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/professors-logic-is-exceptionally-flawed/docview/2923261179/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Full text: In "Basic income may not be the answer" (Feb. 6), the authors, two Ontario university professors, criticize those who advocate for a basic income (BI) for all Canadians on the grounds that they are naïve to think that politicians whose commitments are to the one per cent rather than the entire population, would be swayed by rational arguments about its benefits.

I doubt very much that advocates of BI ignore issues of power and influence. I also suspect, as with most manufactured dichotomies, this is not an either/or proposition. All these policy options should be undertaken to some extent.
Federal conservative governments will never do this. Liberal governments will tinker at the edges. Canadian voters have always easily fallen prey to propaganda about the NDP, to the extent that they cannot see that this is the party that is most likely to discard the concerns of the one per cent in favour of those of the entire population, and, therefore, have never elected a federal NDP government.
For its part, the NDP has aided the other party's efforts by never being honest enough with Canadians to also tell them that to truly develop an equitable society that provides the services we all seem to want, it is the top 30 per cent that should be paying increased taxes, not just the top one per cent.
I think being honest might be worth a try.
Michael Blythe lives in
Scotland, Brant County.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Michael Blythe Michael Blythe lives in Scotland, Brant County.;

Subject: Basic income

Business indexing term: Subject: Basic income

Document 117 of 252

Doug Ford is ignoring Marit Stiles. But she's more interested in someone else's attention

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-ignoring-marit-stiles-shes-more/docview/2923260398/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+ignoring+Marit+Stiles.+But+she%27s+more+interested+in+someone+else%27s+attention&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The woman in the black toque clambers onstage in the mid-winter chill, flanked by union leaders braced for battle.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles is introduced as "a friend of hospital workers" to hundreds of cheering protesters, many wearing the same black toques emblazoned with their rallying cry: "Save our Hospitals!"
Stiles starts with a chant - predictable but popular: "Hey, hey! Ho, ho! Doug Ford has got to go!"
Followed by a boast: "We, the people of Ontario, we have managed to push this government over its heels over and over again."
Followed by a promise: "Solidarity" with the workers of Ontario, "not just in good times but on picket lines too."
Know your audience.
Wrapping up her pep talk, Stiles returns to her perch at Queen's Park just up the street. Sitting in her third-floor offices, the Leader of Ontario's Official Opposition ponders her proximity to power.
She has never been so close and yet so far. Stiles sits directly above the Premier's Office, yet is running far behind the ruling Progressive Conservatives in public opinion polls (while also trailing the rival Liberals).
Can she grow her audience?
Away from the crowds and the speeches, surrounded by family photos and political mementoes, Stiles takes stock of Ontario's political realities. In an interview marking her first full year since taking over the party leadership - unopposed, untested, unknown - she talks about her determination to change how the NDP does politics.
"We are the party of working people - we have those connections to unions and labour," she tells me.
No political party can keep preaching to the converted, nor take any constituency for granted, Stiles argues. Not the NDP, nor Ford's Tories as they aim their populist, affordability appeal to working-class voters.
"We are in a really deeply divided time," Stiles says. "I think Doug Ford is making the same mistake - he has been presumptuous, his assumption that he owns the vote of working people."
Stiles claims credit for leading the charge against Ford's attempts to carve up the Greenbelt's protected lands, forcing him to backtrack and respect his previous promises. She rails against the "selling-off of Ontario" and the "piece by piece privatization" of the public sector, especially in health care.
"This is a corrupt government."
But the voting and polling tells a different story. Ford romped to a re-election victory in 2022 and his Tories maintain a commanding lead in the latest Abacus Data poll last month: 38 per cent PC versus 27 per cent Liberal and 23 per cent NDP.
After losing nine seats in the last election, the New Democrats are down another three MPPs - after a humbling byelection loss in Kitchener Centre (the riding went Green after NDP incumbent Laura Mae Lindo resigned early); and the expulsion of two others from caucus (Mike Mantha for an improper office relationship, and Sarah Jama for repeated insubordination).
Despite the downturn, Stiles is determined to lay the groundwork for fresh gains in unheld ridings (the PCs currently have 79 seats, the NDP 28, Liberals nine and Greens two). The Liberals have reclaimed second place in most polls and grabbed outsized media attention last year during a leadership race that elected Bonnie Crombie.
Now, Ford and the Tories are focusing their attacks and paid ads on Crombie. And largely ignoring Stiles.
"They can go ahead and spend their millions of dollars fighting each other, while we take a different path," she muses. "He's gonna spend a lot of time and money defining who the Liberals are, and we get to define who we are. So I'm actually fine with that."
She insists the Liberals lack credibility on public services after selling off part of Hydro One and privatizing ServiceOntario. "They have to redefine themselves, they have to establish themselves."
How will Stiles redefine and re-establish the New Democrats?
"I think politics is changing, it isn't politics as usual - people are looking for something different," she says, citing the record-low turnout of 43.5 per cent in the last election.
"Why are we seeing such a low voter turnout? I happen to believe it's really not a healthy thing to scold people, blaming people for not voting. I think the question is on us as politicians and political actors - why are you not giving people the reason that they need to feel that there's any hope and purpose in government?"
Good question. Many New Democrats and Liberals blamed their own former leaders in the last election for failing to motivate voters, allowing Ford's Tories to outperform the opposition.
Stiles is determined to learn lessons by touring the province non-stop. And consulting with her fellow NDP leaders across the country about their own campaigns.
"I don't know if I'm allowed to talk about this, but I'll tell you anyway: We've connected over dinner recently, almost all of the NDP leaders across the country and the premiers, and it's really inspiring to talk to folks like (Manitoba Premier) Wab Kinew."
Kinew's private advice?
"You have to have patience ... take the time, do the work properly, line everything up."
Her response?
"I don't have patience!"
But she is mindful that politics, especially provincially, is a long game - out of the media spotlight, out of the public eye, out of the bubble.
"I am ambitious, right? I am competitive, I am a little bit impatient," she says, laughing. "We've waited long enough - and I think the people of Ontario have waited long enough."
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles scrums after Premier Doug Ford faced reporters at Queen's Park on on Sept. 5, 2023.

Subject: Privatization; Voters; Political leadership; Union leadership; Voter behavior; Political parties

Document 118 of 252

Toronto Western Hospital getting more beds and operating rooms to help tackle wait times

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-western-hospital-getting-more-beds/docview/2923117382/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+Western+Hospital+getting+more+beds+and+operating+rooms+to+help+tackle+wait+times&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto Western Hospital is getting a new 15-storey tower with 82 beds and 20 operating rooms aimed at reducing wait times for treatment.
The $1.3-billion project at the busy University Health Network hospital, to be completed within four years, will boost the number of beds at Toronto Western to about 400 and eventually boost surgical capacity at UHN by 20 per cent, officials said Tuesday.
"This is a great investment for our patients ... for Ontario," Dr. Kevin Smith, president of UHN, told a news conference where Premier Doug Ford announced provincial funding of $794 million.
The hospital foundation is also running a fundraising campaign to cover the cost. UHN includes Toronto General Hospital.
"It will help shorten wait times," Ford said of the project for which construction is slated to begin in May.
The tower will be located on what is now a small parking lot on the north side of the hospital atrium near the southeast corner of Bathurst and Nassau Streets, with the parking spots moved underground and the existing patient drop-off lane for cars maintained.
It will include critical-care beds and three highly specialized, state-of-the-art operating rooms for complex neurological and spinal surgeries, such as severe brain trauma and complex care for strokes, allowing Toronto Western to expand its role as a leading centre for neurological care.
The funding announcement came as Health Minister Sylvia Jones prepares to take applications from medical groups to provide more diagnostic and surgical care like cataracts and hip replacements in private clinics - a plan that has generated controversy over concerns about more private involvement in health care and the prospect they will compete with hospitals for doctors, nurses and other staff.
Jones said the government needs to pursue both avenues to provide Ontario's fast-growing population with the necessary care.
"It's not an either-or," she told reporters at Toronto Western, noting it was UHN that first proposed the tower project several years ago and made a case for it.
CAPTION: A rendering of the 15-storey tower that is to be built at Toronto Western Hospital. The tower will add 82 beds and 20 operating rooms.

briefs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/briefs/docview/2922392352/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=briefs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: United States
Storm knocks out L.A. power
A storm of historic proportions unleashed record levels of rain over parts of Los Angeles on Monday, endangering the city's large homeless population, sending mud and boulders down hillsides dotted with multimillion-dollar homes and knocking out power for more than a million people in California. The storm was the second one fuelled by an atmospheric river to hit the state in the past few days. About 1.4 million people in the L.A. area, including the Hollywood Hills and Beverly Hills, were under a flash flood warning Monday. Up to 23 centimetres of rain had fallen in the area, with more expected, said the National Weather Service.
Chile
Hundreds of people missing
Volunteers in central Chile tried to remove charred metal, broken glass and other debris Monday from neighbourhoods devastated by wildfires over the past several days, as officials raised the death toll to 122. Hundreds of people remain missing. The fires appeared to have diminished by Monday morning after burning intensely since Friday on the eastern edge of the city of Viña del Mar.
Manitoba
Parents of dead baby charged
Police in Winnipeg have laid manslaughter charges against the parents of a baby who died last year from a fentanyl overdose. Police say paramedics were called in March to a home where they found the one-year-old girl non-responsive. The child was transported to hospital, where she was pronounced dead. Police say Garry Daniel Adrian Bruce, 38, and Sabrina Faye Boulette, 37, were arrested Friday and remain in custody.
Ontario
Consolidated fares a reality
Ontario will subsidize public transit trips so riders can avoid paying double fares when transferring between transit agencies in Toronto and the surrounding area, Premier Doug Ford said Monday. Ford said the move will save the average commuter who uses two transit agencies about $1,600 per year. "This program will be a game changer for transit riders," Ford said. "It will provide people with more transit options and more convenience." The "One Fare" program applies to transfers between GO Transit, the Toronto Transit Commission, Brampton Transit, Durham Region Transit, MiWay and York Region Transit, the province said.
BRITISH COLUMBIA
Robinson quits over remarks
British Columbia's Post-Secondary Education Minister Selina Robinson is stepping down amid an outcry over her remarks that modern Israel was founded on "a crappy piece of land." Premier David Eby says Robinson's "belittling" remarks were incompatible with her remaining in cabinet, although she will stay in the NDP caucus. Eby had faced mounting calls for the removal of Robinson since the remarks last week that infuriated pro-Palestinian groups and other critics who called them racist and Islamophobic.
