Document 120 of 252

Big Oil betting on Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/big-oil-betting-on-poilievre/docview/2921976643/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Big+Oil+betting+on+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Oil and gas companies are starting to say the quiet part out loud: they hope Pierre Poilievre's Conservative party wins the next federal election and eliminates requirements for them to cut pollution or transition to green energy.
Poilievre opposes everything the current federal government is doing on climate change. He is touring the country to oppose putting a price on pollution and lumps in the Clean Fuel Standard and Clean Electricity Regulation as new carbon taxes. He has condemned the proposed cap on pollution from the oil and gas sector, would kill the federal impact assessment act and promises "to support pipelines south, north, east, west."
This amounts to a "burn, baby, burn" climate plan that would tick every box in the oil lobby's wish list.
Oil executives say they are waiting to see the election results before investing in pollution-cutting measures. The CEO of Yangarra Resources, James Evaskevich, was blunt: "If we get to where we're having to spend a lot of money to become way more draconian with our reductions, then we're going to look at the federal election and go, 'yeah no we're not spending that money, no way.' Because our fervent hope is (Trudeau) is gone."
Not to be outdone, a recent regulatory filing by Enbridge Gas stressed "the political and public policy risk associated with the current Federal Carbon Charge" as a reason for the Ontario Energy Board (OEB) to revisit its finding that a transition away from fossil fuels is urgent and inevitable. Just in case they were being too subtle, they added "as Canada draws closer to the next federal election on or before Oct. 20, 2025, the Conservative Party of Canada - which has been leading most federal election opinion polls since mid-2022 - opposes a federally imposed carbon tax or cap and trade system, including the current federal carbon charge."
Enbridge made the comments as part of its fight against an OEB ruling that would encourage developers to put heat pumps into new subdivisions rather than gas furnaces. Yet the evidence before the OEB says the fossil-free alternative would save money for energy consumers, which is a big problem for Enbridge and its ilk.
For the last half century, fossil fuel executives have been able to lobby against environmental measures with an argument that often boiled down to "you might not love us, but you need us."
That is no longer the case. The International Energy Agency says the energy transition is "unstoppable" because electricity from wind and solar energy are cheaper than new gas or coal, electric vehicles are going mainstream and electrically powered heat pumps can keep our homes warm in the winter and cool in the summer at a lower cost than gas or heating oil.
With economics no longer in their favour, oil and gas executives are turning to politics to defend the energy status quo. In the United States, they look to Donald Trump and the Republican party. In Canada, it is Poilievre and Conservative premiers in Alberta and Saskatchewan.
Poilievre frames his opposition to climate action as defending affordability, but green options are now the cheaper choice, and it is the fossil fuel companies trying to keep them from us. The status quo is now the expensive choice. Conservatives had nothing to say when climate-fuelled extreme weather and wildfires drove over 160,000 Canadians from their homes last summer. The denial reached absurd levels in August, when Poilievre had to cancel "axe the carbon tax" rallies due to the danger posed by climate change-fuelled wildfires.
In the age of climate change-fuelled fires, floods and storms, the only politician looking out for us is one with a science-based plan to rapidly transition off fossil fuels and the guts to implement it.
Keith Stewart is a senior energy strategist with Greenpeace Canada and teaches a course on energy and environmental policy at the University of Toronto.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Keith Stewart Keith Stewart is a senior energy strategist with Greenpeace Canada and teaches a course on energy and environmental policy at the University of Toronto.;

Document 121 of 252

Ford takes aim at 'queen' of carbon tax: Premier puts focus on Crombie at PC party policy convention in Niagara Falls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-takes-aim-at-queen-carbon-tax/docview/2921976090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+takes+aim+at+%27queen%27+of+carbon+tax%3A+Premier+puts+focus+on+Crombie+at+PC+party+policy+convention+in+Niagara+Falls&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Falls Niagara Falls may be the honeymoon capital, but Premier Doug Ford wasn't about to give newly elected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie a political honeymoon. Speaking to about 1,000 Progressive Conservatives gathered at the party's policy convention in Niagara Falls on Saturday night, Ford took aim at Crombie.
"Who you vote for, who's in government, it matters - leadership matters," the premier said, as PC cameras rolled for a new campaign-style TV ad.
"I want to talk about Bonnie Crombie just for a minute. Bonnie is the queen of the carbon tax," he said, referring to the former Mississauga mayor who was once a Liberal MP, although her tenure in Ottawa predated Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Grits.
"Bonnie's never seen a tax she didn't like - during her time as mayor she jacked up property taxes and fought Hwy. 413," said Ford, whose Tories were re-elected in 2022 by promising to build the 60-kilometre freeway linking Hwy. 401 in Milton with Hwy. 400 in Vaughan. "Under Bonnie's watch - as the rest of Ontario was growing and thriving - Mississauga's population shrunk for the first time in its history, as people left the city because of her high-cost, high-tax policies."
Ford didn't mention that Crombie, a three-term mayor, was re-elected in 2022 with 77.1 per cent of the popular vote in Mississauga.
But his partisan speech - which criticized the previous Liberal government and used similar language to a current PC ad blitz attacking the new Grit chief - made clear that he viewed her as his main opponent in the June 2026 election.
Ford also noted the Tories plan to spend almost $185 billion over the next decade on infrastructure. "We're leading the largest expansion in public transit in North America, as we bring GO train service to Niagara, Kitchener and Bowmanville," he said.
Crombie said in a statement that Ford was making "more empty promises" to the people of Ontario. "The reality is the only people who are better off after six years under this scandal-plagued government are a handful of Ford's wealthy friends," the Liberal leader said.
CAPTION:

Document 122 of 252

New AFN chief looks to turn page with Poilievre from Harper-era tensions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-afn-chief-looks-turn-page-with-poilievre/docview/2921975438/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+AFN+chief+looks+to+turn+page+with+Poilievre+from+Harper-era+tensions&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The national chief of the Assembly of First Nations is trying to make inroads with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, hoping to forestall the tensions and angst that marked the party's last time in power.
The legacy of the Idle No More movement has shaped how young Indigenous activists and leaders view the Conservatives, an image that still hangs over the party nearly a decade later.
"I want to be optimistic that he will work with First Nations," Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak said of Poilievre. "Young people were so frustrated (with the previous Conservative government), and out of that was born Idle No More. That's certainly not the treaty relationship that I want to see."
Idle No More was a widespread Indigenous-led protest movement triggered in part by the Jobs and Growth Act, a sweeping and controversial omnibus bill introduced in 2012 by Stephen Harper's majority government.
Indigenous Peoples feared the bill would diminish their rights while making it easier for governments and industry to develop resources without a strict environmental assessment.
The movement grew to encompass environmental and Indigenous rights more broadly, and earned widespread support across Canada and around the world.
Woodhouse, who was elected national chief in December, recalls well how First Nations leaders and the federal Conservative government of the day simply weren't sitting at the same table. Her meeting with Poilievre last month went well, she said. Shortly after her election, Woodhouse also posed for photos with Tory MPs when she attended the party's annual Christmas gathering.
She urged Poilievre to vote against Bill C-53, legislation introduced by the Liberals that seeks to recognize Métis governments in Ontario, Alberta and Saskatchewan.
First Nations have mounted fierce opposition to the bill, fearing it could impinge on their own rights.
While Poilievre has met with Woodhouse, he has yet to attend one of the AFN's national assemblies, where hundreds of First Nations chiefs meet to vote on resolutions and discuss priorities they want to take to the federal government. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, whose successful 2015 campaign promised a new relationship with Indigenous Peoples, often shows up, as does NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh.
CAPTION: Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak

CREDIT: The canadian press

Subject: Native rights; Native North Americans

Document 123 of 252

Poilievre's 'Burn, baby, burn' climate plan is emboldening oil lobby

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poilievres-burn-baby-climate-plan-is-emboldening/docview/2922205150/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre%27s+%27Burn%2C+baby%2C+burn%27+climate+plan+is+emboldening+oil+lobby&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Oil and gas companies are starting to say the quiet part out loud: they hope Pierre Poilievre's Conservative party wins the next federal election and eliminates requirements for them to cut pollution or transition to green energy.
Poilievre opposes everything the current federal government is doing on climate change. He is touring the country to oppose putting a price on pollution and lumps in the Clean Fuel Standard and Clean Electricity Regulation as new carbon taxes. He has condemned the proposed cap on pollution from the oil and gas sector, would kill the federal impact assessment act and promises "to support pipelines south, north, east, west."
This amounts to a "burn, baby, burn" climate plan that would tick every box in the oil lobby's wish list.
Canada's powerful oil and gas industry has taken note.
Oil executives say they are waiting to see the election results before investing in pollution-cutting measures. The CEO of Yangarra Resources, James Evaskevich, was blunt: "If we get to where we're having to spend a lot of money to become way more draconian with our reductions, then we're going to look at the federal election and go, 'yeah no we're not spending that money, no way.' Because our fervent hope is (Trudeau) is gone."
Not to be outdone, a recent regulatory filing by Enbridge Gas stressed "the political and public policy risk associated with the current Federal Carbon Charge" as a reason for the Ontario Energy Board (OEB) to revisit its finding that a transition away from fossil fuels is urgent and inevitable. Just in case they were being too subtle, they added "as Canada draws closer to the next federal election on or before Oct. 20, 2025, the Conservative Party of Canada - which has been leading most federal election opinion polls since mid-2022 - opposes a federally-imposed carbon tax or cap and trade system, including the current federal carbon charge."
Enbridge made the comments as part of its fight against an OEB ruling that would encourage developers to put heat pumps into new subdivisions rather than gas furnaces. Yet the evidence before the OEB says the fossil-free alternative would save money for energy consumers, which is a big problem for Enbridge and its ilk.
For the last half century, fossil fuel executives have been able to lobby against environmental measures with an argument that often boiled down to "you might not love us, but you need us."
That is no longer the case. The International Energy Agency says the energy transition is "unstoppable" because electricity from wind and solar energy are cheaper than new gas or coal, electric vehicles are going mainstream and electrically-powered heat pumps can keep our homes warm in the winter and cool in the summer at a lower cost than gas or heating oil.
With economics no longer in their favour, oil and gas executives are turning to politics to defend the energy status quo. In the United States, they look to Donald Trump and the Republican party. In Canada, it is Poilievre and Conservative premiers in Alberta and Saskatchewan.
Poilievre frames his opposition to climate action as defending affordability, but green options are now the cheaper choice, and it is the fossil fuel companies trying to keep them from us. The status quo is now the expensive choice. Conservatives had nothing to say when climate-fueled extreme weather and wildfires drove over 160,000 Canadians from their homes last summer. The denial reached absurd levels in August, when Poilievre had to cancel "axe the carbon tax" rallies due to the danger posed by climate change-fueled wildfires.
In the age of climate change-fueled fires, floods and storms, the only politician looking out for us is one with a science-based plan to rapidly transition off fossil fuels and the guts to implement it.
CAPTION: Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre rises Thursday during question period in Ottawa. Oil executives say they are waiting to see the election results before investing in pollution-cutting measures, Keith Stewart writes.
CREDIT: Keith Stewart

Subject: Election results; Energy; Fossil fuels; Carbon; Climate change; Political parties; Environmental tax

Doug Ford takes aim at Bonnie Crombie in speech to the Tory troops in Niagara Falls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-takes-aim-at-bonnie-crombie-speech-tory/docview/2921791367/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+takes+aim+at+Bonnie+Crombie+in+speech+to+the+Tory+troops+in+Niagara+Falls&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Falls may be the honeymoon capital, but Premier Doug Ford wasn't about to give newly elected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie a political honeymoon.
Speaking to about 1,000 Progressive Conservatives gathered at the party's policy convention in Niagara Falls on Saturday night, Ford took aim at Crombie.
"Who you vote for, who's in government, it matters - leadership matters," the premier said, as PC cameras rolled for a new campaign-style TV ad.
"I want to talk about Bonnie Crombie just for a minute. Bonnie is the queen of the carbon tax," he said, referring to the former Mississauga mayor who was once a Liberal MP though her tenure in Ottawa predated Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Grits.
"Bonnie's never seen a tax she didn't like - during her time as mayor she jacked up property taxes and fought Highway 413," said Ford, whose Tories were re-elected in 2022 by promising to build the 60-kilometre freeway linking Highway 401 in Milton with Highway 400 in Vaughan.
"Under Bonnie's watch - as the rest of Ontario was growing and thriving - Mississauga's population shrunk for the first time in its history as people left the city because of her high-cost, high-tax policies."
The premier did not mention that Crombie, a three-term mayor, was re-elected in October 2022 with 77.1 per cent of the popular vote in Mississauga.
But his partisan speech - which criticized the previous Liberal government three times and used similar language to a current PC ad blitz attacking the new Grit chief - made clear that he viewed her as his main opponent in the June 2026 election.
Ford did not name NDP Leader Marit Stiles or the New Democrats once even though the official opposition party holds Niagara Falls, which has been represented by MPP Wayne Gates since 2014.
"The Liberals chased away 300,000 jobs, they were an absolute disaster," the premier said, decrying "15 long, long years of Liberal mismanagement" between 2003 and 2018.
"Our record is different, we're on a mission to keep costs down, we cut 10.7 cents per litre off the cost of gas, we scrapped the Liberal road tolls and eliminated the licence plate sticker fee."
Ford noted the Tories plan to spent almost $185 billion over the next decade on infrastructure, including Highway 413, the Bradford Bypass and the Garden City Skyway.
"We're leading the largest expansion in public transit in North America as we bring GO train service to Niagara, Kitchener and Bowmanville," he said.
"Shovels are in the ground on the new Ontario Line subway and the Hazel McCallion Line in Mississauga."
That latter project, an 18-kilometre LRT linking Mississauga and Brampton along Hurontario Street, has been fast-tracked by Ford amid fears Crombie could win many of the 11 local ridings the Tories won there two years ago.
While Ford touted that "across the province we're getting more homes built faster so that young families and newcomers can once again afford to buy a home," he pointedly did not broach the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal that is currently under RCMP investigation.
In a statement, Crombie said Ford was making "more empty promises to the people of Ontario."
"The reality is the only people who are better off after six years under this scandal-plagued government are a handful of Ford's wealthy friends," the Liberal leader said.
Mocking the Tories' 2022 election slogan, "Get It Done," Crombie said the governing party has achieved little.
"The Conservatives like to talk about getting it done but, in fact, have spent more money doing less than any government in Ontario's history," she said.
"Wait times in hospitals have skyrocketed, the dream of home ownership has slipped further away and cuts to our classrooms are leaving students behind. Meanwhile, Ford is preoccupied with outsourcing ServiceOntario to major American corporations and covering up how much it will cost you."
Emphasizing that "the people of Ontario deserve better," Crombie said her party would be criss-crossing the province for "grassroots policy discussions" later this year.
"Together, Ontario Liberals will build a team and a plan to defeat and replace this failed government with one that is squarely focused on the needs of the people of Ontario."
CAPTION: Doug Ford, seen at an event last summer, took aim at Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie last night, telling about 1,000 Progressive Conservatives in Niagara Falls that "during her time as mayor she jacked up property taxes and fought Highway 413."As the premier and his caucus met behind closed doors inside the Niagara Falls Convention Centre on Saturday, hundreds of people lined up outside to protest the provincial government.

Document 125 of 252

Without change there is no change

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/without-change-there-is-no/docview/2921739017/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Without+change+there+is+no+change&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: Penny wise, pound foolish (Jan. 31) and Universal pharmacare must address equity in drug access (Jan. 31).
Both columns were well written and informative. Both said that governments should be paying more. But when are we (society), and some of us with published opinions, going to admit that the government pays for nothing.
Everything we ask for is paid by us (society). Whether it is $10-a-day daycare, universal pharmacare, dental care, tiny homes, safe supply of illegal drugs, food banks, free meals in schools, free or subsidized public transit, subsidized housing ... and the list goes on and on.
Frankly, I (probably many of us) am getting tired of reading day after day and year after year about all these problems that are getting worse even though more and more taxpayer money is being funneled in a misconceived notion we (society) are actually accomplishing anything.
I challenge anyone to show me data that suggests in the last 10 years that the number of food banks have decreased, that there are fewer free meals being served in schools, that the number of opioid deaths are decreasing, that the number of homeless are in decline, that housing is becoming more affordable ... and that list goes on and on.
Come on people, let's admit that "nothing" is being fixed (or at least improved) all because "nothing" is changing.
We keep trying to fix our problems with the same system. Justin Trudeau will be gone in another year and replaced by Pierre Poilievre because Trudeau created our problems. But wait, Trudeau came to power some eight years ago under the same auspicious reason (his predecessor created the problems). So, the circle continues and in another eight years Poilievre will become Canada's ambassador to the UN and we get to start this charade all over again. And it is no different with our neighbour to the south, where we have two seniors sparring in the court of public opinion and neither one of them would last one round.
Every one of those problems listed above can be addressed without overtaxing the taxpayer and without giving up any of our freedoms. The war in Ukraine and the war in Gaza could have been avoided. Now, before anyone says to me, "if you are so confident then what are the solutions?" I do not have the luxury of exceeding the 660-word limit set by the Spectator.
Do you remember when Jagmeet Singh talked about taxing the rich? Now he is talking about taxing the ultra-rich. Do you remember when we talked about the need to help low-income individuals? Now we are talking about helping the low income and the modest income and bordering on the middle income. At this rate, before long we will be talking about helping the ultra-rich.
Did you know:
 3,107 deaths were attributed to opioids in 2016 and 3,970 in the first six months of 2023;
 Today, breakfast is delivered to 160 to 180 schools every week (just for Quebec). This began in 1994 with one school;
 In 1981, the first food bank in Canada was started in Edmonton. Today there are 4,750 nationally;
 Three years ago, Kitchener built "tiny homes" for homeless people as a stepping-stone to permanent housing. Today the same clients have not moved on;
 In 2017, Doug Ford promised to reduce hospital wait times. Ontario is still waiting;
 Less than $10-a-day daycare was introduced in Quebec in 1997 and still sees 51,000 children on waiting lists.
What does all this tell you? Every year we pour more and more tax money into problems that get worse and worse.
When are we going to force the people we elect to change the system so that they do what we want? Democracy is based on rule by majority, not by the whims of people like Trudeau or Poilievre or Ford (or Donald Trump or Joe Biden). These guys don't care what happens 10 years after they are gone, but I sure care about what happens to my family, this country and myself.
CAPTION: We're spending more and more on food banks and homeless shelters, yet it seems the problems are getting worse, Henry Chumienski writes.
CREDIT: Henry Chumienski

Subject: Food programs; Schools; Homeless people

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 126 of 252

Feb. 3: Bring licence stickers fees back, whining from schools pathetic and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-3-bring-licence-stickers-fees-back-whining/docview/2921462252/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+3%3A+Bring+licence+stickers+fees+back%2C+whining+from+schools+pathetic+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bring licence stickers fees back
In the recent spate of articles in The Spectator bemoaning the funding shortfalls for our homeless, our hospitals and schools, to the best of my knowledge, none have acknowledged or pointed to an obvious source of much-needed revenue - the monies that would still be available had the provincial government not cancelled the fees for motorists to renew their licence plate stickers in 2022. Given the millions of cars on Ontario roads, that would add up to hundreds of millions of dollars to provide solutions to our financial difficulties. The current provincial government should reconsider this decision, which appears to be nothing more than a cynical attempt to win votes.
Melvyn Davine, Dundas
Whining from schools pathetic
Re: Cap on foreign students will be devastating (Feb. 1)
Post-secondary institutions were quite content to limit their infrastructure responsibilities for foreign students to a bathroom between classes. If they had actually been concerned where their cash cows ate, slept and accessed health care, they would have done something before the government put the squeeze on them. Their whining on the editorial page is quite frankly predictably pathetic.
Phil Beard, Dundas
LRT decades in the making
LRT was first announced in 2006 when council voted to pursue "Rapid Ready" and the funding from Metrolinx to study feasibility. The funding announcement for LRT in 2015 by then-premier Kathleen Wynne was after nearly 60 votes by council to continue the process, where it should go and many marathon meetings. For those of us that have followed its progress and twists, should the RFP be awarded this year, hopefully it will be ready by 2027-28.
Laura Farr, Hamilton
Criticism isn't helpful
Re: Mayor has exceeded expectations (Jan. 31)
I would like to thank Glenn Gibson for his thoughtful letter regarding the ongoing criticism directed at the mayor by Margaret Shkimba and others.
It's important to remember that these articles are opinion pieces. Opinions are merely opinions and not necessarily based in fact. Ms. Shkimba has a litany of complaints about the mayor. She is not "scrappy" enough, not "mingling with the unwashed" or handing out food in the streets. Where's the actual evidence that she, our councillors and those that work at city hall are doing nothing?
This constant criticism is not helpful and serves only to promote a climate of distrust and hostility. I also worry that this vitriol potentially gives some permission to harass and threaten our politicians, particularly women.
There is no question we are facing a multitude of problems, which at times can seem insurmountable. However, these should be seen as shared problems. Blaming others is not useful and serves only to further polarize the community.
Perhaps we should start with the premise that the mayor and councillors love our city the same way that Ms. Shkimba and the rest of us do. That would allow us to move forward from a place of agreement and mutual respect
Elizabeth Ward, Hamilton
A lack of compassion
"We think sometimes that poverty is only being hungry, naked and homeless. The poverty of being unwanted, unloved and uncared for is the greatest poverty." - Mother Teresa
You know, it's easy for a lot of people to walk away from this social catastrophe we are experiencing because they think they can't change someone's whole life in a single day. It takes time, effort and the ability to understand and share the feelings of others.
There seems to be a tendency to blame these people at times for their own pain, for it is comforting, in a shockingly repellent way that allows them to close up their hearts to ignore the suffering of others. But what they fail to realize is that simple kindness, a gesture can go a long way toward encouraging these poor souls who are stuck in a desolate, desperate place.
The lack of compassion, sensibility and priorities in our city council, that they would even entertain the idea of spending of $60 million on bike lanes at this time, will only further punish and fragment homeless people into being seen as subhuman, further exacerbating the utter misery they have to endure.
Ross Prince, Hamilton
Bike lanes unnecessary here
In comparison to the rest of Canada, Hamilton has one of the largest populations of seniors. Seniors drive or have drivers, take the bus, a taxi, or walk; they don't use bikes. My concern is, why so many bike lanes? Sure enough, we have bike riders, those who cannot afford an automobile (or the gas) and students from our colleges and universities, but these institutions have residences nearby. Serious bike riders use pathways and trails away from heavy motor vehicle traffic, which is great. Another factor is winter. When winter arrives (normally five months of snowy weather), bikes are stored away and bike lanes are not used. What a brilliant idea to have an extensive city wide network of designated bike lanes that are used by such a low percentage of the public and cause traffic congestion. What a tremendous waste of taxpayer dollars to install them. It just doesn't make sense.
John Day, Hamilton
CAPTION: If Premier Doug Ford hadn't ended licence plate sticker fees, we'd have millions of dollars to put toward our most urgent needs, a reader writes.

Subject: Foreign students; Poverty; Councils; Funding; Homeless people

Document 127 of 252

Justin Trudeau needs to stop his Donald Trump trash talk, former ambassador warns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-needs-stop-his-donald-trump-trash/docview/2921460094/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+needs+to+stop+his+Donald+Trump+trash+talk%2C+former+ambassador+warns&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A former Canadian ambassador to Washington says Justin Trudeau should ditch the anti-Trump "MAGA Conservative" rhetoric directed at the Liberals' Canadian political rivals.
David MacNaughton, who co-chaired the Ontario campaign for Trudeau's 2015 election victory, says it's neither a wise domestic political strategy nor is it smart for Canada-U.S. relations.
In an interview Friday, MacNaughton said the prime minister is taking a risk by being seen to take swipes at Donald Trump, the presumptive Republican nominee, in a U.S. presidential election year.
MacNaughton - who Trudeau hailed as key to Canada's successful renegotiation of NAFTA with the Trump administration - worries there could be "blowback" if Trump wins and decides to set his sights on Canada.
Taking indirect shots at Trump would make it a lot harder to fight against Trump's promised 10 per cent tariffs on U.S. imports, or to persuade a new Trump administration that the Trudeau government is a trusted partner on defence and security, he suggested.
"I would be trying to make sure that there weren't any huge blow-ups in our relationship with the Americans between now and our (Canadian) election," he added.
American voters go to the polls in November, while a Canadian federal election is not expected before October 2025.
Moreover, MacNaughton said there's no indication that drawing such a contrast would be a winning electoral strategy for the Liberals here at home.
Public opinion research suggests a large percentage of Canadians feel the country is taking the wrong direction or that it's time for a change in government, MacNaughton said. He drew a comparison to the electorate's mood in 2015 when people "were tired of (Conservative prime minister) Stephen Harper. They wanted a change."
At that time, MacNaughton said, Trudeau was offered all kinds of advice on what kind of campaign to run. The reality, however, was that the public mood was such that Trudeau "didn't have to be spectacular, he just had to be a change from what Stephen Harper was," he said.
"Well, you know what? We're almost in exactly the same situation."
Attacking Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre "by itself isn't going to work anyway," he said. "And secondly, trying to draw a comparison with Donald Trump ... why would you run the risk?"
In MacNaughton's opinion, the bigger challenge for the Trudeau Liberals is to persuade Canadians that they understand the world has changed dramatically since their 2015 election, and even since the most recent election in 2021.
He said they need to demonstrate they understand the economic pain and anxieties that stem from a changed global order, and are prepared to make changes to how they govern - not that they will continue to do more of the same. He suggested if Harper had admitted to mistakes and promised to govern differently in 2015, it may have posed a "dilemma" for the Trudeau campaign back then.
The Liberals have announced a revival of what they call a "Team Canada" approach to getting ready for whoever wins the next U.S. election, but MacNaughton's view, first reported by Politico, is that the Trudeau government should "do the strategy. You don't talk strategy."
When MacNaughton departed the ambassador post in 2019, Trudeau praised him as a "trusted adviser, friend, and counsellor ... whose honesty, moderation, and wisdom were pivotal to Team Canada in our successful renegotiation of NAFTA."
He said the reality now, even with a Democrat currently in the White House, is that it is a time of "increased isolationism and protectionism" and Canada is "vulnerable."
"We need to be seen by the Americans as a trusted friend, ally, partner and, you know, right now, I don't think that feeling is as strong as it has been," he said. "And that's not just this government's fault. That's been going on for some time."
MacNaughton has shared his view with senior Liberals, but is at a loss to explain why Trudeau - who has criticized Poilievre's "ideologically driven MAGA Conservatives" in Parliament - thinks the attacks will work.
"I don't understand it," he said.
The Star has reported on polling data that also suggests Canadians are not persuaded by the Liberals' Trump-Poilievre comparisons.
The Conservatives did not respond Friday to a request for comment.
Conservative MP Randy Hoback, who has long worked on Canada-U.S. issues, recently said the Canada-U.S. relationship is too critical to be jeopardized in order to score "domestic political points."
"Trudeau and his caucus must end their repeated jabs south of the border ... His actions are hazardous to our economy and to our national security," Hoback wrote in an online post.
Trudeau is not the only Canadian political leader to take shots at Trump.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, the Liberals' governing ally in Parliament, last month warned that a Trump election victory would be bad for Canada. Trump is "openly running on an egomaniac, vengeance-filled motive to become the president, and it is incredibly disturbing to watch this," Singh said.
"Donald Trump frankly, is in a ... world of his own. The things that he has done, the things that he says, the type of person he is, there is no other comparison to someone who is as bad for democracy, as bad for people, as bad for the planet as Donald Trump."
With files from Raisa Patel
CAPTION: David MacNaughton, then Canada's ambassador to the United States, speaks to reporters at a Liberal cabinet retreat in Calgary on Jan. 22, 2017.

Document 128 of 252

The Ford government does not have to disclose mandate letters: Supreme Court rules in favour of Ontario's Tories

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-government-does-not-have-disclose-mandate/docview/2921459780/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ford+government+does+not+have+to+disclose+mandate+letters%3A+Supreme+Court+rules+in+favour+of+Ontario%27s+Tories&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government can keep marching orders to cabinet ministers confidential, the Supreme Court of Canada ruled Friday, marking an end to a long-running legal battle over access to the documents.
The CBC had asked - under the province's freedom-of-information law - for the letters written to ministers after Ford won the 2018 election, but the request was denied on the basis that releasing the documents would reveal the substance of cabinet deliberations.
The information and privacy commissioner disagreed, ordering the government to disclose them, and two levels of Ontario courts also sided with the CBC. But the Supreme Court ruled the letters are exempt from public disclosure.
While the outcome was unanimous, one of the justices on the panel of seven who heard the case came to the same conclusion as the majority, but for different reasons.
"Freedom of information (FOI) legislation strikes a balance between the public's need to know and the confidentiality the executive requires to govern effectively," Justice Andromache Karakatsanis wrote for the majority.
"Both are crucial to the proper functioning of our democracy."
Ford's government had contended cabinet confidentiality, candour and solidarity are fundamental to a system where responsible ministers collectively decide government policy, but the CBC argued disclosure is key to an informed public and accountable government.
While there is no doubt public access to government-held information is vital to the democratic process, Karakatsanis wrote, in this case the information and privacy commissioner interpreted the freedom-of-information exemption around cabinet deliberations too narrowly.
"In our constitutional democracy, the confidentiality of Cabinet deliberations is a precondition to responsible government because it enables collective ministerial responsibility," Karakatsanis wrote. "At base, Cabinet confidentialitypromotes executive accountability by permitting private disagreement and candour in ministerial deliberations, despite public solidarity."
The information and privacy commissioner found the letters indicate topics that may have arisen during a cabinet meeting or may be considered at a future meeting, and represent the "end point" of deliberations, rather than the substance.
But, Karakatsanis wrote, the commissioner did not give due consideration to the impact "premature disclosure of policy priorities at an early stage of the process may have on the efficient workings of government."
"Far from being mere 'topics' like items on an agenda, the Letters reflect the views of the Premier on the importance of certain policy priorities, and mark the initiation of a fluid process of policy formulation within Cabinet," the court wrote.
"The Letters are revealing of the substance of Cabinet deliberations, both on their face and when compared against what government actually does."
Kathleen Wynne became the first Ontario premier to proactively disclose the letters, in 2014, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and several other premiers following suit.
CAPTION: The Supreme Court of Canada ruled Friday that Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government can keep marching orders to cabinet ministers confidential.Cole Burston The Canadian Press File Photo

CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Document 129 of 252

Justin Trudeau slams Pierre Poilievre for link to Loblaws lobbying

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-slams-pierre-poilievre-link/docview/2921555157/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+slams+Pierre+Poilievre+for+link+to+Loblaws+lobbying&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals are trying to undermine Conservative criticism over the high cost of food by noting how the lobbying firm of one of Pierre Poilievre's top advisers works for a grocery giant that is raking in big profits.
A day after the Liberals first raised the lobbying connection in the House of Commons, Trudeau brought it up unprompted at a news conference in Waterloo on Friday. The prime minister suggested the fact that top Tory strategist Jenni Byrne's lobbying firm has worked for Loblaws casts doubt on the Conservative leader's credibility as a self-styled champion of ordinary Canadians who are struggling with rising food costs.
"One more thing," Trudeau said, while answering questions from journalists. "Pierre Poilievre has been standing up for months now, pretending that he cares about the high grocery prices faced by Canadians, and it turns out that his top adviser is working as a lobbyist for Loblaws. I think Mr. Poilievre owes some explanations to Canadians."
The Star searched the publicly available lobbying registries for the federal government and all 10 provinces. Byrne herself is not registered as a lobbyist in any of them.
Employees at her firm, however, are registered as lobbyists for Loblaw Companies Ltd. at Queen's Park. According to the registry, six representatives from Jenni Byrne + Associates have registered to lobby Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government on behalf of Loblaws since August 2022. The records say the goals of the lobbying effort are to reduce "red tape within the cannabis sector," and to promote "energy conservation opportunities" in Ontario. They don't mention food or grocery prices.
Sebastian Skamski, a spokesperson for Poilievre, dismissed Trudeau's comments as a desperate attempt to pin blame for rising food costs on the leader of the Official Opposition when the governing Liberals have not prevented them from increasing, despite promising last fall to pressure Loblaws and other big chains to "stabilize" prices.
The cost of groceries has increased faster than overall inflation for more than two years, according to Statistics Canada. In December 2023, the cost of store-bought food was 4.7 per cent higher than one year earlier, compared with overall inflation of 3.4 per cent.
"The Liberals failed and now claim that it's really the leader of the opposition - and not the prime minister and government of Canada - who holds all the power. This is laughable and pathetic," Skamski said in a written statement.
Byrne did not respond to a request for comment from the Star on Friday.
Under Poilievre's leadership, the Conservatives have constantly hammered the Liberal government over rising costs. Poilievre blames huge increases in federal spending during the COVID-19 pandemic, along with central bank measures to increase the money supply, for spurring inflation. On grocery prices, the Conservatives argue Trudeau's carbon pricing system - a policy to reduce emissions that cause climate change by requiring all provinces to have a carbon levy or cap-and-trade system - is responsible for increasing costs of producing and transporting food.
The New Democrats have taken a different tack, and claim it is "greedy" corporations that are needlessly profiting off of struggling Canadians during this recent period of high inflation. Loblaws and other major grocers deny they are responsible for rising prices, and say the drivers of inflation are outside of their control. Yet big grocery chain profits are up along with prices. Loblaws reported more than $1 billion in operating income in the third quarter of 2023, up 7.5 per cent from the same period a year earlier.
On Friday, at an event in Toronto, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said the fact that Byrne's firm lobbies for Loblaws means Poilievre is "never going to take on" big grocery chains.
"He's never going to take on corporate greed. He is never going to take on the real causes of the problems we're going through," Singh said.
With files from Raisa Patel
CAPTION: Jenni Byrne waits to appear as a witness before the Procedure and House Affairs committee meeting in Ottawa on May 11, 2023.

Document 130 of 252

Jagmeet Singh on his new baby's name and why he regrets biking with Doug Ford: 5 things we learned at TMU's Democracy Forum

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jagmeet-singh-on-his-new-babys-name-why-he/docview/2921554954/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jagmeet+Singh+on+his+new+baby%27s+name+and+why+he+regrets+biking+with+Doug+Ford%3A+5+things+we+learned+at+TMU%27s+Democracy+Forum&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh made his second appearance at Toronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum on Friday, speaking to Star columnist and TMU Senior Fellow Martin Regg Cohn about the state of federal politics today.
Here are five things the New Democratic leader revealed during the discussion.
His new daughter's name
Singh shared that his second daughter - who the NDP leader and his wife, Gurkiran Kaur Sidhu, welcomed in mid-December - is named Dani, a moniker meaning "generosity" and "benevolence."
"There is a true kind of generosity at the core of the human spirit," Singh said of the inspiration behind the name.
Singh spent much of the hour-long chat holding the two-month-old, who grew fussy after being minded by a staffer.
His two-year-old daughter, Anhad, was bestowed a name meaning "limitless," prompting Singh to occasionally refer to his governing deal with the minority Liberals as the "Anhad Accord."
(The deal was brokered, in part, due to a congratulatory call Prime Minister Justin Trudeau placed to Singh after Anhad's birth. It remains to be seen what Dani's arrival portends for the future of Canada's political landscape.)
He owes former Conservative leader Erin O'Toole an apology
When Singh first appeared at the Democracy Forum during a virtual 2021 chat, he told Cohn that there was absolutely no way his party would support a minority government led by then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole.
When asked Friday whether he would answer similarly if the next election handed a victory to Pierre Poilievre, Singh had a sharp - and sheepish - response.
"In retrospect, Erin O'Toole seemed like a very reasonable person. I'd like to be very blunt with you," Singh said.
"He believed in science. He thought that if a health-care professional gives us good advice around the medication, we should probably listen to that health-care advice. Like, normal, standard stuff."
Singh admitted he was likely too hard on O'Toole, and said he should probably "call him up" to convey as much.
As for whether he'd have similar sober second thoughts about the prospect of a Poilievre-helmed government, Singh was unequivocal.
"No, man!" the NDP leader exclaimed.
He's trying to spin pharmacare wins
Singh confirmed reporting from the CBC that he's pushing the government for coverage of some essential medicines in exchange for extending the deadline by which pharmacare legislation must be passed.
"Our agreement simply said that there should be progress toward pharmacare, meaning legislation that moves us towards that direction. It didn't actually require (the Liberals) to cover any medication," Singh told Cohn.
"We're going to fight to make sure there will be some medication delivered to all Canadians ... which was never actually in the agreement initially."
According to the governing pact, which could see the New Democrats prop up Justin Trudeau until 2025, the Liberals were meant to pass legislation outlining a framework for a national pharmacare program by the end of last year.
The NDP agreed in late 2023 to let the Liberals introduce the bill by March, but both parties still disagree on what such a program should look like.
He condemned a B.C. NDP minister for her "horribly wrong" Middle East comments
The federal NDP and British Columbia NDP are usually seen pumping each other up, but that wasn't the case on Friday.
Singh was answering audience questions as B.C.'s minister of post-secondary education, Selina Robinson, fielded calls for her resignation after she said this week that the state of Israel was established on "a crappy piece of land with nothing on it."
The remarks have drawn swift criticism from those who say the comments erase Palestine's history before the state of Israel was created in 1948.
Robinson apologized for her remarks and was rebuked by Singh, who was asked to weigh in on the matter after a handful of pro-Palestinian protesters briefly disrupted the event.
"What she said was wrong ... horribly wrong," Singh said.
"She was factually wrong. It was harmful, and it was absolutely wrong to do that. There's no question about it."
He regrets riding a bike with Doug Ford
In 2017, when Singh was the newly-minted leader of the NDP and Doug Ford was not yet Ontario's premier, the duo appeared on the TVO program Political Blind Date: a show that paired political rivals to discuss the issues of the day.
During the episode, Singh took Ford on a bike ride through Toronto's streets - an activity Ford said he had not attempted in decades.
While the pair came out of the program on perfectly friendly terms, Singh had tough words for Ford during Friday's discussion.
"I kind of regret that a bit now," Singh said of the cycling jaunt. "That made him seem like a friendly fellow and he's not."
CAPTION: Jagmeet Singh holds his daughter Dani while speaking at the Toronto Metropolitan University Democracy Forum on Feb. 2, 2024.

Document 131 of 252

Hamilton sees big shift toward intensification

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-sees-big-shift-toward-intensification/docview/2921176820/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+sees+big+shift+toward+intensification&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Another parking lot in downtown Hamilton is poised to sprout highrise residential.
Global asset manager BGO's plan calls for three 27-storey towers and roughly 900 rental units on a sloping property on Main Street West just southeast of Hess Village.
That's a lot of humanity and potential customers for the nearby strip of bars and eateries.
"Which is definitely what these ma and pop places need, for sure," says Maggie Foseid, supervisor at Electric Diner on George Street.
"So it will be exciting to see a whole new wave of potential customers."
The tower plan, which recently received conditional site-plan approval from city hall, is part of a cresting wave of projects bolstering urban intensification around Hamilton.
Denser forms of housing in the city's built-up area have claimed about half of applications in the past five years or so, says Jason Thorne, general manager of planning and economic development.
"In 2023, we were 90 per cent intensification. That's a huge number," he told council in a recent budget presentation.
And a big change. A decade ago, when he started with the city, single-family homes commanded about 85 per cent of the mix, Thorne said.
"That's turned on its head now. We're seeing only about 15 per cent singles, and where we're really seeing the development in housing is multis, apartments, stacked towns ... and in intensification."
A "staging of development" report presented to council late last year offers a snapshot of this shift.
The study flagged potential intensification projects of at least 100 units that have been pitched in the past three years. It found that roughly 18,100 units subject to later-stage planning approvals could be on the horizon.
That doesn't mean necessarily mean they'll be move ahead as pitched, planners note. Some are subject to Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) challenges. Also, approvals are one thing, but shovels in the ground another, as evidenced by some local development plans delayed by a slowing market.
Among the projects are the 27-storey towers planned for the Main West parcel between George, Caroline and Hess streets. Consultants representing the developer didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
Here's a sample of others that stand to change the urban landscape:
163 Jackson St. W.: Television City calls for two 32-storey residential towers and 642 units at the former CHCH headquarters;41 Wilson St.: The Design District involves three 30-storey towers and 931 units at a downtown parking lot;488 Upper Wellington St.: Labour union LIUNA and partner Fengate are building a six-storey rental building with 261 units at Inverness Avenue East, a former Mountain police station site;211 John St. S.: Slate Asset Management plans to construct 14-storey and 27-storey buildings with 769 units at the Corktown Plaza and parking lot.360 King St. W.: Vrancor is working on a 25-storey building with 300 units near Queen Street as part of a multi-phase project at the grounds of an old orphanage;60 Caledon Ave.: Victoria Park Community Homes and Hamilton East Kiwanis Non-Profit Homes are teaming up on 266 units of affordable housing at a former school site off Upper James;20 Reid Ave. N.: Roxborough Park, at the site of a former school and public housing in the east end, involves 164 townhouses. A new 10-storey, 103-unit CityHousing building stands nearby;1842 King St. E.: New Horizon envisions four 13-storey buildings and four four-storey townhouses with 1,341 units between them where Brock University once had a satellite campus in a squat building.
In a different measure, the staging report also examined subdivision applications:
About 17,000 units "could be realized" through draft plans between now and 2026, with an expected mix of 2,800 singles and semis; 4,800 townhouses; 9,400 apartments.
Over time, applications have "skewed" more toward greater density, Heather Travis, a manager in the planning division, said in presenting the report to councillors.
"It's indicative of seeing the development community start to respond to different policies that we have in place encouraging higher-density development and some of the more 'missing-middle' development."
That's framed by council's decision to grow within Hamilton's urban area, rather than sprawling into outlying farmland, which was the focus of a pitched political battle between the province and city officials.
In late 2022, Premier Doug Ford's PC government imposed a roughly 5,000-acre urban expansion on Hamilton, rejecting the city's plan to hold the boundary firm.
Then, about a year later, in an abrupt U-turn, the province announced it would reverse the expansion along with a slew of changes in other municipalities.
With a mind to prevent sprawl, Hamilton has prezoned downtown for highrises and is setting the stage for intensification along major transit routes like the future lower-city LRT corridor.
The city is also allowing for "gentle density" in neighbourhoods where single-detached homes predominate through such housing forms as secondary suites, townhouses and converted triplexes and fourplexes.
That's part of an ongoing effort to update residential zoning. Staff are to present the latest on that to councillors on Feb. 23. A report on mid-rise zoning is expected to follow later this year.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: A developer is planning to build three 27-storey towers on a Main Street West parking lot just southeast of Hess Village.Construction on Television City, two 32-storey residential towers and 642 units at the former CHCH headquarters at 163 Jackson St. W.A developer plans to build three 27-storey towers on a Main Street West parking lot just southeast of Hess Village.A developer plans to build three 27-storey towers on a Main Street West parking lot just southeast of Hess Village.A developer is planning to build three 27-storey towers on a Main Street West parking lot just southeast of Hess Village.

Subject: Councils; Affordable housing; Planning; Housing developments

Title: Hamilton sees big shift toward intensification

Document 132 of 252

Hamilton sees shift toward intensification: Multi-unit houses, stacked townhouses, apartments driving density push

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hamilton-sees-shift-toward-intensification/docview/2921084862/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+sees+shift+toward+intensification%3A+Multi-unit+houses%2C+stacked+townhouses%2C+apartments+driving+density+push&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Denser forms of housing in the city's built-up area have claimed about half of the applications in the past five years or so, says Jason Thorne, general manager of planning and economic development.
"In 2023, we were 90 per cent intensification. That's a huge number," he told council in a recent budget presentation.
And a big change. A decade ago, when he started with the city, single-family homes commanded about 85 per cent of the mix, Thorne said.
"That's turned on its head now. We're seeing only about 15 per cent singles, and where we're really seeing the development in housing is multis, apartments, stacked towns ... and in intensification."
A "staging of development" report presented to council late last year offers a snapshot of this shift. The study flagged potential intensification projects of at least 100 units that have been pitched in the past three years. It found that roughly 18,100 units subject to later-stage planning approvals could be on the horizon.
That doesn't mean necessarily mean they'll be move ahead as pitched, planners note. Some are subject to Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) challenges. Also, approvals are one thing, but shovels in the ground another, as evidenced by some local development plans delayed by a slowing market.
Among the projects are the 27-storey towers planned for the Main West parcel between George, Caroline and Hess streets. Consultants representing the developer didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
Here's a sample of others that stand to change the urban landscape:
163 Jackson St. W.: Television City calls for two 32-storey residential towers and 642 units at the former CHCH headquarters;
41 Wilson St.: The Design District involves three 30-storey towers and 931 units at a downtown parking lot;
488 Upper Wellington St.: Labour union LIUNA and partner Fengate are building a six-storey rental building with 261 units at Inverness Avenue East, a former Mountain police station site;
211 John St. S.: Slate Asset Management plans to construct 14-storey and 27-storey buildings with 769 units at the Corktown Plaza and parking lot.
360 King St. W.: Vrancor is working on a 25-storey building with 300 units near Queen Street as part of a multi-phase project at the grounds of an old orphanage;
60 Caledon Ave.: Victoria Park Community Homes and Hamilton East Kiwanis Non-Profit Homes are teaming up on 266 units of affordable housing at a former school site off Upper James;
20 Reid Ave. N.: Roxborough Park, at the site of a former school and public housing in the east end, involves 164 townhouses. A new 10-storey, 103-unit CityHousing building stands nearby;
1842 King St. E.: New Horizon envisions four 13-storey buildings and four four-storey townhouses with 1,341 units between them where Brock University once had a satellite campus in a squat building.
In a different measure, the staging report also examined subdivision applications:
About 17,000 units "could be realized" through draft plans between now and 2026, with an expected mix of 2,800 singles and semis; 4,800 townhouses; 9,400 apartments.
Over time, applications have "skewed" more toward greater density, Heather Travis, a manager in the planning division, said in presenting the report to councillors.
"It's indicative of seeing the development community start to respond to different policies that we have in place encouraging higher-density development and some of the more 'missing-middle' development."
That's framed by council's decision to grow within Hamilton's urban area, rather than sprawling into outlying farmland, which was the focus of a pitched political battle between the province and city officials.
In late 2022, Premier Doug Ford's PC government imposed a roughly 5,000-acre urban expansion on Hamilton, rejecting the city's plan to hold the boundary firm.
Then, about a year later, in an abrupt U-turn, the province announced it would reverse the expansion along with a slew of changes in other municipalities.
With a mind to prevent sprawl, Hamilton has prezoned downtown for highrises and is setting the stage for intensification along major transit routes like the future lower-city LRT corridor.
The city is also allowing for "gentle density" in neighbourhoods where single-detached homes predominate through such housing forms as secondary suites, townhouses and converted triplexes and fourplexes.
That's part of an ongoing effort to update residential zoning. Staff are to present the latest on that to councillors on Feb. 23. A report on mid-rise zoning is expected to follow later this year.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Construction on the Television City project continues at 163 Jackson St. W.A developer plans to build three 27-storey towers just southeast of Hess Village.Cathie Coward photos The Hamilton Spectator

Supreme Court rules Doug Ford's mandate letters for cabinet ministers can be kept secret

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/supreme-court-rules-doug-fords-mandate-letters/docview/2921084476/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supreme+Court+rules+Doug+Ford%27s+mandate+letters+for+cabinet+ministers+can+be+kept+secret&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's cabinet secrets are safe for now after the Supreme Court ruled his ministers' mandate letters do not have to be made public.
"The mandate letters are protected from disclosure ... The letters are revealing of the substance of cabinet deliberations," the high court ruled Friday in a unanimous decision.
Ford's Progressive Conservative government had spent years fighting the release of the 2018 documents that give ministers their marching orders.
"We've been pretty clear - the premier's been pretty clear - those are captured by cabinet confidentiality and we haven't wavered from that position," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy had told reporters earlier this week.
The top court agreed with the province's argument Friday.
Attorney General Doug Downey was not immediately available for comment.
While cabinet mandate letters have traditionally been kept private, former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne abandoned that practice in 2014. Reading like glorified press releases, they are still available online at the Ontario government's website.
Still, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau followed suit when he took power federally the following year.
After Ford's Conservatives defeated Wynne's Liberals in 2018, the new premier returned to the custom of sealing the documents written to 23 cabinet ministers.
But CBC filed a freedom of information request for the letters that year, which the Tories denied.
The province's information and privacy commissioner determined the broadcaster should have access to the documents, leading to a lengthy - and costly - legal fight with Queen's Park that concluded with Friday morning's high court ruling.
While the decision in the case - known as Attorney General for Ontario v. Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, et al - was much-anticipated, no salacious revelations were expected.
That's because the 2018 mandate letters, resplendent with lofty rhetoric about "accountability," were leaked to Global News last year, generating little buzz at the provincial legislature.
The directives to ministers from the premier are not legally binding, meaning their responsibilities could differ from what is included in the missives.
Ford's government also lost at the Ontario Superior Court of Justice in August 2020 then again at the Court of Appeal for Ontario in January 2022, so the Tories had been anticipating another defeat at the Supreme Court.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Pinewood Toronto Studios on Jan. 17, 2024.

Document 134 of 252

Province misfires on ServiceOntario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-misfires-on-serviceontario/docview/2921084447/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+misfires+on+ServiceOntario&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not difficult to identify a common denominator in the serial policy controversies of the Ontario government.
The halving of Toronto city council. Ontario Place. The Ontario Science Centre. Municipal boundary changes. The off-again, on-again, off-again development of the Greenbelt.
These initiatives and more have been characterized by lack of public consultation, lack of transparency, and the whiff of backroom decision-making to which certain interests are privy while the public is not.
One would have thought that after the epic recent reversals of the Greenbelt scandal - a career and credibility eroding mess still being investigated by the RCMP - that Premier Doug Ford would have wanted to bend over backwards, for at least a time, in the name of demonstrating fair process and inviting public involvement. There was an opportunity to show that hard lessons in governing had been learned and lasting changes made.
But no. Here we are again with the moving of some ServiceOntario outlets into the Staples chain of office supply stores.
Opposition critics are justifiably concerned, given the government's track record, about the inexplicable absence of a competitive bidding process. "There's a lack of transparency, decisions are being made behind closed doors all the time and they're always looking to reward the big corporations," New Democrat MPP Tom Rakocevic (Humber River-Black Creek) told the Toronto Star.
Now, the initiative might be every bit as innocent as Business Services Delivery Minister Todd McCarthy described it.
McCarthy says the move, with six Service Ontario centres to open in Staples locations on Thursday, is part of a three-year pilot project that will save about $1 million and help determine what arrangements work best.
New outlets in Staples will be open 30 per cent longer than the locations they are replacing, he said, and the province has been using retail partners such as Canadian Tire, IDA and Home Hardware for Service Ontario locations for years.
But who's to know?
The Liberals have asked Ontario's Financial Accountability Office for an analysis into the costs of retrofits in Staples centres - to be paid for by the province - and also whether unionized public service jobs might be lost. Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West) said the contracts will provide significant value to large American companies with Ontario paying for renovations and delivering increased foot traffic into their outlets.
The Star's Rob Ferguson reported that there was no competitive bidding because some existing Service Ontario agreements with the private contractors that run them were expiring soon and the government wanted to avoid service gaps.
But with the Ford government, there is always an excuse for haste. And the record shows that government haste has too often resulted in reversals that end up costing the public plenty.
The Ford administration's modus operandi - first a policy announcement, then at some unspecified future date the business case, as happened with the plan to move the Ontario Science Centre - is too often the opposite of what most Ontarians would likely deem sensible and prudent.
The fact that this government can generate controversy in its handling of so comparatively minor a matter is concerning.
And McCarthy's hapless effort this week to explain the matter to reporters - especially on where savings will be found and why the sole-source contracts were necessary - hardly provided reassurance. The trouble for McCarthy and for Ford is that critics - and a good many ordinary Ontarians - are disinclined to give them the benefit of the doubt for the simple reason that the government, as its track record makes clear, hasn't earned it.
The benefit of trust will be won back - assuming, after the Greenbelt fiasco, that matters aren't already too far gone - through durable evidence that lessons have been learned and changes in procedure made.
That means slowing down when necessary, adopting competitive bidding processes as a matter of course, and letting the public in early and often on decisions. No more surprise policy reveals with little evidence to support the looming changes.
It doesn't seem that big an expectation or too difficult a task.
CAPTION:
