Document 135 of 252

'Pierre is going to win': PC insiders privately fear more MPPs will abandon Doug Ford for Team Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-is-going-win-pc-insiders-privately-fear/docview/2921274136/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Pierre+is+going+to+win%27%3A+PC+insiders+privately+fear+more+MPPs+will+abandon+Doug+Ford+for+Team+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Blindsided by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre poaching one of his provincial cabinet ministers to run federally, Premier Doug Ford is girding for more departures.
As the premier gathers with Progressive Conservatives for a policy convention in Niagara Falls this weekend, he is mindful that Poilievre may pilfer more of his caucus for a federal election expected next year.
Ford's turf is fertile ground for the federal Conservatives because PC MPPs represent dozens of ridings that are also held by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals.
That means veterans who have won back-to-back provincial elections - and have local name recognition - could help the rookie federal Tory leader topple Liberal incumbents, especially in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area that is crucial to a Poilievre victory.
"I wouldn't say they're jumping ship," Ford insisted Wednesday in Aurora when asked about the surprise departure of former minister of red tape reduction Parm Gill on Jan. 25 and the potential for others to leave.
"I think the world of Parm, I wish him all the best. He was a federal MP before," he said, referring to Gill's service in the caucus of prime minister Stephen Harper between 2011 and 2015.
Asked what he would say to any of his caucus thinking about departing for federal pastures, Ford said, "My message is to anyone - no matter when I ran my private company or run the government - no one has a handcuff on anyone."
Hours after the premier's comments - and nearly a week after Gill bolted - Poilievre issued a statement.
"Parm will be an asset to our common sense Conservative team in our mission to axe the tax, build the homes, fix the budget and stop the crime," said the federal leader.
Poilievre's enthusiasm for Gill's arrival isn't shared broadly by others within the party, some of whom deride the Ford Tories as "conservative-lite."
Several federal Conservatives admitted to the Star some confusion as to why Gill was brought on since they don't see the ideological stars aligning between the national party and its Ontario cousin.
The unexpected resignation of the Milton MPP, who only won his seat in June 2022 by 1,680 votes, has sent a jolt through Queen's Park.
Sources told the Star the provincial Tories are worried that MPPs representing ridings in Toronto, Peel, Halton and York Region, as well as some in northern Ontario, may jump to Poilievre's surging federal Conservatives.
Public opinion polls show they have a healthy lead over Trudeau's three-term Liberal government with an election scheduled to be held by October 2025.
"Why wouldn't they think about it? Pierre is going to win and they can make a lot more money" as federal representatives, said one concerned Ford Tory, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
Indeed, there was grumbling in PC ranks after the Star reported on Jan. 17 that the premier would keep MPPs' salaries frozen at the same level they have been since 2008.
The base salary for an MPP is $116,550, while Tory parliamentary assistants earn $133,799 and cabinet ministers make $165,851. The MPPs' pension plan was eliminated in 1996.
In contrast, MPs, who receive annual raises in April and have a pension, had base pay of $194,600 last year while federal ministers made $287,400 and Liberal MPs who serve as parliamentary secretaries earn $213,400.
Publicly at least, Ford stresses he is not worried about an exodus, emphasizing his party's resilience.
"I believe that the people of Ontario have a lot of confidence in us - all you have to do is look at the polls," the premier said at Wednesday's crime-fighting announcement with federal Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc.
That was an apparent reference to the Star's monthly tracking poll conducted by Abacus Data.
The most recent survey, released last week, had Ford's Tories holding steady at 38 per cent support, with Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 27 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 23 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at five per cent.
Using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform, Abacus surveyed 995 Ontarians Jan. 18 to 23. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Another high-ranking provincial Tory said fears of the federal party raiding their benches are "overblown" and the premier isn't concerned about a brain drain.
While Ford, who works closely with the Trudeau Liberals and counts Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland as a personal friend, has only met Poilievre briefly, some of his cabinet ministers are supporters of the federal party.
According to Elections Canada disclosures, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, Education Minister Stephen Lecce, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria and Northern Development and Indigenous Affairs Minister Greg Rickford each donated money to the federal Tories last year.
Bethlenfalvy (Pickering-Uxbridge), Calandra (Markham-Stouffville) and Sarkaria (Brampton South) have Liberal MPs as their federal counterparts. Lecce (King-Vaughan) has a Tory MP in his riding.
Rickford's Kenora-Rainy River seat has slightly different boundaries, with part of his riding represented by a Tory and part by a Liberal MP. He was a minister in the Harper government.
Poilievre's inner circle is populated by people who once worked for Ford but are no longer close to the premier, including the federal leader's most influential adviser, Jenni Byrne, deputy leader Melissa Lantsman, chief of staff Ian Todd, party executive director Mike Crase, lawyer Michael Wilson and press secretary Sebastian Skamski, among others.
Despite - or perhaps because of - those long-standing relationships, there are tensions between the federal and provincial parties.
In part, that's because those around Ford and Poilievre are well aware of their political history: Ontario voters traditionally opt for different parties to be in power at Queen's Park and Ottawa at the same time.
The phenomenon even has a name: Underhill's balance theory, named for the famed Canadian political scientist Frank Underhill, who also wrote the Regina Manifesto that led to the modern-day New Democratic Party.
Over the past 80 years, for example, different parties have been in power federally and in Ontario 85 per cent of the time.
That suggests when the Tories are in the ascendance in Ottawa, the Liberals or the New Democrats do better at Queen's Park and vice versa.
Time - and the next federal and provincial elections - will tell if that trend continues.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an event at Pinewood Toronto Studios on Jan. 17, 2024.Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks in the House of Commons on Feb. 1, 2024.Premier Doug Ford, left, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.

Ford government expands primary care teams in effort to fix doctor shortage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-expands-primary-care-teams-effort/docview/2920623946/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+expands+primary+care+teams+in+effort+to+fix+doctor+shortage&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: PETERBOROUGH, Ont.-In a bid to blunt the doctor shortage, Ontario is pulling another 400 physicians and other medical providers into its growing network of primary care teams - a move that should give up to 328,000 people better access to treatment, says Health Minister Sylvia Jones.
Funding of $110 million is going to 78 new and expanded teams of doctors, nurse practitioners, nurses, physiotherapists, nutritionist and other medical professionals across the province - including in Sault Ste. Marie where a major clinic stunned patients last week by announcing 10,000 would be cut loose at the end of May because their doctors have retired or quit.
While it was not clear if the money earmarked for two teams in the Sault will help 10,000 stranded patients, Jones said the fundingwill "close the gap" for 1.3 million in Ontarians who do not have their own family doctor.
"We know there's still more to do," she said.
As an example, Jones noted the $3 million earmarked for the new Peterborough Community Health Centre will enable it to provide care to more than 11,300 patients needing family doctors, mental health services, help with managing chronic diseases like diabetes, and other health needs.
The family health team concept is intended to provide support to doctors so they can see more patients while providing a broader range of care through nurse practitioners, nurses and others on staff at a time when physicians are in short supply for the province's fast-growing and aging population and hospital emergency departments are overwhelmed.
"Many are overworked, some are burned out," Jones acknowledged, speaking of doctors.
CAPTION: Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones makes an announcement on health care with Premier Doug Ford in Toronto on Jan. 16, 2023.

Title: Ford government expands primary care teams in effort to fix doctor shortage

Document 137 of 252

Voters need to be engaged, informed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-need-be-engaged-informed/docview/2920454119/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+need+to+be+engaged%2C+informed&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is common to hear people criticize politicians. In many cases there is much to criticize. But how many people have ever asked themselves if they, the voters, are also at fault?
Let me explain. Look at the 2020 and 2022 American elections. In both, the Republican Party (GOP) had no policy platform whatsoever. They focused on supporting Donald Trump in 2020 and fighting "culture war" issues in 2022. And millions of Americans voted for them.
Since then, there has been utter chaos in the GOP-dominated House of Representatives as many of those elected ran on a platform of anger and grievances and have no logical plan as to how to govern.
It isn't just American voters who do this. In Ontario in 2018, the Doug Ford-led Progressive Conservatives won a large majority government with a platform that had the depth of a rain puddle. Voters didn't vote logically based on issues. They were tired of the incumbent Liberal government, so they gave a huge majority government to a man who had very little experience in government. How has that turned out?
Currently, we see the Conservative Party of Canada (CPC) led by Pierre Poilievre leading in the polls. If an election were held today, the Conservatives would win a large majority. But can you name any clear, detailed, policies that Poilievre and the Conservatives have proposed? He says he will "axe" the carbon tax and fight climate change using technology instead. But he will not or cannot state what technology will be used or if it even exists. He blames Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for high inflation in Canada but fails to mention that high inflation is a fact of life around the world. He rightly criticizes Trudeau for reckless spending and running up debt. Yet, he has not presented Canadians with any plan as to how he will balance the federal budget.
Like the GOP, Poilievre and the CPC have no plan as to how they may govern if they win the next election.
Back in 2016, I was attending a dinner party, and the 2015 federal election came up for discussion. One person mentioned he had voted Liberal as he simply disliked Stephen Harper. I asked him "who would you rather be in power if Canada faced a crisis such as a major war, Trudeau or Harper?"
He answered, "Stephen Harper for sure. But I don't like him. Justin Trudeau is simply more likable."
I shook my head in amazement. He voted, not based on policy, or competence, but on who he liked more. How can one justify this?
Think about this in a different way. Assume you have a family business, and you are seeking a finance manager. One candidate has no relevant experience in finance. They are unable to explain what skills and knowledge they bring to the table and cannot answer your questions in a concise manner. The other candidate has a solid background in finance and can explain what exactly they will do to improve your business. Who do you think you would hire?
If you would not hire someone for your business who was not qualified and couldn't explain what they would do to improve the business, why would you vote for candidates who are just like that?
As voters, we owe it to future generations to only vote for candidates who can present detailed policy alternatives and who have related experience. This also requires voters to be engaged and informed. This isn't hard to do. On your way home from work, listen to the news on the radio. During your lunch hour, read a mainstream newspaper (such as this one.) And take the time to write to your elected officials and let them know what you are thinking. If we all did this, we would see far better qualified people running for office.
Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of five books.

Document 138 of 252

Municipal daycare audits spark concern: Value-for-dollar order from province includes mention of 'third party' option for operations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/municipal-daycare-audits-spark-concern/docview/2920454117/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Municipal+daycare+audits+spark+concern%3A+Value-for-dollar+order+from+province+includes+mention+of+%27third+party%27+option+for+operations&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario has ordered municipalities that directly operate child-care centres to do a value-for-money audit of their programs to determine if they could instead be operated by a "third party," raising concerns about privatization.
The move also has some advocates concerned the provincial government is looking to find savings on the backs of early childhood educators, as higher staff pay in municipal centres is often the reason they can cost more to run.
Ministry of Education funding guidelines sent to licensed child-care operators late last year say municipal child-care administrators should have the independent audits completed by the end of 2024.
"The purpose of the value for money audit is to determine whether provincial funding is being used efficiently and effectively by directly operated centres, and whether the child care services could be offered by a third party provider instead," the ministry wrote.
Funding from the federal $10-a-day program or regular provincial administration funding can be used to pay for the audits, the ministry wrote.
The ministry has also told municipalities that it is cutting administration funding. Ontario is the only province with a mandated role for municipalities in delivering child care and funding for that administration has until now come entirely from the province.
The government announced in 2019 that it was asking municipalities to contribute 20 per cent of the cost of creating spaces, where the province had previously contributed all of it. The province also said administration funding would be split 50-50 going forward, but has since provided $220 million in transitional funding that is now expiring.
The Ontario Coalition for Better Child Care says that will amount to a cut of $85.5 million.
NDP child-care critic Teresa Armstrong said the system is in desperate need of more funding, not less.
"Many families are already having to drain their bank accounts to pay for child care, and providers such as the YMCA are warning of imminent closures if crisis funding isn't received," she wrote in a statement. "These Conservative cuts will only make life even more unaffordable."
The ministry wrote in a memo to municipalities that the transitional funding was in place due to impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and to support the service managers while they "found efficiencies and adjusted their administrative cost structures."
When it comes to the audits, a spokesperson for Education Minister Stephen Lecce said Ontario is committed to "optimizing the municipal child-care system to better serve families."
"The independent third-party review will drive greater transparency on taxpayer dollars and maximize the number of families supported in the areas that need it most," Isha Chaudhuri wrote in a statement. "In short, it's about delivering better value for Ontario parents."
But others are not so sure.
Zeenat Janmohamed, the executive director and senior policy analyst at the Atkinson Centre for Society and Child Development at the Ontario Institute for Studies in Education, said it sounds like privatization is a possibility.
"Those municipally operated child-care programs ... function as model programs, where the staff remain pretty consistent, they get paid good wages and good benefits, and we're not seeing high turnover in those types of programs, as we are in the community sector," she said.
Mandy Koroniak, the director of children's early years in the County of Wellington, said that like the province, the municipality is always interested in ensuring appropriate budgets are maintained.
"In addition to costs, we also consider fair wages, and the fact that in some communities if the county was unable to provide care other options may not be available to families," she wrote in a statement. "This is a concern in rural and high needs communities."
The City of Toronto commissioned a review of its services a few years ago and the report published in 2021 found that city-run centres serve a much higher proportion of vulnerable families than other centres.
The city-run centres are costlier to operate than comparable non-profit or commercial centres, the report said, largely due to paying early childhood educators higher wages.
"(City-run) centre wage costs are higher than comparator centres but they operate efficiently with lower than average administrative and related costs in part due to centralized administration and the benefits from economies of scale in other expenditures," the report said.
Alana Powell, the executive director of the Association of Early Childhood Educators of Ontario, said it sounds like the province is trying to save money by reducing ECE wages.
"The fact that they're more expensive is because they're making an investment back into the program by appropriately compensating early childhood educators and by hiring more qualified staff than regulations require," she said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario is the only province witha mandated role for municipalities in delivering child care and funding for that administration has until now come entirely from the province.DARRYL DYCK The Canadian Press file photo

Ford doesn't want to hike tuition costs: 'We're going to work with our colleges and universities to support them any way we can': Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-doesnt-want-hike-tuition-costs/docview/2920451166/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+doesn%27t+want+to+hike+tuition+costs%3A+%27We%27re+going+to+work+with+our+colleges+and+universities+to+support+them+any+way+we+can%27%3A+Ford&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford said Wednesday he does not want to raise tuition for post-secondary students, despite colleges and universities struggling financially and a panel that recommended otherwise.
Ford's government cut tuition by 10 per cent in 2019 for Canadian students and froze it.
A government-commissioned "blue-ribbon panel" recommended the province unfreeze tuition, raise student aid and increase operating grants to the schools.
"I just don't believe this is the time to go into these students' pockets, especially the ones that are really struggling, and ask for a tuition increase," Ford said at an unrelated news conference about combating car theft.
The province has not said how it will respond to the panel's report other than saying it would work with the schools to find efficiencies, something Ford reiterated.
"We're going to work with our colleges and universities to support them any way we can and drive efficiencies throughout the system," he said.
"You know, there's some classes out there, believe it or not, that have six or seven students. That's unacceptable."
The tuition cut and freeze on domestic students combined with low provincial funding forced many schools to look for different revenue streams. Colleges in particular vastly increased their reliance on international students, who were not subject to the tuition freeze.
But the federal government recently introduced a cap on international students' study permits that will see Ontario's allotment of new visas cut in half.
The move was done, in part, to deal with "bad actors" in private colleges that made false promises of citizenship after graduation and some institutions that are allegedly diploma mills.
There has also been a recent surge in international students, which the federal government said has increased demand for housing. The province recently said post-secondary institutions must guarantee housing for international students, but has not explained how that will work.
Both Colleges Ontario and the Council of Ontario Universities have asked the federal government to cancel or delay implementing the international student visa cap. Both have also called on the province to immediately implement the recommendations from the blue-ribbon panel.
The Canadian Press

Briefs: $121M to fight auto, gun crimesFertility rate hits all-time low

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/briefs/docview/2920450246/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Briefs%3A+%24121M+to+fight+auto%2C+gun+crimesFertility+rate+hits+all-time+low&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
Mass stabbing inquest ends
The jury and coroner at an inquest into a mass stabbing on a Saskatchewan First Nation have made more than two dozen recommendations to improve corrections, policing and security to prevent future tragedies. Jurors made 14 suggestions, including ways to better locate offenders who are unlawfully at large and further funding and training for security on the James Smith Cree Nation. They also said the First Nation should follow through on the process to establish its own police service. Myles Sanderson was unlawfully at large when he killed 11 people and injured 17 others in 2022.
$121M to fight auto, gun crimes
With a vehicle being stolen every 14 minutes in Ontario, Queen's Park and Ottawa are revving up measures to combat auto theft and gun crimes. Federal Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc announced Wednesday $121 million in nationwide funding over five years to battle the criminal gangs profiting from stealing cars and smuggling guns. "People shouldn't have to live in fear of their cars getting stolen in the middle of the night or doors being kicked in at their residence and sometimes at gunpoint," Premier Doug Ford said.
Fertility rate hits all-time low
Statistics Canada says the country's fertility rate reached an all-time low of 1.33 children per woman in 2022. The federal agency says that's part of a downward trend that began in 2009. Canada's fertility rate fell sharply in 2020, then slightly increased the year after that before resuming its downward trend in 2022. Other countries had a similar experience during those years. The decline in the fertility rate between 2021 and 2022 is the largest observed since the baby bust in the early 1970s.

Document 141 of 252

Do we have ourselves to blame for our politicians?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/do-we-have-ourselves-blame-our-politicians/docview/2920436517/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Do+we+have+ourselves+to+blame+for+our+politicians%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is common to hear people criticize politicians. In many cases there is much to criticize. But how many people have ever asked themselves if they, the voters, are also at fault?
Let me explain. Look at the 2020 and 2022 American elections. In both, the Republican Party (GOP) had no policy platform whatsoever. They focused on supporting Donald Trump in 2020 and fighting "culture war" issues in 2022. And millions of Americans voted for them.
Since then, there has been utter chaos in the GOP-dominated House of Representatives as many of those elected ran on a platform of anger and grievances and have no logical plan as to how to govern.
It isn't just American voters who do this. In Ontario in 2018, the Doug Ford-led Progressive Conservatives won a large majority government with a platform that had the depth of a rain puddle. Voters didn't vote logically based on issues. They were tired of the incumbent Liberal government, so they gave a huge majority government to a man who had very little experience in government. How has that turned out?
Currently, we see the Conservative Party of Canada (CPC) led by Pierre Poilievre leading in the polls. If an election were held today, the Conservatives would win a large majority. But can you name any clear, detailed, policies that Poilievre and the Conservatives have proposed? He says he will "axe" the carbon tax and fight climate change using technology instead. But he will not or cannot state what technology will be used or if it even exists. He blames Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for high inflation in Canada but fails to mention that high inflation is a fact of life around the world. He rightly criticizes Trudeau for reckless spending and running up debt. Yet, he has not presented Canadians with any plan as to how he will balance the federal budget.
Like the GOP, Poilievre and the CPC have no plan as to how they may govern if they win the next election.
Back in 2016, I was attending a dinner party, and the 2015 federal election came up for discussion. One person mentioned he had voted Liberal as he simply disliked Stephen Harper. I asked him "who would you rather be in power if Canada faced a crisis such as a major war, Trudeau or Harper?"
He answered, "Stephen Harper for sure. But I don't like him. Justin Trudeau is simply more likable."
I shook my head in amazement. He voted, not based on policy, or competence, but on who he liked more. How can one justify this?
Think about this in a different way. Assume you have a family business, and you are seeking a finance manager. One candidate has no relevant experience in finance. They are unable to explain what skills and knowledge they bring to the table and cannot answer your questions in a concise manner. The other candidate has a solid background in finance and can explain what exactly they will do to improve your business. Who do you think you would hire?
If you would not hire someone for your business who was not qualified and couldn't explain what they would do to improve the business, why would you vote for candidates who are just like that?
As voters, we owe it to future generations to only vote for candidates who can present detailed policy alternatives and who have related experience. This also requires voters to be engaged and informed. This isn't hard to do. On your way home from work, listen to the news on the radio. During your lunch hour, read a mainstream newspaper (such as this one.) And take the time to write to your elected officials and let them know what you are thinking. If we all did this, we would see far better qualified people running for office.
CAPTION: In 2018, the Doug Ford-led Progressive Conservatives won a large majority government with a platform that had the depth of a rain puddle. Voters didn't vote logically based on issues. They were tired of the incumbent Liberal government, Craig Wallace writes.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Location: Canada

People: Harper, Stephen; Trudeau, Justin

Under pressure from Toronto and Quebec, Trudeau government announces $362.4 million in new refugee supports

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/under-pressure-toronto-quebec-trudeau-government/docview/2920739963/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Under+pressure+from+Toronto+and+Quebec%2C+Trudeau+government+announces+%24362.4+million+in+new+refugee+supports&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Under pressure from the City of Toronto and Quebec to foot a bigger portion of the bill for asylum seekers, the federal Liberal government announced $362.4 million in new refugee supports nationwide.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller refused to reveal how much of that will go to Toronto, saying it will be revealed in the coming days. Quebec will get $100 million of that new money, he said Wednesday at a hastily organized news conference outside the House of Commons.
Miller said all levels of government have to work to settle asylum seekers
The money will flow through the interim housing assistance program.
Other than Quebec's share, immediate breakdowns for the hardest-hit cities and regions were not available.
Still, the move represents a big shift by the federal government which up to now has resisted demands by Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow to pony up the $250 million that the city expects to spend this year to take care of refugee claimants.
The Trudeau government has repeatedly argued it already supports Toronto with billions of dollars, and sends billions more to the province which should bear more responsibility.
When Chow threatened to label an additional six per cent tax hike a "federal impacts levy" that would come on top of a city staff-proposed 10.5 per cent increase, members of Trudeau's 23-strong Toronto caucus bristled.
But Chow reached a "new deal" with Premier Doug Ford, whose government agreed to provide $200 million a year to cover shelter costs for homeless populations, with Ford and Chow joining forces to call on the federal government to cover the cost of sheltering refugees.
This is a breaking story. More to come.

Document 143 of 252

Canada's dental benefit is expanding. Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-dental-benefit-is-expanding-heres-what/docview/2920729831/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+dental+benefit+is+expanding.+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Starting Thursday, applications for Canada's dental benefit will open up to an additional group of seniors needing help with their oral health care.If you're 72 years of age and older, and you meet a list of other eligibility criteria, you could soon start receiving coverage for a number of dental services.The benefit, slated to cost $13 billion over five years, was first launched in December for Canadians aged 87 and up. On Wednesday, Citizens' Services Minister Terry Beech said about 408,000 seniors have applied for the benefit so far, clocking in at an average of 20,000 new sign-ups a day.Health Minister Mark Holland also touted the measure as the largest benefit program implemented in Canada's history. The government estimates it could capture up to nine million Canadians, though some health groups have charged that eligibility requirements around income levels leave out millions of others still deserving of aid.The program will be further expanded to other seniors, people with disabilities, and youth under 18 over the course of the year, with remaining Canadians able to access the help in 2025. The rollout is a key requirement of the Liberal-NDP deal that could see the New Democrats prop up the minority Liberals until 2025. Here's what you need to know if you're hoping to apply.I'm 72 or older. Do I qualify?Like all applicants for the program, you're eligible if you don't have access to dental insurance (through your employer, your pension, a benefits company or a family member), if your family's net income is less than $90,000, if you're a Canadian resident, and if you filed your tax return last year.If you're already covered through a provincial, territorial or other federal program, you're still eligible: your coverage will just be co-ordinated between both plans.The federal government has the ability to verify your eligibility details, so if you falsify any information, you could be kicked off the plan and forced to pay up for the services you received.How do I apply?If you qualify, you'll be sent a letter as soon as Feb. 1 from Service Canada inviting you to apply. As of May, applications will switch over to an online process. That letter will include an application code and instructions on how to apply. For seniors, the application process can be completed by phone.If your information checks out, you'll be referred to Sun Life, which will enrol you in the program and send further information, including your member ID card and the date your coverage begins.At that point, you'll be covered for services like cleanings, X-rays, fillings, root canals, dentures and extractions. Some of those procedures, however, won't be covered until this fall. The lowest-income seniors won't have to pay for procedures, but eligible Canadians at other income levels will have to contribute a co-payment.When can I start going to the dentist?You can start receiving services under the plan as early as May, though younger seniors might have to wait a little longer.That's assuming the rollout goes smoothly, which is not guaranteed.Some dental associations have raised the alarm that despite discussions with Health Canada, dentists across the country still don't have a clear picture of how the program would work for providers."Are (patients) going to be able to go to any dentist and access this program? Is it going to be simple for not just the patient, but the dentist?" said Dr. Brock Nicolucci, president of the Ontario Dental Association."You hear about health-care burnout throughout the country and dentistry is not immune to this. So who's going to be doing all that if there is an extra administration burden?"Nicolucci told the Star he's worried some providers might not be able to handle the increased strain, leaving Canadians to seek help elsewhere or forgo receiving care entirely. He said he would have "loved" to see Ottawa finalize how the program would actually work before trumpeting its launch.On Wednesday, Holland said that while he could not promise the rollout would be "perfect," he remains committed to ensuring dentists have the answers they need."We're ready imminently to be able to share information with all providers, with all dentists," Holland said."We'll be able to talk about the fruits of those negotiations very soon."
CAPTION: Minister of Health Mark Holland speaks to reporters in the foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Jan. 31, 2024.

Document 144 of 252

Under pressure from universities, Doug Ford says it's 'not the time' for a tuition increase

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/under-pressure-universities-doug-ford-says-not/docview/2920227608/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Under+pressure+from+universities%2C+Doug+Ford+says+it%27s+%27not+the+time%27+for+a+tuition+increase&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario college and university students won't be paying more tuition this fall, says Premier Doug Ford.
"I just don't believe this is the time to go into these students' pockets - especially the ones that are really struggling - and ask for a tuition increase," Ford said Wednesday morning.
Ford, speaking at an unrelated announcement in Aurora, was asked about a recent blue-ribbon panel looking at post-secondary finances that recommended the provincial tuition freeze be lifted and allowed to increase by five per cent for this fall.
Shortly after taking office in 2018, the Ford government slashed tuition by 10 per cent for students and has not allowed schools to raise rates since.
Universities had been hoping for some flexibility to raise fees and are also hoping for an immediate 10 per cent funding increase the panel also urged.
"We're going to work with our colleges and universities to support them any way we can and drive efficiencies throughout the system," Ford also said.
"There's some classes out there, believe it or not, that have six or seven students. That's unacceptable. So there are areas of improvement but we're always there to support our colleges and universities."
Queen's University is among those that have started to lay off staff and look at axing low enrolment classes. Its provost recently said the school could shut down if no cuts are made, though Principal Patrick Deane sent out a letter last week saying there is "no risk that Queen's in any foreseeable future will close its doors."
Ford was also asked about the recent federal announcement on a cap for international students, and he acknowledged there are some "bad actors" among private colleges.
Public colleges - which have taken on more lucrative partnerships with private ones - and universities have seen an increase in international students, who pay four or five times the tuition of domestic students. Many have done so as a way to make up for stalled government funding.
Ford also said the province didn't drop the ball in keeping international student enrolment at a reasonable rate, given the current housing crunch which has been in part blamed on the number of foreign students that has ballooned under his government.
More to come.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford says he does not want to burden students with a tuition increase this fall, despite calls from universities and colleges to allow for a fee hike to help address financial pressures.Queen's is one of 10 universities across the province running a deficit and looking to make cuts if the government doesn't allow a tuition hike or boost funding. Premier Doug Ford signalled Wednesday that he's not in favour of a tuition increase.

Document 145 of 252

Doug Ford, Justin Trudeau governments announces $121M in federal funding to fight auto theft, gun crimes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-justin-trudeau-governments-announces/docview/2920227404/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%2C+Justin+Trudeau+governments+announces+%24121M+in+federal+funding+to+fight+auto+theft%2C+gun+crimes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a vehicle being stolen every 14 minutes in Ontario, Queen's Park and Ottawa are revving up measures to combat auto theft and gun crimes.
Federal Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc announced Wednesday $121 million in nationwide funding over five years to battle the criminal gangs profiting from stealing cars and smuggling guns.
"People shouldn't have to live in fear of their cars getting stolen in the middle of the night or doors being kicked in at their residence and sometimes at gunpoint," Premier Doug Ford told reporters at the joint announcement at York Regional Police headquarters in Aurora.
Ford praised Ottawa for stepping up, noting the provincial government has already earmarked $51 million over three years "to help police identify and dismantle criminal networks behind auto theft rings."
"I have a message to all the criminals, we're coming after you and we're going to catch you and you're going to jail," the premier said.
Next week, LeBlanc will host a national summit on fighting auto theft in Ottawa.
The minister said the Feb. 8 confab would be a chance for governments, police and insurance companies to share best practices and and "further coordinate joint activities."
Federal Justice Minister Arif Virani said his constituents in Parkdale-High Park "are scared" by the alarming rise in auto theft.
"That's terrifying for people," said Virani.
According to the Insurance Board of Canada, auto insurers paid out $1.2 billion in theft claims in 2022, which is triple what was paid out in 2018 and the trend continued to climb in the first half of last year.
Last fall, Ford announced new "preventing auto thefts" grant for 21 projects using modern crime-fighting tools that focus on focused on "prevention, detection, analysis and enforcement."
These included surveillance equipment and GPS tracking devices for police forces and additional training for investigators and moves designed to "break down silos and address organized criminal activity."
The Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area has become a hotbed of auto theft with gangs stealing vehicles and exporting them abroad.
CAPTION: Doug Ford praised Ottawa for stepping up during the funding announcement.

Subject: Theft; Criminal investigations; Robbery; Funding

Document 146 of 252

Provinces want pause on assisted dying changes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provinces-want-pause-on-assisted-dying-changes/docview/2920059894/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provinces+want+pause+on+assisted+dying+changes&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A majority of provinces and territories are asking the federal government to indefinitely pause its plans to expand eligibility for assisted dying.
A parliamentary committee reported Monday that the health system is not ready for the assisted dying regime to include people whose only condition is a mental illness.
That expansion was set to take effect in March, but Health Minister Mark Holland and Justice Minister Arif Virani say they agree it shouldn't happen so soon.
Health and mental-health ministers from all three territories, along with Alberta, Saskatchewan, Ontario, New Brunswick and British Columbia, asked Holland in a letter on Monday to give them more time to collaborate.
"It is critical that all jurisdictions, health authorities, regulators and (medical assistance in dying) practitioners have sufficient time to implement these safeguards and to address capacity concerns that are expected to result from the expansion," it read.
The expansion date set for March doesn't provide jurisdictions with enough time to get prepared, the letter said.
"Therefore, we encourage you and federal Justice Minister Virani to indefinitely pause the implementation of the expanded (medical assistance in dying) eligibility criteria to enable further collaboration between provinces, territories, and the federal government."
Ministers from Nova Scotia and Prince Edward Island said their provinces are more prepared for the changes, but nonetheless signed on to support their colleagues.
The federal Conservatives are calling on the Liberals to cancel plans for the expansion altogether. The New Democrats, who are supporting the Liberals on key parliamentary votes in exchange for progress on shared legislative priorities, say more mental-health supports need to be in place before any expansion.
Speaking to reporters on this way into the Liberals' weekly cabinet meeting Tuesday, Holland said given that legislation is forthcoming, he is bound by parliamentary privilege and cannot yet detail how much more of a delay the Liberals are prepared to go with.
Former justice minister David Lametti, who introduced that legislation, recently told The Canadian Press that he believes the necessary preparations have been completed and he "wouldn't be afraid personally of moving forward with it."
But Holland said Tuesday that provinces are telling him they still aren't prepared enough.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

Document 147 of 252

Justin Trudeau's government may rebrand its carbon pricing program. But is it too late?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-government-may-rebrand-carbon/docview/2920277751/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+government+may+rebrand+its+carbon+pricing+program.+But+is+it+too+late%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The federal government's discussions around rebranding the rebate program for its carbon pricing system are aimed at ensuring Canadians are aware of what their rebates are for, Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson said Tuesday.
The first public acknowledgment of a potential rebrand of the government's flagship climate policy comes as the Conservatives immediately dismissed the idea, and experts and political insiders raised questions on whether a rebrand would boost perceptions of the carbon price.
"I think the discussions around rebranding really relate to trying to ensure that it's understandable," Wilkinson said on his way into the Liberals' weekly cabinet meeting. "A lot of folks out there don't know what they're getting when they actually get the rebate."
The Star reported Monday that the federal Liberals were taking a "serious look" at how the Climate Action Incentive Payment was being branded, according to a senior government official, who was not authorized to speak on the matter.
The source said there was a multi-department effort weighing a name change for the rebate and examining how to improve labelling from banks across Canada. Any changes would likely come alongside the next quarterly rebate payment in April, they said, although discussions are in early stages and further details are unclear.
But political marketing experts and insiders who spoke to the Star, including those long associated with the Liberal party, said a rebrand may not be enough to fix the Liberals' communications on carbon pricing. At issue, they said, is a pattern of poor messaging that has not resonated with Canadians, and an opposition that has filled the vacuum and capitalized on Canadians' economic concerns to control the narrative.
Catherine McKenna, who first introduced the price on carbon as environment and climate change minister in 2018, said she had worked incessantly after introducing the policy to make sure she and MPs emphasized the rebates to Canadians. But she can't help but feel "disappointed" with how the government has since communicated the policy, she said.
"Whatever you want to call it, you have to ensure you're telling people they're getting money back," she said, calling for an "all-MP effort and "constant communications."
Éric Blais, a frequent commentator on political marketing, said increased government advertising may help the Liberals, but noted Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's first-term efforts to limit partisan government advertising has hindered that possibility. The Canada Revenue Agency has not spent any money on advertising the Climate Action Incentive Payment since 2020.
He said he worries the Liberals might think a rebrand would transform negative perceptions of the policy after it has already been so successfully portrayed as a punitive tax by the Conservatives.
"If you don't brand yourself, someone else will do it for you," Blais told the Star. "That's exactly what happened here."
Carbon pricing aims to encourage consumers and companies to produce less greenhouse gas emissions by making it more expensive. Revenue from the price on carbon is returned to Canadians through a rebate, leaving 80 per cent of those eligible with more money than they pay, according to the Parliamentary Budget Officer.
Indeed, the Conservatives quickly dismissed the idea of a rebrand as "putting lipstick on a pig."
"Now we have their big solution. They are going to give it a new name. They are going to rename the carbon tax," Poilievre said in the House of Commons on Monday. "They think people will not notice their heating bills, gas bills and grocery bills are going through the roof under this tax if they just give it a new name."
"Taking money out of the pockets of Canadians rebranded is still taking money out of their pockets," Conservative deputy leader Melissa Lantsman said Tuesday.
Trudeau and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland dodged reporters' questions on the government's considerations of a rebrand on Monday, although Freeland criticized the Tories' pledge to eliminate the consumer price on carbon, saying the move would take money out of Canadians' pockets.
"The Conservatives want to take carbon price rebate cheques out of the bank accounts of Canadian families," Freeland said in the House of Commons. "That is $1,000 in Ontario and more than $1,000 in Alberta and Saskatchewan. That would hurt Canadians."
Exclusive polling for the Star by Abacus Data suggests Canadians are split on whether carbon pricing was a good policy, even in Liberal strongholds such as Ontario and the Atlantic Provinces. And while 72 per cent of those eligible for the Climate Action Incentive Payment reported being aware of the rebate, only 48 per cent knew it had to do with carbon pricing.
The office of Environment and Climate Change Minister Steven Guilbeault declined to comment Tuesday.
CAPTION: Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson speaks during question period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa.

Document 148 of 252

Sikh advocacy group seeks role at Canada's inquiry into foreign interference

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sikh-advocacy-group-seeks-role-at-canadas-inquiry/docview/2920277735/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sikh+advocacy+group+seeks+role+at+Canada%27s+inquiry+into+foreign+interference&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A prominent Sikh advocacy group wants to play a role at the foreign interference inquiry after commissioner Marie-Josée Hogue added India to the list of countries she's probing as part of her work.
The initial terms of reference for the Hogue commission named China and Russia as the states under the microscope for their actions during the 2019 and 2021 elections. But five days before the start of public hearings this week, Hogue asked the government to hand over information and documents relating to alleged interference by India as well.
Her specific reasons for doing so were not disclosed. The commission's terms allow Hogue to probe "other foreign states or non-state actors."
Sikh organizations in Canada have warned for years that India's actions weren't being taken seriously enough by the Canadian government.
In a report last spring, the B.C. and Ontario associations of gurdwaras cited numerous security briefings that explicitly named the risk of India seeking to influence the diaspora community in Canada.
"Indian officials and intelligence operatives have manufactured news, offered bribes to media outlets for favourable news coverage, amplified targeted messages to disrupt public debate, interfered in electoral processes across the country, and have attempted to manipulate Canadian policymakers," the report alleged.
"This is not a partisan issue as all parties have been impacted by Indian attempts to manipulate electoral outcomes."
Their concerns were further amplified last fall when Prime Minister Justin Trudeau revealed there was credible allegations that India was linked to the killing of a prominent Sikh leader in B.C.
The World Sikh Organization had called for India to be included in the commission's work and is pleased Hogue followed suit, the organization's legal counsel Balpreet Singh told the Star on Tuesday.
"We are looking at requesting the commission to use its discretion to grant us intervener status, given this development," Singh said.
"We are consulting with other community stakeholders and will be reaching out to the commission very shortly."
India denies it has interfered in Canadian affairs, and had initially rejected all assertions it was involved in Hardeep Singh Nijjar's death in Surrey, B.C. The refusal of the Indian government to co-operate in the investigation was cited by the Trudeau Liberals as the reason they made the allegations public. Talks between Delhi and Ottawa are ongoing.
While the formal deadline to apply for status at the commission passed in November, the commissioner can consider an application for status at any time.
Hogue was tasked last year with overseeing a public inquiry into foreign state interference in the last two elections, the latest effort by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to respond to months of media reports detailing apparent incidents of states engaging in Canadian electoral contests, but the public - and those targeted - weren't made aware until well after the fact.
Another demand for an expansion of Hogue's mandate came Tuesday from the Conservative party, which has intervener status at the inquiry.
In a letter, they requested that Hogue also include what it called Iran's campaign of intimidation, repression and interference in the scope of her work.
The party cited revelations Monday that two Canadians were charged in a plot to kill people in the U.S. who had fled Iran.
The letter also notes that in 2021, the federal body charged with election oversight said Iran was engaged in cyber activities aimed at influencing campaigns. It also cites public statements by security officials naming Iran as an active participant in interference campaigns.
"With the many high-profile examples of Iranian interference in Canada, both in relation to our democratic processes and attempts to repress political expression in Canada, it is of the utmost importance that Iran be a subject of interest in the commission's work," says the letter, first reported by the Globe and Mail and obtained by the Star.
The Conservative party has been frustrated at being denied robust access to the proceedings; intervenors have the ability to examine exhibits entered into evidence at the commission, but none of the classified materials available to the parties given full standing.
What can or can't be disclosed publicly over the course of the inquiry is at the heart of the first week of hearings, which on Tuesday heard from a panel of academics with extensive expertise in national security laying out what they think the challenges are in that regard.
CAPTION: Commissioner Justice Marie-Josée Hogue waits behind a curtain on Tuesday before entering the public inquiry looking into foreign interference in federal elections.

Subject: Public inquiries; Public hearings; Prime ministers; Political parties

Location: Iran; Canada; India

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 149 of 252

Ontario seeks approval to refurbish 4 Pickering nuclear reactors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-seeks-approval-refurbish-4-pickering/docview/2919810907/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+seeks+approval+to+refurbish+4+Pickering+nuclear+reactors&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will seek approval to refurbish four aging 1980s Candu nuclear reactors at Pickering - a decade-long project aimed at milking another 30 years of electricity from the power plant.
The move comes 15 years after a refurbishment at the nuclear generating station first slated to close in 2018 was rejected as too expensive by a previous Liberal government following a detailed feasibility study.
But amid soaring demand for electricity and looming federal regulations requiring carbon-free emissions from power plants, Pickering could continue producing enough power for two million homes as Ontario grows "quickly," Energy Minister Todd Smith said Tuesday.
"With all that growth comes new energy needs," Smith said at the plant on the shore of Lake Ontario just east of Toronto. "Our province still needs this station and its workers."
Approval for the multibillion refurbishment for the four reactors known as Pickering B is required from the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission. The four original Pickering A reactors that began operating in 1971 are being retired at the end of this year.
The safety commission is already considering an extension of Pickering B's operating licence by one year to September 2026, four years after the next provincial election. Crews would begin the 11-year refurbishment after that if approval is granted.
Premier Doug Ford's government has been considering the refurbishment plan by Crown-owned Ontario Power Generation since 2022, with OPG dusting off and updating its 2009 study.
It was widely expected the government would back the plan despite opposition from proponents of renewable sources of energy - such as solar and wind, which are falling in price - who warn more nuclear could force electricity prices up.
Nuclear plants at Darlington and Bruce on Lake Huron are currently being refurbished. The price tag at Darlington, for example, is $12 billion. No budget was given for the Pickering project, which would create about 11,000 jobs annually until its completion in the mid-2030s and sustain 6,400 jobs a year once the plant resumes producing.
That experience and a veteran workforce of refurbishment crews can be applied at Pickering, said Ontario Power Generation president Ken Hartwick.
"We have learned a lot about what it takes."
CAPTION: Energy Minister Todd Smith addresses a news conference at the Pickering nuclear generating station in Pickering, Ont. on Jan.30, 2024.

Subject: Nuclear reactors; Electricity; Nuclear power plants

Business indexing term: Subject: Nuclear power plants

Title: Ontario seeks approval to refurbish 4 Pickering nuclear reactors

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 30, 2024

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Document 150 of 252

Where is Hamilton's mayor on our most pressing issues?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-is-hamiltons-mayor-on-our-most-pressing/docview/2919808633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+is+Hamilton%27s+mayor+on+our+most+pressing+issues%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For the last year or so, since the new council took office, I've been watching with dismay as the housing crisis, homelessness, poverty and food insecurity continue to ravage our most vulnerable citizens.
More and more people are slipping through inadequate safety nets, are underhoused, homeless or living in precarious situations.
The fault for this lies squarely with the provincial government, which has abandoned its obligations in public housing and the principles of universal health care and continues to legislate poverty among those who receive social assistance.
The federal government is not to be let off the hook for failing to assist with immigration settlement, particularly for refugees who arrive with next to nothing and find nothing awaiting them. Dragging the Canada Disability Benefit through months and months of consultation continues to hurt people.
The effects of this neglect, besides being felt by those affected, are seen in our neighbourhoods and communities as people do what they can to get their needs met. More and more has been pushed onto the municipal plate in terms of providing emergency services to plug the gaps left by provincial and federal neglect.
We can't escape the effect of this neglect; it exists among us. As the government most immediate to our needs, the municipal government is left to pick up the slack. We demand it from them, for something to be done.
People are angry and fed up with the lack of real solutions to these age-old problems. Nothing is new here. In fact, when I consider the living conditions of some of our neighbours, I'm reminded of Dickensian England, with rapacious capitalists feasting on the poor and downtrodden and politicians getting rich off public service.
Despite the millions and millions we're dedicating to addressing emergency services, it doesn't seem like anything is happening. The emergency is not abating. In fact, it seems to be getting worse. The guy who has been living on York Boulevard for years still lives there. If anyone ever needed help, it's him. Yet there he still lives.
It's not enough for councillors to point out how much our property taxes are going up because of provincial downloading, shrug their shoulders and say "what can we do?" as though they had no power themselves. Maybe they don't. Maybe they don't want it. Maybe they have aspirations toward higher office and need friends? Who knows?
But we elected councillors to fight for our interests, not pussyfoot around and smile nicely at official functions while we're getting hit every which way we look for more money, being gaslit into believing something is getting done.
Where is our champion at Queen's Park? Where is "scrappy" Mayor Andrea Horwath? We need bold words and brave actions to protect us from the abuse of the provincial government. I'm not seeing that.
In fact, I'm not seeing much of the "scrappy" fighter at all. Where are the strong words for Premier Doug Ford? Where is the outrage at what's happening to our people under Progressive Conservative government neglect? Where is the commitment to advocating for the "little guy?" There are no "littler guys" than homeless people, yet I don't see our mayor on the front lines of that fight. I don't see her mingling with the unwashed masses, giving out food or showing compassion on the street.
I hoped the mayor would be more proactive in pushing for the province to step up to its obligations. Maybe use her "strong mayor" powers to effect innovative change or push forward contentious projects, like the HATS project. I hoped she might have a plan of her own, but she seems to be playing catch-up to councillors who have worked on tenant protection policies for years.
People are asking, still, where is Andrea?
CAPTION: I'm not seeing much of Andrea Horwath the "scrappy" fighter, Margaret Shkimba writes. Where are the strong words for Premier Doug Ford? Where is the outrage at what's happening to our people under Progressive Conservative government neglect?

Document 151 of 252

Hamilton hits provincial housing target. But anticipated $12-million payout 'falls short' of funding losses: officials

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-hits-provincial-housing-target/docview/2919807989/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+hits+provincial+housing+target.+But+anticipated+%2412-million+payout+%27falls+short%27+of+funding+losses%3A+officials&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton stands to land $12 million in funding after hitting provincial housing targets last year, but that still won't fill a local fiscal gap caused by other legislation, city officials say.
To receive their share of the Building Faster Fund, municipalities must hit at least 80 per cent of their provincially mandated housing goals.
"We overqualified," Jason Thorne, the city's general manager of economic development and planning, told council during a recent budget presentation.
Hamilton achieved 3,347 housing starts - when construction on homes begins - compared to the annual target, 3,447, or nearly 100 per cent of the mark.
That's according to Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) data posted on the province's online "housing tracker."
The Ontario government "will begin to roll out" the Building Faster Fund allocations to municipalities that have hit or surpassed their targets "in the coming months," Bianca Meta, spokesperson for the Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing, said via email.
The Building Faster Fund, which is to provide $400 million in "new annual funding" for three years, springs from Premier Doug Ford's government's goal to add 1.5 million homes to Ontario's supply by 2031. Hamilton's target is 47,000 units.
The initiative has been met by criticism from municipal leaders, including in Hamilton, who say they're able to approve development applications, but can't force developers to actually start construction.
For instance, in 2023, the city issued permits for 4,263 units compared to the 3,347 actual starts, markers that were both "just shy" of records, Thorne noted.
Overall, it was a "pretty extraordinary year," he said, noting a record-setting $2.5 billion in construction activity in Hamilton, which erased the previous high-water mark of $1.2 billion.
However, the city expects a dip in development-generated revenues in 2024 as the pace of applications starts to slow.
Thorne pointed to a number of factors, including "some general uncertainty" as developers see a drop in sales across all housing types.
"Their borrowing costs are up, their construction costs are up. The skilled-labour shortage is also an impact on them."
One high-profile example of the retreating market revealed itself in December, when the firm behind the redevelopment of the downtown Hamilton City Centre mall into highrise condos announced the project was "on pause."
"The real estate market has fallen off a cliff," IN8 Developments president Darryl Firsten told The Spectator. "But it'll come back, there is still a lot of demand to live in downtown Hamilton."
Amid this "cooling," the hope is that in 2025 development applications will "start to rebound," Thorne told council Friday.
Meanwhile, city officials have lamented the upshot of Bill 23, which lowers the revenue municipalities can draw from development charges through exemptions, reductions and phase-ins.
Cities rely on development charges to help pay for growth-related infrastructure, such as sewer lines, recreation centres and firehalls.
The Building Faster Fund is meant to make "the municipality whole," but its anticipated $12-million payout falls short of the roughly $35 million the city is losing through Bill 23, finance boss Mike Zegarac told council during budget talks.
"So, we'll be made whole by a third," Coun. Brad Clark responded. "So somebody ate the other two-thirds of the pie."
That gap is "being absorbed by local taxpayers and ratepayers," Zegarac noted.
Another consideration is that the city hasn't yet seen detailed criteria that guides how it must direct the funding, whether it's to make up for exemptions or other areas, he added.
On its website, the province says allocations from the Building Faster Fund "can be directed toward housing-enabling infrastructure and other related costs that support community growth."
In her email, Meta told The Spectator "more information will be shared" about the funds "as soon as it's available."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: In 2023, the city issued permits for 4,263 units compared to 3,347 actual housing starts, markers that were both "just shy" of records,Jason Thorne, the city's general manager of economic development and planning, noted.In December, the firm behind the redevelopment of Hamilton City Centre on James Street North announced the project was "on pause."Mike Zegarac, general manager of finances and corporate services, says the city is losing roughly $35 million through Bill 23."So we'll be made whole by a third," Coun. Brad Clark said during Friday budget talks.Hamilton achieved 3,347 housing starts - when construction on homes begins - compared to the annual target, 3,447, or nearly 100 per cent of the mark.

Where's the mayor on our most pressing issues?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wheres-mayor-on-our-most-pressing-issues/docview/2919751090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where%27s+the+mayor+on+our+most+pressing+issues%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For the last year or so, since the new council took office, I've been watching with dismay as the housing crisis, homelessness, poverty and food insecurity continue to ravage our most vulnerable citizens.
More and more people are slipping through inadequate safety nets, are underhoused, homeless or living in precarious situations.
The fault for this lies squarely with the provincial government, which has abandoned its obligations in public housing and the principles of universal health care and continues to legislate poverty among those who receive social assistance.
The federal government is not to be let off the hook for failing to assist with immigration settlement, particularly for refugees who arrive with next to nothing and find nothing awaiting them. Dragging the Canada Disability Benefit through months and months of consultation continues to hurt people.
The effects of this neglect, besides being felt by those affected, are seen in our neighbourhoods and communities as people do what they can to get their needs met. More and more has been pushed onto the municipal plate in terms of providing emergency services to plug the gaps left by provincial and federal neglect.
We can't escape the effect of this neglect; it exists among us. As the government most immediate to our needs, the municipal government is left to pick up the slack. We demand it from them, for something to be done.
People are angry and fed up with the lack of real solutions to these age-old problems. Nothing is new here. In fact, when I consider the living conditions of some of our neighbours, I'm reminded of Dickensian England, with rapacious capitalists feasting on the poor and downtrodden and politicians getting rich off public service.
Despite the millions and millions we're dedicating to addressing emergency services, it doesn't seem like anything is happening. The emergency is not abating. In fact, it seems to be getting worse. The guy who has been living on York Boulevard for years still lives there. If anyone ever needed help, it's him. Yet there he still lives.
It's not enough for councillors to point out how much our property taxes are going up because of provincial downloading, shrug their shoulders and say "what can we do?" as though they had no power themselves. Maybe they don't. Maybe they don't want it. Maybe they have aspirations toward higher office and need friends? Who knows?
But we elected councillors to fight for our interests, not pussyfoot around and smile nicely at official functions while we're getting hit every which way we look for more money, being gaslit into believing something is getting done.
Where is our champion at Queen's Park? Where is "scrappy" Mayor Andrea Horwath? We need bold words and brave actions to protect us from the abuse of the provincial government. I'm not seeing that.
In fact, I'm not seeing much of the "scrappy" fighter at all. Where are the strong words for Premier Doug Ford? Where is the outrage at what's happening to our people under Progressive Conservative government neglect? Where is the commitment to advocating for the "little guy?" There are no "littler guys" than homeless people, yet I don't see our mayor on the front lines of that fight. I don't see her mingling with the unwashed masses, giving out food or showing compassion on the street.
I hoped the mayor would be more proactive in pushing for the province to step up to its obligations. Maybe use her "strong mayor" powers to effect innovative change or push forward contentious projects, like the HATS project. I hoped she might have a plan of her own, but she seems to be playing catch-up to councillors who have worked on tenant protection policies for years.
People are asking, still, where is Andrea?
