Document 154 of 252

Doug Ford government approves three MZOs - with Olivia Chow's blessing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-approves-three-mzos-with/docview/2919899117/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+approves+three+MZOs+-+with+Olivia+Chow%27s+blessing&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government has quietly issued three minister's zoning orders for key Toronto projects with the approval of Mayor Olivia Chow, the Star has learned.
In contrast to past MZOs - a controversial tool used to override local planning decisions that has fallen out of favour due to the Greenbelt scandal - the province worked in lockstep with city hall.
MZOs have gained notoriety amid concerns from municipalities, opposition parties, community groups and environmentalists that the government was invoking too many of them.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, who announced he was revisiting the use of zoning orders six weeks ago, has greenlit the three projects.
The new MZOs, which were posted online Friday, clear the way for:
 The preservation and protection of air flight paths of Ornge emergency helicopters that need to land at heliports at the Hospital for Sick Children on University Avenue and St. Michael's Hospital on Queen Street West.
 The expansion of the University Health Network's Toronto Western Hospital patient tower, a new 13-storey building with 100 in-patient beds and 20 operating theatres on Bathurst Street, just north of Dundas Street West.
 The relocation of Iqbal Halal Foods, a popular grocery story in Thorncliffe Park displaced by Metrolinx in order to build a maintenance and storage facility for the new Ontario Line subway.
Calandra worked closely with Chow on the MZOs.
"The mayor's office was consulted on all three of these," Arianne Robinson, Chow's press secretary, confirmed Monday.
At Queen's Park, Justine Teplycky, Calandra's director of communication, said "these MZOs have now been signed and approved by the minister."
"Minister's zoning orders remain an important tool to get critical local projects moving at the pace that Ontarians need and deserve," she said.
Teplycky noted when Calandra announced he was scaling back MZOs on Dec. 13, he had promised to push forward protection of the air ambulance flight corridor and a new location for the 30-year-old Halal supermarket.
"(It was) a request received from the minister of health and endorsed by Mayor Olivia Chow related to the protection of air flight paths of Ornge emergency helicopters, patients, and crews that need to land at hospitals in downtown Toronto," she said.
"(The second was) a request endorsed by Mayor Chow and Toronto city council intended to support a new location for a regionally significant Halal grocery store in the Thorncliffe Park neighbourhood of Toronto."
City council passed a motion on Dec. 13 "to permit a grocery store on the lands at 100 Thorncliffe Park Dr., following the displacement of the grocery store at 2 Thorncliffe Park Dr., as a result of Metrolinx's expropriation of lands."
But the expansion of the Toronto Western Hospital was not mentioned last month when the Progressive Conservatives heralded a curb in MZOs.
"Additionally, an MZO request was received from the minister of health to deliver on provincial priorities," said Teplycky.
"It was supported by Mayor Chow and Toronto city council relating to the expansion of the ... UHN Toronto Western Hospital patient tower," she said.
"This MZO will facilitate an expansion of the hospital with the development of a new 13-storey building ... as well as a wastewater energy transfer facility site. This MZO has also been approved."
At the time he announced the MZO pause, Calandra warned eight orders not related to housing and 14 others connected to other developments would be revoked because there was not "substantial progress" on them.
"I've always been clear that if we do not see the results respecting the minister zoning order, our government will not hesitate to amend or revoke it," Calandra said in December.
"It sends a clear message that when we issue a zoning order to support priorities like housing, long-term care, we expect to see results," he said.
In the wake of the Tories' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, which is now under investigation by the RCMP, the use of MZOs to expedite development has been widely criticized.
Last August, separate reports by the auditor general and integrity commissioner concluded that certain well-connected developers were "favoured" when Ford opened up 7,400 acres of protected Greenbelt land to build housing.
A month later, the premier was forced to cancel the land swap.
So far, two cabinet ministers and two top aides have parted ways with Ford over a debacle that continues to loom large.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow speak at a news conference at Queen's Park in Toronto on Nov. 27, 2023.

Subject: Relocation; Councils; Halal food; Zoning; Cities; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Grocery stores

Title: Doug Ford government approves three MZOs - with Olivia Chow's blessing

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Document 155 of 252

Amid confusion from Canadians, Justin Trudeau's government is considering rebrand of carbon pricing: source

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/amid-confusion-canadians-justin-trudeaus/docview/2919312295/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Amid+confusion+from+Canadians%2C+Justin+Trudeau%27s+government+is+considering+rebrand+of+carbon+pricing%3A+source&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The federal government is considering a rebrand of the rebate program for its carbon pricing system in an attempt to tackle what it calls confusion and misconceptions about the scheme, the Star has learned.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals have admitted they are losing the communications battle over carbon pricing in the face of a campaign from Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives to undermine the government's flagship climate policy.
Now, exclusive polling from Abacus Data for the Star details Canadians' confusion about the policy.
Ottawa's efforts to better communicate its carbon pricing system come after a year of relentless attacks from federal Conservatives, who routinely blame the "carbon tax" for the rising costs of fuel and food while often neglecting the rebates that Canadians receive under the program.
"The government is taking a serious look at how the climate action incentive payment is being branded," a senior government source told the Star. The multi-department effort includes weighing whether a name change for the rebate would boost perceptions, and ongoing efforts to improve banks' labelling of the quarterly payments. Any changes would come into place as the government rolls out its yearly update on the carbon price and the rebate.
During the national Liberal caucus retreat in Ottawa this week, several MPs who spoke to the Star admitted the Conservatives' attacks have made it difficult to communicate the policy in their ridings, but insisted their constituents remained on board with it.
But the Abacus poll suggests Canadians are split on whether it's a good policy. In Ontario, where the Liberals hold 75 of 121 seats, 45 per cent of respondents said they believed it was a bad policy, compared to 37 per cent who said it was a good one. In the Atlantic Provinces, where the Liberals hold 24 out of 32 seats, 49 per cent believed it was a bad policy, while 31 per cent said it was a good one.
"It's been turned into this serious wedge issue," Milton MP Adam van Koeverden told the Star. "And there's so much misinformation out there about what it is and what it isn't."
Using online panels, Abacus surveyed a representative sample of 2,199 Canadian adults between Jan. 18 and 23. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.09 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Last week, Poilievre was in Prince Rupert, B.C., for his latest "axe the tax" rally. Because B.C. has its own carbon price, the federal levy does not apply.
"The Conservatives are doing their best to muddle the waters," said Vancouver Liberal MP Patrick Weiler.
Liberal MPs who spoke to the Star indicated there was increasing pressure on individual MPs to dispel myths and correct the record on the carbon price with constituents.
Half of those Abacus polled who were eligible to receive the climate action incentive payment were asked about their awareness of what the rebate was for. Of those, 52 per cent did not know what it was for, even though 72 per cent reported being aware of it. Nearly half of all respondents said the carbon levy was a big factor for inflation.
"If there is a rebrand, there's an opportunity to explain it better," said one MP, who the Star granted anonymity because they are not authorized to speak on the matter.
While a rebrand could help increase awareness of the rebate, some former Liberal insiders told the Star the government must ensure it is constantly reminding people that the rebates are greater than any cost increases passed onto consumers. Research from the Parliamentary Budget Officer, supported by many independent economists, shows that eight of 10 eligible Canadians receive more money through carbon pricing rebates than they pay.
Catherine McKenna, who first introduced the carbon pricing program as Trudeau's environment and climate change minister in 2018, said the government's announcement in October of an exemption on home-heating oil gave ammunition to Conservatives' claims the carbon price was contributing to affordability issues.
"You can have the best name in the world (for the rebate), but you have to be willing to be out there constantly defending it," she told the Star.
To Greg MacEachern, a long-time Liberal strategist and a former director of communications during the Paul Martin years, the government had lost the plot long before that.
"I don't want to be unkind," he said. "But I think it was very lackadaisical." MacEachern compared the government's approach to the rebate program to the Stephen Harper years, when the government would use giant prop cheques to emphasize how government programs helped people.
"What they were telling us was it was really tough, then, to let Canadians know what you were doing to help them, and their solution was giant props," he said. "And yet, there is now an expectation that Canadians would get direct deposits in their account, and somehow connect it to the price on carbon. That assumes a lot."
"And that's in a neutral territory," he added. "When you have an opponent, who is focusing everything through a very partisan lens, the challenge just rises exponentially."
One way the government can improve its reach to Canadians, MacEachern said, is through increased advertising of the rebate. The Canada Revenue Agency spent just over $2 million in advertising the rebate in the first two years after the price on carbon was introduced, but has not spent any money to advertise it in the three years since, which included the COVID-19 pandemic.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes an announcement that the government will double the carbon price rebate for rural Canadians beginning next April during a news conference in Ottawa on Oct. 26, 2023.

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Climate action; Environmental policy; Carbon; Prime ministers; Climate change

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 156 of 252

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/2919312137/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Help homeless at GO station
I am writing out of concern for the inhabitants of the Hamilton GO station on Hunter Street. It is now quite effectively - or perhaps I should say ineffectively - become housing for the unhoused. There are many people sleeping in the GO station. The problem is that the conditions in which they sleep (and live) are decidedly inhumane. People are slumped over in chairs (this cannot be good for their backs) or they are sleeping on the hard floors. Since this is now where they live, it is our responsibility as caring citizens to provide proper beds for them. There is so much talk these days of housing the unhoused - and yet nothing is being done for these poor people who live in the bus station.
Here is my suggestion. The washrooms have been effectively (or ineffectively) out of service for months because of the presence of the unhoused there. Why not turn the washrooms into safe injection sites? (Instead, people with addiction issues are regularly harassed by the police in the station.) There is quite a large area outside the washrooms, and if the food dispensers could be moved, beds could be set up there. But there is no reason why beds could not be put up in the rest of the GO station, as there is a lot of room. There is rarely any GO staff at the station, and there is no one to help these unfortunates. Why could there not be social workers there, or at least one or two social workers, who could help them with their problems, find them even better housing and speak to their emotional needs and addiction needs?
I respectively suggest that it is our responsibility as good and charitable citizens in Hamilton to help and support the people who are living in the Hamilton GO station on Hunter Street. Something must be done. The conditions that they live in down there are unhuman and inhumane.
Dr. Sky Gilbert, Hamilton
Spend on housing, not VUT
Hamilton council gives vacant unit tax another chance (Jan. 25)
The city is already struggling with its budget and we bring back to the table something no one wants. This will not resolve the homeless issue in any way. "Staff pegged the cost of implementing the tax at $2.6 million and estimated its annual operating expense at $2.2 million." By my calculation, that's a total of $4.8 million. "Revenue in the first year is expected to be between $3.4 million and $4.3 million." So another deficit we taxpayers should again pick up?
If any owner maintains the property and pays the property taxes, I don't think the city needs to stick its nose in. If the council can find $2.6 million to implement this, why can't it find ways to help the homeless?
Christine Chappel, Hamilton
Fund health care properly
Area hospitals using loans, lines of credit to pay for care (Jan. 25)
We keep hearing from our provincial government that we need more private hospital services. OHIP will pay for all services with no out-of-pocket expenses.
So why does a publicly run hospital service borrow money to keep its doors open? Does anyone think a privately run hospital service will borrow money to stay open? The only options the private system has is the government pays more or we the public will be paying out of pocket to keep the private hospital system afloat.
I am demanding, as a citizen of Ontario, that the Doug Ford government pay off these loans and start funding the health-care system to properly serve the citizens of Ontario.
Wayne Hudyma, Hamilton
Don't privatize LRT operation
A contracted operator, not the city, should run Hamilton's LRT: staff report (Jan. 24)
Why on earth would we put the operation in the hands of a private operator when we have a team ready to take control? This is an absurd idea. Keep this in the hands of locals.
Robert Coxe, Hamilton
In favour of bike lanes
Some good points made by other letter writers regarding bike lanes.
As an avid cyclist, I use them at every opportunity, since they are not unduly dangerous and I am much safer than without them. I am also a motorist. I agree Herkimer between Queen and James is bad. Others are fine. I do implore though, more cyclists to use bike lanes and more people to cycle. Maybe we are not hard-wired to commute to work on a bike like Europeans in cities such as Amsterdam and Dresden.
I do not believe we are really that poor that we can't afford bike lanes. Much more is going to be spent for planners, consultants and contractors for a useless LRT when just a few extra buses would suffice.
Steve Penner, Hamilton
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Jan. 29: Help the homeless at GO station, fund public health care properly and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-29-help-homeless-at-go-station-fund-public/docview/2919312090/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 158 of 252

Has Doug Ford gamed Ontario's land-use planning system in favour of developers?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/has-doug-ford-gamed-ontarios-land-use-planning/docview/2919312084/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Has+Doug+Ford+gamed+Ontario%27s+land-use+planning+system+in+favour+of+developers%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has come to be viewed as a gung-ho politician with a pro-developer bias that frequently arouses public controversy and backlash.
Ford demonstrated this bias in 2023 when, in response to Ontario's need for affordable housing, he rushed to introduce housing development in protected Greenbelt land with a land swap plan.
However, the discovery that certain developers with receptive connections to the premier's top-level staff owned 92 per cent of the Greenbelt land chosen for housing development and stood to make billions, led to a scandal that caused Ford to cancel the land swap.
Ford's pro-developer bias is also evident in the fact that, in his first five years as premier, his government issued over 100 minister's zoning orders (MZOs) - in contrast to the sparse 18 MZOs issued during the previous 15 years of Ontario Liberal government rule.
Granted, MZOs - which are tools that fast-track urgent development projects - are sometimes necessary to expedite municipal approval of land development applications, opponents view them as undemocratic instruments because they overrule municipal planning and zoning regulations and can't be appealed at the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT).
But in the interests of exercising equal justice and fair treatment in issuing MZOs, shouldn't the Ford government use an MZO not just in favour of developers but also in favour of municipalities and communities when faced with developers who place their profit margins over environmental protection, municipal zoning and planning rights and legitimate community issues?
Currently, there are two Ontario golf courses that require MZOs to stop opportunistic developers from demolishing them for housing.
The first is Oakville's iconic Glen Abbey golf course, which in 2021 was in jeopardy of being demolished for development purposes at an OLT hearing, but was saved when Ford's housing minister intervened and extracted a "commitment" from owner-developer ClubLink that it would not build on the golf course.
But the lack of detailed transparency about this commitment raises questions about whether it is legally binding and permanent, or simply a stopgap measure that would enable the developer to resubmit its development application to the OLT. An MZO issued now would ensure Glen Abbey's golf course permanent protection from demolition.
The second is Burlington's Millcroft golf course that owner-developer Millcroft Greens wants to infill with housing, beginning with "Phase 1" - which implicitly signals the developer's intent to demolish the whole golf course, one phase at a time. This green space is in jeopardy of being demolished for housing at an OLT hearing set to begin on March 5.
Millcroft Greens' intent has incited vehement municipal, regional and community opposition because it is viewed as a bad development that will irreversibly decrease precious urban green space that bestows many recreational, environmental, ecological and biodiverse benefits, promoting mental and physical health.
Additionally, Burlington's official plan has consistently zoned the golf course as permanent green space to absorb stormwater and mitigate flooding. Any demolition of the golf course to build housing will not only aggravate flood risk that will raise house insurance costs, but will also claustrophobically densify the neighbourhood with homes not affordable by the average person.
This OLT hearing is also pivotal because if the developer wins, it will set a precedent that will allow developers to demolish any golf courses, open spaces and parks that they own or can buy - including Burlington's La Salle Park, which the City of Hamilton owns and may sell to the highest bidder.
An MZO issued before the OLT hearing would ensure the Millcroft golf course's permanent protection from demolition. But will the Ford government issue an MZO, or will it leave this golf course's fate to the OLT?
Unfortunately, the OLT is perceived as a disreputable tribunal lacking independence, impartiality and fairness because it is tethered to Ford's pro-development policies. As former Hamilton Spectator reporter Steve Buist evidenced in his article published on Sept. 20, 2022, the OLT's decisions favour developers 97 per cent of the time.
This negative perception of the OLT is also reinforced by Ford's More Homes Built Faster Act 2022 (known as Bill 23), which contains extensive pro-development changes to previous legislation.
If passed, Bill 23 will empower the OLT to penalize the losing party of an adjudication by having it pay costs to the winning party. This empowerment will give developers with deep pockets an unfair and deterring financial advantage over cash-strapped municipalities or communities who oppose them.
So, connecting all the dots of Ford's actions above, is Ontario's land-use planning system gamed in favour of developers or not?
CAPTION: Millcroft golf course lands bring recreational, environmental, ecological and biodiverse benefits to the community. These are in danger of being lost, Tony Lo Presti writes.
CREDIT: Tony Lo Presti

Subject: Demolition; Land use planning; Housing developments; Zoning; Empowerment

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing developments

Title: Has Doug Ford gamed Ontario's land-use planning system in favour of developers?

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication date: Jan 29, 2024

Document 159 of 252

Will Ford step in to save golf course lands?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-ford-step-save-golf-course-lands/docview/2919311811/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Ford+step+in+to+save+golf+course+lands%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Additionally, Burlington's official plan has consistently zoned the golf course as permanent green space to absorb stormwater and mitigate flooding.
Any demolition of the golf course to build housing will not only aggravate flood risk that will raise house insurance costs, but will also claustrophobically densify the neighbourhood with homes not affordable by the average person.
This OLT hearing is also pivotal because if the developer wins, it will set a precedent that will allow developers to demolish any golf courses, open spaces and parks that they own or can buy - including Burlington's LaSalle Park, which the City of Hamilton owns and may sell to the highest bidder.
An MZO issued before the OLT hearing would ensure the Millcroft golf course's permanent protection from demolition. But will the Ford government issue an MZO, or will it leave this golf course's fate to the OLT?
Unfortunately, the OLT is perceived as a disreputable tribunal lacking independence, impartiality and fairness because it is tethered to Ford's pro-development policies. As former Hamilton Spectator reporter Steve Buist evidenced in his article published on Sept. 20, 2022, the OLT's decisions favour developers 97 per cent of the time.
This negative perception of the OLT is also reinforced by Ford's More Homes Built Faster Act 2022 (known as Bill 23), which contains extensive pro-development changes to previous legislation.
If passed, Bill 23 will empower the OLT to penalize the losing party of an adjudication by having it pay costs to the winning party. This empowerment will give developers with deep pockets an unfair and deterring financial advantage over cash-strapped municipalities or communities who oppose them.
So, connecting all the dots of Ford's actions above, is Ontario's land-use planning system gamed in favour of developers or not?
Tony Lo Presti is a retired educator and a Millcroft resident.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Tony Lo Presti Tony Lo Presti is a retired educator and a Millcroft resident.;

Former Toronto councillor Karen Stintz hoping to run for Poilievre's Conservatives in return to politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-toronto-councillor-karen-stintz-hoping-run/docview/2919501992/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Toronto+councillor+Karen+Stintz+hoping+to+run+for+Poilievre%27s+Conservatives+in+return+to+politics&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Karen Stintz, the former city councillor and TTC chair, is seeking the Conservative nomination in Eglinton-Lawrence, hoping to help party leader Pierre Poilievre break the federal Liberals' stranglehold on Toronto seats.
Stintz confirmed in an interview that she will announce her bid for the nomination Monday.
"I thought I was out of politics, I thought I was done, but when Pierre got elected (leader), he inspired me," Stintz said in a midtown coffee shop. "I think he's an incredible communicator and I think he's able to give voice to the frustrations that people were feeling but couldn't articulate."
Stintz was elected Eglinton-Lawrence city councillor in 2003 after answering a candidate recruitment ad placed by a ratepayers' group. She served 11 years including four as TTC chair.
She ran for mayor in 2014 but dropped out and left politics before the election that saw John Tory start his first term.
Stintz has spent the past eight years as chief executive of Variety Village but is leaving the recreation centre for children with disabilities as soon as its board hires a replacement for her.
A card-carrying Conservative since Brian Mulroney's party leadership in the 1980s, Stintz calls Marco Mendicino, Eglinton-Lawrence's Liberal MP, "a great guy, he's done a great job. I just think he's in the wrong party."
She accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his Liberals, whose domination of Toronto helped them cling to minority government status in the last election, of failing midtown and the rest of the city.
"There is a profound sense of letdown in this community around how the government has responded to to community needs, the Jewish community in particular," she said, over Trudeau's response to the Israel-Hamas conflict. "It also has to be said the federal government really hasn't given back to Toronto what Toronto has given to it.
"That the mayor (Olivia Chow) has to fight to get money for refugees, which is clearly a federal priority ... I definitely think there is an opportunity for Toronto to make a statement to the current government about how it's not benefiting from its leadership."
Chow's city hall has been at odds with the Toronto Liberal caucus over the federal government's refusal, so far, to pay $250 million in forecast costs to shelter refugee claimants in the city this year. Chow's budget chief Coun. Shelley Carroll has floated the idea of tacking an extra six per cent on to Torontonians' property tax bill, if the refugee funding doesn't flow, and calling it the "federal impacts levy."
Stintz acknowledged that, if she wins the nomination, it won't be easy to convince some voters that Conservatives are the answer to their problems.
The party's power base is Western Canada with little declared love for Toronto, and Polievre's combative conservatism could be a tough sell.
After city staff's proposed budget was unveiled Jan. 10, Poilievre posted on X, formerly Twitter: "Incompetent NDP/Liberal city politicians raise Toronto's taxes 10.5 per cent. Just like Trudeau. Hands in your pocket. Not worth the cost. Common sense Conservatives will cut your taxes." Mayor Chow and city council will set the budget and tax hike on Feb. 14.
Days later the Conservative leader wrote on X: "Incompetent Toronto city politicians - who get big tax money from Trudeau - raise fees on homebuilding." He included a 2022 chart of charges that doesn't reflect later Ontario government legislation that cut, froze and exempted various fees that municipalities, including Toronto, can charge homebuilders.
Stintz said she found Poilievre to be focused and intelligent at a Toronto reception and hopes to get a chance to try to win over voters, including those who have their doubts. "I look forward to working with (Poilievre) and I look forward to changing that perception of him."
CAPTION: Former city councillor Karen Stintz says she hopes to become the Conservative candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence and help Pierre Poilievre's party break into a city long dominated by the ruling Liberals.

Document 161 of 252

10,000 Ontario patients cut loose because of staffing shortages

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/10-000-ontario-patients-cut-loose-because/docview/2919137565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=10%2C000+Ontario+patients+cut+loose+because+of+staffing+shortages&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's growing shortage of doctors and nurse practitioners is hitting full force in Sault Ste. Marie, where a major medical centre is reluctantly cutting 10,000 patients loose at the end of May.
After months of approaches to the Ministry of Health for help, the Group Health Centre issued an open letter to the community warning patients whose primary-care providers have "recently retired or left their practices" will have to seek treatment elsewhere.
"We recognize the profound effect this will have," says the letter from the centre, which will continue to serve 50,000 patients and has been unable to recruit enough replacement medical staff in recent years.
"This has led us to the critical point we find ourselves at today."
Dr. Jodie Stewart, chief executive officer of the Algoma District Medical Group of doctors at the centre's eight locations, said the dramatic move is a last resort in the northern Ontario city of 73,000.
"We know what happens when people don't have good primary care. It's essential to a healthy community," she told the Star.
The centre, founded in 1963 and long hailed as an innovative model for offering a wide range of health-care services and professionals, has 31 doctors left, including some who have come out of retirement.
While Stewart said the clinic has submitted proposals to the Ministry of Health since last spring - seeking funding for incentive payments, and retention and recruiting of doctors and nurse practitioners - the office of Health Minister Sylvia Jones said it was only recently informed of the plan to chop patients from the roster.
"Group Health Care notified the ministry of these changes just over a week ago," said spokesperson Hannah Jensen.
"While the ministry has made several attempts to work with GHC on a possible solution, finding an immediate replacement for these patients on such short notice was not achievable."
Jensen added the government is about to launch the largest expansion of primary-care teams in the province's history, "including two new sites in Sault Ste. Marie" with details coming "soon."
"We will continue to work with partners in Sault Ste. Marie, and across the province, to ensure primary care is available to people where and when they need it."
The primary-care teams are intended to support doctors with other medical and social service professionals providing comprehensive services to patients.
Ontario Medical Association president Dr. Andrew Park, an emergency physician in London, said it was only a matter of time before the growing shortage of doctors hit hard in a big way.
"Ten thousand in one day is spectacular for all the wrong reasons," he told the Star, describing the situation in Sault Ste. Marie as "unprecedented."
"But it's going to become more common."
About 2.3 million Ontarians in a province of 15 million do not have access to a primary-care physician, with two in five people on track to be without a family doctor in the next two years, the OMA says.
Forty per cent of family doctors say they are considering retirement in the next five years, according to an OMA survey of its members.
Park said doctors are doing an average of 19 hours a week of administrative tasks such as filling out insurance forms and other paperwork, which is a "nonsensical" disincentive to stay in the field.
The situation in Sault Ste. Marie is more proof the health-care system is broken and in need of "urgent action" from Premier Doug Ford, said Liberal MPP and health critic Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician who has worked in remote and northern communities.
"Rural health care is in crisis with rampant ER (emergency room) closures, staffing shortages and no access to primary care," he said, noting stranded patients have limited options.
"When a clinic like the one in Sault Ste. Marie says 'we need help and funding to keep our doors open,' the government needs to say 'yes.'"
In its open letter, the Group Health Centre further outlined its predicament.
"The rate of new (health-care) providers entering the field has not kept pace with retirements, and many existing primary-care providers face burnout," officials wrote.
"Despite many proactive efforts, including extensive recruitment initiatives (locally and internationally), securing temporary providers, delaying retirements and re-engaging retired professionals, this situation is no longer sustainable."
"Our immediate focus is to stabilize our operations and continue to provide care to 50,000 patients in our community. We look forward to working with our community partners and the Ministry of Health to build additional capacity for the future."
Sault Ste. Marie Mayor Matthew Shoemaker said on social media the news from the clinic is a discouraging "step back" for the community and called for more provincial support.
CAPTION: Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones makes an announcement on health care with Premier Doug Ford in Toronto on Jan. 16, 2023.

Document 162 of 252

Doug Ford's Tories are ahead, but ominous signs are on the horizon, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-are-ahead-ominous-signs-on/docview/2919137472/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+are+ahead%2C+but+ominous+signs+are+on+the+horizon%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives appear to be holding their own, but there are some potentially ominous signs for the party, a new poll suggests.
The latest Abacus Data tracking poll found the Tories at 38 per cent with Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 27 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 23 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at five per cent.
While the January findings are virtually the same as the December results, trouble may be lurking for the Tories when undecided voters are factored in, said Abacus president David Coletto.
When asked for their "current provincial vote intention," 29 per cent of respondents said PC, 21 per cent Liberal, 18 per cent NDP, four per cent Green, five per cent another party while 23 per cent were undecided.
"That undecided vote is high," said Coletto, noting in his January federal poll only 15 per cent of respondents were undecided.
"Far more of those (provincial) undecideds say they voted PC last time than for the other parties," he said.
Indeed, 22 per cent of undecided voters said they cast Tory ballots in the 2022 election with 14 per cent voting Liberal, eight per cent NDP, two per cent Green, one per cent another party and 53 per cent either couldn't remember or didn't vote.
"That 22 per cent (of former Tory voters now undecided) represents five per cent of eligible voters," said Coletto.
The pollster said that means the race could dramatically tighten if those undecided voters "were to migrate to the Liberals or the New Democrats."
Using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform, Abacus surveyed 995 Ontarians Jan. 18 to 23. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Fewer than one-third - 30 per cent - had a positive impression of Ford while almost half - 49 per cent - had a negative impression with 19 per cent neutral and 23 per cent didn't know enough to say.
That's a net impression rating for the premier of minus 19 per cent.
In contrast, the NDP's Stiles had a 26 per cent positive, 22 per cent negative, 29 per cent neutral and 23 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus four per cent.
Similarly, the Liberals' Crombie was 28 per cent positive, 25 per cent negative, 26 per cent neutral and 22 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus three per cent.
For the Greens' Schreiner, it was 19 per cent positive, 16 per cent negative, 40 per cent neutral and 25 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus three per cent.
Coletto said that indicates the multimillion-dollar advertising blitz the Tories launched against Crombie after she won the Dec. 2 Liberal leadership has so far not resonated with voters.
"It doesn't appear that the attack ads have yet had any impact on her brand," he said.
In a bid to negatively define Crombie, the Tories unleashed TV, radio and online ads criticizing the former Mississauga mayor after she took the Liberal helm.
That's because Ford's party fears she could pose an existential threat to their stronghold in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area over the past two elections.
Breaking down voting intentions in GTHA ridings, Coletto pointed out the Tories were at 37 per cent compared to 32 per cent for the Liberals, 23 per cent for the NDP, five per cent for the Greens and three per cent for other parties.
"It's worth noting that that's where the gap seems to have closed," he said, adding the surprise departure Thursday of former minister of red tape reduction Parm Gill in Milton will not help the provincial Tories in the 905.
Gill resigned in order to join federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's surging team for a national election widely expected next year.
Crombie, who does not have a seat in the legislature, has yet to decide if she will run in a Milton byelection that Ford must call within six months.
The Liberals believe there could be vacancies in Mississauga or Brampton if other ambitious Tory MPPs abandon Ford for Poilievre.
Coletto stressed the Tories, even after the Greenbelt land swap scandal, "are still in a strong position, though there is evidence of vulnerabilities."
In the November Abacus poll - before Crombie won the Grit leadership - the PCs were at 42 per cent, the NDP at 24 per cent, the Liberals at 23 per cent and the Greens at seven per cent.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford gives remarks during a media preview of the Bombardier Aircraft Assembly Centre in Mississauga, Ont., Jan. 22, 2024.

Document 163 of 252

There's much more to the Emergencies Act ruling than a rebuke of Justin Trudeau's government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-much-more-emergencies-act-ruling-than/docview/2918765762/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There%27s+much+more+to+the+Emergencies+Act+ruling+than+a+rebuke+of+Justin+Trudeau%27s+government&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's being framed as one of the most severe rebukes the federal government has received to date: a Federal Court ruling on its use of the Emergencies Act two years ago to end the trucker convoy that occupied the capital for three weeks and saw Canada-U.S. border blockades across the country.
The decision is ammunition for Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who can now say Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "broke the highest law in the land" and "directly violated Canadians' most essential right to freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression."
The Liberal government escaped harsh criticism last year from Court of Appeal Justice Paul Rouleau, who headed the Public Order Emergency Commission tasked with reviewing the law's use. Now it has to contend with a judgment that says the use of the powerful law may have been justified in the eyes of many, but it wasn't legal. The government, unsurprisingly, plans to appeal.
But Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley's decision this week isn't a sweeping indictment. Rather, it's a thoughtful judgment that points out the failings in the law, and finds that on several fronts the government acted reasonably.
Here are some takeaways:
1. The Emergencies Act needs to be updated. For a public order emergency to be declared, the law states that there must be a "threat to the security of Canada" as defined by the Canadian Security Intelligence Services Act, which refers to threats or acts of "serious violence against persons or property." Mosley writes that there are "other valid reasons for declaring an emergency," such as the type of economic disruptions that resulted from the border crossing blockades. However, "the court cannot rewrite the statute and has to take the definition as it reads." If there was a broader and more flexible interpretation of the words "threats to the security of Canada," the judge says, "I would have found that the threshold was satisfied."
2. More public scrutiny should be directed at the Ontario government. For the federal government to lawfully invoke the Emergencies Act, the national emergency needed "to exceed the capacity or authority of a province to deal with it." But Mosley casts doubt on whether that was really the case. He disputes the notion that the emergency was national in scope - something the federal Liberals insist was the case. But his observation raises another question: what if a province doesn't want to respond to the threat at its doorstep?
Mosley notes that Alberta and Quebec were able to adequately respond to the blockades, so why wasn't Ontario? It was only in Ottawa that the local police were unable to deal with the protest, he writes. And it "was only true in Ontario" that the situation exceeded the capacity of a province to deal with the situation, and "that was in part due to the inability of the provincial and municipal authorities to compel tow-truck drivers to assist in the removal of the blockading vehicles," he notes. "It is not clear why that could not have been dealt with under the provincial legislation."
Was the Trudeau government forced to act because Premier Doug Ford wouldn't? And how should any new rewriting of the law account for such a scenario?
3. A noteworthy standard for provincial consultations: Mosley found that a hastily assembled First Ministers meeting, where premiers disagreed on the need for using the Emergencies Act and most felt the law wasn't required in their province, satisfied the requirement for consultation under the act. That's bound to make a few premiers unhappy.
4. More thought is required to ensure regulations apply only to those they target. The judge ruled that some of the regulations were too broad, namely the freezing of bank accounts, which applied nationwide, lacked an objective standard or an appeal mechanism, and affected joint-account holders not involved in the protests. While he also found that protesters who wanted to go to Parliament Hill and peacefully protest had their freedom of expression infringed, he disagreed that the temporary measures breached other Charter rights, such as the freedom of peaceful assembly, freedom of association, or life, liberty and the security of the person.
Mosley's conclusion should be appealed. Residents in Ottawa, for example, will be outraged if the decision stands and taxpayers are on the hook to compensate the occupiers who had their bank accounts frozen. But parts of his ruling make a lot of sense and MPs should pay heed. The next national security incident may not reflect what legislators in the 1980s planned for, and no government should be forced to respond in ways that may be in the public interest but are, in fact, illegal.
No party seems to want to engage on this front - not the Conservatives or the Bloc Québécois, who didn't support the use of the Emergencies Act, nor the New Democrats, who reluctantly went along, nor even the Liberals, who don't want to detract from their affordability agenda. But the Emergencies Act needs updating, regardless of what happens on appeal.
CAPTION: Police clear "Freedom Convoy" protesters from Wellington Street in front of Parliament Hill on Feb. 19, 2022.

Subject: Bank accounts; Emergencies; Demonstrations & protests

Business indexing term: Subject: Bank accounts

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: There's much more to the Emergencies Act ruling than a rebuke of Justin Trudeau's government

Document 164 of 252

Pitches made to trim Hamilton's 7.9 per cent tax hike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pitches-made-trim-hamiltons-7-9-per-cent-tax-hike/docview/2918765760/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pitches+made+to+trim+Hamilton%27s+7.9+per+cent+tax+hike&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Councillors are making pitches to ease the hit to Hamilton taxpayers as they aim to reduce a potential 7.9 per cent tax hike while maintaining services in the city's 2024 budget.
To that end, city politicians have endorsed a call by Coun. Ted McMeekin to set this year's "municipally generated tax cap" at no more than four per cent.
The overall increase of 7.9 per cent - in a gross $2.4-billion combined operating and capital budget - includes other drivers like provincially tied expenses.
Like his colleagues, he has heard from constituents who are concerned about rising taxes amid other escalating costs such as inflation, McMeekin said Friday.
"Several councillors" say they want to see a municipal cap of below four per cent, he noted.
"But for the moment, I think we need to show the initiative, show our taxpayers that we get it, that we're listening," McMeekin said.
"And things are tough out there, and we want to do what we can to get through this together."
The 7.9 per cent increase represents $382 more for an "average" residential property assessed at $385,000, according to the latest provincial valuation in 2016.
Last year, in their first spending plan, the new council landed at a 5.8 per cent increase.
This budget season, city staff and politicians have made a point of parsing out which levels of government contribute to the proposed hike in local property taxes.
This is how officials have broken the 7.9 per cent increase down:
2.7 per cent is attributed to municipal services; 1.6 per cent is for housing and homelessness initiatives; 2.6 per cent springs from provincial legislation that lowers the city's revenue from development charges; 1.0 per cent "placeholder" is for local hospital development projects.
Cutting the 4.3 per cent (the sum of the 2.7 per cent and 1.6 per cent) down to the four per cent target would require shaving about $4 million from the budget.
City politicians suggested that's achievable given a series of proposed measures they're floating to lighten the load on residents this year.
That's to be commended, said Mayor Andrea Horwath, noting councillors "pulled up their sleeves" to find solutions.
"So I'm really confident that we will achieve what Councillor McMeekin has set out and perhaps even further in terms of a target."
Those motions, which are to be hashed out on Tuesday, include:
Coun. Tammy Hwang's pitch to shave about $3.3 million from an enhanced litter-collection program; Coun. Cameron Kroetsch's goal to send the police's nearly $20-million, 10.24 per cent increase back to its governing board for additional review; Coun. Nrinder Nann's request of staff to examine potential savings through "co-ordinated and staggered" implementation of a new multimillion-dollar suite of programs meant to protect tenants; Coun. Mike Spadafora's call to ditch the one per cent "placeholder," which is worth about $12 million, in response to a $462-million request toward local hospital redevelopment projects; Coun. Craig Cassar's suggestion for a longer phase-in of a funding strategy to replenish revenues lost to development-charge exemptions.
With the four per cent "municipally generated tax cap," it's important to be "crystal clear" that residents will still see a tax bill over and above that - perhaps around 6.0 to 6.5 per cent - due to the additional provincial pressures, Coun. Tom Jackson emphasized.
The current 7.9 per cent increase is down from a fall forecast of 14.2 per cent, which staff say they tamed through a series of measures including "efficiency savings," phased-in costs and a $216-millon draw on reserves over four years.
On Friday, councillors expressed support for efforts to reduce the immediate tax hike but expressed concern about the potential hit to taxpayers in the years to come if expenses continue to mount and reserves are diminished.
"I'm worried about that," Coun. Brad Clark said.
Meanwhile, insufficient provincial funding and wrong-headed policies like scaled-back rent control are unfairly leaving municipalities to pick up the slack amid the housing and homelessness crisis, Clark added.
"We're doing this because the city is compassionate," he said of the city's response. "It's not our obligation and responsibility. That is the provincial government's responsibility."
Likewise, Coun. Alex Wilson, who worried about the city "borrowing from the future and not putting the money" into immediate infrastructure needs, echoed Clark's frustration with Premier Doug Ford's government.
Municipalities are "creatures of the province" with limited powers, Wilson said. "And if the province is going to drive up cost, all we can do is be honest about that."
On that note, Coun. John-Paul Danko has placed a motion on Tuesday's agenda calling for the "financial impact of provincial polices and downloaded expenses" to be reflected in a specific line item on 2024 tax bills.
On Friday, the planning and economic department as well as public works gave overviews of their spending plans, following earlier presentations from corporate services, emergency and community services, and the city manager.
The library board, police and conservation authorities have made their pitches, while the general public has offered feedback on the city's overall proposed budget, as well.
Following Tuesday's deliberations, council aims to stick with a condensed budget schedule that has them sign off on the final spending plan on Feb. 15.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Mayor Andrea Horwath has credited councillors for finding creative ways to reduce this year's tax hike.Coun. Brad Clark says he's concerned about future hits to residents if costs keep rising and reserves are diminished.Coun. Alex Wilson argues it's important to make clear to residents the province's roll in their escalating local tax bill.Coun. Ted McMeekin won favour for a proposal to set the"municipally generated tax cap" at no more than four per cent.

Document 165 of 252

Jagmeet Singh has a problem: 'If the NDP are supporting the Liberals, I can't support the NDP'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jagmeet-singh-has-problem-if-ndp-are-supporting/docview/2918765674/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jagmeet+Singh+has+a+problem%3A+%27If+the+NDP+are+supporting+the+Liberals%2C+I+can%27t+support+the+NDP%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: EDMONTON-On the surface, it was a dream canvassing scenario.
As the NDP's Jagmeet Singh and a group of MPs and supporters knocked on doors in the highly sought-after riding of Edmonton Centre, a middle-aged man and his young adult son spotted the leader from afar and hurried across a strip mall parking lot to say hello.
The man, who preferred only to be identified by his first name, Craig, spoke to Singh and the NDP's candidate in the area about his lifelong ties to the New Democrats. His son, meanwhile, asked Singh the most-asked question that came up on doorsteps: what could the NDP leader do for people fed up with trying to find a place to live?
"I hear you, OK?" Singh said. "Housing. We're working on it."
The foursome enthusiastically snapped a photo before parting ways in the biting cold.
But as Singh turned the corner and walked out of sight, Craig had a confession to make.
"Unfortunately, I just think the country, in the state it's in right now, means I can't support the Liberals," he told the Star.
"And if the NDP are supporting the Liberals, I can't support the NDP either."
In many ways, Craig, a local tradesman who has long admired the NDP's championing of the working class, is exactly the sort of voter the New Democrats were hoping to encounter when they chose to hold their winter caucus gathering in Edmonton this week. Growing up in Ontario, Craig's family would sometimes host notable New Democrats, like former MP Svend Robinson, when they passed through town. When it was time for him to cast his first ballot, his vote went to them.
In a province like Alberta, though, any connection to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is a curse. After the NDP struck a governing deal with him, Craig's faith faltered. And while he hasn't written Singh off completely, he's willing to turn to Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives to ease the country's burdens.
The question of how to position the NDP in such a political landscape arose at this week's retreat, where Singh and party insiders hashed out their political priorities before Parliament's return.
"We know people need us because our job has always been to make Ottawa work for people," Singh said at the gathering's close.
"We have shown that we can use our power to get people help."
When MPs and the party's top brass weren't stuck behind closed doors, they were trekking across Edmonton to chat with prospective voters and meet with housing experts, union groups, health-care workers and First Nations leaders. It's part of the NDP's nationwide strategy to boost its seat count by planting itself as the only answer to the much-maligned Liberals and increasingly popular Conservatives.
In Edmonton - where the Liberals hold one seat, the NDP two, and the Conservatives six - the New Democrats see a perfect opening.
"I think there's a lot that's unique about Alberta, and Edmonton in particular, right now," said Anne McGrath, who recently stepped into the new role of Singh's principal secretary.
McGrath, previously the party's national director, moved into the position so she could play a larger role in advising Singh and overseeing the party's governing agreement with the minority Liberals. The supply-and-confidence agreement, as it's known, could see the NDP prop up Trudeau's government until mid-2025 in exchange for action on progressive policies, such as national dental care and pharmacare programs.
Serving as principal secretary is also a job title she held when she worked with Alberta NDP Leader Rachel Notley, when Notley ended more than four decades of Progressive Conservative rule in 2015 by becoming the first New Democratic premier of the province.
"I do think it's very significant that every single seat in Edmonton, with no exception, is an NDP seat provincially," McGrath said of the Alberta NDP's 2023 showing, which failed to return Notley to power but saw her party take in its highest-ever share of the vote.
Indeed, Notley's successes in Edmonton have Singh's NDP casting an optimistic eye to a city that historically tends to veer blue in federal races.
"We're seeing the momentum change and we're seeing the opportunity to build in Edmonton," said Edmonton Strathcona MP Heather McPherson.
"Even in the last election, the number of people who chose to vote for New Democrats maybe only went to two seats, but the number of people - the percentage of that vote - just increased."
Edmonton Griesbach's Blake Desjarlais, the province's other NDP MP, succeeded in wresting the riding from the Conservatives in 2021. He credits the Alberta NDP both with sparking his interest in politics and with building the networks he needed to win.
Part of that network included Notley, who campaigned with Desjarlais door to door.
McGrath said she's "positive" Notley will similarly help NDP candidates during the next federal election, though Notley, who announced last week she intends to end her historic reign as Alberta NDP leader, said Wednesday she hadn't given much thought as to how she might get involved.
Despite her influence in Alberta's New Democratic circles, her impending exit likely won't affect the NDP's fortunes in a province that has a less than favourable view of the federal party, said Duane Bratt, a political science professor at Calgary's Mount Royal University.
Notley - who is adamant she will not join the federal fold - has had frosty relations with the federal party at times over differing positions on climate and energy policies. During the province's election campaign last year, Premier Danielle Smith revelled in falsely painting Notley as a leader controlled by Trudeau and Singh in an attempt to discredit her chief rival. And in late 2023, a former Alberta NDP cabinet minister launched a call for the provincial party to change its name, arguing that too many people believe - albeit incorrectly - that Singh has high levels of influence over Notley.
Those tensions are reflected in the numbers, said Abacus Data CEO David Coletto.
"Provincewide, 28 per cent of Albertans have a positive view of Jagmeet Singh. Forty-six per cent have a negative view. His negatives are higher than in any other region or province, and his positives are lower," said Coletto, who drew his findings from online surveys conducted from Oct. 23, 2023 to Jan. 9, 2024 with a representative sample of 990 Albertan adults. (A comparable margin of error of a random survey with a sample of that size would be 3.2 per cent, 19 times out of 20.)
"If you look at that and you're holding your caucus retreat in Alberta, it's the place where he's the least popular right now."
Sometimes, that's not immediately obvious.
In Edmonton Centre, a Liberal riding that has previously gone to the Conservatives but never the NDP, Singh was approached in the streets by locals eager to congratulate him on the birth of his second daughter, and he drew more than a hundred supporters to a passionate community centre town hall.
The New Democrats have high hopes that their next candidate, former Edmonton Public School Board chair Trisha Estabrooks, will turn that energy into an electoral gain.
In exclusive seat projection data shared with the Star, Abacus's modelling shows that if an election were held now, the New Democrats would hold onto McPherson's seat in Edmonton Strathcona, return Edmonton Griesbach to the Conservatives, and put up a competitive fight in Edmonton Centre - but ultimately lose it to Poilievre.
(The projections, which came from data collected from October to December 2023, are based on modelling using Abacus Data's multi-level regression and poststratification (MRP) methodology.)
"I'm not sure how the NDP's policies are any different than the Liberals because they are operating in an agreement as a coalition," said James Cumming, a Conservative who has faced off against the Liberal's Randy Boissonnault in Edmonton Centre three times, won once, and is seeking the nomination again.
Those aren't just Conservative talking points.
Estabrooks said she hears much of the same from potential voters, prompting her to refer to the Liberal-NDP deal as "an uncomfortable alliance" when she knocks on doors.
"I say, give it time. Look at the gains that the NDP have made because of this agreement ... it's not going to last forever," Estabrooks told the Star, though the NDP has not yet decided whether it will renew the agreement should Trudeau form another minority government.
"I find it uncomfortable because there's so much that I disagree with with the current direction of the Liberal government. And, in politics, sometimes the nuances are lost and I hear that on the doorstep sometimes: 'Oh, you've hitched your wagon to the Liberals.' There's great disdain for that," Estabrooks said.
Many in the party say they just need more time for programs launched under the deal to expand - like dental care - and for other policy proposals, like a pharmacare framework, to materialize.
"I believe that as some of the benefits start to be more and more apparent, that there will be more understanding of the fact that you can extract things from a Liberal government without being tied to that Liberal government," McGrath said of the NDP's central challenge.
"You know, that's not the easiest thing to communicate."
But if the NDP wants to appeal to voters like Craig, the local tradesman who is exhausted by Trudeau's leadership and just wishes his son could afford a place to rent, they're going to have to convey that message as powerfully as they can.
"I know it's hard for the NDP to have a voice in Parliament ... they need to make a way of getting their voice heard," he said.
"But at the same time, I just don't agree with it."
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and NDP candidate for Edmonton Centre, Trisha Estabrooks, meet with a supporter on the street while kicking off the NDP caucus retreat by knocking on doors in Edmonton on Jan. 22, 2024.

Jan. 27: Premier should've acted on 'Freedom Convoy' protests, put spending to a vote and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-27-premier-shouldve-acted-on-freedom-convoy/docview/2918765642/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+27%3A+Premier+should%27ve+acted+on+%27Freedom+Convoy%27+protests%2C+put+spending+to+a+vote+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier should've acted
Re: Use of Emergencies Act was unreasonable (Jan. 24)
Maybe if Premier Doug Ford had shown leadership and moved quickly to help Ottawa deal with the occupation, the feds would not have had to step in. Instead, he disappeared. It's been the same with housing, post-secondary funding, health care - name an issue. Ford is nowhere to be found when there are tough decisions to be made.
Carol Town, Hamilton
What you can buy for $500K
The answer is a public washroom or a restaurant. The correct question is, what can you get for approximately $500,000 in Hamilton? "Firehouse Subs said it costs between $400,000 and $500,000 to build and open one of its restaurants" (Jan. 24). A privately owned restaurant with at least two washrooms can be built for the cost of one public washroom. Satirist and journalist P.J. O'Rourke said it best about government spending, "Enormous effort and elaborate planning are required to waste this much money."
Mark Melanson, Hamilton
Place blame where it belongs
We are all gnashing our teeth toward the wrong set of politicians.
One major portion of the proposed tax increase is the provincial download of infrastructure that previously was paid for by developers. This is the servicing of new areas with water and sewer plus the building of infrastructure for police and recreational use such as community centres.
The next is again a provincially mandated number in the police budget where that budget is a non-debatable amount although it is a take it or leave it amount. That budget is almost all personnel costs so there's not much to do there.
The final one is again provincial which are school budgets. In the past we had more control over this part, but it's now all into one provincial pocket from which the amounts are distributed but the province has the say on how much that increases. So again no input from the city or even the school board.
The city is only trying to increase the property tax that they have any control over - 2.9% is a very reasonable amount considering the inflation pressures. They are trying to trim that as we speak but when your trimming what is the smallest part of the whole pie, it will not mean much.
Go after the provincial government for increasingly taking over our property tax, but not allowing us any control locally.
Ilpo Lehto, Hamilton
LRT report is an insult to HSR
Re: A contracted operator, not the city, should run Hamilton's LRT: staff report (Jan. 24)
So Hamilton's LRT will be operated and maintained by an outside contractor while "customer interface" activities like customer service, security and fare enforcement will be handled by the city. If the city feels it needs to retain the jobs of policing the subcontracted operation, how can it expect taxpayers to put their trust in such a subcontracted operation? This decision is an insult to HSR workers and management as well as another misuse of taxpayers' money.
Jim Young, Burlington
Put spending to a vote
Article after article in the paper has a theme going on; spending money, taking money, needing money. There's an old saying, "You can't get blood from a stone." Our governing body needs to take a long hard look at how our tax dollars are being used. Maybe it's time for a referendum. Ask the taxpayers their opinions on how their tax dollars should be spent. Do we need LRT? Do we need more bike lanes? Do we need a new washroom at Woodland Park? Do we need outside consultants to report on every issue that might be handled "in-house"? The list goes on and on and on. An election year is too far away for changing out our elected officials. Lets have a referendum now!
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Some fatherly advice
My dad always told me not to criticize unless you can offer an alternative solution. So, I suggest creating the project management group responsible for building the $60-million bicycle path to be the same team quarterbacking the LRT. It will never see the light of day.
Terry Johnson, Burlington
Bus barn too expensive
Re: Hamilton calls for funding help with nearly $400-million bus barn (Jan. 24)
Four hundred million dollars for a bus barn. Sounds more like a bus palace to me.
Keith Lankester, Ancaster
Dyer being overly simplistic
Re: Missiles a poor way of sending messages to your enemies (Jan. 22)
I have to say, either Gwynne Dyer is wearing rose-coloured glasses or he's naive as to his opinions on how the military strikes in the Middle East should be addressed. He trivializes how countries use missile attacks as "an expression of displeasure."
I believe it's about far more serious issues than displeasure. It is beyond simplistic to suggest "traditional diplomacy" would work. We are talking about terrorist groups who have no issues with killing their own people and no respect for human life or rights. How do you have diplomatic discussions with evil?
Henrietta Veldman, Stoney Creek
The issue of political silence
Re: Silencing Trump is a bad idea (Jan. 24)
Two opinion pieces back-to-back - Jan. 23 and Jan. 24 - in the The Spec by Henry Giroux and Andrew Phillips, respectively, focus on the issue of political silence. The former and latter writers' opinion pieces write in counterpoint themes. One writer implies the real danger of remaining politically silent as was pre-emptively countered by Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. speeches that rightfully were needed to bring about political change. The other writer implies that the danger of silencing the bombast of Donald Trump would conceal the political agenda of Trump that the writer portends to foresee through the lens of political bias.
Phillips then continues with a summation: "A Trump victory in November certainly isn't inevitable. For that to happen, Americans must keep their eyes and their ears wide open. The news media must do its job, and right now that means letting voters hear what Trump has to say and trusting them to do the right thing." All unbiasedly said in the true spirit of democracy with which I agree.
Mike Pavlov, Hamilton
CAPTION: Smoke bombs disperse "Freedom Convoy" protesters in 2022 as police clear Wellington Street in front of Parliament. A judge has ruled it was unreasonable for the Liberal government to use the Emergencies Act to quell the protests in the national capital and at key border points two years ago.
