Document 167 of 252

Pitches made to trim 7.9% tax hike: Horwath praises councillors for suggesting potential budget solutions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pitches-made-trim-7-9-tax-hike/docview/2918720776/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pitches+made+to+trim+7.9%25+tax+hike%3A+Horwath+praises+councillors+for+suggesting+potential+budget+solutions&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Councillors are making pitches to ease the hit to Hamilton taxpayers as they aim to reduce a potential 7.9 per cent tax hike while maintaining services in the city's 2024 budget.
To that end, city politicians have endorsed a call by Coun. Ted McMeekin to set this year's "municipally generated tax cap" at no more than four per cent.
The overall increase of 7.9 per cent - in a gross $2.4-billion combined operating and capital budget - includes other drivers like provincially tied expenses.
Like his colleagues, he has heard from constituents who are concerned about rising taxes amid other escalating costs such as inflation, McMeekin said Friday.
"Several councillors" say they want to see a municipal cap of below four per cent, he noted.
"But for the moment, I think we need to show the initiative, show our taxpayers that we get it, that we're listening," McMeekin said. "And things are tough out there, and we want to do what we can to get through this together."
The 7.9 per cent increase represents $382 more for an "average" residential property assessed at $385,000, according to the latest provincial valuation in 2016.
Last year, in their first spending plan, the new council landed at a 5.8 per cent increase.
This budget season, city staff and politicians have made a point of parsing out which levels of government contribute to the proposed hike in local property taxes.
This is how officials have broken down the 7.9 per cent increase:
2.7 per cent is attributed to municipal services;
1.6 per cent is for housing and homelessness initiatives;
2.6 per cent springs from provincial legislation that lowers the city's revenue from development charges;
1.0 per cent "placeholder" is for local hospital development projects.
Cutting the 4.3 per cent (the sum of the 2.7 per cent and 1.6 per cent) down to the four per cent target would require shaving about $4 million from the budget. City politicians suggested that's achievable given a series of proposed measures they're floating to lighten the load on residents this year.
That's to be commended, said Mayor Andrea Horwath, noting councillors "pulled up their sleeves" to find solutions.
"So I'm really confident that we will achieve what Councillor McMeekin has set out and perhaps even further in terms of a target."
Those motions, which are to be hashed out on Tuesday, include:
Coun. Tammy Hwang's pitch to shave about $3.3 million from an enhanced litter-collection program;
Coun. Cameron Kroetsch's goal to send the police's nearly $20-million, 10.24 per cent increase back to its governing board for additional review;
Coun. Nrinder Nann's request of staff to examine potential savings through "co-ordinated and staggered" implementation of a new multimillion-dollar suite of programs meant to protect tenants;
Coun. Mike Spadafora's call to ditch the one per cent "placeholder," which is worth about $12 million, in response to a $462-million request toward local hospital redevelopment projects;
Coun. Craig Cassar's suggestion for a longer phase-in of a funding strategy to replenish revenues lost to development-charge exemptions.
With the four per cent "municipally generated tax cap," it's important to be "crystal clear" that residents will still see a tax bill over and above that - perhaps around 6.0 to 6.5 per cent - due to the additional provincial pressures, Coun. Tom Jackson emphasized.
The current 7.9 per cent increase is down from a fall forecast of 14.2 per cent, which staff say they tamed through a series of measures including "efficiency savings," phased-in costs and a $216-millon draw on reserves over four years.
On Friday, councillors expressed support for efforts to reduce the immediate tax hike but expressed concern about the potential hit to taxpayers in the years to come if expenses continue to mount and reserves are diminished.
"I'm worried about that," Coun. Brad Clark said.
Meanwhile, insufficient provincial funding and wrong-headed policies like scaled-back rent control are unfairly leaving municipalities to pick up the slack amid the housing and homelessness crisis, Clark added.
"We're doing this because the city is compassionate," he said of the city's response. "It's not our obligation and responsibility. That is the provincial government's responsibility."
Likewise, Coun. Alex Wilson, who worried about the city "borrowing from the future and not putting the money" into immediate infrastructure needs, echoed Clark's frustration with Premier Doug Ford's government.
Municipalities are "creatures of the province" with limited powers, Wilson said. "And if the province is going to drive up cost, all we can do is be honest about that."
On that note, Coun. John-Paul Danko has placed a motion on Tuesday's agenda calling for the "financial impact of provincial polices and downloaded expenses" to be reflected in a specific line item on 2024 tax bills.
On Friday, the planning and economic department as well as public works gave overviews of their spending plans, following earlier presentations from corporate services, emergency and community services, and the city manager.
The library board, police and conservation authorities have made their pitches, while the general public has offered feedback on the city's overall proposed budget, as well.
Following Tuesday's deliberations, council aims to stick with a condensed budget schedule that has them sign off on the final spending plan on Feb. 15.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Coun. Ted McMeekin won favour for a proposal to set the "municipally generated tax cap" at no more than four per cent.Cathie Coward Metroland

Document 168 of 252

Hamilton's hospitals are short $135 million and counting: St. Joe's nearly $20M in the red after first 9 months of fiscal year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hamiltons-hospitals-are-short-135-million/docview/2918720564/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton%27s+hospitals+are+short+%24135+million+and+counting%3A+St.+Joe%27s+nearly+%2420M+in+the+red+after+first+9+months+of+fiscal+year&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton's hospitals have racked up deficits of nearly $135 million and counting over the last two years.
Both Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS) and St. Joseph's Healthcare broke their silence Friday over the shortfalls that are being seen in virtually every hospital in Ontario.
HHS was short $83.1 million after the first nine months of the fiscal year that started April 1, 2023. The board of directors has approved a deficit of $57.4 million for the entire fiscal year that ends March 31, 2024. The hospital network gave no indication of whether it expects to get the shortfall down to the amount budgeted by the board or whether the deficit will continue to grow instead.
The hospital network has been using commercial operating lines of credit after finishing the last fiscal year on March 31, 2023, short $31.1 million on its $1.6-billion budget.
"Hamilton Health Sciences is facing ongoing financial challenges related to a rising demand for care and higher costs associated with delivering this care," the hospital system said in a previous statement.
St. Joseph's was nearly $20 million in the red after the first nine months of this fiscal year.
Its board of governors has approved a deficit of $46.4 million by the end of the fiscal year on its budget of $775 million.
"St. Joe's continues to work closely with our government partners to understand what funding may be available to address these financial pressures," the hospital network said in a previous statement.
But health-care leaders say money will not be enough to fix the financial stressors that have left Burlington's Joseph Brant Hospital forecasting a deficit of up to $15 million for this fiscal year on its operating budget of $268 million and another up to $15 million for next fiscal year that starts April 1, 2024.
Brant Community Healthcare System has taken out a bank loan in the past year after projecting a deficit of up to $15 million for this fiscal year on its operating budget of $220 million.
Norfolk General Hospital and West Haldimand General Hospital are also in the red this fiscal year but have not yet said by how much.
"Nurses don't want the government to come and bail out the hospitals," said Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO). "Nurses want the government to come and work with hospitals, primary care, nurses, doctors, everybody to solve our collective challenge. That requires immediate action ... It is solvable."
All 141 public hospitals in the province are in an "untenable situation," the Ontario Hospital Association (OHA) warned the health minister, the president of the treasury board and the finance minister in a letter on Dec. 20.
"We're spending quite a lot of time working ... to understand the fundamental underlying reasons for the situation that we're in," said CEO Anthony Dale. "There's a real appetite to drive change and innovate in order to meet the needs of the people of Ontario into the future. "
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones blames the previous Liberal government and the NDP for the strain on hospitals today.
"For a decade the Liberals, supported by the NDP, underfunded the health care system, closing hospitals and hospitals beds, firing nurses and cutting medical school residency spots," the minister's spokesperson Hannah Jensen said in a statement. "Our government inherited a health-care system under pressure."
But Dale and Grinspun say a wide range of factors - some new and some long term - have come together to leave hospitals cash-strapped and both leaders agree on the solution.
"We're caught in a cycle where we're constantly fixated and concerned primarily with the short term," said Dale. "We have to break out of that trap and look squarely toward the future and start thinking about things in a different, more systematic way."
Dale says long-term health-services capacity planning is badly needed in Ontario.
Right now, hospitals don't even know the exact amount of their deficits because funding for the fiscal year that ends in just over two months has not been fully confirmed, HHS said in a statement.
"Let's solve it with multi-year funding," said Grinspun. "Why not announce three-year funding or five-year funding so they can plan and they can actually deliver the quality and safe care that patients need."
How did hospitals get in the red?
Wage suppression legislation
Ontario's Progressive Conservative government passed Bill 124 in 2019, capping public-sector raises at one per cent a year for three years. It was found unconstitutional by an Ontario court in 2022, leading to health-care workers being awarded increases in pay. Nurses alone got what works out to be 11 per cent over two years from an arbitration decision in July.
"The hospitals are on the hook for paying for that," said Grinspun.
The OHA said in its letter to the province that the retroactive payments cost more than $1 billion so far and hospitals have only received partial reimbursement from the Conservative government.
As a result, the OHA says some hospitals have had to take out high-interest loans with banks or have dipped into working capital reserves that are supposed to be used for construction, medical equipment or upgrading information systems.
"It's never a good sign when hospitals are taking money from capital reserves to pay for day-to-day operating," said Dale.
Temp workers
Chronic staff shortages provincewide were exacerbated by the pandemic, leaving Hamilton hospitals alone with 2,700 unfilled jobs by July, including openings for 1,000 nurses.
As the demand for health-care staff increased so did the rates that private agencies charge to supply hospitals with temporary nurses, said the OHA.
"The rate increase has really ballooned the cost of agency nursing," said Dale. "Where this is most acutely felt is smaller and rural hospitals because they're so dependent on agency nursing."
Inflation
"We have the highest inflation the sector has seen in well over two decades," said Dale.
As a result, the cost of running hospitals has gone up. HHS said there have been substantial increases to the price of medical and surgical supplies and equipment, food, energy and drugs.
Growing and aging population
"Ontario is in the midst of an explosion in its population and in addition to that we have an aging population whose health-care needs are changing, quite significantly," said Dale. But the number of hospital beds and the staffing that goes with that have not kept pace, said Grinspun.
At the same time, successive governments starting around 2008 put significant emphasis on making hospitals more efficient. HHS alone has cut costs by $200 million since 2011, including $7 million this fiscal year. It has left Ontario with fewer acute care beds per 1,000 population than any other province or country tracked by the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD).
As a result, hospitals regularly open beds that aren't funded by the province to care for all of the patients who need to be admitted. Juravinski Hospital alone was operating 86 unfunded beds as of Jan. 11 on top of the 414 paid for by the province.
"Fix the hospital system by having the right number of beds for the population," said Grinspun. "In the attempt to be efficient, we went way too far, becoming deficient, not efficient."
Gaps in community care
HHS was caring for 270 patients as of Jan. 11 that were ready to be discharged but were stuck in hospital because they were waiting for services in the community. It's such an issue that HHS and St. Joseph's have been using the former Crowne Plaza Hotel at 150 King St. E. as a satellite health facility for some of these patients known as alternate level of care (ALC) since October 2020.
The problem is provincewide and taking up nearly one in five of Ontario's 22,000 acute care beds with 40 per cent of these patients waiting for long-term care. "That's taking up a significant amount of resources and there's humans behind those numbers who ... need to be discharged to another, more appropriate setting," said Dale.
Overcrowded emergency departments also reveal shortfalls in the community, particularly the lack of primary care that has left about 2.2 million Ontarians without a family doctor. Roughly 53,000 people don't have a family physician in Hamilton, Haldimand and Niagara Northwest .
The OHA recently reported that as of November, the average length of stay in emergency departments for patients waiting to be admitted was the highest it has been in the last 12 months.
"With, so many ALC patients, it becomes very difficult to admit those patients in emerge in a timely manner," said Dale.
Inadequate prevention
"We have approximately 2,000 patients admitted to hospital with quite serious respiratory conditions," said Dale about the situation provincewide. "They've got COVID-19, the flu or RSV. If we had stronger social marketing efforts in the province about the importance of vaccination in order to keep people safe, especially the vulnerable and people with underlying health conditions, how many of those people might have avoided admission to hospital in the first place ."
Hamilton public health reported in December that just 13 per cent of Hamiltonians have had a COVID shot in the past six months.
Hamilton researcher Dawn Bowdish flagged in early January that COVID was still sending high numbers of older adults to hospitals and not enough was being done to protect them. "We have to be very, very serious about the importance of prevention," said Dale. "Keeping people in good health for as long as possible so that they can avoid admission."
jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Registered nurses union CEO Doris Grinspun says multi-year funding for hospitals will help them deliver better care. Juravinski Hospital, part of Hamilton Health Sciences, was operating 86 unfunded beds as of Jan. 11 on top of the 414 paid for by the province.Barry Gray Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Joanna Frketich Reporter

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2918720465/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.20&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier should've
acted on convoy
Re: Use of Emergencies Act was
unreasonable (Jan. 24)
Maybe if Premier Doug Ford had shown leadership and moved quickly to help Ottawa deal with the occupation, the feds would not have had to step in. Instead, he disappeared. It's been the same with housing, post-secondary funding, health care - name an issue. Ford is nowhere to be found when there are tough decisions to be made.
Carol Town, Hamilton
What you can buy
for $500K these days
The answer is a public washroom or a restaurant. The correct question is, what can you get for approximately $500,000 in Hamilton? "Firehouse Subs said it costs between $400,000 and $500,000 to build and open one of its restaurants" (Jan. 24). A privately owned restaurant with at least two washrooms can be built for the cost of one public washroom. Satirist and journalist P.J. O'Rourke said it best about government spending, "Enormous effort and elaborate planning are required to waste this much money."
Mark Melanson, Hamilton
Place blame
where it belongs
We are all gnashing our teeth toward the wrong set of politicians.
One major portion of the proposed tax increase is the provincial download of infrastructure that previously was paid for by developers. This is the servicing of new areas with water and sewer plus the building of infrastructure for police and recreational use such as community centres.
The next is again a provincially mandated number in the police budget where that budget is a non-debatable amount although it is a take it or leave it amount. That budget is almost all personnel costs so there's not much to do there.
The final one is again provincial which are school budgets. In the past we had more control over this part, but it's now all into one provincial pocket from which the amounts are distributed but the province has the say on how much that increases. So again no input from the city or even the school board.
The city is only trying to increase the property tax that they have any control over - 2.9 per cent is a very reasonable amount considering the inflation pressures. They are trying to trim that as we speak, but when you're trimming what is the smallest part of the whole pie, it will not mean much. Go after the provincial government for increasingly taking over our property tax, but not allowing us any control locally.
Ilpo Lehto, Hamilton
LRT report is an insult
Re: A contracted operator, not the city, should run Hamilton's LRT: staff report (Jan. 24)
So Hamilton's LRT will be operated and maintained by an outside contractor while "customer interface" activities like customer service, security and fare enforcement will be handled by the city. If the city feels it needs to retain the jobs of policing the subcontracted operation, how can it expect taxpayers to put their trust in such a subcontracted operation? This decision is an insult to HSR workers and management as well as another misuse of taxpayers' money.
Jim Young, Burlington
Put spending to a vote
Article after article in the paper has a theme going on; spending money, taking money, needing money. There's an old saying, "You can't get blood from a stone."
Our governing body needs to take a long, hard look at how our tax dollars are being used. Maybe it's time for a referendum. Ask the taxpayers their opinions on how their tax dollars should be spent. Do we need LRT? Do we need more bike lanes? Do we need a new washroom at Woodland Park? Do we need outside consultants to report on every issue that might be handled "in-house"? The list goes on and on and on. An election year is too far away for changing out our elected officials. Let's have a referendum now!
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Some fatherly advice
My dad always told me not to criticize unless you can offer an alternative solution. So, I suggest creating the project management group responsible for building the $60-million bicycle path to be the same team quarterbacking the LRT. It will never see the light of day.
Terry Johnson, Burlington
Bus barn too expensive
Re: Hamilton calls for funding help with nearly $400-million bus barn (Jan. 24)
Four hundred million dollars for a bus barn. Sounds more like a bus palace to me.
Keith Lankester, Ancaster
Dyer column is
overly simplistic
Re: Missiles a poor way of sending messages to your enemies (Jan. 22)
I have to say, either Gwynne Dyer is wearing rose-coloured glasses or he's naive as to his opinions on how the military strikes in the Middle East should be addressed. He trivializes how countries use missile attacks as "an expression of displeasure."
I believe it's about far more serious issues than displeasure. It is beyond simplistic to suggest "traditional diplomacy" would work.
We are talking about terrorist groups who have no issues with killing their own people and no respect for human life or rights. How do you have diplomatic discussions with evil?
Henrietta Veldman, Stoney Creek

How area hospitals ended up short tens of millions of dollars

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-area-hospitals-ended-up-short-tens-millions/docview/2918481648/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+area+hospitals+ended+up+short+tens+of+millions+of+dollars&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health-care leaders say money will not be enough to fix the financial stressors that have left area hospitals short tens of millions of dollars.
Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS), St. Joseph's Healthcare, Burlington's Joseph Brant Hospital, Brant Community Healthcare System (BCHS), Norfolk General Hospital and West Haldimand General Hospital are all facing deficits for the fiscal year that ends March 31.
"Nurses don't want the government to come and bail out the hospitals," said Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO). "Nurses want the government to come and work with hospitals, primary care, nurses, doctors, everybody to solve our collective challenge. That requires immediate action ... It is solvable."
All 141 public hospitals in the province are in an "untenable situation," the Ontario Hospital Association (OHA) warned the health minister, the president of the treasury board and the finance minister in a letter on Dec. 20. CEO Anthony Dale says virtually all hospitals are forecasting a shortfall this fiscal year.
"We're spending quite a lot of time working ... to understand the fundamental underlying reasons for the situation that we're in," said Dale. "There's a real appetite to drive change and innovate in order to meet the needs of the people of Ontario into the future. We need stability within Ontario's hospitals and we have to continue to adapt ... The last thing (the sector) wants to do is protect the status quo when it's obvious that there's so much more we can do to improve things."
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones blames the previous Liberal government and the NDP for the strain on hospitals today.
"For a decade the Liberals, supported by the NDP, underfunded the health care system, closing hospitals and hospitals beds, firing nurses and cutting medical school residency spots," the minister's spokesperson Hannah Jensen said in a statement. "Our government inherited a health care system under pressure."
But Dale and Grinspun say a wide range of factors - some new and some long term - have come together to leave hospitals cash-strapped and both leaders agree on the solution.
"We're caught in a cycle where we're constantly fixated and concerned primarily with the short term," said Dale. "We have to break out of that trap and look squarely toward the future and start thinking about things in a different, more systematic way."
Dale says long-term health-services capacity planning is badly needed in Ontario.
Right now, hospitals don't even know the exact amount of their deficits because funding for the fiscal year that ends in just over two months has not been fully confirmed, HHS said in a statement.
"Let's solve it with multi-year funding," said Grinspun. "Why not announce three-year funding or five-year funding so they can plan and they can actually deliver the quality and safe care that patients need."
What is the situation?
Joseph Brant and BCHS are both forecasting deficits up to $15 million. Brant Community Healthcare System has taken out a bank loan in the past year.
Hamilton, Haldimand and Norfolk hospitals have not made public how much their deficits are forecast to be this year, although St. Joseph's says it will provide the number in the coming days.
HHS finished the last fiscal year on March 31, 2023 with a $31.1-million deficit and has been using commercial operating lines of credit. Brantford Community Health System, Joseph Brant and West Haldimand General were also in the red.
How did hospitals get there?
Wage suppression legislation
Ontario's Progressive Conservative government passed Bill 124 in 2019, capping public-sector raises at one per cent a year for three years. It was found unconstitutional by an Ontario court in 2022, leading to health-care workers being awarded increases in pay. Nurses alone got what works out to be 11 per cent over two years from an arbitration decision in July.
"The hospitals are on the hook for paying for that," said Grinspun.
The OHA said in its letter to the province that the retroactive payments cost more than $1 billion so far and hospitals have only received partial reimbursement from the Conservative government.
As a result, the OHA says some hospitals have had to take out high-interest loans with banks or have dipped into working capital reserves that are supposed to be used for construction, medical equipment or upgrading information systems.
"It's never a good sign when hospitals are taking money from capital reserves to pay for day-to-day operating," said Dale.
Temp workers
Chronic staff shortages provincewide were exacerbated by the pandemic, leaving Hamilton hospitals alone with 2,700 unfilled jobs by July, including openings for 1,000 nurses.
As the demand for health-care staff increased so did the rates that private agencies charge to supply hospitals with temporary nurses, said the OHA.
"The rate increase has really ballooned the cost of agency nursing," said Dale. "Where this is most acutely felt is smaller and rural hospitals because they're so dependent on agency nursing ... I'd say that there are a lot of smaller hospitals in rural and remote Ontario that have reached the end of their financial capabilities."
Inflation
"We have the highest inflation the sector has seen in well over two decades," said Dale.
As a result, the cost of running hospitals has gone up. HHS said there have been substantial increases to the price of medical and surgical supplies and equipment, food, energy and drugs.
Growing and aging population
"Ontario is in the midst of an explosion in its population and in addition to that we have an aging population whose health care needs are changing, quite significantly," said Dale.
But the number of hospital beds and the staffing that goes with that have not kept pace, said Grinspun.
At the same time, successive governments starting around 2008 put significant emphasis on making hospitals more efficient. HHS alone has cut costs by $200 million since 2011, including $7 million this fiscal year.
It has left Ontario with fewer acute care beds per 1,000 population than any other province or country tracked by the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD).
As a result, hospitals regularly open beds that aren't funded by the province to care for all of the patients who need to be admitted. Juravinski Hospital alone was operating 86 unfunded beds as of Jan. 11 on top of the 414 paid for by the province.
"Fix the hospital system by having the right number of beds for the population," said Grinspun. "In the attempt to be efficient, we went way too far, becoming deficient, not efficient."
Gaps in community care
HHS was caring for 270 patients as of Jan. 11 that were ready to be discharged but were stuck in hospital because they were waiting for services in the community such as long-term care, home care, rehabilitation, complex continuing care and mental-health care.
It's such an issue that HHS and St. Joseph's have been using the former Crowne Plaza Hotel at 150 King St. E. as a satellite health facility for some of these patients known as alternate level of care (ALC) since October 2020.
The problem is provincewide and taking up nearly one in five of Ontario's 22,000 acute care beds with 40 per cent of these patients waiting for long-term care.
"We have at the moment around 4,000 ALC patients under hospital care," said Dale. "That's taking up a significant amount of resources and there's humans behind those numbers who ... need to be discharged to another, more appropriate setting."
Overcrowded emergency departments also reveal shortfalls in the community, particularly the lack of primary care that has left about 2.2 million Ontarians without a family doctor. Roughly 53,000 people don't have a family physician in Hamilton, Haldimand and Niagara Northwest - the western part of Niagara Region bordering Haldimand, including Grimsby.
"Having patients in the corridors of emergency departments is not the way to take care of them," said Grinspun.
The OHA recently reported that as of November, the average length of stay in emergency departments for patients waiting to be admitted was the highest it has been in the last 12 months.
"With, so many ALC patients, it becomes very difficult to admit those patients in emerge in a timely manner," said Dale.
Inadequate prevention
"We have approximately 2,000 patients admitted to hospital with quite serious respiratory conditions," said Dale about the situation provincewide. "They've got COVID-19, the flu or RSV. If we had stronger social marketing efforts in the province about the importance of vaccination in order to keep people safe, especially the vulnerable and people with underlying health conditions, how many of those people might have avoided admission to hospital in the first place ... Our hospital system is grappling with capacity pressures where there are other solutions available."
Hamilton public health reported in December that just 13 per cent of Hamiltonians have had a COVID shot in the past six months.
A Hamilton researcher flagged in early January that COVID was still sending high numbers of older adults to hospitals and not enough was being done to protect them.
Dawn Bowdish, Canada Research Chair in Aging and Immunity at McMaster University, said more focus needs to be put on keeping older adults out of the hospital for serious respiratory infections, which is associated with a number of short- and long-term health issues.
"We have to be very, very serious about the importance of prevention," said Dale. "Keeping people in good health for as long as possible so that they can avoid admission."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com

Document 171 of 252

Blame Doug Ford for turning a blind eye to student immigration abuses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/blame-doug-ford-turning-blind-eye-student/docview/2918481568/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Blame+Doug+Ford+for+turning+a+blind+eye+to+student+immigration+abuses&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Puppy mills are bad for puppies - but also people and politicians.
That's the new term for private colleges that pander to foreigners who game the system - by applying for student visas, then skipping classes and jumping the jobs queue.
According to Pierre Poilievre, the prime minister allowed "tens of thousands of students to come and go to fake colleges that the Liberal government now admits were now, quote, puppy mills."
J'accuse!
There's just one problem with the federal Conservative leader pointing his accusing finger. In point of fact, those private colleges aren't just Justin Trudeau's problem.
They are Doug Ford's puppy mills.
Which means the premier of our own province - Poilievre's fellow Tory - also owns the problem. Ontario boasts more of these problematic private colleges than anywhere else - far more than our share of Canada's population.
This week, Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced a cap on foreign student visas and new restrictions on work permits as part of a belated crackdown on abuses.
Once upon a time, shady private career schools were dismissed as "diploma mills." Today that's deemed a misnomer, given how many students are aiming at a paycheque - not a graduation certificate.
None of this passes the smell test. But it's been happening under Ford's nose, as Poilievre well knows.
Which is why it's always risky playing politics with immigration. You never know whether driving a wedge will push all the wrong buttons - as when the premier tried to excuse his Greenbelt land grab by saying he needed to satisfy housing demand for new immigrants.
There's plenty of blame to go around - federal and provincial, Liberal and Tory, public and private, educators and entrepreneurs.
What makes Poilievre's public musings so amusing - or laughably unserious - is his political gamesmanship about all those gaming the system. The Conservative leader stressed that visa seekers "are not to blame for (Miller)'s incompetence."
By posing so earnestly as a protector of foreigners, Poilievre is being too clever by half. While some innocent foreigners might be misled by middlemen, many other migrants know precisely what they're up to by leveraging student visas to get a job, not an education.
Let's not insult the intelligence of voters or visa holders about motives. Any approval process based on rules and regulations is open to manipulation - not least an immigration, accreditation and visa system anchored in an overcomplicated federal-provincial framework of overlapping jurisdictions where people fall through the cracks (and seek cover).
Post-pandemic, all that pent-up demand for catch-up visas led everyone to lower their guard, not least the previous immigration minister, Sean Fraser. As his successor, Miller repurposed the term "puppy mills" to describe the fly-by-night immigration and education workers that operate "on top of a massage parlour."
Henceforth, each province will be assigned work permits in proportion to its population. Ontario will be especially hard hit, as it already fills 51 per cent of them with less than 39 per cent of the population.
The problem has been a long time in the making, but the Ford government had eyes only for traditional puppy mills - the ones that breed puppies - when it announced a crackdown last month: The Preventing Unethical Puppy Sales Act, or PUPS Act, will impose a minimum fine of $10,000 for breeders in the business of abusing animals when it is debated in the legislature next month.
But there's been no similar alarm or alacrity so far from Ford's Tories when it comes to outfits that exploit foreigners and dupe our own governments.
While Quebec and B.C. have gotten out ahead of the student visa issue, Ontario allowed problems to fester. On Ford's watch, an entire industry has arisen - an education-immigration complex akin to the old military-industrial complex that raised alarm bells in America decades ago.
There was easy money - lots of it - to be made off of affluent foreign students, and Canadian universities or colleges understandably wanted a piece of the action. But you can have too much of a good thing when a surge of overseas students overwhelms classrooms, campuses, communities, housing and job markets.
A few universities and most colleges got greedy - counting on high-fee foreign students for one-third or even one-half of their tuition revenues. Meanwhile, Ontario's Progressive Conservative government got stingy.
Five years ago, Ford's Tories announced a 10 per cent cut in tuition for domestic students, and have kept them in the deep freeze ever since, while keeping overall subsidies unchanged even as more local students showed up in class. No wonder so many public colleges responded to those foregone revenues by counting on foreign students to make up the difference - leaving them vulnerable to precisely the kind of crackdown coming from Ottawa.
Now, Canada finds itself overshooting its targets while undermining public faith in the integrity of the immigration system and our capacity to integrate migrants. But a perfect storm does not mean the country is drowning.
We cannot be complacent, but we cannot afford to overreact - blaming every blip in the domestic housing market on foreign interlopers. There are many other factors to blame for our housing shortage, from interest rates and labour shortages to zoning roadblocks.
We set targets, see trends, change course and plug the gaps. This is a country that will always need immigrants, always look after refugees, always benefit from foreign students, always need to learn from its mistakes - federal but also provincial and, yes, institutional - without pressing buttons or yanking chains.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford gives remarks during a media preview of the Bombardier aircraft assembly centre in Mississauga on Jan. 22, 2024.

Subject: Foreign students; Passports & visas; Private colleges & universities; Private schools; Immigration

Location: Canada

Document 172 of 252

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/2918481507/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The court gets it wrong again
Federal use of the Emergencies Act was
unreasonable, judge rules (Jan. 23)
South of the border, individual rights trump the collective (group) rights. As a result, in the United States, we are witness to the unrestricted use of firearms resulting in 49,000 gun-related deaths in 2021 (most recent available statistics), and the unfettered propagation of race hatred on social media. In Canada, on the other hand, collective rights supposedly trump the rights of an individual.
I am astounded by the ruling of Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley, who states that the federal government's use of the Emergencies Act in early 2022 to clear convoy protesters was unreasonable and infringed on protesters' Charter rights. Really? What about the rights of Ottawa's citizens who were penned into their local district, harassed and bullied by truckers for weeks. As an oncologist, I would not be surprised if the rates of cancer in this group are increased in 20 years because of the continuous exposure to petroleum fumes.
With all due respect to the Canadian Civil Liberties Association who brought forward the challenge, as a Canadian and member of the Order of Canada, I stand up for the rule of law. The rights of society trump the rights of a rogue group of anti-vaxxers. Bravo to Justice Paul Rouleau's commission that stated, "the decision to invoke the act was appropriate."
Dr. Mark Levine, Hamilton
The story behind the story
One day The Spec headline reads "As many universities run deficits, Mac stays in the black" (Jan 22). The next day it's "Ottawa announces two-year cap on foreign student visas" (Jan 23). These two articles tell the story.
Ontario has the lowest per capita funding among provinces for colleges and universities, while also continuing years of frozen tuition fees. So how have McMaster and other institutions coped? By rapidly increasing foreign students - such that Ontario now also hosts the highest proportion in Canada.
Yes, the feds should have cut back on visas several years ago. They've been asleep at the wheel. But Ontario is equally culpable. Aren't they supposed to be in charge of education? But, as with health care and housing, the Ford government does nothing - either because of incompetence or because of ideology. Either way, Ontarians lose.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Why the Poilievre bashing?
I am perplexed by the constant Pierre Poilievre bashing and unfair comparisons to Donald Trump when the current Liberals have failed Canadians on so many fronts, including a national debt that has surpassed $1 trillion and is costing over $40 billion per year in interest, a carbon tax that has not resulted in reduced emissions and is just adding to the cost of living (verified by the PBO), an underfunded military that is lacking in new equipment and cannot meet NATO commitments, an EV policy that has no infrastructure plan, a flawed immigration/refugee and foreign student policy with no plan to house people which has caused a housing crisis and a declining GDP compared to the U.S. Yes, other than scrapping the carbon tax, Poilievre has not stated how he will address all these issues, and why would he? The election is two years away and the Liberals are self-destructing right now. Timing is everything in politics.
Gary Brown, Hamilton
Let the dead rest in peace
History in their bones (Jan. 23)
I found the story concerning the "research" being conducted on the 150- to 200-year-old human remains discovered underneath a parking lot in Guelph to be a case of talking out of both sides of one's mouth at the same time. Archeologist Mike McCready states the people whose remains are now being researched, fully expected that where they were originally interred would be their final resting place.
Photographing their remains stuffed into boxes and posing grim faced with a skull on the front page of a newspaper is quite a contradiction to your noble platitudes about your job, which is to "make sure their wishes are carried out - to be interred in a safe place where they're not going to be impacted again." Only, of course, when you and your colleagues are done scanning, X-raying and generally poking away at them.
Much has been written about the way people lived during those times, including diet and lack of medical know-how. Is there an astounding discovery to be made here? I highly doubt it.
What I do know is for these lost souls, consent for this public display of their remains was never asked for, nor was it given.
Charice Beard-Chartier, Caledonia
Bike lanes save lives
There are two words that get Spectator letter to the editor contributors' hearts pounding: "bike lanes."
The $60-million price tag is just fodder for anger. The real issue is some people are perpetually 15 seconds late to wherever they're going and hate to be slowed down for any reason. Speeding cars and pickup trucks have earned a bad reputation. Not everyone in Hamilton agrees, but bike lanes save lives; build them.
Gary Ellis, Hamilton

Cut spending where possible
Re: Councillor signals intention to reject Hamilton police budget (Jan. 22)
I commend councillors Cameron Kroetsch and Ted McMeekin for taking concrete steps in dealing with another proposed onerous residential tax increase. At what point will our council realize residents have reached a "tipping point" when it comes to home affordability? Monthly mortgage payments have morphed into tax payments. Gone are the days when property taxes primarily paid for infrastructure and services. Today's complex social issues require a complete reassessment of how cities allocate funds. It is unsustainable to finance new initiatives without eliminating some spending. Priorities change. Clearly it is time to end the practice of indiscriminately passing any amount demanded in our police budget. Like every other municipal shareholder, they need to be part of our city's fiscal solution. Changing times require a new perspective with bolder thinking. Considering the current financial stress on both residents and council, it is time to cut spending where possible and reallocate funds to meet our current priorities. Anything less is an abdication of council's duties.
David Borsellino, Hamilton
Homes over bike lanes
One question our city council needs to answer: Why does $60 million for bike lanes trump money needed for Hamilton's homeless?
Pamela Holland, Stoney Creek

Let's pray that if he wins, he doesn't set up an "ArriveUSA" app as well. I can't image the damage he may cause.
John Brush Burlington

These people were not vetted properly so no one has any idea of how many terrorists were let into that country. Sounds like a wonderful place to go on vacation, eh ? Way to go, Joe. And people want this senile old man to be president again?
R.J. Kovljenic, Ancaster

Document 174 of 252

Music director candidate Caleb Young to guest conduct the HPO

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/music-director-candidate-caleb-young-guest/docview/2918264448/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Music+director+candidate+Caleb+Young+to+guest+conduct+the+HPO&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conductors' careers consist of watersheds and turning points.
Take, for example, Caleb Young, who auditions as music director of the Hamilton Philharmonic Orchestra on Saturday, Feb. 10 at 3 p.m. in FirstOntario Concert Hall.
Though the Asheville, N.C.-born, Berlin, Germany-based Young, 35, had done a bit of conducting in high school, he caught the conducting bug while at the U of Alabama, watershed years for him, during which he played trombone in the orchestra and took weekly conducting lessons with John Ratledge.
"We discussed and conducted all types of repertoire," said Young. "I owe a lot to these three years of serious groundwork. I still think about these lessons almost every time I step in front of an orchestra."
In 2016, two years after graduating with a master's degree in orchestral conducting from the Jacobs School of Music at Indiana University, Young joined the Fort Wayne Philharmonic as assistant conductor. He was later promoted to associate conductor and worked full time with the orchestra until moving to Berlin in 2021. Though he has an ongoing appointment as a guest conductor with the FWP, his move overseas went hand in hand with getting accepted as one of three conductors into the inaugural LEAD! Artists program in Finland and working with Jukka-Pekka Saraste.
"Being invited to join LEAD! Artists was a monumental turning point not only in my career, but also vastly expanded my musical horizons," explained Young.
"Jukka-Pekka offered me a two-year fellowship to personally study and travel with him through the world. Working with him in places like Oslo, Helsinki, London, Berlin, Detroit, Stockholm and many others, was truly a once in a lifetime opportunity. Not only did we discuss the music on the docket that week at an intensely deep level, but I was able to see so many different working environments. I gained so much from my time with him, but what stands out is to always have the deepest inner strength for your vision of the music. This has to be the foundation for everything."
Another turning point came when Young worked as a cover conductor for John Williams. Yes, the John Williams of smash hit soundtracks fame. "Stars Wars." "Superman." "Home Alone." The list goes on and on.
"Working with John Williams has been the greatest privilege of my musical life," confessed Young. "I first worked with John at the Hollywood Bowl around four years ago and we've been working together ever since. It's been incredible to assist him through the world with orchestras like the Vienna Philharmonic and the Berlin Philharmonic. I have to pinch myself every time I get to work with the maestro."
He'll be pinching himself next week, when he serves as cover conductor for Williams's three-night stand with the Los Angeles Philharmonic.
The following week, Young will be in Hamilton. So, what does this North Carolinian transplanted to Berlin know about Hamilton and the HPO?
"Applying for this position was a no-brainer," said Young. "The HPO has a fantastic international reputation, and I want to give credit to the fabulous work (outgoing HPO music director) Gemma (New) has been doing to help promote that."
In case you're wondering, Young is the third music director candidate in a row who personally knows New.
Hmm. Interesting pattern so far, no?
"Gemma and I have known each other for years," added Young. "We met in St. Louis for the first time when she was there (as resident conductor from 2016 to 2020). We've stayed in touch. Really a lovely person."
The HPO has assigned Young an hour-long program consisting of a Canadian work, a concerto, and a standard repertoire piece.
The "Airs d'Espagne (Tunes from Spain)" was composed in 1992 by José Evangelista, Spanish-born, though resident in Montreal from 1970 until his death at the age of 79 last January.
In his 14-minute work for string orchestra, Evangelista strings together 15 Spanish folk melodies, be they love songs, dance tunes, work songs, a lullaby and a Christmas song before ending with a "Song to the Virgin Mary" from a Catalonian mystery play. The melodies are of various lengths, one of which clocks in at a fleeting 18 seconds.
"The melodies are presented as such, or at most repeated, without formal developments or modulations," wrote Evangelista in his program notes. "My purpose is to emphasize the validity of Spanish folk music in its pure linear dimensions, that is, as melody."
However charmingly clothed and sequenced those chosen melodies may be, the piece, as a whole, progresses in fits and starts, sometimes frustratingly so. Young is acutely aware of the challenge he faces.
"We'll need to find a flow that helps tell not just 15 small stories, but a longer flow from start to finish," said Young.
HPO principal horn Jessie Brooks will take the spotlight in Richard Strauss's "Horn Concerto No. 1."
Composed by an 18-year-old Strauss in 1882 and orchestrated the following year, the 15-minute concerto, with its Schumannesque robustness and Mendelssohnian lyricism, has remained a long-standing favourite with horn players and audiences alike.
"This is a warhorse for the horn," said Young. "Strauss gives everyone onstage something to chew on. I'm looking forward to this collaboration."
After intermission, it's Mozart's iconic "Symphony No. 40 in G Minor."
"Mozart 40 is a desert island piece for me," said Young. "What I'm hoping to do is to bring something fresh to the table while respecting what has come before. I find a lot of presentations of this music become too apologetic and overstylized. I'm looking forward to throwing out a bit of that with the chamber pots and getting to work."
Tickets at hpo.org or call 905-526-7756: $30.10 to $114.85 (all-in).
Registration for the HPO's North American Community Recital closes on Friday, Feb. 2 at 4 p.m. The recital on Saturday, March 9 at 2 p.m. in St. Paul's United Church, 29 Park St. W., Dundas, will feature participants performing a single movement or a short piece under five minutes by a North American-based composer. Register at hpo.org/news/.
Countertenor Daniel Taylor has had to bow out of the Nota Bene Players & Singers' "Solo Bach" concert under Howard Dyck on Jan. 27 in St. Paul's United Church, Dundas. Taylor, a close friend of the Honourable Ed Broadbent, has been asked to sing and lead the choir at the state funeral for the late NDP leader on Jan. 28 in Ottawa's Carleton Dominion-Chalmers Centre. Mezzo Jennifer Enns Modolo will jump in for Taylor in Dundas and the group's other concerts in southwest Ontario.
CAPTION: Guest conductor Caleb Young auditions as music director of the Hamilton Philharmonic Orchestra on Feb. 10 at 3 p.m.

Subject: Orchestras; Musicians & conductors; Musical performances

Business indexing term: Subject: Orchestras

Title: Music director candidate Caleb Young to guest conduct the HPO

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 25, 2024

Section: entertainment/music

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodi cals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 175 of 252

Emergencies Act ruling 'kind of hurts': 'It was a great relief to myself and I think many of the citizens of Ottawa,' church official says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/emergencies-act-ruling-kind-hurts/docview/2918134967/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Emergencies+Act+ruling+%27kind+of+hurts%27%3A+%27It+was+a+great+relief+to+myself+and+I+think+many+of+the+citizens+of+Ottawa%2C%27+church+official+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A judge's ruling that the federal Liberals were unjustified in using emergency law to quell a weeks-long protest in Ottawa has left the administrator of a downtown church feeling the court disregarded infringements of her rights.
In a decision released Tuesday, Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley said the government's invocation of the Emergencies Act in February 2022 was unreasonable and led to violation of the constitutional right of free expression.
The Canadian Civil Liberties Association, an applicant in the case, said the ruling serves notice that even in times of crisis, when emotions are running high, no government is above the law and that basic rights and freedoms must be upheld.
Vivian Leir, the longtime administrator of a Presbyterian church near Parliament Hill, said she was dismayed by the court decision after what she experienced during the three-week protest in downtown Ottawa.
The "Freedom Convoy" protest, initially seen as a demonstration against COVID-19 health restrictions, attracted people with a variety of grievances against Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the Liberal government.
Blaring truck horns, diesel fumes and makeshift encampments prompted many businesses to temporarily close their doors and aggravated downtown residents, some of whom were harassed.
The Emergencies Act allowed for temporary measures including regulation and prohibition of public assemblies, the designation of secure places, direction to banks to freeze assets and a ban on support for participants.
Leir said the court ruling has done nothing to change her belief that the government was justified in invoking the emergency powers.
"At the time, when the federal government brought in the Emergencies Act, it was a great relief to myself and I think many of the citizens of Ottawa," she said in an interview Wednesday.
"To hear that they didn't think it was justified when clearly nothing was being done about the situation, it kind of hurts."
Leir testified in the criminal trial of two "Freedom Convoy" organizers about how overwhelmed she felt by the number of big-rig trucks and near-constant horn honking that accompanied the protest. That criminal trial is still ongoing.
A proposed $290-million class-action lawsuit against convoy organizers on behalf of downtown Ottawa residents, workers and business owners is also still unfolding in Ontario court, said Paul Champ, lawyer for the lead plaintiff in the case.
Reflecting on Mosley's ruling, Champ said that invoking the Emergencies Act should be reserved for extraordinary circumstances, "and I don't think that we were necessarily there."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

Document 176 of 252

International student reforms long past due

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/international-student-reforms-long-past-due/docview/2918134886/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=International+student+reforms+long+past+due&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After weeks of foreshadowing, the federal government moved this week to cap the number of international student visas over the next two years. File this policy change in the "better late than never" category.
The number of international students flowing to this country has grown by such epic proportions it is difficult to reach any conclusion other than the federal Liberals were simply sleeping on this file. There have been no shortage of red flags, from Statistics Canada reports warning of the strain on affordable housing and access to social services to provincial auditors warning of an unhealthy dependence on international student fees by post-secondary institutions which are being underfunded.
Over the past two years, the number of international students in this country jumped from 617,000 to more than a million. About a third are in public universities, but the overwhelming majority are in public colleges or private schools, often offering substandard education and a backdoor route to permanent status in this country. Immigration Minister Marc Miller, in announcing he is cutting the number of study permits by 35 per cent to 364,000 this year, is right to target the shady operators who are preying on international students and not doctoral and postgraduate international students at public universities. Miller says hundreds of the private schools should be shut down.
"It is not the intention of this program to have sham commerce degrees and business degrees that are sitting on top of a massage parlour," Miller said in making his announcement.
There are a number of threads to unravel from this announcement. First and foremost, as the minister stressed, this is not an indictment of foreign students. They are hardly responsible for a housing crunch or fears over access to stretched social services. International students were more likely the victims, living in crowded, substandard housing, dealing with a much more expensive country that they had anticipated and receiving diplomas which Miller says were being churned out like "puppy mills."
But these students would not be in this country without federal approval, so Miller is correcting a problem that his government largely created. According to a memo obtained by The Canadian Press, the Trudeau government was warned in 2022 that there was a widening gap between immigration and housing supply, largely driven by the increasing number of international students and temporary foreign workers admitted to this country.
This cap will be most acutely felt in Ontario, home to 51 per cent of international students. The Doug Ford government has twice been warned about a reliance on international student tuition fees, once in November by his own panel on colleges and universities, and in 2021 by the auditor-general who warned him not to increase a dependency on foreign student fees without a post-secondary education plan in place. The panel reported in November that the Progressive Conservatives had the lowest per student post-secondary funding in the country following a tuition cut and freeze that meant colleges and universities had reached the point at which revenue from international student tuition fees was "fundamental to the sector's financial sustainability." Now, Ford is forced into some tough decisions. He will have to decide what schools can bring in international students and what schools should be eliminated, while protecting universities in financial trouble.
Finally, the Liberals - and all governments at all levels - must handle matters of immigration, including temporary foreign workers and international students, with utmost delicacy. To their credit, Canadians have held together on a consensus on the accommodation of immigrants. And to their credit, Canadian politicians have largely resisted any base urge to exploit frustration and anxiety in this country by playing the immigration card.
But the numbers are increasing. Some 500,000 immigrants will arrive next year and this country is going through a population boom during challenging economic times. Immigration will dominate much of the upcoming U.S. presidential election and delicacy is not a feature of debate to the south, particularly from Republicans.
It would not take much to bust that Canadian consensus. We trust our politicians to be vigilant on that score.
CAPTION:

Document 177 of 252

Music director candidate Caleb Young to guest conduct the HPO

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/music-director-candidate-caleb-young-guest/docview/2918134679/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Music+director+candidate+Caleb+Young+to+guest+conduct+the+HPO&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conductors' careers consist of watersheds and turning points.
Take, for example, Caleb Young, who auditions as music director of the Hamilton Philharmonic Orchestra on Saturday, Feb. 10 at 3 p.m. in FirstOntario Concert Hall.
Though the Asheville, N.C.-born, Berlin, Germany-based Young, 35, had done a bit of conducting in high school, he caught the conducting bug while at the U of Alabama, watershed years for him, during which he played trombone in the orchestra and took weekly conducting lessons with John Ratledge.
"We discussed and conducted all types of repertoire," said Young. "I owe a lot to these three years of serious groundwork. I still think about these lessons almost every time I step in front of an orchestra."
In 2016, two years after graduating with a master's degree in orchestral conducting from the Jacobs School of Music at Indiana University, Young joined the Fort Wayne Philharmonic as assistant conductor. He was later promoted to associate conductor and worked full time with the orchestra until moving to Berlin in 2021. Though he has an ongoing appointment as a guest conductor with the FWP, his move overseas went hand in hand with getting accepted as one of three conductors into the inaugural LEAD! Artists program in Finland and working with Jukka-Pekka Saraste.
"Being invited to join LEAD! Artists was a monumental turning point not only in my career, but also vastly expanded my musical horizons," explained Young.
"Jukka-Pekka offered me a two-year fellowship to personally study and travel with him through the world. Working with him in places like Oslo, Helsinki, London, Berlin, Detroit, Stockholm and many others, was truly a once in a lifetime opportunity. Not only did we discuss the music on the docket that week at an intensely deep level, but I was able to see so many different working environments. I gained so much from my time with him, but what stands out is to always have the deepest inner strength for your vision of the music. This has to be the foundation for everything."
Another turning point came when Young worked as a cover conductor for John Williams. Yes, the John Williams of smash hit soundtracks fame. "Stars Wars." "Superman." "Home Alone." The list goes on.
"Working with John Williams has been the greatest privilege of my musical life," confessed Young. "I first worked with John at the Hollywood Bowl around four years ago and we've been working together ever since. It's been incredible to assist him through the world with orchestras like the Vienna Philharmonic and the Berlin Philharmonic. I have to pinch myself every time I get to work with the maestro."
He'll be pinching himself next week, when he serves as cover conductor for Williams's three-night stand with the Los Angeles Philharmonic. The following week, Young will be in Hamilton. So, what does this North Carolinian transplanted to Berlin know about Hamilton and the HPO?
"Applying for this position was a no-brainer," said Young. "The HPO has a fantastic international reputation, and I want to give credit to the fabulous work (outgoing HPO music director) Gemma (New) has been doing to help promote that."
In case you're wondering, Young is the third music director candidate in a row who personally knows New. Hmm. Interesting pattern so far, no?
"Gemma and I have known each other for years," added Young. "We met in St. Louis for the first time when she was there (as resident conductor from 2016 to 2020). We've stayed in touch. Really a lovely person."
The HPO has assigned Young an hour-long program consisting of a Canadian work, a concerto, and a standard repertoire piece.
The "Airs d'Espagne (Tunes from Spain)" was composed in 1992 by José Evangelista, Spanish-born, though resident in Montreal from 1970 until his death at the age of 79 last January.
In his 14-minute work for string orchestra, Evangelista strings together 15 Spanish folk melodies, be they love songs, dance tunes, work songs, a lullaby and a Christmas song before ending with a "Song to the Virgin Mary" from a Catalonian mystery play. The melodies are of various lengths, one of which clocks in at a fleeting 18 seconds.
"The melodies are presented as such, or at most repeated, without formal developments or modulations," wrote Evangelista in his program notes. "My purpose is to emphasize the validity of Spanish folk music in its pure linear dimensions, that is, as melody."
However charmingly clothed and sequenced those chosen melodies may be, the piece, as a whole, progresses in fits and starts, sometimes frustratingly so. Young is acutely aware of the challenge he faces.
"We'll need to find a flow that helps tell not just 15 small stories, but a longer flow from start to finish," said Young.
HPO principal horn Jessie Brooks will take the spotlight in Richard Strauss's "Horn Concerto No. 1."
Composed by an 18-year-old Strauss in 1882 and orchestrated the following year, the 15-minute concerto, with its Schumannesque robustness and Mendelssohnian lyricism, has remained a long-standing favourite with horn players and audiences alike.
"This is a warhorse for the horn," said Young. "Strauss gives everyone onstage something to chew on. I'm looking forward to this collaboration."
After intermission, it's Mozart's iconic "Symphony No. 40 in G Minor."
"Mozart 40 is a desert island piece for me," said Young. "What I'm hoping to do is to bring something fresh to the table while respecting what has come before. I find a lot of presentations of this music become too apologetic and overstylized. I'm looking forward to throwing out a bit of that with the chamber pots and getting to work."
Tickets at hpo.org or call 905-526-7756: $30.10 to $114.85 (all-in).
Registration for the HPO's North American Community Recital closes on Friday, Feb. 2 at 4 p.m. The recital on Saturday, March 9 at 2 p.m. in St. Paul's United Church, 29 Park St. W., Dundas, will feature participants performing a single movement or a short piece under five minutes by a North American-based composer. Register at hpo.org/news/.
Countertenor Daniel Taylor has had to bow out of the Nota Bene Players & Singers' "Solo Bach" concert on Jan. 27 in St. Paul's United Church, Dundas. Taylor, a close friend of the Honourable Ed Broadbent, has been asked to sing and lead the choir at the state funeral for the late NDP leader on Jan. 28 in Ottawa's Carleton Dominion-Chalmers Centre. Mezzo Jennifer Enns Modolo will jump in for Taylor in Dundas and the group's other concerts in southwest Ontario.
Leonard Turnevicius writes about classical music for The Hamilton Spectator.
CAPTION: Hamilton Philharmonic Orchestra has assigned Caleb Young an hour-long program consisting of a Canadian work, a concerto, and a standard repertoire piece.calebwyoung.comPhoto

Document 178 of 252

Under pressure, Ford government readying law to punish local politicians who harass staff

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/under-pressure-ford-government-readying-law/docview/2918319843/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Under+pressure%2C+Ford+government+readying+law+to+punish+local+politicians+who+harass+staff&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Facing the heat from municipalities and opposition parties, the Ford government now says "airtight" legislation that could force local politicians out of office for harassing staff is in the works.
After voting down a private member's bill from Liberal MPP Stephen Blais (Orléans) last year, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra says he is working with the attorney general on a law to address an issue that a number of municipal councils across the province have struggled with.
"I have to ensure that it's constitutional, that it does what it's supposed to do," Calandra told attendees at the annual meeting of the Rural Ontario Municipal Association earlier this week.
"I don't want to do something or bring something forward that can, in any way, make it harder for somebody to get the justice that they are seeking," he said. "I continue to work with the attorney general and continue to work with people on this one, but when we bring something forward, I want to make sure that it is tested at all levels and that it ... (fulfils) what it is that we want to accomplish with it, and that it is airtight."
Currently, local politicians can be removed from office for things such as campaign finance violations, but not for sexually harassing staff. A number of municipalities have been left without any recourse, including in Ottawa, where the city's integrity commissioner found a councillor guilty of harassing staff.
Blais introduced his bill after serving on Ottawa council with Rick Chiarelli, who former staffers said made inappropriate comments, urged them to go braless and shared sexually explicit stories. In the end, Chiarelli was docked pay, but could not be ousted.
Emily McIntosh, of the non-partisan advocacy group The Women of Ontario Say No, called efforts to create legislation "absolutely welcome news."
"When public pressure mounts, there is a tipping point, and we hope that we have hit that tipping point," said the Barrie-area advocate, adding "it would be naive to think that there wasn't support in caucus for this already. I think what this is really recognizing is that is about all Ontarians, and this is about repairing that relationship with municipalities."
While she said new legislation is "long overdue ... this is a non-partisan effort, and we're fully committed to working with this government to see this on the table. The key element now is to make sure that this is prioritized; this is really about workplace safety."
The group was able to get 201 municipalities on side, and at the rural association meeting all three opposition leaders spoke in favour of the need for tighter code of conduct penalties.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles wore the group's "Accountability Now" T-shirt at the meeting, and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said legislation to remove a councillor after harassment is found "is a fundamental thing. We need accountability for those who do the wrong thing."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said without such a law, "we all lose."
"We must demand better," he added.
The Women of Ontario Say No have noted that any staffers who harass others would be fired, and that elected officials need to be held to the same standard.
"We're talking about people that have the most power in communities held to a lower standard than any other workers in Ontario," McIntosh has said.
Blais had proposed that all municipal councillors fall under workplace harassment and violence policies, allowing them to be terminated and even barred from running in the next election for violations if found guilty by an integrity commissioner. Any removal, however, would have to be approved by a judge.
His bill had the support of former premier Kathleen Wynne, who personally spoke to councils in Richmond Hill, Orangeville and Mulmur about it.
The Ford government had previously said that such a bill would give integrity commissioners more power than judges.
Apart from the individual municipalities, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, Ontario Big City Mayors and the Rural Ontario Municipal Association had all asked for help on code of conduct compliance.
Calandra said Monday that "I have had a lot of discussions ... and I've been given a lot of advice on this from many of you" since taking on the municipal affairs portfolio last September. He said he knows there is a "desire to see something done so that people will truly feel safe in the workplace."
CAPTION: Emily Mcintoshof the non-partisan advocacy group The Women of Ontario Say No at Barrie City Hall on March 18, 2023. McIntosh was the leading advocate of a group pushing to get support for a new Liberal bill that would make it easier to remove local politicians from office for harassment.Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra says he is working with the attorney general on a law to address the removal of municipal politicians.
