Document 179 of 252

Ford government stands by use of Emergencies Act against 'Freedom Convoy' despite court decision

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-stands-use-emergencies-act/docview/2918319407/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+stands+by+use+of+Emergencies+Act+against+%27Freedom+Convoy%27+despite+court+decision&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's support for Ottawa triggering the Emergencies Act to end the convoy blockade remains unwavering despite a federal court ruling it was unconstitutional, says the deputy premier.
"We cannot have communities held hostage and not have access to critical infrastructure again," said Sylvia Jones, the province's solicitor general during the winter 2022 protest against COVID-19 pandemic restrictions that left downtown Ottawa effectively under siege for more than three weeks.
"We had ambulances and fire trucks who literally who could not go on certain roads, so something had to be done," Jones told reporters Wednesday in Burlington.
Her remarks came a day after Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley ruled there was no legal justification to use the Emergencies Act to quash the so-called "Freedom Convoy" protest, and that doing so had resulted in a breach of Canadians' Charter rights.
Mosley found the convoy participants' actions did not amount to a "national emergency" that would justify the use of the act, which is reserved for situations beyond the scope of existing laws and regulations.
The federal government, which invoked the act with enthusiastic support from Premier Doug Ford, is planning to appeal the ruling.
Jones, who is now health minister as well as Ford's deputy, indicated Ontario would back that.
"Some of those impacts that were happening in our community, whether it was in Ottawa or in our border communities, had very direct and serious impacts, whether it was economic or socially," she said.
"But I will leave it to the federal government on whether they wish to appeal and how they will do that."
In his decision, Mosley said civil liberties were violated because use of the act led to protesters being barred from participating in demonstrations and having their bank accounts frozen.
"While I agree that the evidence supports the conclusion that the situation was critical and required an urgent resolution by government, the evidence, in my view, does not support the conclusion that it could not have been effectively dealt with under other laws of Canada," he wrote.
But Mosley's findings are contrary to Justice Paul Rouleau's conclusions last year at a legally mandated public inquiry into the act's invocation, where Trudeau and other senior officials testified,
Rouleau found - albeit "with reluctance" - that the federal government had met the legal threshold to impose the law.
While neither Jones nor Ford agreed to testify at the inquiry, the premier has said he stood "shoulder to shoulder" with Trudeau's decision to crack down on the convoy protest.
Asked if Queen's Park could have done more to help convoy protests in Ottawa and on a Windsor road leading to the Ambassador Bridge into the U.S. - a major trade route for supplying auto factories and other industries - Jones noted the province did take extra steps.
"We worked very closely with both municipal leaders, as well as of course enforcement whether that was OPP or city police," she said.
"And we did put some additional measures in place including some new legislation that ensured going forward we could not have blockades that were going to impact our ability to access critical infrastructure or our border communities."
Jones emphasized "we believed at the time" it was legal for the federal government to invoke the Emergencies Act.
"I continue to believe that we need to ensure that demonstrations don't impact communities in a very visceral way."
CAPTION: Health Minister Sylvia Jones listens to questions from reporters following a news conference in Toronto on Jan. 11, 2023.

Document 180 of 252

Jan. 24: Spec readers comment on Hamilton City Hall spending and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-24-spec-readers-comment-on-hamilton-city-hall/docview/2917900558/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+24%3A+Spec+readers+comment+on+Hamilton+City+Hall+spending+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I didn't vote for runaway spending
Re: Hamilton pitches $60-million plan to build more bike lanes, faster (Jan. 19)
It's early morning, and I'm reading my Spectator. Surely I'm still asleep, just having a bad dream. A slug of cold coffee and an eye rub tells me no, it's true. In this economic environment, and within the context of another massive boost to property taxes, city council has approved a staffing increase for councillors' offices that will add more than $1.62 million to an already bloated budget. Justifiable or not, this is not the time for such an increase. Councillors, along with the mayor, need to be looking in closets and under rugs to find efficiencies and savings for the city and its taxpayers, not new ways to spend.
Then, just when you think it can't get worse, it's now said that council is planning to spend a further $60 million it does not have to fast-track expansion of bike lanes. Really? Any suggestion of initiating new ways to spend money, no matter how well-intentioned, should be shelved until the current economic crunch eases. This council was elected on the basis of promising to be better, do things differently, better serve the public and be more transparent. They are failing on all counts. I don't know anyone who voted for runaway spending and taxation. The next municipal election can't come fast enough so we can boot this group of spendthrifts before they do even more harm to already struggling families.
Cameron Stringer, Hamilton
Council has no fiscal acuity
Once again the idealogues at city hall are about to throw taxpayers' $60 million down a rabbit hole in the hope of getting people out of their cars and onto a bike. Anyone paying attention realizes that over the last six years this experiment has been a colossal failure. We are a northern climate city and even in the nice weather riders are few and far between. Cannon Street is rarely used and now they are going to clog up Main Street with another east-west route. The bike share program is already on its third sponsor and council had to bail them out last year with a $200,000 grant. One year in this new council has proven time and again they have no fiscal acuity.
Tom Winning, Hamilton
A sinful decision
It may not be a crime, but it is certainly a sin that Hamilton city council would spend $60 million on new bike lanes while people are living on the street. I don't think I have to say more.
Pat Ward, Hamilton
City should be cutting expenses
Businesses pay a large sum of Hamilton's taxes. The spending of those taxes is embarrassing.
I assume the intention of spending another $60 million on more bike lanes is to eventually keep vehicles out and close more stores. I feel bad for those taxpaying businesses in the city that rely on traffic for their income. You have a group of councillors trying to put you out of business and raising your taxes doing it. The recent news has been tax increases for residents and businesses in Hamilton and then trying to make us feel good because they aren't going up as much as expected. Then within weeks, the councillors are spending more money they don't have to appease a very small group of taxpayers.
Good business practice is to look at income, expenses and efficiency. The city doesn't need to raise taxes, it needs to look at expenses and efficiency. More importantly, putting things in priority spending money to add more bike lanes is not a priority when you have a dissolving infrastructure. A perfect example of wasting tax dollars.
I have nothing against bicycles, but in no way should it take priority over so many more important issues city is facing. Perhaps it's time councillors take a poll and ask taxpayers their thoughts on bike lanes, one-way streets, conditions of roads and inefficiencies.
Bruce Eccles, Hamilton
Focus funds on necessities
Re: Hamilton mayor touts 'very responsible' budget (Jan. 20)
Property taxes should be for services used by the property owners and not to support so many social services. We are having a hard enough time affording things without paying for others.
Our provincial and federal governments need to step up and fund the things we aren't supposed to be paying for. They are the cause of many of the issues in the city.
The property owners in this city do not have unlimited funds for the city to use and it seems to me that this is becoming the norm; need money, up property taxes.
If I were younger I'd be moving.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Some belt-tightening tips
Mayor and city councillors, it's time to tighten the belt. Run city hall as if the money was coming out of your own pockets and running your own household.
If you want to lead by example, you must start with yourselves. Freeze your wage increase and freeze all bonuses. It's time you charge more taxes to these rich corporations that made a killing during COVID-19. Spending millions to change Main Street into two ways is ridiculous. Paying half a million to repair a bathroom is nuts. If Justin Trudeau wants the refugees, then that money should come from federal reserves, not the city's reserves. We need to go into every department and see if they are all needed. Seems there is too much management compared to workers. It's time to stop hiring consultants and use the people we have. Stop taking ridiculous trips.
That would be a start before you come to the homeowners with a huge tax increase. A majority of the taxpayers would agree with me. You be the judge.
Jake Lombardo, Hamilton
It's about needs vs. wants
Seeing as the city is looking for budget input, I thought I would share my two-cents worth.
Given the economic struggles that are facing too many people, it is time to distinguish between what we need and what would be nice, and the nice must go.
LRT - scrap it. There is still no estimate of maintenance costs that I have seen and the debacle in Ottawa is not reassuring, and with many working from home is it still necessary? Have dedicated bus lanes instead with rapid buses. No worries about rails freezing or accidents hampering the service. We have the drivers and maintenance staff already.
Bike lanes - are a nicety. We can't afford them without a survey of usage.
Two-way street conversion - more idling, more pollution and more hazardous left-hand turns. Scrap the idea.
Sidewalk snow clearing - again, a nicety. But will incur extra costs repairing the lawns in the spring, as they are routinely torn up on city sidewalks.
Marg MacNaughton, Hamilton
Don't blame city hall
On Jan. 18 I listened to a Q&A with our local city councillor. The issue of municipal taxes was discussed. I was shocked to learn how much of our city's taxes are as a result of this province's downloads to the municipalities. Before we complain that our city councillors are not managing the city well, we should look to see how much Premier Doug Ford and his government are responsible for our tax burden. While someone may say taxes are taxes, what's important here is that we have control over our municipal taxes, and how we choose to spend them.
Although paying taxes is not something we like to do, they do provide us with a quality of life such as clean water, high-quality sanitation, beautiful parks, libraries, health services and safe roads etc.
Lets get behind our city councillors so that the needs of all people are addressed.
Chris Cardey, Hamilton
Seize the opportunity
Re: Ontario's new interest in renewable energy may be a mirage (Jan. 18)
The fact that the Ford government is green-spinning its commitment to renewables is on par for a cohort that thought that we could pave our way to prosperity in the Greenbelt.
I ask why seemingly mature, rationale adults (Ford et al) don't see opportunity when it spits at them. Climate change, threatening cataclysm on an unimagined scale, is as close as dawn tomorrow. Climate models from around the world are all converging. Put aside the humanitarian and ecological implications, why don't conservatives understand the opportunity? We know which horse is going to win, so why not bet on it?
Climate change will spare no one. Not China, not Canada, not Brazil. The next few decades will see panic emerge as the earth roils from massive injections of energy coming from runaway CO2 emissions. The transition out of hydrocarbons will become a global priority. When fires in the Amazon, Australia, Russia, Canada and Indonesia become inextinguishable, humanity will be desperate for "know how" in sustainable energy.
Now is the time to make our move and get out of gas-fired plants and old generation nuclear plants. With so many fiscal, financial, scientific and cultural levers this energy transition scenario is a canvas made for creative minds. An economic boom based on forging a living harmony with the earth. It's a great time to be alive if we are not afraid to look ahead. The children of future generations are imploring us. Are you listening Mr. Ford?
William Farkas, Hamilton
Call on the military for help
It was stated that one of the issues in using the James Street armouries as a warming shelter is finding someone to run it. How about the military? They should be able to handle such a situation and it might be good training for cadets and others that might be called upon to administer aid in Gaza or Ukraine in the near future. It would be one way the federal government can assist with housing issues without costing any extra money.
Dan O'Neil, Hamilton
Ford should be blamed
I don't understand why we aren't blaming Doug Ford and his government for their part in the housing crisis, the health-care crisis and the slow erosion of the middle class - not to mention all the other problems they are creating and doing nothing about (hello, education? protection of workers? autism programs?).
What has this provincial government done exactly to fix any of it? All Ford has done is make things worse (privatizing health care, which is proving to be more expensive, getting rid of caps on rents, which is having the effect of high rents, and doing virtually nothing to build affordable housing). Instead, everyone is blaming Justin Trudeau and his government for everything, even if it is a provincial responsibility! All Dougie is doing is making the rich richer and the poor poorer. It's not the Canada I know and love. It's really moving to that American-style of me, me, me and my, my, my. Ugh.
Natasha Huyer, Hamilton
His stories touched our hearts
Re: R-E-T-I-R-E-D, find out what it means to me: Jeff Mahoney signs off (Jan. 20)
Thank you for all the stories, big and small, that touched our hearts and gave a voice to the little people and their problems, accomplishments and celebrations.
Lynn Goruk, Hamilton
It's the end of an era
The end of an era. Thanks for the laughs, smiles and the tears. Your stories will be missed.
Marianne Talman, Hamilton
We're too nice to demand better
Re: Make Tim Hortons great again (Jan. 11)
How does an international chain, born in Canada, become a ubiquitous symbol for our penchant for the substandard? We only have to look as far as our polite nature. Canadians notoriously are apologists. The apologies started in our genesis, on the Plains of Abraham, where the victors apologized to the vanquished in the form of many concessions, giving birth to our weak federal system. Canadians are also sorry for wanting cultural content that is not created here, thereby permitting a government to censor our lives. Being polite is in our DNA. As the saying goes, "nice guys finish last." By being nice, we settle for the substandard - a vanilla culture with little heart - and poor doughnuts. Yet, we continue to line up for them. For some perspective, visit any Chic-fil-A (in the U.S.). There, you will be marvel at the quality of the food, the clever advertising and the impeccable service. The downside? You can't order the chicken in French or get a lame Boston cream. Sorry.
Dave Pasian, Hamilton
Pets have better protections
We are a civilized society that recognizes that humane treatment of our pets is not just recommended, but enshrined in our laws: "Canada's laws require that animals receive adequate shelter and care. Wilful failure to provide adequate shelter could lead to prosecution and a fine, jail sentence or prohibition from having custody of animals."
This quote is from the website of Ontario SPCA and Humane Society, specifically addressing heat and cold alerts.
At what point do we credit ourselves with being civilized when in fact, there are countless human beings continuing to live rough on our frigid city streets during cold snaps that experts caution will freeze exposed skin in a few short minutes?
It appears to me that we value our pets more than we value people. Isn't it time we had a law like this to protect humans as well? Maybe then our elected officials, at all levels, would be incentivized to be more creative in seeking solutions - possibly by reprioritizing other frivolous projects - to bring an effective end our homeless crisis.
Brenda Bianchi, Hamilton
CAPTION: Readers weigh in with their thoughts on the city's budget talks, a plan to spend $60 million on bike lanes and more.

Document 182 of 252

As education minister, Stephen Lecce has a lesson for students about protecting democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-education-minister-stephen-lecce-has-lesson/docview/2917776048/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+education+minister%2C+Stephen+Lecce+has+a+lesson+for+students+about+protecting+democracy&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How do we teach tolerance?
Can we educate and engage students on democracy? And arm them against demagoguery and disinformation?
Stephen Lecce knows what won't work - more of the same.
As Ontario's youngest-ever education minister, he took a fresh look at the same old high school civics course. And remembered how forgettable it was in his student days.
Today, election turnouts are in decline while racism and religious intolerance are on the rise - anti-Black racism, antisemitism, Islamophobia. Now, Lecce wants students to engage again - as he did this week at the Democracy Forum at Toronto Metropolitan University.
Turning to students in the audience, he asked for feedback on their recent high school experience in democratic engagement. The education minister offered an educated guess:
"Is it fair to say that for most of you, Civics and Careers was a 'bird course' - you showed up, you did the minimum, and you got like a 75 to 80 per cent?" Leece probed.
"Tell me if I'm wrong."
Heads nodded in agreement.
"So that's your experience - that was mine, and we're of a different generation. And the same is (still) true."
Which proves the point. Even student progressives in our audience, who might disagree on most issues with a Progressive Conservative cabinet minister, could agree that something needs to be done for democracy in our schools.
"What was the point of civics? It wasn't meant to be a spare, it wasn't designed to be a social experience, it was really designed to get young people motivated and interested in democracy, in their government, and to understand how to navigate government," Lecce argued.
Instead of a course "heavily dosed in theory," he wanted to teach students "how to be the agent of change" with real-world case studies. Survey data shows roughly one in four young people "have confidence" in the often unreliable information they get on social media, so he wanted them to gain "usable tools about how to understand sourcing and how to be a critical thinker."
The point isn't to tell students what to think. Just to make them think.
As a student, Lecce blazed his own campaign trail - first volunteering for electioneering with the Tories at age 13, later launching his own bid to be president of the student council at Western University. He won in a landslide.
Upon graduation, he worked in the office of then-prime minister Stephen Harper, rising to become his director of communications - spinning the mainstream media but also mastering social media. Hence his healthy skepticism, especially the excesses of hateful discourse (requiring a thick skin) and harmful disinformation (requiring open eyes).
His political journey - on campus, on Parliament Hill and at Queen's Park - was transformed by his work as a volunteer election observer in Tunisia's first free and fair elections in 2014, years after the Arab Spring implanted democracy in that authoritarian country. I asked him about that experience because I'd been there before as a foreign correspondent, covering the sham elections during Tunisia's time as a police state - when the opposition leaders I'd interviewed were later imprisoned.
"I remember seeing and working with new political party aspirants, people who are running (but) have never debated, never been in a television commercial, because this is not permitted in a dictatorial regime," Lecce told us. "The people I met there were so grateful because they longed for freedom," he recalled.
But after working with activists who are willing to die for democracy, it can be dispiriting to encounter so much indifference to democracy and ignorance of history at home. Hence his passion for reforming the civics curriculum, given the record low turnout - 43.5 per cent - in Ontario's last election.
"When I went abroad, I realized that we have a lot to fight for in this country with a lot to safeguard, a lot to protect ... and young people's belief that they're not reflected within their government."
As part of his pitch for teaching tolerance, Lecce has led the way in Holocaust education and informing students about the Holodomor. More than six million Jews died at the hands of Nazi Germany, and an estimated four million Ukrainians died from the starvation and repression of the old Soviet Union's Communist rulers.
"Civic education and history are actually really important ways we safeguard our democracy and our future," Lecce said. "The Holodomor, the Holocaust, these are brutal examples of political extremism ... some of the darkest moments in human history."
Students on social media are now exposed to disinformation that attempts to "normalize" Holocaust denial, which is why Ontario has shared its pioneering curriculum reforms with provinces across Canada. Schools need to educate and equip "civically engaged people who are motivated to stand up when the other person is oppressed."
For the minister, that has also meant stepping in when school boards have failed to rein in bigotry in their own bailiwicks - not merely in the classroom but the office - from anti-Black racism in York Region to Islamophobia in Peel.
"We did it immediately and swiftly and decisively, without apology, to send a signal culturally that the leaders, the directors, the school board, the trustees, the staff, the teachers ... that there would be zero tolerance."
Teaching tolerance and promoting pluralism starts in schools. But it also takes leadership at the top.
* * *
At our next Democracy Forum at TMU on Feb. 2, our guest will be federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh.
CAPTION: Education Minister Stephen Lecce speaks to journalists at Queen's Park on Aug. 25, 2023.

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Document 183 of 252

Did Ford's office know land swap details earlier than claimed?: A recently discovered internal email related to the Greenbelt scandal suggests premier's office 'was far more involved'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/did-fords-office-know-land-swap-details-earlier/docview/2917499613/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Did+Ford%27s+office+know+land+swap+details+earlier+than+claimed%3F%3A+A+recently+discovered+internal+email+related+to+the+Greenbelt+scandal+suggests+premier%27s+office+%27was+far+more+involved%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A recently discovered internal email related to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal suggests Doug Ford's office "was far more involved" in the controversial land swap than the premier has maintained.
The missive, obtained by the Opposition New Democrats through a freedom of information request, contradicts testimony given under oath to the integrity commissioner last year.
At issue is an Oct. 17, 2022, email exchange - on their private accounts instead of on government servers - between Patrick Sackville, then the premier's principal secretary and now his chief of staff, and Ryan Amato, at the time the top aide to then municipal affairs minister Steve Clark.
In a message from Amato to Sackville that day, the ministerial chief of staff shared a "list of criteria for removals" of 7,400 acres from the two-million-acre environmentally sensitive Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
These included "infrastructure services," such as nearby roads, gas and water mains, hydro lines, police and fire services, among other things.
It also listed "potential offsets" such as the Paris Galt Moraine "green space our government has decided not to move on" and "options to go larger depending on executive interest."
The timing of the email is at odds with testimony made under oath to J. David Wake, the integrity commissioner, for his Aug. 31 report on the Greenbelt land swap.
"Members of the premier's staff also told us they were not involved in the selection of properties," wrote Wake.
"Mr. Sackville said he did not discuss specific properties to be removed or removal criteria with Mr. Amato until the briefing that occurred on October 27, 2022," the ethics watchdog continued.
"Mr. Sackville recalled being first briefed about the Greenbelt project at a meeting facilitated by the cabinet office on October 27, 2022. He told me that he recalled the intentions of the policy were discussed and that it should be 'programmatic,' meaning that it should be something that could be repeated and made standard."
But that was 10 days after the Oct. 17 email in which criteria for removal of the lands from the Greenbelt were addressed. Clark announced the changes on Nov. 4, 2022.
"This email ... shows that the premier's office was far more involved than Mr. Ford claims in the selection of Greenbelt lands for removal," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
"(Progressive) Conservatives very much want their Greenbelt scandal to go away, but it is glaringly clear they can't hide from what they've done," said Stiles, who has asked Wake to "provide an opinion on whether it is consistent with Mr. Sackville's testimony to your office under oath."
"With each new piece of evidence, we are seeing more cracks in the narrative Mr. Ford and his insiders have been trying desperately to get people to believe - that Ford was in the dark about this scheme to carve up prime farmland and green space for land speculators," she said.
Last week, the integrity commissioner advised Stiles he would "need some time to review the evidence, not only of Mr. Sackville, but other witnesses to determine whether there was an inconsistency in the evidence."
"If I determine that there was an inconsistency, I will provide you with my opinion as to what, if any, significance might attach to it. I will get back to you as soon as I have had an opportunity to review the matter," Wake wrote her on Jan. 15.
Sackville declined to comment when contacted by the Star.
But the email was shared with the NDP by cabinet office lawyers as soon as the now chief of staff, who receives hundreds of messages daily, found and forwarded it.
"This record was just discovered upon deeper review," Sackville wrote on Dec. 20 to cabinet office general counsel Don Fawcett.
Two days later, it was handed over to the New Democrats, who had filed a freedom of information request months earlier.
"To ensure all necessary due diligence was exercised, Patrick Sackville ... conducted additional searches within his personal email account," wrote Janet Dadufalza, senior freedom of information manager in cabinet office.
"From this search, an email that is considered to be responsive to your request was found. Partial access is granted," she wrote the NDP on Dec. 22. But "access to the remaining record continues to be denied" because of cabinet privilege.
It is unclear why personal email accounts were being used to conduct official government business.
Amato resigned on Aug. 22. Clark, who remains the MPP for Leeds-Grenville-Thousand Islands and Rideau Lakes, quit Ford's cabinet on Sept. 4.
Through his lawyer, Kathryn Marshall, Amato declined to comment.
Ford has insisted he was out of the loop about the removal of 15 parcels of land from the Greenbelt that the auditor general estimated would be worth $8.28 billion to property owners. "I had nothing to do with the changes in the Greenbelt ... the auditor general cleared us, cleared my office, and the integrity commissioner cleared me and my office," the premier said Oct. 31.
In separate reports last summer, the auditor general and integrity commissioner found certain developers with Tory connections were "favoured" in an unusual process supposedly led by Amato.
On Sept. 21, an embattled Ford finally cancelled the land swap, which is now being investigated by the RCMP.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry," the premier said that day.
"I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise. As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes. We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives."
Other casualties of the imbroglio were Kaleed Rasheed, who was forced out as Ford's business minister and turfed from the Progressive Conservative caucus last September, and Jae Truesdell, who was Ford's housing adviser.
Rasheed and Truesdell were on a controversial 2020 trip to Las Vegas with Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Amin Massoudi, who was the premier's principal secretary at the time.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford listens as Steve Clark, thenOntario's minister of housing,speaks during a news conferencein Mississauga in August of last year.Cole BurstonThe Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Robert Benzie toronto star

Document 184 of 252

Alleged car theft ring operating in GTA busted, police say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alleged-car-theft-ring-operating-gta-busted/docview/2917498897/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alleged+car+theft+ring+operating+in+GTA+busted%2C+police+say&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Police say they have charged 11 people with close to 100 offences after a months-long investigation into an alleged car theft ring operating in the Toronto area and other parts of southwestern Ontario.
The announcement by York Regional Police comes a day after the federal government said it would convene political leaders, law enforcement and auto industry executives next month to try to slow the rise in auto thefts in most major cities.
York Regional Police say a series of searches carried out in multiple cities over the last two months led them to recover 52 stolen vehicles.
They allege the suspects were using residential areas to store vehicles that had recently been stolen in York Region and other parts of southwestern Ontario.
Investigators say the group was targeting high-end vehicles, with planned destinations as far away as Azerbaijan and Georgia.
The suspects are facing 96 charges collectively, including motor vehicle theft, trafficking in property obtained by crime, and possession of an automobile master key.
Federal Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc announced the plan for a national summit to address auto theft on Sunday, saying it would be an opportunity to "take action to deal with something that's increasingly becoming a concern in every part of the country."
LeBlanc said he had discussed the issue with Ontario Premier Doug Ford in recent days.
In a letter sent to LeBlanc in the fall, Ontario's solicitor general, Michael Kerzner, said the province has seen a "staggering increase" in violent vehicle thefts over the last few years. Citing insurance industry group Équité Association, Kerzner said vehicle theft was up by over 48 per cent in Ontario year over year.
CAPTION:

Document 186 of 252

Doug Ford's office fires back at claims of inconsistencies in Greenbelt testimony

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-office-fires-back-at-claims/docview/2917643521/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+office+fires+back+at+claims+of+inconsistencies+in+Greenbelt+testimony&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is downplaying NDP accusations that internal emails reveal inconsistencies in government Greenbelt testimony to Ontario's ethics watchdog.
"I don't know anything about it," Ford told the Star at the new Bombardier business jet factory at Pearson International Airport on Monday.
Through a freedom of information request, the New Democrats obtained an email exchange the opposition party charged contradicts statements given under oath to the integrity commissioner last year.
As first reported by the Star, the issue revolves around an Oct. 17, 2022 email - on private accounts instead of government servers - between Patrick Sackville, then Ford's principal secretary and now his chief of staff, and Ryan Amato, at the time the top aide to then-municipal affairs minister Steve Clark.
The email "shows that the premier's office was far more involved than Mr. Ford claims in the selection of Greenbelt lands for removal," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who asked the integrity commissioner to "provide an opinion on whether it is consistent with Mr. Sackville's testimony to your office under oath."
According to the integrity commissioner, Sackville had testified "he did not discuss specific properties to be removed or removal criteria with Mr. Amato until the briefing that occurred on Oct. 27, 2022."
But Ford's officials said Monday there were no inconsistencies nor any "net-new information" in the recently discovered 15-month-old email from Amato to Sackville.
"Out of an abundance of caution and as part of year-end due diligence, our office conducted additional searches to ensure adherence with proper record keeping practices," the premier's office said.
"Through this process, we found one document, which does not include any net-new information from what has been previously disclosed and mirrors what was being briefed on at that time," it continued.
"Regardless, we have proactively provided this document to all relevant parties, including the integrity commissioner, the auditor general and anyone who made a relevant freedom of information requests."
Indeed, the email, which discussed "criteria" for removing land from the Greenbelt, was handed over to the NDP on Dec. 22, two days after Sackville forwarded it to cabinet office lawyers.
"This record was just discovered upon deeper review," the chief of staff wrote Dec. 20.
Asked why personal email accounts were being used to conduct official business, Ford's office said the government is still working on addressing concerns raised in the Aug. 9 auditor general's report into the Greenbelt affair.
The auditor noted "forwarding government information to personal accounts is not appropriate because of cybersecurity concerns" and that "using nongovernment resources to conduct government business is unacceptable."
In response, the Tories are tackling all 15 of the recommendations made in the report that probed the removal of 7,400 acres from the two-million-acre environmentally sensitive Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area to build housing.
The most significant of those recommendations - cancelling the land swap - was addressed by Ford on Sept. 21 after a separate Greenbelt report on Aug. 30 by the integrity commissioner.
In their parallel probes, the auditor general and integrity commissioner found certain developers with Tory connections were "favoured" in a process supposedly led by Amato, who was Clark's chief of staff.
Amato, who has maintained he "acted appropriately," resigned on Aug. 22, while Clark, who remains the MPP for Leeds-Grenville-Thousand Islands and Rideau Lakes, quit Ford's cabinet on Sept. 4.
The premier has insisted he was not aware of how the 15 parcels of land were selected for removal from the Greenbelt, a rezoning change estimated to be worth $8.28 billion to landowners.
"I had nothing to do with the changes in the Greenbelt ... the auditor general cleared us, cleared my office, and the integrity commissioner cleared me and my office," Ford said Oct. 31.
But when he scrapped the land swap, which is now being investigated by the RCMP, he acknowledged "it was a mistake to open the Greenbelt."
"I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise," the premier said last September.
The scandal also led Ford to part ways with Kaleed Rasheed, who was turfed as public and business service delivery minister and from the PC caucus, and Jae Truesdell, who was the premier's housing adviser.
That's because Rasheed and Truesdell were on a controversial 2020 trip to Las Vegas with Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Amin Massoudi, Ford's then principal secretary.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Pinewood Toronto Studios on Jan. 17, 2024.

Subject: Testimony; Freedom of information

Title: Doug Ford's office fires back at claims of inconsistencies in Greenbelt testimony

Jan. 22: Hamilton's astronomical society a cultural and educational asset and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-22-hamiltons-astronomical-society-cultural/docview/2917305545/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+22%3A+Hamilton%27s+astronomical+society+a+cultural+and+educational+asset+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A cultural and educational asset
Re: Hamilton's astronomers reach for the stars, but trees keep them grounded (Jan. 15)
I am writing to express my appreciation for Kate McCullough's recent article regarding the treatment of the Royal Astronomical Society of Canada (RASC) in Hamilton. McCullough's well-written piece shed light on the City of Hamilton's treatment of RASC and the challenges faced by the Hamilton Observatory.
The article eloquently captured the struggles that RASC has endured, emphasizing the importance of the observatory as a cultural and educational asset for the community. McCullough's ability to convey the detrimental impact of the city's actions over the observatory and its dedicated members resonated deeply.
It is crucial for our communities to be aware of the challenges faced by organizations like the RASC and the potential loss of valuable resources such as the Hamilton Observatory. McCullough's article serves as a poignant reminder of the need for transparent communication and collaboration between local authorities and community groups dedicated to scientific and cultural pursuits.
In highlighting this issue, McCullough has not only brought attention to the mistreatment of RASC, but has also sparked a conversation about the importance of preserving and supporting such vital institutions. I commend her for her insightful reporting and hope that this article prompts positive change and a renewed commitment to fostering scientific and cultural endeavours within our community.
Andrew Blanchard, president RASC (Hamilton)
Discount change doesn't make sense
Re: Loblaw reducing discounts on food items nearing expiry (Jan. 15)
Rather than fall in line with competitors, wouldn't it be better to keep the 50-per-cent discount and have more people shop at their stores? Is Food Basics next to "fall in line" and reduce its 40-per-cent discount to 30?
Jackie Oke, Dundas

Re: Jan. 18 cartoon

Indeed, the country is broken, but why paint Pierre Poilievre with a black brush when he hasn't had a chance to straighten out this country? The cartoonist neglected to add corruption, division, patronage appointments, uncontrolled immigration policies and restriction of the principles of freedom to the depicted building structure.

This was unacceptable behaviour
Re: Hamilton cop treated gun 'like a joke, like a toy, like a prop' (Jan. 18)
A police officer is in a position of authority and should be an example of acceptable behaviour at all times.
Playing and dancing with a gun and pretending to shoot someone is both inappropriate and childish. Not following safety procedures by not using a pistol proofing station, pointing the gun at his former coach officer and pulling the trigger and laughing should be viable reasons for his termination.
He clearly does not understand and respect both the role of a police officer and the gun as a firearm. In acquiring a gun licence, it is reinforced over and over again that you never point a gun at anyone, even in jest. It's not a toy!
He should not only be removed from the police service, but also never be allowed to have a gun licence and own a firearm. The pictures in the Thursday's paper are insulting to all police officers everywhere.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
Where did the money go?
Re: One way to solve health-care crisis (Jan. 19)
It was so gratifying to read the commentary on health care in Ontario. This is truly a scandal to see our health care deteriorating!
The Ontario government has received billions from the federal government specifically for heath care. Remember the provincial health ministers meeting with the federal government? The result was to provide provincial governments with money for health care. What did Doug Ford do with all that money? The commentary clearly states the lack of action by the provincial government.
I have written to my federal MP, demanding an audit from Ontario on the money they received. I have not seen any improvement over the past two years. I still see six or more ambulances parked in front of St. Joe's.
The people of Hamilton and other municipalities worked together to prevent expansion into the Greenbelt - now, we need the same kind of massive protest against the provincial government on its lack of health care funding.
Roman Caruk, Hamilton
We need reputable news sources
Re: We need a radical new approach for the CBC (Jan. 19)
I would love to see the column by Peter McLeod republished and put on the front page of The Spectator. And maybe even underlining the section, "Canadians find themselves inundated by misinformation and exposed to deceptive campaigns sponsored by hostile foreign governments."
It would be such a shame for Canada to lose the CBC, along with so many other reputable news agencies and solid Canadian entertainment providers. This would not just be a shame, it could unfortunately result in us being at risk of false news and subterfuge, which so many people are already subjected to by not reading reputable newspapers such as The Spectator and believing everything that people like Pierre Poilievre spout using trumped up stories as scare tactics to try sway uninformed people.
Of course, the results could be devastating if people vote in another Conservative government that would dismantle valuable government agencies, slashing important environmental, social and health care, etc. legislation and their financial support.
Everyone needs to understand the importance of getting out to vote, understanding who they're voting for, educating themselves as to the people who are running and what they stand for, and voting for the correct people to align with what Canada really needs.
Kathleen Lentz, Hamilton
Donald's on a mission
It seems that Donald Trump, come hell or high water, is determined to be the next president of the United States. Despite the numerous charges brought against him and impending court cases, he has a strong following of voters and the Republican Party is unsure what to do with him.
So this November, do not be surprised if Trump is elected with a landslide victory. He wants the job at a time when most politicians are packing it in or those that should enter politics want nothing to do with political office.
It would take a person with the combined charisma of both JFK and Barack Obama to make a serious challenge to him. I see no one at present in U.S. politics that has this or wants to go nose-to-nose with Donald.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
Houthis just misunderstood?
Re: Canada out of Red Sea (Jan. 16)
Kudos to The Spectator for publishing this beautifully written, factual letter explaining that the misunderstood Houthis are merely attempting to blockade cargo vessels in the Red Sea and mean no harm whatsoever. Their weapons and claims to turn the Red Sea into a graveyard must be unfounded.
I agree with everything the Hamilton Coalition To Stop The War dictates and hates. In fact, I'd I consider renting my basement to Houthis, Hamas and ISIS individuals who find themselves persecuted. These Shia Islamist rebels would be welcome to eat my food, drive my car and meet my sister.
The intelligence of the coalition is so true, never persecute a good terrorist or pirate and show compassion for their numerous causes. Where can I buy a "Death to America" or "Get out of NATO" bumper sticker?
Thanks again to Ken Stone and his Coalition to End War. No one should lose their head over this. Should they?
Wray Brown, Dundas
How much evidence is needed?
Re: Trudeau breaks silence over Canada's position on Israel genocide allegations (Jan. 12)
"Our wholehearted support of the ICJ ... does not mean we support the premise of the (South African) case," said Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Foreign Affairs Minister Melanie Joly added, "the crime of genocide requires the intention to destroy a group because of their nationality, ethnicity, race or religion. Meeting this high threshold requires compelling evidence."
Is Gaza's cratered moonscape not enough "compelling evidence?" Can Trudeau and Joly not see 10,000-plus dead children, thousands more permanently disabled? Flattened neighbourhoods, hospitals, mosques and churches? Targeted journalists? A self-proclaimed siege on food, fuel, water and electricity? Two million displaced? Are these not enough? One wonders if our humanity and civilized society can ever recover.
Our leader's chilling silence and meaningless word games are unconscionable. Meanwhile, Palestinian children suffer in forgotten horror. The Creator must cry themself to sleep at night.
Nobuko and Scott McNie, Hamilton

Document 188 of 252

Doug Ford promising more help for housing in rural Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-promising-more-help-housing-rural/docview/2917304661/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+promising+more+help+for+housing+in+rural+Ontario&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford will allow more remote towns to share in the province's $1.2-billion "building faster fund" to speed up housing construction in rural Ontario.
Ford told the Rural Ontario Municipalities Association's annual conference in Toronto on Monday that he hopes even the smallest centres will take advantage of the new funds.
Announced last summer at the height of the Progressive Conservatives' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, the three-year $1.2-billion program is a sweetener that rewards cities and towns that try to meet the housing construction targets the province has imposed upon them.
While $120-million of the program had been set aside for rural municipalities that were assigned provincial housing benchmarks, smaller towns and hamlets without such targets had been excluded from tapping into that money.
"I know many small, rural and northern municipalities have been waiting patiently to hear how they can access their share of the fund," Ford told hundreds of delegates at the Sheraton Centre Hotel conference.
"Friends, let me assure you we will not leave these communities behind. We will soon set up an application-based process that will allow municipalities which have not been assigned housing targets to access the building faster fund," said Ford.
"Successful applications will receive funding or important infrastructure projects like roads, water or wastewater that will help get more homes built in your communities," he said.
"With this infrastructure rural communities will have the tools to build for the future."
Under the program, unveiled last August at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference, towns and cities that reach 80 per cent of their target each year are eligible for funding based on their share of the government's overall goal of 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
The city of Toronto, for example, has a housing target of 285,000 new homes, which it is on track to exceed, having achieved 146 per cent of the goal so far.
But, mindful that "not every municipality has reached" their housing-construction target, Ford conceded "in many cases a lack of infrastructure is partly to blame."
"So moving forward our government will take unused funds from the building faster fund --potentially hundreds of millions of dollars -- and make them available for housing-enabling infrastructure investments that all municipalities can apply for."
Ford used his speech to tout the actions his Tory government is taking to boost the economy in remote communities, including "building a home-grown EV supply chain connecting critical minerals in northern Ontario to clean steelmakers in Hamilton and Sault Ste. Marie to automakers and battery manufacturers across Ontario."
"Over the past three years, we've attracted more than $28 billion in auto and electric vehicle investments, including Volkswagen's $7 billion investment -- the single largest auto investment in Canadian history -- to build North America's largest battery plant in St. Thomas."
The premier also said that thanks to "our nearly $4 billion, multi-year investment, we're on track to bring high-speed internet access to every region in Ontario by the end of 2025, because in today's digital world, everyone needs access to reliable Internet."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford said he knows small municipalities have been waiting to hear how they can access their share of the fund.

Subject: Strategic materials; Rural areas; Infrastructure; Internet access; Scandals; Municipalities

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Full text: Officer's behaviour
was unacceptable
Hamilton cop treated gun 'like a joke, like a toy, like a prop' (Jan. 18)
A police officer is in a position of authority and should be an example of acceptable behaviour at all times.
Playing and dancing with a gun and pretending to shoot someone is both inappropriate and childish. Not following safety procedures by not using a pistol proofing station, pointing the gun at his former coach officer and pulling the trigger and laughing should be viable reasons for his termination.
He clearly does not understand and respect both the role of a police officer and the gun as a firearm. In acquiring a gun licence, it is reinforced over and over again that you never point a gun at anyone, even in jest. It's not a toy!
He should not only be removed from the police service, but also never be allowed to have a gun licence and own a firearm. The pictures in the Thursday's paper are insulting to all police officers everywhere.

Document 190 of 252

Mad at Justin Trudeau? Tuning him out? The prime minister says he hears you - and he gets it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mad-at-justin-trudeau-tuning-him-out-prime/docview/2916901747/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mad+at+Justin+Trudeau%3F+Tuning+him+out%3F+The+prime+minister+says+he+hears+you+-+and+he+gets+it&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau gets a lot of advice. It goes with the job of being prime minister.
But who does Trudeau seek out when times get tough, as they have so often over the past year?
"Define 'tough times,' because that sounds like Monday to me," Trudeau said when he sat down with me for a wide-ranging, one-on-one interview last week. "Every day is tough. We're running a country in a really, really complex time."
As 2024 gets under way, and as Trudeau prepares to huddle with his cabinet and the larger Liberal caucus to map out the year ahead, there's no let-up in sight for those tough times.
Canada is in the midst of a housing and affordability crisis, which Trudeau's critics - and there are many of them - are all too eager to pin on him. The prime minister has just come out of a round of year-end interviews in which he was asked repeatedly why he was staying on in the job.
Conservatives and their leader, Pierre Poilievre, are surging in the polls. A war in the Middle East is reverberating on the ground in Canada, dividing communities and Trudeau's own Liberal caucus. Ukraine is about to head into its third year under an illegal Russian invasion. Meanwhile, Canada's largest ally and neighbour, the United States, is flirting with the prospect of putting Donald Trump back in the White House, with all the collateral damage that could bring to Canada and the world.
Trudeau has said repeatedly that he doesn't intend to walk away from his job or any of these challenges, so we didn't waste a lot of time in this interview talking about whether he would - or should - pack it in.
We spoke instead about what all these tough times for Trudeau and his government reveal about larger strains in the fabric of Canadian society right now, and what, if anything, an eight-plus-year-old government can do about them.
Trudeau is concerned about where the Canadian consensus on immigration is headed, for instance, as people start to make connections between the influx of newcomers and demands on an already overloaded housing market and health-care system.
"I've long said that one of the most important responsibilities of any Canadian prime minister of government is to protect that consensus," he said. "We are facing a real challenge around that right now."
This is why Poilievre has been talking about tying immigration levels to housing, and why Immigration Minister Marc Miller has been talking about putting more stringent conditions on the inflow of foreign students and temporary workers.
However, Trudeau added, "One of the challenges around getting more housing built is having more construction workers. And one of the challenges we have around wait times in our health-care system is getting more health-care workers. We know that shutting down immigration would not even be a theoretical answer to that."
In other words, it's complicated.
Unfortunately for Trudeau, and progressive politicians and governments of all kinds these days, when times are tough, people aren't in the mood to hear that the problems have complicated causes or solutions. That's true about immigration, but also inflation and the cost of living or foreign interference in our democracy.
Last spring, Trudeau made a remarkable admission - or, at least, one you don't hear prime ministers make that often. He acknowledged that there are Canadians out there who aren't going to take his word for anything, no matter what he says.
So in this interview, I asked him what he was going to do about that - or whether, in fact, there was anything he could do about it.
"First of all, there are people who don't listen to anything any politician is going to say, and that is certainly increasing," he said. It goes hand in hand, Trudeau believes, with the fragmentation of the media landscape and the decline in traditional journalism. "If you don't believe politicians, you don't believe a prime minister, you don't believe all the assembled newspapers and media outlets that are recording what the prime minister is doing. It becomes harder and harder to break through."
A thoughtful long-time Liberal said to me last year that Trudeau and his team had a full grasp on all the antagonism out there toward them. How could they not? Trudeau is dogged by those angry protests everywhere he goes, and the prime minister talked about that at length when we sat down last year for an interview.
This Liberal, however, was worried Trudeau doesn't appreciate the apathy and indifference toward the government and how people may be tuning out whatever they say after eight long years in office.
"I certainly get the indifference," Trudeau said. "The challenge of the Liberal party as a centrist, a centre-left party, is that we don't have those fringes within the party on the far left, or on the far right, that are mobilized and activated about politics all the time."
It's the old dilemma of being in the middle - it's hard to fire people up about standing in the centre. Trudeau does believe, however, that Liberals are up against something relatively new in this climate, which he calls opinion-as-identity politics.
"I don't think that was a feature too much of other times in politics--where what you think about something actually creates the circles and the people that you actually associate with, and it defines who you are."
He also believes that there's a worldwide shift under way within conservatism and on the right, both in Canada and on the larger world stage. Traditional conservatism leaned a lot on respect for institutions and expertise and authority, Trudeau said, but "this particular brand of conservatism that seems to be spreading ... doesn't seem to be much about respecting institutions or protecting institutions ... It's almost anti-institutionalism; it's anti-expertise, it's anti-authority."
Is that what is happening in conservative-led provinces such as Saskatchewan and Alberta, where premiers such as Scott Moe and Danielle Smith have been openly musing about opting out of paying taxes or federal legislation they don't accept? Trudeau isn't sure it stretches that far.
"I don't think it's actually about the Constitution or division of powers. I think it's about trying to capture the mood of people ... It's always been the easiest thing for any provincial leader to do, is blame Ottawa, right? ... It's ever been thus, and it always will be."
But refusing to collect the carbon levy, as Saskatchewan is promising?
"Let's see where we actually go with that," Trudeau says. "Canada is a country of the rule of law."
Many prime ministers, especially the longer they serve in office, say they pay no attention to the media or the polls. Jean Chrétien, for instance, said he never watched the news before bed and Stephen Harper made no secret of his antipathy to the parliamentary press gallery.
Trudeau says he still is a consumer of the media, although he counsels all newcomers to politics to pay little attention to social media.
"But it really is important to be aware of how people are feeling out there ... because it does impact your ability to do the job - not how you feel about the job, but how you're able to actually succeed in the projects that you're putting forward."
He said he is not too fussed about all the talk of his unpopularity. "I think there's a lot of people who are just rightly grumpy at the world right now ... I think there's all sorts of things that are happening that are difficult, (so) it's got to be the prime minister's fault."
Trudeau also notes that he's had a lifetime of training in ignoring personal attacks - and flattery. "I grew up in a situation where I had to brush aside people who hated me because they didn't like my dad. But I also had to learn how to brush off people who loved me, because they loved my dad. Neither of those are necessarily real."
He says he was recently at a ceremony in Richmond Hill to commemorate the victims of the Ukraine International Airlines jetliner that was shot down by Iran four years ago. One long-time Liberal approached him and asked Trudeau how he was feeling. "It's tough," he said. The Liberal agreed, citing the polls and all the attacks on the prime minister.
"No," he said. "It's tough to be here with grieving families."
Another Monday, as he might put it. "The polls, the noise of politics ... that's just par for the course. It doesn't bug me. It's a tough time for Canadians."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with the Star's Susan Delacourt in Guelph on Jan. 12, 2024.

Subject: Institutionalism; Politics; Prime ministers; Immigration

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada; Ukraine

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: Mad at Justin Trudeau? Tuning him out? The prime minister says he hears you - and he gets it

Document 191 of 252

Meet Marnie Cluckie: Hamilton's new city manager

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/meet-marnie-cluckie-hamiltons-new-city-manager/docview/2916700667/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Meet+Marnie+Cluckie%3A+Hamilton%27s+new+city+manager&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton's brand-new city manager Marnie Cluckie got an intense introduction to top-of-mind issues in Hamilton this week as dozens of residents gave feedback on the 2024 municipal budget.
That was a helpful dive into her job, says Cluckie, whose last post was top as Niagara-on-the-Lake's top bureaucrat.
During Tuesday's public-feedback session - with a proposed spending plan that involves a 7.9 per cent residential tax hike - speakers focused on policing costs, the housing crunch, affordable transit and rising costs.
That gave her a "flavour of some of the challenges and opportunities and what the community is feeling, some of the pressure points that they have," Cluckie said.
"There's a whole bunch of issues converging at once for the community," she added, "and the fact that there's a balancing act that's going to need to be struck to address some of those challenges."
Cluckie, a Norfolk County resident whose son studies at McMaster and whose husband works in Hamilton, has returned to Steeltown, where she worked with the city from 2005 to 2015.
She had a range of management roles, including in facilities, customer service and recreation, before overseeing strategic planning and capital delivery. Cluckie also worked in Niagara Region and Halton Region before landing in tiny and touristy Niagara-on-the-Lake.
Cluckie, who started Monday, takes the helm of a $2.4-billion corporation with roughly 8,000 workers during an era of strained municipal finances, an affordability crisis, an infrastructure backlog, as well as political battles with Premier Doug Ford's government over housing, funding and the environment. She takes over for Janette Smith, who retired in October.
Mayor Andrea Horwath, who introduced Cluckie to kick off Tuesday's budget meeting, noted her most recent experience as Niagara-on-the-Lake's chief administrative officer.
"But more importantly, Marnie brings new energy and a new approach. She loves this city. She's worked here ... She cares about government and the role that it plays in people's lives and also cares about excellence and finding the best way to do things for the residents that we all serve."
Here's what Cluckie said in a chat with The Spectator this week about stepping into her new role (the interview has been edited for length):
What drew you back to Hamilton?
"I love Hamilton. I have a passion for public service in general, but Hamilton is home in many ways. I love the rawness of Hamilton; the way the community engages; the passion that they have for a number of things, whether that be supporting the Ticats or the culture and music scene, as well as the way that the community pulls together when there are issues."
How will your job be different here?
"In some ways, it's very different, and in some ways, it's very much the same ... But I think the experience is scalable. We're still dealing with things like development, and how to be more transparent and open and responsive. We did an overhaul in terms of customer service and making sure that we were more customer-centric and really responding to community needs. So some of the themes are very similar, although it's much bigger here, so I think I'll need to, of course, rely on the great team, the talented group that's here for their advice and probably modify my leadership style in certain ways. I've heard Hamilton called the smallest big city. It really has that community feel where everyone knows someone else, the six degrees of separation concept and so I think that piece is a part that I really love about Hamilton that small-town feel but it's abs a big city."
Why do you think you got the job?
"I think I'm well aligned with what council wants right now, with the mayor and council striving to achieve, so they have some core strategic priorities, which I believe are the right ones for this point in time for the City of Hamilton, but I am passionate about this city. I am really interested in doing things differently, about cutting red tape and really making it customer-centric so we're responding to what the community wants. Sometimes you'll see municipalities that are more internally focused, and I think this council really wants us to see our decisions driven by what the community wants by us making sure that we're being responsive to the diverse needs, that we're meeting accessibility requirements, that we are meeting people where they're at and I think that I have that to offer."
What are Hamilton's biggest challenges?
"I said earlier, it's a bit of a balancing act right now, and top of mind is budget, but it comes with meeting the affordability requirements, addressing the housing crisis, helping to create a healthy and well community. There's so many pressures right now for people, shelter, rising rates of inflation. So I think those are the things that are critical that I would be working with staff and council to focus on."
What have you done since Monday?
"So I've done a lot of meeting and learning. As they say, it's like drinking from a firehose right now, but that is my objective right now: is to listen and learn and absorb, so that once I've gotten some of these insights and perspectives, I can come up with an action plan to meet the strategic plan and help to deliver the services of the City of Hamilton. So I'm taking some time to do that, also recognizing there's some urgency around the issues that we're faced with, so that has to happen quickly."
What's your role in provincial relations?
"So I think that that's a critical aspect with this role in combination with the mayor's office and council members, but we need to develop those strong relationships so that our voice can be heard, so that we're working collaboratively to find solutions because things don't happen just at the municipal level, right? The budget, our solutions to complex problems - all of that has to be done in concert and admittedly that doesn't always happen, but I think my role ... building relationships internally, externally, and with other levels of government are important to me and something that I've always been passionate about."
Do you want to change anything about city operations?
"I can't say that it is or isn't yet because these are early days for me, and I think we're lucky that we do have such a talented team here and dedicated workforce that's absolutely committed to their work, but I think that there's an opportunity here from my first look to be very customer-centric ... I also think there's the opportunity to make sure that we're being as open and transparent as possible and very responsive. So I think from my initial first blush, that's an area that I would be dedicated to improving."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Marnie Cluckie, the former chief administrative officer in Niagara-on-the-Lake, started as Hamilton's new city manager Monday.Marnie Cluckie, Hamilton's new city manager, says she's "done a lot of meeting and learning" since Monday.

Subject: Customer services; Councils; Managers; Budgets

Document 192 of 252

Why did Doug Ford's government defeat a bill on sexual harassment?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-did-doug-fords-government-defeat-bill-on/docview/2916700609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+did+Doug+Ford%27s+government+defeat+a+bill+on+sexual+harassment%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government voted down a bill that would allow local politicians found guilty of harassment to be kicked out of office - but with more than 200 municipalities now supporting the idea, there is renewed pressure for the province to act.
The Women of Ontario Say No, a non-partisan advocacy group, continues to garner more local council endorsements and is planning a meeting with staff from the office of the minister of municipal affairs and housing in the hopes of changing the government's mind.
"We're talking about people that have the most power in communities held to a lower standard than any other workers in Ontario," said Emily McIntosh. The Barrie-area resident is part of the advocacy group that last March had 30 local councils supporting such legislation, and now has 201.
The group will also have some high-profile help at next week's annual conference of the Rural Ontario Municipal Association, where both NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner will wear the group's new "Accountability Now" T-shirts or speak to the issue. Newly elected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is also onside.
McIntosh thought the issue would be a no-brainer, given a number of high-profile cases of harassment involving local politicians, "and I thought of course the government would move on it because it's (non-partisan) and it's a human rights issue. It's basic workplace safety, and it's about instilling public trust in publicly elected positions. But that's just not been the case at all."
Liberal MPP Stephen Blais (Orléans) has twice tried to introduce a private member's bill, and at one point had the unanimous support of the legislature, but last May his bill, the Stopping Harassment and Abuse by Local Leaders Act, didn't pass.
Blais - who served on Ottawa city council with former councillor Rick Chiarelli, who was found to have harassed female staffers over several years - was proposing that all municipal councillors fall under workplace harassment and violence policies, allowing them to be fired and even barred from running in the next election for violations.
His bill had the support of former premier Kathleen Wynne, who addressed councils in Richmond Hill, Orangeville and Mulmur about the importance of the legislation.
But in the legislature, PC MPP Charmaine Williams (Brampton Centre), who is associate minister of women's social and economic opportunity, noted when she was a councillor "allegations happened in my city as well."
"Our government has been absolutely crystal clear that we will not tolerate any workplace harassment or discrimination of any kind, full stop. We're not going to tolerate this activity by any councillor in the province," she added. However, "this bill wants to make integrity commissioners more powerful than an appointed judge. If you're accused of something illegal, you get a trial, but this bill wants to replace that and this bill wants to allow the integrity commissioner to remove an elected official without a trial."
Blais had said his bill addressed such concerns by stipulating that a judge would need to approve any integrity commissioner decision before ousting an elected official.
A spokesperson for Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra said last week that the government continues to believe "in the local democratic process and we trust voters to hold local politicians to account at the ballot box. Councillors and heads of council must carry out their duties as elected officials in an ethical and responsible manner."
But the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, Ontario Big City Mayors and the Rural Ontario Municipal Association have all agreed help is needed on code of conduct compliance.
Crombie, who was mayor of Mississauga before being elected leader of the Ontario Liberals, said the bill "would have strengthened municipal policies and processes around workplace harassment."
She called it "deeply upsetting to see the Ford government refuse to support this important initiative. Unlike the Conservatives, Ontario Liberals will pass the principles contained in Bill 5 into law when we form government."
McIntosh said she's heard from municipal workers and even deputy mayors who tells stories of being harassed.
"The problem is there's no incentive to report this behaviour because at the end of the day, the person can retain their position of power," McIntosh said. "We know that this legislation would be preventative in nature ... It's about creating environments where there is truly zero tolerance for violence and harassment."
Stiles, who has posted a video on social media in support of the legislation, called it "outrageous to think that any woman is going to work afraid of being harassed" or feeling unsafe.
"We need accountability, and we need it now."
CAPTION: Emily McIntosh is shown at the Barrie City Hall on March 18, 2023.

Subject: Legislation; Councils; Politics; Politicians; Accountability

Title: Why did Doug Ford's government defeat a bill on sexual harassment?

Meet Marnie Cluckie, Hamilton's new city manager: Official expects 'balancing act' to meet big challenges

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Meet+Marnie+Cluckie%2C+Hamilton%27s+new+city+manager%3A+Official+expects+%27balancing+act%27+to+meet+big+challenges&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/
