Document 194 of 252

Here's what's truly awful about protesting at politicians' homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-whats-truly-awful-about-protesting-at/docview/2916720181/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what%27s+truly+awful+about+protesting+at+politicians%27+homes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a rare day when politicians from rival parties can agree on anything in these troubled and intractable times.
But for one fleeting moment, MPs from all sides of the aisle were on the side of Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly.
Not over Canada's Middle East policy - they still disagree bitterly. All were agreed, however, on when - and where - those disagreements are utterly offside and absolutely off limits.
Joly's Montreal residence - not a constituency office, nor the House of Commons - was targeted for an organized protest last week after her home address was publicized on promotional posters. That so many protesters would converge on her private home - not the House where she is held accountable in the daily question period - was enough to rally so many MPs, right and left, to her side.
They knew a line in the sand had been crossed at the snow-covered lawn outside Joly's home.
Protesters had defiantly set up camp - erecting a pop-up canopy tent (courtesy of the Concordia Student Union), hoisting banners, unfurling flags, deploying megaphones and chanting anti-Israel slogans.
No mere political protest against a public official, this was an invasion of Joly's personal space. And an intrusion into the private lives of her partner and neighbours - none of them involved in the blood sport of political conflict in Canada, nor implicated in the bloodshed of military conflict abroad.
Does it matter what their protest was about? Palestinian rights, Israeli wrongs, Hamas right or wrong, genocide by either side? Can a cause be so undeniably right and one's righteousness so indisputable that the ends justify the means - whatever means necessary?
"This is appalling," NDP foreign affairs critic Heather McPherson wrote on social media. "People do not have to agree with politicians and elected representatives, but to harass them at their private homes is completely and utterly unacceptable."
"This is intimidation not protest," added Conservative deputy leader Melissa Lantsman. "Just like protesting in a predominantly Jewish neighbourhood in Toronto was. Find an office, a consulate, an embassy, a government building, a public square to protest all you want for what you believe in - not a private home."
Much has already been said about the spillover of the Israel-Hamas war onto the home front here in Canada. I've written before that it transgresses every Canadian norm to hide behind tortuous rationalizations while targeting Jewish-owned restaurants, Toronto's downtown Jewish Community Centre, or the Jewish neighbourhood Lantsman was referring to beside Avenue Road and the 401.
I covered the Israeli-Arab conflict on the ground long enough to know that the world needs more Canada, not more of the Middle East's worst excesses - and excuses. The more that Canadians emulate those hatreds, the more we will normalize dehumanization and demonization, antisemitism and Islamophobia.
But that's another argument for another day. On this day last week, a cabinet minister's personal space was violated.
Oh, but vilification is not violence, the protesters insist. And so elected representatives who willingly sign up to be heckled in the House are unwillingly hounded at home - with family and friends, children and elders treated as collateral damage.
Make no mistake, there is no shortage of protests for which protesters can persuade themselves that vigorous public debate extends to a politician's private driveway or sanctuary. The righteous can rationalize their rightness, left right and centre.
The focus on Canada's foreign minister is merely the most recent in an endless series of transgressions. Former premier Kathleen Wynne was targeted by Black Lives Matter activists who showed up on her driveway.
The former Etobicoke home of the current premier, Doug Ford, has attracted protesters who thought he was fair game after hours - including one person who pulled a knife and had to be disarmed by an alert OPP protective detail.
"Every single day, my friends, there's protesters outside my house," Ford said, noting that his neighbours "didn't sign up for this."
Former health minister Christine Elliott faced protests at home by anti-vaxxers who faulted her for pandemic-era restrictions. So, too, did Education Minister Stephen Lecce.
The rise of far-right populism and decline of progressive politics can be explained by a lack of good ideas, but also an absence of good leaders. If we have a crisis of thoughtful leadership today, it's because politicians are exposed to so much abuse - online and now on their doorstep - that no one would think to give up their home life.
This isn't just about protecting politicians, it's about safeguarding our democracy. For the best way to chase worthy people out of politics is to make it not worth their while.
CAPTION: Foreign Affairs Minister Melanie Joly speaks with reporters before a cabinet meeting in Ottawa on Dec. 5, 2023.

Subject: Demonstrations & protests; Politics; Politicians; Diplomatic & consular services

Location: Middle East; Canada; Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Title: Here's what's truly awful about protesting at politicians' homes

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 19, 2024

Section: politic s/political-opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Language of publication: English

ProQuest document ID: 2916720181

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-whats-truly-awful-about-protesting-at/docview/2916720181/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 195 of 252

Jan. 19: Officer needs to learn respect, tear down the leaning tower on Ferrie and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-19-officer-needs-learn-respect-tear-down/docview/2916356183/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+19%3A+Officer+needs+to+learn+respect%2C+tear+down+the+leaning+tower+on+Ferrie+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Officer needs to learn respect
Re: Hamilton cop, who twice misused gun, fighting to keep job (Jan. 17)
A police officer who does not respect a gun is one who should definitely not be a police officer. The article does not state how the colleague reacted when he pulled the trigger. Were they aware that it was not loaded? To acquire a gun licence in Ontario requires two long intellectually challenging days of study, followed by written and oral exams each day. Indeed, they continually reinforce that one is never to point a gun at another person, even in jest. Dancing with a gun in a cruiser while under probation shows that he does not understand that a gun is a lethal weapon, and shows no respect for both the gun and the position of a police officer. If this immature and careless attitude does not lead to his termination, then something is desperately wrong with the system.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
Some things never change
Re: Heard of price-fixing? Say hello to discount-fixing (Jan. 15)
So, Loblaw is ending its 50 per cent expiring-grocery discounts in order to "align itself with the competition." In other words, in the interests of increasing corporate profits, it is clawing back a few dollars from shoppers, many of whom are already hard pressed to make ends meet. I'm reminded of Adam Smith's observation in "The Wealth of Nations" that "people of the same trade seldom meet together, even for merriment and diversion, but the conversation ends in a conspiracy against the public, or in some contrivance to raise prices." Some things never change.
Jim Young, Dundas
Loblaw just doing business
Re: Heard of price-fixing? Say hello to discount-fixing (Jan. 15)
It appears Sylvain Charlebois is once again questioning Loblaw's motivation over its decision to reduce the 50 per cent discount on some items to a constant 30 per cent and suggests the rationale behind this decision is "quite perplexing and an issue for the Competition Bureau to investigate ... otherwise, customers may find similar strategies at other grocery stores. If this isn't collusion, it appears very close." How, if other stores followed suit seeing the benefit, is that collusion? To me, it seems like a good, logical business practice.
As consumers, if we don't like a company policy we certainly have the ability to shop elsewhere more to our liking. Rightly so, the grocers do have an obligation to feed the nation, but also have an important obligation to reward the shareholders with appropriate dividends from these profits, those who have invested wisely in these companies and all other companies, and without those rewards there wouldn't be any industry. It's as simple as that!
The profits earned are also heavily taxed to support the benefits that we as citizens all receive to enhance our way of life and we should never lose sight of that.
Ian Powrie, Burlington
Tear down unsafe house
Re: Residents still displaced by unsafe home in Hamilton's North End (Jan. 17)
Perhaps it's time the city demolishes this structure so the neighbours can get back home. The owner of this building clearly does not care about the repercussions of his selfish approach to the situation.
Bob Takast, Hamilton
The leaning tower on Ferrie
Hamilton showed Doug Ford we don't need him to get houses built. We don't need those pesky rich developers either. The houses may fall over, but we'll get 'em built.
Red Taylor, Hamilton
Rev up renewable energy production
Re: Doug Ford's flip-flop on wind turbines won't undo all the damage he caused (Jan. 10)
When Doug Ford came to power in 2018, many Ontarians were eager to welcome the new government. Kathleen Wynne, and Dalton McGuinty before that, were crucified for privatizing Hydro One, and, as Martin Regg Cohn points out, the Conservatives immediately tore up wind and solar power contracts. I often wonder if there were penalties to be paid for not honouring these agreements. I don't recall reading about it. When wind turbines were already providing much of the energy needed in northern Europe, many Ontarians were complaining about the noise made by these modern windmills, refusing to have them built in their neighbourhoods. My guess is that the opponents of renewable energy are the same voters in favour of building Highway 413, so long as it is not "in my backyard": Let's speed it up, never mind the negative effect it will have on our environment and the neighbouring farmland.
Gudrun Boehm-Johnson, Ancaster
Concerned for health of youth
Re: Poilievre owes queer people answers (Jan. 12)
In the column, the author mentioned that two motions passed by Conservative convention delegates in 2023 "if enacted under a Poilievre government - would restrict the freedom of trans people and ban transgender youth from accessing health services that are supported by every major medical association in North America."
Although not mentioned in the column, my understanding is that the motions include a ban on medical (hormone-related treatments that delay puberty) and surgical intervention for children experiencing gender dysphoria.
I am not sure what encompasses the "health services that are supported by every major medical association in North America," but it is my understanding that WPATH (World Professional Association for Transgender Health) has expressed concerns about puberty blockers and cross-sex hormones because they may be irreversible, cause infertility, prevent accumulation of necessary levels of calcium and impact brain development, since teenage brains are still wiring and unwiring.
If any of what I have written is incorrect, I hope that someone will correct me. It seems to be an issue that is regularly evolving, and I am not taking a side, but rather I am trying to be better informed, because of my concern for the long-term well being of our youth.
Mark Melanson, Hamilton
Facts missing from letter
Re: Focus put on wrong leader (Jan. 15)
Perhaps the letter writer could enlighten us on precisely what "false narrative, outright lies, exaggerations and misinformation" Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre is supposedly spreading across the country and all at taxpayer expense.
I am surprised The Spectator printed a letter of such innuendo with no actual facts.
Les Farrington, Ancaster
CAPTION: A police officer who does not respect a gun is one that should definitely not be a police officer, a reader writes.

Document 196 of 252

Organic waste collection options give Norfolk council food for thought: With provincial deadline looming, rural county mulls how best to divert food scraps from the landfill

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/organic-waste-collection-options-give-norfolk/docview/2916356004/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Organic+waste+collection+options+give+Norfolk+council+food+for+thought%3A+With+provincial+deadline+looming%2C+rural+county+mulls+how+best+to+divert+food+scraps+from+the+landfill&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Keep up the good work - but not on our dime.
That was the answer Norfolk County council gave residents who asked for $7,200 to expand an organic waste collection program in a Port Dover subdivision that keeps 600 pounds of food scraps out of the landfill every week.
Residents of Dover Coast - a 200-home lakeside condominium community catering to urban retirees - currently put their organic waste in three large bins that are picked up weekly by a private company out of St. Catharines as the county does not have a green bin program.
The waste is fed into an anaerobic digester that produces compost while capturing methane and carbon dioxide for future use in generating renewable energy.
Since partnering with Davidson Environmental in September, Dover Coast residents have diverted more than five tonnes of organic waste, Elaine Anderson of the subdivision's Greening Our Community committee told council during budget talks last week.
"One small garbage bag is what we're collecting in a week (per household), because everything's going into the organic waste (bin) or into the recycling program," Anderson said.
The group's waste-diversion efforts started in 2020. With condo rules not allowing backyard composters, they sent food scraps to a local livestock farmer for use as animal feed, diverting more than 10 tonnes of kitchen scraps annually.
Collecting food waste - as well as biodegradable plastic packing, wooden single-use items and paper products - for pickup by Davidson over a full year would divert 25 tonnes and save taxpayers up to $4,000 in haulage and disposal fees, according to a report from county staff.
In exchange for the county paying for bin pickup for the year, the committee promised to share data on participation and diversion rates and meet with other community groups interested in starting a similar program.
"We're very fired up about this program," Anderson said.
Despite glowing words from county staff and CAO Al Meneses' contention that the data gathered for a "relatively minor" cost could be "helpful" when planning Norfolk's own organic waste collection system, council balked at the optics of funding the project.
"I'm not going to support spending taxpayer money to subsidize a private condo corporation," said Coun. Chris Van Paassen.
While calling the project "very commendable," Coun. Linda Vandendriessche noted data on municipal green bin usage was already widely available.
Van Paassen questioned the value of usage date from a group whose members are already dedicated to reducing their carbon footprint.
"I hope they carry on," Van Paassen said before joining a majority of councillors in denying Anderson's request.
Countertop composting
Council did approve spending $21,500 to subsidize the cost of purchasing 150 countertop composters made by a Canadian company called Food Cycle Science, as part of a pilot project to gauge if home composting is a viable option for residents.
According to the company, FoodCycler units take eight hours to turn food scraps - including bones, shells and paper towels - into compost, reducing the size and weight of the organic material by about 85 per cent.
The idea is to have less garbage to collect and put in the landfill, cutting emissions from trucks and reducing greenhouse gasses released by rotting food.
The units come in two sizes - 2.5 litres and five litres - and plug into the wall, working quietly and without odours, the company says.
Over their five- to seven-year lifespan, each FoodCycler unit is estimated to divert at least two tonnes of organic material from landfill.
With help from a federal grant that supports food waste reduction, the company has sold discounted FoodCycler units to more than 100 Ontario municipalities running pilot projects.
The smaller units normally retail at $500, while the larger ones cost $800.
Norfolk council agreed to contribute $100 per unit, with residents to pay $150 or $300, depending on the chosen size.
In exchange for the discounted FoodCycler unit, which is theirs to keep after the pilot ends, participants must agree to track their usage data for 12 weeks.
County staff estimate 150 households using a FoodCycler unit for a year could divert up to 37.5 tonnes of waste from landfill and save Norfolk up to $7,000 in garbage collection and disposal costs.
Andrew Grice, Norfolk's general manager of environmental and infrastructure services, said the county will use five FoodCycler units in house to verify the company's claims about hydro usage and waste conversion.
"It may be a viable option for organics in Norfolk County. It may not," Grice told councillors. "We want to make sure it works before we make an investment."
Mayor Amy Martin sees promise in FoodCyclers as a way to cut costs without having the county pay for third-party services, as would be the case with the Dover Coast plan.
"And it's the environmentally conscious thing to do," Martin said.
Organic solutions
The debate comes as Queen's Park requires Norfolk to start collecting organic waste next year, as part of the province's overall goal of diverting 50 per cent of Ontario's waste.
Food scraps and other organic material make up about 40 per cent of the 14,500 metric tonnes of curbside garbage the county picks up annually.
Without an organics collection program, some 5,800 tonnes of green waste end up in the landfill.
These figures do not account for food scraps turned into compost, which is common in Norfolk's rural areas but less so in the urban centres.
The province is not mandating how municipalities are to collect organic waste, Grice told The Spectator. With Norfolk's current curbside garbage collection contract expiring in 2026, staff will present council with an array of options, including hybrid models that take Norfolk's diverse geography into account.
"We may have a green bin program for our urban settings and maybe not in our rural settings," Grice said.
With participation in Dover Coast's green bin program at 70 per cent of households and "rising each week," Anderson said her community will continue diverting waste from landfill even without Norfolk's financial help.
"We believe in it so dramatically," she said. "We will not stop."
J.P. Antonacci's reporting
is funded by the Canadian
government through its
Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com
CAPTION: Dover Coast residents Patrick Mercuri, left, Elaine Anderson and Ed Tremain could not convince Norfolk County council to fund an organic waste collection program in their Port Dover subdivision.Norfolk County must implement an organic waste collection program by 2025 as part of the province's goal of diverting 50 per cent of Ontario's waste from landfill.J.P. Antonacci MetrolandMetroland file photo

Full text: Officer needs to learn respect
Hamilton cop, who twice misused gun, fighting to keep job (Jan. 17)
A police officer who does not respect a gun is one who should definitely not be a police officer. The article does not state how the colleague reacted when he pulled the trigger. Were they aware that it was not loaded?
To acquire a gun licence in Ontario requires two long intellectually challenging days of study, followed by written and oral exams each day. Indeed, they continually reinforce that one is never to point a gun at another person, even in jest. Dancing with a gun in a cruiser while under probation shows that he does not understand that a gun is a lethal weapon, and shows no respect for both the gun and the position of a police officer. If this immature and careless attitude does not lead to his termination, then something is desperately wrong with the system.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle

So, Loblaw is ending its 50 per cent expiring-grocery discounts in order to "align itself with the competition." Jim Young, Dundas

If this isn't collusion, it appears very close." How, if other stores followed suit seeing the benefit, is that collusion? To me, it seems like a good, logical business practice.
As consumers, if we don't like a company policy we certainly have the ability to shop elsewhere more to our liking. Rightly so, the grocers do have an obligation to feed the nation, but also have an important obligation to reward the shareholders with appropriate dividends from these profits, those who have invested wisely in these companies and all other companies, and without those rewards there wouldn't be any industry. It's as simple as that! The profits earned are also heavily taxed to support the benefits that we as citizens all receive to enhance our way of life and we should never lose sight of that.

My guess is that the opponents of renewable energy are the same voters in favour of building Highway 413, so long as it is not "in my backyard." Let's speed it up, never mind the negative effect it will have on our environment and the neighbouring farmland.

Document 199 of 252

Ford government slammed for Ontario's use of temporary nurses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-slammed-ontarios-use-temporary/docview/2916477892/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+slammed+for+Ontario%27s+use+of+temporary+nurses&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pressure is building on the Ontario government to take action against temporary agencies that charge hospitals high rates for replacement nurses amid a worsening staff shortage and growing hospital deficits.
Citing "price gouging" among the concerns raised in the provincial auditor general's latest annual report, Liberal MPP Adil Shamji has asked the office to conduct a value-for-money audit on the impact of temporary staffing agencies in health care.
This is on top of NDP calls for Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones to cap rates charged to hospitals by the agencies and scrap Bill 124, which capped annual wage hikes for nurses and other public sector workers at one per cent for three years and has been blamed for high attrition rates.
"Temporary staffing agencies are holding our health-care system hostage," Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician, said Thursday.
"They engage in practices like rapid price gouging, with some agencies charging three to four times artificially suppressed public wages. We see dynamic surge pricing, the way you might see it with Uber and it's very busy on a Friday night."
The auditor's report in December found hospitals and nursing homes were overreliant on temporary nurses.
"Worsening staffing shortages have forced hospitals to hire agency nurses at significantly higher hourly rates than permanent staff ... hospitals are spending millions of dollars on agency nurses," the report warned, noting the lack of a cap on rates agencies can charge.
In a statement, the health minister's office said agencies provide important flexibility.
"Hospitals have always had the tool to use agency nurses and did under the former Liberal government. In fact, the proportion of agency nurses has decreased from 3.8 per cent in 2017 to under three per cent in 2022-23," Jones spokesperson Hannah Jensen said.
But with costs of agency nurses rising, Shamji said a value-for-money audit by the auditor general would "reveal the full scope of the financial exploitation and catastrophe that Doug Ford is enabling."
"Cautious estimates suggest that they cost us at least $175 million more than necessary," he added.
The health minister's office said the government remains ready to work with hospitals and the Ontario Hospital Association on finances, with measures to date including a four per cent funding increase to the sector and $44 million to ease emergency room wait times.
"This includes communicating to the OHA and hospital corporations across the province that our government would provide financial support to hospitals facing financial restraint," it said.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford's government should pass legislation capping pay for temporary agency nurses at 10 per cent above hospital rates, follow British Columbia's example of setting a nurse-patient staffing ratio to ensure quality care, and drop its appeal of Bill 124, which a provincial court struck down as unconstitutional.
"Things are only getting worse," Stiles said in a statement. "The premier needs to stop pretending this crisis doesn't exist."
CAPTION: Health Minister Sylvia Jones speaks in Toronto on Jan. 16, 2023.

Subject: Workforce planning; Emergency medical care; Price gouging; Nurses; Labor shortages

Business indexing term: Subject: Workforce planning Price gouging Labor shortages

Document 200 of 252

Ontario's new interest in renewable energy may be a mirage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-new-interest-renewable-energy-may-be/docview/2916060565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+new+interest+in+renewable+energy+may+be+a+mirage&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It looks like Premier Doug Ford might be ready to take some steps to get with the program when it comes to renewable energy.
The man who tore up more than 750 renewable energy contracts right after taking office is now ready to have the province rejoin the rest of the world in reaping the benefits of low cost solar and wind power.
But looks can be deceiving.
Ontario's current plan to secure 5,000 megawatts (MW) of renewable power has a couple of fundamental flaws.
The first problem is that the 5,000 MW target is largely a mirage, and may do little to move the needle on renewable energy use in Ontario. That's because the Independent Electricity System Operator's (IESO) procurement proposal is more about recontracting existing solar and wind projects than building new ones. Any existing project can be considered if it adds 20 per cent more capacity.
Owners of existing solar and wind projects are unlikely to just walk away when their contracts end. Instead, they may change out older solar panels for more powerful new ones, or replace older wind turbines with larger or more efficient ones. A 20 per cent improvement is not a high bar for qualifying existing projects for new contracts, and we estimate that there are enough projects reaching the end of their existing contracts to completely absorb the 5,000 MW allocated by the IESO.
With the 20 per cent improvement in capacity taken into account, this would result in only 850 MW of truly new renewable energy capacity, which would add an underwhelming 10 per cent to Ontario's current wind and solar energy supplies.
The second problem is that while the province uses smoke and mirrors to keep renewable energy development in check, it is racing ahead with plans to add a huge amount of polluting gas-fired power to its grid right in the middle of our growing climate crisis.
The Ford government's insistence that only gas is up to the job of meeting peak demand for electricity is just as outdated as its previous views on renewable energy.
There are many, many ways to meet peak demand without gas - from deploying local rooftop solar, to trading power with Quebec which still has a large summer power surplus. Paying electricity users to shift demand from peak to off-peak periods, using smart technology to make this process seamless, is another way to deal with short-lived spikes in electricity demand on hot summer days.
These options are all cheaper and less climate damaging than building new gas plants. Of course, storing renewable power for use in peak periods has also become easier and more cost effective, using everything from stationary batteries, to Quebec's hydroelectric reservoirs, and batteries in EVs.
It's no coincidence that while the Ford government keeps a tight leash on renewable energy growth, it is holding the door wide open for expensive and time-wasting nuclear projects, with plans to build 6,000 MW of entirely new nuclear capacity at the Bruce and Darlington nuclear stations.
If you don't believe climate change is a problem, waiting 15 to 25 years to bring more low-carbon power online is a reasonable way to reward powerful energy sector special interests, even if it costs three times what you could be paying for solar and wind projects that can be ready in months instead of decades.
If we really want to lower our climate impacts and electricity bills, and create a reliable and resilient power system for an increasingly challenging future, we need to join the rest of the world in rapidly developing wind and solar projects. Offshore wind power in the Great Lakes alone could meet all of Ontario's electricity needs.
That's why we are calling for Ontario to follow the lead of the 130 national governments, including Canada, that have endorsed the COP28 climate conference's call for the world to triple its renewable energy generation capacity.
Renewable energy's time has come, and that's something even Doug Ford can no longer deny. Ontario was once a leader in developing green energy. Now our challenge is to catch up. We can do it by tripling our wind and solar supplies by 2035.
CAPTION: We need to join the rest of the world in rapidly developing wind and solar projects. Offshore wind power in the Great Lakes alone could meet all of Ontario's electricity needs, Jack Gibbons writes.
CREDIT: Jack Gibbons

Subject: Wind power; Electricity; Alternative energy sources; Energy industry; Climate change; Renewable resources

Business indexing term: Subject: Energy industry Renewable resources

Document 201 of 252

New interest in renewables may be a mirage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-interest-renewables-may-be-mirage/docview/2915912945/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+interest+in+renewables+may+be+a+mirage&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It looks like Premier Doug Ford might be ready to take some steps to get with the program when it comes to renewable energy.

Offshore wind power in the Great Lakes alone could meet all of Ontario's electricity needs.

Renewable energy's time has come, and that's something even Doug Ford can no longer deny. Ontario was once a leader in developing green energy. Now our challenge is to catch up. We can do it by tripling our wind and solar supplies by 2035.
Jack Gibbons is chair of the Ontario Clean Air Alliance.

Document 202 of 252

'It's easy for Doug, because he's rich': MPPs haven't had raises since 2008 - and some see that as a problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/easy-doug-because-hes-rich-mpps-havent-had-raises/docview/2916167212/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+easy+for+Doug%2C+because+he%27s+rich%27%3A+MPPs+haven%27t+had+raises+since+2008+-+and+some+see+that+as+a+problem&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPPs, who have not had a pay raise in almost 16 years, will see their salaries frozen for the foreseeable future.
But the continuing freeze is raising questions about attracting the best and brightest to provincial politics if candidates can earn more federally or municipally.
Premier Doug Ford's government has confirmed there will be no change to the wages of provincial legislators, who last had an increase in 2008.
"We don't have any plans to lift the freeze," said Andrew Kennedy, a spokesman for Attorney General Doug Downey, who oversees the pay legislation.
That means MPPs, who earn significantly less than Toronto city councillors or members of Parliament, will continue to fall behind their counterparts at other levels of government who receive annual raises.
Former Liberal premier Dalton McGuinty froze compensation for MPPs, ministers and opposition leaders in 2009 during the global financial crisis.
McGuinty said at the time it was a bid to "lead by example" and promote restraint while Queen's Park was dealing with public-sector unions seeking wage increases.
"In some ways, those of us within the public sector have been sheltered from the full ravages of this private-sector recession," the then-premier said 15 years ago.
The base pay for an MPP remains $116,550 - that's the equivalent of $83,400 in 2008 dollars, according to the Bank of Canada.
If MPPs' salaries had kept pace with inflation, they would earn about $163,000 this year.
McGuinty's initial one-year freeze was extended in 2010, and again in 2012.
His Liberal successor, premier Kathleen Wynne, continued the practice until she was defeated by Ford's Progressive Conservatives in 2018.
While sources told the Star the Tories considered raising MPPs' pay after that election, it was felt that would not be politically palatable.
Similarly, the governing party quietly looked at a hike after its successful 2022 re-election campaign, but again backed off.
"It's easy for Doug, because he's rich," groused one Tory insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
That's a reference to Ford, who makes $208,974 as premier, being the multimillionaire scion of a wealthy family.
Provincial ministers are paid $165,851 while NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who heads the Official Opposition, makes $180,886. Tory MPPs who serve as parliamentary assistants take home $133,217. Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who is not an MPP, is being paid $185,000 by her party.
One MPP, who acknowledged they personally could afford to take a steep pay cut to run for office, said there are problems with not paying competitive salaries.
"You're either attracting people who are already well-off or people whose time is worth $116,550 a year," said the member, noting politicians at the federal and municipal level received annual increases.
Indeed, under city law, Mayor Olivia Chow, who makes $216,116, and city councillors, who earned $128,346 last year, are in line for 3.5 per cent raises because their pay is tied to inflation.
Similarly, MPs, whose base pay was $194,600 last year, will receive an increase in April. MPPs' salaries were originally supposed to be tied to federal members' compensation, paying them 75 per cent of their counterparts in Ottawa. But now, an MPP makes less than 60 per cent of an MP's base.
Also getting bumps are Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who makes $389,200, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who earns $287,400 as leader of the Official Opposition. Federal ministers are also paid $287,400 while Liberal MPs who serve as parliamentary secretaries take home $213,400.
But NDP MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto Danforth) said there is simply "no appetite" at Queen's Park to boost members' pay.
"People are hurting - and the thought that, when their salaries are stagnant or dropping, ours go up just makes people incredibly angry," Tabuns said Wednesday.
"I don't see how you address that in a way that doesn't make people angry," said the veteran MPP, who was first elected in 2006.
Tabuns said he's "not surprised" that Ford's Tories, like previous Liberal governments, are skittish about tackling pay.
"Again, just the frustration and anger that people have about it. We're a level of government that's relatively close to people and they see it and that would be a major headline," he said.
As things stand now, MPPs would only receive an increase if the province, which has a budget deficit, balances its books.
After an expected $2.1-billion surplus in 2021-22 would have automatically triggered a $25,575 hike in MPP pay to $142,125, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy quickly amended the law to prevent that from happening as inflation was rising in September 2022.
One person firmly convinced MPPs deserve a raise is the integrity commissioner, the independent ethics watchdog.
In his 2021-22 annual report, J. David Wake warned "continuing to tie the lifting of the freeze to achieving a balanced budget is problematic for all MPPs."
"After the next election, I respectfully suggest that the government consider lifting the freeze or re-establishing an independent arm's-length process to review MPP salaries, similar to the one my office was involved in from 2001 to 2007," said Wake in 2022.
Some 18 months after that election, his concerns have not been addressed.
CAPTION: Ontario Attorney General Doug Downey holds a news conference at Queen's Park in Toronto on Oct. 24, 2023.

Amazon MGM snaps up prime studio space near Toronto's waterfront for film production

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/amazon-mgm-snaps-up-prime-studio-space-near/docview/2915667138/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Amazon+MGM+snaps+up+prime+studio+space+near+Toronto%27s+waterfront+for+film+production&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amazon MGM is leasing prime studio space near Toronto's waterfront for its burgeoning film production.
News of the multi-year deal with Pinewood Toronto Studios, a sprawling property located on Commissioners Street, was announced Wednesday morning with Premier Doug Ford speaking to the crowd.
"Amazon MGM's long-term commitment to new studio space in Toronto is a tremendous vote of confidence in our province and our workers," he said in a statement.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow was also on hand.
The deal includes use of about 160,000 square feet of five state of the art stages and workshops in the Portlands area of the city.
This is a developing story.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow.Premier Doug Ford and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow were on hand for the announcement on Wednesday morning.

Document 204 of 252

Pierre Poilievre is putting a new face on the Conservative party. Here are some of the people who want to help

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-is-putting-new-face-on/docview/2915667094/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+is+putting+a+new+face+on+the+Conservative+party.+Here+are+some+of+the+people+who+want+to+help&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A prominent conservative commentator wants to run as a Conservative MP in the next federal election, adding to the names being bandied about for who might govern alongside Pierre Poilievre if he wins.
The party currently holds 117 seats, and would need to win at least 172 to form a majority government. Who could fill out those spots is a question coming up more and more often in political conversations as polls suggest the Conservatives are on the road to power.
On Wednesday, the Star has learned, high-profile columnist Sabrina Maddeaux will announce she's seeking the party's nomination in the GTA riding of Aurora-Oak Ridges-Richmond Hill, one of the seats the Tories lost to the Liberals in 2021.
Maddeaux, whose work has appeared in the National Post and elsewhere, told the Star she's decided to try for office to give voice to a cohort - voters under the age of 40 - with which she says the Tories are already gaining traction.
"People continue to be surprised by polls that show millennials and Gen-Z stampeding to the right because of the outdated stereotype that conservatism isn't 'cool,'" said Maddeaux, 35, who grew up in the riding.
"But you know what's really not cool? Not being able to pay rent, buy groceries, or start a family because of Liberal policies that divided the country into economic winners and losers, with younger Canadians deliberately chosen as the disproportionate losers."
Polls point to younger voters - who were crucial to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals coming to power in 2015 - shifting to the Conservatives, as Poilievre keeps his message largely focused on housing and the cost of living.
While Maddeaux still needs to be selected by party members to carry the Tory banner, she's not the only one of her generation hoping to ride a youthful wave of conservative support into office.
An upcoming byelection in Durham will see Jamil Jivani, 36, try to hold onto the seat for the Conservatives following last year's resignation of former party leader Erin O'Toole.
Like Maddeaux, Jivani's conservative outlook and media profile have seen him elevated to a position of prominence in the conservative movement: She nabbed a rare one-on-one interview with Poilievre last fall, while Jivani earned a spot on the party's convention stage.
The Ivy League graduate turned community advocate and broadcaster told thousands of party members he feels his dreams are being dashed under Trudeau's Liberal government, noting he's a renter who can't afford a home.
"I feel the housing crisis in this country on a very personal level and so do many of my future constituents," Jivani told the party delegates.
If Maddeaux and Jivani are successful, they will stand as examples of how Poilievre is rebuilding the party since he launched his leadership bid nearly two years ago.
His campaign brought hundreds of thousands of new party members into the fold, creating a new class of volunteers and donors who continue to see the Tories outpace their rivals when it comes to raking in the cash.
After overhauling the party's inner workings, Poilievre's next step is to find like-minded Conservatives to run under his banner in the next election.
While the notion of finding "star" candidates is part of that conversation, more than one Tory insider has made the point to the Star that to Poilievre, candidates who might already have some public profile aren't as important as those who are willing to do the grunt work it takes to get elected.
That was the message sent late in 2023 when rumours flew that the Conservatives were courting Quebec Finance Minister Eric Girard as potential "star candidate."
As La Presse first reported, Conservative insiders shot down the notion that Girard would get a red carpet rolled out for him just because of who he was, particularly given that he and Poilievre are not aligned on policy ideas like eliminating the Liberals' carbon pricing program.
Poilievre has billed the coming campaign as a "carbon tax election," in which he'll focus on the Liberals' signature policy of a consumer price on carbon. The carbon levy, Liberals argue, is essential to help people curb their use of greenhouse gas emitting fuels; for Poilievre, it is ineffective and too costly.
He made that point crystal clear during an event last week in Northern Ontario, where the Conservatives have already chosen Gaétan Malette to run against NDP MP Charlie Angus in Timmins-James Bay.
There, the Conservatives unveiled an early sign - literally - of what a Poilievre campaign will look like: the party has purchased a billboard featuring Poilievre and Malette together on one side under the banner, "Axe the carbon tax," and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and Angus on the other, under the banner, "Hike the carbon tax."
Among those scouting candidates for the Tories is a mix of longtime party insiders and regional volunteers. While there have only been a few nomination votes so far, the pace is expected to pick up as the new riding boundaries - and in some cases new ridings - come into play later this spring.
The party declined to comment on nominations, saying they are an internal party matter.
While recruiting political newcomers is one avenue, not all of those seeking to run for Poilievre are new to elected life.
In Edmonton, former MP James Cummings is expected to try for the nomination in his old riding, which he lost to the Liberals in 2021.
Alberta is also getting three new seats out of boundary redistribution, providing an opportunity for three new Conservatives in that stronghold.
In B.C., the Conservatives are hoping both to pick up seats and win back ones they previously held. Former MP Nelly Shin is trying for a comeback there to win the nomination in her old stomping ground.
Another well-known conservative personality on the roster for a B.C. riding is Aaron Gunn, who has secured the nomination in North Island-Powell River, after a failed effort to run for leadership of a provincial party and a long career as an advocate for conservative causes.
In 2021, the People's Party of Canada took 4.6 per cent of the vote in the riding, possibly hiving off enough support from the Conservatives to allow the NDP to hold the seat.
The Conservatives want the riding, and it's not the only one they hope to flip by bringing back voters who turned away from them in 2021 in favour of the PPC, in part because of the party's stance on COVID-19.
In Kitchener South-Hespeler, the PPC captured 6.7 per cent of the vote in 2021 and the Tory candidate at the time only lost by two points to the Liberals.
Come the next election, the Conservative candidate will be Matt Strauss, a former interim medical health officer, whose pushback on some COVID-19 control measures made him a lightening rod for criticism during the pandemic.
Meanwhile, in York Centre, the Conservative candidate will be Roman Baber, the former Ontario MPP ousted from the Progressive Conservative caucus by Premier Doug Ford after they clashed over COVID-19 measures. He went on to run for leadership of the federal Conservatives in the contest won by Poilievre.
The Tories nearly won the seat in a 2020 byelection, and now Baber is seeking to turn his past victory in the riding provincially - he had wrested it away from provincial Liberals - into one that would see him defeat federal Liberal cabinet minister Ya'ara Saks.
How soon he - and other candidates - will get the chance to test their mettle against incumbents remains unknown. The next election might not be held until 2025.
CAPTION: National Post columnist Sabrina Maddeaux interviews Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in a video posted to YouTube on Nov. 8, 2022.

Full text: Guess I'm not a true Canuck
Re: At 60, make Tim Hortons great again (Jan. 15)
As someone who avoids Tim Horton's like the plague, I absolutely loved Vinay Menon's article on Tim Hortons. He hit the nail square on the head and was hilarious! I often say to my U.S. cousins that I am probably not a true Canadian as I don't go to Tim Hortons for coffee or watch hockey. Pass on the Dutchie, thank you.
Paul E. Casey, Ancaster
Thanks for the tough love
Re: At 60, make Tim Hortons great again (Jan. 15)
Vinay Menon well done, again! While I am sipping my excellent Keurig blend of coffee, I cringe at the thought of all the people in their cars waiting for their morning brew of dish water! Even the dark roast is not flavourful, nor robust (even though I think they have given me their regular bland brew and have gotten my order wrong again). It seems their only focus is bringing back the iconic doughnuts and forgetting to improve their coffee. Yes, the coffee tastes like suffering, so please ease our suffering and improve your brand! Thank you Mr. Menon for your tough love! It is always appreciated!
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
No one called it gourmet
Re: At 60, make Tim Hortons great again (Jan. 15)
Dear Mr. Menon, not to be negative but you sir sound like snob. Timmies isn't gourmet coffee, nor does it purport to be. I consider myself a bit of a coffee connoisseur, but still enjoy a coffee from Timmies and even McDonald's. I find your rant a bit hyperbolic regarding something as simple as a cup of coffee and wonder how it merited almost a half page in my newspaper. Chill!
Liz Chapple, Hamilton
Esthetics of little concern
Re: Does affordable housing have to lack creativity? (Jan. 16)
I would like to share my thoughts on Margaret Shkimba's column discussing Darko Vranich's proposal to the city. While I agree with her point that "beggars can't be choosers," I believe the emphasis on elements like trees, balconies and innovative designs, which may contribute to higher building costs, is irrelevant in the context of responding to a legacy gift that includes 131 affordable housing units.
For individuals currently residing in tents within our community, the esthetics of the building are of little concern. It is commendable that Mr. Vranich is exploring the possibility of a legacy gift to the city. Our city has a positive history with philanthropists like David Braley and Charlie Juravinsky, who embraced similar acts of generosity for the benefit of the city they loved.
To foster a community that is receptive to such philanthropy, we should strive to be a city that is easy to do business with, rather than one that continuously puts up roadblocks. I sincerely hope that Mr. Vranich perseveres and finds solutions that enable the realization of this impactful project.
Glenn Gibson, Hamilton
Thanks for the insight
Re: Does affordable housing have to lack creativity? (Jan. 16)
Margaret Shkimba, thank you for your insight into the VF (Vranich Fold) and his very generous, planned but not acceptable, contribution to kick-start the relief of Hamilton's homelessness epidemic. Here we are again. There is no free lunch, we all have heard that story before.
But is it not part of the collective responsibility of council and staff to listen, negotiate, improve and suggest options to move this project forward to a slightly more doable level? It appeared that council, rather than be put into a position of responsibility, has thrown the baby out with the bath water one more time. Without a "consultant" in sight to pass the buck to, our elected officials are taking their favourite path, the path of least resistance, and have said thanks but no thanks! This is the answer to progress? Somebody please show leadership!
Philip Winer, Hamilton
Taxes? Lets talk income
Re: Using reserves to ease tax hike doesn't hide this council's spending (Jan. 16)
Rattling off tax increase numbers is meaningless unless compared to income increases, which also build one on top of the other.
Like many conservatives, Scott Radley has only one big story, the incessant screeching about taxes. Also, like many conservatives, he believes his opinion is the final word on what is essential and what is not. Thankfully, he is incorrect. It is not the final word.
If he actually cared about Hamiltonians, he would urge the provincial government to tie all income-support programs to the consumer price index (at the federal level, CPP, OAS and GIS payments are all tied to the CPI), to institute a more progressive income tax system, and to make it easier for workers to form unions. He would also encourage non-unionized workers to unionize.
In reality, he seems to care only about banging on about taxes in a place that pays less taxes than most countries in the OECD and, consequently, takes worse care of its most vulnerable citizens.
Michael Blythe, Brant County
Pollutants prohibit dumping
Re: Dump snow in the lake (Jan. 15)
Montreal and surrounding cities and towns used to blow snow into trucks and dump it in the St. Lawrence until they were convinced that it was full of road salt and various other pollutants. We don't dump Hamilton's snow in the lake or harbour for the same reason.
David Fawcett, Hamilton
CAPTION: "Timmies isn't gourmet coffee, nor does it purport to be. I consider myself a bit of a coffee connoisseur, but still enjoy a coffee from Timmies and even McDonald's," a reader writes.

Document 206 of 252

We're teaching a system that has brought us to the brink

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/were-teaching-system-that-has-brought-us-brink/docview/2915525658/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We%27re+teaching+a+system+that+has+brought+us+to+the+brink&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the fall of 2019, my then 26-year-old son started a graduate degree at McMaster.
His program included teaching assistant responsibilities and he mischievously told me when he started he wanted to use the position to reinforce the stereotype that universities were hotbeds of socialist indoctrination.
If this was the conventional wisdom, he reasoned, then he was prepared to use what little influence he had, to bring his left-wing agenda to bear on a small group of naive, unsuspecting undergraduates.
I'm not sure how successful he was.
The year was disrupted by pandemic restrictions, and I suspect he spent more time grading papers and leading tutorials than pontificating socialist rhetoric.
But I was reminded of his antics when I read about the lawsuit filed against McMaster and its student union by a group alleging the university's environment was "relentlessly menacing and hostile."
Notwithstanding my son's impishness, if one accepts the premise of the lawsuit, one is easily led to believe that universities like McMaster are hubs of counterculture conduct and need to be reigned in.
The thread has been picked up by conservative politicians, who see themselves as front-line generals in the culture wars.
Shortly after taking office in 2018, Doug Ford's government enacted a vague policy to encourage open and respectful debate on college and university campuses.
It also explicitly prohibited "hate speech, discrimination, and other illegal forms of speech," even though these were already covered by provincial statute and the Criminal Code.
At the federal level, Pierre Poilievre has threatened to make grants to post-secondary institutions conditional on their upholding academic freedom and free speech.
It all sounds very reasonable. Obviously, the targets of initiatives like these are the scruffy grad students like my son, and the pampered, tenured faculty that dominate academic life in this country. Or so our conservative politicians want us to believe.
It's more likely Messrs. Poilievre and Ford are shamelessly virtue signalling to their base.
While there is little dispute that faculty members and graduate students are overwhelmingly inclined to self identify as either liberal or moderate, it is less clear that the political identity of the instructors has had any influence of the voting patterns of students over the past six or seven decades.
If it had, we would not have had 40-plus years of neo-liberal, laissez-faire economic policy dutifully practised by both Liberal and Tory governments.
Instead, it looks like it's just the opposite. Or at least asymmetrical. If there's a problem with our post-secondary institutions, it's that programs in business and economics instruct thousands of students on the merits of an unregulated capitalist system.
A system that has brought us a housing crisis, existential climate change and wealth and income inequity.
Need proof? Look no further than the first building you see as you enter the McMaster campus from Sterling Street. Looming immediately to the north is the DeGroote School of Business, named for transport magnate Michael DeGroote.
It's doubtful the thousands of impressionable students who have been educated in these hallowed halls have undergone socialist indoctrination as Mr. Ford and Mr. Poilievre would have you believe.
The same is true for economics instruction. While it's been several decades since I studied the discipline, by all accounts it remains wedded to teaching the virtues of classical economics.
McMaster is not an outlier. Business schools all across the country are named for philanthrocapitalists, from the Rotman School at the University of Toronto to the Lazaridis School at Laurier. These men would not lend their names nor donate their fortunes to schools that might undermine the system that served them so well.
My son's pursuit was ultimately futile. He simply graduated and moved on with his career. What remains firmly in place at our institutions of higher learning, however, is an unencumbered corporate influence that sustains a system that has brought us to the brink.
Tyler Firth lives in Hamilton.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Tyler Firth;

Subject: Teaching; Students; Colleges & universities; Indoctrination; Economics

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities Economics

Document 208 of 252

A problem of their own making

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/problem-their-own-making/docview/2915522562/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+problem+of+their+own+making&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Jan. 1, the government of Saskatchewan removed the federal carbon tax from natural gas bills in the province.
Natural gas is what most residents of Saskatchewan use to heat their homes. This was done to match the federal government's same actions in exempting home heating oil from the carbon tax.
Home heating oil is the main source for heating homes in Atlantic Canada - a longtime Liberal stronghold, while Saskatchewan is a Liberal wasteland. The decision by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal government to exempt home heating oil was blatantly political.
If the Liberals are to have any chance of remaining in power after the next federal election, they must have support from the Maritimes. And polls are showing their support there is lagging, so they made this move to shore up that support.
Now let's be clear. This move by the Saskatchewan government is illegal. The federal law imposing the carbon tax has been upheld by the Supreme Court of Canada. The question now is, what does the Trudeau government do about this move by Premier Scott Moe and the Saskatchewan government?
In Canada, the federal government has the authority to pass laws related to taxation, the Criminal Code, etc. Provinces generally administer and enforce those laws. For example, if you rob a bank here in Hamilton, you will be violating the federal Criminal Code, but you will usually be arrested by the local Hamilton police and tried by an Ontario court.
But what happens if a provincial government refuses to enforce federal law or breaks federal law? There are some examples in Canadian history of this happening.
In 1973, Dr. Henry Morgentaler was acquitted by a Quebec jury of performing illegal abortions. His acquittal was appealed by the Crown, and the Quebec Court of Appeal overturned that acquittal and substituted a sentence of 18 months in jail. In 1975, Morgentaler was again acquitted by a jury on additional charges of performing illegal abortions, but he again was returned to jail. In 1976, after being released from prison, he was again charged and again acquitted.
Quebec then informed Ottawa it would not enforce federal abortion laws. The government of then prime minister Pierre Trudeau took no action in Quebec to enforce the law that was in place at the time. (For the record I am very pro-choice and supported Dr. Morgentaler. I raise this though, as at the time he was breaking the law of the land.)
In December 2022, the Quebec government introduced legislation to make swearing allegiance to King Charles III optional for members of the provincial legislature. The legislation basically states Section 128 of the Constitution doesn't apply to Quebec. Now, no province has the power to unilaterally amend the Constitution. This law passed by Quebec is unconstitutional and thus illegal. But the Trudeau government has taken no action to uphold the Constitution.
On a slightly different note, the Quebec government passed two laws (Bills 21 and 96) that violate the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, but exempted them from court challenges by using the "Not Withstanding Clause." Again, the Trudeau government took no action.
The reality is the current federal government (and that of Pierre Trudeau almost 50 years ago) has always turned a blind eye when Quebec refuses to enforce or even breaks the law. They have set a dangerous precedent by doing so. For if the Trudeau government was to try and enforce the law against Saskatchewan, after their inaction against Quebec, I dare say we may see a constitutional crisis of a magnitude never seen before in Canada.
I am afraid that due to their constant pandering to Quebec, Justin Trudeau and the Liberals are now powerless to act against Saskatchewan. And all Canadians know that.
Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of five books.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Craig Wallace;

Subject: Charter of Rights-Canada; Acquittals & mistrials; Abortion; Carbon; Decades; Prime ministers; Heating; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trudeau, Pierre Elliott; Trudeau, Justin

Title: A problem of their own making

Document 209 of 252

Here's what Justin Trudeau thinks about the coming U.S. election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-what-justin-trudeau-thinks-about-coming-u-s/docview/2915522452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what+Justin+Trudeau+thinks+about+the+coming+U.S.+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau insists he is not taking sides in this year's U.S. election, but he is also making it abundantly clear that neither Canada - nor the world - should be keen for a rerun of the Donald Trump years.
"I mean, the first time through four years of Donald Trump was a real challenge," the prime minister said in a one-on-one conversation with the Star. "Given what Mr. Trump has said about his intentions if he gets re-elected, it would certainly be challenging once again."
Does this mean Trudeau will be rooting for Joe Biden if the November vote comes down a rematch between Biden and Trump, as it now seems?
"There's no question that when there is significantly more alignment, like there is on environment and human rights and international engagement with the current president, with Mr. Biden, I obviously would hope to continue to work with people who are more aligned."
On Monday night, Trump emerged from the Iowa caucuses with strong, almost inevitable momentum to become the Republican candidate for this year's presidential vote.
On Tuesday, during a question-and-answer session with the Montreal Chamber of Commerce, Trudeau was asked what he was doing to prepare the country for the possible re-election of Trump.
"We are going to be ready for the choice that the Americans are going to make in November," Trudeau said. But the prime minister, who was speaking in French, framed that choice in stark terms - one in which democracy is at stake.
"The choice that Americans are going to make: do they want to continue to be optimistic and committed to the future or are they going to choose to step back, out of nostalgia for a moment that never existed?"
Trudeau then went on to tell the crowd in Montreal that the next election in Canada - presumably pitting him against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre - would be a "similar choice."
"It's a conversation that we are going to have."
On the one hand, it isn't surprising that Trudeau would be open about his views on Trump, who came to office determined to tear up the North American Free Trade Agreement and make Canada a casualty of his fierce, America-first protectionist policies. Personally, Trump appeared to hold Trudeau in low regard, famously slamming him as "dishonest and weak" during a 2018 Twitter tantrum. Similarly, Trudeau's tolerance of Trump seemed stretched at times; notably, he was caught on camera in late 2019 laughing at the president with other world leaders at a Buckingham Palace reception.
As one of the longest-serving progressive leaders now on the world stage, Trudeau is one of the few who bears the battle scars of dealing with Trump, and he has spoken before of how he has had to explain to Biden how much America's reputation changed during the stormy Trump years.
Trudeau is also gambling that there's a domestic political payoff for taking on Trump, if it allows him to cast Poilievre and the Conservatives in the same "far-right" mould.
However, a new poll from Abacus Data shows that Canadians are more ambivalent about Trump than they were eight years ago - and while not as polarized as Americans, more divided on party lines than they once were.
Abacus CEO David Coletto reports that in his latest survey of opinions about the U.S. election, not all Canadians - especially Conservatives - share Trudeau's view that a Trump victory would be bad news.
Coletto writes that "66 per cent of Canadians favour Biden over Trump, with a notable divide among Conservative supporters - 57 per cent favouring Trump and 43 per cent Biden. On the other hand, overwhelming majorities of Liberals and NDP supporters prefer Biden. Strikingly, younger Canadians, particularly those under 30, show a higher inclination towards Trump compared to those over 60."
Using online panels, Abacus surveyed 1,500 Canadian adults between Jan. 4 and 9. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.6 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
In an interview with me last Friday in Guelph, Trudeau said he's not daunted by the prospect of a Trump victory - he dealt with him once and he'll do it again, if he must. He said Canada survived Trump by convincing Americans at all levels that their relationship with their northern neighbour was in their self-interest.
"My job is to make sure that we are ready for whatever Americans decide in the next election," he said. "I was able to do that for four years, and I'm going to be able to do that into the future."
Still, I asked, doesn't this mean that this Canadian prime minister is taking a side in an American election?
"It's not my job to take a side," he said.
However, Trudeau added, "My biggest priorities ... (are) building an economy that is fighting climate change and creating good jobs in an inclusive way, and standing up for the international, rules-based order. Those are things that Mr. Trump has explicitly pushed back hard against, that Mr. Biden is very much more aligned with."
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump, left, and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrive for a round table meeting during a NATO leaders meeting in Watford, Hertfordshire, England, onDec. 4, 2019.

Document 210 of 252

Notley says she'll step down as leader of provincial NDP

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/notley-says-shell-step-down-as-leader-provincial/docview/2915521421/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Notley+says+she%27ll+step+down+as+leader+of+provincial+NDP&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former premier Rachel Notley, after almost a decade at the helm of Alberta's NDP, is stepping down from the top job.
Notley, the official Opposition leader, said a leadership race will be called and she will stay on as leader until a replacement is chosen.
That means she will remain on the front bench during the upcoming spring sitting.
Notley said she doesn't know her next steps after that, including whether she will fulfil her current term as the legislature member for Edmonton-Strathcona, a riding she has won handily in five consecutive elections. She also did not rule out running again.
"I just don't know. It's a volatile world, politics," Notley said in an interview before making her departure public Tuesday.
The party will now set the rules and timelines for the race. Caucus members Rakhi Pancholi, David Shepherd, Sarah Hoffman and Kathleen Ganley are rumoured to be eyeing potential leadership runs.
Notley said she will not be endorsing any candidate.
The announcement ends months of speculation over Notley's future after her NDP lost the May 2023 election to Premier Danielle Smith's United Conservatives. Notley's NDP captured 38 of the 87 legislature seats to become the largest official Opposition in provincial history.
It was the second consecutive election loss for the NDP under Notley, which ended a 44-year Progressive-Conservative dynasty in 2015 with a surprise majority government only to be trounced four years later by Jason Kenney's UCP.
Notley was elected as party leader in 2014, commanding a caucus that, except for a short-lived breakthrough in 1986, had been confined to a tiny corner of the legislature with a handful of members who could hold meetings in a subcompact sedan.
Under Notley, the NDP wiped out rival left-centre parties, including the Alberta Liberals, to establish itself as the dominant alternative to the governing right-wing UCP.
Asked what she considers to be signature accomplishments as Alberta's 17th premier, Notley points to getting approval for the Trans Mountain pipeline expansion, building a new Calgary cancer hospital, reducing child poverty, investing in schools and hospitals, phasing out coal-fired electricity, kick-starting renewable energy investment and hiking Alberta's minimum wage.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Rachel Notley she will stay on as leader of the Alberta NDP until a replacement is chosen and then step down.
