Document 211 of 252

Trudeau powerless to act after Saskatchewan breaks carbon tax law

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-powerless-act-after-saskatchewan-breaks/docview/2915729088/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+powerless+to+act+after+Saskatchewan+breaks+carbon+tax+law&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Jan. 1, the government of Saskatchewan removed the federal carbon tax from natural gas bills in the province.
Natural gas is what most residents of Saskatchewan use to heat their homes. This was done to match the federal government's same actions in exempting home heating oil from the carbon tax.
Home heating oil is the main source for heating homes in Atlantic Canada - a long time Liberal stronghold, while Saskatchewan is a Liberal wasteland. The decision by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal government to exempt home heating oil was blatantly political.
If the Liberals are to have any chance of remaining in power after the next federal election, they must have support from the Maritimes. And polls are showing their support there is lagging, so they made this move to shore up that support.
Now let's be clear. This move by the Saskatchewan government is illegal. The federal law imposing the carbon tax has been upheld by the Supreme Court of Canada. The question now is, what does the Trudeau government do about this move by Premier Scott Moe and the Saskatchewan government?
In Canada, the federal government has the authority to pass laws related to taxation, the criminal code, etc. Provinces generally administer and enforce those laws. For example, if you rob a bank here in Hamilton, you will be violating the federal Criminal Code, but you will usually be arrested by the local Hamilton police and tried by an Ontario court.
But what happens if a provincial government refuses to enforce federal law or breaks federal law? There are some examples in Canadian history of this happening.
In 1973, Dr. Henry Morgentaler was acquitted by a Quebec jury of performing illegal abortions. His acquittal was appealed by the Crown, and the Quebec Court of Appeal overturned that acquittal and substituted a sentence of 18 months in jail. In 1975, Morgentaler was again acquitted by a jury on additional charges of performing illegal abortions, but he again was returned to jail. In 1976, after being released from prison, he was again charged with performing illegal abortions and once again acquitted.
Quebec then informed Ottawa that it would not enforce federal abortion laws. The government of then prime minister Pierre Trudeau took no action in Quebec to enforce the law that was in place at the time. (For the record I am very pro-choice and supported Dr. Morgentaler. I raise this though, as at the time he was breaking the law of the land.)
In December 2022, the Quebec government introduced legislation to make swearing allegiance to King Charles III optional for members of the provincial legislature. The Quebec legislation basically states that Section 128 of the Constitution doesn't apply to Quebec. Now, no province has the power to unilaterally amend the Constitution. This law passed by Quebec is unconstitutional and thus illegal. But the Trudeau government has taken no action to uphold the Constitution.
On a slightly different note, the Quebec government passed two laws (Bills 21 and 96) that violate the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, but exempted them from court challenges by using the "Not Withstanding Clause." Again, the Trudeau government took no action here.
The reality is the current federal government (and that of Pierre Trudeau almost 50 years ago) has always turned a blind eye when Quebec refuses to enforce or even breaks the law. They have set a dangerous precedent by doing so. For if the Trudeau government was to try and enforce the law against Saskatchewan, after their inaction against Quebec, I dare say we may see a constitutional crisis of a magnitude never seen before in Canada. Western Canadians would be enraged beyond anything we can imagine.
I am afraid that due to their constant pandering to Quebec, Justin Trudeau and the Liberals are now powerless to act against Saskatchewan. And all Canadians know that.
CAPTION: Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe's government removed the federal carbon tax on natural gas for home heating. The federal Liberals will find it hard to force a reversal, Craig Wallace writes.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Subject: Charter of Rights-Canada; Carbon; Decades; Heating; Environmental tax; Acquittals & mistrials; Abortion; Prime ministers; Natural gas

Document 212 of 252

Universities boost an economic system that has brought us to the brink

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/universities-boost-economic-system-that-has/docview/2915729025/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Universities+boost+an+economic+system+that+has+brought+us+to+the+brink&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the fall of 2019, my then 26-year-old son started a graduate degree at McMaster.
His program included teaching assistant responsibilities and he mischievously told me when he started he wanted to use the position to reinforce the stereotype that universities were hotbeds of socialist indoctrination.
If this was the conventional wisdom, he reasoned, then he was prepared to use what little influence he had, to bring his left-wing agenda to bear on a small group of naive, unsuspecting undergraduates.
I'm not sure how successful he was. The year was disrupted by pandemic restrictions, and I suspect he spent more time grading papers and leading tutorials than pontificating socialist rhetoric. But I was reminded of his antics when I read about the lawsuit filed against McMaster and its student union by a group alleging the university's environment was "relentlessly menacing and hostile."
Notwithstanding my son's impishness, if one accepts the premise of the lawsuit, one is easily led to believe that universities like McMaster are hubs of counterculture conduct and need to be reigned in.
The thread has been picked up by conservative politicians, who see themselves as front-line generals in the culture wars. Shortly after taking office in 2018, Doug Ford's government enacted a vague policy to encourage open and respectful debate on college and university campuses. It also explicitly prohibited "hate speech, discrimination, and other illegal forms of speech," even though these were already covered by provincial statute and the Criminal Code.
At the federal level, Pierre Poilievre has threatened to make grants to post-secondary institutions conditional on their upholding academic freedom and free speech.
It all sounds very reasonable. Obviously, the targets of initiatives like these are the scruffy grad students like my son, and the pampered, tenured faculty that dominate academic life in this country. Or so our conservative politicians want us to believe. It's more likely Messrs. Poilievre and Ford are shamelessly virtue signalling to their base.
While there is little dispute that faculty members and graduate students are overwhelmingly inclined to self identify as either liberal or moderate, it is less clear that the political identity of the instructors has had any influence of the voting patterns of students over the past six or seven decades. If it had, we would not have had 40-plus years of neo-liberal, laissez-faire economic policy dutifully practised by both Liberal and Tory governments.
Instead, it looks like it's just the opposite. Or at least asymmetrical. If there's a problem with our post-secondary institutions, it's that programs in business and economics instruct thousands of students on the merits of an unregulated capitalist system. A system that has brought us a housing crisis, existential climate change and wealth and income inequity.
Need proof? Look no further than the first building you see as you enter the McMaster campus from Sterling Street. Looming immediately to the north is the DeGroote School of Business, named for transport magnate Michael DeGroote.
It's doubtful the thousands of impressionable students who have been educated in these hallowed halls have undergone socialist indoctrination as Mr. Ford and Mr. Poilievre would have you believe.
The same is true for economics instruction. While it's been several decades since I studied the discipline, by all accounts it remains wedded to teaching the virtues of classical economics.
McMaster is not an outlier. Business schools all across the country are named for philanthrocapitalists, from the Rotman School at the University of Toronto to the Lazaridis School at Laurier. These men would not lend their names nor donate their fortunes to schools that might undermine the system that served them so well.
My son's pursuit was ultimately futile. He simply graduated and moved on with his career. What remains firmly in place at our institutions of higher learning, however, is an unencumbered corporate influence that sustains a system that has brought us to the brink.
CAPTION: If there's a problem with our post-secondary institutions, it's that programs in business and economics instruct thousands of students on the merits of an unregulated capitalist system. A system that has brought us a housing crisis, existential climate change and wealth and income inequity, Tyler Firth writes.
CREDIT: Tyler Firth

Subject: Teaching; Students; Colleges & universities; Indoctrination; Economics

Document 213 of 252

Jan. 15: Learn from Ottawa's LRT troubles, make developers help pay for new schools and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-15-learn-ottawas-lrt-troubles-make-developers/docview/2915111686/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+15%3A+Learn+from+Ottawa%27s+LRT+troubles%2C+make+developers+help+pay+for+new+schools+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Learn from Ottawa's LRT troubles
Re: Ottawa grapples with rider woes (Jan. 12)
Any Hamiltonian that reads this article better think twice about support for LRT. A $50-million budget gap for running the Ottawa transit service last year, much of it related to the new LRT. Ever since the start of construction, ridership has dropped drastically and not picked up since its completion in 2019. So now they're going to cut service, cut staff and dip into reserves. It seems the bus BRT system worked much better. If it ain't broke why fix it?
Gary Aikema, Flamborough
Developers should help pay for new schools
Re: School board rethinks plan to relieve 'nightmare' enrolment at Waterdown's Mary Hopkins (Jan. 12)
Developers should foot at least a portion of the cost of new school construction and/or existing school expansion as a precondition of any residential housing project. This is an absolute no-brainer. The Waterdown school crowding situation is just one example. School boards and local government in tandem should examine any proposals and determine the impact on schooling well ahead of any building permits being issued. The provincial government could then support both increased housing density and adequate school funding at the same time.
David Shupe, Dundas
Focus put on wrong leader
Re: No mystery why Liberals are losing public support (Jan. 9)
The column by Professor Emeritus John Redekop claims to have solved a mystery. There is, however, another mystery afoot: how the leader of the federal Conservative party is not held to account for the outright lies, exaggerations and misinformation he is sharing every day as he campaigns across the country (courtesy of us taxpayers, by the way, despite the fact that no campaign has been called).
The professor well knows that blaming the woes of our current economic conditions - conditions shared by countries around the world due to the pandemic and resulting impacts (in which Canada and thus Canadians have fared very well) on the Liberals - is pure misinformation. Blaming the federal government for the appalling state of health care - a provincial responsibility - at Trudeau's feet is another sleight of hand. One need only to look at the massive surplus the Ford government sits on as people die on wait lists and in hallways, and health care staff are run ragged.
There are plenty of things to be critical of in the current government, but it is mysterious that many ignore context and fact, and instead further spread the false narrative offered by the federal Conservative candidate. We get the government we deserve, as we in Ontario discovered after voting in a populist premier with a lot of loud, repeated grievances but no plan, and now suffer daily blows of incompetence, greed and corruption.
Kris Gadjanski, Dundas
Woman should be held criminally responsible
Re: Killer of Grimsby grandmother found not criminally responsible (Jan. 11)
After reading how Gerry Ladouceur's life changed when his wife was killed in an accident by Sonya Kaur Sekhon, a woman who had the misfortune to have schizoaffective disorder, I feel compelled to comment on how I disagree with the judge's decision that Sonya was not criminally responsible for her death.
In my mind, when Sonya made the decision to not take the medication that kept her stable, she made a decision to not be aware of what she was doing and that decision led to the loss of life and I feel she should have been held responsible.
I believe the same thing happened with Paul Pender, who also died due to the fact that his killer chose not to take medication that would have prevented his death.
Schizophrenia is a terrible disease to have and I certainly don't know how to enforce medication being used, but I feel so sorry for the loss of both of these lives due to the missed medication. Losing a loving family member is a very deep tragedy. My deepest condolences to both the Ladoucer and Peller families for their loss.
Anne Laidman, Binbrook
Widen the lens on immigration stories
Re: Feds were warned over housing costs (Jan. 12)
Immigration is a complex, layered matter. So why does our local reporting oversimplify and link it only to pressing housing issues?
I realize The Spec relies more and more on news feeds to fill its pages, but is there no place for editing these feeds to add a local perspective that appreciates the complexity of immigration? It's been widely documented that the feds have dramatically increased immigration levels. And that our provincial government is silent on the issue - no simple action that can be calculated on the back of a napkin. Let's discuss immigration in relation to the increased education, health and social service needs of new arrivals.
Let's also not miss the fact that immigrants are often misled by officials as to their qualifications and employability. Widen the lens, please!
Paul Fralick, Dundas
Arctic defence being ignored
Today, I had the pleasure of meeting Conservative MP Larry Brock (Brantford-Brant) with the intention of asking him one question. What is Prime Minister Justin Trudeau doing about the Chinese and Russians expanding their presence in the Arctic territories? Mr. Brock responded with the answer "Nothing." His response was no surprise.
I can't understand why Trudeau is sending a Canadian warship to the South China Sea when there is a clear and present danger of Russia and China possibly encroaching upon Canadian lands. Russia has already invaded Ukraine and China is preparing for Taiwan. Is Canada's north next? What is Trudeau doing about this?
John Morris, Brantford
Our only hope is sanity will prevail
Re: Trump reporting needs balance (Jan. 12)
First, an editorial is, by definition, an opinion piece. It's supposed to be biased. That's the point!
The writer addresses specific points made in the editorial, noting that Donald Trump is "measured" and "mostly" articulate. I don't have room here to list the unmeasured, inarticulate statements that Trump has made, not to mention the outright lies he has told. Everyone, including the writer, knows them.
Next up "Trump hires talented people to do the job." The vast majority of talented people he hired are now issuing dire warnings that he not be allowed back in the White House.
"He will round up illegal immigrants ..." That he will. Maybe he'll separate their children from them to be lost for months as he did before. That'll teach 'em.
That anyone with a glimmer of intellect, humanity or compassion can defend and support this evil man scares the hell out of me. Trump makes no secret of the fact that he admires the "hard men" like Xi, Putin and Kim and will rule just like them if given the opportunity. Our only hope for November is that sanity will prevail in the hearts and minds of the majority of American voters. Although even if it does, he won't accept defeat.
Derek Elliott, Hamilton
Give this writer a weekly gig
Re: Trump reporting needs balance (Jan. 12)
I have just finished reading the letter from the gentleman in Ancaster, "Trump reporting needs balance." I read that Trump is "measured, funny and articulate" at his rallies, and that he "hires talented people to do the job" who are not sycophants.
As for the one million people in Washington for the Jan. 6 rally (actually about 10,000 according to official sources) who just "broke and entered" and were not insurrectionists, (and didn't really kill anybody) I think that the only response is to give this writer a weekly column so that we can continue to enjoy his humorous and engaging view of current events.
Andy Blackwood, Simcoe
Thanks for the laughs
Re: Trump reporting needs balance (Jan. 12)
Never has the term "drinking the Kool-Aid" been so applicable as this letter defending Donald Trump. Thanks for the Friday morning laughs.
Shawn Findlay, Hamilton
Trump letter problematic
Re: Trump reporting needs balance (Jan. 12)
In his letter, David Gallo laments the balance of coverage concerning Donald Trump. He does himself a disservice by repeating totally fictitious facts and numbers.
His claim that there were an "estimated one million Trump supporters in Washington" on Jan. 6 is absurd and laughable. Law enforcement estimates there may have been as many as 80,000 but the true number is believed to be 10,000. Many were indeed charged with breaking and entering, but plenty have also been charged with assaulting police officers and yes, seditious conspiracy.
Mr. Gallo also states his opinion that at his rallies Trump is "measured" (vows he will seek vengeance on his political opponents), "mostly articulate" (high praise) and "funny." As to this last claim, I suppose that some might find mocking a war veteran's injuries hilarious but I personally do not.
Mark Summers, Waterdown
CAPTION: Local residents should pay attention to all the problems Ottawa is having with its LRT system, a letter writer urges.

Toronto MP Carolyn Bennett to become Canada's ambassador to Denmark: Sources

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-mp-carolyn-bennett-become-canadas/docview/2915110250/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+MP+Carolyn+Bennett+to+become+Canada%27s+ambassador+to+Denmark%3A+Sources&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Outgoing Toronto Liberal MP Carolyn Bennett will soon be named Canada's next ambassador to Denmark, the Star has learned.
The appointment is expected to be announced in the coming days, sources say. The current ambassador, Denis Robert, was appointed to the role in 2021.
Bennett did not respond to a request for comment Tuesday morning.
The 73-year-old former cabinet minister announced in December that she was resigning her seat in Toronto-St. Paul's after more than 26 years in the House of Commons.
She will join other former Liberal cabinet ministers to whom Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has awarded positions aboard. In 2017, Trudeau named outgoing foreign affairs minister Stéphane Dion as ambassador to the European Union and Germany, while John McCallum - who was Trudeau's immigration minister from 2015 to 2017 - was appointed Canada's ambassador to China.
After Trudeau's Liberals won power in 2015, Bennett served as minister of Crown-Indigenous relations for six years. Trudeau shuffled her to the mental health and addictions portfolio in 2021, a role that is charged with helping to address Canada's toxic drug crisis, among other issues.
Last summer, a time of anxiety and frustration within Liberal circles about the government's performance as the party lags in national polls behind Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, Bennett was among a group of veteran ministers who left Trudeau's cabinet after they said they wouldn't run for re-election. They included Mississauga Centre MP Omar Alghabra, Helena Jaczek, and Vancouver's Joyce Murray.
Bennett's departure comes as the electoral map in Canada's biggest city slowly takes shape for the next federal vote, which isn't scheduled until 2025, but could occur sooner if Trudeau's minority government falls to a confidence vote or if the prime minister decides to call the election earlier.
Running to replace Bennett as Liberal MP in Toronto-St. Paul's is Leslie Church, who was previously chief-of-staff to Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland, who remains the MP for University-Rosedale. Former Ontario Health Minister Eric Hoskins was rumoured to be eyeing the nomination there, too, but told the Star Tuesday that he does not currently plan to enter the contest.
Besides Bennett, Beaches-East York Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith has confirmed he won't seek re-election, after his unsuccessful run at the provincial Liberal leadership last year. Kevin Vuong, the Independent MP for Spadina-Fort York, told the Star recently that he intends to run again as an Independent in the next election. The Liberals - who held the riding along with every other seat in Toronto since 2015 until they turfed Vuong days before the last election over a dropped sexual assault charge - have not yet chosen a candidate to face him in the downtown riding.
Meanwhile, the electoral dynamic will be different as the city is slated to drop from 25 ridings to 24, thanks to redrawn boundaries that occur once a decade at the federal level. The current riding of Don Valley East, represented by former provincial cabinet minister Michael Coteau, is set to dissolve into neighbouring constituencies. Coteau did not immediately respond to questions from the Star on Tuesday about whether - or where - he intends to run again.
Another question mark is Independent MP Han Dong in Don Valley North, who left the Liberal caucus last year amid controversy over alleged Chinese government meddling in Canadian politics. Dong has said he was "vindicated" after a report concluded some allegations about his involvement were unfounded, but the Liberal government has not yet said he will return to its caucus.
Dong did not immediately respond when asked Tuesday whether he will run for re-election.
With files from Raisa Patel
CAPTION: Toronto MP Carolyn Bennett rises during question period in the House of Commons on May 30, 2023.

Document 215 of 252

Here's what made Ed Broadbent truly stand out

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-what-made-ed-broadbent-truly-stand-out/docview/2915059566/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what+made+Ed+Broadbent+truly+stand+out&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: He was the prime minister we never had, though we surely had the chance.
Ed Broadbent, who led Canada's New Democrats for 14 years - but never the country - died last week at 87.
He left his mark as an NDP leader and a thought leader. But what made Broadbent truly stand out was as much about personality as policy or party.
This unabashed social democrat exuded an ineffable grace and sophistication, a posh professor in tailored suits with a fondness for pipes and cigars, Bach and books, while fighting the good fight for the working class. Perhaps he embodied the quintessential Canadian campus lefty - progressive, professorial, purposeful - a worthy adversary facing off against prime ministers Pierre Trudeau, John Turner and Brian Mulroney.
Reading the accolades and obituaries, I looked back to see what I'd written about Broadbent in his political prime. As a young reporter on Parliament Hill, riding along on his chartered DC-9 campaign plane, I'd wondered if he might keep gaining altitude in the polls and land in the prime minister's office on voting day.
Ahead of the historic free trade election of 1988, Broadbent had emerged as the most popular leader by far, with the NDP on a roll at 41 per cent support in one survey, compared to 35 per cent for the Liberals and 23 per cent for the Progressive Conservatives. The Liberals were tired, the Tories tarnished and Broadbent's NDP trusted.
"This shows a real shift in attitudes away from seeing the NDP as a protest party," he told me at the time. "We're the new boy in town ... we have the new prominence, we're the new presence."
But it didn't last. In the final election day poll that counted, Broadbent's ambition to transform both his party and his country remained unfulfilled.
While he won a record 43 seats, his New Democrats were shut out of the Quebec (where he'd long plotted a breakthrough) and the Atlantic Provinces. The NDP ended up with a mere 20 per cent of the popular vote, a single percentage point above its 19 per cent showing in 1984.
The party's slogan had been, "This time, Ed Broadbent and the New Democrats." It was neither, and he knew it.
"Now I'm going to listen to Mozart and Bach and Billie Holiday, and I'd like to read a novel like ordinary people," he mused.
Within a year he was gone but not forgotten. He set up the Broadbent Institute that endures to this day, a progressive think tank inspired by the leader who had carried on the intellectual and ideological legacy of his NDP predecessors, David Lewis and Tommy Douglas.
Economic nationalism had been the building block of Broadbent's early political career. Yet free trade became his electoral undoing in the end.
The more the 1988 campaign turned on free trade, the more voters turned against Broadbent's New Democrats, whom Canadians still distrusted on economic matters. That's politics.
Growing up in Oshawa in the shadow of GM, the giant U.S. carmaker, Broadbent had always believed in repatriating economic control to Canada. A reluctant warrior, he had flirted at first with the party's far-left Waffle movement.
But after winning the leadership in 1975, he privately questioned the need for widespread economic interventionism and foreign isolationism. Broadbent was never quite able to bring New Democrats along, as the party faithful kept clinging to traditional policy orthodoxy calling for nationalization (taking over a bank, Bell and the CPR) and nationalism (withdrawing from NATO and Norad).
By the late 1980s, with the party rising in the polls and Broadbent suddenly a contender, New Democrats were on the defensive over their defence policies and economic ambitions. It would take years for the party to adapt to changing economic times.
Yet Broadbent persevered, describing himself to me as "a happy warrior by disposition" thanks to "the genes I inherited."
In truth, Broadbent's childhood had been fraught. The son of an alcoholic gambler, he disliked confrontation and took refuge in intellectual reflection and gentle persuasion.
He liked to tell the story of working in an Oshawa clothing store where the sales staff relegated the ugliest ties to a rack of leftovers and competed to see who could off-load them on customers. Broadbent won the contest every time, endowed with a deceptive ability to seal the deal.
But Broadbent overreached when he pitched his ultimate dream of eliminating the Liberals by transforming Canada into a two-party system.
"It would be healthy for Canada to evolve ... with one party that's left of centre ... like us ... and a conservative party," he mused in the 1988 campaign. "I would like to see it that way."
He quickly confided to his campaign aides that he'd blundered. Sure enough, Liberals rallied to the base, panicked by the idea of political extinction, relegating the NDP to third place once again.
CAPTION: Ed Broadbent speaks in Convocation Hall at the University of Toronto on Oct. 29, 2017.

Subject: Free trade; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade

Document 216 of 252

Former Doug Ford cabinet minister in political limbo as RCMP probes Greenbelt scandal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-doug-ford-cabinet-minister-political-limbo/docview/2915142633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Doug+Ford+cabinet+minister+in+political+limbo+as+RCMP+probes+Greenbelt+scandal&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPP Kaleed Rasheed, ejected from Premier Doug Ford's cabinet and caucus over the Greenbelt scandal, will be stuck in political limbo until the RCMP completes its investigation.
Insisting he did nothing wrong, Rasheed has been banking on Integrity Commissioner J. David Wake to exonerate him so he can return to the Progressive Conservative fold.
But Wake - author of the August report that triggered the dismissal due to a misremembered Las Vegas trip with a Greenbelt developer - has not yet even determined whether to take a second look at the Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP's case.
The ethics watchdog says his hands will remain tied as long as the RCMP criminal probe continues.
Both Rasheed, who wants to clear his name, and NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who seeks more details about what she has dubbed "the now-infamous" Las Vegas jaunt, have asked Wake to do another report.
"No decision has been made regarding the request from Marit Stiles, leader of the Official Opposition, for the integrity commissioner to provide an opinion on whether Kaleed Rasheed has contravened the Members' Integrity Act," said Michelle Renaud, spokesperson for the legislative officer.
"Given that the RCMP has launched a broadly worded investigation into a matter that could overlap with the subject matter of, and individuals named in, the request, the commissioner has informed Ms. Stiles, Kaleed Rasheed and Speaker of the legislative assembly, Ted Arnott, that he will not be proceeding with the request at this time," said Renaud.
Under the Members' Integrity Act, which governs MPPs' conduct, the integrity commissioner "shall suspend" any "inquiry until the police investigation or charge has been finally disposed of, and shall report the suspension to the Speaker."
That means Rasheed, a former public and business service delivery minister who currently sits as an Independent MPP, must await the RCMP probe, which could take years to resolve.
While he did not return repeated messages from the Star seeking comment, friends confide that he had hoped Wake would take on his cause.
"Kaleed was optimistic this could be resolved," said one Tory MPP, speaking confidentially in order to recount a private conversation.
Rasheed, an affable two-term member, remains in contact with many of his former caucus colleagues, who were shocked by his defenestration last September.
"I was sitting near him (in a Niagara Falls conference room) when (a top Ford official) came in and said, 'Kaleed, can I see you for a second?' We never saw him again," said another rattled PC MPP.
Rasheed "just wants to get this cleared up," a third Tory emphasized.
"He wants to get back into the PC caucus. He knows he made a mistake on the dates (of the trip) but he didn't do anything wrong and he doesn't want this hanging over him."
But Rasheed's office provided the wrong dates for a winter 2020 trip to Las Vegas, where he encountered his developer friend Shakir Rehmatullah, who, Wake has concluded, was "more likely than not" tipped off by someone to the government's plans to remove lands from the Greenbelt.
Also on that vacation were Ford's then-principal secretary, Amin Massoudi, and Jae Truesdell, who departed as the premier's director of housing policy the same day as Rasheed was forced from caucus.
On Sept. 20, the premier's office said "if Mr. Rasheed can clear his name through the office of the integrity commissioner, he will be provided an opportunity to return to caucus."
But Ford, who was personally fond of the Mississauga MPP, was privately unhappy that Wake had been misled.
"We expect people to remember the month they took a trip," a source close to the premier said at the time.
"There are generally a lot of questions not answered in light of what was said about what happened."
On Monday, Ford's office confirmed there has been "no change" in the MPP's status.
Rasheed, who has not been seen at Queen's Park since last summer, has maintained he stepped aside "so as not to distract from the important work of the government."
Wake's Aug. 30 report into the Greenbelt debacle found Rasheed was "close friends" with Rehmatullah and said he was "shocked" to see the developer in Las Vegas in December 2019 when they allegedly bumped into each other in a hotel.
It later emerged that the trip was postponed till January 2020 and that Rasheed, Massoudi and Rehmatullah had massages at the same time in the hotel.
Wake's report pointed out Rehmatullah was "the only developer who had lands removed from the Greenbelt who attended the premier's daughter's wedding."
In November 2022, Ford announced he would open up 7,400 acres of the two-million-acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area to spur housing development.
Last summer, separate reports to the legislature by the auditor general and the integrity commissioner concluded "certain developers" were "favoured" in that process.
Amid public outcry and plunging poll numbers, the premier to finally change course on Sept. 21.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry," Ford said at that time.
The Greenbelt fiasco also led to the resignations of former housing minister Steve Clark and a senior aide.
CAPTION: Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP Kaleed Rasheed, shown onSept. 1, 2021, is sitting as an Independent after resigning from the Progressive Conservative caucus in the wake of an integrity commissioner probe into the Ford government's Greenbelt land swap.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals

Location: United States--US; Las Vegas Nevada

Document 217 of 252

'Outrageous': Privately, Justin Trudeau's Toronto MPs are furious at Olivia Chow over her property tax gambit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/outrageous-privately-justin-trudeaus-toronto-mps/docview/2915141534/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Outrageous%27%3A+Privately%2C+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Toronto+MPs+are+furious+at+Olivia+Chow+over+her+property+tax+gambit&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A fault line between the Toronto mayor's office and local Liberal MPs is opening up over a proposed increase to property taxes linked to a perceived shortfall in federal funding support.
Her office, in turn, is accusing Ottawa of dragging its feet when it comes to getting funds out the door and argue local MPs need to fight harder for their city.
At issue is the city's forecast that the cost to house asylum-seekers will rise to at least $250 million in 2024 - and its request for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government to pick up the tab.
Otherwise, Toronto's budget chief Shelley Carroll said she'll pitch adding an extra six per cent "federal impacts levy" onto the already planned property tax hike of 10.5 per cent.
"This is so ridiculously political," one Toronto Liberal MP, who was granted anonymity to discuss the intergovernmental dynamics at play, told the Star.
Those dynamics are indeed sensitive.
Tensions between city hall and Parliament Hill are a tale as old as political time.
But this isn't a great time for Trudeau. His party is being battered in the polls, with at least one pollster saying their projections suggest the Liberal stronghold in Toronto - where the party has dominated in the past three federal elections - is at risk of being breached.
Being blamed for a property tax hike at a time when cost-of-living issues are on everyone's minds is a hit the Liberals don't need.
Several told the Star they see Chow's move as "outrageous" on numerous fronts. They charge that she's shirking the city's responsibility to cover the costs of asylum-seekers, not being transparent enough about the price tag and just expecting Ottawa to step in, figuring the federal Liberals have no political choice.
"It's political and it may be because she calculates we're weaker than (Premier Doug) Ford, but I think we can win on the strength of our record," a Liberal MP said.
Privately and publicly, Liberals point to the $5.47 billion in support their government has provided to Toronto since they took office, including $97 million this past summer towards housing costs for asylum-seekers.
Julie Dzerowicz, a Liberal MP who won the riding of Davenport by a whisker over the NDP in 2021, said her government isn't getting enough credit for its investments.
"We need to be friends," she told the Star. "We have tough times ahead."
Dzerowicz and others told the Star that meetings are ongoing between Ottawa and Toronto officials to figure out next steps for funding.
Complicating those conversations is a tension over who is actually responsible for the costs associated with asylum-seekers.
When a surge in people seeking asylum in Canada began just after Trudeau came to power, cities and provinces argued it was time for the federal government to start footing the bill because that surge was driven by federal policies that were beyond their control.
A fund was launched in 2017 to help, but what was supposed to be a short-term measure has become a long-term expectation from cities. Within the government, discussions are underway about how to manage that expectation, not just for this year but also in the future.
On Monday, Chow said she is having productive conversations - and also thanked the Liberals for the funding the city has received so far.
"I've been speaking to a lot of the members of Parliament from Toronto to answer the questions they have," she told reporters.
"It is a process, and I'm optimistic that we will find some kind of solution."
But her office said there is also too much talking.
A source close to Chow, who was granted anonymity because they are not authorized to speak to the media, said local MPs should be pressuring the Prime Minister's Office to help their city, not complaining about the mayor or city council making them look bad with a tax hike.
"The feds are always willing to take a phone call, respond to text messages, have a photo op, but it has been like getting blood from a stone when it comes to actually funding things," the source said, pointing to funding announcements on housing and even previous refugee funding commitments where the cash isn't flowing as fast as promised.
All the meetings in the world don't matter if the money doesn't get sent along, the source said.
"Nobody quite understands what their calculation is. It's almost like they've decided to give up on Toronto and that doesn't seem to be in anybody's best interest."
Criticism from Toronto that it's being ignored by Ottawa is hardly new. But the political context is very different in this round of fighting, as the Liberals consider what it will take to remain in government after the next federal election.
Abacus Data CEO David Coletto told the Star, however, that just as his national polls suggest the government is in trouble overall, his internal projections suggest a number of Toronto ridings are at risk, including Davenport, Eglinton-Lawrence, Etobicoke Centre, Etobicoke - Lakeshore, Spadina-Harbourfront, Willowdale and York Centre.
Some of those seats are very firmly in the Conservatives' sights, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre moved swiftly to condemn Chow's proposed tax increases.
"This is what you get when you elect NDP/Liberal politicians: massive tax hikes," he posted to his social media account in the wake of Chow's budget reveal.
"Only common sense Conservatives will cut your taxes."
Some Liberals told the Star they see Chow's move as an effort to give NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh a bump - she's a former NDP MP, and her late husband, Jack Layton, led the federal party.
Mélanie Richer, a former adviser to Singh, said she doesn't think that's what's happening, but acknowledged the federal New Democrats have struggled to break through in Toronto in recent years.
Chow's move opens a window, Richer suggested, for Singh to argue that federal Liberals are failing Toronto.
"A lot of what Mayor Chow is doing, there is an opportunity for the NDP to pick up on that because folks in Toronto are upset," she said.
Muhammad Ali, vice-president at Crestview Strategies, and a veteran of Liberal campaigns, called Chow's decision to hold Trudeau responsible for a potential property tax increase "bold," but also likely to backfire.
"If I was Chow, I would've built up a few more big city allies with lots of Liberal seats pushing the same message, demanding relief or face widespread big tax increases," he said.
"But she didn't do that and even (former mayor John) Tory did not win the battle demanding big money relief even leading into 2021 election."
With files from Mahdis Habibinia
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Mayor Olivia Chow make a housing announcement in Toronto on Dec. 21, 2023.

Subject: Local elections; Members of Parliament; Costs; Funding; Politics; Prime ministers; Cities; Property taxes; Tax increases

Document 218 of 252

Young Ontario activists' climate lawsuit against province goes to Court of Appeal on Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/young-ontario-activists-climate-lawsuit-against/docview/2914889867/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Young+Ontario+activists%27+climate+lawsuit+against+province+goes+to+Court+of+Appeal+on+Monday&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The young Ontarians who've spent years fighting the province's climate-change plan are gearing up for another day in court Monday.
The consortium of young Ontarians who, since 2019, have been pursuing legal action against the Ford government over its climate change plan, is headed to the Ontario Court of Appeal this week. These youths, many of whom launched this lawsuit when they were teens, have alleged the province's handling of the climate crisis is so "woefully inadequate" it breached their Charter rights.
In April, after years of effort, they won a Pyrrhic victory. The court agreed with many of their arguments, rejecting Ontario's contentions that the province's emissions have no effect on climate change and its climate plan is meaningless, rather than actual law.
Still, Justice Marie-Andrée Vermette ultimately sided with the province, ruling that while "young people are disproportionately impacted by climate change," that is not the fault of the Ontario government's climate laws.
One of the applicants, Shaelyn Wabegijig, told the Star she's excited to go before the triumvirate of judges in-person Monday after having had to appear virtually due to pandemic restrictions last go around.
She said she can't wait to look the province's representatives "in the eye" as her and her cohorts "fight for their future."
Wabegijig said she hopes these judges will be the ones to finally compel the government to base its emissions targets on "peer-reviewed climate science."
"Justice Vermette made a number of findings of fact that were extremely consistent with the theory of the case we put forward," said lawyer Nader Hasan of Stockwoods LLP in Toronto. "But we say she made some mistakes we're trying to get corrected."
Hasan, who will be representing the youths in court again this week, said he is optimistic about the appeal. But even if he were walking into another defeat, "these issues are so important that, regardless of chances of success, one has to fight."
"Our position is a simple one: Governments should not be above the law when it comes to confronting one of the most pressing and substantial challenges humanity has ever faced," he said. "If we're successful, it'll make it clear that the people have the ability to hold the government's feet to the fire."
The initial case focused on the climate legislation passed by the government of Premier Doug Ford, shortly after he was elected in 2018. The Cap and Trade Cancellation Act repealed more stringent emission reduction targets, passed by former premier Kathleen Wynne's government, and replaced them with targets that would allow 200 additional megatonnes of carbon to be emitted by 2030, the lawsuit alleged.
"Ontario's plan is to curb greenhouse gas emissions by 30 per cent by 2030," said Hasan. "That is far, far, far below what needs to happen on a global scale."
It's also not consistent, the lawsuit claimed, with Canada's Paris Agreement commitments to limit global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius, which Ontario also pledged to uphold, constituting a breach of the Charter rights of the province's youth, who will bear the brunt of climate change.
"Young people have the most to lose," said Hasan. "And they've done nothing wrong. It's their parents and grandparents and great-grandparents who caused this harm, yet they're the ones who are going to suffer."
And so it's their hope that the Court of Appeal will overturn the lower court's findings and rule that Ontario's 2030 target is "unconstitutional and violates youth and future generations' rights to life, security of the person, and equality," said Danielle Gallant, a lawyer for environmental law charity Ecojustice, also working on the case.
"We are out of time to act," she said, "to prevent catastrophic climate change."
With files from Marco Chown Oved
CAPTION: Clockwise from top left: Sophia Mathur, Shelby Gagnon, Beze Gray,Madison Dyck,Shaelyn Wabegijig,Alex Neufeldt and Zoë Keary-Matzner, whose battle with Doug Ford's Ontario government enters a new phase on Monday.

Document 219 of 252

Teen Brooks granted Canadian citizenship, sets sights on Games

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/teen-brooks-granted-canadian-citizenship-sets/docview/2914626499/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teen+Brooks+granted+Canadian+citizenship%2C+sets+sights+on+Games&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Teenage surfing prodigy Erin Brooks has won her fight for Canadian citizenship, opening the door for her to compete for Canada at the Paris Olympics.
The 16-year-old Brooks was born in Texas and grew up in Hawaii but has Canadian ties through her American-born father, Jeff, who is a dual American-Canadian citizen, and her grandfather who was born and raised in Montreal.
Brooks' citizenship bid was initially turned down. But Immigration Minister Marc Miller had a change of heart after a December ruling by Ontario's Superior Court of Justice that it is unconstitutional for Canada to deny automatic citizenship to the children of foreign-born Canadians who grew up abroad.
The Brooks family then refiled their application under a hardship status, based on the recommendation of the Immigration Department, to accelerate the process.
"I love Canada. I have never been prouder to wear the Maple Leaf," Erin Brooks said in a statement released by the family. "To Minister Marc Miller and MP Jenny Kwan, you have changed my life. I believe that I will do something truly special for my country thanks to your gift of citizenship."
Kwan, the NDP's immigration critic, helped advocate for Brooks.
Author Don Chapman, who has spent years working to change the existing citizenship laws, broke the news to the young surfer by phone.
"She just broke down in tears. She was just so excited," Jeff Brooks said from California where his daughter had been competing. "It was a really special moment."
The final opportunity to qualify for the Olympics is at the ISA World Championships in Puerto Rico in February.
Brooks is considered by many a potential challenger for a medal at the Olympics due to the heavy left-hand barrel conditions at Teahupo'o in Tahiti, where the Olympic surfing event is being held.
She captured a silver medal at the ISA World Surfing Games in El Salvador in June and won gold at the ISA World Junior Championships in June 2022.
Dom Domic, Surf Canada's executive director, welcomed the citizenship news.
"After over four years, it looks like the Brooks finally have their happy ending," he said in an email. "I am personally over the moon that Minister Miller will make right the past wrongs and finally our lost Canadians will officially be welcomed home to where they have always belonged."
Canada's citizenship laws are complex, with amendments changing the rules in 2009 and 2015. But essentially Bill C-37 in 2009 ended the extension of citizenship to second generations born abroad.
In an October letter explaining its decision not to grant a "discretionary grant of citizenship," Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada says Brooks did not meet the requirements.
"The application is refused on the basis that the applicant is not stateless, has not experienced special or unusual hardship or provided services of an exceptional value to Canada which warrants a discretionary grant of Canadian citizenship," the letter stated.
It was the latest in a series of setbacks for the Brooks family.
Their home in Lahaina on Maui burned down during the recent wildfires and Brooks' mother is battling cancer. The family now calls Tofino, B.C., home when not on the road nine to 10 months a year with their daughter.
The Canadian Olympic Committee also backed Brooks' bid for citizenship with CEO David Shoemaker saying the teenager has demonstrated "her sincere commitment to compete for Canada and to be Canadian."
In March 2022, Surfing Canada and the Canadian Olympic Committee petitioned the International Surfing Association (ISA) to allow Brooks to compete for Canada as her citizenship application had been filed, but not completed.
The ISA granted the request but changed its mind last June, saying "this decision was taken incorrectly and not in accordance with the applicable ISA rules."
The ISA suspended Brooks' eligibility to compete for Canada, saying it would re-evaluate the decision if "proof of citizenship with a verified document from the Canadian government, was provided.
That prevented Brooks from competing at the Pan Am Games and the ISA World Championships. She has continued to compete in the World Surf League's Qualifying Series.
Brooks has been contacted by other countries interested in her talents. Her grandmother, on her mother's side, is a German citizen and there are also Italian ties. Her father's side of the family has Irish bloodlines, as well.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Erin Brooks, 16, was born in Texas and grew up in Hawaii but has Canadian ties through her American-born father, Jeff, who is a dual American-Canadian citizen, and her grandfather who was born and raisedin Montreal.Dom Domic The Canadian Press File Photo

A hybrid proves a stepping stone to their EV for this couple

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hybrid-proves-stepping-stone-their-ev-this-couple/docview/2913704604/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+hybrid+proves+a+stepping+stone+to+their+EV+for+this+couple&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It turned out to be a life-changing experience.
Six years ago, Sue Pallotta and her husband Andy took a ride in a friend's Chevy Volt plug-in hybrid. That sparked their curiosity about hybrids and electric vehicles, and, in 2018, they leased a plug-in Mitsubishi Outlander hybrid. Last year, they made the move to a fully electric car, a Chevy Bolt SUV.
Pallotta, who lives in Port Perry, explains why she loves the Bolt, her year-round, everyday vehicle:
'In 2018, we ordered a Chevy Volt when the Ontario government offered a rebate (up to $14,000). When Doug Ford was elected, the rebate was cancelled. The Volt wasn't going to be delivered before the rebate ended, so we went to a Plug 'n Drive event in Toronto where we learned a lot about electric cars and (we) tried a Mitsubishi hybrid.
We cancelled the Volt, and leased a Mitsubishi Outlander plug-in hybrid off the lot before the rebate expired, and got $7,500 back. The Mitsubishi salesperson was very knowledgeable about electric cars, compared to what we found at many other dealerships. The Outlander wasn't exactly what we wanted, so we leased it and figured in two or three years the technology would be that much better and we could decide then if we wanted to purchase one.
When the lease was up, we bought the Mitsubishi out and used it as a trade-in. In late 2022, we were ready to go to a fully electric car. We felt the Mitsubishi technology hadn't advanced enough and they only had a plug-in hybrid. Our Mitsubishi hybrid had four-wheel drive and a lot of great features, but didn't go that far in electric mode ... maybe 45 kilometres in summer and less in winter.
We ordered a Chevy Bolt EUV in November, 2022 and got it in January, 2023. It was roomier and sportier-looking than the Mitsubishi. We bought it from a dealership not far from us. We, again, found most salespeople were not super knowledgeable about the electric cars they were selling. We knew more than they did. But we could take it to our local dealership to be serviced - that's one reason we didn't look at Tesla, because we couldn't get one serviced in our area.
Although reducing our carbon footprint mattered, we didn't want to spend a fortune on an electric car. The main reason we bought an electric vehicle was because you save a lot over time and they hold their value better than gas cars. We live on a country road, and they are heavier than gas cars, so it's very stable in winter.
We got a family and friends discount through GM and a $5,000-federal-government incentive to buy an electric car, so the Bolt cost us $43,000. Andy figures in 10 years, it will have paid for itself. I have no fuel costs, no oil changes, no exhaust and the brakes don't wear as quickly. (The car's electric motors help slow it).
I plug the Bolt in at 7 p.m. at night (off-peak hours) in our garage and unplug it at 7 a.m., although I don't plug it in every day. It gets 417 km per charge in summer and about 280 in winter, and I work only eight kilometres away. The only time I got range anxiety was when I had to attend a banquet in November in Oakville. It was cold, so the car didn't get the range it did in warmer weather and I didn't know where the charging stations were. But I didn't run out of charge. Also in November, I went to the Royal Winter Fair and was worried when it took an hour to get out of the parking lot. It only used 10 km of range during that hour, even though I had the heater running. If you are not moving, it hardly uses any charge.
Most malls have free charging stations, including the Oshawa Centre, where I often shop. Their stations are near the entrance, which is amazing. At home, we figure the car charging adds about $25 a month to our hydro bill. Andy figures it would cost $4.88 to fully charge it if it was plugged in every day, but as I'm only making a 16-km round-trip to work, I don't need to plug it in daily.
Andy drives a GMC Silverado and would love to get an electric truck. We use the truck to take my dressage horse to shows and electric trucks don't get good enough range if you're pulling a horse trailer for us to consider getting one at this time."
CAPTION: Sue Pallotta of Port Perry loves her 2023 Chevy Bolt EUV. She never has gas or oil change bills, and a full charge of its battery lasts her for days.
CREDIT: Tracy Hanes Special to the Star

Document 221 of 252

Flip-flop won't undo Ford's damage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/flip-flop-wont-undo-fords-damage/docview/2913644864/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Flip-flop+won%27t+undo+Ford%27s+damage&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Immediately upon winning power in 2018, Doug Ford's Tories unplugged wind and solar power.
Now, five years after ripping up so many signed contracts, there's a reckoning over renewable energy. Back in power after two election cycles, Ford is ready to rehabilitate the wind turbines he once dreamed of uprooting from Ontario.
New year, new policy. Another about-face from Ontario's seat-of-the-pants premier.
Fresh from reversing direction on the Greenbelt, Ford is reversing polarity on green energy. Energy Minister Todd Smith, who like the premier came to power huffing and puffing against wind turbines, is now boasting about bringing them back to the towns that ran them out of town.
All that said, Smith says the Tories will right the wrongs of the last Liberal government. He promises renewables will be restored to Ontario at roughly half of the old rates, and at a far lower political price. That's because the cost of wind and solar has dropped steadily, thanks to innovation and competition. Smith notes the Liberals made the mistake of overriding community objections, while the Tories are giving the final word to local governments before any turbines take root.
There's more. Ontario has belatedly resumed renewable energy swaps with Quebec, after quietly letting their bilateral deal lapse in late 2022. Smith had walked away from a signed agreement for hydroelectricity, negotiated by the Liberals in 2015, but recently signed a new 10-year deal with Quebec.
While it's a relief that Ford and Smith are finally seeing the error of their ways, on this as on other issues, that doesn't diminish the folly and futility of their first approach.
Where once electricity demand was seen as stalling, in line with a faltering economy, the latest projections suggest Ontario is falling behind its future needs for clean power. In short, after wasting the last five years as a bystander while the world passed them by, the Tories had to get back in the energy game to take account of long-term trends.
To be sure, the Liberals paid a price for being too far ahead of the curve with wind turbines. They tried to preposition the province for a new era of green jobs, overpaying for renewables contracts as a way of kick-starting the industry, handing the province a competitive advantage over its rivals.
The backlash came quickly. The idea soon took root that these generous incentive payments were responsible for the perceived rise in electricity rates - a political myth propounded by the Tories when they were in opposition. In fact, renewables always made up such a small proportion of the total energy supply - a fraction of the increase in monthly utility bills. But those concerns were hyped into a pre-election issue in 2018 that magically vanished, postelection, from the political sphere.
By aborting the experiment in renewable power when they came to power in 2018, however, the Tories introduced hidden costs into the system:
First, the "opportunity cost" (as economists call it) of absenting Ontario from the latest currents in renewable energy over the past five years, while the world of electric vehicles (EVs) and battery manufacturing moved by leaps and bounds adds up to a foregone opportunity to keep the province up to speed.
Second, there is the real damage to Ontario's reputation as a reliable place to do business after Ford so recklessly ordered the cancellation of 750 contracts. That he did so while simultaneously declaring, fatuously and hypocritically, that "Ontario is open for business," further eroded his credibility.
Yes, foreign investors are still coming to Ontario to make the most of EV demand, but at the expense of massive incentives from the federal and provincial governments. If Ontario's reputation for reliability on renewables were less chequered, the need for tax incentives might have been lessened for investors seeking a clean energy sanctuary with certainty.
Third, Ontario is falling farther behind its obligation to meet Ottawa's draft Clean Electricity Regulations that speak of a net-zero grid by 2030. We won't know for a while if willing hosts will be found for wind and solar to enable the Ford government to meet its new targets of 2,000 megawatts starting this year, plus an additional 3,000 megawatts in future years, let alone the more ambitious goals required for net-zero.
If the Liberals overdid the overriding of local sentiments, the Tories may find that an entirely hands-off approach won't get them where they need to go. But that, in the end, may be part of the plan - the subtext behind the minister's latest soothing statements.
By going through the motions of renewing renewables, Smith is artfully distracting critics from a more difficult debate about Ontario's excessive reliance on gas-fired power plants that will continue to emit undesirable levels of carbon. But that is increasingly in the nature of power politics in this province - an inability to level with ratepayers and taxpayers about the true cost levels, demand curves and supply lines.
Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial politics
and international affairs for Torstar.
CAPTION:

Document 222 of 252

Several ServiceOntario spots moving to Staples: Unknown number of locations to close in what opposition parties decry as privatization of services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/several-serviceontario-spots-moving-staples/docview/2913404946/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Several+ServiceOntario+spots+moving+to+Staples%3A+Unknown+number+of+locations+to+close+in+what+opposition+parties+decry+as+privatization+of+services&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You could soon be getting your driver's license or health card while surrounded by reams of paper and shiny new printers, instead of the usual grey and green of ServiceOntario's offices.
The Ontario government is shuttering the doors of an unknown number of ServiceOntario locations and will replace them with new counters within Staples stores.
While opposition parties have decried the move as further privatization of public services by Premier Doug Ford's government, many ServiceOntario locations has been privatized and franchised for years.
The new partnership with Staples was announced last month, on Dec. 7, "to help provide services where people already live, work and shop," public service delivery minister Todd McCarthy said in a press release.
However, the original press release from the government did not mention that several ServiceOntario locations would be closing because of the partnership.
Doug Allingham, a spokesperson for McCarthy, confirmed to the Star that some ServiceOntario locations would be closing, but did not say how many or where.
"All ServiceOntario locations that are closing will have a new location open in select Staples Canada stores, not kiosks, and service will remain uninterrupted," Allingham said, while emphasizing that customers will see an increase in service thanks to Staples' longer hours.
"Many ServiceOntario providers are private, including those in communities where locations are moving to Staples Canada stores," he added. "Employees of impacted private services providers are also being provided opportunities to continue employment with Staples Canada."
ServiceOntario already has branches within Canadian Tire stores, including at its Yonge-Davenport and St. Clair-Old Stock Yards locations in Toronto. The partnership began in 2010 under then-premier Dalton McGuinty.
Allingham said the government considered a number of factors when deciding to partner with Staples, including the size of stores, parking availability and number of locations across Ontario.
New Democrat MPP Catherine Fife said the new partnership is "another attempt for Ford to quietly hand over our public services to private corporations," and lamented the "uncertainty" that some public servants would face if ServiceOntario locations were to close.
In the same vein, Green Leader Mike Schreiner took aim the "back room deals" that he alleges the Ford government has prioritized over everyday Ontarians and questioned why Staples was chosen as the government's partner.
Previous governments had also tried introducing automated ServiceOntario kiosks, but those were permanently shut down in 2012 due to security concerns.
But the human element hasn't been without its issues.
In December, several ServiceOntario employees were charged in Toronto police's Project Safari investigation and were alleged to have provided criminals with vehicle and driver information.
CAPTION: The ServiceOntario partnership with Staples was announced in December "to help provide services where people already live, work and shop," according to the provincial government.Vince Talottatoronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star

Catholic hierarchy refuses same-sex blessings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/catholic-hierarchy-refuses-same-sex-blessings/docview/2913404888/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Catholic+hierarchy+refuses+same-sex+blessings&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the greatest rebuke yet to Pope Francis, the Catholic bishops of Africa and Madagascar issued a unified statement Thursday refusing to follow his declaration allowing priests to offer blessings to same-sex couples and asserting that such unions are "contrary to the will of God."
The statement, signed by Congolese Cardinal Fridolin Ambongo on behalf of the symposium of African national bishops conferences, marked the closest thing to a continent-wide dissent from the declaration Francis approved Dec. 18 allowing priests to offer such blessings.
That declaration from the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith has sent shock waves through the Catholic Church, thrilling LGBTQ-plus Catholics as a concrete sign of Francis' message of welcome but alarming conservatives who fear core doctrines of the church are being ignored or violated.
The controversy has deepened a growing chasm between Francis' progressive, reform-minded papacy and the conservative church in much of the world, especially Africa, where the number of Catholics is growing at a faster rate than anywhere else.
The Vatican declaration restated traditional church teaching that marriage is a lifelong union between a man and woman.
But it allowed priests to offer spontaneous, non-liturgical blessings to same-sex couples seeking God's grace in their lives, provided such blessings aren't confused with the rites and rituals of a wedding.
In his statement, Ambongo said it wasn't appropriate for African priests to offer such blessings because of the scandal and confusion it would create.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: The Associated Press

Document 224 of 252

Here's how much Bonnie Crombie will make as Liberal leader

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-how-much-bonnie-crombie-will-make-as/docview/2913516989/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+how+much+Bonnie+Crombie+will+make+as+Liberal+leader&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberals will pay their new leader Bonnie Crombie - who steps down Friday as Mississauga mayor after nine years in the job - a salary of $185,000 a year, the Star has learned.
That will make Crombie the second-highest paid party leader in Ontario after Premier Doug Ford at $208,974 and ahead of Ford's cabinet ministers at $165,851.
Despite heading a small caucus of nine MPPs in third place at the legislature, the $185,000 is slightly more than the $180,886 pay packet earmarked for the head of the official opposition, New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles.
"The way the party looked at this, it's pretty much commensurate with the opposition leader's salary," said a senior party source who spoke confidentially Thursday to discuss internal deliberations.
The $185,000, however, is a "considerable" pay cut, the source added, noting the money is coming from the party's fundraising efforts because Crombie does not have a seat in the legislature where MPPs earn a base salary of $116,550.
As mayor, Crombie earned $293,255 in salary and benefits in 2022 - the last full year for which figures are available - for her role on Peel Region council and for sitting on the board of Alectra Utilities.
She told reporters after winning the leadership Dec. 2 that compensation arrangements would have to be made with party officials and set a fundraising target of $1 million by the end of the year to boost the Liberal war chest for the 2026 provincial election.
Ford taunted the Liberals for that comment.
"Their number-one concern for their new leader is, 'Let's raise a million dollars because someone has to pay my salary' ... that shows me you picked the wrong leader," the premier said in the legislature's question period.
Crombie dismissed that and other attacks from Ford's Progressive Conservatives as "lame" and "desperate" attempts to distract voters after their flip-flop in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal and subsequent criminal investigation by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police.
As a result of Crombie's fundraising appeal, the Liberals exceeded the goal and raised $1.2 million by Dec. 31, bringing to the total for last year to $6 million as they look to rebound after disastrous election defeats in 2018 and 2022 and close what she calls a "considerable" fundraising gap with the Conservatives.
More "ambitious" fundraising targets will be set within weeks with milestones for the rest of the year, the party source told the Star.
Crombie will be at Queen's Park for regular weekly caucus meetings with her MPPs, but is spending much of her time on the road building support for the party while keeping an eye out for MPP resignations that could present an opening for her get a seat in the legislature.
She has ruled out seeking the party nomination in the longtime Conservative stronghold of Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, a sprawling rural riding west of London, that became available when Monte McNaughton resigned as labour minister last fall to return to the private sector.
Conservatives will hold a nomination meeting there at the end of January with Ford expected to call a byelection soon after.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie takes questions from the media at Queens Park on Dec. 5, 2023.

Subject: Nominations; Leadership; Legislatures; Wages & salaries

Former NDP leader Ed Broadbent dies at 87

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-ndp-leader-ed-broadbent-dies-at-87/docview/2913516778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+NDP+leader+Ed+Broadbent+dies+at+87&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Ed Broadbent, the former Ontario MP who led the federal New Democrats from 1975 to 1989, has died at the age of 87.
"It is with the heaviest of hearts that the Broadbent Institute announces the passing of our founder," the think tank Broadbent founded in 2011 said in a statement Thursday.
"Our country has lost a fierce champion for ordinary Canadians, an intellectual who strongly believed in building a good society. Ed devoted decades of his life to fighting for justice and equality in Canada and around the world. As a member of Parliament, leader of the New Democratic Party, president of Rights & Democracy and beyond, he tirelessly advocated for all people, especially those on the margins of society."
More to come.
CAPTION: Ed Broadbent led the federal New Democrats from1975 to 1989.
