Document 226 of 252

Jan. 11: Snow removal for seniors a problem and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-11-snow-removal-seniors-problem-other-letters/docview/2913136558/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+11%3A+Snow+removal+for+seniors+a+problem+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Snow removal a problem
Re: Program could help seniors (Jan. 9)
I read the letter from Roger Lambert with great interest. I'm 72 years old and cannot shovel the snow myself anymore due to a shoulder injury and a bad back.
I wish some young kids would knock on my door and shovel the snow. I've offered a young boy $10, $15 or $20 to shovel for me depending on the amount of snow we get. He doesn't want to do the snow for me.
The City of Hamilton used to have a "snow angel" program, but no one hardly volunteered for it. I was on the waiting list for about 10 years. I'm still hoping someone will come around to help me.
Frank Hansen, Hamilton
PM's vacation a nonstory
Re: Jan. 9 editorial cartoon
I am not a huge fan of The Spec's editorial cartoonist, Graeme MacKay, so I wasn't surprised but saddened that he has fallen for the federal Conservative Party's rhetoric and criticism of Justin Trudeau's holiday vacation to Jamaica.
Criticizing the PM for going on vacation while Canadians suffer? That is such a stretch it defies logic, but then again, the federal Conservatives have recently had problems with the truth.
I understand the purpose of editorial cartooning, but to perpetuate a nonstory seems a little far fetched. Maybe readers should be directed to the editorial cartoon published in the Toronto Star on the same day, which depicts Pierre Poilievre blaming the demise of dinosaurs on Trudeau. Now, that's funny!
Sue Prestedge, Hamilton
No need to hike premiums
Re: Here are the changes to CPP deductions starting in 2024 (Jan. 1)
Here we go again. CPP premiums for workers and employers are going up again. Why, when we are sitting on over $542 billion in this fund?
This is a tax on workers and employers and a deterrent for hiring. There is no way we will ever spend this money, especially the way they hand it out. Seniors need more help these days with inflation and grocery prices.
We seem to be losing our rights at every turn. CPP says premiums are going up and that's the end of the conversation. In the next 200 years, we will not spend this money and at one-per-cent interest a year, that is billions in returns a year. This is an assault on the working class and employers.
Scott Ramsay, Burlington
A study in contrasts
I recently watched a documentary on PBS, "Magical Imperfection: The life and architecture of Raymond Moriyama." What a wonderful man. A great architect who came across as a very knowledgeable and compassionate man.
Once a student at Westdale, he's also recognized on a Canadian stamp for his architectural contributions to society.
It just so happens that he designed Ontario Science Centre and, after watching the documentary, you understand how he put his heart and soul into his structures, taking into account the human aspect and the natural elements related to his projects.
He was also responsible for the Goh Ohn bell commemorating 100 years of Japanese settlement in Canada and, guess what, it is situated at Ontario Place, with plans to move it to make room for Premier Doug Ford's Spa. The shame of Ford's legacy: destroy, destroy, destroy. Heaven help us should we try to preserve history or nature in any form when we can bulldoze it into oblivion.
To quote Moriyama, "The greed is conquering too many people."
Another of his buildings, the Japanese Canadian Cultural Centre, is to be destroyed in Toronto to make room for a condo with lovely views over Don Valley. Think of the money to be made out of the sales there for Ford's developer friends.
While Moriyama had a vision, "What can I do to make Canada a better place," Doug and his buds have only the dollar sign vision and no social consciousness.
Irene Hough, Hamilton
Memory of Jan. 6 still fresh
As I changed my desk calendar to Jan. 6, I momentarily forgot why it rang a bell. And then, all of the emotions that I felt that day three years ago came back.
The first emotions that I felt then were embarrassment and humiliation for our American neighbours. How could something like this happen right at the country's Capitol? Had Americans really sunk so low?
These emotions were followed by fear and concern - of what the country's enemies must be making of all this. The weakness and turmoil of the U.S. being projected abroad. Sure enough, one year later, Russia invaded Ukraine. Two years later, a Chinese spy balloon flew across American skies. Today, North Korean-manufactured missiles bought by Russia rain down on Ukraine.
The last emotions felt that day were of anger and for retribution against the "Insurrector in Chief" who instigated all this. Notwithstanding the fact that hundreds of people who participated in that insurrection have been convicted of various offences, he remains free and, incomprehensibly, will likely stand for election later this year.
However, I have every confidence that, at some point, the law will catch up to him. There is a reason why patience is a virtue. We just need to get through this year and, perhaps, another four after that.
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
CAPTION: Teenagers walk along an unplowed Sherbrooke Street, looking for work shovelling snow. A Hamilton senior writes today that he wishes it was easier to get help clearing his snow.

Subject: Architecture; Rebellions; Employers; Emotions; Editorial cartoons; Vacations; Snow removal

Business indexing term: Subject: Architecture Employers

Location: Russia; Canada; Ukraine

Document 228 of 252

Justin Trudeau responds to calls for probe into allegations of sexual assault by Hamas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-responds-calls-probe-into/docview/2913202758/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+responds+to+calls+for+probe+into+allegations+of+sexual+assault+by+Hamas&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Calls by women from across the political spectrum for Canada to play a role in investigating Hamas's use of sexual violence in its Oct. 7 attacks on Israel are finally being answered. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has ordered two key cabinet ministers to look into exactly how Canada could support a request from former Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne, former deputy Conservative leader Lisa Raitt and others to commit $1 million to support victims and survivors, and send the RCMP to Israel to aid in investigations. But sources privy to Ottawa's deliberations say their first option - a repeat of the mechanism used to support a probe of Russia's use of sexual violence in Ukraine - faltered as that funding was delivered through the International Criminal Court, and Israel does not recognize the court's jurisdiction. Now, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc are exploring alternatives. "Our hearts are with the victims and their families impacted by sexual violence," Trudeau wrote in a letter obtained by the Star that was sent to Wynne, Raitt, former Ontario NDP MPP Cheri DiNovo and others. "Our government will always stand against sexual and gender-based violence and advocate for justice for all victims." Ottawa's move comes as the United Nations is now sending an envoy to Israel to investigate reports of Hamas's sex crimes, according to statements from Israel's Foreign Affairs Ministry reported in The New York Times on Wednesday. Pramila Patten, the special representative of the UN secretary-general on sexual violence in conflict, will take on her investigation as two other UN experts said the reports emerging from Hamas's Oct. 7 attacks suggest they may also qualify as crimes against humanity. Allegations of sexual torture include gang rapes, sexual assaults, mutilations and gunshots to genital areas. Female bodies were found with their clothing pulled up to their waists, with underpants removed or torn or stained with blood, the two noted in a statement released this week. Three months ago, Hamas - which Canada has designated a terrorist organization - infiltrated Israel, killing an estimated 1,200 people and taking as many as 250 people hostage. Roughly half of the hostages have now been released.Israel's retaliation is ongoing and the latest estimates from the Hamas-run government in Gaza say more than 23,200 people have been killed. Its death toll does not distinguish between combatants and civilians. A spokesman for LeBlanc, who oversees the RCMP, told the Star that Canada is committed to seeking justice for victims. "Our government unequivocally condemns the horrific attacks perpetrated by Hamas militants against innocent Israeli citizens on Oct. 7," Jean-Sebastien Comeau said. "We continue to look at ways to support investigative efforts into instances of gender-based violence that took place on Oct. 7, and are committed to seeking justice for victims."In Toronto, Wynne said she was "pleased that the government is seriously working on providing funding that can be used for investigation and support for victims and witnesses of these atrocities."
"It is not question of whether these crimes of rape and sexual assault took place at the hands of Hamas on Oct. 7. We know they did. It is a question now of holding the perpetrators to account," said the former premier.
"For generations the violation of women has been a reality of war that too often has been ignored or minimized because of the context. That cannot happen any longer,"
Wynne's letter was followed by an opinion article in the Star from former B.C. premier Christy Clark, former interim Conservative leader Rona Ambrose, former Liberal Ontario education minister Liz Sandals and Laureen Harper, a prominent Conservative and wife of former prime minister Stephen Harper. They added their voices to a growing chorus demanding Ottawa take action, and pushed back against those who dismissed, downplayed or even denied that Hamas attackers had sexually assaulted Israelis.
Supporting Israeli women matters beyond Israel's borders, the leaders wrote.
"It does not just send a message to the women of Israel that they are believed, but to women who are victims of rape all around the world, that you are believed," they said.
"For those who were murdered and don't have a voice, we must be their voice."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Kathleen Wynne, then Ontario's premier, are shown in Toronto on Oct. 17, 2017.

Subject: Gender-based violence; Criminal investigations; Sex crimes; Murders & murder attempts; Prime ministers; Rape

Location: Canada; Israel

People: Trudeau, Justin; Wynne, Kathleen

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Title: Justin Trudeau responds to calls for probe into allegations of sexual assault by Hamas

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 10, 2024

Section: politics/federal

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 229 of 252

Doug Ford's flip-flop on wind turbines won't undo all the damage he caused

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-flip-flop-on-wind-turbines-wont-undo/docview/2912701996/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+flip-flop+on+wind+turbines+won%27t+undo+all+the+damage+he+caused&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Immediately upon winning power in 2018, Doug Ford's Tories unplugged wind and solar power.
Now, five years after ripping up so many signed contracts, there's a reckoning over renewable energy. Back in power after two election cycles, Ford is ready to rehabilitate the wind turbines he once dreamed of uprooting from Ontario.
New year, new policy. Another about-face from Ontario's seat-of-the-pants premier.
Fresh from reversing direction on the Greenbelt, Ford is reversing polarity on green energy. Energy Minister Todd Smith, who like the premier came to power huffing and puffing against wind turbines, is now boasting about bringing them back to the towns that ran them out of town.
All that said, Smith says the Tories will right the wrongs of the last Liberal government. He promises renewables will be restored to Ontario at roughly half of the old rates, and at a far lower political price.
That's because the cost of wind and solar has dropped steadily, thanks to innovation and competition. Smith notes that the Liberals made the mistake of overriding community objections, while the Tories are giving the final word to local governments before any wind turbines take root.
There's more. Ontario has belatedly resumed renewable energy swaps with Quebec, after quietly letting their bilateral deal lapse in late 2022. Smith had walked away from a signed agreement for hydroelectricity, negotiated by the Liberals in 2015, but recently signed a new 10-year deal with Quebec.
If all this good news sounds too good to be quite true, there's some truth to that. While it's a relief that Ford and Smith are finally seeing the error of their ways, on this as on other issues, that doesn't diminish the folly and futility of their first approach.
Where once electricity demand was seen as stalling, in line with a faltering economy, the latest projections suggest Ontario is falling behind its future needs for clean power. In short, after wasting the last five years as a bystander while the world passed them by, the Tories had to get back in the energy game to take account of long-term trends.
Even a stopped clock is accurate twice a day. But that's small consolation the rest of the time.
To be sure, the Liberals paid a price for being too far ahead of the curve with wind turbines. They tried to preposition the province for a new era of green jobs, overpaying for renewables contracts as a way of kick-starting the industry, handing the province a competitive advantage over its rivals.
The backlash came quickly. The idea soon took root that these generous incentive payments were responsible for the perceived rise in electricity rates - a political myth propounded by the Tories when they were in opposition.
In fact, renewables always made up such a small proportion of the total energy supply - a fraction of the increase in monthly utility bills. But those concerns were hyped into a pre-election issue in 2018 that magically vanished, postelection, from the political sphere.
By aborting the experiment in renewable power when they came to power in 2018, however, the Tories introduced hidden costs into the system:
First, the "opportunity cost" (as economists call it) of absenting Ontario from the latest currents in renewable energy over the past five years, while the world of electric vehicles (EVs) and battery manufacturing moved by leaps and bounds adds up a foregone opportunity to keep the province up to speed.
Second, there is the real damage to Ontario's reputation as a reliable place to do business after Ford so recklessly ordered the cancellation of 750 contracts. That he did so while simultaneously declaring, fatuously and hypocritically, that "Ontario is open for business," further eroded his credibility with private investors.
Yes, foreign investors are still coming to Ontario to make the most of EV demand, but at the expense of massive incentives from the federal and provincial governments. If Ontario's reputation for reliability on renewables were less chequered, the need for generous tax incentives might have been lessened for investors seeking a clean energy sanctuary with certainty. Either way, the province will have to pay a premium to compensate investors for the immeasurable risk of being sandbagged should Ford change his mind yet again.
Third, Ontario is falling farther behind its obligation to meet Ottawa's draft Clean Electricity Regulations that speak of a net-zero grid by 2030. We won't know for a while if willing hosts will be found for wind and solar to enable the Ford government to meet its new targets of 2,000 megawatts starting this year, plus an additional 3,000 megawatts in future years, let alone the more ambitious goals required for net-zero.
If the Liberals overdid the overriding of local sentiments, the Tories may find that an entirely hands-off approach won't get them where they need to go. But that, in the end, may be part of the plan - the subtext behind the minister's latest soothing statements.
By going through the motions of renewing renewables, Smith is artfully distracting critics from a more difficult debate about Ontario's excessive reliance on gas-fired power plants that will continue to emit undesirable levels of carbon. But that is increasingly in the nature of power politics in this province - an inability to level with ratepayers and taxpayers about the true cost levels, demand curves and supply lines.
Just ask the populist premier who talked of removing all of those renewables all those years ago.
CAPTION: While it's good news that Premier Doug Ford and his Energy Minister Todd Smith are finally seeing the error of their ways on renewable energy, it doesn't diminish the folly and futility of their first approach, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Subject: Turbines; Electric rates; Competitive advantage; Electricity; Alternative energy sources; Renewable resources

Business indexing term: Subject: Competitive advantage Renewable resources

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/2912215626/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Board opposed plans
Saving the Henderson General Hospital
on Hamilton Mountain (Jan. 3)
There is one great misconception in Mark Newman's comprehensive article. The news of the proposal to close the Henderson General Hospital all those years ago may have come to public attention through Hamilton Health Sciences, but it did not originate there. It was entirely an initiative of the provincial government. As a lowly member of the HHS board at that time, I can testify to the fact that there was strong opposition from the board, which included such notables as Peter George, president and vice-chancellor of McMaster University; mayor Bob Morrow; and Terry Cooke, then chair of the Region of Hamilton-Wentworth. Refusing to change course, the government sacked the board altogether, and CEO Scott Rowand was replaced by a government-appointed administrator.
Alan McPherson, Ancaster
Home care is insufficient
I recently had the opportunity to observe our home-care services in action, and they are appalling. My friend was caring for her husband at home. He had just been discharged from hospital and was to be provided with three hours of care a day. It was determined that he required two PSWs to come in for one hour, three times a day. I was present on two occasions when two PSWs arrived (I won't go into the difficulty of ensuring there were always two). On the first day, the second PSW to arrive did not even take off his coat prior to attending to my friend's care. On the second occasion, the two were in and out of the house within 14 minutes. So in 14 minutes, only the barest minimum of care was provided - definitely not the 60 minutes he should have received. Our loved ones deserve more, both the patients and their families. I don't know how people can manage if they do not have family or friends that can assist. Sadly, my friend no longer needs their services. Rest in peace, Willie.
B.J. Swanson, Hamilton
Incomprehensible violence
'Screams without words' (Jan. 6)
A letter to the editor without words is my response to the horrific description of the "Screams Without Words" article in Saturday's New York Times collaboration. It details how Hamas subjected Israeli women to sexual violence before killing them. How can one ever comprehend this violence?
Judith Keczan, Lincoln
What is city hall thinking?
Vrancor takes 'step back' from Hamilton
affordable-housing pitch (Jan. 4)
I couldn't believe my eyes when I read the front-page article. What are city politicians and staff thinking? Let me get this straight - the corner of King and Sanford, where I would be afraid to venture out in the dark, across from the most police-visited motel (Spectator statistics) and an area of rampant drug use, prostitution and crime, where a person is willing to spend multiple millions of dollars and donate a building to help alleviate the affordable housing mega crisis in our great city, and roadblocks are put up? What the hell is the city's warped mindset? So one of the two buildings is for-profit. Profit creates jobs and well-being for countless communities. I get it, what the city wants is blank cheques to use as it sees fit. Incredibly generous families such as the Juravinskis, Joyces, DeGrootes and Brayleys have given in the past, but for other purposes. Now, we are in more challenging times and need brick and mortar solutions to the housing crisis. The city is being given a golden opportunity here. Don't blow it.
Steven Hill, Hamilton
Where's Minister Jones?
With so much focus on the problems with health care in this province, I haven't seen Health Minister Sylvia Jones anywhere, even for the proverbial Progressive Conservative photo-op. Maybe I've missed something, but when you read about the amount of hallway medicine going on, ER overload and temporary closures around Ontario, you would think she might come out from hiding and shed some light and hope on the devastating state of affairs they refuse to address. I just think she's another cardboard cut-out of Ford's Progressive Conservatives because we know who makes the decisions.
Robert Panchyson, Burlington
Don't charge for vaccine
I am my mother's primary caregiver. She lives with my husband and I and she has congestive heart failure, COPD and dementia. On a recent visit to her family doctor, he recommended the RSV vaccine due to her health issues. I was more than surprised and disgusted with the fact that our government was charging seniors $280 for this vaccine. All seniors over 65 should be eligible for this vaccine free of charge; many could not afford to have it any other way. If the government looked at this with common sense, which they don't seem to have, they would realize it would lessen the burden on our health-care system by helping to keep our most vulnerable out of hospital. What is our country coming to? As a senior myself, I'm very concerned about the future of our health care for myself and my husband.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton

Jan. 9: Bedbugs on the bus, kids snow clearing for seniors and other letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-9-bedbugs-on-bus-kids-snow-clearing-seniors/docview/2912021388/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+9%3A+Bedbugs+on+the+bus%2C+kids+snow+clearing+for+seniors+and+other+letters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Program could help seniors
When I was a kid during the 1950s and 1960s, a large snowfall meant we could make a bit of money, which was scarce for kids back then.
Friends would gather and go out knocking on doors, asking if residents needed assistance. We would clear corner lots, including a driveway, for 50 cents. That was a lot of money for a 10 year old in the 1950s.
I realize today one of the problems the kids face is they must approach someone's door and ask if they need their snow removed. In today's world, that can be a bit intimidating and part of the reason, I believe, is the lack of social skills the kids are developing with their new cellphone technology.
I presented to the city many years ago a possible solution but was never contacted on this. My proposal is this: Produce large symbols that can easily be attached to windows and easily removed. Colour them red and have three types; a "snow shovel," a "lawn mower" and a "rake" covering all seasons. The elderly homeowner can place this symbol in the window, showing that they need assistance.
It removes the knocking on doors for the kids and asking if they need assistance. Homeowners should be able to obtain these symbols free of charge from the city. It works out to be much cheaper than $12 million.
Roger Lambert, Hagersville
Bus seat mystery solved
Re: Bedbugs are all over Hamilton - but where remains a mystery (Jan. 6)
This article answered one question for me: why do more and more buses have hard plastic seats instead of the previous plush ones? I thought it must be yet another cost cutting move. However, how transit director Maureen Cosyn Heath could say the hard plastic seats are more comfortable, I don't know.
As one who actually uses the HSR, I can say there's no way these seats are more comfortable than the previous ones And in the summer when we're wearing less padding, they will feel even harder and less accommodating!
Sandra Hanmer, Hamilton
Fund homeless initiatives first
I want my tax dollars used to fund the opening of warming centres with pillows, blankets, hot coffee and soup and sandwiches. I do not want my money to go to refurbishing the children's museum, the opening of two-way streets or any more useless roadway projects. I see people sleeping rough next to construction vehicles tearing up Gage Park. Meanwhile, a young man is outside homeless, begging for food and money for shelter. Am I the only one?
Katherine Borthwick, Hamilton
Premier Ford doesn't care
2023 was a hard year for many who had to spend precious time fighting to defeat the Greenbelt scam produced by Premier Doug Ford. Thankfully, there way two strong voices among many in NDP Leader Marit Stiles and common sense Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner. These two kept Ford under their careful watch and reported continuously to us the taxpayers.
It is time Ontario makes a clean sweep of the underhanded Ford and cast a ballot for the two above parities in 2026. Ford is not done with devastating Ontario nature and farm lands. He's now focused on building an unnecessary Highway 413, which according to CBC reporter Mike Crawley will wipe out 29 species at risk including the little Chorus frog. Ford simply does not care.
Hard working Ontario farmers bring billions to the Ontario coffers - once again, no respect from Ford.
The Ford government has launched a court challenge over federal government's plan to conduct an environmental assessment of the Hwy 413 route. Who will pay for this? The taxpayers and generations to come deprived of their rightful inheritance of unique lands.
Ford once again has portrayed himself as underhanded and unwilling to stop until all is paved and Ontario's beauty and worth simply no more. Vote wisely and recall these Ford days.
Faye Suthons, Wainfleet
Bulldogs welcome in new home
Re: Bulldogs turning into a success story in Brantford (Jan. 6)
Wonderful to see the Bulldogs embraced and appreciated by the city of Brantford. That's what happens when you have a mayor and a city council who actually do something.
David Robinson, Ancaster
Liberals are to blame, too
Re: Welfare rates now $200 a month below the Harris cuts of 1995 (Jan. 5)
The column in Saturday's Spectator by John Stapleton on welfare rates is more Conservative Party bashing. He completely sidesteps the 15 years the Liberal Party was in power, in which they could have corrected the problem he is writing about. It would be nice if newspaper contributors would not show their personal party bias, and report the facts.
Ross DePalma, Hamilton
A fan of the self-checkout
I love self-check outs. They're fast, easy and convenient. No one is in line behind me to jab me with their grocery cart, there's no cashier to bang and bruise my carefully selected bag of apples on the counter.
Most of all, I don't have to worry about a machine asking me loudly how I am doing if I don't feel like answering that day, without feeling guilty (about it).
Self-check outs are the best innovation ever. All stores/outlets should adopt self-checkouts.
Concetta Maini, Hamilton
CAPTION: The city should produce large symbols that can easily be attached to windows. The could have three types; a "snow shovel," a "lawn mower" and a "rake" covering all seasons. The elderly homeowner can place this symbol in the window showing that they need assistance, a reader writes.

Document 232 of 252

Provincial budgets are a chance for Hamilton to send a message to Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/provincial-budgets-are-chance-hamilton-send/docview/2912021288/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+budgets+are+a+chance+for+Hamilton+to+send+a+message+to+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Every spring in Ontario brings flowers, warmer weather and surely everyone's favourite: a new provincial budget.
While it may not be the top of mind for everyone, the provincial budget has huge implications for how the province plans to spend its money - and in turn, what critical services and infrastructure will look like for Ontarians over the coming year.
These aren't minor services. The budget will have a huge impact on wait times for ambulances, the number of early childhood educators present in each classroom, the number of nurses available for life-saving procedures, the number of children who will receive autism services -- so many important things that Ontarians rely on are impacted by the provincial budget.
This week, the pre-budget committee will finally return in-person to Hamilton for the first time in right years as it put together the 2024 budget. It'll be the first chance since 2016 for organizations in Hamilton to make their pitch directly to the government about the support they provide to the community, and what kind of support and resources they require to get the job done.
Your official opposition NDP are part of this committee, and we're expecting to hear stories from Hamilton organizations who have had their funding stagnate or cut under this Conservative government. We're expecting to hear about the need for a renewed emphasis on the environment and conservation after a successful fight to save the Greenbelt. We're expecting to again hear about burned out health care and social services professionals who are struggling in under-resourced hospitals.
As our finance critic, MPP Catherine Fife, always reminds me - budgets are about priorities. They reflect the conscious choices of a government and show who they really are putting first.
In last year's budget, we saw Ford's Conservatives sit on billions of dollars in contingency funds instead of investing that money in our public health-care system. We saw an emphasis placed on lining the pockets of land speculators instead of implementing real affordable housing solutions, like rent control. It's clear where their priorities lie.
This year, things are different. From the Hamilton urban boundary expansion to the Greenbelt, we've proved that we can take on this government - and win.
This Wednesday, when Hamilton organizations make their pitch to the government - the NDP will be listening. While Ford and the Conservatives are busy trying to cover up their corruption scandal, we'll be helping to raise your voices right up to the halls of Queen's Park and into the 2024 budget.
Last year in Hamilton, we saw an incredible display of people power. In fact, more organizations signed up this year than there were even time slots available. This week, I know we'll be seeing democracy in action once again as local organizations raise their voices to help our community.
See you there.
CAPTION: The provincial budget will have a huge impact on many important things that Ontarians rely on, such as wait times for ambulances, the number of early childhood educators present in each classroom, the number of nurses available for life-saving procedures and the number of children who will receive autism services, MPP Sandy Shaw writes.
CREDIT: Sandy Shaw

Subject: Autism; Provincial budget; Lifesaving; Budgets

Wonderful to see the Bulldogs embraced and appreciated by the city of Brantford. That's what happens when you have a mayor and a city council who actually do something.
David Robinson, Ancaster
Liberals are to blame, too
Re: Welfare rates now $200 a month
below the Harris cuts of 1995 (Jan. 5)
The column in Saturday's Spectator by John Stapleton on welfare rates is more Conservative party bashing. He completely sidesteps the 15 years the Liberal party was in power, in which they could have corrected the problem he is writing about. It would be nice if newspaper contributors would not show their personal party bias, and report the facts.
Ross DePalma, Hamilton

Document 234 of 252

Budget meeting is a chance to send a message to Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/budget-meeting-is-chance-send-message-ford/docview/2911758196/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Budget+meeting+is+a+chance+to+send+a+message+to+Ford&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Every spring in Ontario brings flowers, warmer weather and surely everyone's favourite: a new provincial budget.
While it may not be the top of mind for everyone, the provincial budget has huge implications for how the province plans to spend its money - and in turn, what critical services and infrastructure will look like for Ontarians over the coming year.
These aren't minor services. The budget will have a huge impact on wait times for ambulances, the number of early childhood educators present in each classroom, the number of nurses available for life-saving procedures, the number of children who will receive autism services - so many important things that Ontarians rely on are impacted by the provincial budget.
This week, the pre-budget committee will finally return in-person to Hamilton for the first time in eight years as it put together the 2024 budget. It'll be the first chance since 2016 for organizations in Hamilton to make their pitch directly to the government about the support they provide to the community, and what kind of support and resources they require to get the job done.
Your official opposition NDP are part of this committee, and we're expecting to hear stories from Hamilton organizations who have had their funding stagnate or cut under this Conservative government. We're expecting to hear about the need for a renewed emphasis on the environment and conservation after a successful fight to save the Greenbelt.
We're expecting to again hear about burned out health care and social services professionals who are struggling in under-resourced hospitals.
As our finance critic, MPP Catherine Fife, always reminds me - budgets are about priorities. They reflect the conscious choices of a government and show who they really are putting first.
In last year's budget, we saw Ford's Conservatives sit on billions of dollars in contingency funds instead of investing that money in our public health-care system. We saw an emphasis placed on lining the pockets of land speculators instead of implementing real affordable housing solutions, like rent control. It's clear where their priorities lie.
This year, things are different. From the Hamilton urban boundary expansion to the Greenbelt, we've proved that we can take on this government - and win.
On Wednesday at the Sheraton Hamilton Hotel, when local organizations make their pitch to the government - the NDP will be listening. While Ford and the Conservatives are busy trying to cover up their corruption scandal, we'll be helping to raise your voices right up to the halls of Queen's Park and into the 2024 budget.
Last year in Hamilton, we saw an incredible display of people power. In fact, more organizations signed up this year than there were even time slots available. This week, I know we'll be seeing democracy in action once again as local organizations raise their voices to help our community.
See you there.
Sandy Shaw is the MPP for Hamilton West -Ancaster -Dundas.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Sandy Shaw Sandy Shaw is the MPP for Hamilton West -Ancaster -Dundas.;

Document 235 of 252

Blame Reagan for distrust of science

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/blame-reagan-distrust-science/docview/2911084650/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Blame+Reagan+for+distrust+of+science&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I have puzzled over the quite odd (in my mind) distrust of science and experts among politicians on the political right and their supporters. This is most evident when we look at the COVID-19 pandemic and climate change.
As the world endured COVID, I was dumbfounded by the (at times) fury and disdain directed by right-wing politicians such as former U.S. president Donald Trump and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre at public health figures such as Dr. Anthony Fauci in the United States and Dr. Teresa Tam in Canada.
We saw former Alberta conservative premier Jason Kenney declaring Alberta "open for business" (against medical advice) in May 2021, when COVID was still rampant. By August 2021, Alberta hospitals were staggering under the strain of treating COVID patients, and some had to be sent to other provinces.
In the U.S. in 2020, we saw Trump flouting mask mandates, bellowing "liberate Michigan" (from public health mandates). Then in 2022, Poilievre openly embraced the so-called "Freedom Convoy," which advocated for the end of all public health mandates.
What we didn't see, (as far as I am aware of) are left-wing or "progressive" politicians questioning the medical science behind pandemic measures. (This doesn't excuse the fact that some progressive politicians appeared somewhat "lax" in enforcing lockdowns and social distancing mandates during civil unrest at this time.)
We have also seen distrust of science with climate change. In the fall of 2022, the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) reported "In 2020, 88 per cent of Democrats agreed with scientific findings that climate change was a major threat to the well-being of the United States, but only 31 per cent of Republicans thought so. Similarly, 94 per cent of Democrats believe that the documented increase in global temperature is due to human activities (again, consistent with the scientific consensus), but only 69 per cent of Republicans do."
In Canada, we see Poilievre vowing to abolish the carbon tax, which is in fact a market-based solution that conservatives would normally embrace to change unwanted behaviour. He refuses to provide Canadians with a science-based alternative. Trump vows to "drill, drill, drill" (for oil) on his first day back in office if he wins the 2024 election.
Why does the right appear to distrust science? Certainly this hasn't been a foundation of conservative thought forever. Republican president Dwight Eisenhower embraced science to get America into the space race in the 1950s. He endorsed medical science as polio vaccines were distributed that same decade. What changed?
I believe this dates back to the 1980s and American president Ronald Reagan. He argued "government is not the solution to our problem, government is the problem." This has been a "mantra" of the conservative movement ever since. But it is problematic.
This implies one should not trust the government. Public health guidelines are laid down by government officials - thus according to conservatives we shouldn't trust them. That is what is being suggested here. It also implies governments are incompetent. Think about these further words from Reagan.
"The nine scariest words in the English language are 'I'm from the government, and I'm here to help.' "
If you truly believe that, are you thus inclined to accept advice or instructions from government medical experts? And if conservatives followed this advice would they not be repudiating their core beliefs?
It appears that many conservatives are trapped in a flawed ideology that does not allow them to respond rationally to serious crises. It isn't logical to embrace a philosophy that may very well lead to death.
Conservatives should look to their past and rethink some of their core beliefs.
Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of five books.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Craig Wallace Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of five books.;

Subject: Public health; Conservatism; Science; Politics; Politicians; Climate change; Pandemics; COVID-19

Document 236 of 252

Saving the Henderson General Hospital: The former institution, now Juravinski Hospital on Concession Street, was slated to be turned into an urgent care centre in 2000. Mountain News reporter Mark Newman recounts the role the community, including the newspaper, played in the fight to stop the plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/saving-henderson-general-hospital/docview/2911084648/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Saving+the+Henderson+General+Hospital%3A+The+former+institution%2C+now+Juravinski+Hospital+on+Concession+Street%2C+was+slated+to+be+turned+into+an+urgent+care+centre+in+2000.+Mountain+News+reporter+Mark+Newman+recounts+the+role+the+community%2C+including+the+newspaper%2C+played+in+the+fight+to+stop+the+plan&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Work on the proposed $2.3-billion redevelopment of Juravinski Hospital is set to begin in mid-2028.
According to Hamilton Health Sciences' website, the ambitious project includes the demolition of the hospital's four oldest wings.
They will be replaced by modern patient care areas, including 417 newly constructed beds, of which 170 will be additional beds beyond the current capacity for a total of 534 beds, and all beds will be moved to single rooms.
There will also be new in-patient units offering expanded critical, cancer and surgical care and in-patient rehabilitation.
The proposed upgrade is in stark contrast to what HHS was proposing for the site nearly 24 years ago.
Faced with a $40-million-plus deficit, HHS officials announced around early 2000 they were going to close the Henderson as an acute care hospital and turn the facility into an acute care centre in a bid to save money.
It was a time when the then-PC government of Mike Harris was trimming health-care spending.
Rumours of the closing started to surface weeks before the announcement.
Debbie Mattina, a Henderson General Hospital X-ray technician and member of Canadian Union of Public Employees Local 794, was also the writer/editor of the local's broadsheet monthly news publication.
Mattina recalled maintenance staffers at the hospital noticing that new equipment was being delivered to the Henderson, recorded and then moved out to other HHS locations, while several departments were being "starved" of supplies.
"When you needed something replaced, it was never replaced," Mattina recalled. "That's what led them to believe something here's going on, they're getting ready to close this hospital."
The union organized a meeting with Hamilton Mountain Liberal MPP Marie Bountrogianni at her nearby office on Concession Street, where they explained their concerns and even arranged for Bountrogianni to tour the hospital to see the shortages for herself.
After a second meeting at Bountrogianni's office, they all agreed something was going on and the Save the Henderson committee was organized, with Mattina as its chair and spokesperson.
The group started speaking publicly and to the media about its concerns.
"We agreed we had to do something, we had to bring it to the public's attention that this was happening," Mattina said.
"We asked the hospital execs (if the Henderson was closing) and they said 'no,' " Bountrogianni recalled.
Despite the denials, HHS officials announced a few weeks later the Henderson site would be turned into an urgent care centre.
"From our perspective, it didn't make any sense to close that hospital," Mattina said.
The committee, with support from other unions and organizations, set to work posting flyers, holding information pickets and candlelight vigils in front of the hospital, as well as other events, including a march from Mountain Drive Park to the hospital for a rally.
In March, a town-hall meeting about the closure packed the auditorium of Sherwood Secondary School.
Bountrogianni, who chaired the meeting, recalled several doctors and other staff from the Henderson spoke against the plan, adding they were concerned about people getting down the Mountain to one of the lower city hospitals if they needed emergency care, especially in poor weather.
"They were talking about how there wouldn't be enough time to help people in urgent situations, especially on the east Mountain," Bountrogianni said.
The Mountain MPP said the effort to save the Henderson included all Hamilton MPPs, including Stoney Creek Tory MPP Brad Clark, who was parliamentary assistant to Health Minister Elizabeth Witmer at the time.
"We agreed that we all would support keeping it as an acute care hospital," Bountrogianni said.
"The three of us (Clark, Bountrogianni and Hamilton West NDP MPP David Christopherson) had created a team Hamilton approach," Clark recalled. "The three of us agreed to put our partisan politics aside and work together for the betterment of Hamilton."
The turning point in the fight to save the Henderson occurred in the spring, when the committee organized two busloads of supporters to rally at Queen's Park and deliver several boxes to Witmer that contained a petition with more than 80,000 signatures to keep the hospital open.
"This was before online petitions," said Bountrogianni, who noted all the questions from the Liberal opposition and some from the NDP during question period that day were about the Henderson hospital.
Following question period, Bountrogianni arranged for Witmer to meet with Mattina and some of the Save the Henderson folks.
Mattina said she told Witmer that in Hamilton geography does matter and it's not as simple as moving Mountain emergency cases to another city hospital if the Henderson is closed.
"I said what most people don't realize is if we have bad weather in Hamilton, the (Mountain) accesses are closed," Mattina said. "I said if those accesses are closed, people are going to die."
Then Mattina said she got personal with the minister.
"I said if something happens to somebody I care about, I'm going to hold you personally responsible," Mattina said.
Even with the strong comments, Mattina said she found Witmer sympathetic to their cause and easy to talk to.
Three days later Mattina said Witmer came to Hamilton with a $43-million cheque to pay off the HHS deficit and later returned to announce not only would the Henderson stay open as an acute care hospital, but the province would look at building a new hospital on site.
A few years later, the Liberal Dalton McGuinty government committed more than $300 million to the new hospital project.
The first phase of the new and current hospital opened in September 2010 and the name was changed to recognize a multimillion-dollar donation to the HHS foundation by Charles and Margaret Juravinski.
"With the exception of getting married and having my kids, that was the event of my life," Mattina said.
She also confirmed that Clark was working behind the scenes and advised the committee on which government officials to speak to and what to say.
"My advice to them was to write directly to the minister of health as well as the premier as well as calling the minister of health and premier's office to let them know what the concerns were," Clark recalled. "There was a bit of a fight between the political decision-makers and the people who were managing the hospitals at the time."
Clark said there was much discussion about the future of the Henderson among the decision-makers in the Harris government.
"We just shared with them the folly from a health-care sector with doing what was being proposed, but also from the political mindset," Clark said. "Why would you pick a fight with so many people in the City of Hamilton about a hospital?"
Ultimately premier Harris made the decision to keep the Henderson open, Clark said.
Ironically, a few months after the province stepped in to keep the hospital open Mattina said she drove her husband Angelo - who was not feeling well - to the emergency department at the Henderson, where he suffered three cardiac arrests and was revived and eventually fitted with a pacemaker.
Mattina said if she had to drive him a further distance to a lower city hospital Angelo might not have survived.
"I would have been a widow 23 years ago," said Mattina, who noted her husband is now on his third pacemaker.
During the Henderson situation, the Mountain News covered all the rallies and vigils and wrote numerous stories and editorials on what the impact of closing the Mountain's only acute care hospital would have on the community.
Clark said the articles were read each day by officials in the Ministry of Health as part of the newspaper clippings they received.
"I think this was the first time that people realized what a significant impact community newspaper had in Hamilton," Clark said.
"The Hamilton Mountain News was the first media outlet that believed (union and opposition MPP concerns) that they were planning on closing down the acute care hospital and turning it into an urgent care centre," Bountrogianni said.
Mattina said the media coverage helped marshal public opinion to pressure the province to keep the Henderson open.
"In my opinion (the Mountain News) was every bit as responsible for winning that fight as we were," Mattina said.
CAPTION: Juravinski Hospital (formerly Henderson General Hospital) on Concession Street was once slated to be turned intoan urgentcare centre.Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Mark Newman Reporter

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/2911084596/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City needs to enforce its parking rules
Enforcement before expansion (Jan. 5)
It looks like we are not the only neighbourhood that the parking enforcement is completely ignored. The writer is absolutely correct that complete ignorance of the 12-hour parking limit presents more of a danger in the winter as the street is now reduced to one lane as the snow plows have to go around these cars parked illegally. Yet absolutely nobody gets ticketed, just as those who don't shovel their sidewalk.
I had to call the bylaw office four times before someone came and ticketed a car parked for weeks in front of our home and made snow plowing almost impossible.
Why are we paying these exorbitant taxes if something as simple as a 12-hour parking limit is completely ignored?
Just enforce the rules and maybe we will see more compliance.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Will condo owners
get a break?
City council is looking at having all sidewalks cleared of snow at a huge cost.
This would increase property taxes by at least one per cent and probably more, depending on which clearing policy they adapt.
Are they going to give condo owners who already pay to have their private roads and sidewalks cleared a one per cent tax decrease? Seems only fair.
Dennis Martin, Binbrook
This relocation is
an abuse of power
Another flawed plan by Ford
(Dec. 11)
The fast-tracked and misguided planned redevelopment of Ontario Place, including relocation of the Ontario Science Centre, is an abuse of power. Ontario Place should remain an affordable, green, family friendly place along the waterfront. That's far better than converting this valuable land for a luxury private spa and indoor water park, built by Austria-based Therme, in a long-term lease.
The project will destroy 850 trees that support habitat for 170 bird species. Yet there has been an inexcusable failure to conduct a full environmental assessment. Relocating the Science Centre, a popular educational resource nestled in the Don Valley, is completely unnecessary. The new centre would be half the size of the existing building, which serves the public well, but needs refurbishment after years of neglect.
Despite a growing public outcry, the government insists on proceeding full speed ahead without providing a full comparison of projected costs and failing to consult the community and key stakeholders. Not only did the Conservatives pass a motion to bypass debate and public hearings, they added insult to injury by adjourning the legislature eight days ahead of schedule.
Critics suspect the government's haste is explained by contractual obligations for the spa and parking requirements. Let's hope grassroots voices, such as Ontario Place for All, will be able to convince Premier Doug Ford and the cabinet to reconsider this ill-advised mistake by the lake.
Gord McNulty, Hamilton
A better way exists
Give HSR drivers portable scanners (Jan. 5)
On a recent trip to northern Europe, I noted that transit vehicles all used all-door entry and each door area had at least one scan device for payment. The driver is no longer responsible to police payment upon entry either. Closer to home you'll find a similar but different system used in the GO trains and in the York Region Express bus system, these two use a station payment system all using the Presto card (plus others) and again the operator does not police the payment. These systems do have the roving payment inspection teams and frequent travellers on GO trains will have seen these in person. For driver safety, let's not move to a portable scanner, but to multiple-door entry with payment scanners at each door. Then have roving payment checkers that can write up the fine on the spot.
Ilpo Lehto, Hamilton
Simplify life
and focus on joy
Joy helps us navigate our life
(Jan. 5)
Shellene Drakes-Tull is right. Simplify life, eliminate unrealistic resolutions, focus on joy. I must admit that 2023 was particularly unjoyful for me. I lost my wife of 56 years suddenly to cancer - two short months after diagnosis.
Joy is currently elusive. I used to sign my emails, "Joyfully" - seeking to instil a little joy in recipients each day.
My colleagues in education with the public school board in past years often thanked me for that. I hope to be able to resume the practice some day - maybe even in 2024. I know the biblical statement, "The joy of the Lord is my strength." I experience tears more than joy just now. But the assurance that Janice is "with the Lord" is the bedrock for my eventual joy in the days to come.
Douglas Agnew, Burlington
Keep up the good work
I really like the great local reporting by J.P. Antonacci. It's always a pleasing read of Haldimand-Norfolk news. Keep up the great work.
Bob Reed, Simcoe
Tired of dropped calls
For those in the west end of Ancaster, our poor cell signal has now been an ongoing issue for four years. It has taken me months to finally find the agency's phone number we should all be complaining to. It's Spectrum at 1-855-465-6307. Ask for the southwestern Ontario office or your complaint will be useless. If you are fed-up with broken up or dropped calls and calls going directly to your voice mail, it's time we complained to this agency as one voice. It's the only way we'll get a new cell tower.
Lonnie Nelligan, Ancaster
Kudos to library staff
Congratulations to Burlington Public Library CEO Lita Barrie and her outstanding team for the service they provide. As a regular user of all facets of the main branch, I can attest to a helpful, attentive staff, great resources, innovative thinking like the theme displayed books in the central hallway, and the story-telling and play area. Keep up the good work!
David Estok, Burlington

Document 238 of 252

Blame Reagan for distrust of science

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/blame-reagan-distrust-science/docview/2911496806/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Blame+Reagan+for+distrust+of+science&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

In Canada we saw former Alberta conservative premier Jason Kenney declaring Alberta "open for business" (against medical advice) in May 2021, when COVID was still rampant. By August 2021, Alberta hospitals were literally staggering under the strain of treating COVID patients, and some patients had to be sent to other provinces for care.
In the U.S. in 2020 we saw Trump flouting mask mandates, bellowing "liberate Michigan" (from public health mandates). Hard to imagine a responsible, rational, nation's leader encouraging people to ignore medical advice. Then in 2022 Poilievre openly embraced the "Freedom Convoys" which advocated for the end of all public health mandates. These are just a few examples.
What we didn't see, (as far as I am aware of) are left-wing or "progressive" politicians questioning the medical science behind pandemic measures. (This doesn't excuse the fact that some progressive politicians appeared somewhat "lax" in enforcing lockdown and social distancing mandates during civil unrest at this time.)
We have also seen distrust of science with climate change. In the fall of 2022 the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) reported "In 2020, 88 per cent of Democrats agreed with scientific findings that climate change was a major threat to the well-being of the United States, but only 31 per cent of Republicans thought so. Similarly, 94 per cent of Democrats believe that the documented increase in global temperature is due to human activities (again, consistent with the scientific consensus), but only 69 per cent of Republicans do."
In Canada, we see Poilievre vowing to abolish the carbon tax which is in fact a market-based solution that conservatives would normally embrace to change unwanted behaviour. He refuses to provide Canadians with a science-based alternative. Former president Trump vows to "drill, drill, drill" (for oil) on his first day back in office if he wins the 2024 election.
Why does the right appear to distrust science? Certainly this hasn't been a foundation of conservative thought forever. Republican president Dwight Eisenhower embraced science to get America into the space race in the 1950s. He and Progressive Conservative prime minister John Diefenbaker endorsed medical science as polio vaccines were distributed that same decade. What changed?
I believe this dates back to the 1980s and American president Ronald Reagan. He argued "government is not the solution to our problem, government is the problem." This has been a "mantra" of the conservative movement ever since. But it is problematic.
First, this implies one should not trust the government. Public health guidelines are laid down by government officials - thus according to conservatives we shouldn't trust them. That is what is being suggested here.
It also implies that governments are incompetent. Think about these further words from former president Reagan.
"The nine scariest words in the English language are 'I'm from the government, and I'm here to help.' "
If you truly believe that, are you thus inclined to accept advice or instructions from government medical experts? And if conservatives followed this advice would they not be repudiating their core beliefs?
It appears that many conservatives are trapped in a flawed ideology that does not allow them to respond rationally to serious crises. It isn't logical to embrace a philosophy that may very well lead to death. Conservatives should look to their past and rethink some of their core beliefs.
CAPTION: American president Ronald Reagan argued "government is not the solution to our problem, government is the problem." This implies one should not trust the government and that governments are incompetent, Craig Wallace writes.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Document 239 of 252

Doug Ford's government called him with a 'real challenge' - so he packed his bags for Washington

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-government-called-him-with-real/docview/2910961079/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+government+called+him+with+a+%27real+challenge%27+-+so+he+packed+his+bags+for+Washington&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A former General Motors executive is putting the pedal to the metal as Ontario's new representative to Washington, D.C., helping the province on the road of "accelerated industrial renewal" sparked by two new electric vehicle battery plants.
"This is not one and done," David Paterson says of the massive Volkswagen and Stellantis EV factories under construction in St. Thomas and Windsor thanks to federal and provincial subsidies that matched American incentives and kept the plants from going to the United States.
"It's an ongoing transformation."
His assignment: create jobs at home by convincing more American businesses to set up shop here, while keeping a close watch on current trade issues and protecting provincial interests in the lead-up to the 2026 renegotiations of the USMCA free trade pact between the U.S., Mexico and Canada.
And there is the added drama of a U.S. presidential election this year with Donald Trump attempting a comeback.
"We'll leave the political decisions to American voters," Paterson says, noting he was on the scene for the last USMCA negotiations during the Trump administration.
"I had a front-row seat being in the auto sector, because probably 90 per cent of the agreement was automotive supply chain-related."
Beyond auto, the fast-paced world of business and technology includes rapid advancements in medical sciences, artificial intelligence and other fields that require a nimble approach to connect investors in the U.S. to research, expertise and markets in Ontario, the third-largest trading partner to the U.S. behind Mexico and China.
Appointed to the three-year posting in November, Paterson, 64, was fresh from retiring as GM Canada's vice-president of corporate and environmental affairs last June - and a Yukon canoe trip with old friends dating to elementary school - when Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli got in touch.
Paterson, whose CV includes senior roles at BlackBerry and Manulife, had previously made a courtesy phone call to Fedeli, letting him know he was leaving the automaker after an eventful decade that saw its massive Oshawa plant shut down and later spring back to life following a nudge from Unifor and the Ingersoll plant transformed for EV production.
"I wished him well and got thinking shortly after that, I didn't ask him, 'What are you going to be doing?' I just figured retirement meant retirement," Fedeli recalls.
"So I called him back and said, 'Hey, I don't know what your plans are, but if you're interested in public service, we've got a real challenge here."
Challenge accepted.
"I'm itching to get there," Paterson says in Toronto as he works on security and other clearances needed for the move to Washington, where he is arranging a place to live.
His predecessor in the $350,000-a-year job operating out of the Canadian embassy was Ian Todd, a former campaign adviser to Premier Doug Ford whose term ended in fall 2021, leaving a two-year gap that was covered by the province's small staff at the embassy and from Fedeli's Toronto office.
The move away from a traditional patronage appointment was intentional, Fedeli said, calling provincial trade representative postings in Washington and other U.S. and international cities "sales offices."
The Progressive Conservative government faced controversy over such patronage appointments in its first term, including a 26-year-old as agent general in New York who played university lacrosse with the son of former Ford chief-of-staff Dean French. French resigned over the nepotism scandal that included appointing his wife's cousin to a similar post in London.
"They're sales driven, not really diplomatic postings ... we want hardcore sales to happen," says Fedeli.
With seasoning in the tech and financial industries from his years at BlackBerry and Manulife, Paterson says he's familiar with the Washington scene and has a solid list of contacts.
"It's a relationship business," he adds, noting his plan is to reach out to governors and senators from U.S. states with strong links to Ontario to build on the existing trade relationships that total $450 billion a year between the two countries.
In a separate interview, Fedeli says he frequently reminds American officials that "nine million Americans wake up every morning to make products to ship to Canada" - which makes minimizing trade barriers equally important to both countries.
Maintaining a watchful eye on any protectionist "Buy American" pushes in the U.S. and the coming USMCA talks is also crucial for "fair treatment" of the auto, agricultural, lumber and other industries so important to Ontario's economy, Fedeli adds.
"All these things are always under siege by the U.S."
CAPTION: David Paterson is shown in Markham, Ont., on Dec. 2, 2021, when he was General Motors Canada's vice-president of corporate and environmental affairs.

Document 240 of 252

Welfare rates now worse than in 1995

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/welfare-rates-now-worse-than-1995/docview/2910805224/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Welfare+rates+now+worse+than+in+1995&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A housing list from the Scarborough Legal Clinic shows that for a single person, the lowest rent for shared accommodation in Scarborough is $650 a month, if you can get it.
Going back to 2018, the same rent could be obtained for $450 to $500 a month.
But if you spend $650 a month on rent and your maximum Ontario Works (OW) allowance is $733 a month, you simply can't afford the basics of food, clothing, personal care, a smartphone and transit.
Without connectivity or transit, you can neither hear nor read about work, nor can you travel to work even if you manage to secure it.
Premier Doug Ford recently spoke to the Empire Club and said: "What drives me crazy is people on Ontario Works - probably three, 400,000 - that are healthy. It really bothers me that we have healthy people sitting at home, collecting your hard-earned dollars. We need to encourage them to contribute back to the province and find gainful employment."
The PC government spent eight years in power from 1995 to 2003 and cut Ontario Works rates by 21.6 per cent and did not increase either OW or ODSP during their entire tenure in power. Between premiers Mike Harris and Ernie Eves, inflation from 1995 to 2003 rose 17.2 per cent.
Ford took power in 2018 and inflation from 2018 to 2023 has been 18 per cent. This means that inflation over two PC tenures since Bill Davis and Frank Miller has risen 35.2 per cent with no increases to Ontario Works and a total of just 12 per cent for ODSP.
The last PC premier to raise OW rates was Davis 39 years ago in 1985.
And now there is now no living PC premier who has raised OW rates. The cumulative effect of multi-decade inaction - whether on housing or climate change - is now coming home to roost. Just look at food bank usage.
Harris lowered OW rates to $520 a month in October 1995 - a 21.6 per cent drop - but even if rates had been raised with inflation from that multi-decade low, they would now stand at $940 a month - literally $207 higher than $733.
In other words, even if you agreed with what Harris did in 1995, for the equivalent rate today, you would need a 28 per cent increase in rates to match it. I actually agree with Ford. I am driven crazy and I am bothered by the same situation that bothers him.
I want an income security system for Ontario Works recipients that is generous enough to allow them to live in a decent place and to have food security. I want them to be able to afford a working smartphone and a transit pass.
I want them to have earning exemptions on Ontario Works that allow them to earn a bit of money before they get cut off, retain some assets and get ahead.
The current system provides an income that is a floor below which people are not permitted to fall, but that same floor is designed to be a ceiling above which they cannot rise.
And that drives me crazy.
John Stapleton worked for the Ontario government in the Ministry of Community and Social Services for 28 years in the areas of social assistance policy and
operations.
CAPTION: Even if you agreed with what Mike Harris did in 1995,for the equivalent rate today, you would need a28 per cent increase in rates to match it, John Stapleton writes.canadian press file photo
