Document 241 of 252

This glass ceiling-breaking Liberal mom is looking to give the Tories a time out

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-glass-ceiling-breaking-liberal-mom-is/docview/2910805216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+glass+ceiling-breaking+Liberal+mom+is+looking+to+give+the+Tories+a+time+out&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Karina Gould knows how to throw a hefty political punch, and she often does it with a smile.
Just six months into her role as government House leader, Gould has redefined what it means to be a prime minister's chief attack dog.
During a fall parliamentary sitting that often looked out of control, political insiders say Gould was a steady thorn in her opponents' sides: calm and cutting at the same time.
They are crediting her with re-energizing the Liberal caucus after the party's poll numbers plummeted last year, while Opposition Conservatives relished in their own gains.
"For the last year, it felt like the Conservatives were the only party competing," said Fred DeLorey, a former national campaign manager for the Conservative Party of Canada.
"It wasn't clear what the Liberals were doing, if they were in search of a narrative or if they didn't understand they needed one. But it feels like in the last month, especially, there's something coming together there, and their attacks are becoming more pointed."
Gould is leading that charge, DeLorey said.
She is the youngest woman to have ever served as a federal cabinet minister, and the first federal minister to give birth while in office.
Her second child is due this month.
Right before Gould went on maternity leave in mid-December and the House adjourned for the holidays, she delivered a final message to Pierre Poilievre and his Conservative party that played heavily on her identity as a mom.
She had just removed an agenda item from the House's order paper after the Tories attempted, for the second time that month, to delay government work and keep MPs working late leading up to Christmas.
"Instead of entertaining his temper tantrum, I'm going to suggest he takes a little bit of a time out," she said about Poilievre in front of the House of Commons.
She stopped short of equating Tory MPs' behaviour to children. That would be too insulting to Canadian children, she said.
In a year-end interview, Gould said she's the same person when cameras are rolling on Parliament Hill as she is when she's at home in Burlington, the riding she represents nestled halfway between Toronto and Niagara Falls.
"There's no performance to me. I'm just telling you how it is and how I see things," Gould said.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau appointed her to lead the Liberals' agenda in the House of Commons in July after she had served as the minister for families, children and social development for nearly two years.
In that role, she dealt with a passport application backlog that caused major delays and political strife. She also helped oversee the Liberal government's marquee $10-a-day child-care plan, which included finalizing a deal with Ontario.
In previous parliamentary sessions, Gould also served as minister for international development and minister for democratic institutions.
The prime minister is clearly chuffed about her performance in the new role.
In a speech at a holiday party for the Liberal caucus in December, Trudeau spent a full two minutes praising Gould's accomplishments. She was the only minister he name-checked.
"We're lucky to have you, as a team and as a country," Trudeau said, and Gould got a prolonged standing ovation.
She admits there was no shortage of crisis and challenges in the fall sitting.
Former Speaker Anthony Rota, a Liberal MP, resigned his post amid controversy in September. During a visit by Ukraine's president to the Canadian Parliament, he honoured a Ukrainian Canadian veteran who fought for a Nazi unit in the Second World War.
"As someone whose family suffered immensely at the hands of the Nazis, it was a very difficult personal moment," Gould said.
Later, questions were raised about Speaker's conduct after Greg Fergus - another Liberal MP - filmed a video that was shown at a partisan event.
"Part of your job as leader is to be able to figure out a path forward," she said, during difficult moments.
As House leader, she helped the minority Liberal government get unanimous support to fast-track a bail-reform bill in September, which became law last month. She also shepherded through a bill that sought to address the affordability crisis by creating a tax break for new rental developments and updated competition laws.
Greg MacEachern, a Liberal strategist and founder of KAN Strategies, said Gould was successful in portraying "a calm competence" throughout. "Karina Gould's style is that of someone who is able to remain calm while the temperature is rising in the House," MacEachern said.
"No Liberals have questions over how she handled things, and they tend to like the contrast between her way of just continuing to speak at a very modulated level, pointing out the facts, driving a hole through the Conservative argument, yet doing it without seeming to sweat."
DeLorey said Gould has proven to be a solid communicator who is able to point out Tory weak points.
"The Conservatives need to make sure they can effectively counter that and push back when it's warranted," he said.
But at this point, he noted, Tories have yet to directly challenge Gould, proving she was the right pick to throw Trudeau's punches.
"She has been landing them, and it hasn't been something we've seen in some time," he said.
When the House resumes sitting in late January after a winter break, Gould will be missing from the front bench as she takes her maternity leave.
Government Whip Steve MacKinnon will become interim House leader, and Gould said she plans to vote for bills virtually and attend cabinet and caucus meetings by phone and video. It's a decision she made after stretching herself thin after having her first child in 2018 and taking just a nine-week break.
"I was very focused on two things: doing my job as then-minister of democratic institutions and MP of Burlington, and being a mom," she said. "I didn't have space for anything else in my life, including the fact that just before I gave birth, I lost my mom very suddenly to cancer, and there was a lot going on."

The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Political insiders have credited government House Leader Karina Gould with re-energizing the Liberal caucus afterthe party'spoll numbers plummeted last year.Adrian Wyld The Canadian Press file photo

Document 242 of 252

Doug Ford's Tories set to abandon lucrative fundraiser in wake of Greenbelt scandal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-set-abandon-lucrative/docview/2910678063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+set+to+abandon+lucrative+fundraiser+in+wake+of+Greenbelt+scandal&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the wake of the Greenbelt land swap scandal, Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are poised to scrap their most lucrative annual fundraiser, the Star has learned.
The "leader's dinner" set a record for Canadian political parties last March, bringing in $6 million to PC campaign coffers.
Held at the cavernous Toronto Congress Centre on Dixon Road, that $1,500-a-plate event was attended by 4,000 people, including property developers and their employees and family members.
But the Greenbelt affair, which is now the subject of an RCMP criminal investigation, has radically altered the landscape.
"No decision has been made on a leader's dinner this year," the party said Thursday in a terse statement one day after the Star inquired about the status of the annual gala.
But behind the scenes, Tories point out such large-scale fundraisers require months to organize, promote and sell tickets. No such activity is happening.
"It's smart not to do it this year," said one senior PC insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
The source noted many developers are still furious at Ford's reversal of the opening up of 7,400 acres of the environmentally sensitive two-million-acre Greenbelt to build houses.
His flip-flop could cost landowners $8.28 billion, according to some estimates.
That means they are far less likely to want to buy tickets to listen a banquet speech from the premier, the official said.
Last month, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, who took over the troubled department in September and has moved to kibosh some controversial decisions, profusely apologized to homebuilders.
"The failures that have happened over the last number of months aren't a failing of your industry," Calandra said Dec. 6 at the Building Industry and Land Development Association's (BILD) annual general meeting luncheon in Woodbridge.
"It's a failing of government - as how we have put together the things that we have wanted to accomplish," he said, candidly admitting the Tories only have themselves to blame.
"The speed at which we moved - and the inability of us to bring people with us - is what has caused the challenges over the last number of months and we will not repeat those mistakes going forward."
Party insiders also concede the optics of a "leader's dinner" are problematic in the aftermath of the Greenbelt saga.
Last August, separate reports to the legislature by the auditor general and the integrity commissioner concluded "certain developers" were "favoured" when Ford opened the Greenbelt to home construction in November 2022.
Public outcry, plunging poll numbers and private kvetching by Tory MPPs, who were hearing about the debacle from constituents across the province, led the premier to finally change course on Sept. 21.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry," Ford said at that time.
"I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise. As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes," he said.
"We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives."
So far, the Greenbelt fiasco has triggered the resignations of two cabinet members - Steve Clark and Kaleed Rasheed - and led to the departures of two senior aides.
Despite that, the Tories are still raising more money than the opposition New Democrats or Liberals, although final official 2023 tallies for the parties from Elections Ontario are not yet available.
While last year's sold-out PC leader's dinner was the largest single political fundraiser in Canadian history, it was closed to the media.
Under previous Tory, Liberal and NDP premiers, such partisan events were open to journalists, but Ford ended that practice in 2019 after the Star revealed lobbyists had been enlisted to sell tickets.
In his 2023 speech, the premier pointedly did not mention the Greenbelt, although he did emphasize the need to boost the supply of housing in fast-growing Ontario.
"We're taking bold action to build 1.5 million new homes (by 2031) to bring the dream of home ownership into reach for those who have been left behind," Ford said last March 2.
"For years, owning a home has been out of reach for too many families - at times, it's felt impossible, but together we're finally doing something about it," he said.
"Friends, this one is critical because we will have 300,000 people a year moving to Ontario, our communities are growing at a rapid pace. That's amazing news - we need every newcomer we can get, every new worker in search of the Ontario dream - and we need housing construction to keep pace."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at Queen's Park on Aug. 9, 2023.

Subject: Fund raising

Document 243 of 252

Hamilton Liberal MP says no CEBA extension on business loans is proposed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-liberal-mp-says-no-ceba-extension-on/docview/2909443143/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+Liberal+MP+says+no+CEBA+extension+on+business+loans+is+proposed&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A national business association's survey for December 2023 found that about 34 per cent of business owners have repaid their Canadian Emergency Business Account (CEBA) loans, but as much as 23 per cent of owners don't believe they will be able to make any payment.
There are about 40 per cent of business owners who are at risk of missing the current payment deadline of January 2024, said Canadian Federation of Independent Business media relations officer Dariya Baiguzhiyeva.
Baiguzhiyeva said that about 250,000 of small businesses across the country "are at risk of closing if the CEBA deadline isn't extended," with about 96,000 of them located in Ontario.
Hamilton Liberal MP and cabinet minister Filomena Tassi said in a recent interview that the Liberal government is not planning to give businesses another extension on their CEBA loans.
Tassi said the Liberal government has already provided three extensions.
"Small businesses can still have options," said Tassi. "We understand it is tough. COVID-19 took a toll. But we provided businesses with unprecedented support. We have already offered extensions."
The Canadian Chamber of Commerce, which represents the Hamilton Chamber of Commerce and over 280 industry associations, sent an open letter in July to Chrystia Freeland, the minister of finance, urging the government to extend the CEBA repayment deadline from Jan. 18, 2024, to the end of 2025 - or at the very least by one year.
In the letter, it states that many businesses "had no choice but to take on this loan due to circumstances beyond their control."
If no extension is allowed, businesses that are unable to pay back their loans will lose access to the forgivable portion of up to $20,000, which will only add to their debt load.
Hamilton Centre NDP MP Matthew Green said in a recent statement that, while the federal government is helping large corporations pay off their debt load, it is ignoring small businesses and non-profits.
"Shop owners have gone through so much with the global pandemic and now the cost-of-living crisis," he said. "We are calling on the Liberal government to support small businesses in Hamilton so they can make it through the new year."
Tassi said CEBA was offered to struggling businesses and non-profit organizations at the height of the COVID-19 pandemic. Eligible businesses were provided financial support to cover short-term operating expenses, payroll and other nondeferrable expenses. The program offered up to $60,000 in interest-free loans for businesses to help survive related shutdowns.
Nearly 900,000 businesses were approved for the program, which distributed just over $49 billion in loans.
The deadline had been extended for an additional year to Dec. 31, 2023, and then Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced a further extension in September for several weeks to avoid pressure during the holiday season. If a business wants a bit longer, they can choose to pay back the loan with interest and the government also extended that deadline an additional year to the end of 2026.
The parliamentary budget office estimates moving the deadline to the end of 2024 would cost the government about $907 million in delayed repayments and foregone interest payments.
CAPTION: Filomena Tassi, centre, Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas MP and the minister responsible for the Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario, said in a recent interview the federal government won't extend the CEBA loan deadline again.

Subject: Small business; Loans; Pandemics; COVID-19

Document 244 of 252

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2909436669/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What's being done on housing?
Homelessness is everywhere. Like COVID-19, it's a pandemic and we're watching what seems to be a slow response of our city officials in addressing the critical issue of homelessness. But without an influx of money from all three levels of government, and without compassionate and effective leadership to provide adequate shelters for more than 1,700 people in this city, the rest of this winter (and the foreseeable future) looks bleak. An influx of federal and provincial money is unlikely. Our governments are mired in debt and going in the wrong directions - they are subsidizing fossil fuel industries with billions of dollars, and planning to build a $10-billion unnecessary superhighway. And they cancelled the basic income pilot program before results could be determined. However, to its credit, Hamilton has developed a comprehensive housing and homelessness plan. I urge the city to provide a complete and transparent update on the current status of its planning efforts to address homelessness. That would do much to foster community trust and demonstrate a genuine commitment to tackling this humanitarian crisis.
James Newman, Hamilton
Transparency in grocery aisles
One of the factors driving grocery prices higher is "shrinkflation," the practice of reducing package size, quantity or colour matching the product in previously clear containers to mask product quantity but maintaining or increasing prices. I think legislation requiring all levels of the system to require manufacturers to modify the labelling of resized packaging to clearly and visibly identify the change in size or volume and the amount of the change is urgently required. I can see the new spin ads "new smaller easier to store packages," "lighter, easier to carry packages," "bottles colour-coded to your table decor," "no messy looking partially full bottles," etc. I would prefer to have my cereal boxes full again, my soup cans completely full and my Heinz ketchup bottles clear.
Carl Easton, Hamilton
There's still time to change
Re: Happy New Year. Peace on Earth? (Jan. 3)
After reading this article by Margaret Shkimba in the comment section of Wednesday's paper, it brought to mind the movie "A Christmas Carol" with Alastair Sim. Imagine Doug Ford playing this character and waking after a night of realization, knowing he could change himself into a caring, compassionate person. There is still time, Doug.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Recognition well deserved
Re: Billy Van, many faces in 'Hilarious House of Frightenstein,' getting his due (Dec. 29)
I knew the picture was that of Billy Van as the Count in "The Hilarious House of Frightenstein" even before reading the headline. But what a delight to see it in print after all these many decades. It brings back many happy memories; thanks for the piece. I watched "Hilarious House of Frightenstein" studiously and just loved the characters Van played, like the Wolfman (D.J.), Grizelda (the witch), the Oracle, the Count and the Librarian, to name a few. (I did a pretty good imitation of Van as the Count and the Librarian back in school, too). But I remember Van was also on "Party Game," along with Dinah Christie and Jack Duffy, which I watched every night on CHCH. Billy Van was so talented, funny and playing the many faces he did seemed to come so easy to him. It's great news that the Canadian Comedy Hall of Fame has finally given Billy Van the recognition he deserves.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
Put economist on committee
Re: New Hamilton advisory committee
to tackle climate crisis (Dec. 16)
The 18 members of the climate committee "bring to the table a variety of expertise, ranging from health care to engineering." Given that sustainable development should address environmental, social and economic issues, in my opinion, it is essential that at least one of the members is from the field of economics. Ideally, that person would be a climate change economist, because they examine the costs of both climate change and climate change adaptation. I consider it important that the committee be open to discussion about minimizing the negative economic impact of any actions recommended and not just address them as an afterthought.
Mark Melanson, Hamilton
Liberals' priorities in doubt
The Trudeau government can't decide on which side it is on in the Israel/Hamas war. It can't decide whether immigration is more important than homelessness and it can't decide whether it is more important to support developing countries vs. clean water/services for Indigenous people in the north. Thank God it can decide that we will all be able to afford and drive zero emission (electric) vehicles in 2035.
Al Dionisi, Stoney Creek
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Document 246 of 252

Divided progressives are the best friends a Conservative leader could have

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/divided-progressives-are-best-friends/docview/2909436044/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Divided+progressives+are+the+best+friends+a+Conservative+leader+could+have&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Most politicians are in the business of telling voters what they want to hear.
But some political parties just want to stay in business - without listening to what voters are telling them.
Progressives are a big part of the problem.
New Democrats, Liberals and Greens are so set on their own ways - and going their own way - that they can't see their way to coming together as a single party. By contrast, right-wing parties - notably the old Reform Party, Canadian Alliance and Progressive Conservatives - have long understood the imperative to unite the right under the umbrella of the Conservative Party of Canada.
Why not respond by rebranding as the combined Liberal Democrats? Rather than unite to fight the right, the centre-left clings to its partisan and picayune divisions - splitting the progressive vote every which way while allowing Tories to triumph with majority governments based on a minority of votes.
In my previous column at year-end, looking ahead to the next two years of electioneering in Canada, I predicted that progressive parties will continue to put their own vested interests ahead of the national and provincial interest. Given how similar they are ideologically, I called it the vanity and inanity of small differences.
The reaction from readers and tweeters suggests I'm hardly alone in worrying about the outlook. So in my first column of the new year, I'd like to give voters their say, both from my email inbox and the Star's letters page.
"It seems ludicrous that the vote on the right side of the political spectrum is always united while the vote on the left side is always split in two," wrote Scott Kennedy.
With national elections expected by 2025, the polls show Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre on track to become Canada's next prime minister. With an Ontario vote coming in 2026, polling suggests Doug Ford will be re-elected easily as premier for another four years after two full terms in power.
Proportional representation could reduce vote-splitting, but PR proposals keep failing in one referendum after another (most recently in Ontario in 2007). Electoral reform won't be on the agenda anytime soon, which is why I raised the idea of uniting Liberals and New Democrats as a thought experiment.
"About time someone suggested uniting the left," wrote Frank White. "We are never going to get PR so let's have the next best thing."
It's not enough to work together after the fact in a minority Parliament, as Liberals and New Democrats have with their confidence and supply arrangement - an informal alliance by which the NDP agrees not to defeat the governing Liberals in exchange for enacting shared goals such as denticare and pharmacare. If Liberals and New Democrats keep splitting the progressive vote in close three-way fights, the Tories could win many more ridings next time.
Patrick J. Mullin, who worked for a Liberal MP in the 1990s, told me about his conversation on Parliament Hill with "the young Reform MP Stephen Harper ... about the problem (of) the right splitting the vote to let the Liberals in under Jean Chrétien." Ever since, Mullin said, he has "pushed bringing the Liberals and NDP and maybe Greens."
But the quintessential Canadian conundrum persists, election after election: Progressives would rather fight than unite.
Matthew Lebo, chair of the political science department at Western University, reached out to describe his surprise after teaching for two decades in the U.S. that Canadian progressives persist in pummelling one another.
"For comparison, the ideological breadth of the American Democratic Party would easily hold the NDP, the Liberals and the Canadian Green Party," he wrote in an email. "To beat the Republican Party, they need to be in the same party. Even Bernie Sanders understood that when he lined up behind Biden in 2016."
In the U.S., Lebo likes to say, "the point of political participation is to keep your least favourite major party from winning. When I say that in Canada, people get mad. Canadians seem to feel everyone should have a party very close to them ideologically."
The result is that the right wing wins. Yet progressives persist in depriving themselves of power.
Another potential benefit to uniting the left is that the right would be forced to broaden its appeal toward the centre, rather than always pandering to its base.
"Now, the Conservatives know that they can win a majority with about 37 per cent of the vote," he argues. "To get there, they can ignore the political centre - they just need to keep the right united. But a New Liberal Democratic Party would force the Conservatives to adapt and moderate."
While the letters and emails in response to my thought experiment were thoughtful and positive, it's worth mentioning the tidal wave of hate, hostility, vulgarisms and venom that the column inspired on social media from self-styled right wingers. No matter - it's hard to take X seriously these days, especially on holidays - and one can't posit that small-minded trolls are representative of Tories at large, just as letter writers aren't especially reflective of the electorate.
That said, scrolling through the screeds - a feeding frenzy of algorithmic apoplexy in the thousands - I couldn't help thinking that the mere thought of a united left had unleashed a torrent of terror among trolls of the far-right. All the more reason for the left to get its act together.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre addresses the national Conservative caucus in Ottawa on Dec.6, 2023.

Subject: Electoral reform; Conservatism; Progressivism; Voters; Political parties

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Hamilton Liberal MP is firm on no CEBA loan extension: Federal government has extended the deadline three times

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hamilton-liberal-mp-is-firm-on-no-ceba-loan/docview/2909434452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+Liberal+MP+is+firm+on+no+CEBA+loan+extension%3A+Federal+government+has+extended+the+deadline+three+times&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A national business association's survey for December 2023 found that about 34 per cent of business owners have repaid their Canadian Emergency Business Account (CEBA) loans, but as much as 23 per cent of owners don't believe they will be able to make any payment.
There are about 40 per cent of business owners who are at risk of missing the current payment deadline of January 2024, said Canadian Federation of Independent Business media relations officer Dariya Baiguzhiyeva.
Baiguzhiyeva said that about 250,000 of small businesses across the country "are at risk of closing if the CEBA deadline isn't extended," with about 96,000 of them located in Ontario.
Hamilton Liberal MP and cabinet minister Filomena Tassi said in a recent interview that the Liberal government is not planning to give businesses another extension on their CEBA loans.
Tassi said the Liberal government has already provided three extensions.
"Small businesses can still have options," said Tassi. "We understand it is tough. COVID-19 took a toll. But we provided businesses with unprecedented support. We have already offered extensions."
The Canadian Chamber of Commerce, which represents the Hamilton Chamber of Commerce and over 280 industry associations, sent an open letter in July to Chrystia Freeland, the minister of finance, urging the government to extend the CEBA repayment deadline from Jan. 18, 2024, to the end of 2025 - or at the very least by one year.
In the letter, it states that many businesses "had no choice but to take on this loan due to circumstances beyond their control."
If no extension is allowed, businesses that are unable to pay back their loans will lose access to the forgivable portion of up to $20,000, which will only add to their debt load.
Hamilton Centre NDP MP Matthew Green said in a recent statement that, while the federal government is helping large corporations pay off their debt load, it is ignoring small businesses and non-profits.
"Shop owners have gone through so much with the global pandemic and now the cost-of-living crisis," he said. "We are calling on the Liberal government to support small businesses in Hamilton so they can make it through the new year."
Tassi said CEBA was offered to struggling businesses and non-profit organizations at the height of the COVID-19 pandemic. Eligible businesses were provided financial support to cover short-term operating expenses, payroll and other nondeferrable expenses. The program offered up to $60,000 in interest-free loans for businesses to help survive related shutdowns.
Nearly 900,000 businesses were approved for the program, which distributed just over $49 billion in loans.
The deadline had been extended for an additional year to Dec. 31, 2023, and then Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced a further extension in September for several weeks to avoid pressure during the holiday season. If a business wants a bit longer, they can choose to pay back the loan with interest and the government also extended that deadline an additional year to the end of 2026.
The parliamentary budget office estimates moving the deadline to the end of 2024 would cost the government about $907 million in delayed repayments and foregone interest payments.
CAPTION: Filomena Tassi, centre, is the minister responsible for the Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario.Kevin Werner Metroland

Political parties in need of reform

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/political-parties-need-reform/docview/2909433365/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Political+parties+in+need+of+reform&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Political parties are normally conservative about their internal rules and processes. There is one exception: when a competitor makes an apparently successful big change in how it is led. Then all the others jump to copy it.
The "tyranny of delegated conventions" was condemned as anti-democratic, smoky backrooms by the Reform Party in the '80s. Far more democratic to allow "one member, one vote." They ignored that we live in a representative democracy. There is no one citizen, one vote in any legislature. Nonetheless, every political party had adopted its version of this tomfoolery.
Alberta's United Conservative Party saw the attack on backrooms as useful to appeal to angry voters. So they began the creation of an unfortunate monster, which is now on the verge of eating the party from within. The idea that clicking an internet box makes you a party member is, in itself, absurd. Not really more democratic than organizers handing out $10 bills to get your signature in the "bad old days."
Now fake memberships have been part of Canadian politics for decades, especially in leadership races. But never on the wholesale level that the internet offers. You have the same problems with proof of IDs, even if the party rules claim every signature is proofed. Can your signature be proofed, by staring at it, or is it as illegible as mine? Ridings can still submit a dozen or more memberships at one time, so long as they been "proofed."
Let's ignore Conservative spokespeople's indignant rage at critics of Pierre Poilievre's claim to have sold hundreds of thousands of new memberships in just a few weeks. Proof will be impossible to find and so eyeballs rolled and eyebrows raised are all that we can do in the face of their claims of an improbable feat - one never before achieved in any party in any campaign ever.
Because there are two more serious problems than vote selling. Your $15 makes you a voting member of the party and you directly elect the leader.
The second problem is that many of "box ticking" new $15 members mostly disappear once the leadership has taken place - many do not even bother to vote even once. So the party organizers look at the two out of three of this instant member class disappearance, and bomb them with email pleas.
When Jeremy Corbyn used similar methods to take over the U.K. Labour Party, one organizer in his own local Labour Party told me that one year later their renewal rate was about 25 per cent of the new Corbynites. Conservatives are facing heavy attrition, too.
And the icing on the cake: CSIS has revealed, late and with little detail as always, that they have evidence that China and Russia had created thousands of keen new Tories as their contribution to Canadian democracy. Ontario Liberals might want to have a look at their thousands of new members in the face of this revelation.
So here's an idea. Is any party brave enough to try to find a system that requires members to have been active for more than one year to vote? Who get elected to represent their fellow committed partisans by them, at an in-person event? Those chosen will no doubt have bragged about their activity in recruiting candidates, canvassing or helping fundraise. In a leadership contest they will have declared who they support. They then represent their riding at a federal or provincial convention - in a large convention hall on live television - as their voters have instructed them. Perhaps, even on a one-time pilot basis.
You might call it "delegated democracy."
Isn't it time our parties behaved less like slippery online gambling promoters, demanding cash from everyone several times a month, according to unknown digital selection prompts?
Robin V. Sears was an NDP strategist for 20 years and later served as a communications adviser to businesses and governments on three continents. He is a freelance contributing columnist for Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Robin V. Sears;

Document 249 of 252

Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives have been 'very good' at demonizing the carbon levy, environment minister admits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-conservatives-have-been-very/docview/2909891769/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+Conservatives+have+been+%27very+good%27+at+demonizing+the+carbon+levy%2C+environment+minister+admits&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The federal environment minister admits Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives have controlled the narrative on carbon pricing, but promises the Liberals will step up their communications game in 2024.
"The Conservatives have been very good at convincing some Canadians that carbon pricing is responsible for everything that's going wrong in their lives," Steven Guilbeault told the Star in an interview.
Guilbeault's admission comes after a year in which debate over Ottawa's carbon pricing program has featured prominently in Parliament, and the Tories have routinely blamed the levy for Canadians' troubles with affordability.
The Liberals have struggled to maintain support for their flagship climate policy, which is expected to remain a hot topic as Poilievre has repeatedly framed the next election as a "referendum on the carbon tax."
The basic idea of a carbon price is to deter people from burning planet-warming fossil fuels by imposing an extra cost when people and businesses use them. Ottawa says most Canadians receive more in carbon rebates than they pay - and most economists agree. But that hasn't stopped the "axe the tax" slogan from dominating Conservative rallies, and public opinion from shifting away from support for the carbon pricing program, polls suggest.
"We need to better communicate, for sure," Guilbeault said. "I'll be the first one to admit that we're not always on our A-game when it comes to communicating on these things."
Guilbeault did not elaborate on how the government planned to fine-tune its messaging, saying only that "we have to explain something that is rather complex, and all (Poilievre) has to say is carbon pricing is making the cost of everything go up."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has also acknowledged the Conservatives have "successfully scapegoated the price on pollution as being the reason everything is expensive right now," in an interview with The Canadian Press, but has stopped short of admitting the government has failed to communicate effectively about its policies.
Trevor Tombe, an economist at the University of Calgary, said it's a tough task for the government to get people on board for any "visible charge," but disputed Guilbeault's claim that the issue is complex. The challenge, he said, is getting Canadians to understand the importance of the policy in tackling climate change and raising revenue.
"The current carbon taxes are simple. Even if one disagrees with them, or if they're not popular, they're simple: 'We're going to charge you for emitting.' That's it," Tombe told the Star. "'Any action we take is going to come with costs.'"
The Bank of Canada said in September the so-called carbon "tax" was contributing 0.15 percentage points to the increase in inflation.
"The contribution that's making to inflation one year to the next is relatively small," Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem said at the time.
But amid affordability concerns, many Canadians will remain unwilling to change their behaviours to address climate change, an issue the Conservatives have effectively capitalized on, said Abacus Data CEO David Coletto.
"People are much more aware and worried about the impact of it (climate change) ... but there's still resistance to it," Coletto told the Star. "Until that changes, I don't think you're gonna get the public putting climate action ahead of cost of living."
Amid a drop in public support and under pressure from his own Atlantic MPs, Trudeau exempted home heating oil from the levy last fall. That led to calls from the Conservatives, the NDP and many provinces for the exemption to be applied to all home heating. First Nations in Ontario launched a lawsuit arguing the rebates were discriminatory. Saskatchewan said it would stop collecting the carbon levy on natural gas.
The government's move - and the response to it - highlighted the Liberals' communications problem, Tombe said: even as Ottawa was exempting heating oil over affordability concerns, it was still pushing back against the criticism that the carbon levy was increasing prices.
In an ideal world, Guilbeault told the Star, the carve-out wouldn't have been necessary in the first place, but he rejected the idea that it means the entire carbon pricing program is faulty.
He said Ottawa will move ahead with scheduled increases until the price of carbon reaches $170 per tonne in 2030, and predicted Poilievre's constant attacks on the Liberals' environmental policies will soon stop resonating with Canadians.
"I think that the tactics and the lies of Poilievre will become old very quick," he said.
However, Guilbeault acknowledged that a Conservative election win could mean the reversal of many of his government's climate policies, including the oil and gas emissions capand a zero-emissions vehicle mandate. Recent modelling from Environment and Climate Change Canada suggested the country was on track to achieve its interim 2026 climate targets.
"Canadians should be very concerned," Guilbeault said.
But he said his focus remains on what he called Canada's most ambitious climate plan ever. Despite rumours he considered quitting after the heating oil carve-out, Guilbeault also continues to insist that he's here to stay.
"I would be happy to fight another federal election on carbon pricing," he told the Star.
"I have no desire to leave."
- With files from Alex Ballingall and The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault speaks in Toronto on Dec.19, 2023.

Subject: Canadians; Climate policy; Environmental policy; Carbon; Climate change; Heating

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Canada; NAICS: 521110

Document 250 of 252

Don't count Ford out yet

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dont-count-ford-out-yet/docview/2909176366/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don%27t+count+Ford+out+yet&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As we enter a new year in politics, progressive voters in Ontario might be forgiven for thinking new Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie, with experience and name recognition other leaders only dream of, might sound the death knell for Doug Ford's scandal plagued Tories.
Coupled with Marit Stiles, recently elected Ontario NDP leader, a feisty parliamentarian who, until now, has been the face of the opposition and the recent Kitchener Centre byelection victory of Doug Schreiner's Ontario Green Party, momentum on the left seems unstoppable.
However, while Ford might face tough question periods from three very capable leaders, his premiership and Queen's Park majority remain unassailable until the 2026 election and even then, the law of unintended political consequences could carry him through that.
Conservatives' mistrust of the McGinty/Wynne years is unlikely to heal any time soon, so Ford's lock on those voters will hold. If fatigue with scandals drives some Tories away, Schreiner's Greens may benefit more than Stiles' NDP, who are anathema to Ford Nation stalwarts. And while Crombie's presence should revive Liberals, will she regain voters who deserted them in 2018 and 2022 for the NDP, who in turn may be net losers in a Liberal/Green resurgence?
The things that could keep Ford in power are not new. Election gimmickry, voter apathy and age-old division on the left gave him his 2018 and 2022 victories. He has never won a majority of the votes cast (40.8 per cent in 2022), and despite his claims to a mandate, fewer than 18 per cent of the electorate voted for him last time.
His 2018 vote grabbing "Buck a Beer" gimmick helped him defeat a tired and mistrusted Liberal government without any serious policy alternatives, and in 2022 his free "car licence stickers" gulled voters while stripping $2 billion from long-term care and health care, providing a smokescreen for his greenbelt/developer boondoggles.
Now, he has announced termination of The Beer Store Master Framework Agreement, two years ahead of its December 2025 expiry, drawing press and public attention from his flip flops, failures and RCMP investigations, conveniently putting "booze in corner stores" just in time for the 2026 election.
Elector apathy will only be overcome by campaigns that excite voters. Wynne and Andrea Horwath failed to do that in 2018 and 2022. And while two new leaders on the left with a resurgent Green Party may fire up voters, vote splitting on the left is still likely to give Ford a 2026 majority.
On most major issues: housing, education, environment, transportation and social policies, the three left-of-centre parties are separated only by semantics and party allegiance. Yet, in a province thoroughly fed up with cronyism and allegations of corruption, where the majority consistently vote other than conservative, vote splitting could, as in previous elections, hand power to Ford with as little as 18 per cent of the electorate.
An October 2023 Abacus poll suggested 80 per cent of Ontarians want an end to Ford's mismanagement. Forty-seven per cent are committed to opposition parties while 33 per cent are looking for "a worthy substitute." In an election, it projects Ford might get 29 per cent of votes. Liberals, NDP and Greens would amass 41 per cent support with four per cent for "other" and a huge 27 per cent undecided.
Ontarians are ready for change, but three separate opposition campaigns and the funding needed to finance them may deprive them of that opportunity. With so much in common, it is time for them to put party vanity and leader's egos aside. Time for a unified effort to elect a merged slate of candidates who share policy goals the majority of Ontarians already support. Time to provide an electoral home for those 27 to 33 per cent who don't want Ford but still seek a winning alternative, to combine fundraising to win that campaign spending battle Tories so often master.
It is time to stop competing with each other to compete more aggressively against Ontario's common enemy, Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives. The alternative should be unthinkable to them.
CAPTION: Vote splitting could, as in previous elections, hand power toDoug Ford with as little as 18 per cent of the electorate, Jim Young writes.
CREDIT: Jim Young

Subject: Apathy; Voters; Elections

Title: Don't count Ford out yet

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 3, 2024

Don't count Ford out yet

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dont-count-ford-out-yet/docview/2909040690/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don%27t+count+Ford+out+yet&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Jim Young is a resident
of Burlington.

Document 252 of 252

Bonnie Crombie smashes fundraising target as Liberals collect $1.2M

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-smashes-fundraising-target-as/docview/2909279009/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+smashes+fundraising+target+as+Liberals+collect+%241.2M&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rookie Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has eclipsed her target of raising $1 million in a month for the third-place party.
Crombie, who won the Liberal crown on Dec. 2, said the Grits brought in more than $1.2 million during her inaugural weeks at the helm.
"On my first day on the job as Ontario Liberal Leader, I set an ambitious goal: to raise $1 million by our 2023 year-end deadline," she said in an email to contributors being sent out this week.
"Well, thanks to thousands of donors like you ... I am so thrilled to announce that we exceeded that goal, raising $1,213,181!"
The Mississauga mayor, who steps down from her municipal duties Jan. 12, emphasized the Liberals have their work cut out for them if they hope to topple Premier Doug Ford's two-term Progressive Conservatives in the next election.
"Ontario Liberals are back, and we're putting in the hard work necessary to defeat Doug Ford in 2026 and replace the scandal-plagued Conservatives with a government that will put the people of Ontario first," said Crombie.
"We know we have to close the considerable fundraising gap between us and the Conservatives, and I'm so grateful to everyone who contributed to doing exactly that last month," she said.
"We still have a lot of work to do between now and 2026 to grow our team, catch up to the Conservatives' fundraising and offer Ontario voters a plan they trust. This year-end fundraising total represents a strong start on our path to victory."
Her announcement comes as the Tories are stepping up a multimillion-dollar advertising blitz attacking the new Liberal chief.
The governing party, which views her as a threat in the seat-rich Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area, hopes to negatively define Crombie for voters using TV, radio and online spots.
In an interview with the Star two weeks ago, she said the Tory ads are to "distract people" from problems like the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is now under RCMP investigation.
Two of Ford's cabinet ministers and a pair of senior aides have resigned or been forced to quit in the wake of the debacle triggered by the premier's since-reversed scheme to open up 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive land to housing development.
Last August, separate watchdog reports from the auditor general and the integrity commissioner concluded certain developers with PC connections were "favoured" in the land swap that ended up being scrapped in September.
The latest Abacus Data poll for the Star suggested Ford's party is weathering the Greenbelt fiasco after a rocky summer.
According to Abacus, the Tories were at 39 per cent support last month compared to 27 per cent for the Liberals, 24 per cent for Marit Stiles's Official Opposition New Democrats and six per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
Using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform, Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians Dec. 7-12. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
In the previous poll in November, the Tories were at 42 per cent support, the NDP at 24 per cent, the Liberals at 23 per cent and the Greens at seven per cent.
The Liberals, who governed Ontario under premiers Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty from 2003 until Ford's 2018 victory, now have only nine MPPs in the 124-member legislature.
That's three shy of official party status, meaning the Grits don't get regular questions to the government during the daily question period and have no standing on legislative committees.
The Tories have 79 MPPs, including Speaker Ted Arnott who does not caucus with them, while the New Democrats have 28 and the Greens have two. There are five Independents and one vacancy.
Nor do the Liberals receive public funding for staff and research, which is why Crombie, who is not an MPP, has made raising money a priority.
"This is the party's strongest year-end fundraising result ever under the new fundraising rules," she wrote to donors. "In fact, it triples our usual year-end fundraising results."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks at a news conference at Queen's Park on Dec. 5, 2023.
